# curiosity

**!!! (Chk Chk Chk) – From a 1980 South African Film and the Juǀʼhoan Click Language** : Named after the 1980 film The Gods Must Be Crazy, which featured a character speaking Juǀʼhoan, an indigenous South African language that uses mouth clicks. In written form, a click is represented by an exclamation mark. The character's name was pronounced approximately "chk chk chk." "You pronounce the man's name as chick, chick, chick." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**"50-Mission Cap" Symbolism** : A "50-mission cap" was a baseball-style cap worn by World War II fighter pilots. A worn cap indicated a seasoned pilot who had survived at least 50 missions. In the context of a Tragically Hip song, Gord Downie used it metaphorically to describe individuals (including his lyrical persona) who intentionally distressed their caps to project an image of having more experience than they genuinely possessed. "A 50-mission cap was a baseball-type cap thing worn by fighter pilots in World War II. If you had one with lots of wear, it meant that you were a seasoned pilot who had survived at least 50 missions." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dnz_Ycd_q7s
**"Advanced Basics" Philosophy** : The title "Advanced Basics" for USS's 2014 album encapsulates a life philosophy: "the simplest things are the hardest things." This concept reflected the band's challenging period during the album's creation, requiring a "one foot in front of the other" approach. "Advanced basics is about that just the title of that song is like a very wonderful life philosophy which is the simplest things are the hardest things." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=N-3eddy4J7g
**"Amadeus" Film and Mozart's Creative Process** : The film "Amadeus" is cited as a favorite, particularly Salieri's observation about Mozart's work containing "no eraser marks" or "corrections," as if he were "taking dictation." This description is used by Ash Boo Shultz to explain his own evolved creative process, where he experiences direct "downloads" of information after shedding personal layers. "In the movie, Amadeus, which is one of my favorite movies, Solary says, when he was looking at Mozart's work, he said, there's no eraser marks. There's no corrections. It's almost like he's taking dictation." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=N-3eddy4J7g
**"Are You Talking to Me?" – Originated with Bruce Springsteen; Passed Through Robert De Niro to Punk Culture via Joe Strummer** : Robert De Niro had a night off from filming Taxi Driver in 1976 and went to a Bruce Springsteen concert. Between songs, Springsteen turned his back to the crowd and waited for the noise to build, then turned and said "Are you talking to me?" De Niro used the line the next day while filming the famous mirror scene, improvising repeatedly — director Martin Scorsese loved it. Separately, Joe Strummer saw Taxi Driver and was so impressed by De Niro's Travis Bickle that he copied the Mohawk haircut for himself and The Clash, which is how the Mohawk became embedded in punk culture. "That is how the Mohawk became ingrained in punk culture." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**"Art Context" Podcast Objective** : The "Art Context, the Architects of Art" podcast aims to provide a platform for music video directors and filmmakers to share their stories, including their career beginnings, inspirations, triumphs, challenges, and the intricate processes of creating music videos, films, and other content. It intends to offer a behind-the-scenes look at the art form. "So our show is pretty much about, it's in the world of music. It's pretty much us sitting down with storytellers that come from music videos." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5PJRCC6EwUY
**"B/W" and "C/W" Terminology** : The abbreviations "B/W" (Backed With) and "C/W" (Coupled With) were used on record labels to indicate the B-side track of a single. These were alternative ways of specifying the secondary song on a two-sided record. "That B Stroke W stands for backed with. It's another way of saying this is the B side. You might also see in rare instances C Stroke W, which stands for coupled with, which makes the same thing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**"Baby Shark" Earnings** : The song "Baby Shark" has generated over $100 million for its owners, Smart Study, a South Korean company, and its education division, Pinkfong. Kim Min-Seok is named as a co-founder and beneficiary of this wealth. "The song Baby Shark has so far made its owners over $100 million." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=72Lei7uug8c
**"Battle of Britpop" Media Frenzy - August 15, 1995** : The synchronized release of Blur's "Country House" and Oasis's "Roll With It" on August 15, 1995, triggered an unprecedented media frenzy in the UK. Tabloids covered personal lives (dancer's vices, Noel Gallagher's mother's drinking), the Financial Times ran financial profiles, bookies took bets, and the BBC covered it on the nightly news with serious gravity. "Every single media outlet in the UK was mobilized for this event." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DSvoppf1dGY
**"Beaver Hours" Loophole and CRTC Intervention** : Early attempts by some Top 40 radio stations to circumvent Cancon rules involved editing Canadian songs down to 60-90 seconds and jamming them between 11 PM and midnight to meet quotas. The CRTC discovered this practice and quickly shut down the loophole, declaring these "beaver hours" illegal and initiating modified rules. "When the CRTC found out about that, they shut that loophole pretty quick and initiated some modified rules, making these beaver hours. Seriously, that's what some people call them, illegal." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6PGgNCaW4xo
**"Black Orpheus" Film's Impact on "Reflektor"** : The Brazilian movie "Black Orpheus," an adaptation of an old Greek story, served as a key influence for Arcade Fire's "Reflektor" album. The band delved into both the rhythmic qualities of the film's music and its overarching themes of isolation and death during the album's creation. "The band also spent some time with a Brazilian movie called Black Orpheus, which is based on an old Greek story." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ir2FfLQJNtY
**"Blackboard Jungle" Film and Audience Reaction** : The 1955 movie *Blackboard Jungle*, depicting a teacher's struggles in a multi-racial inner-city school, sparked widespread rebellious behavior among teenagers, particularly in Britain in 1956, due to its use of Bill Haley's "Rock Around the Clock." Audiences danced in aisles, carved up theater seats, got into fights, and even turned firehoses on each other, prompting calls for the movie to be banned. "Teenagers ate this up, especially in Britain in 1956. It became a thing to dance in the aisles. Carve up the theater seats with knives, get into fights, and even turn firehoses on each other in the theater." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**"Celestial Jukebox" Concept (1994)** : In 1994, law professor Paul Goldstein coined and popularized the term "Celestial Jukebox" to describe his vision of a networked database, accessible to anyone with an internet connection, that would contain all music. "In 1994, a law professor named Paul Goldstein popularized the term Celestial Jukebox." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**"Cold War" Metaphor in Music** : The division between mainstream rock and alternative rock from the 70s to 90s was akin to a "cold war" of musical ideology, outlook, and aspirations. Crossing over was compared to taking a stroll across the DMZ between North and South Korea, highlighting the extreme rigidity of musical allegiances. "During this period, these two tribes were actually locked in a cold war. It was a war of musical ideology, musical outlook, and musical aspirations, and woe to any tribe member who tried to change tribes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2EerEOuP52I
**"Coming of Age" Musical Immersion (Ages 14-24)** : Most individuals are deeply immersed in music and music culture between the ages of 14 and 24. The songs listened to during this period significantly shape an individual's musical taste for their entire life. However, once people reach their mid-20s, adult responsibilities like jobs, families, and mortgages often reduce the time, money, and inclination to actively seek out new music and attend shows. "The songs we listen to then sets our musical taste for the rest of our life." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m7MbV-eCLyE
**"Cool Britannia" / "Swinging London" Era - Mid-90s** : In the mid-90s, London was portrayed as the "most happening place on the planet," culturally, socially, politically, and economically. American publications like Vanity Fair even celebrated this "Swinging London" or "Cool Britannia" phenomenon. "England was portrayed as the place to be, the most happening place on the planet. Even by such American publications as Vanity Fair, Swing and London and Cool Britannia and all that." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DSvoppf1dGY
**"Crate Digging" Defined** : "Crate digging" is a term used to describe the activity of searching for and purchasing used records from various sources such as record shops, record shows, and other collectors. "You may buy your records brand new right off the shelf, which is fine, or you may want to start crate digging. That is, buying used records at shops, records shows, and from other collectors." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IIJySSo276k
**"Devil's Music" Label for Rhythm and Blues** : In the 1930s and 1940s, rhythm and blues was famously labeled "the devil's music," with accusations that it inevitably led to crime. This demonization reflects a historical pattern of religious and moral condemnation directed at emerging genres perceived as a threat to societal values. "This was the devil's music that inevitably led to crime." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**"Diggers" and Scalping in Traditional Ticketing** : "Diggers" were individuals hired by scalpers to stand in line for hours or even days at box offices to buy the maximum number of tickets, which were then resold at inflated prices. This practice was common in the pre-computer era, exploiting the inefficiencies of physical ticket queues. "Well, they were hired by scalpers to scoop up as many tickets as they could. The term for those placeholder people were diggers." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OCkov6FTliE
**"Dildo" – A 16th-Century Classical Music Filler Word** : The word "dildo" originated in 16th-century England as a filler word in songs — the "la la la" of its day. Written compositions of the era for lute and counter-tenor use the word extensively as a lyrical placeholder. "Dildo is a classical music term." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**"Divestiture Laws" – Financial Censorship Forcing Pension Funds to Sell Shares in Music Companies** : A subtle form of financial censorship used in the United States, "divestiture laws" compel state agencies and funds — such as teachers' pension funds — to divest from companies that distribute or control music considered objectionable. For example, a teachers' pension fund could be forced to sell its shares in Seagram, which owned Universal Music, home of Marilyn Manson. "Forced state agencies and funds to sell shares in companies distributing 'objectionable' music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=f4TOWLYRnGs
**"Dun Dun Dun" Audio Meme – From Stravinsky's 1913 Rite of Spring; Modern Form from 1984 Production Record** : The dramatic "dun dun dun" musical cue's first apparent appearance was in Stravinsky's 1913 ballet Rite of Spring. Its contemporary form likely derives from a production record made in 1984, officially titled "Shock Horror A," using the notes D-sharp, C, and F-sharp. "Dun dun dun. Where did that audio meme come from?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**"Elephant Parts" VHS Tape (1981)** : This 1981 VHS tape, produced by video pioneer and former Monkees member Michael Nesmith, contained a skit from which The Tragically Hip took their name. The tape was "making the rounds" when the band formed in 1984. "taking their name from a skit in a 1981 VHS tape making the rounds called Elephant Parts." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dnz_Ycd_q7s
**"Explore Music" Radio and TV Program** : "Explore Music" was an offshoot radio and TV program that primarily focused on music interviews. "For a while, there was an offshoot radio and TV program called Explore Music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bI6TTA-fn7c
**"Facility Fee" for Venues** : In addition to service charges, a "facility fee" may be levied on concert tickets, with the revenue going to the venue to help cover its costs. While venues also generate income from concessions, parking, and alcohol, this fee is an additional way for them to make money from ticket sales. "There may also be a service charge that goes to the venue for the same reason. This became known as a facility fee in some territories." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**"Get Your Own Riffs"** : This phrase originated from a sign reportedly posted on Oasis's rehearsal room door by another band, accusing them of using unoriginal material. The phrase became a symbolic critique of plagiarism and unoriginality in music, reappearing in the discussion of other artists. "the sign said, get your own riffs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L09R2evjyMc
**"Good Vibrations" Title – Inspired by Brian Wilson's Mother's Theory About Dogs and Vibrations** : Brian Wilson's mother told him that dogs could pick up vibrations from people, which is why they bark at some but not others. Wilson originally titled the song "Good Vibes," but after bringing in someone to play a theremin-like instrument, the title became "Good Vibrations." "It's all about the vibrations they sense from individual people, she said." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**"Gothic Goblins" Term** : Ian Astbury, who would later front The Cult, coined the term "Gothic Goblins" to describe the followers of Sex Gang Children, contributing to the evolving lexicon of the goth scene. "The term Goth stuck a little harder after Ian Aspery, who would later front the cult, called the followers of a group called Sex Gang Children, Gothic Goblins." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XaLsZFLrMnI
**"Harvey Weinstein, the Musical" Produced in Northern Ireland** : A musical titled "Harvey Weinstein, the musical" has been produced and is running out of Northern Ireland. This production emerges while Harvey Weinstein continues to face consequences for years of sexual misconduct and abuse. "Somebody is already running Harvey Weinstein, the musical. It's coming out of Northern Ireland." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-N6IM5Nm5iw
**"Heartbreak Beats" Extended Mix** : An extended mix of the song "heartbreak beats" was mentioned as a track favored by alt-rock radio staff in the pre-grunge era, not only for its artistic merit but also for providing practical time buffers during on-air shifts. "The second other first is heartbreak beats, the extended mix." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**"Home Taping Is Killing Music" Campaign (Early 1980s)** : The music industry launched a major campaign in the early 1980s with the slogan "Home Taping Is Killing Music." This initiative aimed to persuade the public that creating mix tapes was both unethical and illegal, urging consumers to buy official pre-recorded versions. However, the campaign largely failed, with the logo ironically becoming a popular meme, even adopted by bands on their merchandise and stage backdrops, and some artists actively encouraged bootlegging. `"There was a big campaign launched in the early 80s, something called Home Taping Is Killing Music, which tried to convince us that this practice was not only unethical but illegal."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**"I'm Alive, She's Dead, I'm Yours" T-shirts – Malcolm McLaren's Marketing** : Following Nancy Spungen's death and Sid Vicious's arrest, Malcolm McLaren's clothing shop on King's Road began selling highly controversial t-shirts that read, "I'm alive, she's dead, I'm yours." "Back at Malcolm McLaren's clothing shop on King's Road, they started selling t-shirts that read, I'm alive, she's dead, I'm yours." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OcylgiXkDP8
**"ICE" - Box Office Corruption** : "ICE" was the term for payoffs made by scalpers to box office managers in exchange for early access to the best printed tickets. This form of corruption was rampant, with estimates suggesting over $10 million annually paid for Broadway shows in New York City in the early 1960s. "The payoff to the box office manager was called ICE." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OCkov6FTliE
**"Jesus Walks" Horn Influence** : The horn section arrangement on Arkells' song "Knockin' at the Door" was directly inspired by the live version of Kanye West's "Jesus Walks." Specifically, the idea came from a Dave Sheepell block party where Kanye West performed with a school marching band, creating a "bomb. Boom. Boom. Boom. Boom." sound that the band sought to interject into their song, alongside a solo trumpet reminiscent of going to battle. "Tony, fashion the horns are like, what it like, kind of like Jesus walks by Kanye West." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kKNn01M8GG4
**"Jingle Bells" – Originally Written for American Thanksgiving (1857)** : "Jingle Bells" was written by James Lord Pierpont and published in 1857 as a song celebrating American Thanksgiving, not Christmas. "It was originally written as a song to celebrate American Thanksgiving." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**"Johnny Apple Seeds of Rock"** : The Ramones earned this nickname because their influence inspired thousands of young people to pick up guitars. Wherever the band played, new local bands seemed to emerge in their wake, spreading their musical legacy. "Some people call them the Johnny Apple Seeds of Rock because wherever they stop to play, new bands sprung up in their wake." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=j8uk7BnbXwU
**"Learned Helplessness" and Artistic Aspiration** : Ash described experiencing a period of "learned helplessness" in his life, which he attributed to childhood trauma. During this time, he characterized himself as a "victim" and a "pothead" whose ambition and organizational skills had diminished. This psychological state, marked by shame over his unfulfilled potential, motivated him to create a personal "script" for a new identity, ultimately leading to the philosophical foundation of USS and its name. "Generally for the most part people either become overachievers or they become victims as a result of traumatic experience in childhood and in my case I went towards the victim side of things and that learned helplessness." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ayPJfDAF_to
**"License Plate Names" Trend** : Around 1979, there was a trend for bands to adopt short, memorable names, referred to as "license plate names," exemplified by groups like INXS, U2, and XTC. "That's back when it was really hot to have what I call a license plate name, you know, in excess, U2, XTC." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gxbuajGKbko
**"Little Drummer Boy" Multiple Versions** : "Little Drummer Boy" has more than 220 recorded versions in nearly a dozen languages, making it a perennial moneymaker since its origin as "Carol of the Drum" in 1941. "Now, to date, there are more than 220 versions of that song in almost a dozen languages." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nYPGQiDL5bw
**"Loudness Wars" and CD Compression** : The "loudness wars" describe a post-recording trend where music, particularly for CDs, is heavily compressed to make it sound louder. This process, while aiming for impact, often introduces significant distortion and eliminates the dynamic nuances between loud and quiet passages, leading to a "pristine digital signal" that ironically sounds "awful." "We've come to know this as the loudness wars. This has introduced a ton of distortion into what should be a pristine digital signal." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D8GU8ZpSgY8
**"Louie Louie" (1963) Chart Success Due to Controversy** : Despite or perhaps because of the intense controversy surrounding its alleged explicit lyrics and its ban in Indiana, the mystique of The Kingsmen's "Louie Louie" grew. College students actively wrote and circulated increasingly dirty fake lyrics, which further amplified the song's notoriety and contributed to its rise on national song charts. "While college students wrote increasingly dirty fake lyrics and passed them around, the mystique of the song grew and grew pushing it up the national song charts." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**"Louie Louie" (1963) Lyrics Controversy** : After its release, rumors spread that The Kingsmen's "Louie Louie" intentionally garbled its lyrics to cover up explicit sexual content about a Jamaican sailor. These alleged "real secret lyrics" were passed among teenagers, fueling a moral panic despite no one knowing for certain what was actually sung. "Rumors began to spread that the Kingsman had intentionally garbled the lyrics to cover up what was actually being sung." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**"Louie Louie" (1963) Publishing Company Challenge** : Amidst the scandal, the publishing company for The Kingsmen's "Louie Louie" offered $1,000 to anyone who could prove that the lyrics to their version were, in fact, obscene. This challenge underscored the uncertainty and debate surrounding the song's actual content. "The song's publishing company offered $1,000 to anyone who could prove that the lyrics to the Kingsman's version of Louis Louis were in fact obscene." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**"Louie Louie" (1963) Recording Conditions** : The Kingsmen's 1963 recording of "Louie Louie" in Portland, Oregon, was a challenging, bare-bones session. It involved a single overhead microphone, forcing singer Joe Eli to sing uncomfortably upwards, exacerbated by new braces on his teeth. The band was rushed, having only one take, leading to discernible errors like the drummer dropping a stick and Eli entering two bars early. "This was a bare-bone session, April 6, 1963." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**"M-O-H" Input in Phone Systems** : Most commercial phone systems include an "M-O-H" input, which stands for "Music On Hold." This dedicated input facilitates the integration of various musical sources for callers awaiting assistance. "And pretty much every commercial phone system comes with an M-O-H input." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D8GU8ZpSgY8
**"Metal Machine Music" Locked Groove** : Lou Reed's "Metal Machine Music" features a locked groove at the end of side four of the double album. This design means that the stylus never runs out, causing the sounds to theoretically continue indefinitely, or until the record is worn out. This extreme feature contributes to the album's reputation as a weaponized and unending sonic assault. "The last groove is locked, meaning that the stylist never gets a chance to run out." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iGav9uJwA_w
**"Metal" as an Adjective in Merriam-Webster** : The Merriam-Webster dictionary was considering or may have declared that the word "metal" can be used as an adjective, typically in informal contexts, as exemplified by phrases such as "that dude is so metal." "By the time you hear this, the Merriam Webster dictionary may have declared that metal can be used as an adjective, as in that dude is so metal." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**"Mr. Obituary" Role for News Networks** : The speaker is frequently contacted by every major news network in Canada (CBC, CTV, Fox, CNN, and a station in Tel Aviv, Israel) for commentary when musicians pass away, earning him the moniker "Mr. Obituary." "When anybody dies, every single news network in Canada calls me for a comment." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4xxgPX-aR90
**"My Way" Is Known as the World's Deadliest Karaoke Song in the Philippines** : Frank Sinatra's "My Way" is documented as the deadliest karaoke song in the Philippines, with more than a dozen killings recorded in connection with it — typically triggered by disputes over poor singing or refusing to share the microphone. "'My Way' by Frank Sinatra is known as 'the world's deadliest karaoke song' in the Philippines, with more than a dozen documented murders." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qtdetc5-MQo
**"Need to Know Operation" Theory for JFK Assassination** : Extensive research into the assassination of JFK has led some to conclude that his death was the result of a "need to know operation." This theory posits a complex conspiracy involving entities such as the CIA, the deep state, Cuban exiles, and "American monsters," suggesting a highly orchestrated plot rather than the actions of a lone gunman. "I'm convinced that his death was the results of a need to know operation involving the CIA, the deep state, Cuban exiles, and American monsters." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1Jt2fUBDusk
**"Never Say Never Again" – Non-Canonical Bond Film (1983)** : In 1983, Sean Connery returned to play James Bond in "Never Say Never Again," a film explicitly stated to be "outside the normal Bond universe." This movie was a remake of 1965's "Thunderball" but produced by a different party who had battled for decades to make it. While reviews and box office were good, its music did not reach traditional Bond standards. "Sean Connery came back to play Bond in Never Say Never Again, which was outside the normal Bond universe." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=K0pgJ7XAlpo
**"New New Wave" Term (Early 2000s)** : British music papers frequently used the term "New New Wave" to describe the new wave revivalist bands emerging from the UK in the early 2000s, such as Bloc Party, The Kaiser Chiefs, Ladytron, The Ting Tings, and The Foals. "In fact, if you read any write-ups in the British music papers, you'll have seen them refer to as New New Wave." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9CNNsqXiL6U
**"No Time to Die" Film Release Delays (2019-2021)** : The James Bond film "No Time to Die" experienced multiple release delays. Originally slated for November 2019, it was pushed to February 2020, then April 2020 for a spring release, and again to November 12, 2020, due to COVID-19. It was further held back for nearly another year, with the UK release on September 30, 2021, and North America on October 8, 2021. "It was supposed to have been in theaters in November 2019, but then for some reason, it was pushed back to February 2020, some marketing thing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=K0pgJ7XAlpo
**"Phono" Setting on Modern Stereos** : Even today, home stereo systems typically feature a function switch labeled "Phono," which is short for phonograph. This persists despite the fact that the device connected, often a modern turntable, is technically more akin to a gramophone based on its historical disc-playing mechanism. "If you have a home stereo, you'll see a function switch that's labeled Fono, short for phonograph, even though really what you've got plugged into the thing is more correctly called a gramophone." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TsiYLGGSJ2U
**"Pink Floyd Issue" at Morton's Field** : Morton's Field in Hamilton had not hosted many concerts for decades due to a historical "Pink Floyd issue" that occurred in the 1970s. This long-standing hiatus meant there was no established formula for putting on events there, allowing Arkells to custom-design their "The Rally" stadium show from scratch. "Yeah, Pink Floyd because of the Pink Floyd issue that happened back in the 70s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kKNn01M8GG4
**"Playlist Burnout" – Infinite Music Choice Causes Listening Fatigue** : The infinite availability of music through streaming can lead to "playlist burnout," whereby music becomes background noise and loses its ability to generate satisfaction. Active listening and deliberate periods of silence are recommended to reset the brain's reward system. "The infinite choice of music can lead to 'playlist burnout,' where music becomes background noise." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0DFmS6MPbFI
**"Polly" Inspiration: Gerald Frant's Crimes** : The Nirvana song "Polly" was inspired by the June 1987 kidnapping, rape, and torture of a 14-year-old hitchhiking girl ("Polly") by Gerald Frant, a serial rapist paroled in 1980. Frant tortured the girl with whips, razors, and a blowtorch at his trailer, but she escaped from his van during a gas stop. Frant was arrested a day later and is serving two consecutive 75-year sentences in Eastern Washington state. "Kurt Cobain wasn't much for reading the newspaper, but one day in June of 1987 he came across a story about a 14 year old girl who had been kidnapped at gunpoint by a freak, ironically named Gerald Frant." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cM3-tINKdvo
**"Positive Punks" Term (1984)** : By 1984, music papers began using the term "positive punks" non-ironically to describe some goth groups. This indicated that these bands focused on art that provoked introspection and examination rather than any kind of revolution. "By 1984, there were Goth bands all over England. The music papers started calling some of these groups positive punks in a completely non-eronic way." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XaLsZFLrMnI
**"Punk" Fanzine (Late 1975)** : A new fanzine, simply called "Punk," appeared at the end of 1975. Initially considered "Teenaged News" after an unreleased New York Dolls song, its name was inspired by The Dictators' 1975 album "Go Girl Crazy," which featured a picture evoking 1950s "punks." This fanzine was dedicated to artists like Lou Reed, The Ramones, Iggy Pop, The Stooges, The New York Dolls, and 60s garage bands, and its circulation helped popularize the term "punk artist" for those featured within it. "But then at the end of 1975, a new fanzine appeared. This fanzine was dedicated to singers like Lou Reed and groups like the Ramones, who appeared at CBGB." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VYi3r0G-ne4
**"Pure Recording" Philosophy** : The pre-1925 acoustic recording method was viewed by purists as "pure recording," seen as a faithful capture of live music. This philosophy held that recording should accurately reflect a single, unedited performance, and any deviation or manipulation was considered illegitimate. "This was seen as pure recording, a faithful capturing of live music. And it was the only way things ever should be done." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**"Radiohead" Band Name – Came from a Talking Heads Song About Radio** : The band name Radiohead derives from "Radio Head," a track from the Talking Heads 1986 album *True Stories* about someone's experience listening to the radio. "That is where the band got their name." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iyGUXdDqIlI
**"Raise the Woof" (Christmas Song for Dogs)** : This Christmas song, specifically designed for dogs, was recorded at Abbey Road and aims to alleviate dog stress with sounds like squeaky toys, bells, and "arphats" set to a reggae beat. It runs less than two minutes, and its sales benefit an animal charity. "It's called Raise the Wolf and Sales go to an animal charity. Runs less than two minutes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nYPGQiDL5bw
**"Rock and Roll Traveler, Great Britain and Ireland" Book** : An excellent travel guide for rock fans wishing to visit historical music sites in the UK and Ireland, published by Fodor's Travel Guide. Although long out of print, it can be found for purchase online at very low prices. "It's by the Fodder's Travel Guide people. It's perfect for the rock fan who wants to visit some history in the UK and Ireland." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yl2poVWguu0
**"Rock is Back" Cry in Early 2000s** : By late 2003 and early 2004, with the success of bands like The Strokes, The White Stripes, and Arcade Fire, a sentiment of "Rock is Back" emerged. This marked a shift after rock music had played "second fiddle to Boy Bands and other pop stars" for a period. "If you were around back then, you might remember cries of Rock is Back after playing Second Fiddled to Boy Bands and other pop stars." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jEfofyobGiI
**"Rock is Dead" Claims (1960-1999)** : Throughout history, there have been recurring declarations that "rock is dead," notably in 1960, 1972, 1988, and 1999. These claims often coincide with periods when pop music dominates the forefront of the industry. "Just like we heard in 1999, 1988, 1972, and 1960." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**"Rock is Dead" Cries - Late 1990s** : As the 1990s drew to a close and Britpop declined, a period of perceived bleakness for alt-rock led to widespread "rock is dead" cries. "It looked pretty bleak for a while too. The rock is dead cries were everywhere." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DSvoppf1dGY
**"Rock is Dead" Trope and Revival** : Historically, every time rock music has been declared "dead," something or someone has emerged to revitalize it, typically through a "back-to-basics" approach. This pattern was observed again with the rise of bands like The Strokes and the Indy Rock Revival. "In the past, every time rock was declared dead, someone or something came along and breathed new life into everything, usually with a back-to-basics approach." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jEfofyobGiI
**"Rock is sick and living in London" (1977)** : This headline appeared on the cover of Rolling Stone magazine on October 20th, 1977, featuring The Sex Pistols. It captured the critical sentiment and controversy surrounding punk rock at the time, with some critics branding it a short-sighted fad. "The October 20th, 1977 edition of Rolling Stone magazine had the sex pistols on the cover, but the headline, Rock is sick and living in London." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9CNNsqXiL6U
**"Selling Out" in 1997 Context** : In 1997, the act of licensing a "cool song" for use in a television commercial, such as Nike's use of The Verve's "Bittersweet Symphony," was widely regarded as the "height of selling out." Such actions were considered uncool and could severely damage a band's artistic integrity in the eyes of their independent fanbase. "Now remember back in 1997, this sort of thing was seen as the height of selling out." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**"Sex, Drugs, and Rock and Roll" Board Game** : A board game titled "Sex, Drugs, and Rock and Roll" has been released, catering to enthusiasts of music and tabletop games. This new offering taps into the iconic themes associated with the rock and roll lifestyle. "There's now one called Sex Drugs and Rock and Roll." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-N6IM5Nm5iw
**"Showbiz Kids" Documentary (2020)** : HBO released a 2020 documentary titled "Showbiz Kids," which featured interviews with various former child stars, including Will Wheaton, Henry Thomas, and Todd Bridges, and had a soundtrack composed by Jeff Tweedy and his sons Spencer and Sam. "In 2020, HBO released a documentary called Showbiz Kids, where child stars got to tell their stories, warts and all." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dY7uB_QQWBc
**"Sidewalk Surfing"** : Early skateboarding was also commonly referred to as "sidewalk surfing," reflecting its origins among surfers who adapted their ocean tricks to dry land, particularly in Southern California. "Skateboarding or sidewalk surfing caught on with some people in Southern California, usually surfers who tried the same tricks on dry land as they did in the ocean." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=44dXy0StjIQ
**"Star-Making Machinery" Quote** : Ian Thorneley references Joni Mitchell's phrase, "the star making machinery behind the popular song," to describe the unseen, complex processes and pressures that musicians face in the industry, especially during album cycles or while touring. "You have to deal with, you know, to quote Johnny Mitchell, the star making machinery behind the popular song." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QIA7V4PxCdM
**"The Christmas Song" Sales Figures** : "The Christmas Song," also known as "Chestnuts Roasting on an Open Fire," has sold in the tens of millions of copies, ensuring substantial annual checks for its rights holders. "It is sold in the tens of millions, and the rights holders get big checks every year." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nYPGQiDL5bw
**"The Crow" Movie's Goth Character** : The lead character in the movie "The Crow" was described as "very, very Goth," highlighting the film's connection to Goth culture and aesthetics. "the movie The Crow, which had a lead character who of course was also very, very Goth." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**"The Day the Music Died" (1957)** : February 3, 1957, is historically referred to as "the day the music died." On this date, pioneering rock and roll musicians Buddy Holly, Richie Valens, and The Big Bopper were all killed when their small plane crashed into a cornfield outside of Mason City, Iowa, marking a significant loss in music history. "February 3rd, 1957 has gone down in history as the day the music died." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hmnMG6f80Sg
**"The Ox-Bow Incident" Quotes** : Gord Downie's song "The East Wind" incorporates "found objects quotes" from various sources, including Walter von Tilburg Clark, who wrote "The Ox-Bow Incident." A specific quote, "night is like a room. It makes the little things inside your head too important," appears in the song and is attributed to Clark, whose 1943 novel was adapted into a film by William Wellman. "So the second verse is night is like a room. It makes the little things inside your head too important. That's Walter von Tilburg Clark who wrote The Oxbow incident." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Um_KEEcCeT4
**"The Watch That Ends the Night" Novel (1957)** : Written by Canadian author Hugh MacLean, this 1957 novel contains a passage paraphrased by Gord Downie in a song from The Tragically Hip's "Fully Completely" album. Downie read the book during the 1991 "Road Apples" tour, and the passage describes a central character's regret over missed opportunities in love due to pride, depression, and a lack of courage. "Gord paraphrases a passage from MacLean's 1957 novel, The Watch That Ends the Night, a book that he picked up to read between gigs on the road apples tour in 1991." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dnz_Ycd_q7s
**"The Year the Music Died" (2016)** : CNN dubbed 2016 "the year the music died" due to the significant number of prominent musicians who passed away. "CNN called 2016, the year the music died, and I swear created a whole new category on Wikipedia." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**"Ticket to Ride" – About Prostitutes in Hamburg with Clean Health Cards** : The Beatles song "Ticket to Ride" sounds like it should be about public transport, but it refers to a card carried by prostitutes in Hamburg certifying that they had a clean bill of health and were therefore legally permitted to work. "I have not been able to listen to that song the same way since." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**"Too Fast to Live" (2004) – Investigation of Nancy's Murder** : This investigative book, published in 2004, posits that Nancy Spungen's murder investigation was mishandled by the NYPD and suggests that drug dealer Rockets Redgler was responsible for her death. "But then a 2004 investigative book called Too Fast to Live believes that it was a drug dealer named Rockets Redgler." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OcylgiXkDP8
**"Visigoth Towers" Origin of Goth Tag** : Some early Goth practitioners claimed their subculture was nicknamed "Goths" because some members lived in a London apartment block called "Visigoth Towers." "Some early Goth practitioners maintained that the real reason they were tagged as Goths was because some of them lived in an apartment block in London that was called Visigoth Towers. Goths, for short." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**"What's the Big Deal About" Series** : The death of a musician provides an opportunity to create a "What's the Big Deal About" series, which aims to explain the significance of these artists to a broader audience who may not be familiar with their careers or cultural impact. "Because how do you conceptualize Shnato Conner or Shane McGowan on their own? But now we can package it better." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4xxgPX-aR90
**"White Christmas" Sales Figures** : "White Christmas" has sold at least 100 million singles across its hundreds of recorded versions, making it a significant moneymaker for its rights holders. "And white Christmas has sold at least 100 million singles." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nYPGQiDL5bw
**"Who Killed Nancy" (Film) – Alternative Theories** : The film "Who Killed Nancy" explores alternative theories regarding Nancy Spungen's death, suggesting she may have killed herself or that another drug dealer named Michael was involved. "Then there's a film called Who Killed Nancy. It says, maybe Nancy killed herself or maybe it was a drug dealer named Michael." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OcylgiXkDP8
**"Why Female Musicians Are Inferior?" Article (1938)** : The February 1938 issue of Downbeat magazine, a premier jazz publication, featured a misogynistic article titled "Why Female Musicians Are Inferior?" The unnamed, male-authored piece asserted that "The Woman Musician was never capable of sending anyone further than the nearest exit," reflecting deep-seated sexism in the music industry. "Let me read you a passage from the February 1938 issue of Downbeat magazine, which was at the time the premier magazine for Jazz Lovers. The article was called Why Female Musicians Are In Furrier?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9GHpC0kCgHY
**"Winter Wonderland" Longevity** : "Winter Wonderland," released in 1934, has been covered by over 200 artists and continues to generate revenue. "And finally, Winter Wonderland. It's from 1934. It's been covered by more than 200 artists and continues to make money." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nYPGQiDL5bw
**10,050 Cielo Drive (Manson Murder Site)** : This address in Benedict Canyon, Los Angeles, was the site of the first Manson family murders on August 8, 1969. Tex Watson, Susan Atkins, Linda Kasabian, and Patricia Krenwinkel brutally murdered Abigail Folger, Woodjick Freierkowski, Jay Sebring, Stephen Parent, and the heavily pregnant actress Sharon Tate, who was renting the property with Roman Polanski. The word "pig" was written in Tate's blood on the living room wall. Trent Reznor later rented this house in July 1992 to record "The Downward Spiral," unknowingly at first, and established his studio in the room where Tate and Sebring were found. "10,050 C.L.O. Drive is where the first of the Manson family murders took place." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DKdF8NgF5Bw
**100th Meridian West Longitude** : The 100th Meridian West Longitude is a geographical line extending from the North Pole, passing through Nunavut, Manitoba, North Dakota, South Dakota, Nebraska, Kansas, Oklahoma, Texas, and Mexico, before reaching the Pacific Ocean. In Canada, this meridian roughly marks the end of the Canadian Shield and the beginning of the Canadian Prairies, while in the U.S., it signifies the start of the Great Plains. This geographical feature forms the basis for The Tragically Hip's song "At the Hundredth Meridian." "A little further west, and you cross 100 degrees west Longitude, the 100 Meridian." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Vs1YyXgLpTE
**12-inch 33 1/3 RPM Long Playing Album (LP)** : A record format adopted by many labels, the 12-inch 33 1/3 RPM long-playing album offered collections of songs. "their 12 inch 33 and a third RPM long playing album." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**12-inch Single (1970s)** : The 12-inch single emerged with the rise of disco and DJ culture, often featuring extended remixes. "And with disco in the rise of early DJ culture came the 12-inch single and the extended remix." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**12-inch Singles / Maxi Singles** : 12-inch singles were records the size of an album, played at either 33 1/3 RPM or 45 RPM. Their larger surface area allowed for the inclusion of multiple songs on the B-side, beyond just the main hit on the A-side. These releases were sometimes referred to as Maxi singles. "Then we have 12 inch singles. These were records the size of an album. They might play it 33 in a third RPM or 45 RPM. The A side would feature what the label hoped would be the hit. Well the B side featured one or more songs that were there. You had 12 inches of violins instead of 7. Why not throw it up? Some refer to these releases as Maxi singles." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**13 Universal Emotions Evoked by Music** : A study involving 2500 participants across North America and China revealed that music can consistently elicit exactly 13 distinct emotions, irrespective of an individual's musical preferences or cultural background. These emotions include amusement, joy, eroticism, beauty, relaxation, sadness, dreaminess, triumph, anxiety, scaringness, annoyance, defiance, and feeling pumped up. "Scientists say that music can bring forth exactly 13 different emotions, regardless of who you are or what kind of music you like, or it seems your cultural background." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**1545 Weevil Trial in St. Julianne, France** : In 1545, winemakers in St. Julianne, France, legally prosecuted weevils for eating their grapevines. The weevils were appointed a defense lawyer, underwent a formal trial, and were found guilty. Almost a year later, the presiding judge issued a proclamation demanding the weevils cease, and surprisingly, the infestations stopped for 40 years, only to resume in 1587, leading to a second, unrecorded trial. "This was all very formal. Documents were drawn up and the weevils were appointed a defense lawyer by the court." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dVJuITJyNTU
**16 2/3 RPM Singles (1950s)** : Special singles designed for Chrysler's in-dash car turntables played at 16 2/3 RPM, but they were limited in availability and format. "And they only played these special 16 and 2 thirds RPM singles that you had to buy at dealers." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**16 and 2/3 RPM – Spoken Word, Children's Records, and Chrysler In-Dash Turntables** : Turntables were briefly manufactured to operate at four speeds: 33⅓, 45, 78, and 16⅔ RPM. Slow-speed records were used for spoken word, children's releases, and as an in-dash turntable option sold by Chrysler in the 1950s. The Chrysler turntables were not practical (too much bouncing), and fewer than 50 titles were released, available only at Chrysler dealers. Audiobook records for the blind were made at speeds as slow as 4⅙ RPM. "Several audiobook records were once manufactured for the blind with speeds of 24, 8 and 1 3rd, and 4 and 1 6th RPM." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=I291j5WBc4k
**171A Studio's Dire Circumstances** : The 171A studio, where the Beastie Boys recorded *Pollywog Stew*, was on the brink of going out of business, with the landlord threatening to sell all the equipment to collect back rents. This situation forced the Beasties to record eight songs in just two days. "Unfortunately, the place was about to go out of business, and the landlord was threatening to sell all the gear so he could collect them back rents." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=l1jPP1FhGo8
**180 Gram Vinyl** : A common specification for modern heavyweight vinyl records, where a square meter of the material weighs 180 grams, resulting in thicker, flatter records with deeper grooves and potential for higher audio quality. "The most common is known as 180 gram vinyl, which means a square meter of the stuff weighs 180 grams." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**1896 Pornographic Cylinder Recording – Gonorrhea Soliloquy; Creator Jailed in Government Sting** : Among the earliest surviving examples of censored recorded material, three wax cylinders from a collection called "The Stag Party" feature dirty jokes and bawdy verses, sold at the outrageous price of $1.50. One is a parody of Hamlet's "To be or not to be" soliloquy in which the speaker laments a case of gonorrhea, titled "To Pee or Not to Pee." The person who made and distributed these recordings was caught in a government sting operation on June 24, 1896, fined, and jailed. These recordings are among the oldest surviving examples demonstrating that censorship of recorded material has existed since the very beginning of the medium. "The person was caught in a government sting operation on June 24, 1896. He was fined and sent to jail for months." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Dt7mb4yyuWM
**1930s Horror Film Aesthetics** : The decor of The Batcave club consciously incorporated design cues from horror movies of the 1930s. "They decorated the place in black and leather and lace with other design cues taken from horror movies from the 1930s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**1950s Nostalgia (Mid-1970s)** : The movie "American Graffiti" and the TV show "Happy Days" triggered a revival of 1950s nostalgia, leading people to romanticize the decade as "the good old days," despite the underlying fear of atomic war that characterized the era. This cultural trend brought 1950s music back to AM radio and reintroduced terms like "punk" into common use. "Meanwhile, the movie American graffiti in the debut of the show Happy Days kicked off the revival of the 1950s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VYi3r0G-ne4
**1954 Labels Announcement on 45s** : In mid-May 1954, the six biggest record labels collectively announced they would ship promotional copies of new songs to radio stations on 45 RPM singles instead of 78s. "In mid-May 1954, the six biggest record labels announced that they would band together to ship promotional copies of new songs to radio stations on 45 instead of on 78." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**1955 Record Prices** : In 1955, an album could cost up to $6 (approximately $53 today), while a 45 RPM single cost around 89 cents (almost $8 today). "Back in 1955, you could expect to spend up to $6 for an album, which is about $53 in today's money. And a 45? Well, that cost is a little 89 cents or almost $8 today." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**1960 45 RPM Sales Market Share** : By 1960, 45 RPM single sales accounted for 20% of the entire music market. "By 1960, 45 sales accounted for 20% of the music market." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**1960s Legacy and Disillusionment** : While the hippie generation achieved victories in civil rights, women's rights, birth control, and ending the draft and Vietnam War, there was a growing sense that the "peace and love" approach to social change had "played itself out." The adult 60s generation faced new realities, and rock music, once a force for change, felt "tired." "But there was also a sense that the whole peace and love approach to social change had pretty much played itself out." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VYi3r0G-ne4
**1962 Quote on Synthetic Personalities** : A 1962 quote highlighted the industry's practice of creating "scores and scores of synthetic phonographic personalities every year." These figures were described as "concocted from varying potions of human voice and electronic manipulation," with the sounds on their records often bearing only a "passing resemblance" to their actual voices. "Every company's create scores and scores of synthetic phonographic personalities every year, concocted from varying potions of human voice and electronic manipulation." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**1968 film - 2001, A Space Odyssey** : This film had a profound impact on David Bowie, significantly influencing his creative output. Its themes of space exploration and existentialism contributed to the inspiration behind his hit song "Space Oddity." "The 1968 film, 2001, A Space Audity, had a huge impact on him." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0xd0Hf5REg4
**1970s British Indie Culture** : By the end of the 1970s, labels like Rough Trade, Stiff, and Virgin were central to indie culture in Britain. These companies were small, nimble, and privately owned, allowing them to quickly record and react to new music. Despite often being underfunded, financial constraints sometimes forced them to be more creative with revenue generation. "Rough Trade, Stiff, Virgin and dozens of others were at the center of indie culture and Britain at the end of the 1970s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dDcD8kg0wEk
**1970s Rock Decline** : By 1973, there was a widespread perception that the original promise of rock music had "gone off the rails," leading to a desire for a "complete reboot." This was attributed to the excesses of prog rock, the perceived detachment of stadium rock bands, and the "awful," "safe and bland and dull" music prevalent on AM radio, exemplified by the #1 song of 1973, "Tie a Yellow Ribbon around the Old Oak Tree." "Things got so dire that a small group of people decided that rock and roll needed an enema, a complete reboot." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gkLMwrthZ9s
**1973 Arab Oil Embargo – Impact on Vinyl** : The 1973 Arab Oil Embargo, a response to the Israeli-Egyptian War, caused oil prices to jump from $3 to $12 a barrel, which drastically increased the cost of petrochemical products, including the raw materials for vinyl records. This forced record labels to cut costs by using thinner, recycled vinyl. "The things that killed vinyl was the 1973 Arab Oil embargo and disco." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**1973 Oil Crisis** : The oil crisis of 1973 had a severe global impact, crippling the economies of the world and also significantly derailing the welfare state system in the UK. "The oil crisis had crippled the economies of the world and the Middle East blew up into war with regularity." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**1977 as a Pivotal Year** : The year 1977 is considered one of those pivotal years in human history where everything significantly changed, drawing parallels to years like 325 AD (Council of Nicaea), 1215 (Magna Carta), 1492 (discoveries), 1789 (revolutions), 1919 (Treaty of Versailles), 1929 (stock market crash), 1942 (World War II), 1968 (unrest), and 1989 (fall of the Iron Curtain). "And somewhere in there is 1977." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gKrAXMG8sTc
**1979 Iranian Revolution – Second Oil Shock** : The Iranian Revolution in 1979 triggered a second oil shock, further increasing costs and leading to even lower quality vinyl production. "Then there was a second oil shock in 1979 after the Iranian Revolution. Higher costs led to even lower quality vinyl." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**1980 Recession Impact on Music** : A severe recession that began in 1980 caused a downturn that eventually led to a period dominated by pop music as the economy recovered around 1983-84. "All that crashed in a terrible recession that began in 1980." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**1980 Vinyl Album Sales Share** : In 1980, before the CD's widespread introduction, vinyl albums accounted for 60% of music sales. "In 1980, with the CD still about two years away, vinyl albums accounted for 60% of music sales." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**1980s Club Culture and DJ Resurrection** : The 1980s saw the "resurrection of the DJ" and the birth of what became known as club culture. This movement shifted the focus from traditional bands to the DJ, who became the central performer creating mixes and making people dance. "High to all this was the resurrection of the DJ and what became club culture of the 1980s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OiqdZfxdBP0
**1980s Rock Artifice** : Rock music in the 1980s had become heavily laden with "artifice and goop," including excessive costuming, makeup, and focus on image in music videos played on MTV, leading to a reactionary movement like grunge that stripped these elements back. "It was a stripping back of all the artifice and goop that had become attached to rock through the 1980s, things like costuming and makeup and how you looked at that music video that played on MTV." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tFBSrKMEZxI
**1980s Vision of Digital Music Archive** : During the 1980s, individuals began envisioning a massive centralized computer capable of storing all of humanity's music in digital format, making any song instantly accessible upon demand. "In the 1980s, some people started to conceive of a giant computer somewhere that could hold humanity's music in digital form." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**1990 7-inch Single Sales Share** : In 1990, 7-inch singles' market share had fallen from about 8% to less than 1%. "Seven inch singles fell from about 8% to a fraction of 1%." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**1990 Vinyl Album Sales Share** : By 1990, vinyl album sales had plummeted to 1% of the music market. "Just 10 years later, that share had dropped to 1%." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**1990s Album Art Production Budget** : During the 1990s, there were significant budgets allocated for the creation of album artwork, allowing for elaborate physical sets and detailed photography, in contrast to modern methods like AI-generated artwork. "Now keep in mind this was the 90s when there was a budget for this sort of thing and we didn't have AI-generated artwork." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sn5TWBFd0XM
**1990s Alternative Rock Landscape** : The late 1990s was a challenging period for alternative rock. Grunge had burned out, "new rock" (nu-metal, blending hard rock and hip hop) was highly polarizing, and mainstream alt-rock seemed dominated by one-hit wonders. Rock's overall standing was further challenged by the ascendance of hip hop, electronic EDM, and the massive popularity of pop music acts like Backstreet Boys, *NSYNC, Britney Spears, and Spice Girls. "The late 90s was not a great time for alternative rock. Grunge had burned itself out." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UpWz3X33vSY
**1990s Canadian Rock and Punk Growth** : The 1990s witnessed a rapid expansion of Canadian rock and punk music, creating a vibrant scene that helped bands like Sum 41 emerge and gain traction quickly. "at that time of the 90s, Canadian rock and punk was growing really, really quick." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6L4xKBFEYkE
**1990s Context for Protest Music (Generation X)** : The 1990s provided ample reasons for protest, particularly for Generation X. This generation faced a brutal recession upon entering the workforce, leading to fears of being unable to match or exceed their parents' lifestyle. Despite being highly educated, they were often under-engaged and underemployed. Globally, the First Gulf War added to anxieties. These factors fueled the rise of alternative music, which channeled the fears, confusion, anger, and hope of this large demographic. "Generation X was not happy. They were hit with a brutal recession just as they were coming into the workplace." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0ARYt4sYT90
**1990s Festival Boom in North America** : While Europeans were accustomed to festivals in varying conditions, North Americans were late to the party, lacking a strong tradition of summer festivals. This changed significantly in the 1990s, driven by the alt-rock nation, which built the traditions and industry for modern summer festivals. "Europeans were pretty used to standing in fields in the mud, in the rain, in the heat, but we North Americans were late to the party." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7JP2tjJibTQ
**1990s Golden Age of Alt-Rock Soundtracks** : The 1990s marked a "golden age" for alt-rock based soundtracks, during which "things peaked" in terms of promotion and commercial success. Soundtracks "blew up" due to strategic marketing, artist financial deals, label promotion, and radio station play, often by companies linked to movie studios, leading to massive sales. "This is the decade when things peaked. Soundtracks just blew up in terms of promotion and commercial success." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**1990s Soundtrack Business Model** : By the 1990s, including a soundtrack became an integral part of every movie's business plan. Soundtracks were seen as cheap to compile and offered "fantastic margins," sometimes even launching musical careers, indicating their significant commercial value and strategic importance for film studios. "But the 1990s, every movie had to have a soundtrack as part of its business plan. They were cheap to compile, and the margins were fantastic." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**1990s Stand-up Sitcom Boom** : The 1990s saw a massive boom in stand-up comics starring in their own TV sitcoms. This trend was spurred by the success of shows like *The Cosby Show* and *Roseanne*, leading networks to scout comedy clubs for well-established stand-up comedians around whom they could build shows. Suddenly, every major network was adding one or two stand-up sitcoms a year. "Thanks to the success of the Cosby Show and Roseanne, networks began searching comedy clubs looking for a well-established Stand Up comics they could build shows around." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6O5TCTO-go4
**1990s as a Transformational Decade** : The 1990s marked a profound transformational decade, signifying "the end of the way music used to be and the beginning of what it would become in the next millennium." This era witnessed the rise of grunge, Britpop, raves, electronic music, Generation X, hip-hop, sampling, and the advent of cell phones, personal computers, the internet, MP3s, and music piracy. Many observers believe it was the "last great decade for music." "The 90s were a transformational decade in so many ways the end of the way music used to be and the beginning of what it would become in the next millennium." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r8AazBVoS7g
**1993 IRA Bombing** : In 1993, the Irish Republican Army (IRA) carried out a bombing that tragically resulted in the deaths of two people. This act of violence inspired the song "Zombie" by The Cranberries. "Zombie, from the Cranberries, is about a 1993 IRA bombing that killed two people." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4PlieAEthSg
**1993 IRA Bombing Inspiration for "Zombie"** : The song "Zombie" by The Cranberries was written in response to a 1993 IRA bombing that caused the deaths of two individuals. "Zombie from the Cranberries is about a 1993 IRA bombing that killed two people." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cM3-tINKdvo
**1994 Woodstock 25th Anniversary – Alt-Rock's Triumphant Peak; Lineup Proved Genre's Dominance** : The 25th anniversary of Woodstock was held in Saugerties, New York, with a lineup that demonstrated just how thoroughly alternative rock had taken over popular music: Red Hot Chili Peppers, Peter Gabriel, Green Day, Nine Inch Nails, Live, Collective Soul, Violent Femmes, Metallica, Blind Melon, the Cranberries, Primus, Porno for Pyros, and more. The conditions were muddy and cold. The same summer's Lollapalooza featured the Smashing Pumpkins, Beastie Boys, the Breeders, the Verve, Green Day, Flaming Lips, and others. "If you were a fan of rock and you were around in the early 1990s, you probably remember that era very, very fondly." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4Jvy4q40tuo
**1995 vs. Today's Internet Access** : The level of global internet access for music distribution in the late 2000s was "the stuff of science fiction" in 1995. The ability to instantly upload a song or video and have it accessible worldwide became a taken-for-granted reality. "That kind of access was the stuff of science fiction in 1995 and today we just take it for granted." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**1997 Global Vinyl Sales** : Global vinyl sales in 1997 were less than $150 million. "In 1997, all vinyl accounted for a tiny fraction of the overall music market. Global sales were less than $150 million." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**1998 Warped Tour Accommodation** : During the 1998 Warped Tour, all participants, including bands and crew, slept either in tents or in Boy Scout camps, reflecting the tour's rugged, no-frills environment. "everyone on the 1998 tour either slept intense or in Boy Scout camps." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jfQaUV6Bq4
**1999 Perception of Vinyl's Demise** : In December 1999, with digital music and CDs dominating, vinyl was widely considered "ancient history." People were disposing of their collections by selling them to used record stores, leaving them at thrift shops, giving them away, or simply throwing them out. The introduction of iTunes was still over a year away. "CDs ruled, and vinyl was ancient history. And while a few people had heard about MP3s, we were still over a year from Apple introducing the iTunes music store. So vinyl was done. Dead." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VlIErdSSVOM
**19th-Century Copyright Law Left a Legal Vacuum for Sound Recordings** : Copyright laws of the nineteenth century were designed for the printed word and left a complete legal vacuum regarding audio recordings, allowing unauthorized recordings to exist without clear legal prohibition. The United Kingdom was the first country to extend copyright protection to sound recordings; the United States did not do so effectively until 1972. "Copyright laws were designed for the printed word, leaving a legal vacuum for audio recordings." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OrHtPVBjXw4
**2000 Global Vinyl Sales** : By 2000, global vinyl sales had dropped to less than $100 million. "Three years later, the number had fallen to less than $100 million." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**2000 Vinyl Market Share** : By 2000, all vinyl formats (albums, EPs, 12-inch, and 7-inch singles) accounted for a barely measurable 0.3% of the market, indicating its statistical disappearance. "By 2000, all vinyl albums, EPs, 12 inch singles, and 17 inch singles, accounted for, well, it was barely measurable. Somewhere around 3 tenths of 1%." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**2006 Global Vinyl Sales** : In 2006, worldwide vinyl sales reached a low of approximately $30 million. "And by 2006, sales accounted for maybe $30 million." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**2008 Financial Crisis Impact on Music** : The financial crisis of 2008 initially suggested a dire outlook but, upon recovery, reinforced the strength of pop music in the pop versus rock cycle, helping to extend its popularity. "The financial crisis of 2008 was a little different. It really did seem that the world was heading over some kind of a cliff." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**2010s Alt Rock Sound Evolution** : In the 2010s, the overall sound of Alt Rock evolved significantly, moving "down Temple and away from being guitar heavy." It incorporated "more keyboards, more samples, more banjos," experiencing "a pop phase more profound than we'd seen since maybe the 1980s." In this context, pop punk, by contrast, "sounded heavy." "overall, Alt Rock moved down Temple and away from being guitar heavy, more keyboards, more samples, more banjos." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pE7TaWSUa-o
**2010s Combacks as Industry Health Indicator** : The comebacks of established "monsters of rock" in the 2010s, such as Tool, Guns N' Roses, Pearl Jam, and Metallica, were crucial for the health of both the recorded music industry and the touring industry, as well as music in general. These acts continued to fuel massive tours and album sales, largely supported by Boomers and Gen Xers. "The comebacks that we saw in the 2010s were an extremely important part of the health of the recorded music industry, also the touring industry, and you know what, music in general." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HSvgRyGiOpE
**2010s Defining Characteristics** : The 2010s decade began on time and was characterized by prosperous economic growth, the rise of authoritarianism, and a massive surge in technology adoption, including smartphones, the explosion of social media, and cord-cutting, significantly impacting how music was consumed. "For the next 10 years, we saw everything from prosperous economic growth to the rise of authoritarianism and technology." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5Y6nHw5xx88
**2013 Global Vinyl Sales** : By 2013, the global market for vinyl records rebounded significantly, reaching nearly $220 million, a level not seen since the 1990s. "By 2013, the global market for vinyl records was worth nearly $220 million." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**2016 - A Difficult Year for Music Deaths** : The year 2016 was particularly impactful for music fans due to the significant number of prominent musicians who passed away, including David Bowie and Prince, highlighting an era where the loss of musical heroes became more frequent. "It hit really hard in 2016. That was a bad year with the deaths of David Bowie and Prince and a bunch of others." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HBS24NwhrE8
**2020 Impact on Touring Industry** : The touring industry faced an "apocalyptic" year in 2020, suffering an estimated $30 billion USD loss due to a lack of gigs and revenues. This led to many musicians being unemployed or driving Uber, no work for roadies, and sound and light companies losing business, with venues shutting down or closing entirely and festivals being cancelled. "It has been a Pocca Liptic for the touring industry. No gigs, no revenues, live music industry probably lost $30 billion US dollars this year." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nYPGQiDL5bw
**21st Century Digital Music Backlash** : In the latter half of the first decade of the 21st century, a small but powerful backlash against digital music began, contributing to vinyl's resurgence. "There was a small but powerful backlash against digital music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**21st Century Superstars Decline** : The 21st century has seen a decline in the ability to create new "superstar acts" of comparable magnitude to previous eras, partly because the enduring popularity of 1990s music and artists like Green Day, Pearl Jam, and Nirvana continues to monopolize airplay and fan attention, making it harder for new artists to break through. "We still have superstars, but they aren't anywhere as big as they used to be." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**21st Century Triggers for Protest (George W. Bush Era)** : In the 2000s, George W. Bush became a focal point for protest music, much like Ronald Reagan in the 80s. Following the 9/11 attacks and the subsequent invasions of Iraq and Afghanistan and the resulting chaos, many musicians picked up their guitars as a means of protest. "What Ronald Reagan was to the 80s, George W. Bush was to the 00s. After 9-11 and the invasion of Iraq and Afghanistan and the mess they created, a lot of musicians took up arms in the form of guitars." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0ARYt4sYT90
**30 Seconds to Mars – Harvard Professor's Thesis on Technological Progress** : Jared and Shannon Leto found the name in a subsection of a Harvard professor's thesis about how fast technology advances — the suggestion being that on an evolutionary scale, humans are close to reaching Mars. They felt the concept described their musical ambitions. "Such a grand thought described perfectly what they wanted to do with their band." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**30-Second Minimum for Streaming Payouts** : A universal rule across all streaming music services dictates that a song must be listened to for a minimum of 30 seconds to qualify as an official play, and anything less than this duration results in no payment to the artist. "In order to qualify for a song as being officially played, you have to listen for at least 30 seconds." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**360 Deal Business Model** : The "360 deal" emerged as a new business relationship where a music industry entity (label, promoter, etc.) provides upfront financial support for an artist in exchange for a percentage of all revenue streams. This includes traditional income, as well as concert ticket sales, merchandise, and licensing, aiming for mutual survival and growth in a changing market. "We began to hear of something called the 360 deal. This is a new type of business relationship between an artist or some kind of music industry entity." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**5,987 Musical Genres** : As of the time of the discussion, the Every Noise At Once website cataloged 5,987 different musical genres, illustrating the extreme granularity and vast expansion of popular music. "Every Noise It Once contains 5,987 different musical genres in the ever-expanding universe of popular music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tdTs-4Irv8c
**50-Mission Cap Origin** : A "50-mission cap" refers to a cloth officer's cap worn in the US Army Air Force during World War II. Pilots often wore these caps under bulky headphones, which would crush them. To restore the cap's shape, pilots would insert a stiff item, such as a piece of cardboard or a hockey card, inside it. This historical detail provides the background for the title of The Tragically Hip's song "50 Mission Cap." "Well, a 50-mission cap refers to a cloth officers cap worn during World War II in the US Army Coron Air Force." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Vs1YyXgLpTE
**6/8 Time Signature Challenges in Music** : Songs written in a 6/8 time signature are noted for being difficult to perform effectively live, are seldom played on radio, and are considered "tough to dance to," often described as a "double waltz." "6'8", grooves are hard to deliver live." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VR5Fncdc2kQ
**606 Studios Location and Description** : The Foo Fighters' recording studio, 606 Studios, is located in Northridge, California. On September 30, 2014, surveillance audio described the studio's exterior as "like a fortress" in a "rough neighborhood," suggesting a formidable and somewhat secluded creative space. "This place looks like a fortress." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Mh-qg1izM98
**7-inch 45 RPM Single** : RCA initially insisted that their 7-inch 45 RPM single was the future, arguing against collections of songs on LPs. "their 7 inch 45 RPM single was the way the future." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**8-Track Tape (Mid-1960s)** : The 8-track tape, invented by the creator of the Lear Corporate Jet, was introduced as an option in Ford cars in the fall of 1965, representing an early portable music format. "First, it was the 8-track tape, invented by the same dude who gave us the Lear Corporate Jet." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**80s Alt-Rock Radio Culture** : In the 1980s, commercial alt-rock radio stations were unique, with no industry-wide charts or airplay tracking; each station curated its music based on local audience preferences, leading to a "staggering breadth of music" on playlists that varied significantly between cities like Toronto (CFNY), Boston (WFNX), and Los Angeles (KROQ). "Each station simply served up the music it thought its local audience would like. And that's why my station, C.F. and Y. and Toronto, sounded so much different than, say, W.F. and X. and Boston or K. Rock and Los Angeles." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**80s Alt-Rock Radio Station Differences** : In the 1980s, commercial alt-rock radio stations operated independently, without industry magazines publishing alternative charts or tracking airplay. This autonomy meant each station curated music based on local audience preferences, resulting in distinct sounds between stations across North America. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**80s Music Video Influence** : The 1980s era, particularly with channels like MTV and Much Music, played a crucial role in making people fall in love with music videos through the storytelling, dancing, and style showcased by artists like Madonna, Peter Gabriel, Phil Collins, Michael Jackson, and Aerosmith. "I came up in the 80s era where that's what made me fall in love with music videos, right? The MTV, much music era, watching videos by like Madonna, Peter Gabriel and like Phil Collins and Michael Jackson and Errol Smith." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kbFfT0pKchI
**9/11 Impact on Music Taste** : The events of 9/11 caused a significant shift in public musical preferences. Following the "unexpected black swan event" that brought down the Twin Towers, "happy, dancing pop music" suddenly felt inappropriate. This led to a public taste shift away from pop and back towards rock, illustrating how "music is always downstream from what's happening in society." "But in the months following 9-11, things started to change. Overnight, it was suddenly very inappropriate to be into happy, dancing pop music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UpWz3X33vSY
**9/11 Inside Job Theory** : The idea that the 9/11 attacks were an "inside government job" is cited as an extreme conspiracy theory. "or 9-11 was an inside government job theories." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pmKjZzypzvg
**90s Music as Top Nostalgia Driver** : Music from the 1990s has emerged as the number one source for nostalgia, surpassing the public's fascination with the 80s, 70s, and 60s. This trend is so strong that even young music fans who were not alive in the 90s have fiercely embraced the music from that era, making it "fresh and top of mind" for them. "It's been so long since the 90s, for example, that music of that era is number one for nostalgia. It clipsing the public's fascination with the 80s, the 70s, and the 60s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZzIqIydZTlA
**911 Call Regarding Chester Bennington's Death (July 20, 2017)** : On July 20, 2017, around 9 AM, a limo driver contacted 911 after Chester Bennington's housekeeper informed him of finding a body. The driver reported that the housekeeper found Chester dead from hanging in his room at his home in Pollock's, confirming no signs of life. An empty vodka bottle was found by the body, but toxicology reports showed nothing. "Around nine in the morning of July 20, what would have been Chris Cornell's 53rd birthday, the family's housekeeper ran out to an arriving limo driver who was there to pick up Chester for a photo shoot. She'd found a body." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qQm7u3W13eM
**924 Gilman Street Punk Club** : This punk club in Oakland was a pivotal venue for the local teen punk scene and played a significant role in Green Day's early career. Tre Cool was a regular there. After Green Day signed a major label deal, some traditional fans from Gilman Street protested their shows, perceiving the move as "selling out." The club remains near where all three Green Day members still reside, not far from San Francisco. "He was a regular at a punk club called 924 Gilman Street, which catered to Oakland's team punk scene." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nyfdGYWelic
**A Great Song's Resilience to Interpretation** : A truly great song possesses an inherent strength that allows it to withstand various interpretations, no matter how much it is "folded, spindled, and mutilated." It will stand up to any artist's take on it.
