"I'll say one more time, a great song will stand up to any interpretation by anyone anywhere in any era in any genre." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yuA9Xor8vfw
**A La Carte Music Purchasing (iTunes)** : The introduction of online retailers like iTunes allowed consumers the freedom to purchase individual songs rather than entire albums, with few exceptions. This "a la carte" model transformed how people bought music, even if they chose not to pay for it at all. "with the introduction of online retailers like iTunes, we no longer have to buy an entire album if we just want one song with a few exceptions of course." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**A Life Less Lived, The Goth Box (2006) Packaging** : This box set, released in 2006 by Rhino, is notable for its unique packaging: the box itself, containing CDs and a DVD, "comes in a leather corset." "And the box, the box of all the CDs and the DVD, it comes in a leather corset. Seriously, it's dressed in a leather corset." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**A Voice for the Innocent** : This organization, which supports victims of rape and sexual abuse, was among the various charities backed by the Warped Tour. "A voice for the innocent supports victims of rape and sexual abuse." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jfQaUV6Bq4
**A to B Music Digital Distribution** : In 1999, A to B music was a digital company that pioneered an early form of online distribution, making 30-second clips of songs such as Blink-182's "What's My Age Again?" available to online users, a practice considered "pretty radical stuff back then." "Thirty-second clips of that song and two others were available to anyone online in 1999. Pretty radical stuff back then." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eljIIqCu5gU
**ACAPE and Musician Classification** : During the 1942 musicians' strike, ACAPE, the main performing rights organization, did not consider country musicians, western performers, and R&B acts to be "real musicians." This classification exempted them from the strike's prohibitions, allowing them to continue recording and performing freely. "Acape also did not consider country musicians, western performers, and R&B acts to be real musicians. As a result, they were not covered by any prohibitions." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**ADHD or OCD as Marfan's Symptom** : ADHD or OCD can manifest as another symptom of Marfan Syndrome. Joey Ramone was diagnosed with OCD at 18, and it continued to affect him throughout his life. "Another symptom of the disorder can be ADHD or OCD." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=j8uk7BnbXwU
**AI Music Generation Process (Contemporary)** : The current methodology for AI music generation involves humans compiling extensive datasets of existing music from all genres and styles. The AI then analyzes these songs to identify patterns, structures, chord progressions, and other fundamental features. After training on this data, the AI generates new music, though human artists are still necessary to refine and perfect the aesthetic aspects, as AI lacks inherent taste. "Once that's done, the AI uses what it has learned to generate new music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**AI for Overcoming Writer's Block (N/A)** : Text-to-music programs can serve as a creative tool for songwriters experiencing writer's block. By inputting imaginative prompts, such as "write me a song that Kurt Cobain would have written on a rainy day but do it on accordion with a polka beat and with Taylor Swift's voice," the AI can generate ideas that, even if messy, may inspire new musical directions. "I can use a text to music program to help generate ideas." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**AI for Pitch Records and Song Demos (N/A)** : Songwriters are now utilizing AI to create "pitch records" or demos by inserting a big star's cloned voice into their material. This allows them to hear a realistic interpretation of their song by the target artist, significantly enhancing the effectiveness of their pitches to music industry figures. "You can use AI to insert that star's voice in any demo you create so you can hear exactly how your song might be interpreted by that artist." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**AI for Royalty-Free Stock Music and Scores (N/A)** : AI is increasingly being employed to generate royalty-free stock music and musical scores, particularly for background music needs. This automation addresses the often "sheer drudgery and very time consuming" nature of composing inconsequential general-purpose music, streamlining production for various media. "Because much of what's required is inconsequential general purpose background music, why not get AI to do it?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**AI for Sound Separation in Recordings (N/A)** : AI technology can effectively separate completed musical recordings into their individual constituent parts, such as isolating vocal tracks or specific instrumental elements. This capability was famously demonstrated with The Beatles' "Now and Then," where AI cleaned and extracted John Lennon's vocals from a 1978 demo. "AI can be used to separate completed recordings into its constituent parts." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**AM Radio Dominance (1970s)** : In the 1970s, AM radio was the primary source for most people to get their music. Despite the emergence of FM rock stations in some cities and more cars equipped with FM radios, AM still "ruled," broadcasting music that was often described as "safe and bland and dull." "AM radio was where most people still got their music back then." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gkLMwrthZ9s
**AM Radio in the Early 1970s** : AM radio in the early 1970s was criticized for playing "bad music," including artists like Freddie Fender, Billy Swan, and Paper Lace, and was seen as having "devolved" into playing bland material. This, combined with the "cancer of Mellow" and "self-indulgence of Prague Rock," contributed to widespread frustration among certain groups, fueling a desire for something different. "And AM Radio had devolved into playing stuff like this." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VYi3r0G-ne4
**AM Radio in the US South** : Driving through the US South with an AM radio on in the middle of the night offered a unique listening experience, often featuring content, such as unconventional preachers, that was unlike anything heard elsewhere. "See, there's nothing like driving through the South with an AM radio on in the middle of the night. Because the stuff that you hear is like, you don't hear that kind of stuff anywhere else in the world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gxbuajGKbko
**AM Top 40 Radio Dominance (1970s)** : Between 1974 and 1976, AM Top 40 radio was the predominant source of music for the vast majority of people, significantly more popular than FM stations that played rock and album cuts. This widespread exposure to what was often perceived as "awful music" on AM radio played a role in fueling the rise of punk. "the vast majority of people got almost all their music from AM top 40 radio." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tZV1lz-obQE
**AM and FM Frequency Standards Vary by World Region – Western Radios Don't Work in Russia or Japan** : AM radio in North America uses the 520-1710 kHz band with stations spaced 10 kHz apart; much of the world uses 9 kHz spacing starting at 531 kHz, meaning North American digital radios cannot properly receive most global AM stations. FM differs even more dramatically: North America runs 87.9–107.9 MHz; parts of the former Soviet Union and Warsaw Pact countries use 65–74 MHz (fully incompatible with Western radios); Japan uses 76–90 MHz (also incompatible). When the US moved FM to its current band on January 15, 1945, all FM radios sold to that point in history became instantly useless. The world is divided by the International Telecommunication Union (founded 1865) into three broadcasting regions with different regulations. "Did you know that the radio that you're listening to right now won't work in Russia or Japan?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JvOEAzmC6l0
**ARPANET TCPIP Test – November 1977** : In November 1977, a computer network called Arpanet, operated by Buffen, successfully tested something called TCPIP, which laid the fundamental groundwork for the internet. "And in November, Buffen's running a computer network called Arpanet successfully tested something called TCPIP, which lay the foundations for the internet." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gKrAXMG8sTc
**ASCAP (American Society of Composers, Authors and Publishers)** : Established artists were part of ASCAP, a major music collective, whose material was avoided by radio stations during a 1940s dispute, leading to exposure for other genres. "performers who weren't part of ASCAP, the big music collective." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Aaron Jablonski's Bar Incident (March 16, 2024)** : In the early hours of March 16, 2024, Aaron Jablonski entered the Overtime Sports Bar in Clearwater, Florida, to find the karaoke machine broken. Enraged, he left, and when someone tried to calm him, he pulled out a 9-millimeter Glock pistol, waved it, and fired at least one shot, leading to charges of aggravated assault and felon in possession of a firearm. "In the early morning hours of March 16, 2024, police say that Aaron Jablonski walked into the overtime sports bar in Clearwater." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dVJuITJyNTU
**Abbey Road Studios - Daily Work Bell** : Since the 1930s, Abbey Road Studios has maintained a tradition of signaling the start of the workday with a "school bell-like" ring at 9 o'clock, prompting everyone to report to work. "but then a bell rang. It was like a school bell, really, signaling that it was 9 o'clock. And it was time for the day to begin." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hjProJd_JrM
**Abbey Road Studios - General Overview and History** : Abbey Road Studios is a historic recording facility in London where countless legendary artists, including The Beatles, Pink Floyd, Oasis, Blur, Muse, Kate Bush, Depeche Mode, Iron Maiden, Queen, U2, Michael Jackson, and Glenn Miller, have made albums. The studio has been run since the 1930s with a strict daily routine, marked by a bell at 9 AM. "And this is how the studio has always been run since the 1930s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hjProJd_JrM
**Abbey Road Studios - Studio 3 Soundtrack Recording** : During a private tour, Abbey Road Studio 3 was observed being used by a 65-piece orchestra putting the "finishing touches on the soundtrack for Harry Potter and the Deathly Hollows Part 2." This studio is noted for its large size, comparable to a couple of basketball courts, with a high ceiling. "Studio 3, where we started, is the size of a couple of basketball courts with a very high ceiling. And when I was there, a 65 piece orchestra was putting the finishing touches on the soundtrack for Harry Potter and the Deathly Hollows Part 2." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hjProJd_JrM
**Abbey Road Studios - Vintage and New Gear** : The entire Abbey Road Studios building is filled with a vast array of recording equipment, ranging from "vintage gear from the 50s, the 40s" to "Brand new stuff waiting to be installed, and everything in between," showcasing its continuous evolution while preserving its historical tools. "And everywhere I went in the building, there was equipment, vintage gear from the 50s, the 40s. Brand new stuff waiting to be installed, and everything in between." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hjProJd_JrM
**Abdal's Compilation (2000)** : A compilation record, referred to as "Abedal's compilation" in the transcript, featured songs that were more than 35 years old and was one of the biggest selling rock records of 2000, underscoring the lack of strong new rock releases. "Abedal's compilation that featured songs that were more than 35 years old." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MK7L0eCpDHc
**Abe Simpson's Prophecy of Musical Aging** : The character Abe Simpson famously articulated a common sentiment about aging and musical taste: "I used to be with it, but then they changed what it was. Now what I'm with isn't it? And what's it seems weird and scary to me. It'll happen to you." This quote from The Simpsons serves as a cultural reference for the inevitable shift in musical relevance and personal preference as one gets older. "I used to be with it, but then they changed what it was. Now what I'm with isn't it? And what's it seems weird and scary to me. It'll happen to you." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m7MbV-eCLyE
**Absence of a Universal Music Metadata Database** : Contrary to popular belief, a comprehensive worldwide database containing the complete metadata for every single song does not exist, primarily due to the immense volume of music that would need to be processed and entered. "You would think that there's a worldwide database somewhere featuring the complete metadata of every single song, but there isn't." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Academic Respectability** : Skateboarding gained respectability as a form of physical activity within education circles, leading to the creation of a skateboarding Hall of Fame and even an event in conjunction with the prestigious John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts. "Skateboarding became a respectable form of physical activity in education circles. There's now a skateboarding home of fame." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=44dXy0StjIQ
**Academic Studies on Sexuality and Music** : There are serious academic studies exploring how sexuality has influenced music throughout history. Nadine Hubs, a professor of music and women's studies at the University of Michigan, has written "The Queer Composition of America Sound," which argues that many major classical American composers were gay and their sexuality can be discerned in their musical works. "Nadine Hubs, a professor of music and women's studies at the University of Michigan, has a book called The Queer Composition of America Sound." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hswtuenFwvM
**Acceleration of Music and Recording Industry** : The natural cycle of generational change and preference has been ongoing since music's invention but significantly accelerated with the birth of the recording industry in the late 1800s. This technological leap enabled faster dissemination and evolution of musical forms, intensifying generational collisions over taste and technology. "This cycle of life has been going on since the invention of music, and it only accelerated with the birth of the recording industry in the late 1800s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Accents in Singing: Flattening Effect** : When people sing, their regional accents tend to disappear approximately 99% of the time. This occurs because the melody and rhythm of a song override normal pronunciation, stretching out vowels and neutralizing phonemes, leading to a more "North American" or "mid-Atlantic" sound. "Where do accents go when we sing? This happens 99% of the time, no matter how thick a broke might be." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Accents in Singing: Phonemes and Melody** : Linguists refer to vowels and consonants as phonemes, which are the sounds that differentiate words. While phonemes are pronounced differently across regions, the melody and rhythm of a song tend to neutralize these distinct pronunciations during singing. Stretching out vowels and the emotional expression in singing contribute to flattening accents. "The melody of a song tends to override how someone would normally pronounce a word and it's phoneme. It cancels out what makes an accent an accent." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Access to Filmmaker Stories** : The "Architects of Art" podcast offers listeners access to the stories of filmmakers and artists who have worked with "the biggest and brightest artists in the entertainment business," detailing their creative journeys and collaborations. "It's like we get that access to these filmmakers to these artists." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kbFfT0pKchI
**Accidental Creation of the 12-Inch Single** : The 12-inch single format was developed by accident in the early 1970s by engineer Tom Moulton. Faced with the urgent need to complete a new mix by the end of a weekend and having run out of standard 7-inch blanks, Moulton improvised by stretching the grooves onto a 12-inch blank disc. This new format was quickly embraced by disco DJs and gained widespread popularity. The first 12-inch single is believed to be "All Because of Holding On" by R&B singer Al Downing. "The twelve inch single was created by accident. In the early 1970s, Tom Molten was working as an engineer and he had to finish a new mix by the end of the weekend. He was out of seven inch blanks, so Molten stretched out the grooves on a twelve inch blank." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HoBx6s3JmVQ
**Acoustic Recording Method (Pre-1925)** : Before 1925, recording sessions were conducted acoustically: musicians gathered in a room around a large acoustic horn, with louder instruments positioned further back for balance. Sound was funneled into the horn, vibrating a diaphragm that jiggled a stylus to cut grooves into a rotating disc, creating a master recording without editing, mixing, or overdubbing. "Musicians were gathered in a big room around a large acoustic horn." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Acoustic Recording Method (Pre-Microphone)** : In the early days of recording, music was captured acoustically using a large horn or funnel that channeled sounds to a diaphragm. A stylus attached to the diaphragm vibrated to cut grooves into a rotating metal disk, which became the master. This method allowed "no edits, no overdubs, no fixing in the mix," requiring perfect, loud performances and complete re-recording if any mistake occurred. "That's because records were made in the acoustic way. But it was arranged around a big horn, basically a large funnel, which channeled sounds down to a diaphragm." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NAroxFbYkRw
**Acoustic Tweezers for Moving Objects** : Scientists have developed "tweezers of sound" that can move objects without physical contact, marking a step towards the concept of a tractor beam. Although current capabilities are limited to moving millimeter-sized objects, this development utilizes sound waves to manipulate physical items. "Scientists have figured out how to make tweezers of sound that can move objects without actually touching them." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-N6IM5Nm5iw
**Acquisition of CFNY Vinyl Library (1996)** : In 1996, when the CFNY radio station moved from a rundown strip mall in Brampton to downtown Toronto, management decided to discard its extensive vinyl library, believing digital music would soon render it obsolete. The speaker, however, hired a U-Haul truck and "stole" the entire library, preserving records with handwritten notes from various station personalities like Chris Shepherd, Pete and Geeds, James Scott, May Potts, and Scott Turner. "So the staff heard about this. And over the next few weeks leading up to the move, records started disappearing... I hired a writer truck. And I backed up the truck to the radio station. And I took the library home." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4xxgPX-aR90
**Action Against Grokster (Peer-to-Peer File Sharing)** : Ortoner was instrumental in helping one of the major movie studios shut down Groxter, a peer-to-peer file-sharing service, and secured a win in that case. "and helping one of the major movie studios shut down Groxter." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wYD_dIvYY5o
**Actor Sibling Rivalries** : Olivia DeHavilland and Joan Fontaine, as well as Eric and Julia Roberts, are examples of actors cited for their "legendarily ugly" and intense sibling relationships in the entertainment industry. "Olivia DeHavilland and Joan Fontaine had a legendarily ugly relationship, Eric and Julia Roberts." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=S6qB5B1PdyA
**Adaptation to Digital Music Landscape (2012)** : By 2012, the music industry was grappling with the rise of digital consumption, including iPhones and streaming, while still struggling to adapt from a physical unit-based sales model. Bands like Billy Talent found that they couldn't compete with free content and focused instead on maintaining the quality of their creative output, as touring remained their primary revenue stream. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ocMHRLQMQY0
**Adolf Rickenbacher's Arrival in America and Marriage** : Adolf Rickenbacher, a young man from Basel, Switzerland, moved to America after his parents' death, settling in Columbus, Ohio, where he met Charlotte. Charlotte was an heir to the Standard Oil fortune through the Rockefeller family, making Adolf's marriage to her a union with significant wealth. "Adolf Rickenbacher moved to America...And that's where he met Charlotte. Charlotte was tangled up somewhere in the Rockefeller family tree, and as such was one of those many eras of the Standard Oil fortune." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**Adolf Rickenbacher's Background** : Adolf Rickenbacher, a Swiss immigrant, had a diverse background. His wife was heir to the Standard Oil fortune, and his early success involved inventing a process for manufacturing plastic knobs for ovens. He owned a tool and die shop in Los Angeles, where he was approached by George Beechum and John Dupira for their guitar projects. "He was a Swiss immigrant, whose wife was heir to the standard oil fortune. His first success was creating a process to manufacture plastic knobs for ovens. Not eventually, he led to his old tool and die shop in Los Angeles, which is where he was found when the guitar guys came calling." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E6cXyaHALBQ
**Advancements in Speech Synthesis (N/A)** : Speech synthesis technology, also known as natural speech generation, progressed to a point where only a few seconds of a person's voice were needed to generate complete phrases and conversations in that specific voice. This marked a significant improvement, moving away from robotic voices like Stephen Hawking's to produce more natural, human-like speech reminiscent of Hal 9000 and contemporary AI assistants. "The program's got better and better and better to the point where all that was needed was a few seconds of someone talking and the program could generate full phrases in conversations in that voice." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Advantages of Modern New Vinyl (Post-2008)** : Since approximately 2008, purchasing new vinyl records has become less risky due to improved manufacturing standards. Modern vinyl is typically made from "virgin" PVC (not recycled) and is produced to be notably heavy, with discs weighing 180, 200, or even 220 grams. This increased weight contributes to better stability on the turntable, reducing warping and allowing the stylus to track grooves more accurately, which can lead to improved sound quality depending on the source material and playback system. "Since about 2008, buying new vinyl has come with less of a risk. The vinyl is virgin, and the records tend to be nice and heavy, with each disc weighing 180, 200 or even 220 grams each." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2g4QSREzpw
**Advocacy for "Penny Per Stream" Payments** : The diminished earnings for artists in the streaming era have led to calls for a standardized payment of one penny per stream, aiming to provide a more substantial, though still potentially insufficient, income compared to past physical sales. "So you can see why there were calls for artists to be paid a penny per stream." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Aesthetic and Artistic Constraints on Note Combinations** : Despite the astronomical number of theoretical note combinations, music must ultimately sound pleasing to the ear and soul. This artistic and aesthetic requirement significantly narrows down the number of *workable* note combinations, increasing the likelihood of unintentional repetition in compositions. "Music has to sound pleasing to the ear and to the soul, too. ...there are only so many combinations of notes that work from an artistic and aesthetic point of view." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L09R2evjyMc
**African-American Contribution to Music History** : The history of rock music and its ongoing development are deeply indebted to people of African descent. Tracing important innovations and accomplishments backward often leads to Black culture, from the rhythms and songs brought by enslaved Africans in 1619 to the modern genres like rock and roll, funk, and hip hop. "We would not be sitting here talking about rock music if it weren't for people of African descent." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hQJ3r-DCoMQ
**Age-Related Music Consumption Habits** : As people age (into their 40s or 45-46+), their music consumption patterns tend to shift. They either revert to listening to the music of their youth exclusively or begin exploring bands they missed during their formative years (14-24). The experience of having heard "an awful lot of music" makes it harder to achieve the same "high" from new discoveries. "You'll go back to the music of your youth and listen to that over and over again for the rest of your life. Or you will go back to the era of when you were 14 to 24 and you'll start investigating bands that you missed the first time around." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4xxgPX-aR90
**Agent Role in the Music Industry** : In the context of music, an agent is specifically the person or organization tasked with securing performance opportunities, or "gigs," for an artist. This role is distinct from that of an agent in the film and television industries, where agents often have broader managerial responsibilities. "In the world of music, an agent is the person or organization responsible for getting the artist gigs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Aging Festival Headliners and Top-Grossing Tours** : The phenomenon of nostalgia is evident in the increasing age of festival headliners, where the average age of a lead singer or solo artist at major festivals is now "at least 50 and is quickly moving higher." Furthermore, excluding contemporary pop acts like Ed Sheeran, Beyonce, and Taylor Swift, the "vast majority of the all-time top-grossing tours" are by acts with an average age of 45 and above. "The average age of a lead singer or solo artist at a major festival is now at least 50 and is quickly moving higher." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m7MbV-eCLyE
**Aging Music Heroes** : Many musical heroes are in their 70s and 80s, approaching the end of their life expectancies according to actuarial tables. "A lot of them are in their 70s and even 80s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**Aging Musical Legends** : Many legendary performers are currently in their 70s and 80s, indicating that the music world will continue to experience the loss of cherished figures in the coming years. "There are so many legendary performers in their 70s and 80s that it's just a matter of time before we'll have to live in a world without them." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HBS24NwhrE8
**Agoura Hills High School Music Scene** : Agoura Hills High School, located approximately 30 miles northwest of downtown Los Angeles, was a notable breeding ground for musical talent, producing the core members of Linkin Park (Mike Shinoda, Brad Delson, Rob Bourdon) and also giving the world Hoobastank. "Agora Hills High School, which by the way is the same high school that gave the world Huba Stank." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YcdidjWaIqE
**Akio Morita's Concerns about Social Isolation** : Sony co-founder Akio Morita expressed significant concern that personal music devices like the Walkman could lead to self-absorption and anti-social behavior among users. Consequently, he initially insisted that the first devices include outputs for two sets of headphones and a microphone input, aiming to encourage sharing and communication. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z9T9wi0vEvc
**Akron, Ohio - Tire Manufacturing Hub** : Akron, Ohio, is historically recognized as a major center for tire manufacturing. This background is relevant to The Black Keys, who hail from Akron, attended Firestone High School, and later recorded an album in an abandoned tire factory. "Their hometown, Akron, was the center of tire manufacturing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ooNAHswiT5Q
**Al Green's "Take Me to the River" – Made More from Big Mouth Billy Bass Than Any Other Source** : The novelty singing fish toy Big Mouth Billy Bass featured Al Green's "Take Me to the River." Green made more money from licensing the song to the Big Mouth Billy Bass product than from any other source for that song. "Green made more money from licensing the song to Big Mouth Billy Bass than he did from any other source." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Alan Freed and the Popularization of "Rock and Roll"** : In 1956, disc jockey Alan Freed, after losing a lawsuit, needed a new catchphrase for his radio show where he played R&B records for a largely white audience. Drawing on his knowledge of blues and R&B slang, he adopted "rock and roll" as his new identifier, subsequently labeling all the music he played as "rock and roll records," despite an unsuccessful attempt to copyright the term. "And all the music he would play on his radio show would forever be referred to as rock and roll." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oRR7N-YUcRE
**Alan Turing's First Computer Music Recording (1951)** : In the late 1940s, British computer pioneer Alan Turing programmed his Mark II machine at his Manchester laboratory to create music. By 1951, the BBC recorded three songs performed by this computer, resulting in the first known recording of computer music, though only parts of that session survive. "In 1951, the BBC showed up with a remote recording unit with a task of capturing three songs performed by Turing's computer." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Album Abandonment vs. Completion** : Many artists face situations where albums are not fully completed but rather "forced to abandon" them due to budget constraints or strict deadlines. Often, the songs that received the most creative effort and "labor poured into them" are ironically the ones that do not make the final cut. "oftentimes, the songs that have had the most labor poured into them are the ones that don't get on." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VR5Fncdc2kQ
**Album Cover Art History – Plain Brown Wrapper to Art Form; First Color Record Jacket in WWII; Sgt. Pepper as Milestone** : Early record covers were plain brown wrappers whose sole purpose was to identify contents. The first color record jacket appeared during World War II. By the late 1950s–early 1960s, most LP records featured portraits of the artist. As the 1960s progressed, artwork concepts became more ambitious, culminating with the Beatles' Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band package. The album cover became essential, spawning design companies like Hypnosis. The 12x12-inch LP gave designers an enormous canvas; with gatefold sleeves, designers had 6–8 square feet to work with. "From the middle 1960s, album art just exploded." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JdSUHkXM1mQ
**Album Dominance in Music Business (Post-1967)** : Following the release of *Sgt. Pepper* in 1967, the album became the primary economic unit of the recorded music industry, with singles transitioning into promotional tools designed to drive album sales, which offered significantly higher profit margins. "From that point on, the album became the currency of the recorded music business." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Album as Artistic Medium Emergence (Post-1965)** : Around 1965, artists like The Beatles and Bob Dylan began to view the album as a creative "palette," leading them to compose longer, more complex, and carefully sequenced collections of songs meant to be experienced as a whole, a shift solidified by releases like 'Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band' in 1967. "But then things began to shift around 1965. The Beatles, Bob Dylan, and a few others looked at the album and thought, I can work with this." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Album as an Artistic Medium (Post-1965 Shift)** : The perception of the album changed around 1965 when artists like The Beatles and Bob Dylan began to utilize it as a larger creative canvas. They started writing longer, more complex, and carefully sequenced collections of songs meant to be heard as a unified work, a paradigm solidified by albums like The Beatles' *Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band*. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Album vs. Single Historical Shift (Pre-1965)** : Before 1965, rock and roll and pop music primarily existed as 7-inch 45 RPM singles, with albums generally reserved for "proper music" like classical, jazz, and soundtracks. Early albums from artists like Elvis Presley and The Beatles were mostly collections of singles and B-sides. "Up until then, rock and roll and pop music lived as 7-inch 45 RPM singles. Albums were reserved for proper music, classical recordings, jazz, soundtracks, Broadway cast recordings, and so on." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Albums for Older Generations (Present)** : In the contemporary music landscape, physical albums are largely considered a format for older individuals who recall traditional music consumption habits, rather than the primary mode for younger generations. "Today? Well, sorry, but they're mostly for older folk who remember the way things used to be." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Alex Steinweiss – Pioneer of Album Art (1938)** : In 1938, 23-year-old graphic designer Alex Steinweiss was hired as Columbia Records' first-ever art director in New York, where he pioneered the concept of album cover artwork. His revolutionary idea to photograph a specially arranged marquee sign for a Richard Rodgers and Lorenz Hart release, and use it as the cover, dramatically increased sales and launched a new creative industry. "In 1938, a 23-year-old graphic designer named Alex Steinweiss was hired as the first-ever art director for Columbia Records in New York." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Alexander Vaternukhov Volition - Fake KGB Story** : A widely circulated but entirely fake story, attributed to an alleged retired KGB agent named Alexander Vaternukhov Volition, claimed that the entire punk movement (Sex Pistols, The Clash, Ramones) was financed by the Soviet secret police. This elaborate hoax, originating from the satirical site World News Daily Report, falsely asserted that punk songs were written by propagandists and psychologists to foster cynicism, revolutionary thinking, and drug use, turning Western youth into a decadent, anti-establishment culture. "He claims that the entire punk movement from the sex pistols to the clash to the Ramones was financed by the Soviet secret police." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YeNWDtN_sHE
**Alexis on Fire – Real Person: World's Only Lactating Contortionist Stripper** : The name comes from a real woman named Alexis Fire, described as the world's only lactating contortionist stripper. She attempted to sue the band for copyright infringement, but because she had never registered the name, nothing came of it. "She's a real person who tried to sue the band for copyright infringement." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Alfred Levy's Patent (1966)** : In 1966, Alfred Levy filed a patent for a machine that automatically played music to callers on hold. This early patent serves as the foundation for virtually every on-hold device used today, whether it provides generic pre-taped music, a radio station, or another musical source. "In 1966, he filed a patent on a machine that automatically played music for callers anytime they were placed on hold." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D8GU8ZpSgY8
**Alice Cooper's James Bond Rejection (1973) – "The Man with the Golden Gun"** : Alice Cooper wrote a theme for the 1973 Bond film The Man with the Golden Gun, but it was rejected. The song later appeared on his album Muscle of Love. "He wrote something for 1973's The Man with the Golden Gun, but it was thrown out." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**Alice in Chains – From a Stoned/Drunk Party Conversation About Alice in Wonderland; Layne Staley's Mom Was Upset** : Originally intended as a jokey drag side project where members would dress in drag and play metal. The name came from a stoned and drunk conversation about Lewis Carroll's Alice in Wonderland. Layne Staley's mother was especially upset with the name's connotations and preferred a different name, but when the band broke up and reformed as Alice in Chains it stuck regardless. "Lane's mom was especially upset with the connection." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**All Things Must Pass (2015) – Tower Records Documentary** : This 2015 documentary chronicles the rise and fall of Tower Records. Directed by Colin Hanks, son of Tom Hanks, it includes perspectives from former employees, such as Dave Grohl. "This is a clip from a 2015 documentary on the rise in fall of Tower Records called All Things Must Pass, which was directed by Colin Hanks, son of Tom." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jD1dWxNLvfE
**All-in Pricing vs. Drip Pricing** : Artists have the option to implement "all-in pricing," where the displayed ticket price includes all fees and charges. However, studies have indicated that fans are just as annoyed by a single, higher all-in price as they are by the "drip, drip, drip" of multiple added fees, leading ticket sellers like Ticketmaster to continue absorbing blame for fragmented pricing to deflect negativity from the artists. "Now here is a weird thing. The artist could ask for all in pricing. That means the price you see includes all the charges for the ticket rolled into one." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Alt Rock Reunions in the 21st Century** : By the first decade of the 21st century, alt rock had been established long enough for many significant acts to have broken up and subsequently been "enticed to reunite." These reunions, including bands like the Pixies, Smashing Pumpkins, and Rage Against The Machine, temporarily injected new life into the scene and drew back long-time fans who might have drifted away. "By the first decade of the 21st century, alt-rock had been around long enough that big axe had broken up and been enticed to reunite." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_yslM5oanRo
**Alt Rock's Fertile Period (2004-2007)** : The years spanning 2004 to 2007 are recognized as one of the most prolific times for alt rock. This era saw major labels releasing quality music from both established "heritage acts" and new bands, while the indie rock scene expanded in popularity and influence. "The period from 2004 to 2007 has gone down in history as one of the most fertile times for alt rock." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7dXtgTkdv9w
**Alt-J – Mac Keyboard Shortcut for the Delta Symbol (Triangle)** : The band's actual name is Delta (Δ), the Greek letter that looks like a triangle. The keyboard shortcut to type a triangle on a Mac is Alt+J. "If you want to type that symbol on a Mac, the keyboard shortcut is, yes, Alt J." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Alt-Rock Revolution Fractures Music Video World (Mid-90s)** : The alt-rock revolution, particularly the rapid rise of grunge, caused a significant "fracture in the music video world" by the mid-1990s. This movement, often disdainful of elaborate videos and MTV's influence, rejected glamour for authenticity and anti-fashion, creating a divide alongside the rise of hip-hop. "This marked a fracture in the music video world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Alternative Music Attitude (Late 70s/80s)** : In the late 1970s and 1980s, alternative music was not defined by a specific sound but by an "anything goes" attitude. This meant any music that was left of center, experimental, or disagreeable to mainstream rock was categorized as alternative, establishing a distinct and tribal separation from mainstream rock. "It was an attitude. And that attitude was, for lack of a better description, anything goes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_yslM5oanRo
**Alternative One-Hit Wonder Criteria** : For a top 50 list of alt-rock one-hit wonders, the criterion used is artists known almost exclusively for just one song among the general audience of alt-rock fans, where that one song significantly overshadows everything else they did. "I looked at artists that are known almost exclusively for just one song amongst the general audience of alt rock fans." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZcF5JMhBj2M
**Alternative's Defining Sound in the 80s** : In the late 1980s, "Alternative" began to be used to describe an attitude in rock, acting as an umbrella term for artists that couldn't be categorized by regular genres. This included singer-songwriters, indie pop, industrial, synth-based, goth, noisy guitar bands, and even rap, signifying anything new, weird, and hated by the mainstream. "If you couldn't categorize a song or an artist by tossing it into any of the regular buckets, then there was only one other bucket you could use, and it quickly filled up." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tFBSrKMEZxI
**Ambiguous Music Mention (1980s Alt-Rock)** : The phrase "audio electronic bass band and battle of loving Chantouce" was mentioned as part of the diverse alt-rock playlists in the early 1990s, suggesting the presence of electronic and bass-driven sounds alongside guitar rock, although the specific reference is unclear. "OK, sure, there was still room for the audio electronic bass band and battle of loving Chantouce." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Amen Break Development** : Around a decade after its 1969 recording, the drum break from The Winston's "Amen Brother" was discovered by hip-hop DJs who began looping it on turntables. With the advent of affordable sampling technology like the Akai S1000, this seven-second, four-bar syncopated break became known as the "Amen Break" and was extensively manipulated by producers. "And someone, and we are not sure who, found Coleman's bit in Amen Brother and started to use it on the decks." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Amen Break Usage** : The "Amen Break" is recognized by Who Sampled as the most sampled piece of music in history, appearing in over 5,309 songs across hip-hop, electronic, and EDM genres. Artists from Slipknot to Oasis have utilized this ubiquitous drum break. "According to the website Who Sampled, which tracks samples and songs, the Amen Break sample has been used no fewer than in 5,309 songs, more than any other sample in the history of music, 5,309 songs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**American Federation of Musicians Strike (July 31, 1942)** : On July 31, 1942, James Petrillo, president of the American Federation of Musicians, ordered a crippling strike due to outrage over royalty payments from American record companies. Union musicians were forbidden from making records, severely impacting Big Bands, while non-union acts and singers (who were not considered "musicians") found new opportunities. "So as of July 31st 1942, in the middle of the war, no union musician was allowed to make any records for any record company." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**American Invasion Backlash** : The "American invasion" of grunge did not sit well with a generation of young British musicians, who felt ignored and rejected by their own country and media. This resentment fueled their determination to create a distinctly British sound. "The American invasion did not sit well with the generation of British musicians." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5q5EnZj1FwE
**American Musician Strike (1942-1944)** : The American musician strike, lasting from 1942 to 1944, significantly pressured the music industry by effectively shutting down the recording industry during that period. This strike exacerbated an already challenging environment for music production, occurring concurrently with the shellac shortage caused by World War II. "There was also a paralyzing musician strike in the US that lasted from 1942 to 1944, which pretty much shut down the recording industry." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_Pi7JLwAeOI
**American Red Cross – Logo Complaint** : The American Red Cross lodged a formal complaint regarding the use of a Red Cross symbol on the nurse's hat worn by Jeanine Lindemulder on early versions of Blink-182's "Enema of the State" album cover. They contended that this constituted an infringement on their official logo, leading to its removal from subsequent album pressings. "The American Red Cross complained. They said that the Red Cross on Jeanine's hat infringed on their logo." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Amusia – Up to 4% of Humans Cannot Experience Music as Pleasurable Sound** : Amusia is a neurological condition, either congenital or resulting from brain injury, that prevents the person from processing pitch and music in a pleasurable way. Those with amusia perceive music as meaningless or unpleasant noise — approximately as distressing as fingernails on a chalkboard. They cannot hum a song, tell a major chord from random piano smashing, or detect when a guitar is out of tune. Melody is meaningless to them. It is estimated that up to 4% of humans have some form of amusia. In tonal languages like Mandarin, where pitch changes the meaning of words, amusia creates additional communication difficulties. "Basically amusia is an inability to hear music as pleasurable sound." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NMwMwqMQpf4
**Amygdala and Emotional Processing** : The amygdala, interconnected with the nucleus accumbens, is the brain region where all emotions are processed and triggered. The sensation of "chills" experienced when listening to a great song originates here, manifesting as a direct result of a dopamine rush. "This is where all our emotions are processed and triggered. Again, when we hear a great song and you feel chills, this is where they come from. That's a dopamine rush." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**Analog vs. Digital Sound Reproduction** : Vinyl records are an analog format, designed to smoothly capture real-world sounds. Compact discs (CDs), established with standards in the 1970s, are digital, sampling sound 44,100 times per second with a 16-bit depth, which can store up to 65,536 levels of information to define dynamic range, operating at 44.1 KHz 16-bit. CDs generally offer superior sound quality to MP3s, as MP3s are compressed files with removed information, which the brain attempts to compensate for. "Vinyl is an analog format, which means it smoothly captures real-world sounds. The compact disc is digital." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2g4QSREzpw
**Analog vs. Digital Source Material for Vinyl** : If the original source material for a vinyl record was recorded digitally, the resulting vinyl record is merely an analog copy of a digital recording, which may diminish the perceived benefits of vinyl. A truly noticeable difference in sound quality is most likely when the entire production chain, from initial studio recording to the final pressing of the record, remains purely analog. "it may not matter if the original material for a vinyl record was recorded digitally. You just end up with an analog copy of a digital recording." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2g4QSREzpw
**Ancient Greek Music Example** : The phrase "Horses on this fine moon made in hollow, so beautiful, pro soligone, esti ta dain, ta teloso hummus, that by day" is cited as an example of the kind of music Plato was referring to when he expressed his strong opinions on its impact. This specific ancient Greek phrase is presented as a representation of the music of his time. "Horses on this fine moon made in hollow, so beautiful, pro soligone, esti ta dain, ta teloso hummus, that by day." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m7MbV-eCLyE
**Ancient Greek and Roman Amphitheaters** : The Greeks and Romans constructed large amphitheaters for public performances and discovered that draping a tent over the top improved acoustics by containing the sound. This technique was notably employed in structures like the Roman Colosseum to enhance audio. "They built large amphitheaters, which they discovered worked better for acoustics if you draped a tent over the top to keep the sound in." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qrrwqdpCjpQ
**Ancient Origins of Tattoos** : Tattoos have a history stretching back thousands of years, with evidence found on mummified remains. Examples include Otsul, discovered in the Swiss Alps after a glacier melted, who had 61 tattoos and lived over 5,000 years ago. Egyptian mummies, Pacific Islanders, ancient African communities, bodies from Iron Age Britain, early Japanese societies, and indigenous peoples of North and South America all engaged in body art. "Archaeologists have found mummified remains that are thousands and thousands of years old that sport tattoos. There's a guy named Otsul. He was found in the Swiss Elps when a glacier melted. He'd been there for over 5,000 years and the dude had 61 tattoos, Egyptian mummies, Pacific Islanders, members of ancient African communities, bodies dating to Iron Age Britain, early Japanese societies, and the indigenous peoples of North and South America have all engaged in this kind of body art." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jZeMmPekKMQ
**Ancient Origins of Ticket Scalping** : Ticket scalping, the practice of reselling tickets at inflated prices, has roots stretching back to ancient times. It's speculated that in ancient Rome, individuals outside the Coliseum likely engaged in similar activities, attempting to sell tickets. "Back in ancient Rome, there were probably guys and togas and sandals outside the Coliseum yelling, Quizz and Tiniot Terpiss, which is, I think, Latin for who's got tickets." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D8GU8ZpSgY8
**Andreas Pavel's Legal Battle with Sony** : After Sony introduced the Walkman in 1979, Andreas Pavel, the inventor of the Stereo Belt, sued Sony three times for patent infringement. Despite initial losses, he ultimately won his third lawsuit, securing a share of royalties. This legal victory resulted in a payout of approximately $10 million for Pavel, considering over 350 million cassette Walkmans were sold globally. `"Well, he sued and lost. Then he sued again and lost. And then he sued a third time and won a share of royalties going forward."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Andreas Pavel's Stereo Belt (1972)** : In 1972, former TV executive Andreas Pavel conceived of a personal portable cassette player while hiking in the Swiss Alps, leading to his invention of the "Stereo Belt." This device comprised a battery-powered cassette player worn on a belt, connected to headphones. The first song he recalled playing on it was "Push Push" by Herbie Mann and Duane Allman. Pavel toured electronics manufacturers like Philips, Grundig, and Yamaha, but his idea was dismissed as "dumb." He filed patents in 1977 and 1978. `"One day in 1972, a former TV executive named Andreas Pavl went for a walk in the Swiss Alps. G, he thought, wouldn't it be nice if I could listen to music while hiking? And this led to the development of a portable personal cassette player he called the stereo belt."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Andreas Pavel: The First Personal Music Player Concept (1972)** : In 1972, while walking in Brazil, Andreas Pavel conceived the revolutionary idea of listening to music on the go. He subsequently invented the "Stereo Belt," a small cassette player with headphones, which represented the very first conceptualization of a personal music player, years before Sony's Walkman would popularize the device. "The bottom line to all this is that Andreas Pavall was the guy who first came up with the concept of the personal music player." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PNiL63fY1Y8
**Andy Earl – Cranberries Photographer** : Andy Earl was the photographer responsible for the cover art of The Cranberries' first two albums, "Everybody Else Is Doing It, So Why Can't We?" (1993) and "No Need to Argue" (1994). He worked closely with art director Callie, notably tasked with finding and utilizing the same couch for both album shoots. "They settled on a shot by photographer Andy Earl." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Anita Hill Hearings (1991)** : The Anita Hill hearings, where she accused Supreme Court Justice Clarence Thomas of sexual harassment, served as a "tremendously polarizing" event across North America in 1991. This event poured "gasoline on things" for the burgeoning Riot Grrrl movement, galvanizing feminists to protest for Hill and against Thomas. "Women was the Anita Hill hearings when she accused Supreme Court Justice Clarence Thomas of sexual harassment, which became a tremendously polarizing thing across North America." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qss5kC2FUx0
**Ann Landers and Dear Abby Rivalry** : Sisters Epi and Pauline Lederer, famous for their newspaper columns Ann Landers and Dear Abby which dispensed relationship advice, ironically spent their own lives antagonizing each other, failing to apply their wisdom to their personal relationship. "Anne Landers and Deer Abbey were actually real people, and they were sisters, Epi and Pauline later her. Despite having newspaper columns that were famous for dishing out all sorts of relationship advice to readers, they didn't really apply the wisdom to themselves. So they spent their lives antagonizing each other." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=S6qB5B1PdyA
**Anne Beverly's Suicide – September 5, 1996** : Sid Vicious's mother, Anne Beverly, lived semi-quietly in London for two decades after Sid's death. Struggling with money and health problems, she committed suicide on September 5, 1996, at the age of 64, by overdosing on pills in her favorite chair. "Then run down by money and health problems she committed suicide on September 5th 1996 by taking an overdose of pills while sitting in her favorite chair." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OcylgiXkDP8
**Annual In Memoriam Program (2024)** : The program serves as an annual remembrance, in 2024, of musicians and music people who passed away over the preceding 12 months, aiming to honor their work. "This is the 2024 in memoriam show." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HBS24NwhrE8
**Anti-Abortion Attacks and Pro-Choice Protests** : Several attacks on legal abortion rights in the early 1990s led to more pro-choice protests, which further galvanized and unified feminist activists, contributing to the context in which the Riot Grrrl movement flourished. These political struggles fueled the movement's urgency. "Then there were several attacks on legal abortion that meant more pro-choice protests. Again, galvanizing stuff." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qss5kC2FUx0
**Anti-Home Taping Campaigns (Early 1980s)** : During the record industry's sales crisis of the early 1980s, education campaigns were launched with slogans such as "home taping is killing music" and "it's illegal." The industry's stance was that consumers should buy multiple copies of an album (e.g., one for home, one for the car) rather than taping. "Like education campaigns were launched with slogans like, home taping is killing music and it's illegal." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2RKchFW42Jc
**Antitrust Concerns Regarding Live Nation/Ticketmaster** : The merger of Live Nation and Ticketmaster in 2010 created a behemoth in the live music industry, leading to ongoing debates about whether the combined entity is "too big and too powerful" and if its practices constitute an antitrust situation. These discussions have persisted for years, with calls for government intervention to potentially break up the company. "Is the Live Nation Ticketmaster combo too big and too powerful? Are there practices that add up to an antitrust situation? That's been debated for years, and unless government step in to break things up, that debate will continue." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xid8CZf4oYo
**Anton Corbijn – Renowned Music Photographer** : Anton Corbijn, a Dutch photographer, is a highly esteemed artist renowned for his extensive body of work with iconic musicians, including U2, Depeche Mode, Coldplay, Bruce Springsteen, Kate Bush, Björk, Simple Minds, R.E.M., and Metallica. Notably, he also photographed physicist Stephen Hawking and designed The Killers' "Sam's Town" album cover. "The photographer was Anton Corbin, the Dutch guy who was a favorite of you two, Depeche Mode, Coldplay, Bruce Springsteen, Kate Bush, Bjork Simple Minds, R.E.M. Metallica, and get this physicist, Stephen Hawking." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Apollo Program and Astronaut Superstars** : In 1969, the Apollo program's push towards a moon landing utterly transfixed David Bowie. At that time, the Apollo astronauts were not just national heroes but "worldwide superstars," a phenomenon difficult to explain to those not alive then, and a key inspiration for Bowie's "Space Oddity." "If you weren't alive in 1969, it's impossible to explain how big the Apollo astronauts were. They were worldwide superstars." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0xd0Hf5REg4
**Apollo Space Program's Unexpected Impact** : The American Apollo space program is credited with saving David Bowie's career, preventing him from a life of photocopying and mime. This event is seen as instrumental in setting him up to become one of the most important artists of the 1970s. "But the thing that saved Bowie from a life of photocopying in mime abuse was the American space program." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6oUkMOkEr9U
**Apple Commercials as Cost-Effective Exposure** : Apple commercials provided massive exposure for many bands that would have otherwise cost millions of dollars in advertising, an amount unaffordable for most. These 30-second spots functioned as highly effective promotional tools, often doubling as music videos. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Apple Computer Incorporation – January 3, 1977** : The company Apple Computer was officially incorporated on January 3, 1977. "January 3rd, 1977, a new company called Apple Computer was incorporated." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gKrAXMG8sTc
**Apple II Release – June 1977** : The Apple II computer went on sale in June 1977. "the Apple II went on sale that June." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gKrAXMG8sTc
**Apple iPod Launch (October 2001)** : Apple introduced the iPod in October 2001, entering the portable MP3 player market. The iPod's success, combined with the legalization of portable MP3 players, accelerated the shift from CDs to MP3s as the preferred music format. "including Apple and their little invention called the iPod in October of 2001, when they entered this space, MP3s were in and CDs on their way out." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Apple's "Placement Only" Compensation Model** : Apple reportedly does not pay artists directly for using their music in commercials; instead, the understanding is that artists benefit solely from the placement itself. The exposure is expected to drive audience interest, prompting viewers to identify and seek out the featured songs and bands. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Apple's Impact on Music Licensing** : Apple has played a crucial role in the evolution of music licensing for commercials, dating back to the early 1980s with the Macintosh. However, its impact became particularly pronounced with the launch of the iPod and later the iPhone, which featured stylish commercials that became highly influential. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Apple's Silhouette Campaign Creator** : Susan Ellen Sangin, an art director at the agency Chia Day, conceived the iconic idea for Apple's iPod commercials featuring silhouettes of people dancing to music on their devices. This approach eliminated the challenge of finding actors with the "right look" and allowed the silhouette shapes and songs to change across different ads. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Application of No-Two-Alike Conjecture to Music** : The conjecture is applied metaphorically to music, where a classical symphony is likened to a complex, crystalline snowflake, while a rock or pop song, with its limited notes and arrangements, is compared to a nano snowflake. This implies that rock/pop songs can theoretically sound alike even if developed independently. "If we're going to get into metaphors, a classical symphony would be like a fully formed, ultra complex, crystalline snowflake. However, a rocker pop song, which contains only a very few notes that can be arranged in only a very limited number of ways, would be like a nano snowflake." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wYD_dIvYY5o
**Arcade Fire – Singer Win Butler's Childhood Story of a Burning Arcade** : Singer Win Butler insists the name comes from a story told to him when he was 12: a high school kid who beat him up kept referencing an arcade fire that allegedly killed a bunch of kids. "It was more of a threat, really." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Architects of Art Podcast** : _Architects of Art_ is a new podcast co-hosted by music video directors Director X and Director X. The show features interviews with storytellers from the music video world, delving into their career trajectories, creative processes, and the making of significant music videos, films, and content. The debut episode featured Dave Meyers. "The cool thing is this show is hosted by Director X and Director X, two of the biggest music video directors on the planet." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IIJySSo276k
**Architects of Art Podcast Focus** : The "Architects of Art" podcast, hosted by Director X and Taj Khrishlow, focuses on giving credit to music video directors as "storytellers." It delves into their journeys, processes, influences, highs, lows, and breakthrough moments in creating "iconic music videos, films and content." "It's pretty much us sitting down with storytellers that come from music videos." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kbFfT0pKchI
**Architecture Influencing Music (Cathedrals)** : The architectural design of large, cavernous, and echoey stone cathedrals profoundly influenced the composition of music from the 12th century onwards. Composers like Hildegard von Bingen developed styles, such as slow, somber liturgical chanting (sacred monophony), that harmonized with the long reverberation times, preventing musical chaos and demonstrating how the "technology of architecture influenced the composition of music." "Because of this echo, because of the architectural design of these places, you have to be very careful with the music play during church services." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NAroxFbYkRw
**Archiving Recorded Music** : The concept of a central repository for knowledge was later extended to recorded music, leading governments, public broadcasters, and companies to systematically collect and archive humanity's audio recordings. "governments, public broadcasters and companies began collecting together as much of human kinds recorded audio as they could." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Arctic Monkeys – Guitarist Jamie Cook Chose It; Possibly Japanese Snow Monkeys** : The band dislikes the name, but guitarist Jamie Cook came up with it without ever fully explaining why, though some suggest he saw a picture of Japanese snow monkeys sitting in a hot spring. "The band hates that name, but that's what happens when you don't give things much thought." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Area 51 / Groom Lake UFO Research Site** : Area 51, specifically Groom Lake in Nevada, is a well-known site associated with UFO research, where Tom DeLonge claims to have experienced "lost time periods" during his investigations. "This happened while he was researching wait for it area 51 He was camping out near Groom Lake in Nevada" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pmKjZzypzvg
**Ari Messle – Boy on "Evil Empire" Cover** : Ari Messle is the real name of the boy portrayed in the painting that became the cover for Rage Against the Machine's "Evil Empire" album. He was 11 years old when the painting was completed by artist Mel Ramos as a birthday gift, arranged by Ari's father, an art dealer and friend of Ramos. "His name is Ari Messle." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Aristoxenus of Tarentum's Stance on Dissonance (335 BC)** : In 335 BC, the Greek theorist Aristoxenus of Tarentum declared that any combination of notes sounding "out of tune" was unwelcome in music. This early pronouncement reflects a historical preference for consonance and a rejection of dissonance in established musical theory. "In 335 BC, the Greek theorist Aristosenus of Tartarum proclaimed that any combination of notes that sounded out of tune was unwelcome in music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=41eKVp8JIhU
**Art Context Podcast** : "Art Context: The Architects of Art" is a new podcast hosted by music video directors Director X and Tadges Christelot. The show focuses on interviewing music video storytellers to explore their creative journeys, influences, and the behind-the-scenes process of making iconic music videos, films, and content. "our show is pretty much about, it's in the world of music. It's pretty much us sitting down with storytellers that come from music videos." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**Art Context Podcast - Focus on Music Video Storytellers** : "Art Context, the Architects of Art" is a new podcast hosted by Director X and Taj Khrichlo, two prominent music video directors. The show aims to highlight and give "flowers" to the "amazing creative artists" behind impactful music videos, focusing on their come-ups, journeys, processes, and the untold stories behind their work, including larger world issues like Black Lives Matter. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Art Context Podcast Concept** : Art Context is a new podcast hosted by Director X and another director named Director X (Tadges Christelot, although not explicitly named as Director X), which focuses on "storytellers that come from music videos." The podcast aims to give "amazing creative artists" the recognition they deserve by exploring their journeys, processes, and influences in creating iconic music videos, films, and content. "Our show is pretty much about, it's in the world of music. It's pretty much us sitting down with storytellers that come from music videos." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YFv6Ln00XjA
**Art Context Podcast Content** : The podcast "Art Context" explores directors' "come up," what drew them to filmmaking, their influences, highs, lows, and breakthrough moments, including the behind-the-scenes process like storyboards, art direction, and collaboration. The debut episode featured director Dave Meyers, discussing his approach to art and broader issues like Black Lives Matter. "The first podcast the debut of the show was with Dave Myers. Another guy that's been in the game for a long, long time. And just talking about that philosophy behind his approach to art, the work he's done. And as well digging into some of the larger world issues out there." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YFv6Ln00XjA
**Art Context Podcast Launch** : "Art Context, the Architects of Art" is a new podcast hosted by prominent music video directors Director X and Taj Khrichlo. The show is dedicated to interviewing "storytellers that come from music videos," aiming to highlight their creative journeys, influences, highs, lows, and breakthrough moments, emphasizing the collaborative process behind iconic visual content. The debut episode featured fellow director Dave Myers. "It's with a host of a brand new podcast called Art Katex, the Architects of Art. The cool thing is this show is hosted by Director X and Taj Khrichlo, two of the biggest music video directors on the planet." "our show is pretty much about, it's in the world of music. It's pretty much us sitting down with storytellers that come from music videos. I feel like we live in the world where we don't give these amazing creative artists, the flowers they deserve." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TsiYLGGSJ2U
**Art Context Podcast – Content and Process** : The podcast interviews "storytellers from music videos," discussing their come-ups, journeys, influences, highs, lows, and breakthrough moments. It delves into the behind-the-scenes creative process, including storyboards, art direction, and collaboration, to provide insight into how music videos are made. "It's pretty much us sitting down with storytellers that come from music videos." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g-tBn8dsSqw
**Art Context Podcast – Discussing World Issues** : The podcast also addresses broader world issues, such as Black Lives Matter, as demonstrated in an interview with director Dave Myers, who discussed his perspective as a white director in the black music world. "Like we have a whole talk about Black Lives Matter on that podcast." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g-tBn8dsSqw
**Art Context Podcast – Mission** : The "Art Context" podcast, hosted by Taj Krishlow and Director X, aims to recognize and celebrate the creative artists behind music videos, whose impactful work often goes unacknowledged. "I feel like we live in the world where we don't give these amazing creative artists, the flowers they deserve." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g-tBn8dsSqw
**Art Context: The Architects of Art Podcast** : "Art Context, the Architects of Art" is a new podcast hosted by renowned music video directors Director X and Director X. The show aims to sit down with storytellers from the music video world to explore their come-ups, journeys, and creative processes behind impactful and iconic music videos, films, and content. "It's with a host of a brand new podcast called Art Katex, the Architects of Art. The cool thing is this show is hosted by Director X and Director X, two of the biggest music video directors on the planet." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LgJ7ww2AgF4
**Art Katex Podcast** : Art Katex, subtitled "the Architects of Art," is a brand new podcast. It is hosted by renowned music video directors Director X and Taj Khrichlo, who are known for their "game-changing videos" for artists like Drake, Coldplay, and Kendrick Lamar. "It's with a host of a brand new podcast called Art Katex, the Architects of Art." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zNIVqKqAlnk
**Art Katex, The Architects of Art (Podcast) – Focus on Music Video Directors** : This new podcast is hosted by music video directors Director X, Tadges Chris Lowe, and Karina Evans. It focuses on telling the stories of music video directors, their creative journeys, influences, highs, lows, and breakthrough moments. The show aims to give these artists the recognition they deserve. "Our show is pretty much about, it's in the world of music. It's pretty much us sitting down with storytellers that come from music videos." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OcylgiXkDP8
**Art and Commerce Intermingling** : The coexistence of art and commerce has always been a reality, but in the present day, this intermingling is "much more obvious" and visible everywhere. This increased awareness is attributed to both widespread commercial placements and a greater public understanding of how the world operates. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Art as Provocation** : Some artists believe the purpose of art is to challenge thinking, face unpleasant realities, speak truth disturbingly, and provoke people out of their safe notions, while others simply seek attention by pushing boundaries. "They believe that the purpose of art is to challenge the way people think, to get them to face unpleasant realities, to speak truth regardless of how disturbing it may be, to be provocative in ways that will shake people out of their safe notions and preconceptions." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SIK2YFSmf9U
**Arthur Fogel (Concert Promoter) - "Who the F Is Arthur Fogel" Documentary** : A documentary film titled "Who the F Is Arthur Fogel" was made about his influential career, serving to introduce the powerful promoter to a wider audience as he previously lacked a Wikipedia page. The film took approximately three years to produce and premiered in Hollywood, eventually airing on HBO Canada. "I served as one of the script editors of this documentary, which ended up being called who the F is Arthur Fogel. Maybe you've seen it on HBO Canada." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hjProJd_JrM
**Arthur Fogel (Concert Promoter) - Influence and Achievements** : Arthur Fogel, head of global touring for Live Nation in Los Angeles and originally from Hamilton, is regarded as the "most powerful man in the concert industry." His significant achievements include reuniting The Police, being the only promoter Madonna trusts, and orchestrating U2's record-breaking 360 tour, which grossed $736 million. "Ron explained that Arthur was the head of global touring for live nation in Los Angeles. That was originally from Hamilton and worked his way up through the business to become the most powerful man in the concert industry." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hjProJd_JrM
**Arthur Miller's "All My Sons" (1947)** : This 1947 play by Arthur Miller, also the author of "Death of a Salesman" and "The Crucible," is based on a true story. It depicts an Ohio man who conspired with the army to approve faulty and defective aircraft engines during World War II, leading to the deaths of 21 pilots. "This play is called All My Sons from 1947. And it's based on a true story about a man also from Ohio who conspired with the army to approve faulty and defective aircraft engines for use in combat in World War II." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8sAxFvktx-A
**Artificial Intelligence and Creativity** : The age of artificial intelligence raises questions about whether computer programs can ever produce legends like David Bowie. The transcript suggests that if Bowie were alive, he would likely be using AI as a creative tool. "Science and computer programs cannot pump out legends like Bowie." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yq2A_o9-ulM
**Artist Adoption of AI for Songwriting (April & July 2023 Surveys)** : Surveys in April and July 2023 showed significant artist adoption of AI tools. An April survey of 1,300 artists found over 60% used AI for songwriting (11% for creation, 20% for production, 31% for mastering). A July survey of 1,600 artists revealed half viewed AI positively, and a third planned to use it for new tunes or marketing/promotion. "In April 2023 a survey of 1,300 artists revealed that over 60% of them were already using AI as some kind of songwriting tool." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Artist Catalog Sale Examples** : Examples of significant catalog sales include Imagine Dragons ($100 million), Justin Bieber ($200 million), Red Hot Chili Peppers ($140 million), and Bruce Springsteen ($550 million). "Imagine Dragons netted $100 million, just in Bieber $200 million. The Chili Peppers $140 million and Bruce Springsteen sold his music for over half a billion dollars." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Artist Living Conditions in NYC (1974)** : Artists struggling to afford housing in New York City were forced to live in undesirable, cheap areas like the Bowery, often in lofts that could cost around $150 a month but lacked basic amenities like toilets, showers, or winter heating. "Because you couldn't afford anything in the good parts of town, you ended up living in the parts of the city that weren't so good. Like the bowry." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Artist Manager Compensation Structure** : Managers are compensated through a commission model, typically receiving between 15% and 20% of the artist's total income stream. This means that the more financially successful the artist becomes, the greater the manager's earnings. "They get anywhere from 15 to 20% of the artist's income stream." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Artist Manager Role** : An artist manager, who can be an individual or an organization, is primarily responsible for handling all business-related tasks, allowing the artist to concentrate solely on their music. Managers aim to develop the artist's career, assist with business decisions, manage media coverage, and promote demos to labels, venues, and agents. "Basically, the manager takes care of all the business stuff so the artist can concentrate on the music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Artist Payouts from Catalog Sales** : When artists sell their song catalogs, they receive a large upfront check. This payout is calculated based on a projection of how much their royalties would earn in the future, even extending beyond their lifetime. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Artist Perseverance** : From the artist's perspective, a path to success often necessitates a willingness to endure "lean and dark times" over an extended period. Bands must be prepared to navigate challenging phases on their journey to achieving some form of breakthrough. "Is the band prepared to live through some lean and dark times on their way to some kind of success?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8sAxFvktx-A
**Artist Reluctance to Price Tickets at True Market Value** : Artists and promoters are often hesitant to price concert tickets at their true market value, even when the secondary market demonstrates a much higher willingness to pay. This reluctance stems from a desire not to appear "greedy" or to be seen as "gouging fans." They aim to cultivate a lifelong fan base, recognizing that charging new, young fans hundreds of dollars for a ticket early in their career could deter them. Instead, they often allow Ticketmaster and scalpers to absorb the criticism for high prices. "Artists and promoters are loathed to come across as greedy to their fans." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sPj3uxmz8H4
**Artist vs. Musician Distinction** : A distinction is made between "musicians" who make art and have other interests and abilities (like Bono), and "artists" who live solely to create art and may be ill-equipped for day-to-day tasks or life in general. "Artists make art, of course, but they also have other interests. They have other pursuits, abilities, hobbies. Bono is an example of an artist." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CPxy_KOv87o
**Artist's Role as a "Wandering Minister"** : The role of a musician is analogized to an "ancient job," resembling a "wandering minister" from 10,000 years ago who would travel, play songs, and offer remedies. This perspective emphasizes the timeless human need for communication, creativity, and artistic expression, irrespective of modern industry structures or motivations for fame. "I always say it's like a wandering minister, my job in this modern world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lGNrJgwQjgg
