**Artists Bypassing Labels** : With the rise of social media and online platforms, artists began to directly connect with fans, bypassing traditional record labels and cultural gatekeepers, fostering stronger fan bonds, gaining global distribution access, and rendering record stores obsolete for downloads, thereby breaking the "yoke of the cultural gatekeepers." "Meanwhile, artists were figuring out ways to go around labels and write to the fans." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Artists Embracing Demos and Unreleased Material** : A significant shift occurred where performers and managers, instead of being "freaked out" by unfinished or unapproved material being released by others, began releasing it themselves. This strategy aimed to "forge a deeper relationship with their best customers, their biggest fans," leading to an "explosion of interesting material" from major bands. "Instead of being all freaked out about this unfinished or unapproved stuff getting released into the wild by someone else, they started doing it themselves." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zT6pRGYLWhw
**Artists Seeking Licensing Deals** : Faced with the financial strain of issues like music piracy, artists began actively approaching their managers to explore opportunities for getting their songs placed in TV commercials. This marked a shift in mindset, from viewing such placements as "selling out" to seeing them as a necessary and effective promotional and revenue-generating strategy. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Artists That Allow Fan Recording – Metallica, Radiohead, Dave Matthews Band, Fugazi** : In addition to Pearl Jam, a number of other major acts have adopted policies permitting or actively encouraging fans to record their concerts, including Metallica, Radiohead, Dave Matthews Band, and Fugazi. "Metallica, Radiohead, Dave Matthews Band, and Fugazi have adopted policies that permit or encourage fan recording." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OrHtPVBjXw4
**Artists vs. Labels Profit Share** : Historically, the music industry was structured to heavily favor record labels, which reaped significantly more profit than artists. Even when bands achieved moderate success after accounting for expenses and splits, their earnings were considerably less than the wealth accumulated by label executives like Jimmy Iovine. "But that's how it goes and that's how the industry was built upon the sort of massacre of the artists and the profit of the labels." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lGNrJgwQjgg
**Asperger's Syndrome as a Later Diagnosis** : A later diagnosis for Richie Edwards was Asperger's, now recognized as a form of autism. This diagnosis helped to explain certain aspects of his personality and behavior, such as his intense "laser focus" on particular subjects and his insistence on maintaining strict routines. "Another diagnosis that came later was aspergers, which we now know as a form of autism. Which would explain a few things, including Richie's laser focus and his insistence on strict routines." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sCwgvHg86Oc
**Asteroid 342843 David Bowie** : A chunk of rock in the asteroid belt is named 342843 David Bowie, a celestial tribute to the influential artist. "The asteroid belt has a chunk of rock called 342843 David Bowie." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yq2A_o9-ulM
**Asteroid Naming Rules** : Asteroids, officially known as minor planets, are open for naming by their discoverers, subject to approval by the International Astronomical Union. Historically, classical musicians dominated asteroid namings, but this trend has shifted in recent decades to include rock and popular music figures. "If you discover a brand new asteroid, then you get to name it, pending approval from the International Astronomical Union, of course." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VMPLplhDk8I
**Astrid Kircher's Role in Beatles Haircut** : Astrid Kircher, Stu Sutcliffe's girlfriend, regularly cut The Beatles' hair into their distinctive new look during their long residency in Hamburg, about a year after Yurgen Volmer's initial cut. She first applied the style to Stu, then George, and finally John and Paul. "About a year later, when the Beatles had their long residency in Hamburg, Astrid Kircher cut their hair regularly into this new look." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Astronomers Coining "Champagne Supernova"** : Astronomers are beginning to use the term "champagne supernova" to describe a specific stage in a star's death. This occurs when a sufficiently sized star collapses and explodes in a supernova, producing an initial burst of light from a shock wave that resembles a bottle of champagne bursting open. "And that's why some astronomers are referring to this stage of a star's death as a champagne supernova." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-N6IM5Nm5iw
**Atari 2600 Release – October 1977** : In October 1977, Atari launched its groundbreaking 2600 video game console. "In October, Atari released the groundbreaking 2600 video game console." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gKrAXMG8sTc
**Athens, Georgia Music Scene** : The Athens, Georgia music scene began in the mid-1970s with The B-52s, followed by other groups like The Fans, Pylon, The Tone-Tones, Love Tractor, and Method Actors. This small scene provided the environment for bands like R.E.M. to form and thrive. "The Athens, Georgia music scene, began in the mid 1970s with the B-52s and more obscure groups like the fans and pylon and the tone tones and love tractor and method actors." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sCtQqVBtCaI
**Aubrey Powell – Album Art Designer** : Aubrey Powell was the creative partner of Storm Thorgerson, forming a renowned design duo responsible for countless iconic album covers. Together, they crafted visual identities for legendary artists such as Pink Floyd, Black Sabbath, Led Zeppelin, AC/DC, The Police, Genesis, Peter Gabriel, ELO, Paul McCartney, and Wings. "Together with his partner, Aubrey Powell, he created some of the most iconic album covers of all time." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Audio Quality of Novelty Vinyl** : The audio quality of novelty vinyl records varies significantly. Picture discs are generally considered to have the worst sound quality, followed by glow-in-the-dark records. Records incorporating glitter or spattered colors can also produce "dodgy" sound. For optimal audio fidelity on vinyl, it is typically recommended to purchase basic black records. "In my experience, picture discs sound the worst, followed by any record made with something that makes them glow in the dark." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2g4QSREzpw
**Audiofiles and Vinyl Preference** : Audiofiles never believed that CDs sounded better than good vinyl on a good turntable, rejecting MP3s as awful and hating over-compressed music on CDs, thus supporting vinyl. "Consumers who never really believe the hype about CD sounding better than a good vinyl record on a good turntable." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Audioslave Naming Rights Payment** : When the band decided on the name "Audioslave," they discovered another band from Liverpool already held the name. To resolve this, they made a payment of $30,000 to the English group. "A quick $30,000 payment to the English groups solved that problem." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vLZla5h4xKQ
**Auditory Cortex Function** : Located directly behind the ears and approximately the size of a thumb, the auditory cortex serves as a primary filter and sorter for all incoming audio information. It breaks down what we hear into fundamental components and then funnels these bits to the appropriate processing areas of the brain, working in conjunction with the cerebellum. "It's the thing that filters and sorts all the audio information coming at us. It breaks down what we hear into rudimentary bits and funnels those bits to the appropriate parts of the brain." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**August 11, 1994 – First Item Ever Sold Securely Over the Internet** : On August 11, 1994, Sting made history when a CD copy of his album *Ten Summoner's Tales* became the first item ever sold securely over the internet, for $12.48 plus shipping and handling. "It sounds quaint now, but back then this was revolutionary." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4Jvy4q40tuo
**August 16, 1974 - A Turning Point for Music** : The night of the Ramones' first gig at CBGB, August 16, 1974, is cited as the moment "music began to change forever." This seemingly insignificant event, with a bass player counting in the first song, is equated to a monumental "let there be light" moment for music. "But back to August 16th, that was the night music began to change forever." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=j8uk7BnbXwU
**Auto-Erotic Asphyxiation Theory (1999)** : In a 1999 interview on *60 Minutes* Australia, she suggested that Michael Hutchence died from auto-erotic asphyxiation gone wrong, a claim that the coroner explicitly refuted due to lack of evidence, but which nonetheless fueled tabloid speculation and was widely accepted by many. "On the Australian version of 60 minutes, she intimated that Michael may have died as the result of solo sex play gone wrong. Auto-erotic asphyxiation, which he took too far." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Auto-Tune Technology Origins** : Auto-tune's core technology originated in the 1990s from Andy Hillbrand's work at Exxon, where he developed methods for interpreting seismic data to locate oil and gas deposits. Hillbrand later realized that the same underlying technology, which could detect, analyze, and modify seismic data, was transferable to audio files, allowing it to "detect, analyze, and modify the pitch of audio files," thereby creating real-time pitch correction for music. "Back in the 1990s, a guy named Andy Hillbrand was working for Exxon. His job was to develop techniques for plumbing the depths of the earth to locate deposits of oil and gas." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7Zniri9uSlc
**Auto-Tune in Live Concerts** : Auto-tune is not confined to studio use; it can also be deployed in live concert settings. This real-time pitch correction technology allows a performer's off-key notes, whether slightly flat or sharp, to be instantly corrected, making it sound as though the artist on stage is "nailing every note perfectly" and ensuring a flawless vocal performance during a show. "Auto-tune can be used in a concert setting, making it sound like the person on stage is nailing every note perfectly." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7Zniri9uSlc
**Auto-Tune's Robotic Effect** : When auto-tune is applied at extreme levels, it ceases to be a subtle pitch correction tool and instead creates a distinctive, "robotic effect" on the vocals. This artificial sound is a direct result of the technology being "turned up all the way to absurd levels" and is recognizable in many recordings. "That robotic effect? That's auto-tune turned up all the way to absurd levels." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7Zniri9uSlc
**Average Annual Music Spending** : On average, individuals spend $156 per year on music. Out of this total, approximately $9 is allocated to streaming music services. "The average person spends $156 a year on music. And of that, an average of $9 goes to streaming music services." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**Average Band Lifespan** : Don Letz, a DJ, filmmaker, and confidant of The Clash, posited that the typical lifespan for a band is seven years. This period is generally sufficient for a group to form, achieve stardom, develop creative differences, and ultimately disband. "The average lifespan of a band is seven years." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q-QP7ePxkQE
**Average Music Spending – Jumped from $45 to $120 Per Year with Streaming** : During the file-sharing era, the average annual spend of a North American music fan was approximately $45. With hundreds of millions now paying for streaming at roughly $9.99 per month, the average spend has risen to at least $120 per year. "There's money coming in." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MQ7PDi-D9Ig
**Average Reading Level of Billboard #1 Lyrics** : An analysis of the lyrics from all songs that reached number one on the Billboard singles charts over the past ten years reveals a consistent pattern: they are, on average, written at a third-grade reading level. "If you analyze the lyrics of all the songs to make it to number one on the billboard singles charts of the last ten years, you'll see that they're at an average third grade reading level." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**Aviation Disaster - 1977 Worst Year** : The year 1977 stands out as one of the most severe in aviation history, marked by frequent violent hijackings, often with fatal outcomes, and numerous passenger plane crashes worldwide resulting in significant loss of life. This grim year also saw the worst aviation disaster of all time, where 583 individuals perished when two 747 aircraft collided on a runway in the Canary Islands. "1977 was one of the worst years for accidents in aviation history... That included the 583 people who were killed when 2-747s collided on a runway in the Canary Islands. That was the worst aviation disaster of all time." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dgzF_KBSQWM
**Avoidance of #MeToo Cases** : The speaker expressed that he would "never touch" #MeToo cases within the music industry where there are allegations but no court convictions. He cited the potential for legal trouble and the fact that adjudicating such cases is not his role. "How do you tackle those cases in which there are allegations made but no conviction in court? So I wouldn't, I'm not going to touch that. It's just asking for trouble and they're asking for possible legal ramifications if you say the wrong thing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4xxgPX-aR90
**Avro Arrow Project Cancellation (1959)** : The Canadian government canceled the Avro Arrow, a state-of-the-art jet fighter developed in the 1950s and considered one of the greatest air machines, on February 20th, 1959. This decision resulted in 50,000 people losing their jobs. However, many of the project's engineers were subsequently hired by the newly formed American organization NASA, becoming integral to the Apollo space program, or found work in Britain and France contributing to the development of Concorde. The cancellation is cited as an example of a "hinge factor," where a seemingly negative event had unforeseen, significant impacts on future projects. "So really, who knows if these two projects would have ever succeeded as well as they did had Prime Minister Diefenbiker not canceled the Avro Arrow?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MTDY6qT11Dc
**Ayn Rand's Influence on Rush** : The objectiveist philosopher Ayn Rand, known for books like *Atlas Shrugged*, directly inspired Rush's 1976 concept album *2112*. "This whole thing was inspired by Ayn Rand, the objectiveist philosopher who wrote books like Atlas Shrugged." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1KyNY3zv6uc
**B-Side as Placeholder or Orphan Song** : The B-side, also known as the flip side, was typically considered to have less commercial potential than the A-side. It might be a deep album cut, an orphan song with no other home, or simply a track chosen to fill the available space on the record. "The other side of the record, the B side, also known as the flip side, was usually, but not always, just a placeholder, something to fill the available space." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**BBC "No News" Broadcast (1930)** : On a day in April 1930, a BBC newscast famously announced that "there is no news." This unusual report was then followed by several minutes of piano music, filling the airtime in the absence of current events. "The answer came on and said, there is no news. This was followed by several minutes of piano music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**BBC Radio 1 - Indie Rock Policy Shift** : During Britpop's peak, BBC Radio 1 briefly altered its music policy to prioritize "more indie rock" over standard commercial pop, reflecting the genre's widespread influence on mainstream radio. "BBC Radio 1 had briefly changed their music policy to include more indie rock, unless of the standard commercial pop." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DSvoppf1dGY
**BBC Radio and TV Influence** : BBC Radio and TV had a profound impact on British music culture. Any song or band played on these national platforms, which reached "every corner of the empire," instantly exposed millions of people to new sounds, accelerating the spread of musical trends. "If a song or a band or a sound got played on BBC Radio and TV, which reaches every corner of the empire, millions of people would be instantly exposed to it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zB5zxycrbnY
**BBC and ITV Reporting of Bowie's Death (2016)** : Within moments of David Bowie's death on January 10, 2016, major news outlets like the BBC and ITV in Britain broke the news, confirming his passing at age 69 after an 18-month battle with cancer and noting his profound influence on music, fashion, and acting. "It's been confirmed that David Bowie, singer and musician, has died at the age of 69 this morning." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yq2A_o9-ulM
**BBC's 66 Miles of Vinyl Shelving** : The BBC holds a vast vinyl collection, with its shelving for records measuring an astounding 66 miles (100 kilometers) in length. "Someone measured the shelving the BBC has for its records. And those shelves extend for sixty six miles, a hundred kilometers of shelves filled with records." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HoBx6s3JmVQ
**BBC's Physical Media Archive** : The British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC) is renowned for its extensive archive of physical media, boasting hundreds of kilometers of shelving dedicated to storing recorded audio. "The BBC famously has hundreds of kilometers of shelving for physical media." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**BTS Army Has Planted Over 113,000 Trees Worldwide** : K-pop fans, particularly the BTS Army, have collectively planted over 113,000 trees around the world, reducing carbon emissions by approximately 28,000 tonnes. "K-pop fans have planted more than 113,000 trees around the world, reducing carbon emissions by approximately 28,000 tons." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qtdetc5-MQo
**Baby Shark on Repeat – Inmates Filed Lawsuit; Officers Resigned** : Inmates at the Oklahoma County Detention Center filed a lawsuit claiming they were tortured by being subjected to "Baby Shark" on repeat for four hours in 2019. Two jail officers responsible resigned and faced misdemeanor cruelty charges. "They were tortured by being subjected to baby shark on repeat for four hours." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**Backlash Against Moshing (21st Century)** : The 21st century saw a major backlash against moshing due to persistent injuries, lawsuits, and escalating insurance premiums for artists, promoters, and venues. This resulted in increased security staff, higher ticket prices, and explicit warnings or bans on behaviors like crowd-surfing and stage-diving at concerts. "The 21st century has seen a major backlash against Moshe." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KMx4bmq8o2Y
**Backsliding in the 80s** : After the initial inclusivity of 70s punk rock, the 1980s saw "backsliding" with genres like hardcore punk becoming "testosterone-driven bro rock," where women were "pushed to the back." This regression created a vacuum for a new female-led movement. "But then there were some backsliding in the 80s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qss5kC2FUx0
**Backwards Masking: Decline and Digital Revival** : Backmasking saw a decline in the era of CDs due to the impossibility of playing a CD backward. However, the practice experienced a resurgence in the late 1990s and early 2000s with the widespread availability of digital sound editing software, which made creating and embedding backward messages much easier. "then back masking sort of went away in the era of the CD because, well, how are you going to spin a CD backward?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Backwards Masking: Definition** : Backwards masking, or backmasking, is a recording technique where a sound or message is recorded forward and then dubbed backward into a song. When the song is played, this specific segment is heard in reverse, creating an audio effect or a hidden message. "This is a recording trick where you record something forward and then dub it backward into a song so that when it's played, that piece is heard backward." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Backwards Masking: Early Film Use (1935)** : Reverse audio effects were incorporated into films as early as 1935, notably appearing in the movie "Gold Diggers of 1935," predating its common use in popular music. "Reverse audio can be found in the movie Gold Diggers of 1935." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Band Breakups Due to Parental Divorce** : A recurring pattern in Dave Grohl's early bands (HG Hancock band and Nameless) was their dissolution due to the parents of band members divorcing, leading to the members moving away. "Okay, you're probably starting to see a pattern here." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8ynrwaUi-sA
**Band Careers with Two Acts** : It is rare but not impossible for a band to have a career with two distinct acts, exemplified by bands like Joy Division/New Order, Barenaked Ladies, Black Sabbath, Green Day, and Blink 182. "It's rare that a band has a career with two acts." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tkLRJiOBu0
**Band Disintegration as a Catalyst for Solo Careers** : One reason for an artist to go solo is when their band, or the relationships within it, completely "blows up" and goes "to hell," leaving the individuals with no choice but to "pick up the pieces" and embark on their own careers. "the band or the relationship with the rest of the band blows up. Things go to hell and the individuals left to pick up the pieces." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-f9jhqAHkQo
**Band Management Bureaucracy** : A significant challenge for Blink-182 during the recording of their "Neighborhoods" album was managing the complex bureaucracy that developed during their hiatus. Each of the three members had acquired separate managers, lawyers, accountants, and publicists, leading to a situation where the three-piece band had four managers at one point, making any decision-making process incredibly cumbersome. "At one point during the recording of neighborhoods, this three-piece band had four managers. Imagine trying to get anything done." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q-QP7ePxkQE
**Band Name Origins – 60 Examples (Bandminology)** : A coinage attributed to the podcast for the study of band name origins, combining etymology with branding: "bandminology." The following entries document how specific bands got their names. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Band Name and Domain Name Strategy** : In 1999, Linkin Park deliberately misspelled "Lincoln" in their name to "Linkin Park" specifically to secure the domain name "Lincolnpark.com." This foresight in leveraging the internet for branding was uncommon for bands at the time. "The reason they deliberately misspelled Lincoln, so they could get the domain, Lincolnpark.com." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cUqdY_T7luM
**Bands with Artistic Sensibility** : Several notable bands are cited as possessing an inherent artistic sensibility without becoming pretentious. These include The White Stripes, Trent Reznor, U2, The Cure, Marilyn Manson, Roxy Music, the Sex Pistols, and Blur. This trait is often attributed to members having attended art school, studied art, or being art collectors. "You too, the cure, Marilyn Manson, Roxy Music, the Sex Pistols, Blur, they all get it without going over the top, mainly because each one of them either A went to art school, or B studied art, or C collect art." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RWbKqqW78yg
**Bands with Multiple Reunions** : Some groups, such as the Happy Mondays, are known for breaking up and reforming multiple times, often driven by recurring financial or tax problems, though such bands are excluded from this specific historical account of "clean breaks and definite reunions." "The happy Mondays come to mind. They get together every time they have tax problems." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yR3xc3ra5tE
**Barack Obama Election Impact on Music Mood** : The election of Barack Obama on a platform of hope, coinciding with a humming economy, contributed to a generally happier mood in music, reflecting societal trends. "Helping along was the election of Barack Obama on his platform of hope." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**Barrett Jones, Recording Engineer** : Barrett Jones was a member of the local band 11th Hour who had a recording studio in his parents' laundry room, which he named Laundry Room Records. He recorded eight songs for Freak Baby and later recorded Mission Impossible. "Dave met someone named Barrett Jones, a member of a local band called 11th Hour, who had a recording studio set up in his parents' laundry room and he called it laundry room records." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8ynrwaUi-sA
**Bastille – Dan Smith's Birthday, July 14; Bastille Day in France** : The band had a hard time finding a name until someone pointed out that singer Dan Smith's birthday is July 14 — Bastille Day in France. "That's Bastille Day in France." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Batman's Gothic Form** : The current form of Batman's aesthetic is noted as being influenced by the goth look, indicating the genre's crossover into mainstream fashion and pop culture. "Would Batman exist in his current form without Goth? Nope." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XaLsZFLrMnI
**Bats Greet Each Other with Death Metal-Like Growls** : Scientists discovered that bats greet one another using growls that are acoustically similar to death metal vocals. "Bats greet each other with growls similar to death metal." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qtdetc5-MQo
**Bauxite Discovery in Jamaica** : In the 1940s, "vast stores of a mineral called Boxite was discovered in the red soil of Jamaica." This discovery initiated a major industrial boom with significant social and economic repercussions for the island. "In the 1940s, vast stores of a mineral called Boxite was discovered in the red soil of Jamaica." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zNIVqKqAlnk
**Bauxite Industry and Jamaican Independence** : The profitability and foreign investment in the bauxite industry played a crucial role in Jamaica's "desire and ability to stop being a colony of Britain and a country all of their own." The economic strength gained contributed to the move towards independence. "This played a role in Jamaica's desire and ability to stop being a colony of Britain and a country all of their own." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zNIVqKqAlnk
**Bauxite Mining in Jamaica (Early 1950s)** : Jamaica holds some of the world's largest bauxite deposits. Large-scale bauxite mining began there in the early 1950s, with companies like Reynolds Metal Company, Alcan, and Kaiser exporting the mineral starting in 1952. This mining had profound social effects, including the expropriation of farmland and the displacement of thousands of Jamaicans to cities like Kingston, exacerbating class divisions. "Jamaica contains some of the largest known deposits of boxite anywhere on the planet. And in the early 1950s, boxite mining began in the country and exported by the Reynolds metal company." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**Bauxite and Aluminum** : Bauxite is a mineral that, when processed, yields aluminum. The importance of aluminum meant that the discovery of bauxite in Jamaica had significant global economic implications. "You process Boxite and you get aluminum." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zNIVqKqAlnk
**Beastie Boys Paul's Boutique Cover (1989) – Photo Is at 99 Rivington Street in Manhattan, Not Brooklyn; Lee's Sportswear, Not Paul's Boutique** : The cover of Paul's Boutique, photographed by Nathaniel Hornblower (Adam Yauch's alter ego), shows a shop supposedly in Brooklyn — but the photo was actually taken at 99 Rivington Street at the corner of Ludlow on the Lower East Side of Manhattan, close to where the Beastie Boys first rehearsed at 59 Christie Street near Hester and Canal. The store in the photo is actually called Lee's Sportswear. You can spot an Al Green record, a record by the Modern Lovers, and Dwight Gooden's New York Mets jersey hanging in the image. Today a coffee shop a few feet away is called Paul's Boutique — named after the album, not the other way around. "The shop in the photo is actually a store called Lee's Sportswear." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JdSUHkXM1mQ
**Beastie Boys' Evolution and Influence** : The Beastie Boys transformed from a perceived novelty act to respected artists, growing as musicians, businessmen, and humans. They took risks, experimented, and branched out, ultimately cementing their reputation as one of the most important and influential bands across multiple genres for at least two generations by the time of Adam Yauch's death in 2012. "And by the time it all came to an end with the death of Adam Yaka, the spring of 2012, the Beastie Boys had cemented a reputation as one of the most important bands of not one, but at least two generations." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9_BTe15NooI
**Beatboxes in Organs (Late 1950s)** : By the end of the 1950s, "beatboxes" (early drum machine functions) were integrated into organs, such as those made by Yamaha (Electones) or Wurlitzer. This marked a step towards combining rhythm automation with other instruments. "By the end of the 1950s, beatboxes were put into organs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OiqdZfxdBP0
**Beats Per Minute (BPM) as a Tempo Measurement** : BPM, or beats per minute, is a fundamental measurement of tempo, widely recognized in DJ culture for selecting music suitable for the dance floor. It ranges from 80-100 BPM for rock and hip hop, 120-135 BPM for house music, and 200+ BPM for genres like Gabber and Speedcore. "It means beats per minute. It's a measurement of tempo." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zWNzlSxrj6s
**Beats by Dre and Audio Quality** : Jimmy Iovine's involvement in creating Beats with Dr. Dre was partly a strategic business move, but also motivated by a concern for the "lack of quality in the headphones" available at the time, particularly earbuds. The venture aimed to address this perceived deficiency in audio consumption. "Part of the whole premise of Jimmy doing beats with Dre was some of it was business, which says paid off, Jimmy's quite a good marketing guy, was because of the lack of quality in the headphones." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lGNrJgwQjgg
**Beck's Religious Affiliation** : Musician Beck is a Scientologist. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CkSIErb0hxY
**Bela Lugosi - Vampire Actor** : The world's most famous vampire actor, whose portrayal of Dracula in movies inspired the Bauhaus song "Bela Lugosi's Dead," a foundational track for the goth scene due to its themes of vampires, death, bats, and bell towers. "And of course, Bella Legosi, the actor famous for playing Dracula in the movies." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XaLsZFLrMnI
**Belarusian President's Control Over Radio Music** : Alexander Lukashenko, the president and dictator of Belarus, exercises complete control over the music played on Belarusian radio. He enforces a 20% limit on songs by foreign artists and has banned so many local performers he dislikes that radio stations struggle to find enough music to fill their airtime. "Alexander Lukashenko is the president and dictator of Belarus. He has complete control over what artists get played on Belarusian radio. Songs by foreign artists are limited to 20%. That he has banned so many local performers because he doesn't like them that Belarusian radio stations have a hard time finding enough music to play." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**Bell Labs' Role in Big Muff** : Mike Matthews, founder of Electroharmonics, hired Bob Meyer, an engineer from Bell Labs, to develop the Big Muff pedal in 1969. Meyer's expertise was sought to create a foot pedal that could deliver both on-demand fuzz and significant sustain, which became key features of the influential Big Muff. "He hired an engineer at Bell Labs, named Bob Meyer, to come up with a foot pedal that could provide on-demand fuzz, but also one that could make a guitar sound ring for a long time." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iGav9uJwA_w
**Beneath the Boardwalk Demos** : "Beneath the Boardwalk" was the title given to a collection of Arctic Monkeys demo tracks by a fan named Mark Bull, also known as The Sheriff. This title referenced a regular venue where the band performed. The collection, which eventually contained 18 songs, was instrumental in the band's online breakthrough as fans downloaded and shared the music. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YvcnHKI4oIw
**Benefits of Apple Commercial Placement - Music Video Replacement** : For artists, Apple commercial placements offer a dual benefit: they act as a 30-second advertisement for their music, essentially doubling as a music video. This eliminates the need to spend thousands on producing a video that might not get airplay or be discovered on YouTube. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Benefits of Hobbies for Musicians** : Everyone, including musicians, needs a break from their primary work to rest the brain, recharge, and seek new inspiration. Engaging in hobbies allows individuals to step away from their instruments, explore other interests, and become more well-rounded. For successful musicians, hobbies also offer an opportunity to indulge in long-held dreams and spend accumulated wealth. "Everybody needs a break from whatever it is that they do. You gotta rest the brain, recharge and go in a search for new inspiration." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xc85sLj0Cnk
**Benefits of Music Licensing** : Licensing music offers a dual advantage: artists are compensated financially, and their music gains significant exposure by reaching a broad audience through various media. This exposure can lead to positive outcomes for the artist's career, including increased sales and public recognition. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Benefits of Music Licensing in Ads** : Getting paid to have music featured in a TV commercial proved to be highly effective for promotion. This led artists, facing financial pressures like music piracy, to proactively seek out managers to secure licensing deals as a new and vital strategy for income and exposure. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Best Dressed Briton in History** : The BBC once named David Bowie the best-dressed Briton in history, acknowledging his profound and trendsetting influence on fashion throughout his career. "The BBC once named in the best dressed Britain in history." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yq2A_o9-ulM
**Beyoncé Travels with Her Own Toilet Seat and Reportedly Uses Only Red Toilet Paper** : Beyoncé travels with personal toilet seats and is rumored to use only red toilet paper. "Travels with her own toilet seats and, according to rumors, uses only red toilet paper." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qtdetc5-MQo
**Big Wreck – Named After a Disastrously Bad Band Rehearsal in 1994** : In 1994, the band had a rehearsal where everything went wrong. Guitarist Brian Doherty declared the session had been "a big wreck." The name stuck. "The practice session had been a big wreck." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Big-horned Desert Sheep – Photography Prop** : A stuffed big-horned desert sheep, which holds the status of Nevada's official state animal, was borrowed from a museum and positioned alongside a beat-up camper trailer for The Killers' "Sam's Town" album cover. This prop contributed to photographer Anton Corbijn's artistic theme of "tired and faded glory." "There's also a big horned desert sheep lying next to the trailer." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Bill Barilko's Disappearance and the Toronto Maple Leafs** : Bill Barilko, a defenseman for the Toronto Maple Leafs who won four Stanley Cups in five years, scored an overtime goal on April 21, 1951, against Jerry McNeil of the Montreal Canadiens in the fifth game of the Stanley Cup finals, securing the cup for the Leafs in a series where all five games went to overtime. On August 26, 1951, he disappeared during a fishing trip in Quebec in a small float plane piloted by his dentist friend, Henry Hudson. The crash, likely due to pilot error, bad weather, and an overloaded aircraft, resulted in both men's deaths. The wreckage was not discovered until June 6, 1962, approximately 100 kilometers north of Cochran, Ontario, indicating the plane was at least 35 miles off course. During the entire period of Barilko's disappearance, the Toronto Maple Leafs did not win another Stanley Cup, giving rise to the legend. "During the whole time Borrillco was missing, the Leafs did not win another Stanley Cup, so there's the truth behind the legend." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Vs1YyXgLpTE
**Bill Gates Microchip Vaccination Theory** : A conspiracy theory posits that Bill Gates promotes vaccinations because he intends to inject miniature tracking chips into all people. "or that Bill Gates did big on vaccinations because he wants to inject miniature tracking chips into all of us for some reason." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pmKjZzypzvg
**Bill Gates and CD-ROMs as a Catalyst for Digital Music** : The theory posits that Bill Gates's demand for computers to come standard with CD-ROM drives (initially for Windows software installation) inadvertently sparked the digital music revolution. This led to the discovery that CD-ROM drives could play music CDs and transfer digital files to hard drives, facilitating the shift from physical plastic to digital file sharing at light speed. "And it all began when Bill Gates demanded that computers come standard with CD-ROM drives so they could install Windows." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r8AazBVoS7g
**Bill Grundy TV Episode** : This notorious television episode involved the Sex Pistols and journalist Bill Grundy, during which Grundy made suggestive comments to Siouxsie Sioux. "In fact, if you've ever seen or heard the famous Bill Grundy TV episode with the pistols, the woman Grundy hits on is, in fact, Suzy Su." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**Bill Grundy TV Show (December 1976)** : A famous television appearance by the Sex Pistols, accompanied by their fans, the Bromley Contingent, which included Susan Ballion. "including to the famous Bill Grundy TV show in December 1976." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XaLsZFLrMnI
**Bill Grundy's TV Persona** : Bill Grundy was a renowned TV personality and host of "Today" on ITV, known for being the first TV host to present The Beatles in 1962. He was also known to "Tipple before and during a show." "Bill was the real thing, kind of like the Oprah of his day. He was the first TV host to present the Beatles back in 62, and later he hosted several other national TV shows." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Bill Hicks Dedication** : Radiohead's album "The Bends" was dedicated to comedian Bill Hicks, who died of pancreatic cancer at age 32. He is mentioned as "the same guy, tool reviewers." "The album, the Benz, was dedicated to comedian Bill Hicks, the same guy, tool reviewers." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JUvDp3_RBH0
**Billboard Singles Charts Methodology** : Billboard is the primary compiler of charts in North America, employing a complex methodology to determine which songs will chart. Despite this, "Ready and Steady" by DA managed to appear on the charts without known official release, sales, or airplay. "Billboard is the biggest compiler of charts in North America and follows a complex methodology when it comes to determining which songs will chart." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UsPoPUByjYw
**Billy Crystal's Babysitter – Billie Holiday** : Billy Crystal's father, Jack Crystal, owned Commodore Records. When Jack needed a babysitter for young Billy, he asked one of the artists on his label — Billie Holiday — to watch him. "That artist was Billy Holiday." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**Billy Idol Stole His Own Master Tapes to Force an Artwork Change** : Billy Idol was such a heroin addict in the early 1980s that when he was fighting with his record label over a flaw in the Rebel Yell album artwork, he snuck into Electric Lady Studios in New York, stole the master tapes, and gave them to his drug dealer. He then called the record company and informed them that the dealer would have bootlegs on the street within a week unless the artwork was fixed. The label capitulated. "Billy got his way." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Billy Joel – Never Sells Front Row Seats; Richard Lewis Referenced in "My Life"** : Billy Joel never sells front row seats at his concerts; those rows are kept empty and gifted to people with nosebleed tickets. Also: in his song "My Life," the lyric about an old friend doing a stand-up routine in LA is a reference to comedian Richard Lewis, who died in February 2024. "He never sells front row seats to any of his shows." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Billy Talent – Character in the Canadian Film Hardcore Logo** : Named after a character in the Canadian rock-and-roll film Hardcore Logo, played by Calum Keith Rennie — later known for Battlestar Galactica and Californication. In the film, "Talent" is spelled with two L's. "You know this story." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Binaural Hearing** : The human auditory system has two ears, which is the minimum required to pinpoint sound location, a concept known as binaural hearing. "This is called Bynoral Hearing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Biological Basis of Music Appreciation** : Scientists suggest there is no clear evolutionary or biological reason for humans to have music, as the species could survive without it. However, the human brain is hardwired to crave and create music, a trait even found in Neanderthals, indicating a deep, possibly language-related, connection. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHN6db7m3DM
**Bird Houses (1960s)** : This is an example song by the "Unknown Mystery 60s Group," whose origin from a reel-to-reel tape found in a Philadelphia flea market remains shrouded in mystery, with ongoing debate about the group's authenticity. "It's called Bird Houses." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UsPoPUByjYw
**Birds Singing in Their Sleep** : It has been observed that birds engage in singing even while they are asleep, a phenomenon that may be related to memory consolidation or processing daytime experiences. "Birds singing in their sleep. Who knew?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**Birmingham and the British Industrial Revolution** : Birmingham, England, became a major center of the British Industrial Revolution, driven significantly by its textile industry. The mechanization of textiles necessitated supporting industries like steam engine manufacturing, which also aided nearby mining. This industrial growth led to increased steel production and a population boom as factories, including new sheet metal and fabrication plants, required a constant labor supply. "Birmingham, England, became one of the centers of the British Industrial Revolution, largely because of the textile industry in the city." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**Birth of Punk Rock in New York (1973-1974)** : The simultaneous collapse of the Mercer Art Center in August 1973, which displaced New York's "weird music freaks," and the subsequent rise of CBGB as a music venue provided the essential conditions for punk rock's birth. Television's pioneering gigs at CBGB, starting in December 1973, attracted former Mercer Art Center regulars and led owner Hilly Crystal to pivot his club's music policy, making CBGB "ground zero" for punk rock throughout 1974 and the rest of the decade. "The collapse of the Mercer Art Center and the rise of CBGBs in New York. Two events that when combined together gave punk rock a place to be born." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MTDY6qT11Dc
**Birth of Ticketed Events and Scalping (Ancient Times)** : The practice of selling tickets emerged as a way for performers and venues to generate revenue and control attendance. Simultaneously, the phenomenon of scalping was born, with individuals selling tickets at inflated prices outside sold-out events, as seen even in ancient Greek and Roman theaters. "Selling tickets was how the performer and the venue made money and it also limited attendance to any given show. And thus, both the ticketed event and the scalper was born." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OCkov6FTliE
**Birth of YouTube (December 15, 2005)** : YouTube officially launched on December 15, 2005, founded by former PayPal employees Steve Chen, Chad Hurley, and Jawed Karim. The platform was created to address the difficulty of sharing videos online in 2005, with various origin stories including frustration over the Janet Jackson Super Bowl incident and an initial idea for a video dating site. "YouTube was born. And it was launched on December 15th, 2005." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Bizarre TikTok Challenge** : A peculiar musical TikTok challenge emerged that involved using mothers' breasts as bongo drums. "There was a weird musical TikTok challenge this year that involved using your mums boobs as bongo drums." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HoBx6s3JmVQ
**Black Lives Matter Discussion on Art Context** : The debut episode of the "Art Context" podcast featured director Dave Meyers, and included a discussion about "Black Lives Matter." This conversation explored broader world issues and Meyers' perspective as a white director who emerged in the Black music world. "Like we have a whole talk about Black Lives Matter on that podcast. And being a white director, his perspective coming up in a black music world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LgJ7ww2AgF4
**Black Lives Matter Movement Reference** : The First Amendment debate sparked by Body Count's song "Cop Killer" was noted for sharing many elements with the later Black Lives Matter movement, particularly concerning social justice and police issues. "Metal thing blew up into a big first amendment debate in the US, which had many of the same elements as the Black Lives Matter movement." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PdCAjFW04Oc
**Black Rock Coalition** : The Black Rock Coalition is a non-profit organization dedicated to providing assistance and support for Black artists within the music industry. "Black Rock Coalition, and nonprofit that provides help for Black artists." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hQJ3r-DCoMQ
**Blackboard Jungle Audience Reaction (1955)** : The 1955 film *Blackboard Jungle*, intended to warn about juvenile delinquency, ironically attracted delinquents and wannabes. When Bill Haley and the Comets' "Rock Around the Clock" played, kids would stand up and dance in theaters, occasionally leading to violence and vandalism. The movie was subsequently banned in many cities and throughout the UK due to this disruptive audience behavior. "The movie was supposed to warn of the growing danger of juvenile delinquency. Instead, it attracted delinquents and delinquent wannabes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**Blackwork Tattoos - History and Characteristics** : Blackwork tattoos are a style with a history dating back thousands of years. Ancient peoples used such body art to signify identity, loyalties, and authority, a designation that evolved into "tribal tattoo." This style entered modern tattoo culture towards the end of the 1980s, influenced by the book *Modern Primitives*. Due to the extensive ink required, Blackwork pieces demand many hours of tattooing and are associated with significant pain. "This is called a Blackwork tattoo and has a history going back thousands of years. Ancient peoples use such body art to promote their identity, their loyalties, and their authority, and it's the designation as a tribal tattoo." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jZeMmPekKMQ
**Blaming Bob Geldof** : She hysterically claimed that Michael Hutchence's argument with Bob Geldof was the direct cause of his death, stating, "He killed Michael." She later vacillated between this accusation and the auto-erotic asphyxiation theory. "And then there were the hysterical shriekings of Paula Yates, who maintained that it was the argument with Bob Geldoff that took Michael to the brick. He killed Michael. She said of Geldoff." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Blank Cassette Market** : When cassettes saw a resurgence in popularity among hipsters, the primary consumers for blank cassettes were police forces, who utilized them for suspect interviews. "the biggest clients for blank cassettes were police forces who used them for suspect interviews." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=72Lei7uug8c
**Blanket Performing Rights Licenses Explained** : All venues hosting public events are required to hold an annual performing rights license, a fee paid to cover the use of copyrighted music, live or recorded. These fees are distributed to artists whose music is played, with the cost varying by venue size. As a "blanket license," it technically allows the use of any song without seeking specific permission, forming the basis of political campaigns' arguments for music use. "And it's a blanket license, meaning that no one has to seek permission to use any particular song." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GYUuTbOvyak
**Blind Melon – Brad Smith's Father's Description of Hippies; Referenced Cheech & Chong** : Guitarist Brad Smith's father used "blind melon" to describe hippies. He may have borrowed it from a Cheech and Chong character named "Blind Melon Chitlin," itself a parody of real 1920s blues musician Blind Lemon Jefferson. "His father used to describe hippies this way." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Blink-182 "Enema of the State" Cover – Red Cross Symbol Removed Under Geneva Convention Complaint** : The original album cover featured a red cross on the nurse's hat. The American Red Cross informed the band that this violated the Geneva Convention since the band was not a medical entity. The cross was changed to the image that appears on the final release. "Blink understood and changed it to what we see now." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**Blink-182 Co-headlining Tour (2001)** : A pivotal co-headlining tour in 2001 with Blink-182 proved instrumental in revitalizing Green Day's career. The tour helped them reconnect with a younger audience, who were largely attending to see Blink-182, and solidified Green Day's status as "punk rock elder statesman," providing crucial momentum for their subsequent album. "What saved Green Day was a co-headlining tour with Blink 182 in 2001." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nyfdGYWelic
**Blink-182 – Multiple Stories; Most Likely Random; Possibly "F*** Off" in Poway High School Code** : Originally just Blink, forced to add a suffix due to an Irish band with prior rights. The 182 has multiple explanations: times Tony Montana says the F-word in Scarface (widely cited but disputed), Mark Hoppus's ideal weight in pounds, a ship his grandfather served on. Most likely it was pulled randomly out of the air during a phone call with the label. Additional theory: at Tom DeLonge's high school in Poway, California, "182" was code for the F-word (8th letter = H? No: B=18th letter... actually under this theory "go" meaning 182). DeLonge has discussed this, suggesting the name should be pronounced "Blink F**k Off." "Blink 182 or Blink 182 if you want to go with the Tom DeLong explanation." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Bloody Sunday (January 30, 1972)** : On January 30, 1972, during a banned march of up to 15,000 protesters in Derry, Northern Ireland, British soldiers opened fire after stones were thrown, resulting in 26 people shot, 13 dying at the scene, and one dying later. This event is known as the Bogside Massacre or Bloody Sunday. "The day became known as the Bogside Massacre to some and Bloody Sunday to others." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cM3-tINKdvo
**Bloody Sunday Massacre (1972)** : On January 30, 1972, in Derry, Northern Ireland, a group of unarmed protesters marched despite a ban on parades imposed by the mayor to de-escalate "The Troubles" between Irish Protestants and Catholics. Up to 15,000 people took to the streets, and when stones were thrown at British soldiers, the situation escalated. Ultimately, 26 people were shot, with 13 dying at the scene and one later. The event became known as the Bogside Massacre to some, and Bloody Sunday to others, influencing U2's song "Sunday Bloody Sunday." "On January 30, 1972, a group of unarmed protesters were marching in the northern Irish town of Derry." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4PlieAEthSg
**Blow-up Globe – Photography Prop** : A blurry, spinning blow-up globe was brought to the "Definitely Maybe" album cover photoshoot by Oasis's sound guy Mark Coyle and roadie Phil Smith. This prop was strategically placed to symbolize the band's ambitious aspirations for "world domination." "That blurry spinning thing is a blow up globe." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Blur vs Oasis Chart War (August 1995)** : A rivalry between Blur and Oasis escalated into a full-on war, culminating in a direct chart battle on August 14, 1995, when both bands released their singles (`Country House` and `Roll With It`) on the same day. This media-hyped event, dubbed "London versus the North," saw record sales across Britain jump 41% that week, with Blur ultimately winning the single battle. "The party started to peak in the summer of 1995, and the best part was the war between blur and oasis." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5q5EnZj1FwE
**Blur vs Oasis Rivalry (1995)** : The rivalry between Blur and Oasis, which intensified into "full on hatred" by summer 1995, became a central and media-fueling aspect of Britpop's peak. Blur was portrayed as "articulate defenders of the realm," while Oasis was seen as "foul mouth, hard drinking, drug-taken yobbs." "The party reached its peak in the summer of 1995, and the best part was this war between blur and oasis." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vzyMSSlD7Yg
**Blur – Label Hated "Seymour"; Told Them to Pick From a List** : When Blur signed their record deal they were called Seymour, but the label hated it. The band was presented with a series of alternatives and chose Blur. "So they were presented with a series of new names, told to pick one." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Bob Dole's Political Career** : Republican politician Bob Dole was offered Jim Grohl's services as a consultant after Robert Taft, Jr. left politics, an offer Jim Grohl declined. Dole later lost the 1996 presidential election to Bill Clinton, effectively ending his career as a political consultant. "Jim Grohl was offered a job working for Republican Bob Dole, but he turned that down. And that was just as well because Dole got killed by Bill Clinton when he ran for president in 1996." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8ynrwaUi-sA
**Bob Ezrin's Recording Facility** : Enigma Digital is a recording facility run by Bob Ezrin, alongside his other business and community pursuits. "Bob also runs a recording facility called Enigma Digital." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkSKxm_U9Yc
**Bob Hardy's Creative Pursuits** : Beyond his role as bassist for Franz Ferdinand, Bob Hardy is a multidisciplinary artist. He writes graphic novels, with one notable project being based on the development of viaducts. He also collaborated on an almanac with his aunt, Françoise Hardy, who is an astrologer and musician. Additionally, he is deeply involved in video games and served as a consultant for the development of the game Rock Band. "Bob also writes graphic novels. Last I heard he was working on one based on the development of Viaducts." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RWbKqqW78yg
**Bob Roberts Tattoo Studio** : Bob Roberts Tattoo in Los Angeles is identified as one of the early and very famous tattoo shops in the city. Its owner, Bob Roberts, was a highly sought-after artist, often booked years in advance, and was known to have tattooed Flea from Red Hot Chili Peppers. "Bob Roberts tattoo was one of the early tattoo shops in Los Angeles." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jZeMmPekKMQ
**Bond Theme Musical Commonalities (1962-Present)** : Over the decades, many James Bond themes share common musical characteristics: most are in a minor key, possess a forbidding and serious tone, and always feature a significant orchestral arrangement. These elements constitute "the major bit of continuity" that producers believe defines a proper Bond theme, distinguishing it from other action movie themes. "Most are in a minor key. There's almost always a forbidding serious tone and like I said a few minutes ago there has to be some kind of big orchestral arrangement." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=K0pgJ7XAlpo
**Book: Rip It Up and Start Again, Post Punk 1978 to 1984** : Written by Simon Frith, this book is an excellent recommended reading that delves deeply into the concepts of New Wave and Post Punk, covering the period from 1978 to 1984. "there's an excellent book on the whole concept of New Wave and Post Punk called Rip It Up and Start Again, Post Punk 1978 to 1984 by Simon Frith." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9CNNsqXiL6U
**Bookies and Media Frenzy** : During the Blur vs. Oasis chart war, bookmakers took bets on the outcome, and non-stop media coverage from all papers, magazines, and radio stations contributed to the frenzy, with everyone taking sides and even the state BBC getting "mad for it." "Bookies took bets, everybody took sides." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5q5EnZj1FwE
**Boomer and Gen X Fuel for Massive Tours and Album Sales** : Boomers and Gen Xers continued to be the primary demographic fueling massive rock tours and purchasing a significant number of albums in the 2010s. This sustained the commercial viability of established acts and their comebacks. "The BOOMers and Gen Xers continue to fuel these massive tours while continuing to buy non-insignificant numbers of albums." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HSvgRyGiOpE
**Bootlegging and Organized Crime – Mafia, IRA, Al-Qaeda Connections** : The bootleg trade has historically had documented links to organized crime. Allegations have included connections to the Mafia, claims that bootleg profits funded the IRA in the 1980s, and more recently accusations that revenues in the South American "triple frontier" region funded Al-Qaeda. "Has had historical ties to organized crime, from the Mafia to accusations of funding the IRA in the 80s or Al-Qaeda in the South American 'triple frontier.'" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Uly2cT2vc28
**Bootlegging of Unreleased Material** : Unreleased or demo material from famous musicians, even if "never supposed to be released," often finds its way to the public through bootlegs. For example, Dave Grohl's 16 demo songs from 1994, despite being discarded, appeared on an "unofficial CD" (a bootleg) released in Japan in 1995. Similarly, his Pre-Foo Fighters demos were "immediately bootlegged" after limited distribution to friends. "But, like I said, nothing that Dave has ever done seems to disappear." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_AY5lS-FmlU
**Bootlegging of the Show** : The speaker's show has been extensively bootlegged over the years. This includes cassette tapes being sold for $40 each in independent record stores (such as in Chicago) and instances of the show being broadcast over shortwave radio at festivals. Additionally, torrent sites are populated with these shows. The speaker acknowledges there is "nothing we can do about it." "My sister was living in Chicago for a while. She went into an independent record store and they had a whole bunch of cassettes out into the counter that were were my shows taped off the radio somewhere. And they were selling them for 40 bucks a piece." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4xxgPX-aR90
**Border Blaster Mexican Radio Stations – 500,000-Watt Signals Covering the Continent** : While US and Canadian AM stations were capped at 50,000 watts, Mexican stations just across the US border operated at 100,000, 250,000, or even 500,000 watts — powerful enough for signals to reach across North America and sometimes Europe. Birds approaching transmitters were literally cooked by the electromagnetic radiation. The most famous was XERF AM in Ciudad Acuña (across from Del Rio, Texas), associated with Wolfman Jack, which could reportedly be heard continuously from New York to LA. XERB near Tijuana claimed 500,000 watts while branding itself as "the mighty 1090 in Hollywood" to seem like a US station. "Animals coming too close to the transmitters would be literally cooked to death." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iyGUXdDqIlI
**Borderless World of Music via YouTube** : YouTube's global availability and legal monetization flattened the music industry, effectively eliminating geographic barriers. This allowed genres like K-pop, Latin music, and Afrobeat to explode internationally, making music exist in a "borderless world" where artists can reach audiences worldwide. "Geographic barriers are gone. K-pop can be seen everywhere. Latin music exploded. Afrobeat has gone international. And music now exists in a borderless world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Borderline Personality Disorder Diagnosis** : Richie Edwards was diagnosed with Borderline Personality Disorder (BPD) at Priory Hospital, a condition that was relatively newly named at the time. This diagnosis helped to explain many of his personal struggles, including issues with identity, difficulties in friendships, career choices, sexuality, and tendencies to detach from reality or experience dissociative states. "Two weeks into his stay at the primary, Richie was diagnosed with borderline personality disorder, a newly named condition at the time." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sCwgvHg86Oc
**Boredom with Conventional Rock and Pop (Late 1960s)** : By the end of the 1960s, a growing number of music fans became bored with conventional rock and pop. They actively sought out and reveled in music that was different and challenging, especially if it was disagreeable to the mainstream and hippie culture. "By the end of the 1960s, there was a growing number of music fans who were bored with conventional rock and pop." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6oUkMOkEr9U
**Boston Area Music Scene** : The Boston area fostered a rich musical subculture, boasting 11 universities and colleges, plus the prestigious Berklee College of Music. This concentration of institutions attracted people from around the world to share ideas, leading to the emergence of many influential indie bands. "For some cities, it was an embarrassment of riches. For example, there are 11 universities and colleges in the Boston area, plus the prestigious Berkeley College of Music. This means people from all over the world meet in the Boston area to share ideas." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sCtQqVBtCaI
**Boston Crew** : In Boston, followers of Ian MacKaye's straight-edge philosophy were so intense that they would often brawl with anyone they saw drinking or doing drugs at their shows. These self-appointed "moral police" were known locally as the "Boston Crow." "Those self-affointed more police were locally known as the Boston Crow. You didn't want to mess with them." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PEHkKfmmDiI
**Bots and Instant Sell-outs** : Online ticket sales are heavily targeted by scalpers using automated software programs called "bots." These bots, combined with the high-speed processing capabilities of ticketing systems like Ticketmaster, can hammer online box offices with thousands of requests per minute, causing a limited public ticket inventory to sell out within 60 seconds. "Plus we have scalpers using software buying programs called bots to hammer the online box office with requests for tickets." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Boutique Labels** : Boutique labels are record companies that appear to be independent but are, in fact, carefully disguised subsidiaries of a major label. They are established to offer the "street cred" associated with indie labels while still operating under a major's extensive resources and distribution network. "The first was the creation of boutique labels. These are record companies that look and sound indie, but are actually carefully disguised subsidiaries of a major label." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ft8nruhWCas
**Bowie Bonds as Asset-Backed Securities (1997)** : The "Bowie Bonds," created by David Bowie and investment banker David Pullman in 1997, were a financial instrument where 287 songs from 25 albums recorded before 1990 became asset-backed securities. Bowie received $55 million upfront in exchange for forfeiting 10 years of royalty income. "David Bowie and an investment banker named David Pullman created a financial instrument called Bowie Bonds. To use Wall Street speak, 287 songs from 25 albums recorded before 1990 became asset-backed securities. In exchange for forfeiting royalty income for a period of 10 years, Bowie received an immediate payment of $55 million." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Bowie Shrines (2016)** : Following David Bowie's death, the building on Lafayette Street where he lived in New York became a shrine, as did a mural of him in Brixton, his birthplace. Other tributes and shrines also appeared in cities such as Berlin and Los Angeles. "The building on Lafayette Street where he lived became a shrine." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yq2A_o9-ulM
**Box Set Including Berlin Wall Piece** : The Scorpions, a major rock band from Germany, released a special box set that contains an authentic piece of the Berlin Wall. "The Scorpions. The biggest rock band out of Germany have a box set that comes with an actual chunk of the Berlin wall." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HoBx6s3JmVQ
**Box Sets as Source for Demos** : Box sets have become "big business" during an era of declining CD sales, offering a high-profit margin for artists willing to undertake the extensive and legally complex process of sorting, cleaning, and authenticating old tapes and recording sessions. Fans are willing to pay a "hefty premium" for the comprehensive package, including liner notes, books, booklets, and extra artwork, and the limited nature of many box sets can also make them an investment. "Box sets have become big business in the era of declining CD sales." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zT6pRGYLWhw
**Brain Connectivity and Chills** : Research from California indicates that individuals who frequently experience chills when listening to music possess a greater number of connecting fibers between their auditory cortex and the brain regions responsible for processing emotions. This enhanced communication allows for a more fluid interplay between music and emotions, leading to a distinct physical response, suggesting such individuals have an "extra special brain." "people who get chills when listening to music have more fibers connecting the auditory cortex to those parts of the brain that deal with processing emotions." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**Brain Wave Music – Neural Oscillations Can Be Transposed Into Audible Music; Used to Treat Depression and Anxiety** : Brain activity produces electrical neural oscillations (brain waves): delta, theta, alpha, and beta waves at very low frequencies. Scientists discovered that by transposing these waves up into audible frequency ranges, the resulting sounds can be used therapeutically. Doctors have found that playing a patient's own brain waves back to them appears to reboot brain chemistry in ways that treat insomnia, anxiety, and depression. Classically trained cellist Katinka Kleijn demonstrated the technique by playing her own EEG brain waves live alongside her cello. "The music of your own brain can be used to treat certain neurological and psychological conditions." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NMwMwqMQpf4
**Brain's Ability to Correctly Identify Reversed Music** : An experiment demonstrated that even when presented with a half-second clip of a song played in reverse, the brain's extremely fast processing of musical memories allowed for correct identification of the song. This illustrates the remarkable efficiency and robustness of our memory system for music. "Yet your brain would have none of that. It worked so fast with your musical memories that it was able to correctly identify the song even though it was hearing it wrong." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**Brain's Effort to Process Compressed MP3s (Theory 2)** : Another theory posits that when the brain receives an MP3, it subconsciously registers that something is missing, even if the ear can't consciously detect the stripped-out audio. The brain then attempts to fill in these blanks, either by accessing analog memories or by finding nothing, which takes extra milliseconds before the music reaches consciousness. This additional processing effort disrupts connections with emotional centers, stunting dopamine production and leading to a diminished sense of enjoyment compared to uncompressed or analog music. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHN6db7m3DM
**Brain's Musical Hardwiring** : The human brain, a three-pound organ containing billions of neurons, appears to be naturally hardwired for music, possessing special areas exclusively devoted to processing musical information. Possible evolutionary theories suggest this might be a result of ancient ancestors imitating bird song, a development related to language, a tool for storytelling (as details are easier to remember with music), or a phenomenon that evolved alongside religious rituals and chants. "There is no obvious biological reason for it, but our brain seemed to be hardwired for music. There were special areas of the brain devoted to just dealing with music and nothing else." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**Branches of Surf Music** : The surf music phenomenon developed into distinct branches, primarily original guitar-heavy surf music and vocal surf (e.g., The Beach Boys, Jan and Dean). A subset known as Hot Rod Rock also emerged, shifting the focus from the beach to the street. "Here's a split. There was the original surf music that was heavy on the guitar. And then we have Vocal Surf, which is where we find the Beach Boys, Jan and Dean, the RIP Chords and the Riviera." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pOeChe_D1Ag
**Breakdown of Music Silos** : The rigid "us versus them" tribalism that characterized rock music for decades, where fans were either alternative or mainstream, has diminished. Today, individuals can enjoy diverse music genres without stigma, exemplified by liking both The Beatles and a Norwegian death metal band. "all the silos have broken down. Johnny Rotten walked into Malcolm McLaren's clothing shop in 1975. He was immediately considered for the job of lead singer of the sex pistols, based on the fact that he was wearing an eye-hate pink Floyd t-shirt." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Breakdown of Traditional Cultural Gatekeepers** : In earlier eras, record labels, radio stations, and record stores acted as powerful cultural filters, influencing what music was considered good or widely available. The internet, particularly through file sharing, liberated music listeners from these traditional gatekeepers, allowing thousands of indie bands to be discovered and strengthening the position of indie artists and labels. "The internet had delivered them from the traditional cultural gatekeepers and it made it possible for thousands upon thousands upon thousands of indie bands to be discovered." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=raRCZ0Y5up8
**Breast Cancer Awareness Group** : Various breast cancer awareness groups received support from the philanthropic efforts of the Warped Tour. "There's a breast cancer awareness group." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jfQaUV6Bq4
**Breene Language in Star Trek** : The alien species known as the Breene in the "Star Trek: The Next Generation" television series has a language that is based on the sounds from Lou Reed's "Metal Machine Music." This unusual influence highlights the unexpected cultural impact and legacy of Reed's experimental album. "If you're into Star Trek, specifically the next generation series, you might remember an alien species called the Breene. Their language in the TV show is based on middle machine music sounds." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iGav9uJwA_w
**Brenda Spencer's Background and Attack** : On January 29, 1979, 16-year-old Brenda Spencer, living in poverty with her divorced father in San Diego, opened fire on Cleveland Elementary School, located across the street from her home. Despite being a good photographer, she disliked school and had been sent to a special facility for problematic kids, reporting suicidal and depressed tendencies. An earlier bike accident had resulted in a head injury, and people later learned she had a temporal lobe problem affecting emotion. For Christmas 1978, her father bought her a Ruger semi-automatic rifle with a telescopic sight and 500 rounds of ammunition, despite her asking for a simple radio. She killed Principal Burton Wag and a custodian, and injured eight children. "Brenda Spencer was a 16-year-old girl living with her divorce dad in San Diego." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4PlieAEthSg
**Brian Cannon – Oasis Art Director** : Brian Cannon designed the artwork for Oasis's debut album "Definitely Maybe" and continued his role for the band's subsequent four years of releases. For the "Definitely Maybe" cover, Cannon drew specific inspiration from Jan Van Eyck's 15th-century painting, "The Arnolfini Portrait," for its sophisticated use of visual metaphors. "Brian Cannon designed the artwork." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Brian May Uses Banjo Strings on Guitar – Creates Signature Queen Sound** : Brian May uses banjo strings on his guitars instead of standard guitar strings because they are lighter, thinner, and bend more easily. This technique is credited with producing Queen's signature guitar tone. "They're lighter and thinner and thus can bend much easier and that's how we got the signature Queen sound." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**Brisbane Punk Scene Emergence (Late 1973/1974)** : Simultaneously with developments in New York and Cleveland, something "weird" was happening in Brisbane, Australia. Bands like Kid Galahad and the Eternals (who became The Saints), influenced by The Stooges and The Velvet Underground, developed a distinct, fast-paced "garage band" sound by late 1973 or 1974. Their 1976 single "(I'm) Stranded" caused a sensation in the UK, impacting the global punk rock movement. "Meanwhile though, something weird was also happening on the other side of the planet in Brisbane, Australia." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VYi3r0G-ne4
**Brit Pop as a "War" (August 1995)** : In August 1995, the Brit Pop scene was characterized by an intense "record war" that pitted its "two biggest stars," Oasis and Blur, against each other. This rivalry transcended typical competition, becoming a national obsession that was extensively covered by all music papers, magazines, and daily newspapers, with bookmakers even accepting bets on which band's single would claim the number one spot. "This was much more than a rivalry. This was a war." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8UIBWyUt38g
**British Art School's Role in Music** : English art schools play a unique and significant role in British music, acting as breeding grounds for new musicians. Students can enroll as young as 16, and these institutions prioritize creativity over the discipline emphasized in universities. This British phenomenon has produced bands ranging from the Sex Pistols (whose first gig was at an art school) to Blur and Franz Ferdinand. "This is where you could take courses in painting and photography and sculpture and so on. And you can get into an English art school as soon as you turn 16." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gKrAXMG8sTc
**British Commonwealth Immigration (1960s)** : Facing a labor shortage, Britain made it easier for citizens of the Commonwealth, including many Jamaicans, to immigrate to the home country. These immigrants brought their music, particularly Ska, with them, contributing to its spread and popularity in Britain. "Britain was suffering a labor shortage, and decided to make it easier for citizens of the Commonwealth to immigrate to the home country." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**British Gas - Parts Department** : Before forming Oasis, Noel Gallagher worked as a "swaggering employee in the parts department of British gas" after his time as a roadie for Inspiral Carpets. "now a swaggering employee in the parts department of British gas, made his move." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DSvoppf1dGY
**British Isles' Geographic Impact on Music** : The unique geography of the British Isles, as the only English-speaking territory for thousands of miles with 60 million people in a small space, fostered rapid musical innovation. This concentrated environment allowed ideas, trends, and sounds to move very quickly, with national exposure from BBC Radio and TV instantly reaching millions. "Much of this innovation was made possible by simple geography." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zB5zxycrbnY
**British Music Media Influence** : In the early 1980s, the British music media was exceptionally powerful and influential, capable of shaping music trends and chart performance. This was evident when they declared technopop dead and goth the new sound, immediately resulting in goth artists populating the charts. "And because the British music media was so powerful and influential back then, the charts immediately began to fill with songs featuring people dressed in black and wearing mascara, and deathly white makeup and big teased black hair, often carefully crimped." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XaLsZFLrMnI
**British Music Press - Hype and Guardianship** : The British music press, described as "self-appointed guardians of rock and roll culture," played a pivotal role in hyping new musical movements, from Madchester to grunge and Britpop, often through cover stories and strong opinions. "That's when the British music press, those self-appointed guardians of rock and roll culture in the UK, decided to hype what was happening in other cities." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DSvoppf1dGY
**British Music Press Desperation** : The British music press, with "dozens of these publications, some putting out issues every week," was desperate for new sounds and scenes to cover following the decline of previous movements, making them eager to embrace new homegrown indie bands.
