"This made the British music press desperate." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5q5EnZj1FwE
**British Music Press Embracing Britpop** : Key British music publications played a significant role in promoting the new wave of homegrown indie bands that would become Britpop. Monthly magazines like *Select* and *The Face*, along with weekly papers *NME* and *Melody Maker*, dedicated extensive coverage to the emerging scene. "There was a new monthly magazine called Select, which began to devote many, many pages to this new generation of homegrown indie bands. Another magazine called The Face began to do the same. And the weekly papers, the NME and Melodymaker, some thirtieth into what appeared to be a new and very viable local music scene." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vzyMSSlD7Yg
**British Music Press Philosophy** : The British music press operated on a philosophy of discovering new music and scenes, building up bands with hype and exposure, and then abandoning them for the "next shiny thing." This rapid cycle was driven by intense competition among publications, leading to a "massive churn in sounds and trends." "these publications believe that it was their duty to discover new music, to discover new scenes, build up the bands to hype an exposure and then abandon them for the next shiny thing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zB5zxycrbnY
**British Music Press Reception of US Grunge** : Initially, the British music press was "only too happy to welcome their new alien overlords" (US grunge/alt-rock), needing content to write about. "The British music press was only too happy to welcome their new alien overlords." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vzyMSSlD7Yg
**British Music Press's Role** : The British music press, including weekly magazines and tabloids like NME, Sounds, and Melody Maker, was crucial for promoting new music. These publications constantly competed to cover the latest trends, break scoops, and discover new artists, contributing to a rapid churn of sounds. "All these magazines and tabloids, not just coming up monthly, but weekly. Each of them covering the latest in the coolest and each trying to outdo each other with scoops and discoveries." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zB5zxycrbnY
**British Music Rivalries - Historical Context** : British music has a history of rivalries, including The Beatles vs. The Stones, Sex Pistols vs. The Clash, and The Stone Roses vs. Happy Mondays. While most were "friendly sorts of battles," the Blur vs. Oasis conflict was particularly "vicious." "English music has always had its rivalries, Beatles versus the Stones, Sex Pistols versus the Clash, Stone Roses versus the Happy Mondays, but those were friendly sorts of battles." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DSvoppf1dGY
**British Music Scene - Warp Speed** : The British music scene has historically operated at "warp speed," with songs, bands, and sounds rapidly appearing and disappearing, even before the internet era. This phenomenon is attributed to the densely populated island, a robust national broadcasting network, and hyper-competitive music media. "The British music scene has always operated at warp speed. Songs and bands and sounds have always come and gone very quickly, even before the age of the internet." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DSvoppf1dGY
**British Music Tabloids' Vital Role** : In an era where the British public primarily relied on the state-run BBC for music, and the BBC played limited new releases, weekly music tabloids like the NME, along with three other fiercely competing publications, became indispensable. These papers served as the primary conduits for discovering and breaking new music, with intense competition to identify and champion the "next big thing." "At the time, there were four music tabloids, and because the British public was stuck mainly with the state BBC, there wasn't a lot of new music played on the radio." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jhgZrwtYE2g
**British Music Void in Early 1990s** : A vacuum in British music in the early 1990s, caused by the decline of 1980s club/rave culture, the end of the Manchester scene (Stone Roses, Happy Mondays), and the failure of shoegaze to gain mass appeal, allowed American grunge and alt-rock (coinciding with Nirvana's *Nevermind*) to fill the pop culture space in the British Isles. "And you can blame a void in British music. The club and rave culture that had swept the nation in the 1980s had waned. The Manchester scene featuring the stone roses and the happy Mondays was pretty much over. Shougays failed to ignite the masses." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vzyMSSlD7Yg
**British Musicians' Reaction to American Invasion** : A generation of young British musicians felt ignored and rejected by their own country due to the American grunge invasion, which they viewed as coming from a country led by Republican presidents "bent on world domination." This sentiment motivated them to act. "But this American invasion did not sit well with the generation of British musicians. They not only felt ignored by their own country, but rejected." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vzyMSSlD7Yg
**British National Service and its End (1939-1960)** : Between 1939 and 1957, all British men aged 18 to 41 were required to serve in national service, many fighting in the 1940s. The obligation ended in 1957 for those born on or after October 1, 1939, and was abolished entirely in November 1960. This exemption freed thousands of young men to pursue music, leading to a boom in British bands and the British Invasion. "However, that obligation officially came to an end in 1957, and he went born on or after October 1, 1939 was now exempt." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**British Newspaper's Racist Review (1956)** : A British newspaper review from 1956 launched a deeply racist attack on rock, calling it "deplorable" and "tribal," asserting it was "from America." The review linked rock to a lineage of "Negro" music, including ragtime, blues, jazz, hot cha-cha, and boogie-woogie, which it claimed "surely originated in the jungle," and questioned whether this was "the Negro's revenge." "It's deplorable. It is tribal and it is from America. It follows ragtime, blues, jazz, hot cha-cha, and the boogie-woogie, which surely originated in the jungle. We sometimes wonder whether this is the Negro's revenge." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**British Postal Stamps** : David Bowie has been featured on British postal stamps, cementing his status as a cultural icon in his home country. "He's been on British postal stamps." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yq2A_o9-ulM
**British Press Reaction to Rock and Roll** : The British press widely condemned rock and roll in response to the disturbances associated with *Blackboard Jungle* in 1956. They accused the new music of "corrupting the nation's youth," reflecting a moral panic about its influence on post-war generations. "The British press jumped all over the story with the position that this new rock and roll music was corrupting the nation's youth." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**British Psychologist Simon Manchip's Interpretation (Post-1994)** : British psychologist Simon Manchip stated that Kurt Cobain's suicide note, particularly the phrases "I feel guilty beyond words" and "I don't feel the passion anymore," indicated mental illness. Manchip advocated for media to focus more on Cobain's depression rather than sensationalizing his death alongside other rock stars like Jimi Hendrix and Jim Morrison. "British psychologist Simon Manchip says Cobain's suicide note, which reads, I feel guilty beyond words. I don't feel the passion anymore. Indicates mental illness." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sKWgbxk46eM
**British Radio vs. Press (Pre-1970s)** : Before the 1970s, Britain lacked legal, private, commercial radio because the government deemed radio broadcasting too important for private citizens. Consequently, despite a thriving 1960s music scene with bands like The Beatles and The Rolling Stones, it was nearly impossible to hear these British bands on British radio, a gap filled by the printed music media. "Britain didn't get legal, private, commercial radio until the 1970s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zB5zxycrbnY
**Britpop Bands (Mid-1990s)** : In the mid-1990s, as Britpop gained momentum, a multitude of bands joined the scene, including Shed Seven, Echobelly, Dodgy, Gene, Supergrass, Menswear, Cast, Ocean Colour Scene, Sleeper, Salad, Whiteouts, Marion, Northern Uproar, Ash, These Animal Men, and Black Grape. "We had Shed 7, Echo Belly, Dodgy, Jean, Supergrass, Men's Wearer that was cast in Ocean Color scene and sleeper, Salad, Whiteouts, Marion, Northern Uproar, Ash, these animal men, and Black Rape." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vzyMSSlD7Yg
**Britpop Co-opted by Establishment (1997)** : Tony Blair's election as Prime Minister and his subsequent invitation of Noel Gallagher to 10 Downing Street had "bad optics" for Britpop, leading to the perception that this "music of the people" had been co-opted by the government and establishment. "All it said was that this music of the people had been co-opted by the establishment and the government." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vzyMSSlD7Yg
**Broca's Area and Musical Expression** : Broca's area, located between the frontal and temporal lobes, is a crucial brain region involved not only in the production of speech but also in the expression of music, such as singing or playing an instrument. It is situated in close proximity to Wernicke's area and the auditory cortex. "This is the spot that not only helps us produce speech, but it's what we use to express music, like playing an instrument or singing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**Broken Social Scene – Kevin Drew's Jokey Gig as "John Tesh Jr. and the Broken Social Scene"** : Kevin Drew had a keyboard setup he likened to soft music king John Tesh. He played a jokey gig under the name "John Tesh Jr. and the Broken Social Scene." The second half of that name stuck for his next project. "The last half of that name stuck." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Bromley Contingent** : The Bromley Contingent was a group of fans, including Siouxsie Sioux, who famously followed the Sex Pistols from gig to gig. "Singer Suzy Su was a member of a group of fans called the Bromley Contention, who followed the sex pistols around from gig to gig." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**Brunel Music Rating Inventory for Workout Music** : The Brunel Music Rating Inventory is a questionnaire developed over 20 years of research to rate the motivational qualities of music for sports and exercise. A key element is matching song tempo to workout intensity, with ideal tempos ranging from 120 beats per minute (for a brisk stroll) to 160 bpm (for a runner), roughly aligning with heart rates. Regular tempos are preferred, as changes in intensity or time signatures can disrupt rhythm. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHN6db7m3DM
**Bryan Adams Concert Cancelled by "Fatberg" (2025, Perth, Australia)** : A Bryan Adams concert in Perth, Australia, in 2025 was cancelled due to a massive fatberg — an enormous accumulation of fat and waste — in nearby sewers that threatened to overflow the venue's bathrooms during the show. "A concert was cancelled due to a 'fatberg' in nearby sewers that threatened to overflow the venue's bathrooms." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0DFmS6MPbFI
**Bryan Cranston Joined Composers Guild to Collect Royalties for Humming on Malcolm in the Middle** : Actor Bryan Cranston joined a composers guild in order to collect royalties for humming and whistling songs in his role on the television series *Malcolm in the Middle*. "Joined a composers guild to receive royalties for humming and whistling songs in his role." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0DFmS6MPbFI
**Buddy Holly Plane Crash Site** : The field near Mason City, Iowa, where Buddy Holly's plane crashed in 1959, is recognized as a significant rock and roll shrine. This site represents a place that people visit due to its historical importance and association with music legend. "The field where Buddy Holly's playing crashed near Mason City, Iowa in 1959." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sKHdTSfDd6E
**Burt Bacharach – Songwriter** : A picture of the esteemed American songwriter Burt Bacharach is prominently positioned on the left side of the "Definitely Maybe" album cover. This was a direct contribution from Noel Gallagher, signifying his deep admiration and love for Bacharach's music. "Towards the front, on the left side is a picture of American songwriter Bert Bacarack. Nol loved Bacarack, so that was his contribution." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Bus Tour Dangers and Accidents** : Life on the road for musicians is fraught with dangers, especially when driving at night, in all kinds of weather, and through various traffic conditions, often while heavy on the gas to make up for lost time. Inevitable mechanical failures further contribute to the risk, leading to situations where "things go really, really wrong and people get hurt," including severe injuries and fatalities for band members, crew, and drivers. "Sometimes things go really, really wrong and people get hurt." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DgN0vBIugLc
**Bush – Suggested by a Music Video Director; Named "Bush X" in Canada Due to Trademark Conflict** : When Gavin Rossdale's band was preparing to release their debut album Sixteen Stone, a music video director suggested "Bush" — short, memorable, graphical, with a mildly suggestive sexual edge. The band was then called Future Primitive. In Canada, however, an existing 1970s band named Bush held the trademark, so Rossdale's group legally traded as "Bush X" in Canada until the conflict was resolved years later. "Until that trademark issue got sorted a few years later, Bush had to legally trade under the name Bush X, but only in Canada." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Butthole Surfers – Accidental Stage Introduction of a Song Title as a Band Name (1984)** : The stage introducer confused the band's name with the title of one of their songs at a 1984 gig. No one can now remember what name they were actually using. Previous names included The Dick Clark Five, Nine Centimeter Worm Makes Its Own Food, The Vodka Family Winstons, Ashtray Babyheads, Eadnzer's Gay, Zip Code Rapists, and several names involving Fred Astaire's anatomy. The Butthole Surfers name stuck because the gig went well, and they eventually had alt-rock radio hits. "Against all odds, they not only survived with that name, but had a couple of alt-rock radio hits." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Buyer Motivations and Artist Legacy in Catalog Sales** : Companies purchasing music catalogs view them as lucrative long-term investments, seeking to exploit and monetize songs correctly over decades. A beneficial side effect for artists is that their music remains in the public consciousness for a significantly longer period, potentially 50 to 75 years longer than it might otherwise. "On the other side, the purchasing companies and their investors see owning song catalogs to be a very lucrative long term proposition if the songs are exploited and monetized correctly." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GYUuTbOvyak
**C86 Cassette Compilation** : In 1986, the NME (New Musical Express) resurrected the idea of offering a cassette with one of its issues, naming it C86 (a play on the year and cassette length labels like C60, C90, C120). This collection featured 22 bands from various UK indie labels, offering power pop and jangly guitar-based songs that reflected the contemporary state of new music in Britain. "They called it C86, a play on the year, 1986, and how cassette lengths were labeled. There was C60, C90, C120. That was based on the number of minutes that could be recorded on both sides." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VOv5mT1dJd8
**C86 as a Shorthand Term** : The C86 cassette proved so successful that its title became a shorthand term to describe a specific sound and attitude within a certain type of music. It showed independent musicians across the UK that they were not alone and that there was significant interest in their work, leading to an explosion of new bands, though most did not achieve widespread success. "It was so successful that C86 became a shorthand term to describe a certain sound and attitude when it came to a certain type of music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VOv5mT1dJd8
**CBC show The Bridge - "I Got Mine" Theme** : The Black Keys' song "I Got Mine" was selected and used as the opening theme for the CBC television series *The Bridge*. "Remember the CBC show The Bridge? That's the opening theme." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PmwfC4HR3_I
**CBGB - Club History and Transformation** : CBGB, originally named "CBGB-UMPFUG" (for Country, Bluegrass, Blues, and Other Music for Uplifting Gourmandizers) by owner Hilly Crystal, opened in the winter of 1973. Despite its location in the notoriously bad Bowery area of New York City, surrounded by flop houses, drug dens, and ex-convicts, it transformed from a slow Sunday night venue to the epicenter of a new rock scene after Television's debut on March 31, 1974. "The owner of the club was a 42 year old ex-marine named Hilly Crystal. And when he bought the club, he named it after his music policy, the kinds of acts he would book, country, blue grass and blues and other music for uplifting gourmandizers." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VYi3r0G-ne4
**CBGB - Hilly Crystal's Rock-Only Policy (1974)** : After Television's successful residency at CBGB began in March 1974, attracting crowds displaced from the Mercer Arts Center, owner Hilly Crystal decided to institute a "rock-only policy" within a few months, cementing the club's role as a rock venue. "Within a few months of television's debut, Hilly decided to institute a rock-only policy." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VYi3r0G-ne4
**CBGB - Location and Atmosphere (1973)** : In winter 1973, CBGB was located in the Bowery, one of New York City's worst neighborhoods. The area was notorious for flop houses, halfway houses, drug dens filled with Vietnam vets and heroin junkies, ex-convicts, and mentally ill people. The club itself smelled of "stale wine and beer, urine, feces," and had "legendary bathrooms" with terrible plumbing, prompting patrons to relieve themselves elsewhere. "But for whatever reason, Hilly Crystal decided that he'd go ahead anyway." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VYi3r0G-ne4
**CBGB's Full Name and "Gorman Dising"** : The famous punk bar CBGB in New York has a full and proper name: "CBGB on Fug," which stands for "Country, Bluegrass, Blues, and other music for uplifting Gorman Dizers." This term "Gorman Dising" was speculated as a possible anagrammatic origin for the band name "Imagine Dragons," although the band has never confirmed it. "The full and proper name of that club is CBGB on Fug. And that honestly stands for Country, Bluegrass, Blues, and other music for uplifting Gorman Dizers." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GmYlTwI_TQQ
**CBGB's Genesis (March 1974)** : Originally a "crappy, scuzzy country, bluegrass and blues club," CBGB began allowing newer rock-oriented bands to play in March 1974, with bands earning door money while the bar kept alcohol sales. Its unique atmosphere, long and narrow room, sparse stage, and lack of dressing rooms, combined with cheap rents in the Bowery, attracted a community of disaffected, left-wing, artsy, anti-establishment artists. "This was the situation. When a crappy, scuzzy country, bluegrass and blues club called CBGB started allowing some newer rock-oriented bands to play." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**CD Adoption Accelerated by Vinyl Quality Decline** : While superior audio technology like the CD would eventually prevail, the rapid adoption of compact discs starting in late 1982 was significantly accelerated by the severe decline in vinyl quality throughout the 1970s and early 1980s. The poor manufacturing standards of vinyl records created a strong consumer demand for a higher-fidelity alternative. "But you have to wonder if we would have got the compact disc as soon as we did, and if we would have all adopted them so quickly, had vinyl quality been up to snuff through the 1970s and early 80s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NAroxFbYkRw
**CD Audio Specifications: Sampling Frequency and Resolution** : The compact disc standard set the sampling frequency at 44.1 kHz, meaning the recording technology captured real-world analog audio 44,100 times per second. The resolution was established at 16 bits, which aligned with the data storage capabilities of computers during that era. "It was decided that the sampling frequency would be 44.1 kHz. This means the recording technology grabbed real-world audio and a log audio. 44,100 times a second. Resolution was set at 16 bits, which is related to how the computers of the day store data." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**CD Industry Push (Late 1981)** : By late 1981, record labels, in dire financial straits due to the recession, were "brow beaten" into accepting the compact disc as vinyl's successor, leading to extensive consumer marketing campaigns. "But by late 1981, the labels were in such bad shape financially that they had all been brow beaten into accepting the compact disc as the successor to vinyl." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**CD Malware Controversy and Lawsuits** : Record labels engaged in misguided attempts to deter copying by embedding malware, specifically a rootkit, onto some CDs. If a user tried to rip such a CD, the rootkit would install itself on their machine, preventing copying. This unethical practice led to a series of lawsuits filed by music consumers. "And then there were misguided attempts of putting malware on CDs. If you tried to riff this CD from a major record label, something called a root kit would install itself on your machine, preventing you from copying anything. And that ended really, really badly with a bunch of lawsuits being filed by and on behalf of music consumers." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**CD Marketing: "Perfect Digital Sound Forever"** : Consumers were bombarded with marketing campaigns that promised "perfect digital sound forever" for CDs, contrasting with the poor quality of late 70s/early 80s vinyl. "And this is when we, the consumers, began to get bombarded with campaigns that promised perfect digital sound forever." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**CD Playback Direction and Speed Changes** : Unlike vinyl records, which play from the outside in, compact discs are recorded in a spiral and play from the inside out. Digital speeds change during playback; Track 1 plays at 480 RPM, and as the laser moves towards the outer edge, the rotational speed gradually slows to approximately 210 RPM. "Information is recorded in a spiral, just like a vinyl record, except that CDs play from the inside out. Digital speeds changed as the disk played. Track 1 plays at 480 RPM. As the laser moves towards the outer edge, rotational speeds slows to about 210 RPM." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**CD Player Features and Revolutionary Skip Button** : The first CD players, such as Sony's CDP-101 (released late 1982), offered instant track skipping, a feature that was considered revolutionary at the time. This allowed users to jump directly to any song on the disk without the need for fast-forwarding or rewinding, a significant improvement over previous formats. `"You could instantly skip a song, no more fast forward or rewind, instant access to any track on the disk."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**CD Player Skip Button's Impact** : The "skip button" on CD players profoundly affected listener attention spans and tolerance levels for music. It accustomed listeners to quickly bypass disliked tracks, influencing how entire albums are consumed. "Think about what the skip button on CD players did for our attention spans and tolerances when it came to music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**CD Players' Impact on Album Integrity** : The advent of CD players and their skip functionality marked the initial erosion of the album's integrity, enabling listeners to bypass disliked songs or even reorder tracks, rather than experiencing the album as a continuous artistic statement. "This was the first dent in the wall for the integrity of the album." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**CD Ripping** : The advent of MP3 technology and the subsequent release of its source code by a hacker led to a surge in "CD ripping." This allowed individuals to easily convert physical CDs into digital files, which could then be shared freely over the internet, further disrupting the traditional music industry. "Things could even worse when a hacker found the source code. He created an even better version, set it free on the internet, and well, CD ripping went wild." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**CD Ripping and MP3 Sharing** : The combination of CD-ROM drives in personal computers and MP3 compression software enabled users to "rip" digital music files from compact discs, converting them into smaller, more portable MP3s, which computer-literate music fans then began sharing online, often via platforms like IRC. "It wasn't long before software appeared that allowed anyone to take a compact disk, place it into a CD-ROM drive and a typical personal computer, and ripped those digital music files they contained to a much smaller and much more portable size, the MP3." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**CD Sales Death Spiral (2000-2018)** : Following its peak in 2000, CD sales entered a "death spiral." Global sales plummeted from $25 billion in 2000 to approximately $5 billion in 2018, representing a 90% drop from its all-time highs. "Global sales went from 25 billion in 2000 to around 5 billion in 2018. That drop was 90% from its all-time highs, 90%." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**CD Sales Decline (2000-2009)** : Global CD sales plummeted from a peak of $26 billion USD in 2000 to approximately $9 billion in 2009, with further declines anticipated, indicating a significant crisis for the recorded music industry. "Global CD sales had dropped from a high of $26 billion US dollars in 2000 to around 9 billion in 2009 with no bottom in sight." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**CD Sales Peak (2000)** : The peak of compact disc sales occurred in 2000, with 943 million units sold in the United States alone and a global total of 2.44 billion units. "The high watermark of the CD came in 2000 when 943 million were sold just in the United States. Worldwide, 2.44 billion." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**CD Sales in Freefall and Record Stores Closing (2000s)** : By 2008, the recorded music industry had drastically changed due to music piracy, with CD sales in "freefall for the entire decade" and record stores closing down. This financial downturn significantly impacted budgets for marketing and promotion, including music videos. "CD sales had been in freefall for the entire decade with no signs of slowing. Record stores were closing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**CD Shoplifting Problem and Longbox Solution** : The compact size of CDs made them highly susceptible to shoplifting, a concern for retailers. This problem was addressed through the introduction of the "Longbox," a paper or plastic package measuring approximately 6 inches wide and 12 inches long. Its larger dimensions prevented easy concealment and allowed it to be displayed in existing 12-inch vinyl record bins. "Another reason retailers were skeptical had to do with the CD size. They were so small that they were very easily shoplifted. Those problems were solved by a special sort of packaging called the Longbox." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**CD Single Phasing Out in North America** : The phasing out of the CD single in North America contributed to fan frustration. It meant that to acquire a single track, even from a one-hit wonder, consumers often had to purchase an entire album costing $18 or more, despite promises of falling CD prices. "the CD single was being phased out of North America. That meant that even if you did like a song put up by one of those one-hit wonders, you had to pony up 18 bucks or more just to get that one track." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**CD Singles (Mid-1980s)** : CD singles emerged in the mid-1980s as CDs gained popularity, functioning similarly to 7-inch vinyl singles as counterparts to full albums. They could hold significantly more music than vinyl singles and came in two sizes: the standard 5-inch (CD5) and a smaller 3-inch version that often required an adapter and largely disappeared by the mid-1990s. "CD singles came in two sizes. The most common was the same size as a standard compact disc and was sometimes called a CD 5 because they're 5 inches in diameter." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**CD Singles as Source for Demos** : Although less prominent in North America, CD singles were historically used to entice fans to purchase them by including bonus tracks not available elsewhere. This strategy was particularly mastered by British bands like Oasis and Blur, providing another avenue for fans to acquire unique recordings. "But one of the many ways fans could be persuaded to buy a CD single version of a song was by tempting them with some bonus tracks that aren't available anywhere else." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zT6pRGYLWhw
**CD's Lack of Emotional Cachet Compared to Vinyl** : Compact discs, unlike vinyl records, are noted for not possessing the same "emotional cache." There is a current trend indicating that vinyl may once again outsell CDs for the first time since the mid-1980s. "CDs don't have the same emotional cache as vinyl. We're heading toward a time when vinyl may just outsell CDs again for the first time since the middle 1980s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**CD's Potential for Harshness** : While properly constructed CD recordings avoid vinyl's noise artifacts, poorly mastered CDs can suffer from harshness or a high-frequency shrillness. This issue often arises when master tapes originally prepared for vinyl are directly transferred to CD without specific remastering. "However, CDs can have an issue with harshness, a high frequency shrillness that can be really off-putting." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**CDI Discs and Anti-Ripping Features** : CDI (Compact Disc Interactive) discs, like The Cranberries' "Doors and Windows," combined music with multimedia content. While playable in both computers and CD players, some had features that prevented ripping to a computer, and Track 1 on the Crenbury's disc could produce ear-piercing static if played in a regular CD player without caution. "This is a CDI disc, meaning that it contains all sorts of multimedia content as well as music. This one could be played in a computer and a CD player. But you need to turn down your CD player for Track 1 because it just might read it as ear piercing static. Want to rip it to your computer? Uh-uh, wouldn't let you." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**CDs Outselling Vinyl (1988)** : Compact discs surpassed vinyl records in sales by 1988, indicating a significant shift in consumer preference and market dominance for the new digital format. "Then the bottom dropped out of the vinyl market with CDs outselling the old format by 1988." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**CDs Overtaking Cassettes (1991)** : In 1991, the compact disc overtook the cassette as the biggest-selling format for pre-recorded music. The cassette had previously held this position for a period, but the CD's rapid growth led to its new market leadership. "In 1991, the cassette, which for a while, had been the biggest selling format for pre-recorded music, was overtaken by the CD." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**CERN's Large Hadron Collider** : Brian Cox, a former keyboardist for D:Ream, went on to work as a physicist at CERN's Large Hadron Collider in Switzerland after pursuing his doctorate in philosophy and high-energy particle physics. "he's been a full on physicist, even working at CERN's Large Hadron Collider in Switzerland." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jMfH9xAOpI
**CFNY Film Documentary** : A documentary film covering "the spirit of radio CFNY years," spanning from 1977 to 1992, is currently in its final editing stages. The production involved extensive interviews in locations such as London, Glasgow, Manchester, Liverpool (including with Peter Hook), Abbey Road Studios, Vancouver, San Francisco, Los Angeles, and Las Vegas. The documentary aims to explore how the radio station introduced music to the masses and influenced the growth of alternative music in North America. "We are doing a documentary, a film documentary on the spirit of radio CF and Y years. So that would be 1977 to 1992 and we're in the final editing stages right now." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4xxgPX-aR90
**CIA Wind of Change Operation Theory** : A theory suggests that the metal power ballad "Wind of Change" by the Scorpions was a product of a CIA operation. The alleged goal was to destabilize Soviet society with its message of change and revolution, contributing to the fall of the Soviet Union by the end of 1991. This theory postulates that the CIA commissioned the song, arranged for the Scorpions to record it, and facilitated its success in the Eastern Bloc. "For years rumors swirled about this song. It is said that it was the product of a CIA operation designed to destabilize Soviet society with its message of change and revolution." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YeNWDtN_sHE
**COVID-19 Impact on Record Stores** : The COVID-19 pandemic led to the closure of "a lot of record stores," affecting both those selling new music and those trading in used vinyl, adding to the challenges faced by the vinyl industry. "COVID-19. That killed off a lot of record stores, both those that traded in new music and those that sold used vinyl." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**COVID-19 Pandemic as Creative Catalyst (2020)** : The pandemic, by limiting other activities, provided an environment where the Red Hot Chili Peppers could focus intensely on songwriting, leading to over a hundred new songs for "Unlimited Love." "They wrote constantly from January to October 2020 as COVID raged all around them. As Anthony said, with the pandemic, there was nothing to do but write songs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CPxy_KOv87o
**COVID-19 Pandemic's Acceleration of Digital Music Transition** : The COVID-19 pandemic, causing a two-year halt in live music (concerts, festivals), severely impacted artists and venues. It forced a rapid acceleration of the music industry's digital transition, leading to a surge in virtual concerts, live streams, and the explosion of TikTok for music discovery. Revenue shifted to crowdfunding, merchandise, and platforms like Patreon, while streaming services like Spotify, Apple Music, and YouTube saw massive user spikes. While some artists created "COVID music" about isolation and resilience, widespread home production using similar software led to a "flattening of music." Post-pandemic, ticket prices surged due to inflation, and logistical issues like tour bus shortages emerged. Despite its devastation, COVID-19 significantly propelled the industry and fan consumption deeper into the digital realm. "COVID-19 was unspeakably awful. But it did accelerate the music industry's transition into the digital realm, and the effects will be felt not only on the industry, but also on music fans." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nX8uAFDzDYM
**COVID-19's Influence on Creative Process** : The COVID-19 pandemic, despite its challenges, unexpectedly sparked new creativity for bands like 5440. Forced to connect remotely via Zoom calls, band members shared historical stories and anecdotes, which directly became the "impetus" for writing new songs and forming the basis of an upcoming album. "Brad came up with the idea of, you know, doing a Zoom call... this became the impetus, which is for the record that's going to come out next, because we just started telling stories and, you know, fun and funny stuff about our history." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=W9gdKha0PLg
**Cage the Elephant – Multiple Conflicting Stories; Band Won't Tell** : The band offers conflicting origin stories — a mentally deranged man yelling "you gotta cage the elephant" at them, a fortune teller turning over a card and screaming it. They've said, "We'll never tell." "There's a bunch of others, the band's coy." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Cake – Not About Pastry; About Something That Sticks to Your Shoe** : Formed in Sacramento in 1991, Cake chose their name to convey something that "insidiously becomes part of your life" — the way something cakes onto the bottom of your shoe. "Cake onto your shoe, and it's just sort of there until you get rid of it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Calabasas Celebrity Enclave** : Calabasas, California, is described as a "gated enormous" community and celebrity hub, where figures like Will Smith, Justin Bieber, and Travis Barker reside. John Mayer's old mansion in this area served as a studio for USS to write songs like "Who's With Me." "That's what we'll Smith lives that's where Justin Bieber lives that's where Travis Barker lives." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=N-3eddy4J7g
**Call for Rock and Roll Regulation** : A newspaper columnist in the mid-1950s argued that if rock and roll caused problems comparable to alcohol, it should be "controlled and regulated in exactly the same way." This illustrates the severity of the perceived threat and calls for governmental intervention against the new music genre. "And a newspaper columnist said that if rock and roll was going to cause the same sorts of problems as alcohol, then it should be controlled and regulated in exactly the same way." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Callie – Cranberries Art Director** : Callie was the art director hired for The Cranberries' 1993 debut album, "Everybody Else Is Doing It, So Why Can't We?" Her role was instrumental in establishing the band's early visual aesthetic, including actively seeking out and reusing the same couch for their subsequent album, "No Need to Argue," and its reappearance in 2017. "An art director called Callie was hired." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Camden Pub Gatherings** : In the winter of 1993-94, like-minded individuals, likely those involved in the burgeoning Britpop scene, began to gather in places like The Good Mixer pub in Camden and a regular Thursday night event at a venue called Syndrome on Oxford Street. "Like-minded people began to gather in places like The Good Mixer, which is a pub in Camden." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5q5EnZj1FwE
**Campaigns of Record Burning (1950s)** : In the 1950s, widespread protests occurred at radio stations, coupled with demands to ban "race music" (R&B) and rock records. These efforts culminated in public events where R&B and rock records were physically melted in large bonfires, symbolizing the intense cultural and religious opposition to these emerging musical forms. "There were protests at radio stations, demands to have this music band, and plenty of events where R&B and rock records were melted in huge bonfires." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=41eKVp8JIhU
**Can You Plagiarize Yourself?** : It is bizarre but true that a songwriter can be sued for plagiarizing themselves, and can even lose the case, especially if the copyrights to their earlier work are owned by someone else. "A couple of minutes ago, I posed this question. Can a songwriter be sued for ripping off himself? The answer is yes he can." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wYD_dIvYY5o
**CanCon Controversies and Edge Cases – Dallas Green + Pink, Pop Evil, Arcade Fire** : Dallas Green of Alexis on Fire and City and Colour collaborated with Pink on a 2014 album called "You and Me." Because files were exchanged between home studios across countries, a CRTC ruling was needed. The album received CanCon certification because the CRTC ruled Green's contribution as a lead artist was sufficient for a full "A" — but this came retroactively, after the album was already released. Pop Evil, a Michigan band with a Canadian founder (Lee Kelly), also required a CRTC ruling on whether their songs qualified. Arcade Fire has American-born members but qualifies as a Canadian act because they are landed immigrants. "You do not want to screw up your cancon numbers." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-JWO_jYXwEM
**CanCon Raised to 35% on January 3, 1999; Most New Stations Promise 40%** : After 20+ years of development, the Canadian music scene was strong enough that when quotas were raised from 30 to 35% on January 3, 1999, "almost nobody noticed." Since 2000, most new radio station applications have voluntarily promised a 40% CanCon quota — evidence that programmers believe such a ratio works with audiences. Canada is the fifth or sixth largest music market in the world, regularly competing with Australia for that ranking, always behind the US, Japan, UK, Germany, and France. "One of us is the US, Japan, the UK, Germany and France, all of which have larger populations than us." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-JWO_jYXwEM
**CanCon's Industrial Impact – Labels, Recording Studios, and Infrastructure Created by Mandate** : By creating artificial demand for Canadian music, the CanCon rules required the creation of a domestic music industry infrastructure: recording studios, producers, engineers, promoters, booking agents, and record labels. Within a year of the rules taking effect, new Canadian labels appeared including GRT, Attic, Stony Plain, Aquarius, Nimbus 9, Axe, and Daffodil. Initially, Canadian records were often technically and artistically inferior; stations created so-called "Beaver Hours," playing heavily edited Canadian songs between 11 p.m. and midnight to meet quotas in one concentrated block. The CRTC closed that loophole. "For a while, Canadian meant bad." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-JWO_jYXwEM
**Canada's Reaction to Gord's Diagnosis** : The news of Gord Downie's incurable brain tumor, announced on May 24, 2016, had a profound impact across Canada, becoming the lead story everywhere and hitting the nation "like a bomb." "This news hit Canada like a bomb. Lead story everywhere." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Um_KEEcCeT4
**Canada's Record Production Output** : Canada represents approximately 2% of the global market for recorded music but significantly over-contributes to the production of that music, indicating a disproportionate number of world-class record producers from the country. "Canada represents about 2% of the world's market for recorded music. But when it comes to making this recorded music, we do way more than our fair share." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkSKxm_U9Yc
**Canadian Broadcast Standards Council (CBSC)** : This council became involved when an unedited version of The Headstones' song "Cubically Contained," containing clear profanity, was broadcast on radio at 5:30 PM. The CBSC received complaints from listeners, particularly those concerned about children, highlighting its role in overseeing broadcast content and standards. "Then there's something called the Canadian Brock East Anders Council. And of course a bunch of people were listing very concerned, you know, what about the children?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=y7kmkWJ54ww
**Canadian Content (Cancon) Rules** : This federal law in Canada mandates that all radio stations allocate 35% of their music airtime to Canadian artists. The primary objective is to foster the development of Canadian performers and ensure they receive adequate airtime within their own country, thereby preventing an overwhelming influx of foreign music. In Quebec, an additional language component is included in this law. "This is a federal law that requires all radio stations to devote 35% of the music they play to Canadian artists." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dMsEj62KFEw
**Canadian Content (Cancon) Rules Adoption (1971)** : As of January 18, 1971, Canadian radio stations were legally required to devote 30% of their music programming to Canadian artists. This mandate, driven by rising national pride after Canada's centennial, was both cultural protectionism and an industrial strategy, creating demand that spurred the development of a domestic music infrastructure. "As of January 18, 1971, all radio stations were required by law to devote 30% of the music they played to records by Canadian artists." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6PGgNCaW4xo
**Canadian Content Laws (January 1971)** : Introduced in January 1971, these laws mandated that Canadian radio stations allocate 30% of their playlists to Canadian artists. This created an artificial demand for domestic music, which, despite initial opposition, stimulated the growth of a modern Canadian music industry with expanded infrastructure, allowing Canadian artists to build successful careers within the country. "But then came the Canadian content laws in January 1971, and overnight it became law that Canadian radio stations had to devote 30% of their playlists to Canadian artists." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=P0pOgz3ZobE
**Canadian Content Regulations (CanCon) – January 18, 1971; 30% Canadian Music Required on AM Radio** : On January 18, 1971, the Canadian Radio Television and Communications Commission (CRTC) mandated that 30% of all music heard on Canadian AM radio stations had to be of Canadian origin. The regulation was developed largely through lobbying by Stan Klees and Walt Grealis, co-founders of RPM Magazine (established Toronto, 1964), Canada's first domestic music industry trade publication tracking radio and charts. Grealis also conceived the Gold Leaf Awards in 1970 (renamed the Juno Awards in 1971, named after CRTC head Pierre Juneau), which began as an annual column with a small wine and cheese party for 250 people. The first Juno Awards took place February 22, 1971. "It turned Canada into a global musical powerhouse eventually." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-JWO_jYXwEM
**Canadian Economic Hardship (Mid-Late 1970s)** : In the mid-to-late 1970s, Canada, like other Western nations, experienced significant economic difficulties, including a national debt explosion and rising interest rates. "Like everywhere else in the middle to late 70s, Canada was having a really rough time economically." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Canadian FM Radio Regulations (Decades prior to 1990s)** : For decades, FM radio in Canada was heavily regulated to maintain its distinctiveness from AM radio. Regulations included a ban on playing the same song more than 18 times a week and a limit of 49% of a playlist dedicated to top 40 records. FM stations were also mandated to meet "stringent talk requirements," categorized into surveillance (weather, traffic), mosaic talk (60+ seconds on a specific topic), news in public affairs, and foreground programming (self-contained shows at least 30 minutes long on specific topics). These foreground programming requirements were eventually "ditched sometime in the 1990s." "For decades, FM Radio in Canada was highly, highly regulated." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bI6TTA-fn7c
**Canadian Mourning for Gord Downie (2017)** : Following Gord Downie's death on October 18, 2017, the entire country of Canada entered a period of mourning, succinctly captured by a tweet that stated, "Canada closed, death in the family." "Canada closed, death in the family." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oMBmdfrA47Q
**Canadian Music Industry Maturation (1971-1990s)** : The Canadian music industry underwent a roughly 20-year maturation process, evolving into a world-class entity by the 1990s, at which point Canadian artists were perceived as capable of competing globally. "It took about 20 years for our music industry to mature into something truly world class. And by the time we got to the 1990s, there was this sense that our best could compete with anyone or anything in the world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=P0pOgz3ZobE
**Canadian Music Industry Pre-1971** : Prior to 1971, Canada's music industry was considered a "backwater," lacking the infrastructure of a "first world industry." Major labels maintained only branch offices, and aspiring artists typically had to move to the United States to achieve significant success. "Before 1971, there really wasn't much of a Canadian music industry. Canada was basically a backwater, a place where the big labels had branch offices." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=P0pOgz3ZobE
**Canadian Music Renaissance (Early 1990s)** : The Canadian music scene experienced a renaissance in the early 1990s, with prominent bands like The Tragically Hip, Our Lady Peace, and Sloan emerging during this period. "The Canadian music scene enjoyed a period of renaissance with the rise of the tragically hip, Arlady piece, Sloan, and so many others." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vzyMSSlD7Yg
**Canadian Music Success Post-2000** : Since 2000, Canada has continued to "punch far above its weight" on the world stage, producing global superstars like Justin Bieber, The Weeknd, Drake, and Arcade Fire. Beyond these megastars, many Canadian artists have contributed to an "international coolness factor" that is hard to quantify by traditional record sales metrics. "since 2000 the country has continued to punch far, far above its weight in the world stage, with artists like Justin Bieber, the weekend, Drake and Arcade Fire." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6PGgNCaW4xo
**Canadian Performing Rights Organizations** : Canada has SOCAN (Society of Composers, Authors, and Music Publishers of Canada) which grants licenses for public music performance and distributes fees to songwriters, composers, and music publishers. Additionally, Resound collects "neighboring rights" fees for musicians on a recording and the record label, and the Canadian Musical Reproduction Rights Agency (CMRRA) collects fees for making copies of songs. "The main one in Canada is called So Can, that stands for the Society of Composers, Authors, and Music Publishers of Canada." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Canadian Polymer Coins Can Be Used as a Record Needle to Play Music Very Faintly** : Canadian polymer coins are thin enough that they can function as a stylus on a rotating record player, reproducing music very faintly. "Canadian polymer coins are so thin they can be used as a needle to play music very faintly on a turntable." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qtdetc5-MQo
**Canadian Producers as Cultural Export** : Canadian record producers, known for their ability to make good bands sound great, are considered one of Canada's most significant cultural exports for the foreseeable future. "Bottom line is that for the foreseeable future one of our greatest cultural exports will continue to be the people who know how to make a good band sound great." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkSKxm_U9Yc
**Canadian Radio "F-Bomb" Rule (French Language)** : A Canadian regulatory body has ruled that the use of the "F-bomb" is permissible on Canadian radio, provided it is broadcast in French. The justification is that the word has become very common in the French language, and its use by a French speaker on radio is considered less offensive than if spoken in English. "A regulatory body has ruled it's okay to use the F-bomb on Canadian radio as long as it's broadcast in French." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**Canadian Soundtrack Preservation (1975)** : Despite *The Rocky Horror Picture Show* soundtrack being a commercial disaster and deleted by record companies globally, it remained in print in Canada. Imports of the Canadian release were crucial in keeping *Rocky Horror* alive in the United States, allowing its cult phenomenon to develop through midnight screenings. "Except in Canada, where it stayed in print, and imports of the Canadian release kept Rocky Horror alive in the United States." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**Capital Radio UK (October 16, 1973) – First Private Commercial Radio Station in Britain; Punks Couldn't Get Airplay** : Until October 16, 1973, all legal radio broadcasting in the UK came from the publicly funded BBC. That day, Capital FM signed on at 95.8 FM with 557 AM as the country's first private commercial station. For years Capital played safe mainstream pop. When punk emerged, both the BBC and Capital refused to play it, infuriating The Clash, who wrote a song explicitly about their frustration. "That greatly annoyed the Clash, who couldn't get airplay anywhere." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JvOEAzmC6l0
**Capitol Records Building – Flashing "Hollywood" in Morse Code Since 1956** : The iconic Capitol Records building in Los Angeles is a 13-story circular structure topped with a needle-like flashing light. That light has been blinking out the word "Hollywood" in Morse code continuously since the building opened in 1956. "It has been blinking out Hollywood in Morse code since it opened in 1956." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Car Radio Ban Attempt (1930s) – Massachusetts Registrar Tried to Outlaw In-Car Radios** : The first car radio appeared in 1930 as the Motorola 5T71, a delicate vacuum-tube device that could add as much as $120 to a $600 car. George A. Parker, the registrar of motor vehicles in Massachusetts, campaigned to ban radios in cars on distracted driving grounds, coming close to having his way. The public rose up against him and shut him down; Parker redirected his efforts toward drunk driving. "The good citizens of Massachusetts rose up as one and slathered him down." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iyGUXdDqIlI
**Car Seat Headrest – Singer Will Toledo Rehearsed Vocals in the Back Seat of the Family Car** : At 17, Will Toledo needed a quiet, soundproofed space to rehearse. The best available was the back seat of the family car, staring at the front headrests. "He rehearsed his vocals in the most quiet and most soundproofed place he knew, the back seat of the family car." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Caravan Player (South Asia)** : In South Asia, a boombox-like device known as a Caravan is available, which comes preloaded with 5,000 copyright-free Hindi songs. This device offers a complete, self-contained music solution, eliminating the need for additional purchases or downloads. "For example, you can buy a device called a caravan, which is a boom box-like player, preloaded with 5,000 copyright-free Hindi songs. If that's all you want, nothing more to buy, nothing more to download." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**Carl Heinz Brandenburg: MP3 Algorithm Development (1991-1995)** : Carl Heinz Brandenburg and his team at the Fraunhofer Institute pioneered the development of the MP3 algorithm, establishing the mathematical principles by 1991. Their research focused on how the human brain selectively perceives music, enabling them to strip out "invisible sound" from audio files. This process reduced file sizes to about one-tenth of the original, making audio transmission over phone lines significantly faster and easier. The algorithm was refined and accepted as an international standard by 1994, becoming widely available by 1995, coincident with the internet's public emergence. "Brennenberg and his crew worked out the math by 1991. By 1994 it had been refined and accepted as an international standard. By 1995 it was in the wild." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PNiL63fY1Y8
**Carl Linnaeus and Taxonomy** : The firm rules for naming living things on Earth are traceable to Carl Linnaeus, a Swedish scientist who originated the idea of using Latin for naming and classifying all things, living and dead. This system, known as taxonomy, utilizes Latin because it is a dead language, ensuring neutrality and international agreement without offense. "These rules can be traced to a guy named Carl Leneas, a Swedish scientist who came up with the idea of using Latin for naming and classifying things both living in dead." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VMPLplhDk8I
**Cases of Extreme Censorship** : The ongoing history of new music series will explore instances of extreme censorship in music, indicating its significant impact on artists and the industry. "We'll look at two cases of extreme censorship." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Somq2MoRi5Q
**Cassette Audio Quality Improvements (1971)** : The initial sound quality of compact cassettes, envisioned for dictation, was "less than desirable." However, significant improvements came in 1971 with the replacement of ferric oxide tape coatings by Chromium Dioxide, and later by pure metal particles, dramatically enhancing sound fidelity. The same year, Dolby-type B noise reduction was introduced, effectively reducing the inherent "annoying hiss" of cassettes, making them suitable for high-fidelity listening and integral to high-end stereo setups. `"It would be years before better quality magnetic coatings came to market. The original Farric oxide on the tape was eventually replaced by Chromium Doxide in 1971, followed later by pure metal particles, greatly improving sound, and making the cassette suitable for proper high-fi listening."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Cassette Nomenclature** : During the cassette era, magnetic tapes were typically labeled according to their total recording duration. For example, a 30-minute cassette, offering 15 minutes of recording time per side, was denoted as a C30, while 60-minute and 90-minute cassettes were labeled C60 and C90, respectively. The NME's influential *C86* compilation ingeniously played on this established nomenclature, integrating the year of its release (1986) into the title. "If you remember the cassette era, you'll know that tapes came in various lengths." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jhgZrwtYE2g
**Cassette Sales Decline and Niche Markets (Mid-1980s - Present)** : Cassette sales peaked in the mid-1980s and early 1990s, even selling as well as CDs in some territories until 1993, but then experienced a rapid decline globally. However, cassettes remained popular in regions such as Africa and Southeast Asia due to their resilience against heat, humidity, and dust. They also retained significance in Japan, despite its reputation as a "gadget-crazy country," where some technological transitions were slower. Furthermore, cassettes are currently a substantial business for the prison population, with specialized screw-less, non-weaponizable cassettes being produced for over 2 million incarcerated individuals in the US. `"After peaking in the middle 80s and early 90s, in some territories, tape sold as well as CDs until 1993, cassette sales began a precipitous decline in most of the world."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Cassette Singles (1980s-1990s)** : Cassette singles, the tape equivalent of 7-inch 45 RPM vinyl singles, were prevalent in the 1980s and 90s. These often adhered to the A-side and B-side tradition, mirroring their vinyl counterparts. "Not Cassette singles, the tape version of the 7 inch 45 RPM single that was around for a while in the 80s and 90s, often had an A side and a B side in keeping with the vinyl tradition." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**Cassette Tape Recording Format** : Cassettes were a widely used analog magnetic tape recording format designed for both audio recording and playback. Although the technology began to emerge in the 1960s, its peak cultural impact and widespread adoption as a dominant medium for audio consumption occurred throughout the 1970s and 1980s. "Cassettes were an analog magnetic tape recording format for audio recording and playback, although the birth and growth of the cassette began in the 1960s, its cultural moment took place during the 1970s and 1980s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZleIqEpaNio
**Cassette Technology's Flaws** : Cassettes are described as an "old, inferior technology" whose attempted resurrection is misguided due to inherent flaws. They deliver poor sound quality, are prone to jamming, and restrict mix tape creation to real-time. Over time, the glue binding magnetic particles to the tape dries out, leading to degradation and the "forever irreparable" loss of music information. Additionally, their plastic boxes have fragile hinges, and cassettes frequently end up damaged in car footwells or melt on dashboards, further compounded by the absence of factory cassette players in modern automobiles. "Cossets are an old, inferior technology that served us well in their day and serve no purpose whatsoever." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7Zniri9uSlc
**Cassettes (Mid-1970s)** : Cassettes, invented by Philips (the co-developer of the compact disc), became popular, contributing to record stores being crowded with multiple formats by the mid-1970s. "Then there was the cassettes invented by Phillips, the company that later co-developed the compact disc." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Cassettes vs. Portable CD Players** : Cassettes offered the distinct advantage of allowing users to create their own mix tapes, a capability not present in portable CD players, which relied exclusively on pre-recorded discs. This presented a trade-off between the creative freedom of mix tapes (despite requiring fast forwarding and rewinding) and the instant accessibility of digital tracks from CDs. "The big advantage of cassettes was that you could make your own mix tapes. That was the trade off and a log mix tapes that you had to fast forward and rewind or instantly accessible digital tracks, but only from pre-recorded disks." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**Cast Away From Here (c. 2005)** : This song is a low-quality recording believed to have been made from a radio broadcast in Seville, Spain, in 2005. It has been given the placeholder title "Cast Away From Here," but its artist and full details remain unknown. "It's a recording made from the radio. We think in Seville, Spain in 2005." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UsPoPUByjYw
**Cat Music Preferences Study (2002)** : In 2002, a team of Austrian psychologists conducted a five-day study where they filmed cats exposed to various musical genres. They observed that cats moved closer to speakers when they enjoyed the music and moved away when they disliked it. The study concluded that cats generally prefer music with a fast beat and deep bass frequencies, while showing a distinct aversion to high frequencies and, notably, guitar solos. "Back in 2002, a team of Austrian psychologists, very bored psychologists, apparently, spent five days filming cats as they were exposed to various kinds of music. They discovered that when cats heard something they liked, they moved closer to the speakers. When they heard something they didn't like, they moved away. And here's what they found. Cats really seem to dig music with a really fast beat. What's more, are they were into tracks with deep bass, songs with high frequencies? Not so much. So what does this mean for you and your cat? Well, techno would be good, so with certain types of death metal. Just stay away from guitar solos. Cats don't like guitar solos." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0D4wGWI-_z8
**Cat and Mouse Game with Bootleggers** : For many years, a "cat and mouse game" existed between record labels, artists, and bootleggers, with hardcore fans caught in the middle, hoping to get their hands on unreleased material. Bootleggers often moved their operations offshore to countries like Italy, Singapore, and Indonesia, where copyright laws were perceived as "a little looser." "For years, we had this cat and mouse game between the labels and the artists and the bootleggers." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zT6pRGYLWhw
**Catalog Sale as Accelerated Future Royalties** : The money artists receive from selling their catalog is essentially an accelerated payout of royalties they would have earned over time anyway. This means they get a large lump sum upfront rather than waiting for smaller, periodic royalty checks. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Catalog Sales for Younger Artists** : Catalog sales are also becoming attractive to younger artists, offering financial freedom despite giving up future royalties on older songs. They can still earn from new music and use the substantial cash influx for philanthropy, non-musical investments, activism, or estate planning, eliminating financial worry about new album success. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Catalyst for Smartphone Market Boom** : The iPhone's introduction spurred every other technology company to enter the smartphone market, leading to a widespread proliferation of "supercomputers in their pockets" for the public and contributing to what is described as "permanent attention deficit disorder." "Again, the iPhone was not the first smartphone, but because of the iPhone, everyone got into this space." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Catholic Church and Major/Minor System** : Approximately a thousand years after Pope Gregory's decree, the Catholic Church played a role in encouraging the shift towards the major and minor system of keys and chords that is predominantly used in music today. "A thousand years later, the Catholic Church helped encourage the transition to the major and minor system of keys and cords that we use today." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g-tBn8dsSqw
**Catholic Church's Stance on Devil's Triad** : Contrary to popular myth, the Catholic Church never issued an official ban on the "devil's triad" (flattened fifth). However, the Church generally discouraged its use because "it sounded weird," preferring harmonious sounds. Despite this, some found these chords "powerful and cool," while others perceived their dissonance as too chilling or evil-sounding for music, which they believed should be pleasing to the ears and soul. "Now contrary to myth, the Catholic Church never banned the devil's triad. But they weren't happy about people using it because, well, it sounded weird." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=41eKVp8JIhU
**Cats Dislike Guitar Solos** : A 2002 study conducted by Austrian psychologists on cat musical preferences found that while cats generally enjoy music with a fast beat and deep bass, they tend to react negatively to high frequencies and, specifically, do not like guitar solos. "Cats don't like guitar solos." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0D4wGWI-_z8
**Caudate and Musical Anticipation** : The caudate, a specific sub-region within the brain, possesses the remarkable ability to anticipate the emotional climax of a song up to 15 seconds before it actually occurs. This predictive capacity is why songs that progressively build intensity before a significant drop or release evoke such a strong, pleasurable sensation, as the brain instinctively senses a forthcoming "big payoff." "the co-date can somehow anticipate the emotional climax of a song up to 15 seconds before it happens." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**Causes of Death (2010s Musicians)** : Most musicians mentioned as having died in the 2010s passed away from medical issues or old age, but a significant number, including Chris Cornell and Chester Bennington, died by suicide, with two particularly shocking cases occurring within two months of each other in 2017. "Most of the names I mentioned died from some kind of medical issue or old age, but there were also many suicides, and perhaps two of the most shocking came within two months of each other in 2017." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**Cautionary Tales in Musicians' Passings** : The lives and deaths of many musicians contain "cautionary tales," suggesting lessons or warnings to be learned from their experiences. "And there are more than a few cautionary tales in some of these pastings." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HBS24NwhrE8
**Celebrity Endorsement for Fashion Brands** : Celebrity endorsement is a highly effective strategy for fashion companies, particularly those with limited advertising budgets. The example of Ugg Boots demonstrates how giving free products to highly photographed celebrities, such as Pamela Anderson, can lead to exponential sales increases and make a product a seasonal fashion must-have. "Earlier on, we talked about how good it is for business, for celebrity to be spotted wearing your stuff. Take the case of the Ugg Boot. This is a great study in this whole thing. The company had no money to advertise their boots in the traditional sense. So they simply gave away a few pair to the most photographed celebrities in the world. And they started with Pamela Anderson. And every time she turned up in People or Us magazine wearing a pair of Uggs, sales went through the roof. Uggs became the fashion boot for his season. And the company made a fortune, all for the price of a couple of pairs of free boots." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mhQ36Y-c9fM
**Celebrity Skin and Body Fluids – Company Selling Rock Star Bacteria, Skin Cells, and Fecal Matter** : A company called Celebrity Skin and Body Fluids, founded anonymously by former Hollywood personal assistants with access to their employers' private spaces, offered for sale biological material attributed to various rock musicians. Documented inventory included: Dave Navarro's personal bacteria at $5.75 per batch; Michael Stipe of R.E.M.'s dead skin cells at $12.75; Flea of the Red Hot Chili Peppers' fecal matter at $27 per cubic inch; and Billy Corgan of the Smashing Pumpkins' fecal matter at $30 per cubic inch (priced higher due to scarcity). "Why would anyone want any of this stuff? Beyond me, unless of course you were engaged in some kind of weird cloning experiment." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lyYsTon_NSc
**Censorship and Groups Like PMRC** : The history of controversial lyrics is intertwined with attempts at censorship, exemplified by "crusades of various groups like the PMRC and others who seek to shut down free speech through music." These efforts highlight ongoing societal concerns about the moral influence of musical content. "We can talk about modern-day censorship, the crusades of various groups like the PMRC and others who seek to shut down free speech through music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Challenge of Song Identification in Streaming** : A critical component for the streaming payment system is the precise identification of each played song, which presents an extremely complex challenge to eliminate all potential errors in attributing plays. "How do you identify a specific song so that you've eliminated all possibility of error?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Challenges for Indie Artists/Labels** : Indie artists and labels face significant operational burdens, including manufacturing records, securing distribution into stores, collecting sales revenue, managing audits, taxes, contracts, and handling extensive promotional efforts. These tasks typically require large staffs at major labels, often leaving indie artists with limited time for creative work. "However, it also means that you're on the hook for all the risks and hassles. This includes manufacturing your records, actually getting them into as many record stores as you can, and then collecting the money from the sales of those records." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ft8nruhWCas
**Challenges for Musicians Post-Original Band** : After experiencing the creation and performance of their own original music and albums in a band, it becomes extremely difficult for musicians to transition back to being a "working musician" who solely plays other people's songs. "Once you've done that you can't. It's very hard to go back to being like a working musician that just playing in the music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zBd13KtYlAA
**Challenges for New Musicians in the Internet Era** : Making it in the music business has always been difficult, requiring not just talent but also significant luck to be in the right place at the right time with the right sound, image, and attitude. Since the internet disrupted the industry around 2000 with free digital files, streaming, social media, and YouTube, it has become even harder for new acts to be heard above the exponentially increased competition and noise. "It has never been harder for a new act to be heard above the noise of everyone else." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GmYlTwI_TQQ
**Challenges for Post-2000 Artists** : Artists emerging after 2000 face significant struggles in building careers due to an overwhelming volume of music, intense competition, and excessive choice for music fans, making it exceptionally difficult to gain recognition in the post-internet era. "But artists that have risen after 2000, let's generally be in a struggle, largely because it's almost impossible to make a name for yourself in the post-internet era." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Challenges for Struggling Musicians** : Struggling performers, who form a vast underclass, are forced to spend much of their time on non-creative activities to make ends meet, making it difficult for them to develop, experiment, and mature creatively into something great. "They spend so much of their time doing things other than creating music that it's a challenge to be creative and to experiment and to develop and mature into something great." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Challenges in Concert Promotion and Financial Settlement** : As concerts grew in scale, promoters like Bill Graham faced complex challenges: deciding between general admission or seated formats, managing inventory across various venue types (theaters, arenas, stadiums), knowing which outlets had best seats, reconciling unsold tickets, and accurately settling payments with bands at the end of the night. "How do you accurately settle up with the gate receipts at the end of the night? The band's going to want to get paid. How much do you pay them?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OCkov6FTliE
**Challenges of Band Longevity** : Maintaining a band's lineup over many years presents significant challenges, including the constant close proximity of members, cramped conditions on tour, record company issues, personal issues, and the general fragility of human relationships. Despite these difficulties, some bands, like Billy Talent, Radiohead, U2, and ZZ Top, manage to maintain a consistent lineup for decades. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ocMHRLQMQY0
**Challenges of Creating Box Sets** : Compiling a box set is a "very long time" consuming and difficult process. It involves sorting through old material, which is often lost, misplaced, mislabeled, or unlabeled. Establishing the provenance of each song, cleaning up recordings, and navigating extensive legal hurdles to prevent lawsuits are all necessary steps. "Often the tapes or discs are lost or misplaced or mislabeled or not labeled at all." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zT6pRGYLWhw
**Challenges of Creating Public Band Records** : Bands often face difficulties in ensuring the accuracy of their public record, relying on publicists, record labels, and marketing teams who frequently produce overly flowery and insubstantial bios and press releases. While online resources like allmusic.com and Wikipedia offer improvements, they often rely on these initial, less informative official sources or secondary articles. "But you're often at the mercy of someone else when it comes to creating that record. First, there's your publicist and anyone at your record label dealing with marketing. They issue bios and press releases where the writer seems to have been paid by the word." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WSe0wdljSGA
**Challenges of Indie Labels for Bands** : Running an indie band and label is difficult due to the extensive business demands: getting albums into stores, promoting releases, tracking sales, and collecting money. These tasks are a significant drain on time and energy, which often drove bands like Sonic Youth, R.E.M., and Hüsker Dü to sign with major labels. "The business of getting your albums into the stores, promoting releases, and going through the process of tracking albums sold and the hassle of collecting that money, that's just a huge drain on time and energy and everything else." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gkLMwrthZ9s
**Challenges of Record Contracts for Artists** : Young and emerging artists often face significant difficulties in understanding and navigating complex record contracts, which can lead to exploitative situations. David Bowie's "weird" contract with Main Man resulted in him receiving minimal profits despite millions in album sales, while The Clash's manager Bernie Rhodes negotiated a "very poor" deal. Even U2's young members sought help from a label marketing manager to comprehend their contracts. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PzyvXmDKFUc
**Challenges of Singing the Devil's Tri Tone** : A practical reason for the historical avoidance of the "devil's tri tone" in some musical contexts was the inherent difficulty of singing such combinations of notes. This vocal challenge was likely a primary factor in its infrequent use, more so than any formal prohibition. "Yeah, such combinations of notes were hard to sing. But that more than anything was why they stepped around the devil's tri tone whenever possible." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=41eKVp8JIhU
**Challenges with Anti-Bot Laws** : Governments globally, including in Canada, the US, Australia, Ireland, and the UK, are attempting to combat scalper bots with anti-bot laws. However, the effectiveness of these laws is significantly hindered by the borderless nature of the internet. Bot operators can simply relocate their operations to jurisdictions with less stringent regulations, enabling them to continue attacking ticketing systems in other countries from the outside. This makes comprehensive enforcement extremely difficult. "But because the internet knows no borders, operators can just move to places where they can attack those jurisdictions from the outside." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sPj3uxmz8H4
**Chanel Glove – Photography Prop** : A black Chanel glove, which was a leftover prop from a previous photoshoot, was spontaneously utilized by photographer Colin Lane for the controversial international cover of The Strokes' "Is This It." The glove adorned the hand of his then-girlfriend in the striking image. "There were some props left around from a previous shoot, including that Chanel glove." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Changes in Song Intros** : The average instrumental lead-up to vocals in a pop song has drastically shortened from about 17 seconds in the 1980s to less than five seconds today, a direct consequence of the need to immediately engage listeners in the streaming era.
