"Now in the 80s, the average instrumental lead up to the vocals of a pop song was around 17 seconds. Now it's less than five." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Changing Attitudes to Music Possession** : Modern attitudes towards music ownership are shifting, with less emphasis on physical possession. The ability to access any song on demand from "the cloud" reduces the perceived need to personally own the music, especially with seamless internet access in environments like cars. "If I can access any song I want any time I feel like it, who says I actually have to have that music on my person? Why can't I just get it from the cloud that mysterious part of the internet that has the ability to store anything?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Changing Fan Attitudes (Modern Era)** : The strict musical tribalism of earlier decades has largely dissipated among young people today. It is now considered "perfectly okay, in fact admirable and inviable" to appreciate a wide range of artists, from contemporary acts like Arcade Fire and Coldplay to classics like The Beatles, Led Zeppelin, Marvin Gaye, James Brown, and Elvis Presley. "Today, that's really not the situation with a lot of young people. That snobbishness is disappearing on both sides." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ft8nruhWCas
**Changing Metrics for Superstardom** : Traditional metrics like album sales and chart positions are considered "so 20th century" and are no longer reliable indicators of an act's popularity due to drastic changes in the music industry since 2000. "The music industry has changed drastically since 2000, so we need different metrics. Album sales and chart positions are, you know, so 20th century." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Changing Relationship with Music in the Streaming Era** : The shift from purchasing physical albums to streaming has altered the listener's relationship with music; the financial investment in an album once fostered a deeper appreciation and bond with the artist, an obligation that has diminished with the "infinite jukebox" where music is easily accessible for little to no cost, leading to shrunk attention spans, frequent skipping of unfamiliar songs, and a lack of contextualization for artists, songs, or genres, ultimately making music feel more "disposable" and less valued. "The financial relationship we had to that piece of plastic strengthened the bond you had with that artist." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Changing Role of Radio and Music Television (Mid-2000s)** : The traditional support for bands from outlets like MuchMusic began to wane in the mid-2000s, as networks started shifting older-generation bands to less prominent or specialized channels (e.g., "MuchLOUD"). This indicated a changing landscape where established acts were no longer considered "cool" enough for mainstream rotation, impacting their visibility and airplay. "much music isn't going to play. We're not cool anymore." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=W9gdKha0PLg
**Chanie Wenjack's Story** : Chanie Wenjack was a young Indigenous boy caught in the residential school system. He was sent to a residential school in Kenora, Ontario, from which he ran away. He tragically attempted to walk 600 kilometers back home but died of exposure alongside a railway track, a story that profoundly inspired Gord Downie's "Secret Path" project and the Chanie Wenjack Fund. "Cheney-Wenjack was caught up in the whole residential school's travesty. He was sent to Canora, Ontario, but he ran away. And he tried to walk the 600 kilometers home, but he'd never made it, dying of exposure alongside a railway track." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Um_KEEcCeT4
**Charles Dickens' Book Tour and Scalping (1867)** : A situation similar to Jenny Lind's ticket scandal occurred in 1867 during Charles Dickens' book tour of America. "A similar situation arose in 1867 when Charles Dickens went on a book tour of America." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D8GU8ZpSgY8
**Charles Manson TikTok Virality (2022) – "Home Is Where You're Happy" (1970) Used in Autumn Videos** : For unknown reasons, a 1970 song by Charles Manson entitled "Home Is Where You're Happy" went viral on TikTok in 2022, used by hundreds of creators as the soundtrack to videos celebrating the fall season. **Chateau Marmont Hotel - Iconic LA Location** : The Chateau Marmont Hotel on Sunset Boulevard in Los Angeles is a notable location mentioned multiple times, serving as Courtney Love's residence at one point and later as the venue for the after-party of the "Who the F Is Arthur Fogel" documentary premiere, attended by Lady Gaga and Stewart Copeland. **Chinese Pop Fans Cause Food Shortage and Show Cancellation (2021)** : In China, a boy band creation program sponsored by a dairy company offered fans extra voting power for scanning QR codes on milk bottle caps. Fans orchestrated an online campaign to purchase millions of dollars worth of the milk solely for the caps, then poured the liquid down the drain, leading to a food shortage. This widespread food waste outraged authorities, who responded by implementing new anti-food waste laws and canceling the boy band show. "Fans created an online drive to buy millions of dollars worth of this drink just for the bottle caps." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-N6IM5Nm5iw
**Chris Hadfield Zero G Video (2013)** : Prior to Ok Go's atmospheric Zero G music video, Commander Chris Hadfield released a Zero G video aboard the International Space Station in 2013. "And before you point out that Commander Chris Hadfield did a Zero G video aboard the International Space Station in 2013, you are right." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hmnMG6f80Sg
**Christmas Music as an Evergreen Revenue Stream** : Releasing Christmas music is a strategic career move for artists and labels, as widespread adoption of a Christmas song can create an "evergreen revenue stream" that generates income for decades. This is evident from the massive sales and royalties generated by successful holiday tracks. "This is a smart career move too, because if your song gets widespread pickup, then you've got an evergreen revenue stream that will continue for decades." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nYPGQiDL5bw
**Christmas Song for Dogs (2020)** : A Christmas song specifically for dogs was created in the UK in 2020 by a vet and a dog behavioralist. Recorded at Abbey Road, it incorporates sounds dogs enjoy, such as squeaky toys, bells, and "arphats," and is set to a reggae beat, aiming to reduce stress. Sales proceeds go to an animal charity. "Taking all this research and more into account, we now have a Christmas song for dogs in 2020." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nYPGQiDL5bw
**Christopher Reeve Context** : Christopher Reeve, the actor famous for playing Superman, is mentioned to provide a clear context for the severity of Dryden Mitchell's C2 vertebrae injury. Mitchell's injury was the same type of vertebral damage that paralyzed Reeve, highlighting its life-altering potential. "Vertabrae C2, the same one that paralyzed actor Christopher Reeve." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DgN0vBIugLc
**Chromesthesia Condition** : Chromesthesia is a neurological condition where music or sound is perceived as structures of light or color. Tori Amos is cited as an individual who experiences this, seeing music as colors, which reportedly aided her piano playing by allowing her to follow the colors in addition to the sounds of the notes. "music appeared to her as structures of light, which is a condition known as chromatizia. This helped her play because in addition to the sounds of the notes, she could just follow the colors." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dJiSZ4X0F2g
**Chrysler In-Dash Turntables (1950s)** : In the 1950s, Chrysler briefly offered in-dash turntables in some cars, but they only played special 16 2/3 RPM singles, with fewer than 50 records available, and the venture was deemed a disaster. "Now believe it or not, Chrysler offered in-dash turntables in some of their cars in the 1950s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Chuck (UN Official)** : Chuck was a UN official who played a heroic role in saving Sum 41 and 40 other individuals when they were trapped in a hotel during an African civil war in the Congo. He continuously moved the group to safer rooms as the hotel was under attack from mortar rounds, RPGs, and stray bullets, eventually organizing armored tanks for their extraction after three days. "Chuck was the guy he happened to be at the hotel with us. He was with the UN and he was the one who kept moving us around and keeping us safe from room to room." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6L4xKBFEYkE
**Circle Pits and Wall of Death** : As slam dancing evolved, larger gatherings at gigs saw the formation of "circles of slam dancers" at various points in the crowd. These intense formations became known as circle pits, and sometimes escalated into the "wall of death," where two sides of the pit would charge at each other. "These became known as circle pits and sometimes the wall of death." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KMx4bmq8o2Y
**City and Colour – Dallas Green; City = Dallas; Colour = Green** : "Terribly obvious yet not." Dallas Green. Get it? "City and color, which is terribly obvious yet not." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Class Prejudice in England** : An anecdotal example illustrates class prejudice where one might feel uncomfortable with a working-class Mancunian pilot but reassured by a pilot named "Giles from Rugby" (implying an upper-class background), suggesting that background affects perception of competence. This reflects the class dynamics prevalent during the Madchester era. "if I got an airplane in the guy, the pilot says we're going to fly... he's got a mancha of tracks and I might get me off this plane... But if he's called Giles and he speaks from rugby, I feel okay." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KF_adyXf86Q
**Classification of Musicians (Homo Blatodia)** : A humorous, non-scientific classification suggests a new subspecies, Homo Blatodia, named after the cockroach genus. Within this, two lineages are proposed: "periplaneta, philogenosa, otherwise known as the smokey brown cockroach" to classify Keith Richards, and "periplaneta americana, the American cockroach" to classify John Frusciante, owing to his perceived indestructibility despite a hard life. "And if I'm sitting with my taxonomy flow chart, this is where I write the name John Fruciante, the, let's call it, occasional guitarist of the red hot chili peppers." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TESrEzVHLCc
**Clement Adler's Pioneering Streaming** : In 1881, French engineer Clement Adler demonstrated the Tiatrophone, a system that effectively "streamed" live opera and symphony performances from Parisian stages to remote locations via telephone lines. His work predated modern streaming by over a century and established the foundational concept of delivering live audio remotely. "So can we say that Clement Adler was the inventor of streaming music? Sure, why not?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Cleonides' Opposition to Unclean Sounds (2nd Century)** : In the 2nd century, Cleonides, described as a "busybody," similarly urged musicians to refrain from using "unclean sounds." This continued a historical trend of musical authorities and commentators cautioning against or condemning certain sounds or intervals deemed undesirable or non-conforming. "Cleonitis, a busy body who lived in the second century, also employed musicians to stay away from these unclean sounds." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=41eKVp8JIhU
**Cleveland Punk Scene Emergence (Mid-1970s)** : While New York is considered "ground zero" for punk, Cleveland played a crucial role. Music fans in Cleveland, exposed to Iggy Pop and The Velvet Underground, nurtured "several extreme groups" that were anti-hippie, anti-establishment, and experimental, rejecting "flower power" and "Prague rock" and eschewing major labels. This scene, featuring bands like Pere Ubu, contributed significantly to the germination of punk rock by 1975. "Although New York has become enshrined as ground zero for the entire punk rock revolution of the middle 1970s, the city and the CBGB scene did get some help from Cleveland." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VYi3r0G-ne4
**Club Scene and Vinyl Survival** : The club scene was a significant reason for vinyl's survival, as DJs and turntableists preferred its tactile nature for mixing, scratching, and sampling. "The club scene is a big reason why it survived." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Clyde Stubblefield's Recognition and Support** : Unlike Gregory Coleman, Clyde Stubblefield received some recognition, including various awards and a set of autographed sticks in the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame, despite not getting credit or royalties for his "Funky Drummer" break. Prince even paid tens of thousands for his healthcare, and he received an honorary doctorate before his death in 2017. "Clyde fared a lot better than Gregory Coleman. He received no credits on the James Brown song, nor did he get any royalties from the hundreds of times his playing was sampled." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Clyde Stubblefield's Unsung Status** : Despite significant recognition from within the music community, including praise from artists and induction into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame for his influential "Funky Drummer" break, Clyde Stubblefield's widespread public recognition remained limited. He shaped music profoundly, "albeit unintentionally and very much uncompensated." "Yet you got to wonder how many people outside of the music community know of Clyde's Stubblefield and the incredible effect he had on shaping music with that one drum brick, albeit unintentionally and very much uncompensated." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Cocaine and Audio Perception** : During the recording of *Be Here Now*, it was noted that cocaine consumption can negatively affect one's hearing and audio perception, influencing production decisions such as Noel Gallagher's insistence on making the record sound "bigger and louder" with excessive layers of guitars and boosted high frequencies. "Cocaine messes with your hearing and your audio perception." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=S6qB5B1PdyA
**Cocaine's Effect on Hearing** : Cocaine's impact on high-frequency hearing was noted as a reason for the "shrill" or "screechy" sound of Oasis's *Be Here Now*, as it sounded fine to Noel Gallagher, who was heavily using at the time, but not to "normal years." "And that's because cocaine messes with your ability to hear high frequencies." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5q5EnZj1FwE
**Code Words for Sex in Lyrics (1950s)** : In the 1950s, words like "rock and roll," "writer," "grind," and "grass" became standard "dirty record vocabulary." These terms, through "cunning inflections and constant usage," served as coded language to describe sexual activity in song lyrics, contributing to moral outrage even when the words themselves seemed innocuous. "By cunning inflections and constant usage, words such as rock and roll, writer, grind, and grass have been standard, dirty record vocabulary." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Coffee and Post-Concert Hearing Impairment** : Following exposure to high volumes of music at a concert which can cause temporary hearing impairment, consuming coffee is ill-advised. The combination of caffeine and audio overstimulation has been shown to extend the duration required for hearing to return to its normal state. "Caffeine plus audio overstimulation equals more time for your hearing to get back to normal." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**Cold Case Detective's Work Process (Ongoing)** : When a cold case detective reviews a file, such as Mike Suzuki's review of Kurt Cobain's death, their methodology often involves meticulously examining existing evidence, seeking new details, and consulting experts. Suzuki, for instance, reconstructed the gun firing sequence with a firearms expert, revealing "nothing weird" about the shell's landing or the gun's position relative to the body, thereby discrediting theories of a staged suicide. "He also went through some 35-millimeter film that had been taken of the crime scene. This helped him reconstruct how the gun was fired." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1Jt2fUBDusk
**Cold War Tensions** : The Cold War between the United States and the Soviet Union continued, with the two superpowers "always snarling at each other," maintaining an atmosphere of international tension and threat. "And of course there was the Cold War with the US and the Soviet Union, always snarling at each other." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Coldplay X&Y Cover (2005) – Baudot Code; Supposed to Say "X and Y" but Actually Reads "X96" Due to Encoding Error** : Coldplay hired design company Tappin Gofton for X&Y after liking their work on the Chemical Brothers' Push the Button. Tappin Gofton researched Baudot code — a binary telegraph code invented in 1874 by Emil Baudot as International Telegraph Alphabet Number One — and designed the cover using colored blocks representing Baudot encoded letters. The colors were their addition; original Baudot code was black and white. The design was intended to spell out "X and Y." However, somebody got the encoding wrong — to a Baudot expert, the cover actually reads "X96." "To a Baudoe code pro, the album is actually apparently called X96." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JdSUHkXM1mQ
**Coldplay – Adopted from Another Band Who Had the Name from a Poetry Collection** : Originally called Starfish at university. Another Oxford group called Coldplay (two words) had taken their name from a poetry collection called "Children's Reflections Coldplay." That band was going defunct and agreed to give up the name. "That other band were in the process of going defunct so Starfish asked them if they could have their name." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Colin Lane – The Strokes Photographer** : Colin Lane was the photographer who captured the image for the controversial international cover of The Strokes' 2001 debut album, "Is This It." The photograph, featuring his then-girlfriend's naked rear and hip with a black Chanel glove, was a spontaneous shot taken as she stepped out of the shower. Lane has maintained the anonymity of the woman. "That shot was taken by Colin Lane of his girlfriend." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Collaborative, Data-Driven Songwriting** : Modern songwriting, driven by audience engagement data, often requires the combined efforts of multiple musicians, beatmakers, producers, and lyricists to craft a single song optimized for streaming success. "The middies of musicians and beatmakers and producers and lyricists are needed to write a single song using audience engagement data." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Collapse of 1960s Promises (Mid-1970s)** : By the mid-1970s, the idealistic "hippie values of peace and love" associated with the 1960s had largely "collapsed," contributing to a turbulent societal atmosphere. "The promises of the 1960s with its hippie values of peace and love had collapsed." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Collapse of Post-War Consensus (Late 1970s UK)** : After decades of a "post-war consensus" where left and right British governments generally cooperated, this era collapsed. Over about six years, the political right began to attribute the UK's economic problems to the welfare state created by this consensus. "After decades of what was known as the post-war consensus, an era where the left and right in British government got along, the whole thing collapsed." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Collapse of Recorded Music Sales (2000-2009)** : Between 2000 and 2009, sales of recorded music experienced a dramatic collapse after decades of growth. CD sales were cut in half globally, with the U.S. market dropping 52% and annual sales plummeting from nearly a billion discs to 374 million by 2009. "Between 2000 and the start of 2009, CD sales were cut in half in just about every territory around the world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Collectibility of Rare Cassettes** : Certain rare cassette releases, particularly those from influential artists or early solo projects, can become highly collectible. For instance, a copy of Dave Grohl's solo album *Pocketwatch*, released as "Late" on a cassette-only label, can trade for "$700 or more" in good condition due to its rarity and historical significance. "A copy of an album called Pocketwatch and Good Condition trades for $700 or more." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_AY5lS-FmlU
**College Radio's Growing Power** : Though listenership wasn't huge, campus stations became increasingly organized under national and international associations, with conferences, magazines, and their own music charts. This organization significantly amplified college radio's power within the broader music industry. "Now listenership wasn't huge, but as campus stations became more organized under national and international associations with conferences and magazines and even their own music charts, college radio became more and more powerful in the general scheme of the music industry." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sCtQqVBtCaI
**College Radio's Rise and Influence** : By the mid-1980s, college radio became a highly important and influential factor in music, welcoming indie bands that commercial radio and MTV largely ignored. These stations, not dependent on ratings for revenue, could be more daring and experimental, making them the primary source for the newest and most exciting music for many. "By the mid-80s, college radio was becoming a very important and very influential factor in music. While most commercial radio stations were unwilling to play anything that wasn't on a major label and on MTV, indie bands were welcomed with open arms on campus and nonprofit public radio." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sCtQqVBtCaI
**College Rock Network and Charts** : As the 1980s progressed, a network developed among campus radio stations in the US and Canada that played college rock. Airplay was tracked, leading to the creation of charts that provided valuable data. This data was then utilized by bands and their labels for strategic decisions, such as planning tours. "Eventually, a network developed between these campus radio stations. Airplay was tracked, which led to charts, and these charts provided data used by the bands and their labels to determine things like tours." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LgJ7ww2AgF4
**Collision of Music and Technology** : The proliferation of smartphones is predicted to have a transformative impact on music and society in the next decade, akin to the transistor radio's influence in the 1950s and 60s. Constant internet connectivity will make entertainment, information, and communication universally accessible through touchscreens. Previous technological shifts, such as MP3 players, drastically altered public interaction by promoting isolation through headphones, while cell phones have changed private conversations into often one-sided public broadcasts. "the proliferation of smartphones are going to be to the next 10 years with the transistor radio west to the 50s and 60s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yl2poVWguu0
**Columbia's LP (June 1948) – Polyvinyl Chloride from Sewer Pipe Industry; Microgrooves; 24 Minutes Per Side** : Columbia Records introduced the long-playing album in June 1948 using polyvinyl chloride (PVC), which had first gone into use in the 1920s for sewer pipes. PVC was far more durable than the shellac used for 78s and allowed grooves to be placed much closer together using a tiny stylus — Columbia called these microgrooves. A standard 78 held a spiral about 270 feet long (roughly 4 minutes per side); the new LP had a spiral over 1,600 feet long, yielding up to 24 minutes of music per side. LP stands for Long Playing. "Columbia picked up on the older technology as they were experimenting with the new substance called polyvinyl chloride, which first went into use in the 1920s for sewer pipes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=I291j5WBc4k
**Columbine Killings (1999) and Marilyn Manson** : The Columbine killings in Colorado on April 20, 1999, led to significant public blame placed on Marilyn Manson, reportedly because the two perpetrators were fans of his music. This association resulted in Manson being unwelcome in Colorado for several years. "The South African samurai slasherns evoked memories of the Columbine killings in Colorado on April 20, 1999." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Columbine Shooting and Goth Vilification** : The Columbine High School shooting, where perpetrators Eric and Dylan were linked to Marilyn Manson's music, "did more to vilify the whole notion of Goth than any other in the history of this music." "This one event did more to vilify the whole notion of Goth than any other in the history of this music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**Coming of Age with Music** : The period of "coming of age" with music typically begins in high school and concludes upon reaching adulthood (roughly ages 14 to 24). During this time, music plays a central role in self-discovery, bonding with like-minded individuals, and projecting one's identity to the world. "We all go through a period of coming of age with music. This is the period in our lives when music is central to everything we do. We use music to figure out who we are. We use it to bond with other like-minded people. And we use music to project our identity to the world. We use it to say, this is who I am. That period, and I am generalizing here, begins when we enter high school and ends when we get around to becoming adults. So that's roughly 10 years, ages 14 to 24, plus or minus a couple of years." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=v_am7ifh7Pk
**Commercial Radio Economics** : Commercial radio stations operate on revenues dependent on ratings, which require appealing to the largest number of people for as long and as often as possible; this reality means that while hardcore music fans exist, "billions of people who just want songs they can sing along to in the car." "And since the radio stations' revenues depend on ratings, higher ratings, equals more revenues, commercial alt-rock stations struggled along for month to month, always with the specter of a format change, looming overhead." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Commercial Stereo Vinyl Records (1957)** : A commercially viable method for manufacturing vinyl records that played back in stereo was not developed until 1957. "And it wasn't until 1957 that somebody figured out a commercially viable way to manufacture vinyl records that played back in stereo." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Common Headbanging Injuries** : By the 1980s, as headbanging became widespread, people began sustaining various injuries. These included strained necks, whiplash, damage to carotid arteries, and even neck fractures. Medical literature documented cases such as a 50-year-old German man who developed a subdural hematoma requiring surgery after enthusiastic headbanging at a concert. "everyone was banging their heads by the time the 80s began and people had begun to hurt themselves." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bferkEWvJg0
**Common Two-Sibling Bands** : While the focus of this exploration is on larger family units, many bands commonly feature two siblings, such as Oasis, Radiohead, Jesus and Mary Chain, Good Charlotte, and 30 Seconds to Mars (brothers); Tegan and Sara and The Breeders (sisters); and No Doubt, Broods, and The Carpenters (brother-sister combinations). "Okay, there were plenty of bands featuring two brothers. Okay, Oasis, Radiohead, Jesus Marychain, Good Charlotte, 30 seconds to Mars, and lots of groups featuring sisters like Teagan and Sarah, the breeders, and a chunk of brother sister outfits, like no doubt, broods and I guess the Carpenters." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gxbuajGKbko
**Compact Cassette (Philips, 1963) – Democratized Tape Trading** : The compact cassette, invented by Philips in 1963, miniaturized magnetic tape technology and made it portable and affordable, triggering an explosion in tape-trading culture that would sustain the bootleg community through the pre-internet era. "Made tape technology portable and accessible, greatly boosting the 'tape trading culture.'" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OrHtPVBjXw4
**Compact Cassette Influence on CD Diameter (early 1960s)** : Philips, having developed the compact cassette in the early 1960s, used its diagonal width of 11.5 centimeters as a reference point during their early compact disc development, influencing the initial size they settled on. "Because Phillips also developed the cassette in the early 1960s. The diagonal width of a cassette is 11.5 centimeters." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Compact Disc (CD) – Description** : The compact disc was described as a shiny, one-sided plastic and aluminum disc that played music with a laser, tracking from the inside outwards, and could hold 74 to 79 minutes of uninterrupted music. "This was a shiny, one-sided plastic and aluminum disc that played with a laser." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Compact Disc Dominance in 2000** : In 2000, compact discs were extremely popular, selling by hundreds of millions, which led to the music industry being highly profitable. "They were still selling by hundreds of millions, ensuring that the music industry was drowning in money." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Compact Disc Technology Worldwide Rollout (March 1983)** : Compact Disc Technology was officially rolled out worldwide in March 1983, ushering in an unprecedented 17-year boom for the recorded music industry as fans bought their favorite albums again on the new format. "Compact Disk Technology was rolled out worldwide in March of 1983. And for the next 17 years, the recorded music industry experienced a boom unlike it had ever seen before." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Compact Disc as Fastest Growing Entertainment Product** : The compact disc was, at the time, recognized as the fastest growing entertainment product in history. Sales figures illustrate this rapid expansion: 61 million CDs sold in 1985, 140 million in 1986, 1 billion by 1992, and 2 billion by 1993. "The CompactDisc was quite simply the fastest growing entertainment product in the history of the universe. 61 million CDs were sold in 1985, 140 million in 1986, and then the numbers just kept getting bigger and bigger and bigger. 1 billion in 1992, 2 billion in 1993." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Compact Disc as a Seed of Industry Disruption** : The compact disc, released in 1982 by Sony (Japan) and Philips (Netherlands), stored music as digital files but lacked copy protection, allowing for "perfectly and infinitely copied" songs, which unknowingly planted the seeds for the eventual decline of the traditional music industry. "The seeds for the death of the traditional music industry had been sewn back in 1982 with the release of the compact disk." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Compact Personal Jukebox** : The Compact Personal Jukebox was a portable music player equipped with a hard drive, boasting a capacity of 4.8 gigabytes. This allowed it to store approximately 1200 songs, representing a substantial increase in storage compared to earlier flash-memory-based devices. "Then came the compact personal jukebox. It had a hard drive with a capacity of 4.8 gigabytes, which was good for about 1200 songs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**Comparison of 1972 vs. 1982 Record Quality** : Records pressed in 1972 were generally thicker, heavier, and of superior physical quality compared to those pressed just ten years later, around 1982, reflecting the economic and material changes that impacted vinyl production during that decade. "Now it's really weird because a record press in 1972 is often thicker, heavier, and of better physical quality than a record press ten years later." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IIJySSo276k
**Competition from Entire Musical History** : Current artists now face unprecedented competition, not just from contemporaries but from the entire musical history of the human race, which is instantly accessible via streaming. This makes it far more difficult for new acts to "break through" and achieve widespread consensus for superstar status. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Complexity of Rider Negotiation and Enforcement** : Crafting a list of requests in a rider that can be consistently enforced night after night, across various venues, cities, and countries, is described as a "delicate and essential thing," with some bands being more adept at this than others. "Coming up with a list of requests that can be enforced night after night, venue after venue, city after city and country after country, is it delicate and essential thing?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=z2hvUDtBxho
**Concept Album Definition** : A concept album is a record where all the songs and accompanying imagery are unified by a specific theme or thesis, with the music, lyrics, and artwork collaborating to tell a story. "These are records where all the songs and all the imagery is somehow tied together by some kind of unifying theme or thesis." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Dm5N6yDRxXQ
**Concept Replaces Spectacle in Video Production** : With new, affordable technology, "concept replaced spectacle" in music video production. Artists no longer needed massive budgets or glossy productions reminiscent of the 80s and 90s to create impactful content; instead, the goal was to create something "amazing and wonderful" that would make people say "wow, that's cool," and share it. "The new tech meant that you didn't need a lot of money and gear to pull off something amazing and wonderful. It didn't have to be as glossy or slick as videos from the 80s or 90s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Concept of the Library** : The idea of collecting all available knowledge in one central place originated many centuries ago, providing a single location for people to find answers to their questions. "thus the concept of the library was born." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Conceptual Art Trend (1940s-1950s)** : After the success of early album artwork, the trend shifted from simply featuring performers to creating conceptual art that aimed to capture the mood or energy of the music within the album. Examples include a blue sleeve with a piano under a New York street light for Gershwin's Rhapsody in Blue, and the emergence of surrealistic designs, new graphic languages, and diverse illustration techniques. "The original trend was creating conceptual art designed to capture the mood or the energy of the music within the album." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pwebM3TfSdg
**Concert Costs and Insurance Risks** : Staging a concert or a tour is not only expensive but also risky, with liability being a significant concern. The money from a single ticket is divided among the artist, promoter, venue, ticket seller, and an increasing portion goes to insurance companies to cover potential lawsuits from fan injuries or fatalities. Actuarial tables have been rewritten to reflect increased risk, leading to higher insurance premiums that are then passed on to fans through increased ticket prices. "Insurance premiums go up, and these costs are passed along to the fans in the form of increased ticket prices." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7v1REnqKuXE
**Concert Dynamics: Opening Act Sound** : Opening acts at concerts typically do not sound as loud or "big" as the headliner. This is often because they are not given full use of the PA system, a deliberate decision to prevent them from overshadowing or "blowing off the stage" the main act. "In most cases, they don't sound as big as the headliner. And that's usually because they don't get full use of the PA." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Concert Dynamics: Singer Vocal Adaptations** : During live performances, a main act's singing may differ from studio recordings due to the physical demands on the human voice. Singers often adapt their performance by avoiding high, powerful notes night after night to prevent vocal strain, injury, or cancelling tours. "mostly it's the physical frailties of the human voice. It's hard to hit high, powerful notes night after night." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Concert Dynamics: Volume Ramp-up** : It is a common practice for sound engineers to gradually increase the volume of background music played through the PA system between an opening act and the headliner. This "old sound man trick" is designed to build energy and excitement in the venue before the main performer takes the stage. "the closer it comes to the main event, the louder the music gets. This is an old sound man trick designed to build the energy in the building before the headliner hits the stage." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Concert Industry's "Good Enough" Standard (Pre-1990s)** : For decades, the concert industry operated under the premise that "a certain level was good enough for the audience." After experiencing years of poor sound, most attendees "expected the sound to be reasonably lousy and distorted," leading to a stagnation in significant sound innovation until later. "The concert industry had long come to the conclusion that a certain level was good enough for the audience." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qrrwqdpCjpQ
**Concert Nostalgia Boom and Legacy Acts** : The resurgence of interest in "heritage acts" or "legacy bands" from the 1960s, 70s, and 80s has led to a "Concert Nostalgia Boom." These artists now tour in front of very large audiences, with fans willing to pay high prices to see them. This phenomenon is partly driven by the recorded music industry's failure to cultivate a comparable number of new arena- and stadium-filling acts, forcing promoters like Live Nation and AEG to rely on older acts who appeal to an older audience with more disposable income. "Promoters like Live Nation and AEG have had to turn to these older acts who appeal largely to older audiences. We have more disposable income and who are more than willing to relieve their youth one more time." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZzIqIydZTlA
**Concert Rider Function and Importance** : The concert rider is a crucial part of the contract with a promoter, specifying what an act must be provided with before, during, and after a show. Its purpose is to ensure artists are in a "good headspace" to play a great show, covering both mundane logistical details and specific artist requirements. "This brings me to the concert rider, the part of the contract with the promoter that specifies what an act has to be provided with before, during, and after a show." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=z2hvUDtBxho
**Concert Sound as "Piled-Up Experiments" (1970s)** : During the 1970s, concert sound systems were often described as "piled up experiments" — hand-built systems that were not thoroughly tested before being deployed on the road. Companies supplying this gear frequently used a "mix-and-match approach" with random components from various manufacturers, indicative of an industry in its early, experimental phase. "I've heard them described as piled up experiments, hand-built systems that weren't really properly tested for performance before being sent out on the road." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qrrwqdpCjpQ
**Concert Ticket Allocation and Holdbacks** : Not all tickets for a concert are released for sale simultaneously; promoters may release blocks over time, adjusting prices based on demand. A significant portion of tickets, known as "holdbacks," are reserved for specific purposes before general public sale. These include allocations for fan club presales, credit card offers (like American Express's Front of the Line), radio station and media contests, record label requests, the band's and venue's guest lists, and sponsors. The number of these holdback tickets is dynamic. Consequently, for a venue with a stated capacity of 15,000 seats, perhaps only half or less (e.g., 5,000 to 7,000) may actually be available to the general public when tickets officially go on sale. "So in the end, how many of those 15,000 tickets are actually available for sale when the clock hits 10 o'clock on Friday morning and the general public can get in on the action? Maybe half, but probably less." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sPj3uxmz8H4
**Concert Ticket Price Influences: Competition Among Promoters** : Counterintuitively, competition among promoters for popular acts drives concert ticket prices up. Promoters bid against each other by offering increasingly larger guaranteed payments to artists, which are minimum amounts paid regardless of ticket sales. The winning promoter then recoups these higher guarantees and other costs by raising ticket prices, which ultimately trickle down to the fans. "When a band is ready to go on tour, their agent lets promoters in various cities and territories know if there is more than one promoter in a certain city or territory, those who want the show will bid on it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Concert Ticket Price Influences: Fair Market Value Realization** : Artists and managers have belatedly recognized that concert tickets have historically been underpriced compared to other entertainment events like sports and theater. They are now actively seeking to price tickets at their "fair market value," reflecting the true worth of the performance and demand. "Artists and managers have finally realized that they've been underpricing their concert tickets for years relative to other events like sports and theater." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Concert Ticket Price Influences: Industry Pricing Power** : A third reason for rising concert ticket prices is the realization by artists, managers, promoters, and venues that audiences were willing to pay higher prices for shows. This understanding led to a concerted effort within the industry to increase ticket costs, moving towards "fair market value." "And third, prices went up because the people in the industry, artists, managers, promoters, venues, knew that they could raise prices and we would still pay them." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Concert Ticket Price Influences: Inflation and Touring Economics** : Concert ticket prices have significantly increased over the last three decades due to general inflation and a fundamental shift in touring economics. Historically, tours promoted albums, with record sales covering costs. Today, new records often serve as a pretext for tours, as most artist revenue now comes from live performances. "First, inflation, everything costs more. It's just the way things work. Second, the economics of touring have changed." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Concert Ticket Price Influences: Merchandise Sales Markup** : Bands set profit targets for merchandise sales, and because venues typically take a significant cut (20-50%) of merch sales, artists often compensate by charging higher prices for t-shirts and other merchandise. This strategy contributes to the overall cost for fans attending concerts. "Merch sales go mostly to the band. Although the venue will take a cut of sales, maybe 20, 25%, I've heard as high as 50% in some cases." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Concert Ticketing "Holdbacks"** : A significant portion of concert tickets are held back from the general public sale for various purposes: fan clubs (1,000 tickets in a 15,000 capacity venue example), VIP seating (1,000 tickets), credit card VIP programs (2,000 tickets), artist's use for friends/family (100 tickets), venue promotional use (100 tickets), radio station contests (400 tickets), record label use (100 tickets), and seat licenses (500 tickets for season ticket holders). This can reduce the publicly available pool dramatically. "The first category is called holdbacks. These are tickets reserved for specific purposes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Concerts that Changed the World (Beyond Obvious Choices)** : While high-profile events like Woodstock, Altamont, and Live Aid have had lasting implications, the focus is on "below the radar gigs" that had subtle, long-term effects. These less obvious concerts are seen as having musical impacts equivalent to small historical triggers. "But are these the only gigs that changed the world? Absolutely not. They're just the most obvious, the ones that most people know." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=huAvyInzz3Q
**Confidential Settlements in Music Disputes** : Many sonic similarity disputes between artists are resolved "behind closed doors" through confidential settlements. This "sort of thing happens all the time" to "keep the piece," with the public typically not being privy to the details. "All teams on both sides are the equation are mobilized. Discussions are held, usually very calmly. And in the end, everything is settled behind closed doors with the public not seeing anything or being privy to anything." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rfObVld9rIE
**Connections TV Show (1970s)** : The BBC premiered a show called 'Connections' in the 1970s, starring science historian James Burke. Over about 20 years and 70 shows, Burke demonstrated how seemingly unconnected scientific achievements, discoveries, and historical events were actually deeply interconnected, forming the basis of modern society, technology, and medicine. "Burke showed how seemingly unconnected scientific achievements and discoveries made it with historical events. And it turns out that they were actually connected, but not in ways you'd have ever expected." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_Pi7JLwAeOI
**Consensus as a Popularity Metric** : Evaluating an act's popularity based on its place in the public's consciousness and the concept of "consensus" may be a more appropriate metric than sales in the current music landscape. "Well, maybe a better way to evaluate an act's popularity is its place in the public's consciousness. And this is where we need to examine the concept of consensus." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Consequence of John Frusciante's Drug Addiction** : During his severe heroin and cocaine addiction, John Frusciante experienced "cocaine psychosis," a condition brought on by excessive use of coke and crack binges lasting for days. This mental state manifested as hallucinations, such as seeing "snakes in his eyes," leading him to make bizarre calls to figures like Perry Farrell. "He later learned that the term for this was cocaine psychosis brought on by way too much coke and crack pinches that would last for days." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TESrEzVHLCc
**Consequence of Removing Shoes During Flight** : During the Learjet 60 crash, Travis Barker had, unusually, taken off his shoes when he took his seat. This seemingly minor detail had severe consequences: when he became covered in jet fuel and caught fire, his fuel-soaked socks continued to burn on his feet and legs, exacerbating his injuries significantly. "This one time, this one time, he took a shoes off when he took a seat. And that meant that fuel soaked his socks and they just kept burning and burning." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q-QP7ePxkQE
**Conservative Approach of Talent Shows** : Popular television talent competitions like American Idol, The Voice, X Factor, and even musical-drama series like Glee are noted for promoting a very conservative and technically polished approach to singing. This emphasis on conventional vocal perfection might make it difficult for artists with truly unusual or experimental voices to succeed in such platforms today. "Idol and the voice and X Factor and Glee all push a very conservative approach to singing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7_ZmZxnSOnE
**Conservative Power Shift (UK, May 4, 1979)** : The "winter of discontent" led to the shattering of the left's political power in Britain, resulting in the Conservative party taking over. Margaret Thatcher became Prime Minister on May 4, 1979, and her stringent policies, though aimed at economic control, did not endear her to British punks, ensuring that a significant portion of British music remained highly political and critical of her for years. "The winter of discontent shattered the left's hold on power in Britain and the conservatives took over. Margaret Fatcher became Prime Minister on May 4, 1979." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Consolidation of Record Labels** : Over time, the music industry saw a significant consolidation of record labels. In the early 1960s, there were at least 3,000 independent labels in America alone. As the industry matured, bigger labels either acquired smaller ones, merged, or went out of business, reducing the number of major labels to six or seven by the 1970s, and eventually down to just three (Universal, Sony, Warner) in the present day. "In the early 1960s, there were at least 3,000 such labels in existence in America alone. But as the music industry grew, bigger labels either swallowed up smaller ones merged with each other or just plain went out of business." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dDcD8kg0wEk
**Constraints on Pleasing Music** : Despite a vast number of potential note combinations, "you just can't stick a bunch of tones and semi-tones together and expect them to sound good." Music needs to be "pleasing to the ear and has to reach into the soul," which significantly narrows down the number of musically viable combinations, especially within specific genres like rock. "Music has to sound pleasing to the ear and has to reach into the soul, right?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rfObVld9rIE
**Consumer Reaction to Early CDs** : The initial experience of hearing CDs caused people to "freak out" and "loved it" because they were tired of the poor quality, static, skips, and pops of contemporary vinyl. "the first time the people heard CDs, they freaked out. They loved it. We were tired of bad quality vinyl. We couldn't abandon it fast enough." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Consumer-Grade Gear Rivals Pro Equipment** : The cost of making music videos dropped dramatically due to consumer-grade gear becoming as good as or even superior to the equipment once required for professional video shoots. This technological leap, exemplified by advances in smartphone cameras, freed artists from punishing budgets. "Meanwhile, the cost of making a music video dropped dramatically thanks to consumer grade gear that was as good as or better than the gear that was once required to make a pro shot video." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Contemporary Challenges for Canadian Musicians** : Despite past successes, Canadian musicians face significant challenges in the modern era, including setbacks from COVID-19, gentrification and rising real estate prices making it difficult to find places to live, work, rehearse, and perform, and the struggle to earn a living wage in the streaming era. Additionally, the absence of Cancon quotas on streaming platforms is theorized to make it harder for Canadians to discover Canadian music. "And yes, gentrification and rising real estate prices are making it tougher for musicians to find places to live and work and rehearse and perform. And yes, there were huge, huge problems when it comes to musicians earning a living wage in the streaming era." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6PGgNCaW4xo
**Contemporary Record Store Experience** : Despite changes in the music industry, record stores remain valuable spaces for browsing, shopping, and cultural engagement. Visitors can learn by exploring the racks, observing other patrons, and conversing with staff, who often work for the love of music rather than profit. "Although record stores aren't what they used to be, there are still fantastic places to hang out, browse, and shop." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jD1dWxNLvfE
**Continued Presence of Heritage Acts** : A significant factor explaining why 21st-century acts struggle to reach the same heights as older ones is the continued activity of first and second-generation rock bands (e.g., The Stones, Pink Floyd members, U2, Eagles, The Who, Dylan, Paul McCartney) who still make records and tour, competing for audience attention and money. "Another issue that could explain why 21st century acts haven't been able to scale the same height as older ones is because those old acts are still with us." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Contradictory Word Pairs** : Several common word pairs are noted for their inherent contradiction despite frequent usage, including "Alone together," "Defining silence," "Definitely maybe," "Random order," "Walking Dead," and "Original copy." "Here's a list of words that should not go together, but do. Alone together. Defining silence. Definitely maybe. Random order, walking dead, original copy." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pE7TaWSUa-o
**Contrast of North American New Wave vs. British Post-Punk** : In North America, new wave was a commercial success, marketed as a milder form of punk, enriching labels and artists. In Britain, however, punk's initial intensity burnt out quickly by 1979, giving way to a more complex, experimental, and moodier sound called post-punk, reflecting the country's severe economic and social hardships. "In North America, these bands and these sounds were marketed as new wave, the less angry, less violent, less political, and more well-behaved cousin of punk rock. And it worked very well too. Lots of bands became famous and record labels got rich. However, things were different on the other side of the Atlantic." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9uTORLOm5Yg
**Controversial Music Choice for Donald Trump's Campaign** : Donald Trump's presidential campaign was criticized for its music selections, notably playing Creedence Clearwater Revival's "Fortunate Son." This song was originally inspired by the issue of wealthy individuals receiving draft deferments during the Vietnam War, a situation that mirrored Trump's own five draft deferments. The use of the song was further controversial as it occurred simultaneously with reports that Trump had referred to soldiers and war dead as "losers and suckers." "Whoever chose the music for his presidential campaign was awful. At one stop, they played fortune at sun by credence clearwater revival, which was inspired by the kids of rich and connected parents getting draft affirmants for Vietnam, which is exactly what happened with Donald Trump five times." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HoBx6s3JmVQ
**Controversy Over Political Campaign Music Use (2010s-2020s)** : A persistent controversy arose from artists opposing Donald Trump's use of their music at his political rallies. While venues typically hold blanket performing rights licenses covering such use, many artists, including R.E.M., Bruce Springsteen, Queen, and The Rolling Stones, filed cease and desist orders and lawsuits, unwilling to have their music associated with the campaign. "a lot of artists do not want their music being associated with Trump. Many complained, all the way to filing cease and desist orders and lawsuits." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GYUuTbOvyak
**Cool Britannia Collapse** : The Cool Britannia movement, tied to the Britpop era, ultimately collapsed due to factors including overexposure of artists, public boredom with the style, and widespread drug use within the music scene. "Cool Britannia collapsed under the weight of overexposure, boredom, and drugs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vzyMSSlD7Yg
**Cool Britannica / Swing and London Feature** : Even America's *Vanity Fair* magazine felt compelled to run a feature story on "Cool Britannica" and "Swing and London," indicating the widespread cultural impact and international recognition of the Britpop era. "Even America's Vanity Fair magazine felt compelled to do a feature story on Cool Britannica and Swing and London." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5q5EnZj1FwE
**Copyright - Legal Concept and History** : Copyright is a legal concept from the 15th-century printing press era, granting the right to copy an intellectual work. Unauthorized copying is piracy, making copyright a foundational concept of Western law. "Copyright is a legal concept that goes back centuries to the days of the first printing press in the 15th century. If you wanted to copy something, you had to secure permission the right to copy from the author. If you didn't, that's called piracy. Copyright is one of the foundational concepts of Western law." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Copyright Infringement** : This is a legal charge that can be brought against someone for unauthorized use of copyrighted material, as seen in the Mark Anofrio vs. Trent Reznor case. "He charged Trent with copyright infringement and unfair competition." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wYD_dIvYY5o
**Copyright Infringement Trials - "Note by Note" Comparison** : In copyright infringement trials, the judge and jury do not simply listen to recordings. Instead, "both sides get deep into music theory with their arguments," and the decision relies solely on "the notes that make up the song," as plotted on sheet music. "The only thing that matters are just the notes, how the song looks when it's plotted out on sheet music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rfObVld9rIE
**Copyright Infringement Trials - Production vs. Notes** : During copyright trials, while recordings "may sound the same," this introduces "other elements like production techniques and outboard effects," which change the "nature of the song." However, these elements are disregarded, and "the only thing that matters are just the notes." "Now, the recording may sound the same, but that introduces other elements like production techniques and outboard effects, which changes the nature of the song." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rfObVld9rIE
**Copyright Limitations** : Certain musical elements are not protected by copyright, including song titles, rhythms and beats (e.g., the "Bo Diddley beat" or the "shave and a haircut, two bits" rhythm), and chord progressions. This means these elements can be used by different artists without legal repercussions. "One title is for one. If you wanted to, you could write a piano ballad and call it smells like teen spirit and there is nothing anybody could do." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4cZfegexwwk
**Copyright Material Resolution** : The widespread issue of illegally uploaded copyrighted material on YouTube was eventually resolved through a combination of legal efforts and technological advancements. "Now all that was eventually sorted out with lawyers and technology." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Copyright Recapture for Artists** : Many new artists initially sign deals that assign the copyrights to their material, both songs and master recordings, to others. However, through persistent renegotiations over time, it is possible for artists to regain ownership of these crucial intellectual properties. "And we were able to recapture all the copyrights of the songs, which had been, you know, we did the usual sort of new artist deals at the beginning." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Copyright and Master Ownership in Licensing** : An artist stands to earn "big dollars" from a licensing deal if they own both the copyright to the song (publishing rights) and the master recording. However, these rights are commonly shared with a music publisher and a record company, respectively. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Cordiospinal Excitability (Urge to Dance)** : A song featuring a compelling beat or groove can promote something known as "cordiospinal excitability," which is essentially the urge to dance. This physiological response involves pumping extra blood to the legs and changes to heart rate and breathing patterns, allowing the body to instinctively sync with the music. "if a song has a good beat or a good groove, it promotes something called cordiospinal excitability, otherwise known as the urge to dance." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**Corduroy Road Explanation** : A "corduroy road" is a historical type of road construction where planks of wood, pieces of timber, or logs are laid across ground that would otherwise be too muddy or boggy to support a proper road. This term is explained in the context of understanding specific lyrics within The Tragically Hip's song "At the Hundredth Meridian." "Well, these were roads made by laying planks of wood or pieces of tinder or logs across ground that would be otherwise too muddy or boggy to construct a real road." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Vs1YyXgLpTE
**Core Business of Record Stores** : A record store's fundamental business model involves securing physical space, amassing inventory, employing staff, and undertaking all necessary activities to sell recorded music on behalf of record labels. This venture inherently carries substantial risk, as predicting sales success beyond major superstar releases is difficult. "It acquires space, a masses inventory, hires people, and does whatever it has to do to sell recorded music on behalf of record labels." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jD1dWxNLvfE
**Coronavirus Impact on Moshing** : The coronavirus pandemic introduced a new, invisible deterrent to moshing. The idea of close physical contact with sweaty strangers and exposure to bodily fluids made the activity unappealing, potentially marking a temporary or permanent end to moshing until widespread immunity is achieved. "The idea of slamming up against sweaty strangers and all their bodily fluids and spit isn't the kind of thing that sounds very good anymore." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KMx4bmq8o2Y
**Corporate Influence on Music Industry** : Trent Reznor criticized the music industry for its corporate, pre-packaged, and lifeless approach to music, contrasting it with what he saw as the dead spirit of rock and roll. He expressed disdain for labels pushing for "radio-friendly" songs and making creative compromises for commercial gain, stating he would rather not be a band if it meant becoming a "folk commercial." "I think most of the music that's out right now is pretty more in-between. Safe and pretty prepackaged and corporate and lifeless." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=M3nUQtja0qc
**Correct Plural of "Vinyl"** : The correct plural form of the word "vinyl" is "vinyl," akin to "deer." Referring to multiple records as "vinyls" is considered incorrect and a tell-tale sign of an inexperienced collector or "newbie." "The plural of vinyl is like the plural of deer. You have a single deer named Bambi or whatever, and you also had a herd of deer, not deers. You also have a piece of vinyl, a single record, and also a collection of vinyl." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2g4QSREzpw
**Cosmological Big Bang Comparison for Early Rock** : The emergence of rock music in the early 1950s is likened to the cosmological Big Bang, signifying its explosive and foundational impact on the musical landscape. "When beginners as spark in the early 1950s turned out to be the musical equivalent of the cosmological Big Bang." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tdTs-4Irv8c
**Cosmology Analogy to Punk Rock** : The Big Bang theory, describing the universe's origin from an infinitely dense singularity 16 billion years ago, is used as an analogy for punk rock in the mid-1970s. Punk is seen as a "musical big bang" whose ideas and attitudes spread out and coalesced into new musical "life forms." "Sometime around 16 billion years ago, there was this infinitely dense and infinitely tiny thing called a singularity... If we look at the punk rock of the middle 1970s, we can think of this as a musical big bang." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9uTORLOm5Yg
**Cost and Time Investment of Tattoos** : Tattoos are not cheap; Goldfinger drummer Darren Fyfer estimated spending between $30,000 to $50,000 on his own tattoos. The time spent in the tattoo chair can also be extensive, with Fyfer estimating a cumulative week of tattooing, ranging from one to eight hours per session depending on size and complexity. "They're not cheap. No, they're not. Well over, maybe 50 grand. You're kidding. All over, yeah. Maybe 30, 35. Okay. And how long do you think you spent in a chair? Another great question. I don't know how to answer it. If I had to venture a guess, I would say probably a week." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jZeMmPekKMQ
**Cost of Clearing "Two Tickets to Paradise" for "The Office"** : A joke in the American version of "The Office," where Michael Scott quotes lyrics from Eddie Money's song "Two Tickets to Paradise" ("I got two tickets to Paradise, pack your bags and we leave the day after tomorrow"), incurred a cost of $60,000 to clear the rights for television broadcast. "That joke costs sixty thousand dollars to clear for you some television." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HoBx6s3JmVQ
**Countdown to Rocket Launches** : The tradition of a countdown to a rocket launch, seemingly obvious, was actually adopted by NASA from a 1929 German silent film titled "Fraumund" (Women in the Moon). This film, considered an early serious sci-fi movie, featured a countdown from six for its rocket launches. "NASA took the idea from a 1929 silent film called Fraumund, which translates from German as Women in the Moon." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**Courtney Love's Stripper Past** : Prior to her mainstream musical career, Courtney Love reportedly worked as a stripper, with her activities spanning from Seattle to Japan. This detail is presented as a widely known fact rather than a secret. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CkSIErb0hxY
**Cover Song Permission and Royalties** : In the music industry, artists typically do not need to ask the original composer's permission to cover a song. Instead, they must give proper credit to the original artist so that royalties can be paid, a system covered by the concept of a "blanket license." "When it comes to covering songs, you don't have to ask permission of the original artist." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yuA9Xor8vfw
**Crapulence** : A real word that describes the sick feeling experienced after eating or drinking too much. "And the word, crapulence, is real. It's that sick feeling that you get when you eat or drink too much." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial Share-A-Like License** : This license was used by Trent Reznor for Nine Inch Nails' instrumental album "Ghosts I–IV," released in 2008. It allowed anyone to use the album's content for non-profit purposes, encouraging other composers. However, when a Dutch beatmaker remixed "34 Ghosts 4" and sold it, and Lil Nas X then used it for the commercially successful "Old Town Road," it constituted a violation. This led to Trent Reznor and Atticus Ross receiving official songwriting credits and sharing in "Old Town Road"'s chart records, including its 19 weeks at number one on the Billboard Hot 100. "And in an interesting twist, Ghosts 1-4 was released under a Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial Share-A-Like License. This meant that anyone could use anything on the album for anything at all as long as it was not for profit." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DKdF8NgF5Bw
**Creative Control vs. Commercial Demands** : The narrative frequently contrasts the desire for artistic expression and creative freedom with the commercial demands and statistical analyses imposed by some record label executives to engineer hit songs. The second iteration of Big Wreck was notably freed from these pressures by SRO Anthem. "the statistics say that a hit should essentially you should hit the chorus before, you know, all that. It was just like, we're just making music here." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QIA7V4PxCdM
**Creative Differences Leading to Band Breakup** : When individual band members develop new musical ideas that diverge from the group's established vision, it can lead to "fractures and tension and confusion," often resulting in a band's breakup. Such splits are frequently attributed to "creative differences." "The band can break up using the ever popular creative differences issue" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-f9jhqAHkQo
**Creative Plateau in Artist Careers** : A common challenge artists face is reaching a "creative plateau," where they either exhaust their creative ideas and begin to repeat themselves, or a group dynamic shifts significantly, leading to a situation where the band effectively becomes a solo project, as seen with The Police or The Pretenders. "Most artists run out of steam somewhere along the way. They either reach a creative plateau and just start repeating themselves or a group might become a solo singer..." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Creativity Versus Schedule** : The nature of creative work, particularly in music, defies rigid scheduling; true creativity and inspiration are spontaneous, contrasting sharply with the predictable, assembly-line production model of manufacturing. "You cannot be brilliant on a schedule. Creativity and inspiration happens when it happens." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vt6DVj7S6nk
**Creators' Rights and AI Training Data (N/A)** : A central concern in the AI music landscape is creators' rights, specifically regarding compensation for original works used to train AI programs. Since AI, while intelligent, is not inherently creative but learns from existing human-made material, there's a strong argument that those who created the training data deserve recognition and financial remuneration. "Should someone be compensated if work that they created or own is used to train an AI program?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Creed Syndrome** : The "Creed Syndrome" describes a phase in the rock cycle where new artists appear to be derivative copies of original acts, making the music feel too safe and watered down, contributing to the decline of alt-rock in the late 1990s. "Green also entered the phase of any rock cycle where the new artist coming out seemed like a copy of a copy of a copy of the original, too derivative, too safe, too watered down. We can call this the Creed Syndrome." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7JP2tjJibTQ
**Crime Buster – Comic Book Inspiration** : The boy depicted in the painting on Rage Against the Machine's "Evil Empire" album cover is an altered version of a character from a 1940s/50s comic book series called Crime Buster. The original painting, inspired by this hero, featured a "C" on the boy's chest, which was subsequently changed to an "E" for the album artwork. "There was a comic book hero called Crime Buster." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Criminality and Mental Health as Rock Star Behaviors** : Some rock stars live in their own reality where normal societal rules don't apply, engaging in behaviors that are thoughtless, selfish, overly audacious, negligent, self-destructive, incredibly violent, and even criminal. For some, this is a lifestyle; for others, it's linked to illness, uncontrolled passions, negligence, or misadventure. "They live in their own reality, where the normal rules of society just don't apply." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5vKSjpvVQa8
**Criminalization of Homosexuality (Historical Context)** : In the past, it was legally criminal to be anything other than heterosexual. Any indication of being non-straight could lead to severe trouble, with career suicide being the least of one's concerns. "Once upon a time, and really not that long ago, it was illegal, criminal, in fact, to be anything other than heterosexual." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hswtuenFwvM
**Critical Importance of Metadata for Streaming** : Accurate and comprehensive metadata is paramount in the era of digital streaming. The more thoroughly a song is tagged with metadata, the easier it becomes to precisely identify that specific recording, ensuring it is correctly credited and that appropriate royalty payments are disbursed. "The better you tag your song, the more metadata you include in that file, the easier it will be to identify this exact recording, so that when it's streamed, it'll be lard correctly and you'll be paid." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Critical Importance of Publishing Ownership** : Owning one's publishing rights is an extremely significant aspect of intellectual property in the music industry. Artists are frequently deceived into signing away these rights to record labels, managers, or producers, which can lead to major disputes and financial exploitation. "An ownership of one's publishing is a very, very, very big deal." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Criticism of Streaming Payment Model** : A major criticism of the current streaming payment system is that it disproportionately benefits established and famous artists, effectively marginalizing smaller artists who struggle to earn revenue. "The big criticism here is that the system favors the already rich and the already famous, and the smaller artists get squeezed out of any revenues." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Critique of Major Record Companies** : Stephen Patrick Morrissey of The Smiths expressed distress that pop music in the early 1980s had become "vacuous" and that "major record companies" had once again "hijacked" music, reflecting a common sentiment among independent artists. "According to Morrissey, music had once again been hijacked by the major record companies." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9uTORLOm5Yg
**Critique of Music Streaming** : Music streaming, while convenient, is described as "organized noise" that lacks context, depth, and background information about songs, artists, albums, scenes, sounds, history, or recording processes. This absence makes music less interesting compared to having rich contextual information. "streaming is magic, but it's really organized noise." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bI6TTA-fn7c
**Crocodile Dundee (Film Investment)** : Michael Hutchence made an investment in this small Australian film, which later proved to be quite profitable. "Michael had also invested in a small Australian film called Crocodile Dundee that turned a nice profit." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Cross-Generational Fanbase for Heritage Acts** : Audiences for heritage acts now include both original fans seeking to relive their youth and younger people who discovered the music through streaming, parents, or older siblings' collections. "But if you look around at any one of these shows, there's a lot of younger people too." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Cross-Generational Music Consumption** : In the 2010s, music became significantly more cross-generational than ever before, with audiences at shows by bands like Metallica and Soundgarden spanning a wide range of ages, from "grey hairs to preteens." While generation gaps still existed, it became increasingly common for parents and children to share a love for the same music and artists. "Music is now far more cross-generational than it's ever been." "But now, more than ever, it's very common for a parent and their child to share a love for the same music, the same act." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HSvgRyGiOpE
**Crossover of Music and True Crime** : There is an intersection between music and true crime, which is explored in a separate podcast focusing on "Crime and Mayhem in the music industry." "All episodes involve the intersection of music with true crime." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5vKSjpvVQa8
**Cultural Enforcement of Short Hair (1930s-1950s)** : In the West during the 1930s, 40s, and 50s, short hair was culturally enforced for white men. Those who maintained long hair were often labeled as effeminate, subversive, or unhygienic. "Short hair was culturally enforced in the West. White men who didn't keep things trim were considered everything from effeminate to subversive to unhygienic." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bferkEWvJg0
**Cultural Impact of Pop Albums** : Some albums achieve a level of success that extends beyond critical acclaim and commercial sales, becoming cultural phenomena that have a lasting impact for years, even decades, shaping public consciousness and perception. "An album is a critical success. It turns into a commercial smash and every once in a while, every once in a long, long while, it turns into a cultural phenomenon with an impact that last years, maybe decades." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VR5Fncdc2kQ
**Cultural Storms in Music** : Musical, social, political, and demographic conditions converge to create trends that capture imagination. These storms typically last about six years, starting with a two-year build-up, followed by a 12-18 month frenzied peak, and then a long, slow decline. "These storms tend to last about six years." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vzyMSSlD7Yg
**Cumulative CD Sales Reaching Half a Billion (1989)** : By 1989, the cumulative sales of compact discs had reached an impressive half a billion units worldwide, demonstrating the rapid growth and adoption of the new format. "And by 1989, half a billion disks had been sold." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Cup Holders in Venues for Increased Sales** : The widespread presence of cup holders in amphitheaters, arenas, and stadiums serves a purpose beyond convenience: they are primarily designed to enable attendees to purchase and hold two drinks simultaneously. This encourages greater alcohol sales, as fans can store a second beer while consuming their first. "They're there so you can buy two drinks at once and have some place to put your second beer while you drink your first." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Curly Toes (unknown date)** : An unknown artist, given the title "Curly Toes," was featured on the 2000 compilation album "Songs in the Key of Z," which focused on outsider music. The song was reportedly found on a tape by someone digging in a dumpster, and despite being on a released album, no leads regarding the artist's identity emerged for a decade after the album's release. "It's claimed that this was found on a tape by someone who is digging in a dumpster." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UsPoPUByjYw
**Current AI Music Programs by Tech Giants (N/A)** : Major tech companies are actively developing AI music programs: Meta with Music Gen, Google with Lyria and Music LM, OpenAI with MusicNet, and Stability AI with Stable Audio. These programs are continuously advancing their capabilities in generating both music and vocals. "Meta has an AI and music program called Music Gen. Google has Lyria and Music LM. Open Eye is still working on things like MusicNet and Stability AI has a program called Stable Audio." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
