**Current Cancon Quota** : The minimum Canadian content requirement for radio stations today is 35%. Some stations voluntarily exceed this minimum, incorporating quotas of 40% or even 45% into their licenses. "Today, the minimum requirement is 35%. Some stations voluntarily wrote into their licenses a percent higher than that, 40%, or even 45%." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Current Landscape of Catalog Buyers** : Approximately a dozen well-capitalized companies are currently active in buying song catalogs, including Primary Wave, Concord, Round Hill, Iconic Artists, Reach Music, Kilometer Music Group, and Iconic Last. Major label publishing arms (Universal, Sony, Warner) are also active, often acquiring catalogs from their long-standing signings. "approximately a dozen companies buying up song catalogues these days, which primary wave, Concord, Round Hill, iconic artists, reach music, kilometer music group, and iconic last. That's just a short list. Then you add in the publishing arms of labels like Universal and Sony and Warner, who were also scooping up catalogues, usually of artists who have been their signings for years." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Current Market for Vinyl Records** : In the present day, buying vinyl records is considered "hot and cool again." This trend extends beyond new releases to vintage records found in record shows, specialized stores, and even mainstream retailers like Urban Outfitters. Traditional brick-and-mortar stores, Amazon (the biggest seller globally), and record labels issuing expensive box sets almost universally stock and include high-quality vinyl. Artists now recognize vinyl as an essential format for new album and single releases. "Everyone knows by now that buying vinyl records is hot and cool again. And it's not just new releases... Vinyl is everywhere. Traditional bricks and mortar record stores, once it still exists, stock vinyl again. Amazon is the biggest seller of vinyl in the world. Record labels are issuing expensive box sets that almost always come with high quality vinyl pieces. And it's to the point where almost any artist knows that if they're going to cover the marketplace with a new release, they have to put their album or even their single out on vinyl." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VlIErdSSVOM
**Custody Battle** : He launched a highly publicized custody battle for his three daughters with Paula Yates: Fifi, Trixabelle, Peaches, and Pixie. "Meanwhile Bob Geldoff launched a custody battle for the three children he fathered with Paula. There was Fifi, Trixabel, Peaches, and Pixie." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Custody and Adoption** : Following Paula Yates's death, he gained custody of all her children and adopted Heavenly Hiraani Tiger Lily, Michael Hutchence's daughter. "Bob Geldoff took custody of all the kids and adopted Tiger Lily, who was Michael's daughter." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Cycles of Men's Long Hair in History** : The fashion for men's hair length has cycled for hundreds of years, predating the rock era. Ancient Greek men, for example, could have long hair without criticism. Periodically, long hair would fall out of style, prompting critique, such as the 1842 London Saturday Journal article criticizing long-haired musicians. "Hair length on men has gone in cycles for hundreds and hundreds of years. Long hair musicians has gone together for centuries." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bferkEWvJg0
**Cycles of Music Revivals** : The universe, including music, moves in cycles where things are born, build up, peak, and then fade into a period of stasis or hibernation before something triggers a rebirth. This cyclical nature explains periodic revivals of certain genres and scenes. "The universe moves in cycles. Things are born, build up, peak, and fade away. But they don't necessarily die." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gkLMwrthZ9s
**Cynical Approach to Christian Rock** : Some musicians cynically view Christian rock as an easy path to financial success, believing that by creating simple songs about loving Jesus, they can tap into a large built-in Christian audience eager to purchase their music. "If we just play songs about how much we love Jesus, all the Christians will buy our craft." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g-tBn8dsSqw
**Cynthia Plaster Caster – Rock Star Penis Mold Collection Since 1968, Kiss Song, Documentary** : Chicago-born Cynthia Albritten (known professionally as Cynthia Plaster Caster) developed her unique hobby after a high school art class assignment in plaster casting around 1968. Her specialty is making plaster molds of the penises of willing rock musicians; confirmed subjects include Jimi Hendrix and Jello Biafra of the Dead Kennedys, among many others. She also casts willing women's breasts. She maintains records of those who attempted but were not "up to the task." Kiss wrote "Plaster Caster" about her on their *Love Gun* album. A documentary film about her was made, and she continues to exhibit her work in art galleries. "Her thing was getting plaster casts of rock star penises." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lyYsTon_NSc
**Cynthia Plastercaster's Legacy and Death** : Cynthia Albritton, famously known as Cynthia Plastercaster, was an infamous groupie from the late 1960s and early 1970s who gained notoriety for creating plaster casts of musicians' genitals, including Jimi Hendrix. She died of cerebral vascular disease on April 21st at the age of 74. "Cynthia Albritten, better known as Cynthia Plastercaster and infamous groupie of the late 60s and early 70s, who made her name by creating plaster casts of musicians' junk. That included Jimmy Hendrix. She died of cerebral vascular disease on April 21 at the age of 74." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sdPeii-8Ysw
**D22 Club - Beijing Punk Venue** : D22 was a specific punk club located on the "outskirts of Beijing, China," where legendary record executive Seymour Stein went to listen to local Chinese punk and alternative bands around 1 AM, demonstrating his interest in emerging global music scenes. "We're going up to this punk club called D22 to see some of the local talent." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hjProJd_JrM
**DAB Digital Audio Broadcasting – Norway 99.7%, Canada and US 0%** : Digital Audio Broadcasting is an entirely separate set of frequencies broadcasting MP2 digital signals, developed in Europe as a replacement for analog AM and FM. While Norway has achieved 99.7% population coverage, Switzerland 99.5%, and Germany 98%, the United States refused to adopt the European standard (citing interference with military frequencies), meaning Canada and the US have zero DAB coverage. European countries satisfied with DAB have begun shutting down their AM and FM transmitters entirely, leaving North America technologically behind. As a compromise, the US developed "HD Radio" — a trademark (like Kleenex) for digital signals piggybacked on existing FM broadcasts, standard in cars since around 2012 but attracting negligible public awareness. "Ask a person on the street about HD radio and they'll go, what?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iyGUXdDqIlI
**DARPA's Social Media Analysis of Pop Stars (2014)** : In 2014, the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA), based within the Pentagon, received funding to analyze tweets from Lady Gaga and Justin Bieber. The agency was specifically looking for linguistic cues, patterns of information flow, and the detection of sentiment or opinion within information spread through social media, suspecting that some tweets might be fake and used to influence public opinion. "In 2014 DARPA, the defense advanced research projects agency, which is based inside the Pentagon, was funded to analyze tweets sent out by Lady Gaga and Justin Bieber." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**DIY (Do It Yourself) Vibe** : Tom DeLonge's side project Box Car Racer was noted for having an "old DIY vibe" reminiscent of early Blink 182, which resonated with Mark Hoppus. "It had that old DIY vibe of early Blink." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tkLRJiOBu0
**DIY Ethic and Indie Labels Popularization** : The hardcore scene was instrumental in popularizing the concept of forming one's own record label (the "do-it-yourself" ethic), as many bands resorted to this to release their music due to lack of major label interest. "It was through hardcore that the idea of forming your own record label was popularized." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PEHkKfmmDiI
**DIY Ethic as Inspiration** : The radical act of two individuals in Manchester starting their own record label, as seen with the Buzzcocks and New Hormones, served as a massive inspiration for countless others determined to get underground music heard. In the four years following this event, over 900 independent records were released in Britain using a similar self-release model. "This was a massive inspiration for other people who were determined to get this underground music heard. In the next four years, more than 900 indie records were released in Britain this way." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VOv5mT1dJd8
**DIY Ethic in Music Strengthened by Technology** : The do-it-yourself (DIY) ethic in music, particularly within genres like punk, has become stronger due to technology democratizing and decentralizing music. While record labels once assisted with promotion, the intense competition and "noise" in the modern music landscape necessitate artists working harder than ever to get noticed independently. "In fact, I think that DIY ethic has become stronger since technology democratized and decentralized all of music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HSvgRyGiOpE
**DJ Ramone's Nintendo 64 Album – All 15 Songs Fit Within 8 Megabyte Cartridge** : Hamburg-based DJ Ramone released an album called R64 available only on a Nintendo 64 cartridge. All 15 songs had to fit within the 8-megabyte capacity. He has previously released albums for Super NES and Game Boy as well. "If you wanted to play it, you had to find a functioning console." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**DMZ Visit - North/South Korea** : While visiting the South Korean side of the DMZ, the speaker was able to technically step onto the North Korean side by entering a hut where negotiations take place. A conference table within the hut had a line down the middle representing the border, allowing one to be on the "other side" while remaining inside the hut. "Well, I went through the South Korean side and went on the South Korean DMZ side and there's these huts in the middle where they do all the negotiations and there's a conference table with a line down the middle that represents the border between North and South Korea. And as long as you stay in the hut, you could go on the other side of the conference table. Oh, yeah. So technically, I have been to South Korea. Amazing. Very cool." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4xxgPX-aR90
**DNA Testing Advancement - Cold Case Solution** : The ability to extract crucial information from body fluids and residues through DNA testing, combined with the establishment of international databases, became instrumental in solving cold cases that were previously untraceable, such as the Mia Zapata murder. "The problem was that in 1993 the technology for extracting the necessary information from DNA wasn't available yet. By this time all kinds of important information could be extracted from any kind of body fluid or residue. There was also an international database that allowed law enforcement to follow up on cold cases by comparing DNA samples." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZcYKZ5ovoy4
**Daft Punk – Named After a Melody Maker Review That Called Their Previous Band "Daft Punky Thrash"** : They previously recorded under the name Darlin'. A bad review in the UK music weekly Melody Maker described their sound as "daft punky thrash." They killed Darlin' and adopted the insult as their new name. "After that they killed Darlin and went with Daft Punk, which you have to admit is a much better name." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Daily Mail (1920) Criticism of Dancing** : An editorial in the *Daily Mail* in 1920 lamented the death of "graceful dancing" in ballrooms, specifically condemning the Foxtrot and One-step. The editorial suggested these dances expressed "ugly manners," representing a "modern hiltor skelter" and a regression to "Polynesian round the campfire with the Tom Tom" — a racially charged critique. "Graceful dancing is dead in ballrooms. We conclude that Foxtrot and one step, Expressor Audit Ugly Manners, our modern hiltor skelter, they show the wheel coming again full circle, back to the Polynesian round the campfire with the Tom Tom." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Daily Mail (1920) Linking Dancing to Immorality** : The 1920 *Daily Mail* editorial further linked the perceived decline in dancing manners to broader social issues, questioning whether "daily cases in the divorce court and elsewhere lead us to suppose that manners have become uncontrollable like the Foxtrot." This illustrates a pattern of blaming new cultural expressions for societal moral decay. "Don't the daily cases in the divorce court and elsewhere lead us to suppose that manners have become uncontrollable like the Foxtrot, which images them on the ballroom floor." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Daily Mirror (1956) Rock and Roll Demise Prediction** : In 1956, the *Daily Mirror* confidently declared "Rock and roll is out of date, old-fashioned." The paper suggested that "think-a-fold music" (skiffle) was the new coming craze, as London's espresso bars were transforming into modern versions of old-time music halls, a sign they interpreted as rock's passing. "This is from the Daily Mirror in 1956. Rock and roll is out of date, old-fashioned." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Daily Music for Well-being** : A survey of 7,500 music fans by Paris-based streaming service D-Zer suggests that listening to at least 78 minutes of music daily is necessary for proper mental and physical well-being. "it's necessary to listen to at least 78 minutes of music a day to ensure proper mental and physical well-being." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=72Lei7uug8c
**Dan Brown Plagiarism Charges** : Author Dan Brown was subjected to legal proceedings at least twice, facing charges of plagiarizing elements of his novel "The Da Vinci Code." This illustrates that accusations of plagiarism are not unique to the music industry. "Or how about Dan Brown who was hauled through the courts at least twice on charges of plagiarizing bits of the Da Vinci code?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L09R2evjyMc
**Dan Rather Assault (October 1986)** : In October 1986, CBS News anchor Dan Rather was assaulted on Park Avenue in New York City by two well-dressed white men who repeatedly punched and kicked him while yelling, "Kenneth, what's the frequency?" The men disappeared and were never caught, initially leading to accusations that Rather fabricated the incident, though a doorman witnessed it. The attacker was later identified as William Tager, a mentally ill man who believed media, specifically Rather and CBS News, were beaming signals into his head. "Suddenly two well-dressed white men get in his way. The tall one with a mustache looks at Rather and says, Kenneth, what's the frequency?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cM3-tINKdvo
**Dan Rather's Assault (1986)** : In October 1986, CBS News Anchor Dan Rather was attacked on Park Avenue in New York City by two well-dressed white men. One repeatedly asked, "Kenneth, what's the frequency?" while punching and kicking him. Rather ran into a nearby building lobby, where a doorman witnessed the attack before the assailants disappeared. They were never caught, and their true motive was never discovered. Although some accused Rather of fabricating the incident, the doorman confirmed his account. "CBS News Anchor Dan Rather is walking down Park Avenue in New York City after having dinner at a friend's house. Suddenly, two well-dressed white men get in his way." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4PlieAEthSg
**Dancing Boosts Intelligence** : According to a medical study conducted in the UK, dancing has a positive effect on cognitive abilities. Engaging in activities like a single salsa lesson can significantly boost understanding, focus, and memory by as much as 18%. "According to a medical study in the UK, dancing makes you more intelligent. A single salsa lesson can boost your understanding focus and memory by as much as 18%." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**Dancing Mania (1920s)** : Following the Great War, the 1920s saw an "outbreak of dancing mania" characterized by much more enthusiastic dancing than what older generations were accustomed to. This new, more energetic style challenged established social norms regarding public dance. "Let me take you back to 1920 when there was an outbreak of dancing mania following the Great War." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Dangers of Stage Diving** : Stage diving, particularly when involving crowd participation, has led to serious injuries and even fatalities. An incident at an Everclear concert in 1997, where a professional football player dove into the crowd and crushed a fan, resulted in significant injuries and a $1.2 million lawsuit. "People have been seriously injured and have even died as a result of either diving or having someone fall on top of them." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bferkEWvJg0
**Danish Cinnamon Tradition** : In Denmark, if a person is 25 years old and single on Valentine's Day, their family and friends reportedly throw cinnamon at them. The origins or reasons for this tradition are not widely known. "If you're 25 years old and it's Valentine's Day and you're single, your family and friends are apparently supposed to throw cinnamon at you." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bferkEWvJg0
**Dante Ariola – Korn Co-Art Director** : Dante Ariola served as the co-art director for Korn's 1994 debut album photoshoot. He was the person responsible for casting the menacing, lobster-like shadow that features prominently on the album cover, contributing to its sinister atmosphere. "The guy casting the shadow was Dante Ariola, who was the co-art director on the shoot." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Dartmoor Wildlife Radio Station** : In rural England, there is a unique radio station located in the Dartmoor area that broadcasts no music. Instead, its programming consists entirely of the ambient sounds of wildlife and the local environment. "There is a radio station in rural England that does not play music. All it offers is the sound of wildlife and the local environment around the area of Dartmoor." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**Dassler Brothers Rivalry (Adidas and Puma)** : Adolf and Rudolf Dassler, who owned a shoe company together, split their business after World War II due to ideological differences (Rudolf being "a little more international socialism than Adolf"), leading to the creation of the rival brands Adidas and Puma. "At often Rudolph Dassler were good young Nazis who owned a shoe company, but Rudolph was a little more international socialism than Adolf, and after World War II, their company split into two. They became Adidas and Puma." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=S6qB5B1PdyA
**Data Analytics for Artist Careers** : Artists are encouraged to leverage streaming data analytics to advance their careers, as the industry has become a "moneyball" environment where insights from platforms like Spotify are crucial for identifying trends and opportunities. "Spotify and the rest of them have turned into moneyball." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Data-Driven Music Creation and Promotion** : Streaming data now dictates music culture, allowing for real-time identification of traction by genre, length, key, and chord progressions, and promoting a paradigm where engagement data, rather than inherent "goodness," drives music creation and promotion optimized for maximum revenue. "This data is also dictating the course of music culture. We can see what sorts of songs are getting traction at any given second." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**DataPlay Disk (Tiny Format)** : Another attempted format was DataPlay, a disk so minuscule that it was considered a choking hazard, indicating an ill-conceived design. "There was data play, a disk so tiny that was actually a choking hazard." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Daughter with Michael Hutchence** : She had a daughter, Heavenly Hiraani Tiger Lily, with Michael Hutchence. "Michael and Paula were the parents of a baby girl named Heveli, Herani, Tiger Lily." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Dave Alexander's Death** : Original Stooges bass player Dave Alexander contracted a severe case of pancreatitis due to his heavy drinking and died in a hospital at the age of 27 in February 1975, not long after the band's initial breakup. "Original bass player Dave Alexander contracted a serious case of pancreatitis due to his drinking. He died in hospital at age 27 in February of 1975." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=w-rihv544I8
**Dave Alexander's Search for The Beatles' Meaning** : Dave Alexander, the original bassist for The Stooges, dropped out of high school 45 minutes into his senior year. He then embarked on a motorcycle trip across the UK, on a personal quest "to find the true meaning of the Beatles." "And Dave dropped out of high school 45 minutes into a senior year and ended up riding a motorcycle across the UK on a search to find the true meaning of the Beatles." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=w-rihv544I8
**Dave Goldman – Blink-182 Photographer** : Dave Goldman was the photographer who directed the photoshoot for Blink-182's "Enema of the State" album cover on March 12, 1999. He later admitted to being completely oblivious to both Jeanine Lindemulder's profession as a porn star and the identity of Blink-182 at the time of the shoot. His spontaneous idea for including a rubber glove directly inspired the album's final title. "The photographer was Dave Goldman, who oversaw the shoot on March 12, 1999." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Dave Grohl's Admiration for Nandi Bushell** : Dave Grohl is described as a "huge, huge fan" of young drumming prodigy Nandi Bushell, indicating recognition from a prominent figure in rock music for her exceptional talent at a young age. "Dave Grohl is a huge, huge fan of Nandy Bushel." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9GHpC0kCgHY
**Dave Meyers as a Music Video Director** : Dave Meyers, a music video director with extensive experience, was the guest on the debut episode of the _Architects of Art_ podcast. He discussed his artistic philosophy and addressed broader societal issues like Black Lives Matter from his perspective as a white director in the black music world. "The first podcast that debut of the show was with Dave Myers. Another guy that's been in the game for a long, long time." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IIJySSo276k
**Dave Smith on Foo Fighters Road Crew** : Dave Smith was employed as a member of the Foo Fighters' road crew for a period. This detail was mentioned in the context of indicating that despite Dave Grohl's abrupt departure from his previous band Dane Bramage, not all personal connections were severed. "Dave Smith ended up working as a member of the Fouffleiters Road Crew for a time, so not all the bridges were burned." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8ynrwaUi-sA
**David Berkowitz (Son of Sam) as Subject of Songs** : In modern times, over two dozen songs have been written about David Berkowitz, the Son of Sam killer. Artists such as the Beastie Boys, Elliot Smith, and Lana Del Rey have contributed to this body of work, memorializing the notorious serial killer. "In modern times there are more than two dozen songs about David Berkowitz, the son of Sam Killer." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**David Bowie's Labyrinth Choreographer – Dr. Crusher from Star Trek** : In the 1986 film Labyrinth, the choreographer listed in the credits as "Cheryl McFadden" is actually Gates McFadden, known as Dr. Crusher on Star Trek: The Next Generation. She taught David Bowie his dance moves for the film. "Dr. Crusher of Star Trek once taught dance moves to David Bowie." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**David Cope's Experiments in Musical Intelligence (EMI)** : David Cope developed EMI, a computer program capable of scanning compositions by renowned composers such as J.S. Bach, Chopin, and Stravinsky to extract their stylistic data points and generate new pieces. EMI later released the album *Classical Music Composed by Computer, Experiments in Musical Intelligence* and even learned to compose ragtime in the style of Scott Joplin. "David Cope, the man behind this computer called EMI... had figured out a way for EMI to scan compositions by famous composers, draw out the styles from the data points, and then crank out a new piece that was a reasonable facsimile of the real thing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**David Cross - "10 A.M. Automatic" Video Director** : Comedian David Cross took on the role of director for the music video accompanying The Black Keys' single "10 A.M. Automatic." "the video was directed by comedian David Cross." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PmwfC4HR3_I
**David Milgaard - Wrongful Imprisonment** : David Milgaard was a Canadian individual who was wrongfully imprisoned, a story that inspired The Tragically Hip's song "Wheat Kings" from their album "Fully Completely." "Weekings, the story of the wrongfully imprisoned David Milgar." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oMBmdfrA47Q
**David Milgaard Wrongful Conviction** : David Milgaard, a Manitoba man, was wrongfully convicted at age 16 for the 1970 rape and murder of 20-year-old Saskatoon nursing assistant Gail Miller, based on false testimony from two friends. He was sentenced to life in prison on January 31, 1970, and served 21 years before his release, eventually being exonerated and cleared of all crimes two years later. Inquiries into the miscarriage of justice led to Larry Fisher, the landlord of one of the false witnesses, being arrested, convicted, and imprisoned for Miller's murder, where he died in 2015. "Although he's never mentioned by name, the tragically hip song Weep Kings is about David Milgard, a Manitoba man who was convicted for the rape and murder of a 20 year old Saskatoon nursing assistant named Gail Miller." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cM3-tINKdvo
**David Milgard's Wrongful Conviction** : David Milgard, a 16-year-old Manitoba man, was wrongfully convicted for the rape and murder of 20-year-old Saskatoon nursing assistant Gail Miller, whose body was found in a snowbank. Based on false testimony from two friends, Milgard was sentenced to life in prison on January 31, 1970, and remained incarcerated for 21 years before his release. He was fully exonerated and cleared of all crimes two years later. Inquiries into this miscarriage of justice led to the arrest and conviction of Larry Fisher, the landlord of one of the false witnesses, for Gail Miller's murder. Fisher died in jail in 2015. "David Milgard, a Manitoba man who was convicted for the rape and murder of a 20 year old Saskatoon nursing assistant named Gail Miller." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4PlieAEthSg
**David Wood's Role in Slamming (1977)** : David Wood, a roadie for the LA punk band The Weirdos, is a contender for originating slam dancing. In 1977, he began intentionally slamming into people at shows at the Roxy in Los Angeles, around the time The Weirdos released their single "Destroy All Music." "We might want a credit adude named David Wood. He was a roadie for an LA punk band called The Weirdos." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KMx4bmq8o2Y
**Dawson College Shooting (2006)** : On September 13, 2006, Kimveer Gill entered Dawson College in Montreal, where he killed one student and injured 19 others before committing suicide. Gill, a conspiracy theorist and 9/11 denier, was a "huge fan" of Megadeth and quoted their song "A Tout le Monde" in a suicide note on his blog. "On September 13th, 2006, more than 10 years later, Kim Viergear went into Dawson College in Montreal, killing one student and injuring 19 others before shooting himself." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Daylight Savings Time Invention** : The concept of daylight savings time, predating World War I, was originated by the great-great-grandfather of Coldplay's Chris Martin. "The great, great grandfather of Cold Placing or Chris Martin is the guy who came up with the concept of daylight savings time in the days before World War I." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QfiRRtsZoYU
**De-rockification of Major Music Festivals** : Major music festivals like Lollapalooza and Coachella have significantly evolved away from their original rock roots, particularly starting around 2008. Lollapalooza, initially a full-blown touring festival of alternative and rock bands in the 1990s, is now primarily a pop and hip-hop event. Similarly, Coachella, which began in 1999 with a focus on rock, shifted towards pop and hip-hop headliners due to a cultural shift in America, where these genres became dominant among the festival-going demographic. While both festivals still include token rock appearances, their primary lineup strategies cater to youth-driven preferences for pop and hip-hop to ensure ticket sales. "The evolution of two major major music festivals away from their rock roots. When La La Palosa was first conceived in 1990, it was a full-blown touring festival of Weirdos, featuring the best in left-of-center and alternative bands." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZzIqIydZTlA
**Deadline Magazine** : A British magazine from the late 1980s that featured comic strips and drawings by various English artists, including Jamie Hewlett, who co-created "Tank Girl" for the publication. "By the late 1980s, he was working for a British magazine called Deadline, which featured comic strips and drawings by a variety of English artists." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iVdTCqpd798
**Deals with Ticket Brokers/Scalpers** : Promoters, ticket sellers, and venues often have existing deals with ticket brokers, sometimes referred to as scalpers, who resell tickets on the secondary market. This practice allows the primary sellers to unload tickets, transferring financial risk to the secondary market, and records those tickets as sold, even though they haven't reached the end consumer yet. "The promoter, the ticket seller, and the venue may have existing deals with ticket brokers, companies that resell tickets on the secondary market." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Death (2014)** : One of Bob Geldof's daughters with Paula Yates, she tragically died of a heroin overdose in 2014 at the age of 25. "And as one final tragedy, Peaches, one of the daughters Geldoff had with Paula, died of a heroin overdose herself in 2014. She was 25." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Death (September 17, 2000)** : She died on September 17, 2000, at age 41, from a heroin overdose. Her death, which occurred on her daughter Pixie's 10th birthday with her four-year-old daughter Tiger Lily present, was attributed to snorting very pure heroin after two years of abstinence, leading to a lack of tolerance. "She died. It was September 17th, 2000, not quite three years after Michael passed away. It was on her daughter Pixie's 10th birthday. She was with her four-year-old daughter, Tiger Lily. She decided to snort some very pure heroin. And because she had no tolerance after being off the drug for two years, she owed deed and died. She was 41." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Death Cab for Cutie – 1967 British Novelty Song About a Woman Killed in a Taxi** : The Bonzo Dog Doodah Band recorded a song called "Death Cab for Cutie" on their 1967 album Gorilla, which was itself based on a tabloid headline about a woman killed in a taxi cab accident. "That in turn was based on a tabloid headline for a story about a woman who was killed in an accident involving a taxi." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Death Goths** : These are "purists" within the Goth scene who are faithful to the original musical, fashion, and lifestyle aesthetics established in the late 1970s. "And then there are the purists, the so-called death Goths." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**Death and Speculation** : His death led to widespread confusion, speculation, and conspiracy theories, which is compared to the aftermath of Michael Hutchence's death. "Think back to all the confusion and speculation and conspiracy theories that popped up in the wake of Kurt Cobain's death." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Death of Album Art – CD Shrank Canvas from 12-Inch to 5-Inch; Now Digital Means No Physical Canvas at All** : The 12x12-inch canvas of the LP shrank to the 5x5-inch booklet of the CD, though CDs allowed multi-page booklets to compensate. Now, as music goes fully digital and album sales continue to decline, the physical canvas for album art is essentially gone. Hundreds of billions of dollars that once went to music now go to DVDs, video games, streaming TV, smartphones, and other entertainment, leading to a dramatically declining need for album cover art. "We could be witnessing the death of an art that extends back nearly a century. And that would be sad." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JdSUHkXM1mQ
**Death of Brandon Lee (March 1994) – The Crow** : Brandon Lee, son of martial arts legend Bruce Lee, was accidentally shot and fatally wounded in the abdomen when an improperly loaded prop gun discharged during the filming of "The Crow." Despite his death, the movie was completed, and its soundtrack was released in late March 1994. "Brandon, if you remember, was accidentally shot when an improperly loaded prop gun went off, wounding him fatally in the abdomen." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TDwagoyMvck
**Death of Music Video Monoculture (Mid-90s)** : By the mid-1990s, the "monoculture of the music video," where all genres flowed uninterruptedly, was declared dead due to the alt-rock revolution and the rise of hip-hop. The industry fragmented into distinct "scenes," compelling video channels to devote special programs to specific genres, like *120 Minutes*, *Headbangers' Ball*, and *Yo MTV Raps*. "The monoculture of the music video was dead." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Decades as Momentum-Driven Periods** : Decades don't simply end when the calendar flips to a new year; they often carry momentum or a "hangover" that extends their influence for one to three years. For instance, the 1950s arguably continued until 1963, and the 1960s' influence stretched into 1972-ish. "Decades have momentum, sometimes a hangover, that carries things forward for a year or two or even three afterwards." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5Y6nHw5xx88
**Decimation of the Musician Middle Class** : The dramatic reduction in artist income from streaming and declining sales has "decimated the middle class of musicians," which historically served as the breeding ground for superstars, creating a gap between huge top acts and a large underclass of struggling performers. "This has decimated the middle class of musicians, the traditional breeding ground for superstars." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Decline and Resurgence of Vintage Synths** : People initially perceived old analog keyboards as junk, preferring sterile, digitally sampled alternatives. However, by the late 2000s, producers grew tired of digital sounds and sought out vintage 80s analog keyboards, driving up their prices due to their unique depth and character. "People think they're junk, so they throw them out, and they prefer that sterile, digitally sampled keyboards, which sound terrible. ... These old analog machines have sounds that you just can't get from modern digital ones." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1KF5MwJVYng
**Decline in High-Fidelity Sound Pursuit** : Since the turn of the millennium, there has been a societal shift away from prioritizing "pure, clear sound," with many now content with the fidelity offered by cheap earbuds or laptop speakers. This contrasts sharply with the 1970s, 80s, and 90s, when a passionate pursuit of "perfect sound" (big, tight bass; accurate mid-range; bright, smooth highs) led many—predominantly men—to spend "thousands upon thousands of dollars" on elaborate home stereo equipment and car audio systems, engaging in detailed comparisons of technical specifications and brands. "But sometime after the turn of the millennium, society shrunk away from pure, clear sound." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7Zniri9uSlc
**Decline in Musical Tribalism** : Younger generations of music fans, those who came of age in the new millennium, are far more "ecumenical" in their tastes and less tribal than previous generations. Technology allows them to freely sample music across all genres and eras, frequently switching between rock, hip hop, pop, classic rock, dance, and EDM. "People who came of age musically in the new millennium aren't nearly as tribal as previous generations." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Decline in Superstar Rock Acts** : The music industry has done a "terrible job" of generating new superstar rock acts on the same level as in previous decades. "We have done a terrible job of generating new superstar rock acts on the same level that we used to." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Decline of "Star-Making Machinery" in the 21st Century** : In the 2000s, with the rise of streaming and the decline of physical media and record stores, the power of traditional "cultural gatekeepers" (record labels, radio stations, music magazines, video channels) significantly diminished. They could no longer create a consensus around "superstar" acts, as individual listeners became their own music directors with vast access to music. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Decline of 8-Track Format (1997)** : The 8-track format was the first of the older music formats to be phased out, with the last major label release on 8-track being Fleetwood Mac's "Greatest Hits" in 1997. "When it came to old formats, eight tracks were the first to go. The last major label release on eight track was Fleetwood Max Greatest Hits in 1997." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Decline of CD Profits & Rise of Streaming** : The significant profit margins once associated with CD sales are irrecoverable, as streaming has fundamentally replaced traditional methods of music consumption, leading to a drastically different and less lucrative revenue model for artists compared to physical sales. "Those margins and profits on CDs are not coming back. Streaming has supplanted the old ways when it comes to the way people consume music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Decline of Concept Albums (1980s-1990s)** : Concept albums largely disappeared in the 1980s and 1990s after being deemed "overblown pompous ego trips" that lacked intellectual substance. This decline was significantly fueled by the punk and Alt Rock movements, which viewed such projects as outdated "vestiges of old rock" needing to be removed from cultural relevance. "In the 80s and 90s, the concept album all but disappeared." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Dm5N6yDRxXQ
**Decline of Cultural Gatekeepers and Consensus** : In the past, five major "cultural gatekeepers" (radio stations, record stores, record labels, music magazines, and video channels) determined popular music, creating scarcity and strong consensus around artists like KISS, who became "very socially important." Today, with streaming, this ubiquity and consensus are gone; even huge artists like Taylor Swift do not achieve the same level of universal recognition as bands of previous eras. "There were five major cultural gatekeepers. There were radio stations, record stores, record labels, music magazines, and I guess after a while video channels. Those were the things that determined the music that we listened to." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4xxgPX-aR90
**Decline of Early 1990s Alt-Rock Scenes** : By the late 1990s, the "awesomeness" of early 1990s alternative rock subgenres such as grunge, industrial, Manchester, and Britpop had faded. This left a void and a sense of disillusionment among fans, exacerbated by the lingering specter of Kurt Cobain's suicide, and rock was "flailing" to find the "next big thing." "The awesomeness of the alternation of the early 1990s, with grunge and industrial and Manchester and Britpop, had faded away." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Decline of Independent Live Music Venues** : Many independent live music venues closed during the 2010s due to high rents, gentrification, or an inability to generate sufficient revenue. This reduction in performance spaces meant fewer opportunities for new bands to develop their sound and gain live experience. "Many, many independent live music venues closed during the 2010s because owners were unable to make enough money to keep the lights on, squeezed up by higher rents or became victims of gentrification." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5Y6nHw5xx88
**Decline of Music Singles (1990s)** : Throughout the 1990s, the record industry intentionally phased out the single format, including seven-inch vinyl, CD singles, and casingles. This strategy aimed to compel consumers to purchase entire albums, often costing around $20, to obtain desired songs, thereby increasing album sales and profit margins for labels. "But by the end of the 1990s, the output of singles cratered." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r8AazBVoS7g
**Decline of Package Tours (Late 1960s)** : The package tour approach, common since the 1950s, began to decline in the late 1960s as the music business started producing artists with enough individual appeal and drawing power to tour independently and fill larger venues like arenas and stadiums, where the real money was made. "This package tour approach was pretty common until the late 1960s when the music business was producing artists big enough to tour on their own and play arenas and later stadiums." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jfQaUV6Bq4
**Decline of Physical Music Formats** : The demand for physical music formats such as records, CDs, and tapes is shrinking, becoming a niche market. Generations of music listeners have grown up without ever visiting a record store or handling physical media, experiencing music as ephemeral digital files. "While there will always probably be a demand for music on physical formats, it's going to shrink and shrink until it's just a very nichey thing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**Decline of Physical Music Retail and CD-ROM Drives** : As CD sales continued to fall, record store chains began going out of business, and big box stores largely stopped stocking CDs. Concurrently, new computers ceased coming with built-in CD-ROM drives, and the availability of standalone CD players drastically decreased, even disappearing from new cars. "Record chains began to go out of business because fewer people were buying discs. Big box store stopped carrying CDs. Computers stopped coming with built-in CD ROM drives and the number of CD players you could actually go out and buy had dropped drastically." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Decline of Record Stores and Music Overload** : Record stores faced widespread closures due to the collapse of physical product sales, while the internet's unfiltered proliferation of music led to an overwhelming "too much music out there" phenomenon and a sense of "fatigue" among listeners. "Record stores started going out of business." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Decline of Record Stores and Rise of Online Retailers** : The necessity of visiting physical record stores diminished with the advent of online platforms like iTunes, P2P programs, and torrent sites, which offered instant access to music. Online ordering also provided a more convenient way to obtain physical copies, eliminating stock issues. "Many people really don't feel the need to go to a record store anymore. iTunes, P2P programs, and torrent sites give us almost instant access to whatever we want, often whenever the spirit moves us and wherever we happen to be." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Decline of Royalty-Backed Securities (By 2011)** : The idea of packaging songs as asset-backed securities, as seen with Bowie Bonds and similar attempts by James Brown and the Isley Brothers, was largely dead by 2011, primarily due to the drop in record sales and royalties caused by digital disruption. "And by the time we got to about 2011, the idea of packaging songs this way was pretty much dead" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Decline of Soundtrack Sales in Streaming Era** : Soundtracks continue to be crucial for movie marketing but are no longer the "big sellers they used to be," with the exception of vinyl releases like *Guardians of the Galaxy*. The "golden age" of mall-based soundtrack purchases in the 80s and 90s has diminished significantly in the era of Netflix and streaming services. "Soundtracks continued to be an important part of any movie marketing plan, but outside of movies like Guardians of the Galaxy, which has done very well as a vinyl release, they aren't the big sellers they used to be." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**Decline of Traditional Gatekeepers** : The music industry underwent significant changes with the decline of physical sales and the rise of streaming in the 2000s, leading to the diminished power of "old cultural gatekeepers" such as record labels, radio stations, record stores, music magazines, and video channels, as they could no longer "create consensus among the public about what was good music." "The old cultural gatekeepers, record labels, radio stations, record stores, music magazines and video channels saw their powers of persuasion greatly diminished." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Decline of Traditional Music Formats** : Sales of CDs and global album sales are projected to continue their decline, while the purchase of individual digital tracks is experiencing a significant collapse. "CD sales will continue to fall. The global album sales continue to decline. And the purchase of digital tracks are cratering." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Decline of Traditional Music Gatekeepers (Post-2000)** : The rise of the internet around 2000 led to a rapid decline in the power of traditional cultural gatekeepers in the music industry. Record labels saw their century-old business model of selling physical plastic collapse. Record stores experienced reduced foot traffic, leading to the closure of major international chains. Music magazines suffered a collapse in revenue as labels cut advertising and consumers sought information online. YouTube effectively "killed off music videos on television." Only radio, though still powerful, popular, and profitable, now shares its dominance with the digital world. "The power, the traditional cultural gatekeepers enjoyed before the rise of the internet, waned quickly." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xid8CZf4oYo
**Decline of Traditional Music Promotion Channels** : The traditional avenues for music promotion have significantly diminished: the number of alternative radio stations in America has drastically decreased from roughly a thousand to around 180, and MTV and VH1 no longer primarily broadcast music videos. The number of music magazines and physical record stores has also dwindled. This fragmentation necessitates artists finding new methods, such as crowdfunding platforms like Pledge Music or social media, to raise awareness for their music. "So if I, excuse me if I use an American example, but when we began it back and there, I think there were a thousand alternative stations." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lGNrJgwQjgg
**Decline of Traditional Record Stores** : While record stores continue to exist, their numbers have significantly dwindled compared to their "glory days" from the 1960s to the late 1990s. Many legendary stores and chains have permanently disappeared, reflecting a shift in how music is acquired and consumed. "Record stores are still with us, but there were fewer and fewer of them." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jD1dWxNLvfE
**Decline of Vinyl Quality (Mid-1970s to Early 1980s)** : The oil crisis of the mid-1970s significantly impacted vinyl record quality. Petrochemical products, including vinyl, became more expensive, leading manufacturers to produce thinner records and recycle more vinyl. This recycling introduced impurities that resulted in new pressings sounding worse, with increased clicks, pops, low-frequency rumble, and greater susceptibility to scratches and damage, ultimately pushing consumers towards CDs. "Petrochemical products like vinyl became more expensive. To save money, records became thinner. Lots of vinyl was recycled, which introduced impurities that caused new pressings to sound worse, with clicks and pops and low-frequency rumble, and they were easily scratched and damaged." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Declining CD Player Costs (Late 1990s)** : While CD prices remained high, the cost of CD players steadily decreased throughout the 1990s. By the end of the decade, a wide range of players was available, from ultra high-end audiophile models costing thousands of dollars to affordable options priced around $100 or even less. "And while the price of music in this format stayed stubbornly high, the cost of buying a CD player kept coming down and down and down. By the end of the 90s, there was a CD player for everyone from ultra high-end audio file models costing thousands of dollars to some that sold for around $100, or even less at the local drugstore." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Declining Licensing Fees for Indie Artists** : While licensing deals initially offered a significant income boost for indie bands, the widespread adoption of this strategy led to increased competition and a subsequent decrease in the amount of money offered for placements, especially for lesser-known acts. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Declining Record Sales Impact** : The music industry has seen a significant shift where "nobody's buying records" anymore, and the revenue model is "all about streaming." This decline in physical sales is a driving factor for artists, especially older generations, to sell their catalogs. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Decriminalization of Homosexuality in the UK (Late 1960s)** : Homosexuality was decriminalized in the UK approximately a decade before February 1978, indicating a shift in legal and social attitudes toward the LGBTQ community during the late 1960s. "about a decade after homosexuality was decriminalized in the UK." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hswtuenFwvM
**Dee Dee Ramone's Failed Robbery Attempt** : In his youth, Douglas Colvin (Dee Dee Ramone) attempted to rob a neighborhood drug store. However, he made a mistake and ended up breaking into the laundromat located next door instead. "One of them, I think it was DD, once tried to rob a neighborhood drug store, it broke into the laundromat next door of a mistake." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=j8uk7BnbXwU
**Deep Dream: Text-to-Song Generation (N/A)** : The Deep Dream program allows users to input a song idea via text, select a participating singer's voice, and receive a 30-second song complete with lyrics. This illustrates AI's advanced capability to generate an entire short song structure from a textual prompt. "Deep dream allows you to type in an idea for a song, choose a participating singer for a voice, and the program will return with a 30-second song complete with lyrics." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Deep Jazz by G. Sun Kim (N/A)** : Deep Jazz was a neural network project developed by G. Sun Kim, a 20-year-old sophomore at Princeton University's computer science faculty. Created as part of a hacking competition, it utilized Google's Deep Dream and Jeffrey Lims' thesis to generate jazz music, employing note probabilities to ensure a non-robotic, fluid sound. Kim publicly shared the source code, declaring it "an AI built to make jazz." "He created it as part of a hacking competition using a Google Photo Generator called Deep Dream." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Deep Six Compilation (1985)** : This compilation, released on the CZ label in the fall of 1985, featured contributions from several local Seattle bands. Although it took two years to sell all 2000 copies, it was a pivotal record as the first time people could hear and recognize the distinct sound emerging from their city. "But then in the fall of 1985, several local bands contributed material to a compilation called Deep Six, a label called CZ. Although it took two years for all 2000 copies to sell out, this was a really important record. The first time people could hear what was happening in their city." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sCtQqVBtCaI
**Defense of Dan Brown in DaVinci Code Plagiarism Case** : Ortoner successfully defended "DaVinci Code" author Dan Brown against plagiarism charges, resulting in a win for Brown. "defending DaVinci code author Dan Brown against plagiarism charges, and he won." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wYD_dIvYY5o
**Defense of YouTube in Negative Land Case** : Charles B. Ortoner represented YouTube in the infamous YouTube vs. Negative Land issue, taking YouTube's side and winning the case. "Among his other cases have been the infamous YouTube versus negative land issue where he took YouTube side and won." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wYD_dIvYY5o
**Definition and Components of Metadata** : Metadata refers to the descriptive information embedded within a digital audio file, encompassing details such as song title, artist, album title, composer, genre, release year, song length, track number, artwork, lyrics, bit rate, and sampling rate, all of which are crucial for identification. "Metadata is the information about a song embedded within the digital file." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Definition of Immersive Music** : Immersive music is characterized as sound that envelops the listener in layers of sound, texture, and sometimes noise. It creates a mesmerizing, narcotic, transcenducing, and soothing experience, drawing the listener in. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kFLMkvJAaUY
**Definition of Indie Music** : Indie music refers to artists who undertake all aspects of their work independently, without assistance from major established record labels. This often includes self-producing albums or even launching and running their own record labels to release their music. "indie, as far as we're concerned, means artists who have done everything on their own with no help, that is independent from one of the big established record labels." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LgJ7ww2AgF4
**Definition of Plagiarism** : Plagiarism in music, and in general, is defined as passing off someone else's original work as one's own without acknowledging the true creator. It is considered cheating, unethical, dishonest, and a form of theft, capable of ruining reputations if proven. "Plagiarism is when you pass off someone else's work as your own without ever acknowledging the original creator. And plagiarism is a nasty thing. It's cheating. It's unethical. It's dishonest and it's theft, plain and simple." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L09R2evjyMc
**Definition of Poetry** : A high school English teacher defined poetry as the "efficient use of words," aiming for "maximum emotion and wisdom in the fewest number of words possible." This definition highlights the concise yet impactful nature of true poetic expression, a quality exemplified by accomplished lyricists like Gord Downie. "She called it efficient use of words, maximum emotion and wisdom in the fewest number of words possible." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dnz_Ycd_q7s
**Definition of Song Catalog** : A song catalog refers to all the songs an artist has had a hand in creating, meaning they wrote all or a portion of these songs, and may own a piece of the publishing. "First of all, when we say song catalog, we mean all the songs and artists has had a hand in creating. That is, they have written it all or a portion of these songs. That means they may own a piece of the publishing of these songs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Definition of Streaming** : Streaming is defined as the ability to listen to songs transmitted to a device over the internet on demand. Full-featured streaming services allow access to any song on any number of devices, anytime and anywhere, exemplified by platforms like Spotify, Apple Music, and Deezer. "This is the ability to listen to songs transmitted to your device over the internet on demand." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Definition of a "Superstar" Act** : A superstar act is characterized by selling tens of millions of units, drawing tens of thousands of people who would "crawl over broken glass" for tickets, possessing a deep catalog of hits released over many years, and achieving a consensus among millions of people that they are great and worthy of love and devotion. "I'm talking about an act that sells music by the tens of millions of units." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Definition of a Concert Ticket** : A concert ticket is defined as a contract involving the attendee, the performing act, the promoter, and the venue. It grants admission to a specific event at a stated time and place, and can be either purchased or, in some cases, provided free for attendance tracking. "A concert ticket is a contract between you, an act, a promoter, and a venue that allows you admission to a specific event at a stated time and place." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OCkov6FTliE
**Definition of a Generation (ISGG)** : The International Society of Genetic Genealogy (ISGG) specifies the length of a generation to be between 29 and 31 years, aligning closely with Wikipedia's definition regarding the child-parent child cycle of human existence. "The International Society of Genetic Genealogy sets the length of a generation between 29 and 31 years." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=v_am7ifh7Pk
**Definition of a Generation (Wikipedia)** : According to Wikipedia, a generation is defined as approximately 30 years, representing the time required for children to be born, grow up, become adults, and then have children of their own. "Well, according to Wikipedia, it's about 30 years, which is the time it takes children to be born, grow up, become adults, and then start to have children of their own." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=v_am7ifh7Pk
**Definition of a Poet in Rock** : A true poet, particularly in rock music, is characterized as someone who weaves words in a way that is open to diverse personal interpretations and leads to various truths, speaking to individuals uniquely. This ability to evoke deep meaning without explicit explanation is considered the mark of a poet. "Someone who weaves words in a way that's open to all kinds of personal interpretation and pathways to certain truths." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dnz_Ycd_q7s
**Del Glover – Leaked 20,000+ Unreleased Albums Online Over 11 Years** : Del Glover, a temporary warehouse employee, was responsible for leaking at least 20,000 unreleased albums online over an eleven-year period. His motivation was not financial gain but the desire to be the first to release material. "Leaked at least 20,000 unreleased albums online in 11 years, motivated by the desire to be the first, not by profit." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Uly2cT2vc28
**Delay Towers for Outdoor Festivals** : To address sound weakening and synchronization issues at large outdoor festivals, "delay towers" were introduced. These secondary speaker sets were placed strategically where primary sound began to fade, ensuring that people in the back could hear almost as well as those near the stage and that sound matched visual cues. "These were secondary sets of speakers placed where the sound from the primary speakers began to weaken." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qrrwqdpCjpQ
**Demise of 80s Alt-Rock Radio** : The unique era of 1980s commercial alt-rock radio is considered "an air in media whose time has passed," with public listening habits and demands having changed irrevocably over the last 40 years, making a return to that format unlikely. "Commercial Alt Rock Radio of the 80s was an air in media whose time has passed." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Demise of Digital Rights Management (DRM)** : Digital Rights Management (DRM), which previously restricted the sharing and transfer of legitimately purchased music across devices, became obsolete. The music industry's efforts to lock down music and sue file-sharers ultimately failed, leading to a landscape free of these technological barriers. "digital rights management, those locks that made it very, very difficult to share music, even when it came to moving legitimately purchased songs from one device to another that you all own, that's all dead. DRM is done." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Demise of Monoculture and Rise of Individual Curation (2020s)** : The "monoculture" of music consumption, where everyone listened to or was aware of generally the same thing, has disappeared. It has been replaced by individual curation, driven by algorithm-powered discovery, meaning shared music experiences are less common than in the MTV era. "The monoculture, this idea that everyone listening or watching generally the same thing, or at least aware of what everyone else is listening to, is gone. It's been replaced by individual curation of music consumption, pushed forward by algorithm-powered discovery." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Democratization of Music Creation and Distribution Leading to Glut** : The barrier to entry for releasing music has drastically lowered due to cheap and powerful home recording equipment and easy uploading to streaming platforms, YouTube, and TikTok. This offers "instant global distribution" and cuts out traditional "cultural gatekeepers" like record labels, radio, and magazines, which previously filtered new music, allowing only about 2,500 new albums per year. While promoting democratization, this ease of entry has resulted in a massive "glut of music," with over 100,000 songs uploaded daily. By early 2025, approximately 202 million songs will be available, but an estimated 25% (over 50 million) have never been heard, illustrating a diluted and cheaper music consumption experience. "Today, though, the barrier to entry for anyone wanting to release music is so low that anyone can do it. Home recording equipment is cheap and powerful, and uploading your music to the streaming music platforms can be done with a push of a button." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZzIqIydZTlA
**Demographic Shift and Musical Tastes** : Industry observers noted that a demographic shift was imminent in the early 2000s, as pop fans (twins and teens) would age into young adults. Historically, such shifts correspond with a change in musical tastes, as these older demographics typically seek music with "more substance and authenticity and meaning." "All the kids that were into backstreet boys and in-sync and Britney Spears would soon grow older. If the past was any indication, that kind of demographic shift means a shift in musical tastes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7dXtgTkdv9w
**Demographic Shift: Generation X's Impact** : By the late 1980s, a new generation of young music fans, Generation X, emerged without the cynicism towards domestic music prevalent in the 1960s and 70s. They embraced Canadian music on radio and MuchMusic, making it their own and fueling the rise of Canadian artists. "They simply just did not grow up with the cynicism regarding domestic music that was present in the 1960s and 70s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6PGgNCaW4xo
**Demographics of Music Heroes' Deaths** : Due to their age and demanding lifestyles, many music heroes are reaching the end of their statistical life expectancies, leading to an increasing number of deaths. "The actuarial tables say that they're approaching the end of their life expectancies." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**Demonstration of Double-Sided Records (1910)** : A demonstration recording from 1910 promoted the innovative "Columbia Double Disc Records," which featured different selections on each side. These records were explicitly not for sale but served to illustrate the "double value" proposition, offering two recordings for only a few cents more than a single-sided record, playable on any disc machine, including Columbia Graphophones or Victor Talking Machines. "The purpose of this record is to demonstrate the Columbia Double Disc Records." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jD1dWxNLvfE
**Denmark Street as UK's Tin Pan Alley** : Denmark Street, a short street in London located just south of the Oxford Tube station, has been historically referred to as the "tin pan alley of the UK." Its significance dates back to the 1920s, having been home to various music publishers, record label offices, music stores, pubs, and rehearsal rooms, making it a hub for music creation and industry in the United Kingdom. "For example, there's a short street in London just south of the Oxford Tube station called Denmark Street. This has been called the tin pan alley of the UK. This history goes back to the 1920s and involves some music publishers and record label offices and music stores and pubs and rehearsal rooms." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TsiYLGGSJ2U
**Denver Airport Conspiracy Theory** : The idea of a hidden truth or conspiracy surrounding the Denver airport is mentioned as a notable conspiracy theory. "we got the truth about the Denver airport. Oh, that's a good one." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pmKjZzypzvg
**Departure of Edison and Berliner from the Record Business** : By the time of the Great Depression, both Thomas Edison and Emil Berliner had exited the record industry. Edison ultimately abandoned his phonograph system. Berliner, having amassed wealth from his gramophone invention, redirected his efforts to campaigning for milk pasteurization and attempting to build helicopters. "By the way, both Thomas Edison and Emil Berliner were out of the record game by this time. Edison just gave up on his phonograph system, and Berliner took his money and became a campaigner for the pasteurization of milk, and he also tried to build some helicopters." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VlIErdSSVOM
**Depeche Mode – Name of a French Fashion Magazine; Translates as "Fast Fashion"** : The name is directly taken from a French fashion magazine called Dépêche Mode. "It translates as fast fashion." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Derek Whibley's Childhood in Ajax** : Derek Whibley was born and raised in Scarborough, Ontario, before moving to Ajax at the age of 12, where he spent most of his high school and teenage years. He formed his first bands in Ajax around age 13. "I was born and raised in Scarborough and then moved out to Ajax when I was about 12." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6L4xKBFEYkE
**Designers Dressing Musicians** : The practice of designers collaborating with musicians to create their wardrobes has a long history, dating back to the 1960s with John Stephen designing for The Who and Tommy Nutter creating suits for Mick Jagger. This trend continued with Stephen Sprouse dressing Deborah Harry of Blondie and Alexander McQueen partnering with David Bowie, underscoring the fashion industry's understanding of celebrity influence. "English designers in the 1960s knew this, which is why a guy named John Stephen designed clothes for the who, and a guy named Tommy Nutter made suits for Mick Jagger. ...Meanwhile, David Bowie paired with Alexander McQueen. In New York, Stephen Sprouse dressed up as neighbor, Debra Harry of Blondie." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mhQ36Y-c9fM
**Despacito's Energy Cost – 5 Billion Streams Could Heat 40,000 Homes for a Year** : An analysis of the energy required to stream "Despacito" by Luis Fonsi and Daddy Yankee 5 billion times found that the equivalent electricity could have heated 40,000 homes for an entire year. "40,000 homes could have been heated for a year with the equivalent amount of electricity." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Destruction of Traditional Music Video Channels** : YouTube's rise effectively dismantled traditional music video channels, centralizing the consumption of music videos onto its own platform. "And in the process, YouTube destroyed music video channels..." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Detective Tom Grant's Subsequent Investigation (Post-1994)** : After initially completing his assignment to find Kurt Cobain, Detective Tom Grant felt "something about Kurt's death really began to nag." He subsequently began collecting information on his own time, unpaid, believing the case was not "cut and dried." "But something about Kurt's death really began to nag at Tom. To him, this case wasn't cut and dried, so he began to collect information on his own time, unpaid." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sKWgbxk46eM
**Deterioration of Vinyl Quality (Late 1970s/Early 1980s)** : Due to the 1973 oil crisis, the recording industry began using recycled, impure vinyl and producing thinner records to cut costs. This resulted in "more rumble, more hiss, more clicks, more pops," shallower grooves causing skips, and increased warping. By the late 1970s and early 1980s, the quality of new vinyl records was often inferior to older, used records, making consumers eager for the superior audio offered by the compact disc when it arrived in late 1982. "Vinyl quality deteriorated to the point where it was so bad that when the CD came along in late 1982, it was exactly what lovers of good sound were looking for." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NAroxFbYkRw
**Detroit Techno Pioneers** : The Detroit techno scene, initially very small, can be traced back to three friends: Kevin Saunderson, Juan Atkins, and Derrick May. They were influenced by a wide range of artists including early electronic pioneers, Kraftwerk, David Bowie, English techno pop, Afrika Bambaataa, Motown, disco, and general dance music, aiming to create funky dance music with affordable synthesizers. "In fact, this scene was so small that we traced to just three friends, Kevin Sanderson, Juan Atkins, and Derek May." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OiqdZfxdBP0
**Development of Digital Recording Technology** : Digital recording techniques originated with digital machines developed by NHK in Japan in 1967, with the first digital recordings made there in 1971. These early digital recordings still used magnetic tape but stored a digital signal. Experiments continued throughout the 1970s, paving the way for the full digital audio workstation in the 1990s. "Digital machines came first. They were developed by NHK, the National Broadcaster of Japan in 1967." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r8AazBVoS7g
**Developmental Psychology and Personal Growth** : The transformation of Ash Boo Shultz from a control-averse, internally struggling artist to a collaborative and connected performer is described as an "incredible beautiful experiment" rooted in developmental psychology. His journey from hysterically leaving songwriting sessions to openly connecting with an audience on stage highlights the success of this personal evolution. "Isn't it an incredible expect credible beautiful experiment Alan when you can take the same person which is what developmental psychology is all about you can take the same person who who hysterically had to leave the idea of working with another person on a song in 2011 and then you can fast forward to 2017 and now you're singing who's with me and I'm I'm overjoyed to say that this public experiment has been successful." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=N-3eddy4J7g
**Diabolicus in Musicia / Devil's Tritone** : The Catholic Church once deemed a specific chord so "evil" it was believed to be composed by the devil, labeling it "Diabolicus in Musicia" ("the devil in music"). This dissonant chord, which evoked a disturbing emotional response, was banned for composers. Today, this "devil's tritone" is commonly used, notably in the theme song for "The Simpsons." "It once declared that a cord sounded so, um, well, evil that it had had to be composed by the devil himself." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**Diabolus in Musica – The Devil's Interval** : "Diabolus in musica," meaning "the sound of the devil in music," refers to a specific chord known as the devil's interval, or more technically, a flattened fifth (a triad played one chord fret down on the guitar). This basic chord construction, especially when its dissonance was amplified, so deeply disturbed religious leaders that some believed merely playing it could summon demons. "This was a specific chord that became known as the devil's interval. Or if you want to employ some musical theory, a chord known as a flattered fifth, a triad played one chord fret down in the guitar." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=41eKVp8JIhU
**Diamond Record Sales Achievement** : In America, a diamond record signifies the sale of 10 million physical copies of an album. While past mega-sellers like Michael Jackson's "Thriller" (65 million), AC/DC's "Back in Black" (50 million), and Pink Floyd's "Dark Side of the Moon" (45 million) achieved significantly higher numbers, current mega-sellers typically reach around 2.5 million copies. The era of such massive physical sales is believed to be over due to declining physical sales and the rise of music streaming. "In America this means you've sold 10 million copies, 10 million physical copies of your album." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Epz5Sn151bk
**Difficulty Transitioning from Rock Star Life** : After years immersed in the "rock star bubble," many musicians find it challenging to transition back to being a "civilian" or "normal person," missing the crowds and gigs. "And after living in a rock star bubble for years, it's hard to make the transition to being a civilian, a normal person." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yR3xc3ra5tE
**Difficulty for New Bands to Gain Traction** : In the contemporary music industry, new bands releasing a debut record face a very narrow window, typically 6 to 13 weeks after the first single's release, to generate significant traction with radio, retail, and fans. Failure to achieve this, or to succeed with a second single, can severely jeopardize a band's career. "If you're a new band with a debut record, you've got anywhere from 6 to 13 weeks to make an impression once that first single comes out." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ooNAHswiT5Q
**Difficulty of 21st Century Rock Superstardom** : It is significantly more challenging for rock acts to achieve superstar status in the 21st century compared to previous eras, largely due to drastic changes in the music industry and how fans consume music. "Why is it so much harder to be a superstar rock act in the 21st century?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Difficulty of Transitioning from Novelty Act to Serious Artist** : It is extremely challenging for artists pigeonholed as a novelty act to transition into a respected artist with gravitas and serious artistic merit, a feat that the Beastie Boys managed to achieve over their career. "it's even more difficult to move from being pigeonholed as a novelty act to one that carries gravitas and serious artistic merit." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9_BTe15NooI
**Digital Era Impact on Music Sales** : The advent of the digital era irrevocably ended the "glory days" of physical music sales. Physical album sales are in decline, digital album sales have plateaued and are now falling, and profit margins on music sales have become "razor-thin." Streaming services are further "cannibalizing" any remaining sales, forcing a return to pre-1965 business models for many artists. "Then came the digital era, which killed off those glory days forever." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lGNrJgwQjgg
**Digital Jukebox Concept** : Professor Dieter Selzer, an expert in psychoacoustics and advisor to Carlheinz Brandenburg, envisioned a "digital jukebox" in the 1980s. This system would store all the world's music on a central server, accessible by anyone with a telephone line, a concept that was initially rejected as impossible for a patent. "Selzer had this dream of what he called a digital jukebox. A system whereby all the music in the world would be stored on a central server which then could be accessed by anyone with a telephone line." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Digital Jukebox to Streaming Evolution** : The concept of the digital jukebox, initially proposed by Professor Selzer, evolved into the modern streaming music service as internet exchange of songs became widespread. This progression allowed for on-demand access to music libraries, fulfilling the original vision. "As people got into the idea of exchanging songs over the internet, we really didn't have much further to go, at least in a tactical sense, before we went back to Professor Seltsor's idea of the digital jukebox." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Digital Music Consumption** : The increasing popularity of downloading and digital platforms like iTunes led to fans typically buying individual songs, challenging the traditional album format and prompting record labels to seek strategies, such as concept albums, to preserve it. "See fans were getting far too used to the idea of being able to just buy one song at a time and not buying full albums." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1KyNY3zv6uc
**Digital Music Dominance and Industry Shift** : The music industry has transitioned deeply into a digital world, where virtually every song is instantly available via streaming services like Spotify, Apple Music, YouTube, SoundCloud, and Bandcamp. This shift has eliminated the complex and expensive overheads of manufacturing, warehousing, transporting, and distributing physical products, making digital distribution significantly more efficient and profitable. "Now with streaming, there is almost, now with streaming, there's no physical product. All the expensive overhead and those big fixed costs are gone. Digital distribution is so much more efficient and profitable on every single level." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**Digital Music Origins (Microsoft vs. IBM)** : The rise of streaming music services can be traced back to the rivalry between Microsoft and IBM in the early 1990s to develop a user-friendly graphical operating system for personal computers.
