Microsoft's Windows 95, a large program that necessitated the use of CD-ROMs for installation, inadvertently made CD-ROM drives ubiquitous, which then allowed users to rip music from CDs to their hard drives, ultimately leading to MP3s and streaming. "We can trace the rise of streaming music services back to a fight between Microsoft and IBM in the early 1990s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NAroxFbYkRw
**Digital Music as the "Major Killer" of CD Singles** : The increasing consumer preference for listening to and acquiring individual songs digitally is identified as the primary reason for the decline in sales of all compact discs, including CD singles. Most labels abandoned the CD single format completely around 2010. "But the major killer of the CD single has been digital music. Consumers are more and more concerned about listening to and to a lesser extent acquiring individual songs. That is killing sales of all compact discs, including the CD single." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**Digital Trickery and Lack of Talent** : Digital recording and editing technologies, particularly sophisticated software like Pro Tools, can be used to mask a performer's lack of talent. While beneficial for production, this capability means artists are not required to be as "spot on" during recording sessions, as "glaring mistakes" can be digitally corrected in post-production. This practice is criticized for leading to "unreal and unnatural recordings," contrasting with the analog era where artists had to perfect their parts and imperfections were sometimes valued for their beauty. "If you could fix it in the mix, it absolves you of being spot on during recording." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7Zniri9uSlc
**Diminished Artist Payouts from Streaming** : Artists receive only a fraction of a cent per stream, comparable to radio play, which is significantly less than the income from selling a digital file or CD. This, combined with declining CD sales, has led to a shrinking flow of money to artists and composers. "A single stream pays a fraction of a cent, much in the same way that a single play on the radio pays." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Direct Artist-Fan Relationships (2020s)** : In the contemporary music landscape, the relationships between artists and fans have become more direct. This shift is a consequence of the demise of traditional gatekeepers like VJs and music video channels, allowing artists to connect directly with their audience through platforms like YouTube and social media. "VJs are no longer there as cultural guides. The old macro culture is gone, replaced by micro scenes, and the relationships between artists and fans are more direct." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Director X's Music Video Career** : Director X is one of the biggest music video directors globally, responsible for "game-changing videos" from artists like Drake, Coldplay, Kendrick Lamar, Alicia Keys, Puff Daddy, Sisqo, Destiny's Child, Justin Bieber, 2 Chainz, Rosalia, Iggy Azalea, Shaggy, Beenie Man, Ariana Grande, and John Mayer. He has been active in the industry since the late 1990s and remains relevant. "These guys are responsible for game-changing videos from artists like Drake and Coldplay and Kendrick Lamar and so many more." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YFv6Ln00XjA
**Disappearing Album Artwork in Digital Age** : In the era of downloads and streaming, the physical space for album artwork has diminished from 12x12 or 5x5 inches to mere pixels, often as small as 225x225 pixels on platforms like Wikipedia. This reduction makes detailed artistic expression difficult and largely overlooked by consumers. "Instead of 12 by 12 or 5 by 5 inches, we have images measured in pixels." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pwebM3TfSdg
**Disbursement Error due to Generic Song Titles** : When a generic title like "I Love You" is streamed, services must identify the correct artist, and if metadata is faulty, they often default to the most popular song with that title, potentially misdirecting royalties to an unrelated artist. "The streaming music services have to figure out which I love you it is, which artists it really belongs to." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Disclaimer for Sensitive Content** : The content of the narrative includes discussions of suicide and suicidal ideation, which may be disturbing to some listeners. Support resources, such as calling or texting 988 for crisis intervention and 911 for immediate danger, are highlighted. "This episode discusses suicide and suicidal ideation and some people might find it disturbing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sCwgvHg86Oc
**Discovery of Aluminum (1824)** : In 1824, Danish physicist and chemist Hans Christian Orsted developed a method to produce aluminum metal, a process so arduous that for a time, aluminum was more valuable than gold. By 1889, an efficient method of production was discovered, which involved purifying bauxite. "But it wasn't until 1824 that a Danish physicist and chemist named Hans Christian Orsted figured out how to produce aluminum metal." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**Discovery of New Elements** : Scientists have discovered four new elements (113, 115, 117, and 118) using particle accelerators, which involve bombarding existing elements to jam more protons and neutrons into a nucleus. These synthetic elements are highly unstable, existing for a second or less, but their creation advances the understanding of nature's fundamental building blocks. "Using these particle accelerators, existing elements were bombarded with subatomic particles in hopes of jamming more protons and neutrons into a nucleus thereby creating a brand new element." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VMPLplhDk8I
**Discovery of Oil in Pennsylvania (1859)** : Oil was first discovered in Pennsylvania in 1859, though its uses beyond burning and lubrication were initially unknown. This discovery triggered the Pennsylvania oil rush around Titusville, marking the beginning of the modern oil industry with companies drilling wells throughout the region. "Oil was first discovered in Pennsylvania in 1859." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**Discrot (CD Deterioration)** : This term describes a physical degradation process that affects CDRs, where the layers of the disc begin to separate. This condition can lead to the disc being in bad shape, suffering from distortion, and skips, as observed with Pinchico's "Death Metal" EP. "It suffered from something called Discrot, which happens when the layers of a CDR begin to separate." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UsPoPUByjYw
**Disintermediation's Continuing Impact** : As the 2000s progressed, the process of "disintermediation," which involved breaking down the traditional arrangements between cultural gatekeepers and the music industry, continued to integrate the music experience with online activities, following the initial disruptions caused by file sharing, iTunes, and iPods. "this disintermediation, this breaking of the older arrangement between the traditional cultural gatekeepers in the new world just kept going." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Disney's CDVU Plus Failure** : Disney attempted a similar initiative to Warner Music Group's digital liner notes with a product called "CDVU Plus." However, this effort to enhance digital music with interactive content did not gain traction or significant interest from consumers. "Disney tried something similar called CDVU Plus, but nobody cared." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**Dispelling Rock Star Myths** : Contrary to popular belief that all rock stars are drunken, drugged-out individuals with low intellect, evidence shows many are highly educated and accomplished, challenging the simplistic "caveman" stereotype. "Over the decades, most people have come to believe that rock stars of both sexes are drunken, drugged out sex machines who had they not learned to play an instrument would have had a hard time holding down the worst kind of minimum wage job." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QfiRRtsZoYU
**Disposable Nature of Fashion and Music Cycles** : Fashion, described as one of the most disposable artistic endeavors with styles lasting only a single season, serves as an analogy for music. Trends in music also appear and disappear, only to be resurrected 10, 15, or 20 years later by new generations, making music, like fashion, a "renewable resource." "Much of what's created isn't designed last more than a single season, and once the season is over, it's time to toss out all the old couture and buy new stuff." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5q5EnZj1FwE
**Disputed First Artificially Faded Song (1930s)** : The identity of the first song to be artificially faded out on record is difficult to ascertain, with "Beyond the Blue Horizon" by George Olsen (1930) and "Old Man Harlem" by the Dorsey Brothers (1933) both being put forth as possibilities. Electronic fading became simple with magnetic tape in the 1950s. "For songs that were the first to be artificially faded out, that's hard to say." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gP3oQeYEvso
**Dissolution of the Soviet Union (December 26, 1991)** : The Soviet Union officially ceased to exist on December 26, 1991, breaking apart into separate independent countries. This historic event marked the end of the Cold War and the collapse of international communism, a process to which Western soft power, including rock music, is argued to have contributed significantly. "And on December 26, 1991 the Soviet Union ceases to exist and breaks apart into the separate countries that we see today." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YeNWDtN_sHE
**Distinction Between Similar Sounding Songs and Plagiarism** : It is crucial to be careful, as just because two songs sound similar does not automatically mean plagiarism. This is related to the "no-two-alike conjecture" breaking down when dealing with a limited number of variables, such as musical notes in rock music. "Just because two songs sound similar, doesn't necessarily mean that someone is ripping off someone else." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wYD_dIvYY5o
**Distortion Definition** : Distortion, also known as overdrive, occurs when an electrical signal is pushed past the capacity of an amplifier's circuitry, making it too loud or powerful. This process causes the signal to clip, where its peaks and troughs are cut off, introducing new characteristics like overtones, sustain, and compression. "Distortion is also known as overdrive. Basically it means you've pushed the electrical signal past what the circuitry and the amplifier can handle." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iGav9uJwA_w
**Diverse British Music Scene of Early 1980s** : By the early 1980s, British music diversified significantly, moving in "all directions at once." This included the quirkiness of The Cure, the goth sounds of Bauhaus, the harsh industrial music of Throbbing Gristle, the four-octave voice of Kate Bush, a ska revival, and deepened interest in reggae and rockabilly, all prioritizing unfettered self-expression over traditional rock virtuosity. "By the beginning of the 1980s, it was obvious that music in Britain had gone off in all directions at once. This wave of fresh new talent was unconcerned about being good enough to tear off a lead zeppelin style guitar solo." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9uTORLOm5Yg
**Diverse Early Retailers for Records** : Beyond dedicated record stores and furniture shops, records were sold in a variety of other retail outlets, including department stores such as Sears, Kresge's, Kmart, and Woolworths, as well as independent stores and even drug stores. These establishments typically featured small displays with four to six bins, holding around thirty records each, with no more than two copies per title. "Department stores had them. Sears, Cresgies, Cammart, Woolworths." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jD1dWxNLvfE
**Diverse Music-Related Smartphone Uses (Post-2007)** : Modern smartphones, heavily influenced by the iPhone, are used for a vast array of music-related activities: playing/streaming music, creating playlists, making music and music videos, watching music videos, identifying songs with apps like Shazam, live-streaming concerts, distributing music, engaging with artists on social media, and purchasing merchandise and tickets. "Today we use our smartphones to play music files, stream music, make playlists, make music, make music videos, watch music videos, identify songs with a nap like Shazam, live stream concerts, distribute and share music, create social media posts, stay in close contact with their favorite artists by merch and by tickets to concerts and so much more." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Doc Kauffman's Innovations and Collaboration with Leo Fender** : Doc Kauffman, a lap pedal steel guitarist and former Rickenbacker employee, invented the Vibrola, a precursor to the whammy bar. He collaborated with Leo Fender, with Leo handling electronics and Doc focusing on guitar design, on building lap steel guitars. Doc eventually moved on by 1946. "One day he met Doc Kaufman. He was a lap pedal steel guitarist, not a wine guitarist. He once had a job with Rick and Bokker. In fact, he was the guy who invented something called the Vibrola, which would later evolve into something we now call the Whammy Bar. Leo and Doc started building up lap steel guitars. Leo handled the electronics and Doc took care of guitar design. But by 1946, Doc had decided to move on." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E6cXyaHALBQ
**Dogs Prefer Reggae Music** : Scientific research has demonstrated that dogs, when exposed to different musical genres, display a preference for reggae. "Scientists discovered that dogs prefer reggae music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qtdetc5-MQo
**Dogs' Response to Music for Behavioral Issues** : Extensive scientific studies have explored how dogs respond to various types of music, particularly in efforts to help animals suffering from separation anxiety, hyperactivity, or fear of thunderstorms. Research indicates that dogs are most soothed by music that aligns closely with their own heartbeat. "There has actually been plenty of scientific study into this area as dog people are looking to help animals with separation anxiety, animals that are hyperactive or are scared of thunderstorms." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**Dolby Surround Mix in Videos (1991)** : In November 1991, Michael Jackson's "Black or White" video notably featured a Dolby surround mix, a remarkable detail considering most televisions were still in mono at the time. This advanced audio production highlighted the video's status as a major cultural event and its cutting-edge technical ambition. "In an era when most TVs were still in mono, this came with a Dolby surround mix." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Dolly Parton – Available Only by Fax; Funded Buffy the Vampire Slayer; Lost a Dolly Parton Look-Alike Contest** : Dolly Parton communicates only by fax. Her production company funded the creation of the Buffy the Vampire Slayer series, and she and star Sarah Michelle Gellar share the same birthday. She once entered a secret Dolly Parton look-alike contest and lost. "Dolly once entered a secret Dolly Parton, like contest and lost." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Dominance and Rejection of the Fade-Out (Post-1950s)** : From the 1950s onward, with modern recording techniques and magnetic tape, the fade-out became a common way to end songs, with more than half of all rock songs before the alternative era using it. However, punk and New Wave movements largely shunned the fade-out, preferring definitive song endings, a legacy seen in bands like Pearl Jam and Nirvana. "Since the 1950s, performers, producers, and engineers have used the fader out as a way of bringing a song to a close." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gP3oQeYEvso
**Dominance of Live Nation Entertainment** : Live Nation Entertainment, formed by the merger of Ticketmaster and Live Nation, is estimated to sell approximately 70% of all concert tickets worldwide, including for most major acts. Despite opposition and antitrust scrutiny, the company has maintained its structure. "Ticket master sells maybe 70% of all concert tickets worldwide, including for most of the biggest acts in the world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Dominance of Pre-Internet Rock Bands in Touring** : In the 2010s, the biggest rock bands capable of filling arenas, amphitheaters, and stadiums were overwhelmingly those who had established their careers and achieved many big hits before the internet disrupted the music industry around 2000. Very few new rock acts emerged post-2000 with the same touring power. "The biggest rock bands in the world were still those who managed to establish careers with many big hits before the internet came along and disrupted everything." "And since 2000, very few new rock acts, relatively speaking when compared to the olden days, have emerged who can do the same thing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HSvgRyGiOpE
**Dominance of Streaming Revenue** : The current music economy is primarily driven by streaming. This shift has diminished traditional record sales as a primary income source, pushing artists to explore new revenue streams like catalog sales. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Don Letts' Band Lifespan Theory** : Don Letts, a British DJ and filmmaker involved in the original punk scene and close to The Clash, theorizes that the average "proper lifespan" for most bands is seven to nine years. He believes this period allows bands to "be born, to rise, to peak, and then explode," typically due to internal pressures such as creative differences and diverging lifestyles. "He believes the average and proper lifespan of most bands is seven years, nine at the outside. He said that was enough time for them to be born, to rise, to peak, and then explode, usually due to some kind of internal pressures." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=t2uXHP9cBdM
**Donald Bartholome's Fictional Connection to Rather Attack** : Writer Paul Limbert-Almond discovered striking coincidences in the fictional stories of Texas writer Donald Bartholome, which often featured a character named Kenneth, the phrase "what is the frequency," and a newsman character named "Mr. Lather," portrayed as evil and unscrupulous. Bartholome worked in Houston journalism and media in the late 1950s and early 1960s, at the same time Dan Rather was working in Texas. This raises the question of whether the two men crossed paths and if a lingering grudge could have existed for decades. "A writer for Harper's magazine named Paul Limbert-Almond found some interesting coincidences while reading the stories of a Texas writer named Donald Bartholome." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4PlieAEthSg
**Donna's Domain Website** : This website operates as a consumer's guide, specifically rating the "penis size and sexual ability of dozens of rock stars." It features various critiques and reviews concerning prominent musicians. The website address is metal-sludge.com. "Donna has a website that acts as something of a consumer's guide. This site writes the penis size and sexual ability of dozens of rock stars." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dMsEj62KFEw
**Dopamine and Musical Pleasure** : The brain areas—nucleus accumbens, amygdala, and cerebellum—secrete dopamine, a natural "feel-good" hormone. When music, particularly a song that induces chills or makes hair stand up, triggers dopamine, the body experiences pleasure and associates that song with feeling good, creating a chemical link between music and positive emotion. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHN6db7m3DM
**Dorian Mode and Ancient Greek War Music** : The ancient Greeks used music, specifically a special marching music named after the Dorian Greeks called Dorian mode, to motivate and psychologically prepare for war. This practice highlights an early recognition of music's power to influence physical and mental states. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHN6db7m3DM
**Dot Com Crash Impact on Warner Brothers** : Warner Brothers' attempt at a stock offering in the mid-2000s, which coincided with their dispute with Linkin Park, was notably affected by the broader financial climate "in the wake of the dot com crash," leading to a less favorable market reception than anticipated. "Again, this is in the wake of the dot com crash." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YcdidjWaIqE
**Dotcom Bubble Impact** : The dotcom bubble was cited as the reason why Sony's plans to create a series of animated shorts for The Smashing Pumpkins' *Machina* concept never came to fruition. "Dotcom bubble you understand." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1KyNY3zv6uc
**Double A-Side Single Concept** : A "double A-side single" is a historical vinyl record format where a band released two songs on a 7-inch single, both of which were considered equally strong and expected to be hits, without one being designated as a secondary or "B-side" track. Arcade Fire utilized this traditional release strategy for their "The Suburbs" single, a practice famously employed by bands like The Beatles and The Rolling Stones. "That's a reference back to the old days of vinyl when a group would release two great songs on a 17-single, both were regarded equally as good and both were expected to be hits." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ir2FfLQJNtY
**Dr. Edward Gelb's Conclusion on Lie Detector Test (1996)** : After administering a lie detector test to Elden Hoke on March 6, 1996, Dr. Edward Gelb concluded that there was a 0.01% chance Hoke was lying. This result implied a 99.99% probability that Hoke genuinely believed he was telling the complete and absolute truth regarding Courtney Love's alleged murder-for-hire offer. "Dr. Gelb concluded that there was a 0.01% chance that Elden Hoek was lying." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sKWgbxk46eM
**Dr. Edward Gelb's Polygraph Expertise (Post-1994)** : Dr. Edward Gelb, the polygraph expert who conducted Elden Hoke's lie detector test, is also noted for having administered the lie detector test for O.J. Simpson. This connection adds a layer of notoriety to Gelb's involvement in high-profile cases. "The guy conducting the test was Dr. Edward Gelb, the same guy who gave O.J. Simpson his lie detector test." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sKWgbxk46eM
**Dr. Frankenforder's Batcave Influence** : The Batcave's musical ethos sought to blend "glam era David Bowie" with the theatricality of Dr. Frankenforder from The Rocky Horror Picture Show, suggesting an embrace of dramatic performance and diverse sexual expression. "Instead, the goal was to reinvent glam era David Bowie and cross it with Dr. Frankenforder from the Rocky Horror Picture Show." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**Dr. Peter Goldmark and the Invention of Vinyl Records** : Dr. Peter Goldmark, chief engineer for Columbia Records, spearheaded the development of vinyl records in the 1940s. Faced with a shellac shortage due to World War II, Goldmark's team innovated by using polyvinyl chloride (vinyl), a material previously used for sewer pipes, as a record substrate. This groundbreaking invention led to more durable records that could hold five times more music per side than shellac discs, fundamentally transforming the recording industry with the introduction of the LP and single formats. "This is where Columbia records stepped in with their chief engineer, a guy by the name of Dr. Peter Goldmark." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_Pi7JLwAeOI
**Dr. Seuss Quote** : A quote from Dr. Seuss, "Don't cry because it's over. Smile because it happened," was used as a fitting closing sentiment for a program dedicated to The Tragically Hip after Gord Downie's passing, encapsulating the appreciation for his life and work. "It goes like this. Don't cry because it's over. Smile because it happened." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Um_KEEcCeT4
**Dramatic Audio Quote (N/A)** : An unidentified audio clip, presented as part of the "sound of history," states, "The councils. They've banned my concepts, and they take one money." The quote is presented without direct attribution or context within the transcript. "The councils. They've banned my concepts, and they take one money." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MTDY6qT11Dc
**Drawbacks of Traditional Box Office Ticketing** : Lining up for physical tickets was problematic: it excluded those unable to wait for extended periods (e.g., people with disabilities, out-of-town fans), lacked transparency regarding seat inventory across multiple outlets, and was highly susceptible to scalping and corruption. "Lining up for physical tickets was awful and flawed and open to all kinds of corruption." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OCkov6FTliE
**Dream Instruments** : Many musicians harbor a clear vision of the instruments they aspire to own, often making do with what they can afford initially while never losing sight of the goal to one day acquire an iconic rock and roll machine. These dream instruments are desired globally regardless of skill level, valued for their sound, playability, and association with famous musicians. "If you know what you're doing, you can get the job done on any instrument. But if you're able to choose anything, I'm Alain Cross." (Ignoring the host intro, the statement about choice stands as a general curiosity). ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Y0HW8LQKvJw
**Drug Problems Leading to Britpop Couple's Split** : The relationship between Justine Frischmann (Elastica) and Damon Albarn (Blur), considered Britpop's "first couple," ended largely due to "druck problems" (likely drug problems), further signaling the decline of the era. "Justin and Damon all of our broke up, mostly overdruck problems. Dick Pop's first couple were no more." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vzyMSSlD7Yg
**Drug Smugglers Hid Ketamine in Kinder Eggs Inserted Into Their Bodies at Creamfields** : Marshall Maddock and Harry Hewitt-Simms tried to smuggle ketamine into the Creamfields music festival by concealing drug packets in Kinder eggs stuffed into their rectums. Specially trained sniffer dogs found a small amount of drugs on them; when they refused further searches, an intimate examination discovered the concealed eggs. Both were sentenced to 18 months in prison. "Each man was found to have kinder eggs stuffed up their bum's each containing 10 packets of ketamine." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Drum Recording Technique ("Chamber of Density")** : A specialized drum recording technique involves capturing drums in a dry, small room, then routing the sound through a band's PA system set up in an adjacent, empty warehouse. The PA is then re-mic'd to achieve a more powerful, "harder hitting" sound, creating a unique "chamber of density" effect without conventional reverb. This method was notably used by U2 for "Bullet the Blue Sky" and later by Rage Against the Machine for "Killing in the Name" in 1992. "For you technical people out there there’s kind of a variation on the reverb chamber but without reverb, a chamber of density." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L03Yk1SHRNM
**Drummers' Significant Contributions to Rock Music – Beyond the Spotlight** : Drummers have made very significant, yet often underappreciated, contributions to music throughout the history of rock and roll, a genre where "all the glory is gone to the singers and the guitar players." Without the input of these individuals, the music we listen to today "might not have been possible." This includes those who altered the course of rock music, even if they weren't necessarily the "best drummers or the most talented drummers." "drummers have made some very significant Contributions to music and without these people what we listened to today Might not have been possible." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gsSTJWN6cUo
**Dual Objectives of Cancon** : The Canadian content rules served a dual purpose: a cultural strategy to expose Canadians to music from their own country rather than solely American and British fare, and an industrial strategy to foster a domestic music creation infrastructure including studios, labels, producers, engineers, agents, and promoters. "First, make sure that Canadians got to hear music from other Canadians instead of just a steady diet of American and British music. In that sense, it was a cultural strategy. But this was also an industrial strategy." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Duran Duran's "Hungry Like the Wolf" as an Example** : The video for Duran Duran's "Hungry Like the Wolf" is cited as an example of the kind of imaginative, complex storytelling that captivated Director X and that their podcast seeks to explore the creation of. "So imagine if you could go back in the days and sit down with the Rand Rand and talk about the hungry like a wolf video, like what the hell? I can tell you guys being this jungle and and and just going through this crazy, crazy story." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YFv6Ln00XjA
**Duration of Licensed Music Snippets** : Unless the licensing deal is for an entire soundtrack album, the agreement typically involves using only a short "snippet" of a song. These snippets commonly range from 15, 20, or 30 seconds in length. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Dying 10-inch 78 RPM Record (Early 1950s)** : In the early 1950s, the 10-inch 78 RPM record format was in decline, being surpassed by newer formats. "We had the dying 10 inch 78 RPM record." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Dynamic Pricing Controversy and Price Moderation** : Dynamic pricing often causes initial controversy when tours go on sale, especially for popular acts, as high demand can lead to exorbitant initial prices (e.g., $5,000 for nosebleed seats). However, demand typically moderates after the first few days, and algorithms adjust, causing prices to fall, a fact rarely reported in the news. "This causes controversy during the first couple of days the tour goes on sale, especially for a super popular act." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Dynamic Pricing Mechanism** : Dynamic pricing, similar to that used for hotel rooms and airline tickets, adjusts concert ticket prices up or down based on real-time demand. Algorithms aim to find the "fair market value" where artist popularity meets fan willingness to pay. Unlike scalping, the increased revenue from dynamic pricing directly benefits the artist, not a secondary seller. "This is where dynamic pricing comes into play. Just like hotel rooms and airline tickets and Uber rides, go up and down with demand." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Dynamic Pricing Schemes** : The implementation of dynamic pricing schemes, which adjust ticket prices based on real-time demand, plays a role in the escalating costs, often causing prices to rise more than they fall. "And eighth, schemes like dynamic pricing where we see the price of concert tickets rise and sometimes fall depending on demand." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Dysania** : This is a condition describing the inability or extreme difficulty in getting out of bed in the morning. "Can't get out of bed in the morning. Well, you have a condition known as dysania." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**EP (Extended Play)** : By the mid-1970s, a new format called the EP (Extended Play) emerged, functioning as a mini-album with more than two but typically less than seven songs. "A new thing called the EP had emerged. This was like a mini album that had more than two songs but usually less than seven." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Earl "Madman" Muntz's Stereo Pack (1962)** : Earl "Madman" Muntz, a television seller, adapted the Fidelapack cartridge technology for cars in 1962, calling his invention the Stereo Pack. Introduced in California and Florida, it featured four tracks—two for each side of a stereo album. A distinctive "Colunque" sound indicated the machine was realigning its playback heads to switch between the two pairs of tracks, offering a new mobile listening experience superior to the Highway High Phi. `"So in 1962, Earl introduced what he called the Stereo Pack in California and Florida."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Early 1950s Format War (LP vs. 45)** : A "massive format war" occurred in the early 1950s between the 12-inch 33 1/3 RPM LP and the 7-inch 45 RPM single, with RCA advocating for singles. "It was a massive format war." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Early 1970s Socio-Political Climate** : The early 1970s were characterized as a "bad hangover from the 60s," marked by the failure of peace and love ideals, economic struggles (first oil crisis, inflation, recession, unemployment), the Cold War, violent events like the Kent State shootings, and a corrupt American presidency. This somber, conservative mood was a backdrop for the emergence of radical new music. "The early 70s were like a bad hangover from the 60s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VYi3r0G-ne4
**Early 1980s Sociopolitical Climate** : The early 1980s were marked by a volatile political landscape, with the Cold War nearing a dangerous flashpoint between the US and the USSR. Society was undergoing significant shifts, compounded by a severe recession where interest rates surpassed 20%. This tense environment created an "interesting time for music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kFLMkvJAaUY
**Early 1990s Music Diversity** : The early 1990s were a period of significant musical diversity, characterized not only by the dominance of Grunge but also by a resurgence in punk rock, the emergence of Britpop, a spike in industrial music production, and a fresh approach to Goth. Additionally, many unique bands defied genre classification, often grouped under the "nebulous umbrella term" of "alternative." "The early 1990s were an awesome time for new music... The big thing of course was Grunge, but there was also a resurgence in punk rock. We got Britpop, there was a spike in the production of industrial music, along with a fresh approach to Goth." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=t2uXHP9cBdM
**Early 1990s Music Scene** : The early 1990s were characterized by a constant stream of new bands and incredible songs, with this vibrant period reportedly running from late 1989 through to late 1986 (likely a transcript error, meant 1996) or early 1997. "it seemed that every week brought an awesome new band with an incredible song." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vzyMSSlD7Yg
**Early 20th Century Context for Electricity** : In the early 20th century, electricity was still a relatively new concept, and electric household appliances were just beginning to gain traction. Even owning a radio was considered a novel and futuristic possession, highlighting the nascent stage of electrical integration into daily life. "Electric household appliances were just starting to catch on and even having a radio was still a novel thing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E6cXyaHALBQ
**Early 80s Music Culture Divide** : In the early 1980s, the music landscape was marked by a significant cultural divide, separating urban areas from suburbs, people of color from white audiences, and rockers from rappers. This polarization underscored a broader societal split that impacted how new music genres like rap and hip-hop were received. "There was a huge divide. Urban areas versus the suburbs. People of color versus white. Rockers versus rappers." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cYwk-B3qwwk
**Early 90s Record Label "Gold Rush"** : The early 1990s saw a significant rush by record labels to sign bands that had an "aggressive sounding" style or simply "a whiff of alternative about them." This intense period of recruitment helped propel bands like L7 into the mainstream. "Because there was a gold rush to sign up aggressive sounding grins like bands around that time, L7 was swept away into the hands of producer Butch Vig." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Eo32mAZd8DI
**Early Acoustics - Stonehenge** : Some archaeologists believe that part of Stonehenge's design involved principles of natural amplification, suggesting an ancient understanding of how to enhance music and chanting through acoustic properties. "Some archaeologists think that part of the design of Stonehenge involved creating something that would naturally amplify music and chanting." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qrrwqdpCjpQ
**Early Applications of Amplification Technology** : The initial convergence of vacuum tubes, speakers, and widely available electricity led to the invention of radio. Subsequently, amplifiers capable of converting electrical energy back into sound enabled the development of public address (PA) systems and sound systems for movie theaters. "The first collision of these three inventions was radio, and then amplifiers that could turn any sound that had been converted into electrical energy back into sound. This translated into PA systems and sound systems for movie theaters." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E6cXyaHALBQ
**Early Bootleg Records** : In the past, access to everything a band did was tightly controlled by distribution systems, making it difficult for fans to acquire unreleased material. The best fans could hope for were rare, elusive, and often illegal bootleg records, which were unauthorized recordings issued by shadowy labels without the artist's permission. These typically featured live performances, as they were the easiest to make, but some also contained vault material never designed for public hearing. "About the best you could hope for was for one of those rare, elusive and highly legal bootleg records. Unauthorized recordings issued by some shadowy label without the permission of the artist." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zT6pRGYLWhw
**Early Britpop Gathering Spots** : In the nascent stages of Britpop, like-minded individuals gathered at specific London venues, including The Good Mixer pub in Camden and a regular Thursday night club evening at Cindrome on Oxford Street. "like minded people began to gather in places like the Good Mixer, which was a pub in Camden. On Oxford Street there was a regular Thursday night club evening at a venue called Cindrome." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vzyMSSlD7Yg
**Early Canadian Music Industry State** : Prior to the 1970s, there was barely a Canadian music industry to speak of. It consisted mostly of small local labels and offices that were branch plants of foreign record companies, indicating a lack of robust domestic infrastructure to support Canadian artists. "Meanwhile, there was barely a Canadian music industry to speak of. There were a bunch of small labels. But the industry was essentially a bunch of offices that were branched plants of foreign record companies." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6PGgNCaW4xo
**Early Concert Ticket Sales (Pre-Computerization)** : In the past, buying concert tickets involved visiting physical box offices, often located within theaters, arenas, or department stores, like the "Attractions Box Office" (ABO) or "Celebrity Box Office" (CBO). Buyers would pay cash for pre-printed paper tickets. For high-demand shows, long lines, sometimes extending for days, were common. There was no guarantee of tickets, and individuals might hire others to stand in line to purchase the maximum allowed. The distribution of pre-printed tickets across multiple box offices meant choosing a location was a gamble for securing good seats. "For the longest time the rule for buying concert tickets was, stampede and those who survived got tickets." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sPj3uxmz8H4
**Early Criticism of Music and Youth in Ancient Greece (2500 years ago)** : Approximately 2500 years ago, critics in ancient Greece were greatly concerned about the music created by young people. They warned that these sounds would induce "excessive emotionality," leading to immoral behavior, a decline in discipline, immodesty, poor character, distraction from intellectual pursuits, and irrational thinking. "Twenty-five hundred years ago, critics were up and arms over the music being made by young people, saying that these sounds would eventually lead to something they called excessive emotionality." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=41eKVp8JIhU
**Early Demonstration Recordings (1906)** : In 1906, demonstration recordings, such as "I am the Edison hologram," were utilized in stores by salespeople to introduce customers to the novel concept of recorded audio. These recordings aimed to showcase the entertainment capabilities of phonographs and records, highlighting their ability to provide melody, tales, laughter, and music for dancing or evoking memories. "Salespeople would play this in the store as a way of introducing customers to this new idea of recorded audio." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jD1dWxNLvfE
**Early Digital Recording Limitations** : Despite early efforts like Roy Cooder's "Bop to You Drop" (1978) and The English Beat's "Special Beat Service" (1981) using digital recording, the absence of digital mixing boards at the time meant these albums still underwent analog mixing and mastering steps. Artists like Stevie Wonder, Christopher Cross, and Fleetwood Mac also made partially digital recordings. "However, there was no such thing as a digital mixing board at the time, so while it was recorded digitally, it still went through two analog steps for mixing and mastering." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**Early Email and Internet Access (1980s)** : Before the public internet became widespread, email was already in use, with David Bowie engaging with it as early as the late 1980s. This early exposure informed his understanding of the internet's future potential. "He'd been going online since the late 1980s. That's when he first started using email and that's before the public Internet opened up." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yq2A_o9-ulM
**Early Fame and Documentation** : When individuals achieve a certain level of fame, "everything you do will be recorded somewhere" and becomes "out of your control." This perpetual documentation by fans, management, social media teams, and record labels means a famous person's life "becomes one ever-expanding Wikipedia page." "Fans are going to do it, your management team will do it, your social media people will make sure it gets done, probably somebody at your label, they'll just keep doing it and doing it and doing it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_AY5lS-FmlU
**Early Forms of Protest in Art** : For centuries, people have composed satiric poems and songs as a form of protest. However, they had to exercise extreme caution regarding their audience to avoid being declared seditious traitors, with others often targeted as socialists and communists. "People have written satiric poems and songs for centuries. They just had to be careful about who heard them less they declared seditious traders." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0ARYt4sYT90
**Early Goth Fashion (1818 Influence)** : Fashion served as a unifying element for the Goth scene. Black was the predominant color, complemented by styles popular 150 years prior, evoking the clothing Mary Shelley might have worn in 1818 while writing "Frankenstein," extending to hair and makeup. "Fashion also became a glue for this new scene. Black was the dominant color. Black would have to do until something darker came along." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**Early Hardcore Touring Network (The List)** : DOA compiled and circulated "the list," a comprehensive network of punk-friendly venues and places to stay across North America. This list was shared among dozens, potentially hundreds, of like-minded bands, greatly expanding the touring reach of punk and hardcore throughout the continent. "DOA compiled something called the list. This was a growing list of punk-friendly venues and places to stay across North America that was made available and then traded among dozens, maybe hundreds of other like-minded bands." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PEHkKfmmDiI
**Early Influential Figures** : Beyond religious figures and scientists, music has its own highly influential figures such as Elvis, Chuck Berry, The Beatles, Dylan, Marley, Hendrix, Prince, and David Bowie, who profoundly shaped the evolution of life on Earth. "Elvis, Chuck Berry, The Beatles, Dylan, Marley, Hendrix, Prince, and David Bowie." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yq2A_o9-ulM
**Early Instances of Gradual Musical Endings (1772, 1918)** : The concept of a gradual musical ending predates electronic fade-outs. Joseph Haydn's "Farewell Symphony" (1772) ended with musicians leaving the stage one by one. Gustav Holst's "The Planets" (1918) included instructions for a women's chorus to sing repeatedly from an unseen room as a door slowly closed, creating an auditory fade-out. "In 1772, Joseph Hayden wrote Symphony 45, which was Nick Dam the Farewell Symphony. The instructions for the performance are actually quite complicated. The last movement is called Neptune." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gP3oQeYEvso
**Early Instrumentals in Rock and Roll** : Instrumentals played a crucial role in early rock and roll, particularly in exploring new sounds and possibilities with the electric guitar. "Instrumentals were an important part of early rock and roll, especially when it came to exploring new sounds with the electric guitar." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pOeChe_D1Ag
**Early Internet Music Sharing Challenges (Pre-1999)** : Initially, using the internet for music sharing was cumbersome due to slow uploading/downloading speeds and clunky, awful software. This made early digital music distribution awkward and inefficient for users. "It was awkward at first because uploading and downloading took forever and the software you had to use was clunky and awful." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Early Internet Music Trading (Early 1990s)** : People began trading music online in the early 1990s, though the process was initially slow and buggy. The music industry was aware of this activity but did not consider file trading a serious threat, focusing instead on the rise of home CD burners. "People had been trading music online since the early 1990s, but it was a slow buggy process. The music industry knew all about it, but they really didn't take file trading seriously." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Early Jamaican Music Industry** : As Shuffle music gained popularity, especially among returning US soldiers, "a number of entrepreneurial artists got into the business of recording, manufacturing, selling, and broadcasting shuffle records." This marks the early development of a local music industry in Jamaica. "They wanted to take records home with them, so a number of entrepreneurial artists got into the business of recording, manufacturing, selling, and broadcasting shuffle records." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zNIVqKqAlnk
**Early Lack of Record Standardization** : In the early days of disc records, there was a complete absence of industry standards for record sizes and playback directions. Companies produced records in various widths—7-inch, 10-inch, 12-inch, 14-inch, 16-inch, and even 21-inch by NeoPhone in 1904. Additionally, some machines and records were designed to play from the inside out, rather than the more common outside-in. "But even once the idea of disks caught on, there was little in the way of standards. Some companies made records that were seven inches wide. Others issued ten-inch disks. Still others like twelve inches. Fourteen and sixteen-inch disks weren't uncommon. In a 1904, a British company called NeoPhone issued records that were twenty-one inches wide... And get this, some machines and records were designed to play the disk from the inside out, not the outside in." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VlIErdSSVOM
**Early MP3 Adoption (2001)** : By 2001, millions of people had grown comfortable sharing music using MP3s, and early, albeit "clunky," portable devices were emerging to allow listening to MP3s on the go. "By 2001, millions of people were very comfortable sharing music via MP3s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Early MP3 Trading (2000)** : By 2000, MP3s existed, and people had started trading music files online, although the experience was generally clunky and frustrating for most, unless they used Napster, which had been available for about six months. "We had MP3s, and we had begun to trade music files online, but that was still a fairly clunky and frustrating experience for most people..." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Early MP3 Users and Hard Drive Limitations (Mid-90s)** : In the mid-1990s, the first users of MP3 technology were individuals who converted (ripped) CDs onto their computer hard drives. Given that a 100-megabyte hard drive was considered substantial at the time and a full CD contained 640 megabytes, MP3 compression was essential for saving space and enabling users to store a selective collection of music. "And the first users were people who ripped CDs to their computer and hard drives. Now, remember, in the middle of the 90s, it was a really big deal if you had a 100-megabyte hard drive. A full CD was 640 megabytes, so you really needed to conserve space. You had to be picky with what you ripped to your hard drive. And Mb3's helped do the trick." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**Early Mechanical Music Production** : In ancient times, all music was produced mechanically, involving physical actions like hitting with hands or sticks, strumming, plucking, or forcing air over reeds or through valves. The advent of electricity later revolutionized this process. "Once upon a time, all music was made mechanically." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OiqdZfxdBP0
**Early Music Boxes (c. 1770)** : Music boxes, first invented in Switzerland around 1770, were mechanical devices that produced tunes. They functioned by a rotating metal cylinder with precisely placed bumps that plucked metal prongs, highlighting an early, non-intelligent form of automated music creation. "They were first invented in Switzerland somewhere around 1770." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Early On-Demand Music Acquisition** : For over a century, the primary method for individuals to listen to music on demand was through ownership, typically by purchasing physical media like a "piece of plastic" for a set price. This transaction represented both an emotional and financial investment, encouraging listeners to extract maximum enjoyment from their purchase. "for over a hundred years, the only way you could hear music on demand was to own it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jD1dWxNLvfE
**Early Playback Devices - Phonographs and Gramophones** : The first successful devices for playing music were phonographs and gramophones. Phonographs initially used Edison's cylinders, later transitioning to his rotating disc format. Gramophones, however, utilized Berliner's rotating disc idea, which ultimately prevailed in the format war, making modern turntables descendants of gramophones, despite often being referred to as phonographs. These early devices were bulky, housed in large wooden cabinets, and powered by heavy wind-up or electric motors. Prior to the 1930s, they lacked amplifiers and volume control, with volume determined solely by the size and shape of their acoustic horns. `"The first successful devices for playing back music were phonographs and gramophones."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Early Podcasting Attempt (2007)** : An attempt to launch podcasting for the show in 2007 did not gain traction. The prevailing concern at the time was that a podcast would "catapultize" or cannibalize the radio show's listenership, leading to its cancellation despite the potential for early adoption. "If we put out a podcast, well, that'll catapalize our radio. Yeah. So we're not going to do that. And we could have been doing this podcast in 2007, but there just wasn't a neat, it wasn't a market for it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4xxgPX-aR90
**Early Pre-Computer Ticketing Methods** : Before computers, concert tickets were acquired primarily through three methods: purchasing pre-printed hard tickets directly from a venue's box office, mailing a letter and check to receive a voucher exchangeable for tickets, or buying from official external ticket agencies that charged a fee of about $1.50. "In those early pre-computer days, there were three ways to buy a concert ticket." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OCkov6FTliE
**Early Pre-recorded Cassettes (1965)** : Record labels began releasing pre-recorded cassettes, known as "Music Cassettes," in Europe as early as 1965. These expanded to North America the following July, with 49 titles released simultaneously. From the late 1970s through the early 1990s, cassettes dominated portable music, becoming a major revenue source for record companies and stereo equipment manufacturers, including those specializing in aftermarket car stereos. `"Record label started releasing pre-recorded cassettes. This happened as early as 1965, starting first in Europe under the name Music Cassettes, and then they appeared the following July and North America, with 49 titles being released simultaneously."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Early Precursors to Concept Albums (1930s-1960s)** : The idea of an album as a cohesive unit can be traced back to 78 RPM recordings of show tunes in the 1930s and folk music collections that told stories. Later examples include Frank Sinatra's thematic "Songs for Lovers" in the 1950s (though without a unifying narrative), Johnny Cash's 1960 album *Ride This Train* (featuring train songs), and Ray Charles's *The Genius Hits the Road* (songs about specific locations). "Well, I guess we could go back to 78 RPM recordings of the 30s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Dm5N6yDRxXQ
**Early Radio Show Production (Analog Era)** : The first 250 episodes of the show involved a labor-intensive analog production process. Voice tracks were recorded on quarter-inch tape, then manually edited with razor blades and grease pencils. Music was laid onto multi-track tape in real time, and the entire production was mixed down to two-track tape for real-time radio playback. "So that's how it was for first 250 shows until we moved out of Brampton and went digital. Yeah, and that will go. It was a lot of work." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4xxgPX-aR90
**Early Radio Stations and Record Store Collaboration** : In the early days of radio, experimental stations like XWA in Montreal, one of the world's first, and KDKA in Pittsburgh, relied on local record stores for their programming. Engineers would obtain phonograph records from shops like Layton Brothers record store, which provided them for free in exchange for on-air announcements acknowledging their source. "When engineers got bored with speaking, they grabbed some phonograph records from the Layton Brothers record store down the street." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jD1dWxNLvfE
**Early Rap Stars and Musical Genius** : In the early to mid-1980s, despite the existence of "genuine stars" and "genuine musical geniuses" like Run DMC, Curtis Blow, Africa Bambaataa, Grandmaster Flash, and LL Cool J, the rap scene and its sounds were largely "not taken seriously" by a significant portion of the music market. "It didn't matter that there were genuine stars, genuine musical geniuses. One DMC, Curtis Blow, Africa Babata, Grandmaster Flash, LL Cool J, the scene and the sounds were just not taken seriously." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=l1jPP1FhGo8
**Early Recognition for Iggy Pop** : By grade 10, James Newell Osterberg (Iggy) was one of the most recognizable students at Ann Arbor High School near Detroit, known for his interest in politics and deep immersion in music, particularly Motown and Chicago blues. "By grade 10 he was one of the most recognizable kids at Ann Arbor High School near Detroit." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=w-rihv544I8
**Early Record Speed Confusion** : In the initial period of record players, there was no standard playback speed. Emil Berliner's original disks turned at 70 RPM, but other manufacturers' records could range from 50 to 120 RPM. This lack of standardization led to widespread consumer confusion and records that were incompatible with many players. "Berliner's original disks turned at seventy RPM, but other companies went anywhere from fifty to 120 RPM. Naturally, this resulted in a lot of incompatible records and a lot of consumer confusion." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VlIErdSSVOM
**Early Recorded Music Packaging - Edison Cylinders** : In the nascent days of the recorded music industry, Edison cylinders featured Thomas Edison's name most prominently on the packaging, printed larger than either the song title or the artist's name. No artwork was present. "If you bought one of the super old school Edison cylinders, Thomas Edison's name was the most prominent thing that you saw in the packaging." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pwebM3TfSdg
**Early Recorded Music Packaging - Flat Discs** : Flat rotating discs were sold in plain sleeves, with the only visual element being circular cutouts that allowed the record's center label to be seen. Special records might have decorated sleeves, but this was rare. Packaging was typically stark, practical, and cheap, made of brown paper or cardboard, and offered little protection for the fragile shellac records. "When the flat rotating disc came along, they were sold in plain sleeves. Their only nod to the visual was the part of the sleeve with the circular cutouts that allowed you to see the centered label of the record." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pwebM3TfSdg
**Early Recorded Music Sales Venues (Late 1880s)** : When pre-recorded music, in the form of Edison cylinders and Berliner discs, was first introduced in the late 1880s, it was primarily sold in furniture stores. These establishments also sold phonographs and gramophones, which were initially regarded as pieces of fine furniture, making their sale alongside records a logical pairing. "When pre-recorded music was introduced in the late 1880s, these Edison cylinders and Berliner discs were sold at furniture stores, the same places that sold phonographs and gramophones, which made sense, given that these first units were looked upon as fine furniture." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jD1dWxNLvfE
**Early Recording Fade-Outs** : An 1895 outdoor recording called "The Spirit of 76," featuring a marching band, achieved a fade-out by having the band march away from the acoustic recording horn, naturally diminishing the volume. A 1930 recording by George Olson's Big Band, "Beyond the Blue Horizon," ended with a train receding into the distance, simulating a fade. "The Spirit of 76 that featured a marching band. As the song winds down, the band marches away from the acoustic recording horn." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**Early Resistance to Streaming** : At the dawn of the 21st century, music fans, accustomed to collecting and possessing music, were generally reluctant to embrace streaming due to the perception that it amounted to "renting" music that would vanish upon subscription cancellation. "The knock again streaming was that you just rented it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Early Rock Concert Sound Systems (1950s-1960s)** : In the 1950s and 60s, a "proper concert sound system" did not exist. Most setups consisted of a combination mixer-amplifier powering two PA columns, positioned on either side of the stage, each containing two 12-inch speakers. These columns "weren't much bigger than standard guitar amps, and frankly, they weren't very loud or clear." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qrrwqdpCjpQ
**Early Smartphone Predecessors** : Mid-1990s predecessors to modern smartphones, such as Palm Treo and Sidekick, were generally "clunky and heavy and awful," with the Blackberry later emerging as a "game changer." "The four runners, too, today's smartphone started appearing in the middle 90s..." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Early Sony Digital Storage Development (1977-1978)** : Prior to collaborating with Philips, Sony had a head start in digital storage technology. In September 1977, they demonstrated an optical disk with a 30-centimeter diameter (matching a vinyl LP), capable of holding 60 minutes of music. A year later, they advanced this technology to a disk that could hold 150 minutes, showcasing their expertise in digital audio storage. `"Meanwhile, in Japan, Sony was working on digital recording machines and first demonstrated an optical disk that was 30 centimeters in diameter, the same diameter as a vinyl LP back in September of 1977."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Early Sony Optical Disk Prototypes (September 1977)** : In September 1977, Sony demonstrated an optical disk with a 30-centimeter diameter, matching a vinyl LP, capable of storing 60 minutes of music. A year later, they developed a 30-centimeter disk that could hold up to 150 minutes of audio. "Meanwhile, in Japan, Sony was working on digital recording machines and first demonstrated an optical disk that was 30 centimeters in diameter. So the same diameter as a vinyl LP, one foot. This was September 1977." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Early Sound Manipulation Techniques** : Before electronic instruments became widespread, musicians and engineers utilized various methods to alter the sounds of traditional instruments like guitars, bass, drums, and keyboards. These included effects pedals, studio tricks, and even "happy accidents" that occurred during the recording process, which provided plenty of creative possibilities for several decades. "There were plenty of ways to manipulate the sounds of those instruments, you know, effects, pedals, studio tricks, happy accidents that happened when you least expected them." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5PJRCC6EwUY
**Early Stereo System (Tiatrophone)** : The Tiatrophone system, demonstrated in 1881, featured a rudimentary form of stereo sound decades before it was officially "invented." It achieved this by placing 40 telephone transmitters on the left side of a stage and another 40 on the right, providing listeners with a spatialized audio experience in their homes. "And in stereo, too, this is like 70 years before stereo was invented because you had 40 transmitters put along the left side of the stage and another 40 along the right." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Early Telephone Exchange Design (George Koi)** : George Koi revolutionized telephone service by conceiving of a central exchange where all phones would connect, replacing the impractical system of direct wires between every two callers. He devised the idea for a specialized telephone operator to manually connect callers using short wires and durable, quick-connect plugs, ultimately leading to his invention of the quarter-inch funnel plug. "It was his idea that all phones be connected to a central exchange." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NAroxFbYkRw
**Early Use of "Rocking and Rolling" in Secular Music** : Trixie Smith's September 1922 record, "My Man Rocks Me with One Steady Roll," is recognized as the first example of the phrase "rocking and rolling" being used in a secular song, explicitly referring to sex. This historical detail provides a direct lineage for the term that would later define the rock and roll genre. "And as far as anyone can tell, this song, which is about sex in case you miss the memo, is the first example of the use of rocking and rolling to be used in a secular song." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oRR7N-YUcRE
**Early Video Sharing Sites Pre-YouTube** : While YouTube quickly became dominant, it was not the first video sharing site; Vimeo existed prior. However, Vimeo "never really developed as a standalone platform" in the way YouTube did. "Now YouTube wasn't the first video sharing site. Vimeo was actually first, but never really developed as a standalone platform." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Earworm** : This term describes a phenomenon where a snippet or clip of a song repeatedly plays in one's head, often feeling like it's on a continuous loop. "That's when a clip of a song keeps running through your head over and over and over again on a loop and a drive you crazy." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4cZfegexwwk
**Earworms – Involuntary Musical Imagery; Brain Fills in Silence; Best Cure Is a Sudoku** : When a song cuts out, the brain automatically continues it for a few seconds using stored musical memory. Sometimes a snippet catches in a loop — the neurological phenomenon known as an earworm. Almost 100% of the population experiences earworms. Key facts: they last longer and are more irritating for women; more than 75% of earworms involve lyrics; people with OCD are more susceptible; most loops are no longer than 30 seconds (the approximate buffer size of auditory cortex RAM). For some people, earworms are debilitating, persisting for months or years; only strong OCD medication helps. For most people, doing a sudoku puzzle appears to be an effective cure — the kind of brain processing required flushes the audio cache of the auditory cortex. "The best cure for an earworm seems to be to do a sudoku." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NMwMwqMQpf4
**Ease of Modern Music Creation** : The ease of music creation today is exemplified by software like Ableton Live and GarageBand (with its included "Beats and Stims"), songwriting methods such as "Top Lines and Beats," and the ability to purchase "ready-made beats online for a few bucks." Once created, a song can be uploaded to Spotify "for nothing" and made available globally. This contrasts with older methods that required mastering instruments and navigating a limited industry. "Others will point to the ease with which music can be created today." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m7MbV-eCLyE
**Easy Eye Sound - Dan Auerbach's Nashville Studio** : Easy Eye Sound is Dan Auerbach's personal "butter zone studio" in Nashville, where The Black Keys recorded *El Camino* in a relaxed environment with functional gear. "Dan had butter zone studio by this time, a place called Easy Eye Sound". ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PmwfC4HR3_I
**Ecclesiastes Inspiration** : The discussion on the cyclical nature of music revivals was introduced with a reading from Ecclesiastes, Chapter 3, Verses 1-3, which speaks to "a time to every purpose under the heavens." "We've been getting this episode with a reading from Ecclesiastes, Chapter 3, Versus 1-3." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gkLMwrthZ9s
**Echo and the Bunnymen – Just a Dumb Name They Chose from Several Dumb Options** : Legend says Echo was a drum machine or piece of gear. Not true. The band considered many silly names and chose this one. An alternate option that was seriously on the table: "Gilrol and the Fan Extractors." "The echo in echo in the bunnyman was the name of a drum machine or some other piece of gear. Not true." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Eco-Friendly Vinyl Initiatives** : Efforts are underway to develop more environmentally sustainable vinyl records. Some European pressing plants are achieving nearly 99% carbon neutrality in their manufacturing processes. Other companies are exploring the use of recycled vinyl, although historically (e.g., during the 1970s oil crisis), recycled vinyl was often impure. Experiments also include biodegradable PVC substitutes and even edible vinyl alternatives. "people are working on creating more eco-friendly vinyl records. There are a couple of pressing plants in Europe that approach being 99% carbon neutral in their manufacturing process." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2g4QSREzpw
**Econoline Crush – A Ford Econoline Van Jammed Full of Band and Equipment** : Ford manufactured a van called the Econoline. Being crammed with your band and all your equipment into one of these vans — the classic touring band experience — was an Econoline Crush. "If you and your equipment is jammed into that van, it's an Econoline Crush." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Economic Impact on New Wave** : A severe economic downturn in the late 1970s and early 1980s, marked by the disco bubble bursting and a worldwide recession, inadvertently benefited New Wave. Record labels found it cheaper to produce and release New Wave records than those by larger mainstream acts, leading to increased investment in the genre. "Record labels found that it was cheaper to produce and release records by this new generation of new wave bands than it was to do the same with bigger mainstream acts." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bYsV3ozbk4M
**Economic Incentive for Albums (Post-1965)** : Record labels found albums significantly more attractive than singles due to "profit margins [that] were so much higher on albums." This economic incentive drove the industry's shift away from singles and into the album-dominated world that has largely persisted until the streaming era. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Economic Return on Investment in Canadian Music** : A study by the Canadian Independent Music Association indicates that for every dollar invested into Canadian music, there is a return of $1.22 in economic benefits. This positive return underscores the financial justification for continued government and broadcaster investment in the Canadian music sector. "A study by the Canadian Independent Music Association says that for every dollar put into Canadian music, the return is a dollar 22 in economic benefits, which is pretty good." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6PGgNCaW4xo
**Economic and Strategic Drivers for Residencies** : The growth of larger performance spaces in Las Vegas hotels, coupled with the city's ambition to be more than a gambling destination, spurred an increase in artists signing residency deals. For performers, residencies offered a stable, lucrative engagement without the exhaustion of constant touring. "With more and more hotels opening bigger and bigger performance spaces, and with Vegas trying to diversify from being just a gambling destination, the number of artists signed to Vegas residences increased." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GYUuTbOvyak
**Ecumenical Tastes of Modern Youth** : Today's 15-year-olds have access to 120 million songs via streaming, encompassing the "entire library of recorded human musical history." This exposure leads to highly diverse and eclectic musical tastes, unlike previous generations who focused primarily on contemporary music. A Humber College student, for example, might listen to Drake, AC/DC, The Beatles, and Billie Holiday. "Your kid is absorbing far more music to influence him in his musical development than we ever did, because we were only focused on our largely on what was happening to us contemporaries, as contemporaries." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4xxgPX-aR90
**Ed Cobb - Songwriter of Dirty Water and Tainted Love** : Ed Cobb was the songwriter responsible for "Dirty Water," famously recorded by The Standells in 1965. He also notably penned "Tainted Love" in just 15 minutes, which became a massive synth classic hit in the early 1980s when covered by Soft Cell, after several earlier releases. "By the way, the writer of that song was Ed Cobb. A year earlier, he dashed off another song in 15 minutes that was released several times before it became a massive hit in the early 1980s when it was covered by soft-cell." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UpWz3X33vSY
**Ed Force One** : Iron Maiden's chartered jet for touring, a Boeing 747, is known as Ed Force One, and is sometimes captained by the band's frontman, Bruce Dickinson, who holds a commercial airline pilot's license. "He's been the captain of Ed Force One, Iron Maiden's chartered jet for when they tour." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jMfH9xAOpI
**Ed Sullivan Theater Marquee Performance** : Audioslave's debut performance on November 25, 2002, was unconventional, taking place on top of the marquee of the Ed Sullivan Theater as part of a _Late Show with David Letterman_ appearance. "They played on top of the marquee on the Ed Sullivan theater as part of an appearance on the late show with David Letterman." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vLZla5h4xKQ
**Eddie Vedder's Classmate Incident** : During Eddie Vedder's school days in San Diego, a classmate named Brian brought a sawed-off shotgun to school. Brian hid the gun in the bushes until lunchtime, then retrieved it, brought it into the building, and shot up a fish tank. Fortunately, he was disarmed before he could cause any further damage. This incident freaked out Vedder, who had previously had a confrontation with Brian and wondered if being in the wrong place at the wrong time could have led to more severe outcomes. "One of his classmates, a kid named Brian, brought a gun to school. It was a sought-off shotgun that he hid in the bushes until lunchtime." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4PlieAEthSg
**Eddie Vedder's Pearl Jam Audition Tape – Networking via Jack Irons** : Eddie Vedder secured his audition for Mookie Blaylock (which became Pearl Jam) through an intricate networking chain. Chris Cornell passed a cassette tape to Jack Irons, who in turn gave it to Eddie Vedder after they played basketball. Vedder then added his lyrics and vocals to the tape and mailed it back to Stone Gossard, Jeff Ament, and Mike McCready in Seattle, ultimately landing him the gig. "Jack received a cassette tape, which he was asked to pass along to any promising young singer. It just so happened that Jack played basketball with this surfer kid from San Diego named Eddie Vetter. Eddie had a pretty good voice, so Jack gave Eddie this cassette, who then wrote some lyrics and dubbed his singing onto the tape, and then mailed it back to Stone, Jeff and Mike and Seattle." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lip0bZX7hBE
**Egalitarianism in Alt Rock** : The explosion of alternative music in the 1990s fostered a sense of egalitarianism, where previously fringe ideas and sounds were welcomed into the mainstream. Being "weird" was not only tolerated but encouraged, leading to the celebration and promotion of artists who challenged mainstream tastes with their strangeness. "From the earliest days, being weird was not only tolerated but encouraged. People who annoyed the mainstream with their strangeness were celebrated and promoted." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=h6snk0m4200
**Eight-Track Decline and Collectible Status** : Eight-track popularity began to wane in the late 1970s, with record stores ceasing to stock them, though they remained available at truck stops and through clubs like Columbia Records and Tapes into the 1980s. The last major label pre-recorded eight-track release was Fleetwood Mac's `Greatest Hits` in 1987, with some Reader's Digest collections appearing in 1988. Curiously, certain eight-tracks have become highly collectible, with a limited-run Frank Sinatra tape being the most valuable, trading for up to $5,000 US. `"The popularity of eight tracks went into decline in the late 1970s. Record stores stopped stocking them. Although you can still find them at truck stops for a while."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Eight-Track System Challenges** : Despite its innovation, the eight-track format had several notable drawbacks. Playback heads could become misaligned, or if the tape didn't run perfectly flat, music from adjacent tracks would "bleed into others." Users could fast forward but not rewind. Track changes were accompanied by a distinct "Colunque" sound. Albums often required song reordering to fit evenly across the four pairs of tracks, and sometimes songs would fade out, transition with a "Colunque," and then fade back in. `"Well, not really, because if the playback heads were misaligned or if the tape didn't run perfectly flat, music from adjacent tracks would bleed into others."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Eight-Track's Early Success and Durability** : The eight-track was the first practical and workable method for playing user-selected music in a car. It offered better audio quality than AM radio (before FM car radios were common) and proved more durable against abuse than other pre-recorded music formats, although tapes could still jam. Ford was an early adopter, installing 65,000 units in 1966 Mustangs, Thunderbirds, and Lincolns, and the format spread to other manufacturers, including Rolls Royce. By 1967, eight-track players were available for home stereos and as portable battery-powered units, with RadioShack selling many. `"Still, the eight track was the first practical and workable way to play your choice of music in the car."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Elden Hoke's Death (April 18, 1997)** : Elden Hoke, the frontman of The Mentors who claimed Courtney Love offered him money to kill Kurt Cobain, died on April 18, 1997. His obituary stated he was decapitated when hit by a train while walking home from a gig, and he was in a state of alcoholic intoxication at the time. "Elduche was decapitated when he was hit by a train walking home from a gig on April 18, 1997." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1Jt2fUBDusk
**Elders' Attitudes Towards Young People's Music (Late 19th/Early 20th Century)** : The negative attitudes of older generations towards the music of the young intensified in the late 19th and early 20th century. While church hymns and classical music were deemed acceptable ("uplifting and sophisticated"), folk songs were considered "vulgar," and show tunes were thought to be dangerously energetic. The emergence of jazz from New Orleans around 1900 was particularly condemned as "vile and evil," especially due to its association with African Americans. "These attitudes of elders towards music of the young only accelerated in the late 19th and early 20th century." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m7MbV-eCLyE
**Electric Guitar Distortion Pre-Fuzz Pedal** : Before the widespread availability of fuzz pedals, achieving distorted guitar sounds was challenging. Some performers deliberately overrode amplifiers or physically damaged speakers to create growly, powerful sounds, but these were uncontrollable and difficult to duplicate. Accidental equipment failures, such as the faulty transformer that created Grady Martin's fuzz sound, were also sources of unique, distorted tones. "There were a few performers who realized that by deliberately overdrive the amplifier or actually causing physical damage to the speaker, you could distort the guitar sound into something growly and powerful, but it was impossible to control those sounds." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6SdPPsmpgJg
**Electrical Audio Studio Ownership** : Electrical Audio, a recording studio located in Chicago, is owned by Steve Albini. This studio is notable for having recorded Nirvana's "In Utero" album, and later the Foo Fighters' "Something from Nothing." "That track was recorded at electrical audio in Chicago, the studio owned by Steve Elbeni, a guy who recorded Nirvana's in utero album." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Mh-qg1izM98
