**Electrical Recording (1925)** : Introduced in 1925, electrical recording revolutionized the industry by incorporating microphones and amplifiers. This marked a significant departure from older acoustic methods, allowing for greater fidelity and flexibility in capturing sound. "Alright, fast forward to 1925 and the introduction of electrical recording." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Electrification of America (Early 20th Century)** : The electrification of America was a major societal development in the early 20th century, providing widespread access to electricity. This infrastructure was a prerequisite for many electronic inventions, including those that would lead to amplified sound. "America needed access to electricity. The electrification of America, a story in itself, was a major deal in the early 20th century." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**Electronic Fade-Outs Emerge (c. 1950s)** : Song fade-outs, as commonly known, first appeared around 1950, coinciding with the shift from direct-to-disk recording to magnetic tape. Magnetic tape allowed for editing and multi-track recording, making electronic fade-outs possible by gradually turning down input volume from microphones. "Songfade-outs, as we know them, first appeared sometime around 1950." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**Electronics and Future Music** : The increasing affordability and power of synthesizers and related electronics in the 1980s attracted tech-minded musicians uninterested in traditional instruments. This segment of the music world equated synthesizers with the "music of the future," which in turn connected to science fiction, a realm strongly associated with nerds. "First of all, tech-minded people into music who weren't interested in traditional instruments were attracted to the technology." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=h6snk0m4200
**Elephant 6 Collective** : The Elephant 6 Collective is mentioned as a group with "certainly something to be discovered" for those interested in the broader landscape of indie music, implying its significant cultural or musical relevance within the scene, potentially appealing to fans of Tweetpop or related genres. "There's certainly something to be discovered with the Elephant 6 collective." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jhgZrwtYE2g
**Elimination of CD Singles (Late 1990s)** : The recorded music industry made a strategic decision to eliminate the CD single, which was the digital equivalent of the traditional 7-inch single. This move was perceived as cynical, aimed at compelling consumers to purchase entire albums even if they only desired one specific song. "The recorded music industry made the cynical decision to eliminate the CD single, which was the digital equivalent of the old 7-inch single. The attitude seemed to be, what, you want just that one song on the album? Too bad, no more singles for you. You buy the whole album." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Elliot Kahn's Dual Career** : Elliot Kahn, the lawyer responsible for pitching Green Day to various record labels, also had a career as a musician. He was a singer in Sha Na Na, a 1950s revival group. "Elliot was this lawyer, but he was also a musician. Anyone remember the 50s revival group, Shanaana? Yeah, Elliot used to be part of that. That was his singing gig." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nyfdGYWelic
**Elton John Had a Knee Replaced and Wears the Kneecap as a Necklace** : Elton John has had both knees replaced and had one of the removed kneecaps fashioned into a necklace, which he wears for special occasions. "He's had one of these kneecaps fashioned into a necklace." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Elvis Presley's Graceland** : Graceland, Elvis Presley's former home, is acknowledged as a major and widely recognized shrine in rock and roll history. It stands as a prime example of a place that people make pilgrimages to because of its strong association with a legendary artist. "Elvis's Graceland doesn't get much bigger than that, doesn't." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sKHdTSfDd6E
**Elvis Presley's Rejected Movie Roles – Cat on a Hot Tin Roof, West Side Story, Midnight Cowboy, The Godfather, Willy Wonka** : Colonel Tom Parker turned down numerous prestigious film offers for Elvis, including Cat on a Hot Tin Roof, West Side Story, Midnight Cowboy, and The Godfather (the role of Tom Hagen, which went to Robert Duvall). Elvis was also briefly considered for Willy Wonka in Charlie and the Chocolate Factory, which went to Gene Wilder. "Can you imagine Elvis being in that role?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**Email-Themed Coffee Shop "Melted Vinyl Coffee"** : There is an email-themed coffee shop named "Melted Vinyl Coffee" located in Brea, California. This establishment has quickly gained popularity on TikTok. "there's now an email themed coffee shop. It's called melted vinyl coffee. It's in Brea, California. And they become quick thing on TikTok." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HoBx6s3JmVQ
**Embarrassment of Riches in Early 90s** : The early 1990s were marked by an "embarrassment of riches," where it seemed that "a week did not go by without hearing some exciting new songs from some brilliant new artists," indicating a highly vibrant and prolific music scene. "For a few years, it seemed that a week did not go by without hearing some exciting new songs from some brilliant new artists. It really was an embarrassment of riches." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=amHre9ZZFkU
**Embedded Artwork in Digital Formats** : Digital music formats like MP3s, Apple's M4A, and ACC have the capability to contain artwork directly within the files, referred to as CoverArt or CoverImages. However, consumers often don't notice these, and when they do, the images are frequently low-resolution JPEGs. "MP3s, Apple's M4A and ACC all have the ability to contain artwork within the files. But when you're happy people take the time to notice it, and if you do bother to look at this art, it's often a low-resolution JPEG image." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pwebM3TfSdg
**Emergence of "Turntable" and "Record Player"** : In the late 1950s and throughout the 1960s, as high-fidelity audio systems gained popularity in homes, the terms "turntable" and "record player" emerged. These new terms were perceived as more modern and aligned with the evolving technology than the older designations of "phonograph" and "gramophone." "The final step in this etymology came in the late 1950s and through the 1960s, when high fidelity audio systems started becoming really popular in homes. The word turntable, or the term record player, sounded way more modern than the old words, phonograph, and gramophone." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TsiYLGGSJ2U
**Emergence of AIDS Epidemic** : In 1981, a mysterious disease initially known as GRID (Gay-Related Immune Deficiency) began to appear, affecting gay men, hemophiliacs, and Haitian immigrants, though it received limited media attention. By July 1982, it was renamed AIDS (Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome), and in May 1983, the causative virus, HIV, was isolated. The epidemic rapidly escalated, with 7,669 AIDS cases reported in the U.S. by the end of 1984, a number that nearly doubled to 15,948 just months later. "By the end of 1984, a total of 7,669 AIDS cases have been reported in the United States." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Somq2MoRi5Q
**Emergence of Bonus Tracks** : The concept of a "bonus track" evolved from the B-side, particularly with the advent of 12-inch singles that offered more space for additional songs. These tracks were considered extra content beyond the main single and sometimes included valuable, non-album material. "But it was also a new kind of B side, something that we began to call a bonus track." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**Emergence of Kazaa and BitTorrent (2001)** : Following legal challenges against Napster that ultimately led to its demise, two other file-sharing programs, Kazaa and BitTorrent, were introduced in 2001, further fueling the growth of online music piracy. "But then in 2001, just as Napster was being sued out of existence, two other programs were introduced into the wild. There was Kazah and BitTorrent." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Emergence of Modern Ticket Scalping (Mid-1800s)** : The contemporary form of ticket scalping emerged in the mid-1800s. "Sidewalk men" would acquire tickets for events and resell them at a premium to people waiting outside theaters. "But ticket scalping, as we know it today, first appeared in the mid 1800s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D8GU8ZpSgY8
**Emergence of Music Licensing Industry** : Following the success of early music licensing deals in advertising, a new industry emerged dedicated solely to navigating the legal complexities of licensing real music from real artists for commercial use. These companies aimed to connect advertisers with meaningful, non-generic music to help sell goods and services. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Emergence of Rude Boys** : The competitive nature of the sound system scene, combined with growing alienation, unemployment, and impoverishment among music fans, led to the emergence of a new group known as "Rude Boys." They originated from the ghettos of Kingston, Ocho Rios, and Montego Bay. "This became a very competitive thing, and eventually a new group of alienated, unemployed, and impoverished music fans called Rude Boys emerged from the ghettos of Kingston, Ocho Rios and Montego Bay." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zNIVqKqAlnk
**Emergence of the 78 RPM Standard** : Eventually, the industry converged on 78 RPM as the standard playback speed, determined to offer the best balance of sound quality, optimal capacity per side, and minimal wear. However, even this standard varied slightly based on regional electricity supply: 78.26 revolutions per minute in North America (60 hertz, 120 volts) versus 77.92 revolutions per minute in Europe/UK (50 hertz, 220 or 240 volts), a difference detectable by "sensitive ears." "Eventually, though, everyone came together and decided that seventy-eight RPM would provide the best level of sound, the optimum capacity per side, and the least wear." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VlIErdSSVOM
**Emerging Rock Star Species (Rock Starryous Achidious)** : Evolutionary studies propose the emergence of a more advanced species of rock star, dubbed "rock starryous achidious," which diverges from the dominant, backward "caveman" stereotype. "Scientists call these emerging species rock starryous achidious, and the body of evidence is growing larger every day." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QfiRRtsZoYU
**Emil Berliner's Gramophone** : In the late 19th century, German inventor Emil Berliner conceptualized the gramophone, a device for storing music as microscopic bumps in a spiral groove on a flat, rotating platter. "Goes all the way back to the late 19th century to a German inventor named Emil Berliner." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IIJySSo276k
**Emmanuel Jacques Murder (Toronto, 1977)** : The sexual assault and murder of Emmanuel Jacques, a 12-year-old Portuguese immigrant who was lured into an apartment, raped for 12 hours, and drowned in a sink above a massage parlor, profoundly shocked Toronto. This incident shattered the city's self-image as "good, moral, and proper," a place where it was even "tough to get a drink or anything else on a Sunday," making it ripe for the shake-up that punk rock offered. "And then there was the sexual assault and murder of Emmanuel Jacques, a 12-year-old Portuguese immigrant who was lured into an apartment above a massage parlor where he was raped over a 12-hour period before he was drowned in a sink." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Emo Kid Stereotype** : Over the 1980s and 1990s, the underground gestation of emotional hardcore culture led to the creation of a stereotypical "emo kid," often depicted with black hair, long bangs, black clothes, and glasses with thick rims. This image also carried stigmas such as depression and self-harm. "Emotional culture gestated underground through the 80s and 90s, resulting in the creation of the stereotypical emo kid with his or her black hair, the long bangs, the black clothes, the glasses with the thick rims." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_yslM5oanRo
**Empire Records (1995) Soundtrack Hits** : The soundtrack for the 1995 movie *Empire Records*, which starred Liv Tyler and Renée Zellweger, was "loaded with tracks from the 80s and 90s," though only 16 were released on the CD. It produced two legitimate hits: "Till I Hear It From You" by the Gin Blossoms and "A Girl Like You" from Edwin Collins. "The movie is loaded with tracks from the 80s and 90s, although only 16 were released on the soundtrack CD." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**Empowerment of Individual Music Fans** : With the advent of the internet, the traditional gatekeepers of music are gone, and power now lies with individual music fans. They are free to pursue any musical interests, acting as their own A&R persons, music directors, editors, and curators, and no longer need external sources to dictate what they listen to. "With the internet, the power lies with the individual music fan." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Encore Revival in Rock and Roll** : The encore tradition was revived in the 1950s and 60s with the advent of rock and roll. Artists would leave the stage after their main set only to return for a few more songs, possibly as a spontaneous response to an excited audience or influenced by Broadway productions where actors returned for extra bows. "When Rock came along in the 50s and 60s, the tradition was revived. At the end of a set, artists would leave the stage only to return to play a few more minutes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bferkEWvJg0
**Encore Tradition Origins (18th Century)** : The practice of encores dates back to at least the 18th century, a time before recorded music when live performance was the only way to hear favorite pieces. Audiences, or wealthy patrons, would shout "Encore" (French for "again") or "Unotera" (Italian for "one other") to demand a repeat of popular songs or parts of pieces, even during a performance. "This is a tradition that goes back to at least the 18th century." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bferkEWvJg0
**Encores in Dance Halls and Vaudeville** : By 1900, the idea of an encore was largely considered "uncouth," but the tradition persisted in dance halls and vaudeville. Due to the lack of recorded music, audiences would yell "Encore" for popular songs, compelling performers to play them multiple times. "When we get to dance halls and vaudeville, the same thing happened. You couldn't hear songs any other way than to see them live." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bferkEWvJg0
**End of the Wave** : The Britpop "wave" concluded precisely on schedule, about six years after its inception, replaced by new trends like the Spice Girls and girl power, demonstrating the cyclical nature of music trends. "About six years had gone by, and the wave was over." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5q5EnZj1FwE
**Enduring Desire for Physical Music** : A segment of the audience continues to desire physical music ownership. Bands and labels respond by issuing special editions and features, such as T-shirts or posters, that cannot be digitally downloaded, catering to collectors and those who value tangible media. "A portion of us will still want to own music in a physical form. Both bands and record labels will continue to issue special editions and feature stuff that you can't download." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Enduring Musical Influences** : New Wave's influences continue to be felt today, fragmenting into various subgenres like Electroclash, Cold Wave, Dark Wave, Synth Wave, and Chill Wave, as well as terms like Post Disco, New Pop, and New Wave. "New Wave's influences continue to be felt today." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9CNNsqXiL6U
**Enduring Popularity of 90s Music** : Music from the 1990s, particularly grunge and related acts, was so popular and good that audiences continued to demand it into the 21st century. Many acts from that era remained active and popular, while even defunct bands like Nirvana and Stone Temple Pilots maintained significant fanbases, making it challenging for new artists to break through due to the limited "oxygen" and airplay available. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Enduring Role of Albums in the Music Industry** : Despite the streaming-driven focus on singles, albums still serve as the primary "currency" of the recorded music industry. Contracts, awards, and sales in physical stores (CDs and vinyl LPs) are still based on albums, ensuring their short-term survival even as CD sales decline annually. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Enduring Success of Pre-2000 Artists** : Artists who achieved success before the year 2000, such as Pearl Jam, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Green Day, The Offspring, The Rolling Stones, Paul McCartney, and U2, often continue to thrive today, sustained by the career momentum built in the pre-internet era. "The acts that thrived pre-2000 are in many cases still thriving today." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Engineered Electronic Sound Revolution** : The ability for musicians to create previously unimagined sounds was born when engineers began to manipulate electricity in novel ways. This innovation led to an "explosion of new, amazing music" predominantly based on electronic sounds, with experimentation taking off in the 1960s. "But when engineers started messing with electricity in new ways, it became possible for musicians to create sounds that not only we'd never heard before, but never imagined hearing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5PJRCC6EwUY
**English Mood and Goth Music** : The gloomy music made by early goths was seen as reflective of the mood in England during the late 1970s and early 1980s, characterized by high unemployment, endless strikes, recession, and Margaret Thatcher's policies, especially impacting young people. "Whatever the case, these music fans began making music that was equal to the mood in England at the time. There was high unemployment, there were endless strikes, a recession, market thatcher, very popular, high unemployment, especially for young people." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XaLsZFLrMnI
**English Working Class Mentality of "Knowing Your Place"** : A strong "class thing in England," especially within the working class, dictates a mentality of "you don't get above your station." This concept implies that even when successful, individuals are expected to "know your place" and not appear overly enthusiastic or self-important, a trait exemplified by Shaun Ryder's "not enjoying it" persona. "It's a class thing in England. So working class thing, you don't get above your station." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=q_Scyr2czh8
**English Working-Class "Know Your Place" Mentality** : A deep-seated class dynamic in England, especially prevalent in the working class, instills a sense of "knowing your place" and not getting "above your station," even when achieving success. This can lead to insecurity among working-class individuals in positions of authority or fame. "if you're working class and you're, and you make something, you know, you do something... you've got to know, you know, you know, you know, you know, you're place, it's strange." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KF_adyXf86Q
**Enigma Veyron EV1D Speakers (Million-Dollar Audio)** : For audiophiles and those in the top 1% income bracket, there exists a set of speakers called the Enigma Veyron EV1D, produced by Karma. These speakers are equipped with Omega F drivers, and their tweeter cones are covered in diamonds, retailing for over $1 million per pair. "there's a set of speakers called the Enigma Veyron EV1D. Here from a manufacturer called Karma, equipped with something called Omega F. Drivers, the surfaces of the tweeter cones are covered in diamonds. These things retail for over $1 million a pair." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**Entrenched UK Class System (1970s)** : The class system in the UK was "firmly entrenched," with opportunities for "electricity and advancement" primarily linked to birth rather than individual ability. "The class system was firmly entrenched. The electricity in advancement was linked to birth, not ability." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Environmental Groups** : The Warped Tour also supported various environmental groups as part of its broad philanthropic outreach. "and various groups dealing with the environment." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jfQaUV6Bq4
**Environmental Impact of Music Consumption (Physical vs. Streaming)** : Contrary to popular belief, physical music formats like vinyl records and CDs can be more environmentally friendly than streaming, depending on consumption patterns. Streaming and downloading music require energy for servers, routers, and playback devices. However, playing a downloaded song consumes significantly less energy than repeatedly streaming it. An estimated 80% reduction in carbon emissions from digital music consumption could be achieved if all streamed songs were downloaded instead. If an album is streamed 27 times, it becomes more ecologically sound to purchase a physical copy. "If you stream an album 27 times, it's actually better for the planet just to go ahead and buy a physical copy of it, be that a vinyl record or a CD." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2g4QSREzpw
**Eric Stefani Left No Doubt to Animate The Simpsons** : Eric Stefani, brother of Gwen Stefani and keyboardist in No Doubt, quit the band before it really took off to become an animator for The Simpsons. He did contribute some parts to the Tragic Kingdom album before leaving. Separately, Tunde Adebimpe of TV on the Radio also worked as an animator for The Simpsons; David Bowie collected some of his artwork. "He quit before the band really took off." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Erosion of Cultural Gatekeepers by Streaming** : Prior to streaming, entities like record labels, radio stations, and print media acted as cultural gatekeepers, filtering music for quality and commercial viability, thus fostering a general consensus about popular music. Streaming, by offering access to millions of songs on demand, transformed individuals into their own gatekeepers, leading to a fragmented listening landscape where a broad consensus is rare beyond a few superstars. "For decades and decades and decades, music had to go through a series of cultural gatekeepers, record labels, record stores, radio stations, print media." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5Y6nHw5xx88
**Erosion of Royalty Income for Older Acts** : The past reliable system where acts could generate substantial, consistent royalty checks from decades-old album sales (e.g., The Doors going platinum annually, Beastie Boys selling 2 million *Licensed to Ill* annually, Eagles/Fleetwood Mac retiring on royalties) has ceased. These "retirement annuities" have shrunk, forcing even established acts to tour to maintain their lifestyle and cash flow. "That's not happening anymore. These royalty checks, which were like retirement annuities, have shrunk and shrunk and shrunk." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Estate Planning Benefits of Catalog Sales** : Selling a catalog can simplify estate planning, allowing artists to determine the fate of their money and property after death, avoiding disputes that have plagued estates of artists like Elvis, Jimi Hendrix, and Prince, who died without wills. "There are other advantages too, estate planning. You can determine what will happen with your money and all your property when you die. So many stars from Elvis to Jimmy Hendrix to Prince died without wheels or died without having their affairs in order." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Ethical Refusals in Licensing Deals** : Some artists maintain strong ethical boundaries, choosing to refuse licensing offers for certain types of products or campaigns. Examples include products promoting meat, anything related to the military, or beer companies, reflecting personal or moral objections. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Ethical and Legal Debates on AI Voice Cloning (Early 2024)** : The rise of AI-generated music employing cloned voices and styles of existing artists has ignited significant moral, ethical, and legal controversies. As of early 2024, legal frameworks are struggling to keep pace with the rapid technological advancements, raising critical questions about unauthorized voice usage, intellectual property, and fair compensation for artists whose work informs AI models. "As of early 2024 this is a case where the law lags behind the technology." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Etymology of "Nerd"** : The word "nerd" first appeared in a 1500s book titled "a true discourse of the assault committed upon the most noble prince Prince William of Orange, County of NASA, Marquess de la Verre and C," written by John Jarkuy Speniel. It fell out of use around 1725 before being revived by Dr. Seuss in 1950 to describe a peculiar creature. "According to these nerds, nerd, the word, shows up for the first time in a book called a true discourse of the assault committed upon the most noble prince Prince William of Orange, County of NASA, Marquess de la Verre and C, written by John Jarkuy Speniel." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=h6snk0m4200
**European Encore Bans** : Encores were not always appreciated; at one point, many European opera houses, including those in Italy and Germany, banned them, deeming them too disruptive. Emperor Joseph II of Austria, a fan of Mozart, was among the first to make such a ruling. "At one point, many European opera houses banned Encore's, saying that they were too disruptive." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bferkEWvJg0
**Everett True - Melody Maker Writer** : Everett True was a writer for the British music paper Melody Maker. Subpop flew him to Seattle in early 1989, where he wrote "very favorable things" about grunge, resulting in a cover story on March 18, 1989, which significantly boosted grunge hype in the UK. "This guy's name was Everett True. And he wrote some very favorable things about what he saw and heard on his trip to America." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DSvoppf1dGY
**Every Noise At Once Website** : Every Noise At Once is a website attempting to create an algorithmically generated, readability-adjusted scatter plot of the musical genre space, based on data tracked and analyzed by Spotify for 5,987 distinct genres. "head over to a site called Every Noise It Once. This is an attempt at, and here's the best definition I've found, to create an algorithmically generated readability-adjusted scatter plot of the musical genre space based on data tracked and analyzed for 5,987 genres shaped distinctions by Spotify." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tdTs-4Irv8c
**Evils of Fluoridation Conspiracy Theory** : The belief in the "evils of fluoridation" is listed as a conspiracy theory. "The evils of fluoridation" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pmKjZzypzvg
**Evolution of A/B Side Designation** : Initially, 7-inch singles treated both sides equally, labeled A and B merely for serial numbering (e.g., 2830-A, 28030-B) with no preference. By the early 1960s, as 45 RPM singles outsold albums, record labels began designating the song with the greatest commercial potential as the A-side for radio airplay priority, while the B-side became a placeholder. "But as the relationship between the recorded music industry and radio developed, the label started choosing which songs they wanted to be played. And by the early 1960s, which is when RPM singles far outsold albums, the song that was to be given priority by the record company and hopefully by the radio station, because it had the greatest commercial potential, was increasingly dedicated as the A side." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**Evolution of Album Artwork Industry** : The success of the first album cover artwork by Alex Steinweiss led to the creation of an entirely new industry, with design studios worldwide dedicated to creating album art. This profession faced challenges as album formats shrunk from 12x12 inch vinyl to smaller CD covers (6x6 or 5x5 inches) and then to digital files and streaming, though the resurgence of vinyl has revitalized interest in the art form. "A whole new industry was created." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Evolution of Album Artwork Presentation** : The glory of album artwork faced decline with the advent of digital formats. Initially, it was shrunk to CD size, then reduced to a postage stamp-sized image on platforms like iTunes, and became almost non-existent with streaming services. In pre-digital and pre-CD days, artwork was crucial for the listening experience, mystery, mystique, and marketing of music, sometimes even prompting purchases solely based on a cool-looking cover. Album artwork was once highly revered, inspiring coffee table book collections like *The 100 Best Album Covers* and *The Album Cover Album*, which treated rock and pop album covers with respect and offered insights into both the musical artists and the artists who created the artwork. "For a while, we were in real danger of seeing the glories of album artwork disappear." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sn5TWBFd0XM
**Evolution of Bass Instrument Role** : The evolution of the bass instrument from tubas to double basses, and finally to the electric bass like the Fender Precision, marked a significant shift in music. The electric bass offered solutions to volume, portability, and playability that its predecessors could not, quickly becoming the new standard. "Throughout the 1950s, the big double bass disappeared, and it was replaced with a relatively tiny Fender Precision, which was easier to transport, play, amplify, and modify." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Y0HW8LQKvJw
**Evolution of FM Radio Rules (1960s)** : Changes to Canadian FM radio rules in the 1960s prevented stations from simply re-broadcasting AM content. New, stringent rules limited song plays to 18 times a week and restricted hit songs (Top 40) to under 49% of the playlist, forcing programmers to explore albums for more diverse material. This also created time for longer album rock tracks due to limited commercial minutes, with 30% of these songs also needing to be Canadian. "One of the original rules was that it was illegal to play a song more than 18 times a week on any given station. It meant that stations had to play more songs to fill the broadcast day." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6PGgNCaW4xo
**Evolution of Fade-Out Styles** : Over the years, different types of fade-outs developed, including the quick fade for AM radio, the very fast Motown fade, and the long, slow FM fade, exemplified by The Eagles' "Hotel California." Fade-outs have cycled in and out of popularity, big in the 60s/70s, less so in the 80s/90s, and hit-or-miss today. "Over the years, different types of fades appeared. There was the quick fade for AM radio." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**Evolution of Hologram and Virtual Performances** : The concept of virtual performers has roots in the late 19th-century "Pepper's Ghost" illusion. Modern advancements, starting with a virtual Elvis Presley in 2007 and notably a lifelike Tupac Shakur at Coachella in 2012, have progressed through CGI and body doubles (Michael Jackson, 2014) to full hologram tours for deceased artists and virtual performances for living bands like ABBA. "The roots of holograms or something close to them goes all the way back to the late 19th century with an illusion called pepper's ghosts." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GYUuTbOvyak
**Evolution of Music Video Success Metrics (Online Era)** : In the online era, the measurement of music video success shifted from broadcast popularity to digital metrics like view count, "stickiness" (how long viewers watched), click-through rates to other artist videos, and shares (email, social media, website). This data became "extremely valuable" for artists and labels. "The measurement of success for music videos changed too. How many views? How sticky is a video?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Evolution of Musical Preferences Today** : The rigid tribal rules in music have largely broken down in the modern era, leading to a more "ecumenical" approach where the prevailing philosophy is to respect all music and listen to whatever one wants. This openness is seen as healthy for expanding musical vistas and influences. "Today, though, those rigid tribal rules have broken down. If you entered an era where we're very ecumenical about music, more and more the prevailing philosophy is luck. Respect all music, just listen to what you want." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2EerEOuP52I
**Evolution of Musical Sensibilities by Fuzz (1960s)** : The advent of the fuzz pedal in the 1960s triggered a significant shift in musical tastes and sensibilities. Sounds that, a mere decade earlier, might have been dismissed as "painful noise" began to be perceived as desirable and good. This unexpected evolution in taste fundamentally changed what was considered acceptable and appealing in music, leading to new sonic possibilities. "Ten years earlier we might have dismissed this new sound as painful noise. But thanks to the fuzz pedal, our taste began to evolve in a completely unexpected direction." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PNiL63fY1Y8
**Evolution of Publishing Industry** : Publishing became more complex with the advent of recorded music (cylinders, rotating discs in early 20th century), then movies, radio, television, various formats (vinyl, tape, CDs), digital downloads, and now streaming. Publishers represent songwriters, lyricists, and composers to ensure compensation for commercial use. "Publishing became much more complicated when we started recording music on cylinders and rotating discs at the beginning of the 20th century. Then came movies, radio, television, all the different formats like vinyl tape and CDs, then digital downloads and now streaming." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Evolution of Sitcom Storytelling (Pre-Seinfeld vs. Seinfeld)** : Before Seinfeld, most sitcoms typically followed an A story and a B story, with a "runner" joke loosely continuing throughout the episode without much more narrative integration. Seinfeld, however, revolutionized this by having each character pursue their own storyline, which then converged in the final moments to create a joke "larger than its parts." "Before Seinfeld, most sitcoms broke down into an A story and a B story, and the surrounding material could take the form of a so-called runner, a joke that continued throughout the episode and told a very loose story but didn't do much more than that." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6O5TCTO-go4
**Evolution of Songwriting for Streaming** : To counter high skip rates and ensure artists get paid, modern songwriting has evolved to prioritize holding listener attention within the crucial first 30 seconds, leading to shorter intros, front-loaded choruses, and an abundance of immediate hooks. "It's become the goal of artists and composers and producers to write songs that will hold our attention for that important first thirty seconds." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Evolution of Time Signatures in Popular Music** : Up until the early 1900s, the beat of a song was not a primary focus in music genres like classical, folk, or religious music. Waltz time (3/4) and marching music (2/4) were common. However, with the rise of the piano and the spread of jazz, particularly ragtime's use of the left hand for a steady rhythm, four-four time became the standardized beat for most popular songs throughout the 20th century. "Up until the early 1900s, the beat of a song really wasn't that much of a big deal. The most common time signature was three quarter time, wallstime, followed by the two four time of marching music, which was a really, really big deal until sometime after 1900. But with the rise of the piano and the spread of jazz, especially in early form called ragtime, which was among the first new musical forms to use the left hand and the lower notes to supply a steady rhythm to what the right hand was playing, time signatures fell into four four time, the standard beat for most of today's popular songs. Number four was the beat of the 20th century." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dJiSZ4X0F2g
**Evolution of Tribalism in Rock** : During the height of grunge, rock music was highly tribal, with clear divisions between "mainstream rockers" and "alternative kids," and no switching teams. Today, while tribal aspects persist, it's more acceptable to have diverse artists like The Smashing Pumpkins, The Beatles, Black Sabbath, and new indie bands, along with hip hop and pop, on the same playlist. "At the height of the Grunge days, it was all pretty tribal on one side with the mainstream rockers and the other the alternative kids. You were either in one group or the other, no changing teams, that's the way it was." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tFBSrKMEZxI
**Evolution of Underground Music** : Independent labels fostered the "experimental, low-fi, developed underground" music scene. This underground gave rise to proto-power pop groups like Jonathan Richman and the Modern Lovers and punk bands such as the Ramones. These bands, in turn, influenced acts like the Sex Pistols, leading to the formation of hundreds, if not thousands, of new bands in the UK. Simultaneously, Island Records, through Bob Marley, exported reggae globally. "The walked out experimental low five developed underground, which begat the proto-power pop of groups like Jonathan Richmond and the modern lovers and punks like the Ramones, both of which found favor with the sex pistols who then spurred the formation of hundreds of not thousands of bands in the UK." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dDcD8kg0wEk
**Evolution of iPod Models** : The original iPod was followed by a diverse range of models, including the Nano, the diminutive Shuffle, and the "super cool" iPod Touch. The first-generation iPod Touch featured a large touchscreen, Wi-Fi connectivity, and up to 32 gigabytes of storage, reflecting the continuous innovation in the portable music device market. "Most people were better off with the growing line of iPods, from the original style, to the nano, to the tiny shuffle, to the super cool iPod touch, which had a big touch screen and could connect to Wi-Fi and had up to 32 gigs of storage within its first generation model." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**Evolutionary Purpose of Music - Collective Voice** : Early humans used music as a collective voice to bring individuals together during times of celebration, sadness, rituals, and war. This function helped foster community and cohesion within early human tribes. "It brought individuals together in time of celebration and sadness for rituals and for war." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_Pi7JLwAeOI
**Evolutionary Purpose of Music - Mating** : Charles Darwin theorized that human appreciation for music might be linked to the mating calls of birds and other animals, suggesting that the brain's music centers are connected to the species' need for reproduction. This theory aligns with the observation that nearly 90% of all songs ever written are related to love or sex. "He thought human appreciation of music might somehow be related to the mating calls of birds and other creatures." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_Pi7JLwAeOI
**Evolutionary Purpose of Music - Preserving Stories** : Before written language existed, music was crucial for preserving ancient stories, myths, folk tales, and histories across thousands of generations. Setting important cultural information to music allowed it to be stored and passed down, aiding the survival of cultures from early human migrations out of Africa through ancient Roman and Greek civilizations. "It's now thought that myths and folk tales and histories managed to survive for so many years because they were stored and passed down as songs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_Pi7JLwAeOI
**Evolutionary Purpose of Music - Raising Young** : Another theory suggests music evolved from the need to raise young, specifically through lullabies. Anthropologists have found no societies that do not use lullabies to calm babies, which serves as a survival mechanism by preventing crying that could alert predators to vulnerable infants. "Anthropologists have yet to find any societies that don't use lullabies to calm babies and to stop them from crying." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_Pi7JLwAeOI
**Evolving Landscape of Corporate Endorsements in Music** : Initially, bands like 5440 maintained a strict anti-commercial stance, rejecting offers from cigarette companies and insisting on contractual control over stage branding. However, by the late 1990s, the battle was lost as major corporations (Molson, Bud, Labatt's, Coca-Cola) began directly sponsoring venues and promoters, making it impossible for bands to perform without their branding present, thus removing artists' choice. "the Moulson and the cigarette companies went directly to the venues, went directly to the promoters where if you wanted to play at all, you had to see Moulson or Bud or LaBaz or, you know, players light or whatever it was, or Coke, you didn't have a choice anymore." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=W9gdKha0PLg
**Exacerbation of Pitfalls by Fame** : For well-known artists, the insane pressure, crazy schedules, hedonistic lifestyle, and living in a bubble of fame can worsen these inherent pitfalls, leading to severe negative consequences. "And if they manage to become well known for their arts, the insane pressure, crazy schedules, hedonistic lifestyle, and living in a bubble of fame can exacerbate things until, well, until things get very, very bad." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CPxy_KOv87o
**Example of Poor CD Remastering** : Early CDs from the 1980s sometimes suffered from poor sound quality because labels simply transferred master tapes intended for vinyl directly to CD without remastering. This could lead to issues like background hiss, lost bass, and rolled-off trebles, as heard on some copies of Elvis Costello's "My Aim Is True." "I have some CDs from the 1980s where they didn't bother to remaster them for the new format. All they did was take the master tapes they used to press the album and transfer that to CD as is." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**Example of Poor Vinyl Remastering** : Some vinyl records issued since 2001 exhibit poor sound quality when the equalization of a CD master is simply transferred to vinyl without proper adjustment. This "shoe-horning" can lead to distortions and issues with low frequencies. "Then again, I have some vinyl records issued since 2001 where the label did not bother to change the equalization of the master to something that worked on vinyl." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**Exceptions to the Seven-Year Band Lifespan** : While many bands follow a seven-year cycle from formation to breakup, several notable groups have defied this trend, surviving multiple such periods. These exceptions include U2, The Rolling Stones, Oasis, Green Day, and Foo Fighters, demonstrating remarkable longevity. "Groups that have survived multiple seven-year cycles." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q-QP7ePxkQE
**Exclusive Ticketing Inventory Deals** : Ticketmaster secured exclusive deals with venues where it was granted the entire inventory of tickets for a given show, rather than sharing it with multiple sellers. This system benefited fans by providing a single point of purchase and venues by ensuring consistent business relationships over several years, similar to exclusive concession deals for beverages. "Instead of sharing the inventory of tickets with multiple ticket sellers, Ticketmaster signed deals where they were given the entire inventory for a given show." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Expansion of CD Manufacturing Plants (1984-1988)** : Following the initial two plants, Sony opened the first US-based CD manufacturing plant in Terre Haute, Indiana, in October 1984. By the end of 1988, the number of CD manufacturing plants globally had expanded to at least 50. "Another milestone was the opening of the first US-based manufacturing plant. A Sony facility went online in Tarot Hote, Indiana, in October 1984. By the end of 1988, there were at least 50 plants around the world cranking out these new disks." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Explicit Language in Music** : Fifty years ago, swearing in songs was shocking. While seemingly innocent songs were misinterpreted and raunchy songs with suggestive titles existed (e.g., "New Rubin on that Darn Old Thing" by Oscars Chicago Swingers, "Hot Nuts" by Lil Johnson, "60 Minute Man" by The Dominoes), explicit swearing was not intentionally recorded until The Fugs' "C.I.A. Man" in 1965. "Cussing in songs goes back decades and decades." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**Exploitation of Nostalgia by the Music Industry** : Marketers, the music industry, and concert promoters actively leverage the powerful force of nostalgia. This explains why "legacy bands" (acts popular before 2000) continue to "fill arenas," with Gen Xers and increasing numbers of Gen Y fans willing to pay significant amounts to "relive their youth" by seeing bands they connected with as teenagers. "Nostalgia can be a very, very powerful thing. Marketers know this. The music industry knows this. And promoters know this." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m7MbV-eCLyE
**Explosion in Band Logo Design** : Album art led to a significant increase in the design of band logos, which became powerful marketing tools. Examples include The Rolling Stones' "Lips and Tongue" and Motorhead's "Devilhead." "Album art led to an explosion in the design of band logos. A logo is a very powerful marketing tool." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pwebM3TfSdg
**Explosion of British Pride - 1990s** : The 1990s witnessed an "explosion of pride in Britishness," a level not seen since The Beatles' arrival in the US in 1964. This cultural shift was a core element of the Britpop era. "It was an explosion of pride in Britishness that we really hadn't seen since February 7, 1964 when Pan Am Flight 101 from London touched down at JFK Airport in New York carrying a band called The Beatles." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DSvoppf1dGY
**Exponential Genre Proliferation in 2010s** : Thanks to accessible technology for music creation and distribution, the rate of change and addition of new genres in the 2010s became exponential, making it challenging to track and categorize. "Now though, thanks to technology that makes it possible for virtually anyone to make and distribute their own music, the changes and additions feel exponential, completely overwhelming." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tdTs-4Irv8c
**Exponential Upload Growth** : In 2007, approximately six hours of video were uploaded to YouTube every minute. This volume dramatically increased to over 500 hours per minute by 2024, equating to 12,000 hours per day, or 82 years' worth of video added daily to its servers. "In 2007, about six hours of video were uploaded every minute. Today, that's over 500 hours per minute or 12,000 hours a day to put it another way that's 82 years worth of video added to YouTube servers every single day." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Extended Mixes on 80s Radio** : In the 1980s, radio announcers had significant leeway in choosing music, often preferring extended mixes over album or single mixes. These longer versions were perceived as artistically superior and also provided announcers more time to manage station operations like filing records or answering request lines. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Extended Remix (1970s)** : The extended remix gained prominence with the 12-inch single during the rise of disco and DJ culture in the 1970s. "And with disco in the rise of early DJ culture came the 12-inch single and the extended remix." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Extensive Early Record Dealer Network (1906)** : By 1906, the United States boasted an extensive network of 25,000 record dealers, most of which were furniture stores. This widespread distribution system was established and supported by the major record companies of the era, Victor and Edison. "In 1906, there were 25,000 record dealers just in the United States." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jD1dWxNLvfE
**F**ked Up – Deliberately Offensive Name for a Band Planned to Last 10 Shows** : The group was deliberately formed with members expected not to get along, intending to play 10 shows and break up — hence the name. Instead, they recorded many albums, toured up to 150 shows per year, opened for the Foo Fighters, and won the 2009 Polaris Music Prize. They performed at a festival called the Festival of F Bands, held in a town called Fing, Austria, alongside fellow Canadians Holy F**k, Oakland's F, and Britain's F Buttons. "The situation is definitely F'd up, especially considering that the joke has gone much, much further than anyone expected." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**FACTOR and Video FACT – Funding Bodies Born from CanCon (1982 and 1984)** : The Foundation to Assist Canadian Talent on Records (FACTOR) was introduced in 1982, requiring radio stations to contribute funds distributed to artists passing industry juries. The money can be used for recording, touring, and general artist development. When foreign artists learn that Canadian artists have access to such a fund, the common reaction is shock and envy. In 1984, MuchMusic launched as Canada's music video channel, and Video FACT was established to fund Canadian music video production. "When foreign artists find out that Canadian artists have access to such a fund, they just can't believe it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-JWO_jYXwEM
**FBI Investigation into "Louie Louie" Lyrics** : The unintelligible lyrics of The Kingsmen's song "Louie Louie" were rumored to be pornographic, prompting a six-month sound analysis test by the FBI. However, the investigation failed to prove the alleged pornographic content. "The completely unintelligible lyrics were rumored to be pornographic, but not even a six-month sound analysis test by the FBI could prove that." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6oUkMOkEr9U
**FBI Investigation of "Louie Louie" (1963)** : The FBI became involved in the "Louie Louie" controversy, spending two years analyzing The Kingsmen's recording, both forwards and backwards, in search of any evidence that it was a "dirty song." Their extensive investigation ultimately concluded that the lyrics were "unintelligible at any speed." "And even the FBI got involved, analyzing the recording backwards and forwards, looking for any kind of clue that it was in fact a dirty song." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**FEND** : FEND is an anti-opioid abuse group that benefited from the Warped Tour's charitable work. "Fend is an anti-opioid abuse group." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jfQaUV6Bq4
**FIFA 19 Video Game Music Placement** : Bakar's indie single "Big Dreams" unexpectedly gained widespread exposure when it was featured in the FIFA 19 video game. "ended up on the FIFA 19 video game." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PdCAjFW04Oc
**FLQ Crisis (1970)** : The FLQ (Front de libération du Québec) crisis in 1970 involved the kidnapping and murder of Quebec deputy premier Pierre Laporte. This historical event served as the inspiration for The Tragically Hip's song "Locked in the Trunk of a Car." "goes back to 1970, the kidnapping and murder of Quebec deputy premier Pierre La Port during the FLQ crisis." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oMBmdfrA47Q
**FM Radio Broadcasts as Bootleg Sources (1970s–1990s)** : For several decades, FM radio broadcasts of concerts served as a major source of bootleg material. These broadcasts sometimes compelled record labels to issue official releases — as occurred with Elton John in 1970 and David Bowie in 1972 — to preempt the unauthorized recordings. "Radio broadcasts of concerts were an important source of bootlegs, sometimes forcing labels to release official versions (e.g. Elton John 1970, David Bowie 1972)." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OrHtPVBjXw4
**FM Radio Invention and Edwin Armstrong's Tragic End** : Edwin Howard Armstrong, one of the engineers who put KDKA on air, discovered Frequency Modulation (FM) while working for RCA. After more than a decade developing the mathematics and science, the first FM broadcast was made on June 9, 1934, at exactly 10:23am from the top of the Empire State Building — an organ recital that sounded remarkable compared to AM. However, RCA suppressed FM to protect their lucrative AM radio business and patent portfolio, spending years tying Armstrong up in courts. On February 1, 1954, broken and depressed after decades of legal battles, Armstrong dressed in his best suit, put on his hat and gloves, and jumped from his 13th floor apartment window. "On February 1, 1954, broken and depressed, Howard dressed in his best suit and overcoat, put on his hat and gloves and jumped from his 13th floor window." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JvOEAzmC6l0
**FM Radio Invention and Tragic Outcome (1933)** : FM radio was invented by Edward Howard Armstrong in 1933. Despite this groundbreaking innovation, his patents were challenged by the RCA Corporation in an unfounded legal dispute. Armstrong ultimately lost control of his invention, which left him penniless and deeply distressed, leading him to tragically end his life by jumping from his 13th-floor window. "FM radio was invented by a dude named Edward Howard Armstrong back in 1933." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PNiL63fY1Y8
**FM Radio as "Bastard Child" (1960s)** : FM radio was initially considered the "bastard child of broadcasting" because few advertisers bought commercials on these stations, allowing DJs ample time to play music, including longer album tracks. "FM was the bastard child of broadcasting. Nobody wanted to buy commercials on those stations so there was lots of time for music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**FM Surpassed AM in 1978 – AM Kept Alive by Talk Radio; Canada Had Unique Diversity Rules** : AM radio dominated broadcasting from its invention in the 1920s all the way to 1978, when FM listening finally exceeded AM in the United States for the first time. AM survived primarily through right-wing talk radio and sports radio. In Canada, unique regulations protected AM by legally prohibiting FM stations from replicating AM programming formats: no more than 49% of songs could be hits (top 40 songs), no song could be played more than 18 times per week, and stations were required to run programming spanning multiple genres by law. FM stations also faced extensive spoken word quotas. These rules were dropped in the 1990s but left a lasting imprint on how Canadian radio sounds compared to its American counterpart. "In Canada, AM stations playing music continued to be very successful well into the 80s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iyGUXdDqIlI
**FUMS (Fuck U MS)** : FUMS is a charity initiative created by Billy Talent, their management, and record label to raise money for Multiple Sclerosis research, demonstrating the music community's support for those affected by the disease. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ocMHRLQMQY0
**Facebook's Rise and MySpace's Decline** : Facebook, launched on February 4, 2004, rapidly eclipsed MySpace in the social networking arena, marginalizing it despite not being as music-focused, thereby shifting the landscape of online social interaction. "Facebook, which first appeared on February 4, 2004." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Factor (Foundation to Assist Canadian Talent on Record) Establishment (1981)** : Established in 1981, Factor proved "fabulously successful" in funding emerging Canadian talent for recording and touring. It filled a gap in Canada, which lacked Britain's tuition-free art school tradition that had produced numerous musicians, including British invasion bands. "One solution came on in 1981, the establishment of Factor, the foundation to assist Canadian talent on record. Factor, which still exists today, was fabulously successful in funding emerging talent in this country when it came to things like recording and touring." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6PGgNCaW4xo
**Factors for Vinyl Collectibility** : The two primary factors that make a vinyl record collectible are extreme rarity and high demand. A record, even if it's the only copy, is worthless if no one desires it. "Well, first of all, it has to be very rare. And second, it has to be in demand." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tbmssCZ05ro
**Fad Branding (1967)** : By 1967, skateboards lost public appeal and were branded as a fleeting fad, similar to a hula hoop, and consequently became largely irrelevant for about a decade. "But by 1967 skateboards lost any cache they had. They were branded as a fad, kind of like a hula hoop, that means anything to you." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=44dXy0StjIQ
**Fading Music Discovery Through B-sides** : The traditional method of discovering hidden gems by flipping over a record's B-side or finding bonus tracks on imported CD singles is disappearing in the all-digital, streaming world. This shift represents a loss of a specific type of unexpected musical discovery. "There's no more finding a song like Yellow Lead Better from Pearl Jam, which was originally a bonus track on an imported version of the Jeremy CD single." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**Failed Custom CD Service - Music Maker** : Music Maker was a company that raised millions to launch a service offering custom-burnt CDs, where customers could select individual songs from a database to be burned onto a CD, with aspirations of placing these machines in every record store, but the concept ultimately "didn't happen." "You might remember another attempted modernization, a company called Music Maker." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Failed Drug Smuggling Attempt with Vegemite** : In Australia, an individual attempted to smuggle pills into a music festival by slathering them with Vegemite and hiding the stash in plastic bags wrapped around his private parts. The attempt failed when a drug detection dog at the gate immediately located the concealed drugs. "He slathered his pills with a vegemite, and then hit his stash in plastic bags, wrapped around his package. He didn't count on there being a very good drug dog at the gate who went right for the drugs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**Failed Early Industry Streaming Services** : Industry-backed streaming attempts like Pressplay and MusicNet failed largely due to their complexity, confusing user experience, and inability for labels to cross-license music between competing services, compounded by issues with digital rights management and CD burning rules, ultimately rendering them "doomed from the start." "There were a couple of industry-sponsored disasters in the form of press play and music net." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Failed High Fidelity Digital Disk Attempts (HD CD & Super Audio CD)** : The industry attempted to update CD technology, which was based on 1970s/80s standards, with two incompatible high-fidelity digital disk formats: the HD CD and the Super Audio CD. "There were two attempts at high fidelity digital disks. Remember that CD technology is still rooted in standards that were created in the late 1970s and early 80s. So let's update that. There was the HD CD and the Super Audio CD. Two standards that were, yep, not compatible." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Failure of Digital Album Sales** : Digital albums, once expected to compensate for the decline in physical product sales, have become the worst-performing section of the market, dropping at a rate of 25% per year for the past few years. "Digital albums have been dropping at a rate of 25% per year for the last couple of years." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Failure of Walkman Sharing Features** : Despite Akio Morita's intentions, the original Walkman's features designed to facilitate music sharing and conversation (dual headphone outputs, microphone input) ultimately did not achieve their goal, and the device inadvertently contributed to the rise of "self-absorbed anti-social music fans." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z9T9wi0vEvc
**Faked Moon Landing Theory** : A theory suggests that the moon landing was faked, with Stanley Kubrick allegedly directing it on a soundstage. "Or the idea that the moon landing was faked, Stanley Kubrick did it on a sound state someplace" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pmKjZzypzvg
**Fall Out Boy – From a Simpsons Character** : Named after a character in The Simpsons — the radioactive mascot sidekick to Radioactive Man. "Remember that Simpsons episode?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Fall of the Berlin Wall (1989)** : In the autumn of 1989, the Berlin Wall, a heavily guarded concrete barrier that physically and ideologically divided East and West Berlin for decades, fell. This momentous event symbolized the end of the Cold War and was recalled in the Jesus Jones song "Right Here, Right Now." "Right here, right now from Jesus Jones, recalls the fall of the Berlin Wall in the fall of 1989." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4PlieAEthSg
**Fall of the Berlin Wall (1989) Inspiration** : The fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989 is recalled in the Jesus Jones song "Right Here, Right Now." "Right here, right now from Jesus Jones, recalls the fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cM3-tINKdvo
**Fall of the Berlin Wall (November 1989)** : The Berlin Wall, a potent symbol of the Cold War and Soviet control, came down in November of 1989. This event marked a pivotal moment in the dissolution of the Eastern Bloc and the wider communist system. "The Berlin Wall comes down in November of 1989." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YeNWDtN_sHE
**Fan Abandonment of Popular Artists** : A common phenomenon where a segment of early fans will "blow off" a band or artist once they achieve widespread popularity, often perceiving this success as a "personal betrayal" because the artist is no longer their "little secret treasure." This behavior, termed "the opposite of bandwagonism," occurs despite most musicians' desire for their art to reach a broad audience and regardless of whether the band continues to produce quality music. "Fans who abandoned a group just because they become popular and then blame the band as if this was some kind of personal betrayal." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7Zniri9uSlc
**Fan Acceptance Driving Continued Price Increases** : Concert ticket prices are expected to remain high as long as fans continue to pay them, demonstrating that the economics of delivering expensive, high-quality shows will not change until consumers push back by leaving seats empty. "But until fans say enough, an artist starts seeing rows and rows of empty seats, nothing's going to change." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Fan Dissatisfaction with Music Industry (Late 1990s)** : In the late 1990s, music fans, particularly college students, expressed growing dissatisfaction with the music industry due to high CD prices and limited selection. Rock fans were especially discouraged by the dominance of pop music like Backstreet Boys and Britney Spears. "At the same time, there was growing dissatisfaction by music fans about the price and selection of music, especially amongst college students." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Fan Entitlement vs. Market Reality** : There's a common misconception among fans that they "deserve" to buy front-row seats at a low, fixed price, rather than accepting that concert tickets, like other high-demand goods or services, are subject to market forces and supply-and-demand pricing. "You do not deserve To buy a front row seat for 50 bucks just like you can't get a first class airline ticket to Singapore for a hundred bucks You pay the going rate." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Fan Websites as Source for Demos** : Some bands, like Pearl Jam, are "open about everything they create" and actively encourage fans to take initiative by setting up dedicated fan websites. These sites often freely distribute "material that's never ever been officially released," and the bands' "silence is encouragement," making them a valuable source for finding unique demos and recordings. "Pearl Jam doesn't seem to mind fans that take some extra initiative and set up special websites dedicated to them and their music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zT6pRGYLWhw
**Fan Willingness to Pay More for Shows** : Promoters discovered that fans were willing to pay higher prices for concert tickets, which encouraged a continuous escalation of pricing. "Third, the more managers and promoters pushed the envelope, they found that fans were willing to pay more." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Fan-Generated Videos and TikTok** : The rise of online platforms introduced the phenomenon of "fan generated video," where users create their own content using a song as a soundtrack, such as on TikTok. This signifies a shift towards multiple visual expressions of a single song, beyond official releases. "And then there's the whole fan generated video thing, such as using a song as a soundtrack to a TikTok video." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Fashion and Music Inseparability** : Rock music and fashion are profoundly interconnected and almost inseparable, primarily because rock inherently functions as a form of showbiz. To effectively connect with an audience, a compelling visual presentation is deemed essential, making fashion an integral part of the rock experience. "Rock and fashion are so tightly connected that they're pretty much inseparable. After all, rock is showbiz and if you want to connect, you need to look good, right?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mhQ36Y-c9fM
**Fashion as Tribal Affiliation** : A fundamental function of music fashion is its ability to signify tribal affiliation, allowing individuals to visually express their connection to specific subcultures, musical tastes, or social groups. This is evident in how punks dress distinctively from ska fans. "One of the most important functions of music fashion is that it can be used to signify your tribal affiliation, which brings us to our first concept, the concept of the uniform." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mhQ36Y-c9fM
**Fashion's Influence on Music Management** : The intersection of fashion and music played a significant role in the careers of several managers, notably Malcolm McLaren and Bernie Rhodes. McLaren, who came from a fashion background and owned several shops on Kings Road, explicitly conceived the Sex Pistols as a "living, breathing advertisement" for his store. He also managed Bow Wow Wow specifically to showcase Vivienne Westwood's new fashions, highlighting how style and image were integral to his management philosophy. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PzyvXmDKFUc
**Fastest Rapper Record (2007)** : The record for the world's fastest rapper was held by Rebel XD, who demonstrated his skill by rapping 852 syllables in just 42 seconds. This impressive display of rapid-fire lyrical delivery occurred during a competition in Chicago on July 27, 2007. "Rebel XD, who wrapped 852 syllables in 42 seconds at a competition in Chicago in July the 27th of 2007." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zWNzlSxrj6s
**Fatal Learjet Crash (2008)** : On September 19, 2008, less than a month after Jerry Finn's death, a chartered Learjet 60 carrying Travis Barker and DJ AM (among others) crashed in Columbia, South Carolina. The crash was caused by underinflated tires that burst during takeoff, leading to the collapse of the landing gear. The plane veered off the runway, erupted in sparks, crashed through barriers, and exploded about 60 seconds after Travis and DJ AM managed to escape. Pilots James Bland and Sarah Lemon, along with two other passengers, perished, leaving Travis and DJ AM as the sole survivors. "The tires, which had been underinflated, had burst." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q-QP7ePxkQE
**Feed Our Children Now** : This charity was one of many supported by the Warped Tour, which often collected over 300,000 pounds of food during a single tour for such organizations. "The tour has supported a lot of charities over the year, like feed our children now, and it wasn't unusual for more than 300,000 pounds of food to be collected over the course of one tour." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jfQaUV6Bq4
**Feedback Definition** : Feedback occurs when a signal is routed back into its own original input within a circuit. In its uncontrolled form, this manifests as sharp, high-pitched squeals and shrieks from PA systems and amplifiers. However, feedback can also be intentionally harnessed and controlled to produce a distinct type of distortion, creating a musical landscape. "Feedback. This is when you take a signal and run it back into the original input of a circuit." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iGav9uJwA_w
**Felice LaZae – Model on "Sam's Town" Cover** : Felice LaZae, a model and singer, is the "miserable looking woman" depicted on the cover of The Killers' "Sam's Town" album. Photographer Anton Corbijn specifically cast her to embody his conceptual theme of "tired and faded glory" for the album's visual narrative. "She is a model and singer named Felice Lise." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Fella Production Company** : Fella is a production company co-founded by Director X and Tadges Christelot. The company achieved a breakthrough with the music video for DJ Khaled, Drake, and Justin Bieber's song "Topstar." "We broke our production company, Fella, with this music video for DJ Khaled Drake and Bieber called Topstar." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**Fella Production Company & Popstar Video** : Fella is a production company co-founded by Director X and Director X, two individuals who originated from Brampton, Ontario. The company achieved a significant breakthrough with the music video for DJ Khaled's song "Popstar," featuring Drake and Justin Bieber. "We broke our production company, Fella, with this music video for DJ Khaled, Drake, and Bieber called Popstar." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LgJ7ww2AgF4
**Fella Production Company Breakthrough** : Director X and his collaborator broke their production company, Fella, with the music video for DJ Khaled, Drake, and Justin Bieber's song "Popstar." This moment marked a significant milestone in their "crazy journey" from Brampton, Ontario, to global recognition. "We broke our production company, Fella, with this music video for DJ Khaled, Drake and Bieber called Popstar." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YFv6Ln00XjA
**Female Bassists Who Broke Barriers** : Tina Weymouth's pioneering role inspired a wave of female bassists, including Melissa Auf der Maur (Whom, Smashing Pumpkins), D'Arcy Wretzky (Smashing Pumpkins), Kim Deal (Pixies, The Breeders), Kim Gordon (Sonic Youth), Nikki Monninger (Silversun Pickups), and Sean Yseult (White Zombie). "And a lot of that started when people first heard and saw Tina Waymoff with the talking heads." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kbFfT0pKchI
**Female Managers in Rock History** : Susan Silver is highlighted as "maybe the most important woman of the Grunge era that you've never heard about," and as "one of the most successful female managers in rock history." She started managing bands in Seattle in 1983, including The U-Men, Screaming Trees, Soundgarden, and Alice in Chains, demonstrating significant influence in a male-dominated industry. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PzyvXmDKFUc
**Female Priestesses as Early Drummers** : Archaeological evidence from a shrine in Turkey, dating back 9300 years, depicts a female figure, likely a priestess, playing a frame drum. This suggests that women, often in roles as religious leaders, were historically in charge of ritual and religious drumming. "We have archeological evidence of drummers from the wall of a shrine in present date Turkey that dates back 9300 years. It's a human figure with a frame drum and it's a woman, probably a priestess of some sort." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9GHpC0kCgHY
**Fetal Hearing Development and Loud Music Exposure** : A baby's ear structure is fully developed by 24 weeks of a normal pregnancy. While the amniotic sac and mother's body muffle sounds in quiet environments, prolonged exposure to high volume levels from sources like rock concerts, band practices, loud recording sessions near drums or amps, or loud car stereos can potentially damage a baby's hearing development. It is advised to avoid such environments until delivery. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHN6db7m3DM
**Fidelapack - Endless Loop Tape Cartridge** : Bernard Casino conceived the idea of an endless tape loop wound onto a single reel. George Isch enhanced this by enclosing the mechanism in a plastic case to prevent direct contact with the tape. This quarter-inch wide magnetic tape, running at 3.7-8 inches per second, was inserted into a player, and a metal foil strip (later an inaudible tone) signaled the end of its loop. Named the Fidelapack, it was widely adopted by radio stations from the late 1950s for playing music, commercials, and jingles, becoming known as "Surprise Carts." `"Casino came up with the idea of winding tape onto a single reel, joining the beginning and the end of that length of tape into an endless loop."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Fidget Spinners as Hottest Band Merch (2017)** : In 2017, fidget spinners emerged as the hottest band merchandise item. Numerous artists, including Arcade Fire, David Bowie, Prince, Black Flag, The Misfits, Nirvana, Queens of the Stone Age, and Fall Out Boy, sold branded versions of these popular toys. "The hottest band merch of 2017 seems to have been fidget spinners. Everybody sold branded fidget spinners, arcade fire, David Bowie, Prince Black Flag, the misfits, Nirvana, Queens of the Stone Age, followed boy, weird." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**File Sharing's Popularity and "A La Carte" Music** : Napster and other file-sharing platforms gained immense popularity by enabling "a la carte" acquisition of music, allowing users to select individual songs rather than full albums, a desirable feature amplified by the music being free. "So when Napster and other file sharing programs made it possible to acquire music alacart, just like ordering from a sushi menu, for example, people went nuts." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Film "Tommy" (1975)** : The 1975 movie "Tommy" is referenced in the context of Elton John's memorable portrayal of the Pinball Wizard, where he sported remarkably tall Doc Martens. "And anyone remember the movie Tommy from 1975? Elton John, the pinball wizard. That was him wearing a pair of docks that were at least six feet high." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mhQ36Y-c9fM
**Film and Music Video Director David Meyers Interview** : David Meyers, a long-time director, was the first guest on the Art Context podcast. His interview delved into his artistic philosophy, past work, and broader societal issues like Black Lives Matter, particularly his perspective as a white director working in the black music world. "The first podcast that debut of the show was with Dave Myers. Another guy that's been in the game for a long, long time, and just talking about the philosophy behind his approach to art." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bYsV3ozbk4M
**Finalizing Music Licensing Agreement** : A song is granted permission for use in a project only after all specific conditions of the licensing agreement have been thoroughly negotiated and settled. These conditions include details about editing, campaign length, and platform distribution. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Financial Incentives Driving Safety Enhancements** : The primary driver for improvements in concert and festival safety is financial, specifically the high costs associated with lawsuits that arise from tragic incidents. These substantial payouts, as demonstrated by the experiences of Live Nation and Travis Scott, lead to increased insurance premiums, which are then passed on to consumers through higher concert ticket prices. "The biggest incentive to improve things is money. Sorry to say that, but it's true." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pgchw2h87aw
**Financial Motivation for Reunions** : A common thread in many band reunion stories is money. Promoters are willing to pay significant sums for old bands to tour again, as there is substantial profit to be made from nostalgia. Reunions offer an excellent and quick way for musicians, who often lack a traditional pension plan, to replenish their retirement funds, with longer separations often leading to larger financial rewards. "There was a lot of money to be made in nostalgia. If you can keep everyone together long enough, and if you can keep everyone from killing each other, a reunion is an excellent way to top up the retirement fund very, very quickly." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DJGFUlQTUhQ
**Financial Security and Future Earnings for Artists** : Receiving a large upfront payment from a catalog sale provides artists with financial security, allowing them to continue creating new music (which they would own as the sale typically covers back catalog), and earn significant income from touring. Artists do not have to pay the company to perform the songs they sold. "Getting all that money up front also means that you should never, ever, ever have to worry about the financials of your band going forward... You can still make more new music that you will own because remember, the purchaser is only buying your back catalog... And you can still make boatloads of money by touring. And before you ask, the artist does not have to pay the company to perform the songs that they sold." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Financial Struggles of Commercial Alt-Rock Radio (80s)** : Commercial alt-rock stations in the 1980s frequently struggled financially, operating month-to-month with the constant threat of a format change. Salaries for staff, who were typically passionate music fans on a mission, were often very low. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Financial World's Interest in Song Catalogs** : The financial world has recognized song catalogs as valuable assets, leading to the rise of specialized companies. These entities acquire the rights to great songs, anticipating "nice, regular rates of return" through royalty generation over decades. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
