**Fine Young Cannibals – All Three Members Are Vegetarian** : Despite the band's name, all three members of Fine Young Cannibals are vegetarian. "All three members of the fine young cannibals are vegetarian." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Finite Number of Musical Combinations** : Despite the initial impression of infinite possibilities, the number of ways notes can be used is "very, very big but definitely finite." For playing each note once, it's "around 479 billion, 1,600." When considering different note durations (whole, half, quarter, etc.), the number exceeds "one quintillion possible combinations," which would take "33 billion, 63 million, 236 thousand, 360 years" to play if a new combination was played every second, more than five times the age of the universe. "The numbers are very, very big but definitely finite. Somewhere around 479 billion, 1,600." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rfObVld9rIE
**First All-Christmas Radio Format Flip** : A radio station in Ohio became the first in North America to switch to an all-Christmas music format for the year, making the change on September 25th at precisely 12:25 PM. The time was chosen as a playful reference to Christmas. "The radio station in Ohio was the first station in North America to flip to an all Christmas format this year. They made the flip on September 25th and at exactly 12 25 pm. 12 25." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HoBx6s3JmVQ
**First All-Transistor Car Radio (1956)** : Within a year of the Regency TR-1's introduction, Chrysler and Philco collaborated to develop the first all-transistor car radio. This was offered as an option in the 1956 Chrysler 300 for an additional $150, equivalent to approximately $1,800 today. `"And in less than a year, the first all transistor car radio was developed by Chrysler and Philco."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**First Amendment Debate** : The controversy surrounding Body Count's song "Cop Killer" escalated into a significant First Amendment debate in the United States, raising issues similar to those later seen in the Black Lives Matter movement. "Metal thing blew up into a big first amendment debate in the US, which had many of the same elements as the Black Lives Matter movement." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PdCAjFW04Oc
**First CD Sale at Target Canada (2013)** : When Target stores first opened their doors in Canada in 2013, the very first CD appears to have been sold in Guelph, Ontario. The album purchased was a Michael Bolton CD. "When target stores first opened in Canada in 2013, the first CD appears to have been sold in Guelph, Ontario. And it was a Michael Bolton CD." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**First DJ – Canadian-Born Al Jarvis, Los Angeles, 1932** : In the early days of radio, virtually all broadcast content was live. Musician unions actively resisted the playing of records on air, arguing it threatened their livelihoods. It wasn't until 1932 that a Canadian-born announcer named Al Jarvis convinced his boss at an LA radio station to let him create a show consisting entirely of him playing records and talking between songs. In 1935, New Yorker Martin Block launched "Make Believe Ballroom," featuring 15-minute imaginary "performances" by major artists who were, in reality, just records being played. The term "disc jockey" first appeared in print in the showbiz paper Variety on July 23, 1941. By the 1950s, thousands of people were calling themselves disc jockeys. "The first ever proper radio DJ was born in Canada." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JvOEAzmC6l0
**First Generation Goth (Late 1970s - 1985)** : This period marks the initial development of Goth, from its origins in the late 1970s up to approximately 1985. "first generation Goth, which extended to about 1985." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**First Internet Stream (Baseball Game)** : Rob Glazer, a passionate Yankees fan, set up one of the very first internet audio streams: a baseball game between the Yankees and Seattle on September 5, 1995. This event marked a significant early application of his Real Audio technology. "Glazer was a huge Yankees fan, so one of the very first streams he set up was a game between them in Seattle on September 5, 1995." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**First Karaoke Machine (1971)** : The first known karaoke machine was invented by Dissukey Inui in 1971, and it utilized eight-track tapes for playback. This marked a significant cultural innovation, bringing interactive singing to the masses through the eight-track format. `"And the first ever known karaoke machine, invented by a guy named Dissukey Inui in 1971, used eight tracks."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**First Legal Pay-Per-Download Music Service (1995)** : Sonic Net, a New York-based company, launched in 1995 as the first to legally offer pay-per-download music singles. Artists set prices between one and three dollars and kept all the revenue. Despite its pioneering role, Sonic Net didn't become a powerhouse due to being ahead of its time; slow modem speeds meant downloads could take anywhere from five minutes to five hours, and the audio quality, described as "a little better than a bad AM radio" or "an AM radio in a 72 Nova," was poor as widespread MP3 use had not yet occurred. "Sonic Net was the first, and look at where we are today." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MTDY6qT11Dc
**First Meeting with Michael Hutchence (1985)** : As host of the British TV show *The Tube* in 1985, she met Michael Hutchence, who immediately invited her to his hotel room. Though she declined, a strong connection was evident, and they may have begun an affair while she was still with Bob Geldof. "In 1985, Hutchins was a guest on the British TV show The Tube. The host was Paula Yates, and she, for the last 10 years, had been with Bob Geldoff, he of the Bumpton Rats and the organizer of Live Aid." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**First Modern Concerts (17th Century)** : Concerts as they are recognized today originated in the 17th century, initially at European universities. This period marked the beginning of public, admission-charging musical performances. "The first concerts as we know them today began in the 17th century at European universities." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OCkov6FTliE
**First Moshpits and Slam Dancing** : Hardcore music is associated with the emergence of the first moshpits and slam dancing. These intense forms of audience participation were initially observed in California and Washington, D.C. **First Movie Soundtrack Record Release (1938)** : The first movie soundtrack believed to be released as a record was for *Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs* in 1938. However, this early release faced the technical challenge of being divided across multiple 10-inch, 78 RPM records, requiring constant listener interaction every four minutes. "The first movie soundtrack to be released as a record seems to have been snow white in the Seven Dwarfs in 1938." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**First Online Ticket Sale (September 11, 1996)** : Online purchasing became possible on Ticketmaster's website starting September 11, 1996. The very first online ticket sale on that day was for a Seattle Mariners game. When Ticketmaster contacted the buyer, his motivation was simply "I don't like people." "For the record, the first online ticket sale took place on that day, September 11th, 1996, and it was one ticket to a Seattle Mariners game." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**First Portable CD Player - Sony Discman D50 (1984)** : Sony introduced the Discman D50, the first portable CD player, in November 1984. It retailed for $500, which would be approximately $1,100 in today's money. While Discmen and their clones were popular for a time, they were notorious for skipping, making them impractical for activities like jogging, even after "severe anti-shock technology" was implemented. `"The first portable CD player was Sony's Discman, which was called the D50. It sold for $500, $1,100 today. And it came out in November 1984."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**First Public CD Demonstration (1981 on "Tomorrow's World")** : The compact disc technology was publicly demonstrated for the first time on the BBC show "Tomorrow's World" in 1981. The demonstration showcased a disk capable of 60 minutes of digital music, recorded as electronic pulses, and explained how a laser beam, rather than a needle, read the 2.5-mile long track of microscopic pits and spaces. "The first public demonstration of the technology, apparently was on a BBC show called Tomorrow's World in 1981. It's a long playing record from here to here, 60 minutes of digital music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**First Purpose-Built Skate Parks** : The mid-1970s saw the emergence of the first purpose-built skate parks, a development coinciding with the resurrection of skateboarding and improvements in equipment. "Competition's resumed and the first purpose-built skate parks started showing up." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=44dXy0StjIQ
**First Radio Commercial (August 1922) – WEAF New York, $50 for 10 Minutes; First Jingle Was Wheaties, 1926** : WEAF, an AM station in New York City with no parent company to subsidize costs, pioneered the modern radio commercial in August 1922 by selling 10 minutes of air time to the Hawthorne Court apartment complex in Jackson Heights for $50 — the first paid radio advertisement. By October 1922, the station had made $550 in ad sales. The first radio commercial jingle — a singing advertisement — was broadcast by Wheaties breakfast cereal in 1926. By 1930, approximately 90% of American radio stations were running commercials. "As far as we can tell, the modern radio commercial was born in August 1922." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iyGUXdDqIlI
**First Skaters** : While the exact identity of the first skateboarder is unknown, it is speculated to have been a California surfer seeking land-based fun when waves were absent or for similar thrills without getting wet. "We don't know who the first skateboarder was but it was probably some California surfer who wanted the same kind of fun without getting wet or wanted to have fun when the waves just weren't there." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=44dXy0StjIQ
**First Song Performed in Space (1965)** : On December 7, 1965, astronauts Wally Schirra and Thomas Stafford performed "Jingle Bells" aboard Gemini 6 using a harmonica and hand bells they had stowed away. Although few people beyond mission control heard their amateur playing, this marked the first documented musical performance in space. "On December 7, 1965, Wally Shira and Thomas Stafford pulled out a harmonica and some hand bells that they'd stowed away aboard Gemini 6 and performed jingle bells for the planet." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**First Sound Guy - Gaspar P. Schotto (1657)** : Gaspar P. Schotto is recognized as the first true sound guy, publishing a book on acoustics titled "Megave Universalis" in 1657. He was a pioneer in systematically studying "the behavior of sound," with a particular interest in its distribution, amplification, collection, reflection, and how it traversed spaces of varying sizes and shapes. "The first true sound guy was Gaspar P. Schotto. He wrote a book on acoustics called Megave Universalis in 1657." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qrrwqdpCjpQ
**First YouTube Video (April 23, 2005)** : The first video ever uploaded to YouTube was on April 23, 2005, titled "Me at the Zoo," featuring co-founder Jawed Karim at the San Diego Zoo. **Flaws of CD Jewel Cases** : Compact disc jewel cases had several recurring design flaws, including hinges that frequently cracked and broke, preventing the case from staying closed, and the central hub that secured the disc often breaking, which put the CD at risk of scratches or falling out. "The hinges on the jewel case always cracked and broke, making it impossible for the case to stay closed. **Flexi-Discs (Eva-Tone Soundsheet)** : Flexi-discs are thin, pliable sheets of vinyl designed to be included as inserts in books or magazines. They first appeared in 1962, initially marketed as the Eva-Tone Soundsheet. The Beatles notably embraced this format, sending out special Christmas flexi-discs to their fan club from 1963 to 1969. Throughout the 1970s, flexi-discs were commonly found in various publications, including Mad Magazine, National Geographic, and music magazines. The format continues to be produced, appearing on Record Store Day and favored by punk bands and artists like the Foo Fighters. "Flexi disks first appeared in 1962, and were known as the Eva-Tone Soundsheet." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2g4QSREzpw
**Florida Legislator Withheld $100,000+ from Public Radio Station for "Frequently Praising Kurt Cobain" (1997)** : In 1997, a Florida legislator successfully blocked more than $100,000 in funding to public radio station WMNF as punishment for its programming, which included what was characterized as "frequent praise of musician Kurt Cobain." **Flow Machines AI System (2016)** : Launched in Paris in 2016, Flow Machines was an AI system designed to aid composers in writing music in specific styles. The software analyzed 13,000 lead sheets across various global genres to generate melodies, which human composer Benoit Carey then developed into full tracks, culminating in "Daddy's Car," the first AI-generated pop song. "Flow machines was built on software that analyzed 13,000 lead sheets." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Focus on String Amplification** : George Beechum theorized that the guitar's amplifying sound originated from its body, influenced by its strings. He therefore aimed to eliminate the body's role and directly amplify the strings themselves, leading to the development of the pickup. "He realized that the amplifying sound of a guitar came from the guitar's body. Sounds conferred to it by its strings. So why not just eliminate that second part? Why not just amplify the strings themselves?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E6cXyaHALBQ
**Fodors Rock and Roll Traveler Books** : Fodors, a travel guide publisher, previously released a series of books called *Rock and Roll Traveler*. These included editions for the US and another covering the UK and Ireland, designed to help fans plan musical pilgrimages. Although they are now out of print, used copies are still available. "Fodders, the Travel Guide people, used to have a couple of books called Rock and Roll Traveler." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sKHdTSfDd6E
**Foo Fighters – WWII Pilots' Term for Unexplained Balls of Fire** : In WWII skies over Europe, pilots on both sides reported mysterious fast-moving balls of fire they called Foo Fighters (from the French "feu" for fire). Dave Grohl considers the name a mistake and a stupid band name, but the UFO connection was appealing. **Ford F-Series Built-in MP3 Laptop (2006)** : In 2006, Ford offered an option for F-Series Pickups that included a built-in laptop in the cab, capable of sending emails and playing MP3s. **Foreign Investment in Jamaican Bauxite (early 1960s)** : In the early 1960s, "tens of millions of dollars of foreign investment was flowing into Jamaica" to further expand bauxite extraction. This influx of capital intensified the mining efforts and their associated societal impacts. "By the early 60s, tens of millions of dollars of foreign investment was flowing into Jamaica so that more and more Boxite could be dug out of the ground." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zNIVqKqAlnk
**Foreshadowing Music Television** : A "crazy idea" is mentioned for a future chapter: a 24-hour TV channel dedicated solely to showing "short films based around single songs," a concept dismissed as "goofy" but clearly alluding to the advent of music video channels. "Oh, and someone also came up with this crazy idea of a 24-hour TV channel that showed nothing but short films based around single songs, which is goofy." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9uTORLOm5Yg
**Format Transitions and Retailer Disagreement (Early 1980s)** : In the early 1980s, a period of disagreement arose between record stores and labels as stores were asked to adopt the new compact disc (CD) format. Retailers, having already invested heavily in shelving for 45s, albums, 8-tracks, and cassettes, were reluctant to incur further costs. The issue was resolved when it was demonstrated that two CDs could be stacked in the same space as a 12-inch album, and the struggling industry needed the CD to revitalize sales during a deep recession. "But when it was pointed out that you could stack two CDs side by side in the same space as a 12 inch album, they got the point." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jD1dWxNLvfE
**Format War of the Late 1940s** : The late 1940s saw a significant "format war" in the music industry between Columbia Records' 12-inch 33 1/3 RPM long-playing album, designed for multiple songs per side, and RCA's 7-inch 45 RPM record, which typically featured one song per side. "This is when we encountered the format war between Columbia's 12-inch 33- to 3rd RPM long-playing album that held lots of songs per side, and RCA's 7-inch 45 RPM record, which stuck with the standard one song per side." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**Formulaic Music Creation** : The reliance on streaming data has fostered a formulaic approach to music creation, akin to the movie industry's superhero genre, where artists and labels employ data-driven formulas to produce similar-sounding songs designed for maximum revenue, explaining why much new music sounds alike. "With music, everyone is swinging for the fences using the same data to create the same formulas. And ultimately, the same sorts of songs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Fortunate Sonic Coincidence** : An anecdote recounts a songwriter who, having co-written a multi-platinum, top-20 hit in 1985, received a call years later from a European band's manager. The manager acknowledged their upcoming album contained a song with "pronounced melodic and structural similarities" to the friend's song, claiming independent discovery. An agreement was made to avoid a lawsuit, granting the friend royalties from the new song. The European album sold "8 million copies," and their song went "all the way to number one in America," resulting in "major league royalty checks" for the friend for a song he didn't write. "For him, this unfortunate sonic coincidence was very fortunate." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rfObVld9rIE
**Fortunate Sonic Coincidence and Royalty Deal** : An anecdote detailing a friend who co-wrote a multi-platinum, Top 20 song in 1985. Years later, a European band with a similar song, expecting it to be a massive hit, approached him for a deal to avoid a lawsuit, offering royalties based on album sales and single chart performance. The European album sold 8 million copies, and the song became a number one hit in America, providing the friend with significant and ongoing royalties for a song he didn't write. "For him, this unfortunate sonic coincidence was very fortunate." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4cZfegexwwk
**Foster the People – Misheard Band Introduction Stuck** : Singer Mark Foster intended the name to be "Foster and the People" with himself as a named frontman. People kept missing the "and" when he introduced the group, and "Foster the People" stuck. "People kept missing the AND whenever he introduced the group." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Foundations of Vinyl Record Specifications (1927)** : In 1927, JP Maxfield conducted calculations crucial for sound-on-film technology that inadvertently established the physical standards for vinyl records. He determined that to achieve maximum sound fidelity and volume with minimum noise over 10 to 11 minute film reels, the grooves on a 12-inch disc needed to be packed at approximately 100 grooves per inch, rotating at 33 and a third revolutions per minute. These dimensions and speed remain standard for vinyl albums today. "JP did some calculations in 1927 and he worked out that sustaining maximum sound fidelity and volume and minimum noise over 10 or 11 minutes required the grooves on the record to be packed in at about 100 to the inch on a 12 inch disc rotating at 33 and a third revolutions per minute." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PNiL63fY1Y8
**Founders of YouTube** : YouTube was founded by Steve Chen, Chad Hurley, and Jawed Karim, all of whom previously worked at PayPal and became wealthy following the company's acquisition by eBay. "Steve Chen, Chad Hurley and Joe Karim all worked at PayPal. When the company was bought by eBay, they made a lot of money." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Founding of Q Prime and Specialization** : Cliff Bernstein, who started in A&R at Mercury Records in the 1970s, and Peter Mensch, a former college radio DJ, co-founded the management company Q Prime in 1982. Their first client was Def Leppard, and the company quickly specialized in heavy rock, eventually managing an extensive roster including AC/DC, Scorpions, Metallica, and The Red Hot Chili Peppers. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PzyvXmDKFUc
**Fragility and Composition of Early 78 RPM Records** : Early 78 RPM records were highly fragile, described as being "worse than glass" or "like delicate China." They were made from a mixture of shellac, cotton, a binding agent, powdered slate or limestone, and a wax lubricant. This composition made them wear out quickly, with obtaining 125 plays considered lucky, and prone to breaking easily. "But they were very fragile. They were made of a weird mix of shellac, cotton, some kind of binding agent, powdered slate or limestone, and a wax lubricant." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VlIErdSSVOM
**Fragmentation by New Metal and File Sharing** : The once cohesive alternative scene began to fragment significantly after grunge's peak. The emergence of new metal further polarized tastes, and the widespread adoption of file sharing in the late 1990s via programs like Napster allowed individuals to explore diverse music freely, ending any hope of reuniting the "old alternative nation." "With new metal came along a few years later, things were further polarized. And when file sharing began to take off in the late 1990s thanks to programs like the original Napster, everyone could suddenly explore every facet of music for virtually no cost on their own." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tFBSrKMEZxI

"the whole genesis of everything goth is Mary Shelley's novel Frankenstein, which was published in 1818." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Fraunhofer Institute for Digital Media Technology** : This German research lab, co-created by Carlheinz Brandenburg, was instrumental in the development of the MP3 compression algorithms. The institute played a key role in the scientific and technological advancements that led to the worldwide standard for digital audio. **Fraunhofer Society** : The Fraunhofer Society is the entity under which the Fraunhofer Institute for Digital Media Technology operates. **French Cultural Protection (Francon)** : France has a cultural protection statute, known as Francon, which is similar to Canada's Cancon rules. However, the French requirement is notably higher, mandating that French radio stations play approximately 60% French content. "France, for example, has a similar statute which puts Francon at around 60%, as opposed to where I'm 35." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dMsEj62KFEw
**Fritz Flummer's Magdenifun Invention (1927-1935)** : Engineer Fritz Flummer developed a solution for cigarettes burning down to knuckles by creating metal stripes on cigarette foil. Applying this knowledge to magnetic recording, in 1927 he created a thin paper strip coated with iron oxide and lacquer, for which he received a patent in 1928. He licensed his invention, called the Magdenifun, in 1932, and it debuted at the Berlin radio show in 1935. "In 1927, he created a very thin paper strip that was coated with iron oxide and held to the paper with lacquer. A patent was granted the following year. And, in 1932, he'd licensed the rights to his invention, which he called the Magdenifun." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**Front-Heavy Songwriting** : Songwriting has adapted to short attention spans by becoming "front heavy," with more songs starting directly with the chorus, jamming more hooks into the first half-minute, and incorporating familiar samples to immediately grab listeners. "More songs are starting with the chorus. More hooks are jammed into that first half minute. Songs are front heavy with familiar samples." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Front-of-Stage Crush Dynamics** : "Front of stage crush" is a common and dangerous phenomenon at concerts where the crowd surges towards the stage barrier, crushing individuals against the barricade or each other. This often leads to people falling and being trampled. In severe cases, a "crowd collapse" can occur, where a large number of people fall rapidly, often with tragic consequences. "Some fall and are trampled. And in some cases, there's what security people call a crowd collapse, where a lot of people suddenly go down very quickly." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7v1REnqKuXE
**Fugazi – Murky Origins: NYC Limo Company, French Land Mine, or Vietnam War Acronym** : Multiple theories: a shady New York City limo company called Fugazi; derived from the French "fugasse," a type of landmine used in both the Korean and Vietnam Wars; or an acronym: "F'd Up, Got Ambushed, Zipped In a Body Bag." "The name origin is a bit murky." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Fundamental Challenge of Live Sound Amplification** : The core problem in live sound reinforcement is to amplify small sound sources on a stage to reach large numbers of people in a vast space while achieving "maximum realism and minimum distortion." This technical challenge remains complex, requiring sophisticated equipment and acoustic understanding. "To make this work, you have to amplify the small sound sources on a stage to reach large numbers of people in a large space with maximum realism and minimum distortion." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qrrwqdpCjpQ
**Funding for Monty Python and the Holy Grail** : The comedy film "Monty Python and the Holy Grail" received crucial funding from major rock bands. Pink Floyd contributed £200,000 from the earnings of their album "Dark Side of the Moon," and later persuaded Led Zeppelin and Genesis to also invest in the movie. "If it hadn't been for the success of Pink Floyd's Dark Side of the Moon album, the movie Monty Python and the Holy Grail might have never been made." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HoBx6s3JmVQ
**Funeral Behavior** : At Michael Hutchence's funeral, she wore a low-cut dress, which garnered significant attention. One story alleges that before the coffin was closed, she slipped a gram of heroin into his pocket. "And one story is that before the coffin was closed, Paula slipped a gram of heroin into Michael's pocket." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Funky Drummer Break Usage** : Clyde Stubblefield's eight-bar drum break from James Brown's 1969 song "Funky Drummer" has been sampled and looped in at least 1,700 different songs. Its influence spans a wide range of genres and artists, from hip-hop pioneers like Public Enemy and the Beastie Boys to pop stars like Britney Spears and rock bands like Nine Inch Nails. "That break has been sampled and looped in at least 1700 different songs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Future Consolidation in Streaming** : The streaming music industry is expected to undergo consolidation, leading to the existing dominant companies growing even larger. "If anything, there will be consolidation in the streaming space, meaning that these companies will just get bigger." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Future Instrument Coverage** : There are many other iconic instruments that could be explored, including the Fender Telecaster, the Yamaha DX7 synth, the saxophone (one of rock's earliest instruments), and drums. These instruments represent further avenues for investigation into musical history. "Perhaps in the future we can look at things like the Fender Telecaster, the M-Hod DX7 synth, the saxophone, which is one of rocks earliest instruments by the way." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Y0HW8LQKvJw
**Future Program Topics (Part Two)** : The ongoing history of indie music will continue with Part Two, which will delve deeper into developments in the late 1970s and early 1980s, with a particular focus on the terms "alternative" and "college rock." "On part two of our history of indie music, we'll look at more of what happened in the late 1970s as well as the early 1980s. And we're going to start encountering the words alternative and college rock a lot more." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dDcD8kg0wEk
**Future Revivals** : While scenes like Industrial, Goth, Shoegaze, and Britpop have never truly disappeared, they might again be adopted en masse by new generations, much like Punk, Ska, Emo, Garage Rock, and New Wave, taking these sounds to new places. "Who knows what sounds might come around again in the future to dominate our attention again." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9CNNsqXiL6U
**Future Unsung Heroes** : The second part of the "unsung heroes of music" program will cover a diverse set of figures, including someone with unique hairstyles, a drummer who should have been exceptionally wealthy, and a woman potentially responsible for inventing punk music. "On the second part of this program on unsung heroes of music, we're going to expand the palette just a little bit to include someone with an interesting taste in hairstyles. There will be a drummer who should have made a billion dollars and the woman who may have invented punk." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6SdPPsmpgJg
**Future of CD as a Niche Format for Curation** : The compact disc is not expected to completely disappear but will evolve into a niche format, similar to vinyl, catering to individuals who prioritize the physical possession and careful curation of music. This caters to "personal music curation" as distinct from the convenience of broad "music consumption" via streaming. "Will the CD disappear? No. But it will become a niche format for people who want to possess music, just like those who like vinyl. I think we're looking at a split in the way we consume music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Future of the Album** : While albums remain the "currency of the recorded music industry" for contracts and awards, and physical formats like CDs and vinyl still sell, signs suggest the album is fading as artists increasingly adopt a "drip, drip, drip of new songs" strategy (like in hip hop) to keep fans continuously engaged, replacing the traditional album-recording-and-touring cycle. "First of all, albums are still the currency of the recorded music industry. Contracts are based on albums." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Future of the Music Business** : The music business will continue to exist, but its "old ways of doing things are gone forever." The industry faces ongoing challenges in monetizing music and ensuring artists are paid while meeting consumer demands. Solutions will require copyright reform, tariff adjustments, and international agreements beyond just price reductions. "the music business will continue to exist, although the old ways of doing things are gone forever. There are still years of trying to figure out how to make music pay and how musicians can get paid while still filling the needs, wishes and demands of the consumer." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Fuzz Definition** : Fuzz is a specific type of distortion that was initially discovered when amplification equipment malfunctioned or became damaged. Once these unique "fuzz tones" were identified, significant effort was invested in creating add-on electronic devices to intentionally generate fuzz on demand. "Fuzz is a special type of distortion that was discovered when amplification equipment malfunctioned or was damaged." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iGav9uJwA_w
**Gallagher Brothers' Father** : Noel and Liam Gallagher, along with their older brother Paul, had a difficult relationship with their father, Tommy, who was an alcoholic and physically abused his children. "They had a bad relationship with their father, Tommy. He was an alcoholic. He beat his kids." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=S6qB5B1PdyA
**Garbage – Insult from a Friend Adopted as a Name** : When Butch Vig, Duke Erikson, and Steve Marker were assembling the band in Madison, Wisconsin in 1993, a friend commented that the new material "sounded like garbage." They took it as a cool suggestion for something irreverent. "A friend commented that this new stuff sounded like garbage." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Gary Kemp Born with One Kidney; Martin Kemp Born with Three** : Gary Kemp of Spandau Ballet was born with only one kidney. When it later failed, younger brother Martin Kemp (also in Spandau Ballet) agreed to donate one of his kidneys — and it turned out Martin was born with three. "He later developed a condition that caused that single kidney to fail." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Gen X Aging Out of Intense Music Engagement** : By 1997, as the economy improved and Generation X aged into adulthood with its "adult-sized problems and challenges," they no longer had the "time or inclination to be into music as much as they were in the early part of the decade." "With the economy improving and Gen X aging into adulthood and the workforce with its adult-sized problems and challenges, Gen X no longer had the time or inclination to be into music as much as they were in the early part of the decade." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=amHre9ZZFkU
**Gen X's Anger in the 90s** : For much of the 1990s, Generation X was described as "supremely pissed off," and this collective anger was reflected in the hard and heavy music of Alt Rock, particularly genres like industrial. "And for much of the decade, they were supremely pissed off." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=amHre9ZZFkU
**Gen Y's Pop Phase** : Generation X was supplanted by Generation Y, who were "into the pop phase of their lives," gravitating towards artists like the Spice Girls, Backstreet Boys, Puff Daddy, and En Vogue. "Gen X was being supplanted by Gen Y and they were into the pop phase of their lives." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=amHre9ZZFkU
**General Causes of Rock Musician Deaths** : The vast majority of deaths in the world of rock music are attributed to health issues, drug use, suicide, accidents, or age-related causes. Murder accounts for a significantly smaller, though tragic, portion of musician fatalities. "The vast majority of deaths in the world of rock have been health, drug, suicide, accident, or age-related." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q6dAX6HCjgw
**General Definition of Royalties** : A royalty represents the income that the owner of a song receives from its various uses. This can include "airplay royalties" collected by performing rights agencies for public performances, and "mechanical royalties" earned from a song's inclusion on physical formats like CDs and their subsequent sales. "A royalty is the income the owner of a song gets from the use of the song." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**General Disappearances of Notable Figures** : Historically, many individuals have vanished without a trace, often becoming notorious cases. Examples include Amelia Earhart (missing since 1937 after attempting to fly around the world), DB Cooper (hijacker missing since 1971 with $200,000 ransom), Teamster President Jimmy Hatha (missing since 1975), and Maddie McCann (English child missing since 2007 from a resort in Portugal). "We still haven't found Amelia Earhart, who has been missing since 1937 when she tried to fly around the world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sCwgvHg86Oc
**General Lack of Mainstream Gay-Oriented Music (Pre-Punk)** : Prior to the punk rock revolution, most gay-oriented music remained "safely closeted away from mainstream eyes and ears," highlighting the need for a musical movement to open things up for wider acceptance. "Still, most of this music was safely closeted away from mainstream eyes and ears." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hswtuenFwvM
**General Sibling Rivalries (Various)** : Many famous siblings have had intense rivalries, illustrating that familial ties don't guarantee harmony. Examples include columnists Ann Landers and Dear Abby (Epi and Pauline Lederer), shoe company founders Adolf and Rudolf Dassler (Adidas and Puma), actresses Olivia DeHavilland and Joan Fontaine, and actors Eric and Julia Roberts. "Just because you're related to someone doesn't mean you're going to get along, doesn't mean you're going to like each other." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_b3KRS-Yx_s
**General Tire Factory - Studio Conversion** : The Black Keys repurposed the second floor of a former General Tire factory in East Akron, Ohio, as their recording studio for the *Rubber Factory* album. General Tire had ceased operations at the plant in 1982. "the band-in-tire factory in East Akron that was once run by General Tyre, but they bailed out in 1982." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PmwfC4HR3_I
**General Tour Bus Accident (2002)** : In February 2002, a band's bus was rolling through Kentucky when a front-end tire blowout caused it to swerve violently across the highway. Fortunately, the driver was able to bring the vehicle under control, and there was no other traffic nearby, preventing what could have been a much more serious accident. "There's bus was rolling through Kentucky in February 2002 when there was a front end tire blowouts and he gets swirving all over the highway." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DgN0vBIugLc
**Generation X Consumption** : During the 1990s, Generation X was a major demographic driving the music industry, purchasing a lot of music and attending many shows, contributing to the financial viability of festivals and touring acts. "Gen X was buying a lot of music and going to a lot of shows." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7JP2tjJibTQ
**Generation X Demographics and Disillusionment** : As the 1990s began, Generation X faced a challenging economic and social landscape marked by a brutal recession, widespread overeducation, and underemployment. This demographic group, aged 15-25, felt disillusioned, jaded, angry, and anxious about their future, concerned they wouldn't achieve the living standards of their parents. Their discontent was exacerbated by events like the Gulf War. "Much of Gen X was overeducated and under-employed." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r8AazBVoS7g
**Generation X's Demand for Meaningful Music** : Demographics, specifically Generation X (the sons and daughters of the baby boom), played a huge part in the alt-rock revolution, as they came of age and started "demanding music that meant something to them." "Demographics played a huge part as Generation X, the sons and daughters of the baby boom, came with age and started demanding music that meant something to them." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=amHre9ZZFkU
**Generation X's Discontent** : Generation X, the children of baby boomers, found the late 1980s a "pretty dire time to be a rock fan" due to the "absolutely terrible" pop music (New Kids on the Block, Debbie Gibson, Tiffany) and the prevalence of "rich rock stars with cars and chicks and private jets and mansions in the South of France playing the same old classic rock songs to baby boomers." "If you were part of Generation X, the children of the baby boomers, it was a pretty dire time to be a rock fan." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tFBSrKMEZxI
**Generation X's Economic and Social Mood** : At the beginning of the 1990s, Gen X faced a recession, unemployment, a crisis of confidence, and the First Gulf War, which created a "dark mood" that demanded heavier, angrier, and more aggressive music than the hair metal ballads and pop of the late 1980s. "The mood was dark and that called for music to reflect that, something heavier and angrier and more aggressive than the endless hair metal ballads and the pop music that we heard at the end of the 1980s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tFBSrKMEZxI
**Generation X's Musical Coming of Age** : A large segment of Gen X, aged 15-25, was at a crucial point in their lives where music is most defining. They rejected their parents' and older siblings' music—late 80s classic rock "dinosaurs" (Van Halen, Aerosmith, Lynyrd Skynyrd), and pop and hair metal—in favor of authentic music created by and for their own generation. "Gen X was also at the point in their lives when they were coming of age musically. A huge bulge of them were in that sweet spot age range, 15 to 25. The time in our lives when music seems to be one of the most defining things of our being." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tFBSrKMEZxI
**Generation X's Musical Identity** : Generation X, musically coming of age (15-25), actively rejected the music of their parents and older siblings, including late 1980s classic rock (Van Halen, Aerosmith, Leonard Skinner, Pink Floyd, Elton John), 80s pop (New Kids on the Block, Tiffany, Debbie Gibson), and hair metal (excess, makeup, power ballads). They sought authentic, humble music made by "their own people" that resonated with their hopes, dreams, fears, and anger. "Gen X was not interested in what their parents or older brothers and sisters were into." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r8AazBVoS7g
**Generation Y Musical Preferences** : Generation Y, entering their years of musical awareness in the late 1990s, preferred pop-oriented music over the brooding grunge or intense post-punk alt-rock of previous generations. Their tastes contributed to the rise of pop acts like the Spice Girls and Britney Spears. "Generation Y just wasn't into brooding grunge or any of the kind of intense post punk all rock that their forebearers were. Their tastes leaned much more to the pop side." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7JP2tjJibTQ
**Generational Clash over "It"** : Generations often find themselves at odds with cultural trends, famously encapsulated by the sentiment: "I used to be with it, but then they changed what it was. Now what I'm with, isn't it? And what's it seems weird and scary to me?" This cyclical experience of feeling out of touch with new ideas is presented as a natural part of life, occurring since the invention of music. "I used to be with it, but then they changed what it was. Now what I'm with, isn't it? And what's it seems weird and scary to me?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Generational Divide in Music Taste** : The preference of younger generations for music that their parents dislike, thereby establishing their own distinct musical identity, is a recurring "parent child dynamic over music" that has been observed for over a century. "This is all part of the parent child dynamic over music that's been repeated every generation for the past 100 years or longer." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5bQz8R0gbXc
**Generational Music Consumption Habits (Gen Z)** : Gen Z, comprising individuals born between the late 1990s and early 2010s, exhibits high levels of music engagement, streaming music daily and averaging approximately four and a half hours of listening per day. This consumption rate surpasses that of Gen Y, Gen X, and boomers, although previous generations were equally immersed in music during their youth. "Studies showed that they are streaming music every single day, and they average somewhere around four and a half hours of music listening every single day." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m7MbV-eCLyE
**Generational Music Taste** : Every generation inherently believes the music of their youth is "the greatest music of all time." For individuals aged 14 to 24 in the 1990s, the music from that decade will remain "comfort food" for the rest of their lives, highlighting a universal phenomenon of strong attachment to music heard during formative years. "Every generation has a biological right to believe the music of their youth is the greatest music of all time..." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bI6TTA-fn7c
**Generational Right to Believe Youth Music is Greatest** : A fundamental truth about music and age is that "every generation has the right to believe that the music of their youth is the greatest music of all time." This belief is universal and immune to individual exceptions. "every generation has a right to believe that the music of their youth is the greatest music of all time." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m7MbV-eCLyE
**Generational Shift in Music Consumption** : In 1992, a 15-year-old was uninterested in 20-year-old music like Elvis or The Beatles, viewing it as "old music." Today's 15-year-old, however, might appreciate a 25-year-old Nirvana song, suggesting a new "ecumenical world" where "good music is good music regardless of age" due to technology. "A 15 year old kid in 1992 could not get enough Nirvana. He couldn't care less about music that was 20 years old. Like Elvis the Beatles, the Stones, that's old music. That meant nothing to this kid." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tFBSrKMEZxI
**Generational Shift in Music Creation Tools** : Generations raised on electronics are increasingly choosing not to create music with traditional instruments like the guitar, instead opting for tools such as laptops, iPads, Ableton Live, and various programmable keyboard devices. "generations brought up on electronics are opting out of creating music with traditional instruments like the guitar. Instead, they use laptops and iPads and gears like Ableton Live and any number of programmable keyboard devices." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oLC49nCf0ZM
**Generational Shifts and Music Preference** : Approximately every decade, demographic changes impact musical tastes. Generation X, which had fueled the alt rock of the 1990s, aged into adulthood. They were succeeded by Generation Y (Millennials), a larger cohort born between the early 1980s and late 1990s, who initially favored pop but later sought more meaningful, harder, raw, and authentic music, especially after the shock of 9/11. "This shift went hand in hand with one of those demographic time bombs that we see every 10 years or so." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=raRCZ0Y5up8
**Generational Shifts in Music Agenda (Millennials/Gen Z)** : The mid-2000s saw a demographic shift where Generation Y (Millennials), who were "children of the 80s and 90s," began setting the music agenda, creating music for other Millennials. As the decade ended, Generation Z (born mid-1990s onward), a large cohort, started influencing the music marketplace, contributing to shifts in popular genres. "The artists setting the agenda for the middle odds and forward were children of the 80s and 90s. Millennials making music for other millennials." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_yslM5oanRo
**Genre Prioritization in Music Promotion** : Hip Hop, Rap, R&B, and Pop genres receive the bulk of label promotional efforts and resources due to their demonstrated streaming success in the "musical moneyball" environment, leaving other genres like Rock, Country, or Jazz with residual attention. "So the label sped all their time and money promoting that music. And if there's anything left over, it goes to rock or country or jazz or whatever." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**George Beechum's Vision for Guitar Amplification** : George Beechum realized that the same technology used to amplify faint radio signals could be applied to a guitar to increase its volume. He sought a method to convert string vibrations directly into electrical energy, bypassing the issues of microphones and feedback. "George Beachham realized that if this new technology could make a faint radio signal loud, why couldn't it do the same thing for a guitar? ... Well, yeah, you could just take a microphone in front of a regular guitar and run that through an amp, but Mike's got in the way and often resulted in wild feedback. There had to be something better." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E6cXyaHALBQ
**George Best – Footballer** : George Best was a famous Manchester United player, whose picture is visibly placed in the window on the cover of Oasis's "Definitely Maybe" album. This inclusion was a personal tribute, as George Best was rhythm guitarist Paul "Bonehead" Arthurs' favorite footballer. "Look at the window and you'll see a picture of George Best, the famous Manchester United player. He was Bohnhead's favorite." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Gerald Frant's Crimes** : In June 1987, Gerald Frant, a serial rapist who had previously served time for the rape of a 12-year-old girl and was paroled in 1980, kidnapped a 14-year-old girl at gunpoint in Tacoma, Washington. He took her to his trailer and subjected her to torture with whips, razors, and a blowtorch while she was tied to a pulley apparatus. The girl, referred to as Paulie, managed to escape from his van when he stopped for gas, creating a commotion that led to Frant's arrest a day later. He is currently serving two consecutive 75-year sentences in Eastern Washington State. "He was a serial rapist who had already served time for the rape of a 12 year old." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4PlieAEthSg
**Gerald Simpson (A Guy Called Gerald) at The Hacienda** : Gerald Simpson, known as A Guy Called Gerald, was a DJ from Manchester who frequently manned the turntables at The Hacienda, the infamous club owned by New Order and Factory Records. His role exemplifies the rising prominence of DJs in the burgeoning UK club culture. "A guy called Gerald. The name he went by was Gerald Simpson. He was a DJ from Manchester, who often man the turntables at the Haseyenda, the infamous club owned by New Order and Factory Records." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OiqdZfxdBP0
**German Musicians Seeking Musical Identity (Early 1970s)** : Influenced by the exposure to electronic sounds through West German Radio's experimental studio, many young German musicians in the early 1970s turned to electronics. This was a deliberate effort to create a musical identity distinct from the rock and roll produced by British and American artists. "many young Germans turned to electronics as a way of creating a musical identity distinct from the rock and roll that the British and the Americans were making." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OiqdZfxdBP0
**German Reunification and U2's Flight (October 2, 1990)** : On October 2, 1990, a British Airways flight carrying U2 into Tegal Airport in Berlin became the last commercial flight to enter East Germany before its official reunification with West Germany at midnight. The city was filled with celebrations of a united Germany. The pilot, in a state of excitement, circled Berlin to allow passengers to view landmarks like the Brandenburg Gate and Hansa Studios (where U2 was headed to work on their next album). "On October 2nd, 1990, you two, boarded a British Airways flight into Tegal Airport in Berlin. That was the last commercial flight into East Germany before East Germany ceased to exist." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Epz5Sn151bk
**Ghostwriter's "Heart on My Sleeve" (2023)** : In April 2023, an individual known as Ghostwriter created and released a fake duet titled "Heart on My Sleeve," featuring AI-generated voices that mimicked Drake and The Weeknd. This two-minute track immediately drew intense public attention and sparked widespread debates concerning the ethical and legal ramifications of using artists' voices without their consent. "The one that got everybody's attention was a fake duet between a fake Drake and a fake the weekend created and posted in April 2023 by someone named Ghost Rider." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Gibson Bankruptcy (2018)** : The decline in electric guitar sales has significantly impacted manufacturers, leading to Gibson filing for bankruptcy in 2018. "And all of this has hurt manufacturers like Gibson, who filed for bankruptcy in 2018." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oLC49nCf0ZM
**Gibson Fuzz Tone Sales Fluctuation** : Despite Gibson shipping 5,000 Maestro Fuzz Tone units to distributors after its 1962 release, only dozens were sold throughout all of 1964. The pedal initially sat on shelves, failing to catch on with guitarists. However, sales dramatically increased after Keith Richards used a Maestro Fuzzbox pedal on The Rolling Stones' hit "(I Can't Get No) Satisfaction" in the summer of 1965, turning it into a sought-after effect. "Gibson sent 5,000 Fuzz tone units to distributors, but they all just sat on the shelf." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iGav9uJwA_w
**Glasgow School of Art** : Bob Hardy studied painting at the Glasgow School of Art and frequently exhibits his artwork. His background in art, combined with Alex Kapranos's interest in art, helped forge their connection before forming Franz Ferdinand. "He studied painting at the Glasgow School of Art and often exhibits his creations." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RWbKqqW78yg
**Glenn Miller's Disappearance (1944)** : Band leader Glenn Miller is noted as one of the earliest prominent musicians to die in a plane crash. His plane went down over the English Channel in 1944, and he was never seen again. "The first seems to be Band leader Glenn Miller when he was never seen again when his plane went down over the English Channel in 1944." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dgzF_KBSQWM
**Glioblastoma Brain Cancer** : Glioblastoma is a very aggressive form of brain cancer that typically forms in the cerebral hemispheres, with no known cause or cure. Gord Downie's case was primary glioblastoma, characterized by rapidly forming tumors that cause quick symptoms from within the brain itself, first found in his left temporal lobe. The tumors are invasive and infiltrative, making complete surgical removal impossible and leading to recurrence. "He had a form of brain cancer called glioblastoma. It usually forms in the cerebral hemispheres and is very, very aggressive. There was no known cause and no cure." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Um_KEEcCeT4
**Global Indigenous Music Contributions** : While the discussion focuses on Indigenous peoples of North America, the original peoples of other countries like Brazil, Australia, and New Zealand have also made their own contributions to rock music. "And keep in mind that we've only focused on the Indigenous peoples of North America. The original people of places like Brazil and Australia and New Zealand, so many other countries, have made their own contributions to rock." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YFv6Ln00XjA
**Global Music Expansion via Streaming** : Streaming services have "flattened out the world" of music, making global genres like K-pop massively popular, leading to major growth in Bollywood songs from India, and an explosion of interesting Latin music worldwide. This vast global library far surpasses the inventory of traditional record stores. "The world has flattened out. K-pop is massive. One genre that saw major growth in the decade was Bollywood songs from India. Plus, there's been an explosion in interesting Latin music all over the world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5Y6nHw5xx88
**Global Music Vault Under Construction in Norway** : The Global Music Vault is currently under construction on an island in Norway, designed to be a secure repository for digital music. The ambitious project aims to store and protect music for a millennium, with a design intended to withstand even a nuclear strike, and already has artists like The Beatles' organization on board. "This will be a place where digital music will be stored and protected, it's hoped, for a thousand years." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-N6IM5Nm5iw
**Global Niche CD Markets (Japan, Germany, South Africa, South Korea)** : While global CD sales have plummeted, specific countries such as Japan, Germany, South Africa, and South Korea still maintain relatively strong CD markets. However, even these markets are beginning to experience erosion due to the increasing adoption of streaming music services. "Japan, Germany, South Africa and South Korea have very solid CD markets. But even those countries are starting to see erosion in CD sales, largely because of the adoption of streaming music services." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Global Political and Social Context for Art** : Around the time of Billy Talent's "Afraid of Heights" album, global events such as Brexit, the European refugee crisis, the rise of the right, and the political climate surrounding Donald Trump created a demand for "angry, aggressive, outspoken art of all sorts." The band believes their music, which addresses these realities, fit the timing. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ocMHRLQMQY0
**Global Recorded Music Business Value (Early 2021)** : At the start of 2021, the entire global recorded music industry had an estimated value of approximately $21 billion. "The entire global recorded music business was worth somewhere around $21 billion at the beginning of 2021." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Global Rock Concert Fatalities (1974-2003)** : According to a study, 136 people died at rock concerts around the world between 1974 and 2003, with many more suffering injuries. This highlights the inherent risks associated with large gatherings at music events, despite safety precautions. "According to a study on the subject, 136 people died at rock concerts around the world between 1974 and 2003, and many, many, many others have been injured." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7v1REnqKuXE
**Global Skate Park Expansion** : More cities and towns worldwide have been building skateboard parks, exemplified by a massive 12,000 square foot park with a 5,000 seat stadium dedicated to skateboarding in China. "In China, there's a 12,000 square foot park complete with a 5,000 seat stadium just for skateboarding." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=44dXy0StjIQ
**Global Streaming Service Access** : Currently, nearly every country globally, with the exception of North Korea, has access to some form of music streaming service. Regional streaming companies like Jooks, Potarian, Anghami, Ghana, Melon, and KK Box serve markets in India, the Middle East, and China, respectively. "And today, outside of North Korea, every country on the planet has some kind of access to some sort of streaming service. Although, wait a minute, there is a North Korean streaming TV service called, and I did not make this up. It is called Manbang that translates as everywhere. Places like India and the Middle East and China have their own streaming companies. They have names like Jooks and Potarian, Anghami and Ghana, Melon and KK Box." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**Globella** : This is the scientific or less-common name for the space located between one's eyebrows. "Have you ever heard of a globella? That's the space between our eyebrows." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Gloomy 1970s England** : The late 1970s in England were characterized by a "dark gloomy time" marked by high unemployment, strikes, and recession, particularly impacting young people and contributing to their gravitation towards dark music. "Well, it was a dark gloomy time in England. High unemployment, strikes, recession." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**Gold Rush for Artists' Music Catalogs (Late 2010s)** : The late 2010s initiated a "gold rush" where companies like Primary Wave and Hypnosis Song Fund began aggressively buying artists' music catalogs. This trend offered artists upfront payments for their life's work, providing long-term financial security, investment capital, and tax advantages by converting future royalties into capital gains, taxed at a lower rate. "For artists, this was a pretty cool opportunity. Yes, they sold off their life's work, but for a price determined by a multiple of what their songs generated over a given period of time." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GYUuTbOvyak
**Golden Age of Black Sitcoms in the 1990s** : The period is regarded as a "cultural touchstone" and a "90s golden age when blacks, it comes rural television." While *A Different World* was concluding, *The Fresh Prince of Bel-Air* had recently premiered, and *Living Single* would debut on Fox just a year after *Martin*. "These days, the show is regarded as a cultural touchstone, part of a 90s golden age when blacks, it comes rural television." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6O5TCTO-go4
**Golden Age of Compact Discs (1990s)** : The 1990s were a period of significant financial prosperity in the music industry, largely due to the "Golden Age of Compact Discs," which saw music sales increase year after year. This economic boom, coupled with Generation X's buying habits, facilitated the rise of many music festivals. "Music sales were going up and up and up and up and up every year thanks to the Golden Age of Compact Discs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7JP2tjJibTQ
**Golden Age of Concept Albums (1970s)** : The 1970s marked the "golden age" of the concept album, a period when many progressive rock bands and others embraced the format extensively. "The golden age of the concept album was in the 1970s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Dm5N6yDRxXQ
**Golden Age of Music Video (80s and 90s)** : The 1980s and 1990s are remembered as the "golden age of the music video," during which channels like MTV exerted powerful musical, cultural, and political influence globally. During this era, having a video for every single was a requirement for aspiring stars, leading to fierce competition and escalating production values. "The 80s and 90s were the golden age of the music video. MTV was a powerful musical, cultural, and even political force that extended beyond the United States." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Golden Age of Synthesizers (Mid-1970s - Mid-1980s)** : This era marked a transformative period where electronic machines began to create unprecedented sounds, opening up vast new possibilities for music. Synthesizers became sufficiently small, affordable, and powerful to significantly influence musical composition and direction. "So many new possibilities opened up during an era that became known as the Golden Age of synthesizers, everything changed and changed fast." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0fQQubt9UTI
**Goldfish Can Distinguish Music by Different Composers – 75% Accuracy for Bach vs. Stravinsky** : Scientists played music by Bach and Stravinsky for goldfish and found the fish could identify the correct composer approximately 75% of the time. "Scientists played music by Bach and Stravinsky for the fish." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**Google Acquisition of YouTube** : Google purchased YouTube on October 9, 2006, despite significant copyright challenges arising from user-uploaded musical content. "Google who bought YouTube on October 9, 2006." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Google InSynth (Neural Synthesizer) (N/A)** : Google's InSynth, or Neural Synthesizer, is a program that leverages deep neural networks to produce distinctive sounds that are often difficult or impossible to create through other means. Google offers a website where users can interact with this program and explore its sonic capabilities. "InSynth actually stands for Neural Synthesizer. It uses deep neural networks to create interesting sounds that are difficult or even impossible to produce elsewhere." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Google Lyria: Humming to Guitar Riff (N/A)** : Google's Lyria program offers an intuitive application for musicians: users can hum a melody into their phone, and Lyria will then transform that humming into a guitar riff. This feature showcases AI's ability to translate simple vocal input into instrument-specific musical output, aiding in songwriting. "All you have to do is hum a melody into your phone and Lyria will turn that humming into a guitar riff." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Google WaveNet for Natural Speech (N/A)** : Google introduced WaveNet, an AI program specifically designed to generate natural-sounding speech. It was trained on tens of thousands of speech samples and analyzed sounds 24,000 times per second to predict subsequent sounds, significantly enhancing speech synthesis technology and contributing to the natural voices of modern AI assistants like Alexa and Siri. "Google introduced something called wave net. It specialized in generating natural sounding speech." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Gorillaz.com** : The official website for Gorillaz, which at one point allowed users to see what Kong Studios looked like. It was temporarily shut down or abandoned during Phase 2 (Slowboat to Hades), but reappeared in December 2004 to announce the "Demon Days" album. During Phase 3 (Escape to Plastic Beach), it offered a full tour of the virtual Plastic Island. "We can, however, see what it looks like at gorillas.com." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iVdTCqpd798
**Goth & Industrial Comparison** : Goth and Industrial music share characteristics like darkness, introspection, and black fashion, but Industrial is significantly harder and heavier. Goth is softer, slower, and more keyboard-based, with dance club influences, while Industrial uses guitars, sharp keyboards, and heavy beats. "A close cousin of Goth is industrial music. My Goth, it's dark and often introspective, and the dominant fashion color is, of course, black." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**Goth Fashion (Detailed)** : Goth fashion, a form of artistic expression, includes dog collars, lace, leather, velvet, fishnet stockings, ruffled shirts, stilettos or Doc Martens, and often black painted nails. These elements contribute to the distinctive "dressed for a never-ending funeral" aesthetic. "They wore dog collars and lace and leather and velvet and fishnet stockings and ruffled shirts and stilettos or dock martens and painted nails, often black." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XaLsZFLrMnI
**Goth Fashion Appeal** : The "Goth look" is described as having something "really appealing and luring" about it. "There's something really appealing and luring about the Goth look." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**Goth Fashion in Pop Culture** : Goth fashion has extensively spread throughout pop culture, influencing movies, television, mainstream fashion, literature, and art. "And look how cough fashion is spread throughout pop culture. Movies, TV, mainstream fashion, literature, art." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**Goth Longevity** : Goth has existed for decades and is firmly established, with no signs of disappearing soon. "God has been around for decades and it is not going away anytime soon." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**Goth Magazine Recommendations** : Drop Dead magazine and Gothic Beauty are specifically recommended as Goth-themed publications worth exploring. "There's Drop Dead magazine, there's Gothic Beauty that's another and a bunch of other fanzines." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**Goth Public Misunderstanding** : Goth Rock is generally "least understood" by the general public and mass media, often perceived as "scary and intimidating" if the underlying culture is not appreciated. "But it's also one of the least understood or at least by the general public in the mass media." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**Goth Subculture Initiation** : To be "initiated and accepted" into the Goth subculture, individuals are expected to "know your stuff" and be prepared to debate and defend their knowledge. "If you're going to be initiated and accepted, you'd better know your stuff." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**Goth Subculture Types** : The Goth subculture encompasses various types, including traditional Goths, neo-Goths, mall Goths, mini-Goths, and "baby bats" (teenagers too young for Goth clubs). "You have your traditional Goths and your neo-Goths, mall Goths, mini-Goths and baby bats or teenagers who are too young to get into the Goth clubs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**Goth Subculture and Violent Incidents** : The Goth subculture and its followers are frequently and unfairly targeted when individuals commit acts of violence, such as the shootings at Columbine or Dawson College, leading to widespread stigma for the entire group due to the extreme actions of a "tiny, tiny, tiny few." "Goth music and Goth followers are often targeted when someone flips out and does something weird or awful, like a shooting up combine or Dawson College." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**Goth's Big Break (Early 1983)** : The British music media, weary of happy, bouncy, lightweight technopop that had been everywhere for a couple of years, declared it "boring and dead" in early 1983. This declaration positioned goth as the new sound and led to songs featuring black-clad artists with distinctive makeup and hair filling the charts. "Goth's Big Break came in early 1983. And we're getting a little weary of the happy, bouncy, lightweight techno pop that had been everywhere for the past couple of years." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XaLsZFLrMnI
**Gothic Architecture Reference** : Gothic architecture, exemplified by medieval churches, provides a historical and aesthetic root for the term "Gothic." "Going back further, there's Gothic architecture exemplified in the churches of the Middle Ages." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**Gothic Cathedral Acoustics** : Gothic cathedrals, primarily built from stone, reflect approximately 98% of all sound, creating highly echoey environments with reverberation times potentially exceeding three seconds. These acoustics made intelligible human speech difficult but perfectly suited for music with long notes, which took advantage of the natural amplification and reverberation, explaining why mass was chanted instead of spoken. "Hearing intelligible human speech is pretty much impossible in a Gothic cathedral." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qrrwqdpCjpQ
**Gothic Literature Influence** : Nineteenth-century Gothic literature, with Mary Shelley's "Frankenstein" as a prime example, served as a significant influence on the themes and atmosphere of early Goth music. "This music was Gothic in the sense that it was reminiscent of themes in 19th century literature known as Gothic. The original novel Frankenstein is an example of Gothic literature." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**Grady Martin: Indirect Inventor of the Power Cord (1960)** : Grady Martin is identified as the indirect inventor of the "power cord," which in this context refers to the pioneering of distorted electric guitar sound. In 1960, during a session with Marty Robbins, Martin's amplifier and guitar unexpectedly produced a cool, fuzzy, distorted sound. This led him and others to devise a way to reproduce this sound on command via an electronic circuit and foot pedal, effectively creating the fuzz box and ushering in an era of powerful, distorted guitar tones. "Grady Martin. He is the indirect inventor of the power cord." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PNiL63fY1Y8
**Grammys Awards Naming Origin** : The prestigious American music awards are officially known as the "Grammys," a shortened form of "gramophone." This name was chosen over "Eddies," which would have honored Thomas Edison, in a decision that ultimately favored the gramophone as the iconic symbol for recorded music. "This is why we call those American music awards the Grammys. They were almost called the Eddies, after Edison, but Grammys, short for gramophone, won out." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TsiYLGGSJ2U
**Gramophone Magazine's Criticism of Electrical Recording** : The editor of *Gramophone Magazine* was highly critical of the new electrical recording technology introduced in 1925. He stated that "the new noise of electrical recording is quite unbearable" and doubted that "any audience could sit and listen nowadays to hours of electrical recording and remain sane." "The editor of Gramophone Magazine wrote that the new noise of electrical recording is quite unbearable." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Grand Funk Railroad Bassist – Roadie Replaced Amplifier Tubes Mid-Song** : Mel Schacher, bass player for Grand Funk Railroad, drove his amplifier so hard during shows that a roadie sat on a stool directly behind the amp and replaced output tubes one by one as they blew out during performances. "A roadie sat on a stool behind the amp and replaced tubes mid-song as they blew out." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Grateful Dead Sponsored the 1992 Lithuanian Olympic Basketball Team** : The Grateful Dead sponsored the Lithuanian men's Olympic basketball team in 1992. Lithuania was struggling financially after gaining independence following the fall of the Soviet Union. The team ended up beating Russia for the bronze medal. "The grateful dad sponsored the Lithuanian Men's Olympic basketball team." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Great Pacific Garbage Patch / Pacific Trash Vortex** : According to environmental legends, this is an area where Pacific Ocean currents collect waterborne plastic pollution. It served as a significant inspiration for the Gorillaz album "Plastic Beach," with "Plastic Island" becoming the band's new headquarters within this vortex. "Now according to environmental legends, Pacific Ocean currents drive all kinds of waterborne plastic pollution into an area that's become known as the Great Pacific Garbage Patch or the Pacific Trash Vortex." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iVdTCqpd798
**Great Songs and Multiple Interpretations** : A truly great song can be interpreted multiple times by different people and still sound good, whether performed by a full band or a single person. Fans appreciate hearing songs done in different ways, sometimes preferring the cover to the original or finding new dimensions in it. "The best compositions not only sound great when played by a full band, but could also sound great when performed by one person around a campfire." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yuA9Xor8vfw
**Greatest Hits Album as a Bad Omen** : The release of a greatest hits album for a band that is officially on hiatus is often perceived as a negative sign by the record company, typically indicating that the label has lost hope for the group's future or reunion. "Whenever you see a label release a greatest hits album for a group that's supposedly on hiatus, that's usually bad news." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q-QP7ePxkQE
**Green Day Members' Early Musical Influences** : Before forming Sweet Children, Billy Joe Armstrong and Mike Dirnt played cover songs in early bands, including Van Halen's "Ain't Talkin' 'Bout Love" and Ozzy Osbourne's "Crazy Train." Green Day as a band later expressed admiration for The Ramones and a deliberate aversion to the sound of bands like Journey and Jefferson Airplane. "Over the next year, they formed a couple of bands that played songs like Van Halen's Ain't Talking About Love and Ozzie's Crazy Train." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nyfdGYWelic
**Green Day – A Day Spent Smoking Weed; From an Early Billy Joe Armstrong Song** : The name refers to a day spent smoking marijuana ("a green day"). It started as an early Billy Joe Armstrong song written when the group was still called Sweet Children. "That's all about a day, smoking weed, a green day." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Gregory Coleman's Unsung Status** : Despite playing the "Amen Break," sampled in over 5,309 songs, drummer Gregory Coleman of The Winston's died homeless and broke in 2006, unaware of his profound impact on music history and without receiving any royalties. He remains an archetypal "unsung hero." "Gregory Coleman actually died homeless and broke in 2006, not knowing about what he'd done for music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Growing Market for Catalog Sales (Heritage Artists & Estates)** : The market for buying and selling song catalogs is expected to continue growing, driven by "heritage artists" (older acts) who want to organize their financial affairs before they die, and by the estates of deceased artists looking to maximize and extend the legacy and income of the artist's work. "The business of buying and selling song catalogs is only going to grow, especially with the number of heritage artists, which are the older acts who want to put their affairs in order before they die, growing by the day." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Growth of MP3 Playback in Phones (2005)** : By 2005, mobile phones equipped with MP3 playback capabilities had become increasingly common, particularly in South Korea, where over half of all new phones sold featured this functionality. This trend highlighted the growing demand for integrated music features in mobile devices. "Listen, by 2005, more than half the phones being bought in South Korea had MP3 playback capabilities." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**Growth of Mega-Stores and Retail Competition (1990s)** : The 1990s saw a significant expansion of record stores, including the rise of mega-stores like HMV, Tower Records, and Virgin Records, alongside chains such as Sam the Record Man, Sam Goodie, Camelot, and Sunrise, Music World. These retailers fiercely competed to sell CDs for record labels, signifying a boom in physical music retail. "More CDs were sold and more record stores opened. And not just stores, mega stores." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r8AazBVoS7g
**Grunge's Perfect Confluence** : The rise of grunge was propelled by a "perfect confluence of events and conditions" involving art, economics, demographics, and social issues, primarily centered around Generation X. "It arrived at one of those perfect confluence of events and conditions involving art, economics, demographics, and social issues." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tFBSrKMEZxI
**Guidance on Buying Used Vinyl** : Buyers are advised to exercise caution when purchasing vinyl records pressed between the mid-1970s and early 1980s, as manufacturing quality during this period was notoriously poor, often involving recycled, impure PVC and lighter records (e.g., 90 grams). Exceptions include audiophile editions made from virgin vinyl or records produced before 1972. Similarly, records from the 1980s and 1990s should be scrutinized, as many were likely direct-from-CD pressings that were not properly mastered for vinyl and generally exhibit inferior sound. "Be very careful buying vinyl, but was pressed in the mid to late 70s and early 1980s. Manufacturing quality, and I can't overemphasize this enough, was terrible during that period." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2g4QSREzpw
**Guitar Customization Trend** : The Fender Stratocaster's design inherently allowed players to easily take it apart and modify it in countless ways.
