This functionality led to other manufacturers adopting customizable features in their own guitars, setting a trend for personalized instruments. "Other manufacturers took note and made their guitars customizable too." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Y0HW8LQKvJw
**Guitar Volume Problem and Gender Stereotypes** : The guitar's inherent quietness for many years contributed to its perception as a "feminine" instrument. It was considered one of the few instruments, besides the piano, appropriate for young ladies, and its low volume confined its enjoyment to specific, intimate settings. "For years, its inherent quietness was considered very feminine. The Spanish guitar was one of the few instruments outside the piano that was considered appropriate for young ladies to play. And because it didn't produce much volume, it could really only be enjoyed in certain settings." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E6cXyaHALBQ
**HMV Retail Innovations (1990s)** : HMV introduced several customer-centric innovations in the 1990s that were revolutionary for music retail. These included providing shopping baskets and carts due to the large number of CDs people bought, a loyalty card program (buy 10 CDs, get the 11th free), and remarkably, a return policy for unwanted CDs, reflecting the lucrative nature of the market. "One of the biggest innovations from HMV in the 90s was to provide customers with shopping baskets and shopping carts." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r8AazBVoS7g
**Haitian Earthquake of January 12, 2010** : On January 12, 2010, Haiti was struck by a severe 7.0 magnitude earthquake, a catastrophic event that led to over 200,000 fatalities and rendered more than 2 million people homeless. Following this disaster, members of Arcade Fire made regular, unpublicized trips to Haiti to assist directly on the ground. "After the 7.0 earthquake of January 12, 2010, which killed over 200,000 and left upwards of 2 million homeless, arcade fires have been making regular trips to Haiti to help out on the ground." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ir2FfLQJNtY
**Halifax: "The New Seattle" (1991-1993)** : Between late 1991 and 1993, Halifax experienced a period where it was discovered by America and even suspected, at one point, to be the "new Seattle" due to its vibrant music scene. This perception was largely influenced by bands like Sloan signing with labels like Sub Pop. "There was a time between late 1991 and 1993 that America discovered Halifax. It was even, at one point, suspected to be the new Seattle, largely because of this band who ended up being signed to Subpop for their first album." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6PGgNCaW4xo
**Hamilton Ambient Music Scene** : From late 1979 to early 1980s, Brian Eno and Daniel Landwom collaborated in Hamilton, producing approximately "half a dozen ambient records." During this period, they refined a distinctive "textural sound" characterized by building sounds with different octaves, which later influenced their work with U2. "We made a half a dozen ambient records in Hamilton and we had refined this textural sound that Brian was very excited about and we found through various experiments, we found a way of building sounds with different octaves." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L03Yk1SHRNM
**Hamilton Recording Hub** : Located in the east end of downtown Hamilton, Grand Avenue Studios was a new recording facility where Daniel Lanois began working in 1976, spending countless hours producing records for various artists including Rafi. The studio was later sold by Daniel and his brother Bob in the mid-1980s. "In 1976 Dan got a job at a new recording studio in the east end of downtown Hamilton called Grand Avenue Studios where he spent countless hours making records with tons of different performers including Rafi, the children's entertainer." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkSKxm_U9Yc
**Hannah's Affinity for Punk Rock and Underground Music** : Hannah, the victim profiled in the episode, cultivated a distinct personal identity rooted in punk rock culture. Her body became a "canvas of ink and water" after getting her first tattoo at 16, with some of these designs drawing inspiration from her "infatuation of punk rock." She explicitly identified as "a punk," which informed her choice of avoiding "preppier clothes," and her musical tastes leaned towards "anything that's more obscure and underground," favoring sounds that were "loud and not mainstream." "Some are inspired by her infatuation of punk rock." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GQuKQqbXqSM
**Hard Rock Empire Ownership** : The entire Hard Rock Empire, which includes 185 cafes, 25 hotels, and 12 casinos across 74 countries, is owned by the Seminole Tribe of Florida. "I should point out, speaking of the Hard Rock Hotel, that the entire Hard Rock Empire is actually owned by the Seminal Tribe of Florida. 185 cafes, 25 hotels, 12 casinos, 74 countries." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YFv6Ln00XjA
**Hardcore Dancing (New York City)** : Hardcore dancing, which originated in New York City, introduced specific moves like "wind milling" and "axe handling." These movements likely involved aggressive arm and body motions, contributing to the physical intensity of the mosh pit. "And one of the moves was called wind milling and another was axe handling." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KMx4bmq8o2Y
**Hardcore Logo (1996) Movie Background** : The 1996 movie "Hardcore Logo" was deliberately crafted to appear as a genuine documentary about real musicians, creating an illusion and mythology around its events, characters, and music. The film was based on a book written by Michael Turner, and its director, Bruce McDonald, was evasive about the band Swamp Baby to preserve this illusion. "The whole thing about Hardcore logo is that it's supposed to be a real documentary about some real musicians." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yl2poVWguu0
**Hardcore as Foundation for Indie Labels** : Thanks to the political climate under Ronald Reagan, hardcore music was born and created a demand for a new generation of independent record labels. These labels, initially focused on hardcore, quickly expanded their scope far beyond their original genre. "From line to all this hardcore history is that thanks to Ronald Reagan, hardcore was born and helped create the need, desire, and demand for a new generation of independent record labels that would quickly extend far beyond what these labels were all about." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VOv5mT1dJd8
**Hardcore's Role in Popularizing DIY Labels** : The hardcore scene was instrumental in popularizing the concept of forming one's own record label. This do-it-yourself approach became a widespread strategy for bands unable to secure deals with mainstream labels. "It was through hardcore that the idea of forming your own record label was popularized." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0qigzi1j81U
**Harry Saltzman's Dislike of "Goldfinger" Theme (1964)** : Harry Saltzman, one of the producers of the James Bond film "Goldfinger," vehemently hated Shirley Bassey's title theme. He sent a note back stating, "That's the worst blanking song I've ever heard in my blanking life," and desired another theme song, but time constraints forced its inclusion in the film, where it became a top 40 hit. "Harry Salisman, one of the producers of the film absolutely hated it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=K0pgJ7XAlpo
**Hate for New Forms of Vocal Expression** : The advent of the microphone, which enabled new singing styles like crooning, marked the beginning of decades of intense criticism and "hate" for evolving forms of vocal expression. This pattern of disapproval affected various artists and genres, including R&B acts, Elvis, The Beatles, Dylan, Jagger, New Wavers, rappers, and even death metal vocalists. "This was the beginning of decades of hate for new forms of vocal expression." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Hatsune Miku: Virtual Pop Star (2007)** : Launched in 2007, Hatsune Miku is a Japanese virtual pop star, presented as a 16-year-old cartoon character, who performs live on stage using advanced hologram technology. While not a true AI creation, she represents an early form of artificial performer, offering a model for musical acts free from the typical human artist challenges. "You can go back as far as 2007 for Hatsune Miku, a Japanese virtual pop star who really isn't a true AI creation, but she is a cartoon character who performs live on stage using some pretty interesting hologram type tech." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**HeadCount** : HeadCount is an organization focused on voter registration, which was supported by the philanthropic initiatives of the Warped Tour. "Headcount is all about voter registration." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jfQaUV6Bq4
**Health Benefits of Singing in the Shower** : A small study conducted in the UK suggests that the simple act of singing in the shower every morning can improve both physical and mental well-being. Participants reported feeling generally better about everything after just five days of this routine. "Singing in the shower might actually be good for your physical and mental health." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**Hearing Acuity and Musical Preference with Age** : As individuals age, their "hearing acuity" changes, often resulting in a lower tolerance for loud sounds and high frequencies. This physiological change can contribute to a preference for milder music, as high volume, thumping beats, and high BPMs become less appealing. The stress and cortisol from adult life further reduce the need for adrenaline-inducing music, which may even become irritating. "We can, over time, acquire a lower tolerance for loud sounds and audio with high frequencies, so you can see why that would be an issue." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m7MbV-eCLyE
**Heath Ledger's Joker Portrayal Influence** : Heath Ledger's critically acclaimed portrayal of the Joker character was based on old television interviews with musician Tom Waits. The similarities between Waits's mannerisms in these videos and Ledger's performance are noted as being obvious. "Heath Ledger's portrayal of the Joker was based on old TV interviews with Tom Waits." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**Heavy Metal Element Classification** : Elements 113, 115, 117, and 118, despite being the heaviest yet discovered, are classified as non-metals on the periodic table because they occupy the right-hand side, an area reserved for non-metals. Element 117 is a halogen, and 118 is a noble gas, while 113 and 115 have only distant or predicted transitional metal properties, not fitting the "heavy metal" designation. "if we look at the periodic table, you'll see that these new elements occupy space on the right hand side. This is the area of the table reserved for non-metals. Actual metal elements are grouped on the left side of the table." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VMPLplhDk8I
**Hereditary Characteristics in Creative Households** : The concept that growing up in a creative household often leads to hereditary characteristics or learned abilities being passed down from parent to child is explored. This suggests that children in such environments are likely to pick up creative traits from their surroundings or genetic predispositions. "I suppose if you grow up in a creative household, something's going to rub off, whether it be something in your jeans, or just because of what goes on around you as you're growing up." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MmXDbXI6Sqk
**Herman Hollerith: Father of Modern Computing (Unspecified Year)** : Herman Hollerith is credited as the father of modern computing due to his pioneering theories and inventions, particularly his punched card system. His work directly led to the formation of companies that would eventually become International Business Machines (IBM), and is considered foundational to the existence of modern tech giants like Intel and Microsoft, and devices like the iPod. "Hollerith then could be considered the father of modern computing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PNiL63fY1Y8
**Heroin and Drug Use - Britpop's Decline** : Rampant drug use, particularly heroin, among many artists contributed significantly to the fragmentation, splintering, and eventual collapse of the Britpop scene by the end of 1997. "And then there were the drugs, heroin especially. And so many artists were into so many chemicals that everything just began to fragment and splinter and fall apart." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DSvoppf1dGY
**High CD Profit Margins (1990s)** : Despite new manufacturing plants coming online annually and increasing sales volumes, CD prices remained stubbornly high in the 1990s, often $14, $15, $16, or more. While the manufacturing cost per CD dropped to about $1 for labels, they maintained high retail prices, leading to substantial profit margins. "Labels had brought the cost of manufacturing a CD down to about $1. Yet the retail price remained $14, $15, $16 or more. So talk about a fat profit margin." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**High Earnings Potential from Licensing** : With careful management and a bit of luck, a single song can generate millions of dollars for an artist through strategic licensing deals across various media, highlighting the significant financial potential of this revenue stream. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**High Fidelity (2000) Movie Scene** : A scene from the 2000 movie *High Fidelity* serves as an opening anecdote to introduce the band Belle and Sebastian. In the scene, record store owner Rob and his employee Dick are enjoying a new Belle and Sebastian record, only for Jack Black's character, Barry, to vociferously reject the music and dramatically rip the cassette from the machine. "There's a scene in the 2000 movie High Fidelity that introduced a lot of people to the band Belen Sebastian." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jhgZrwtYE2g
**High School Musical Soundtrack Sales (2006)** : The "High School Musical" soundtrack was a top-selling album in 2006, selling 3.7 million copies. Its commercial success was notable during a period of overall declining recorded music sales, highlighting its specific appeal. "2006, high school musical, 3.7 million." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**High-Concept Music Videos Pervasive (Mid-Late 90s)** : High-concept music videos became pervasive in the mid to late 1990s, with artists like Radiohead, Björk, and Jamiroquai (with "Virtual Insanity") leading the way. This era saw a return to glamour in pop music with expensive videos from stars like Britney Spears and the Backstreet Boys, while hip-hop continued to emphasize themes of money and status. "High and concept music videos were everywhere in the mid to late 1990s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**High-Selling Movie Soundtracks (Sales Figures)** : Several movie soundtracks achieved extraordinary sales figures. Notable examples include *Purple Rain* (25 million copies), *Titanic* (30 million copies), *Dirty Dancing* (32 million copies), *Grease* (38 million copies), *Saturday Night Fever* (40 million copies), and *The Bodyguard* (45 million copies). Even *Space Jam* from 1996 sold 6 million copies. "Princess Purple Rain, 25 million copies, Titanic, 30 million copies, Dirty Dancing, 32 million, Greece, 38 million, Saturday Night Fever, 40 million, the Bodyguard, 45 million, even Space Jam from 1996 sold 6 million." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**Higher Fidelity of Physical Formats (CDs and Vinyl)** : For the time being, physical formats like compact discs and vinyl records continue to offer higher fidelity and better sonic quality compared to standard digital files. However, this is gradually changing with the advancement of high-resolution audio formats. "For the time being, these formats, compact discs and vinyl, deliver higher fidelity and better sonic quality than we can get from digital files. Now, that's slowly changing with formats like high resolution audio, which are fantastic, by the way." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Highest Gig in the World (Evolution)** : The record for the highest gig in the world by altitude has been progressively broken. Jamiroquai initially set the record at 35,000 feet on February 27, 2007. This was surpassed by The Black Eyed Peas at 41,000 feet on September 8, 2009. James Blunt then raised it to 42,080 feet on June 28, 2010, and finally, Tony Hadley and Kim Wilde reached 43,000 feet on March 10, 2013, which is close to the operational ceiling of current passenger jets. "Jumaricwai, at least at first. On February 27th, 2007, he and his band boarded a private 757 charted by Sony Ericsson, the mobile phone company." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hmnMG6f80Sg
**Highest Value Unsold Catalogs** : Catalogs identified as potential high-value sales in the future include Pink Floyd, Metallica, and U2. The Beatles catalog is considered the "biggest prize" but is unlikely to be sold again due to its complex history and current ownership. "As you want Pink Floyd's catalog might go for it or Metallica's or here's one, you too. The biggest prize, of course, would be the Beatles, but given what Paul and Ringo and the Estates of John and George have gone through, I just can't see any of those songs going up for sale again, ever, at least not in the near future." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Hinge Points in History** : Singular events that changed everything that came after. Some are obvious, like the fall of the Berlin Wall, while others are subtle, like a newspaper clipping in Sarajevo in April 1914 announcing Archduke Francis Ferdinand's visit, which led to his assassination by Gavriel Princeep on June 28th, triggering World War I. This concept is used to frame the discussion of influential, below-the-radar rock concerts. "History is full of what academics call hinge points. These are singular events that changed everything that came after." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=huAvyInzz3Q
**Hip Hop's Role in Alt Rock's Expansion** : The advent of hip hop and rap significantly broadened the scope of alt rock, enabling it to "stretch even further" and enlarge its fanbase, making this influence one of the most critical musical evolutions of the 1990s. "hip hop and rap allowed alt rock to stretch even further and to make the tent for fans even bigger. That will go down as one of the most important musical evolutions of the 90s" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5bQz8R0gbXc
**Hip-Hop Culture & Disco's Influence on Dance Music** : By the 1980s, hip-hop culture was gaining significant establishment. While disco had previously made dancing "uncool" for a certain demographic, this perception eventually shifted, leading people to embrace dancing again. This context facilitated the emergence of new danceable sounds in post-punk, incorporating elements from various genres. "As the new decade began, which is to say the 1980s, hip-hop culture was becoming more and more established, and while disco had made dancing very uncool for a certain segment of the population, it was only a matter of time before that changed and people started moving again to the music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LgJ7ww2AgF4
**Hip-Pocket Records - Failed Flexi Discs (1966-1969)** : In 1966, Filco and Ford attempted to sell "Hip-Pocket Records" concurrently with eight-track players. These were thin, bendable plastic Flexi Discs, with Atlantic, Mercury, and Roulette Records releasing singles by artists like Chuck Berry, Tommy James, Ben Morrison, Sonny and Cher, and Neil Diamond for $0.69 at Ford dealerships and Woolworth stores. While playable on regular turntables, Filco Ford also offered the "Mini Radio Fono," a pocket-sized turntable. Another company, Ameracom, sold "Pocket Discs" (teaming with Apple Records for Beatles singles) in vending machines for 50 cents. The format proved unsuccessful and was discontinued by 1969. `"At the same time, Filco and Ford were installing tens of thousands of eight track players into cars in 1966. They were also trying to sell hip-pocket records."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Hipgnosis Design Studio** : Based out of London, Hipgnosis was a prominent design studio known for its work for Pink Floyd, Led Zeppelin, ACDC, and Peter Gabriel. The company was founded by Strom Thorgerson, Aubrey Powell, and later included Peter Christopherson. Hipgnosis had a successful run until its dissolution in 1983, with Thorgerson continuing album art creation until his death in 2013. Their designs were characterized by their enigmatic and surreal qualities, prompting viewers to interpret deeper messages. "Three guys were behind the company, Strom Thorgerson, Aubrey Powell, and eventually Peter Christofferson. They had a great run that ended with the dissolution of the company in 1983, and Thorgerson continued to create album art until he died in 2013." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pwebM3TfSdg
**Hipgnosis – Album Design Company** : Hipgnosis was a celebrated album design company with which Storm Thorgerson was famously associated, creating many iconic covers for rock and progressive artists. However, by the time he designed Audioslave's 2002 debut album cover, Thorgerson was no longer formally working with Hipgnosis. "By the time Audio Slave came along, Storm was no longer with hypnosis, the famous album design company." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Hippie Culture in 1967** : The formation of The Stooges in 1967 coincided with the peak of hippie culture, characterized by phrases like "turning on, tuning in and dropping out," alongside significant LSD use. This cultural backdrop influenced the band's initial acid-fueled formation. "It was 1967. Hippie culture was at its peak. He was turning on, tuning in and dropping out and dropping a lot of acid." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=w-rihv544I8
**Hipster Aesthetic** : The hipster ideal of coolness involved an aesthetic that suggested detachment from mainstream media, as if one had "never seen television." "The way to be cool wasn't to look like a television star. It was to look like, as though you've never seen television." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**Hipster Culture Characteristics** : Hipster culture quietly championed forgotten styles of clothing, beer, cigarettes, and music; embraced retro as cool; valued environmentalism; sought irony in making "nerdy" things cool; and emphasized sustainable, organic living and being recognized for individuality. "Long forgotten styles of clothing, beer, cigarettes, and music were becoming popular again. Retro was cool, the environment was precious, and old was the new new." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**Hipster Culture Origins (2000s)** : Hipster culture emerged around 2000, evolving into ingrained attitudes, fashion, and trends by 2010, marking an "uprising" against mainstream society. "hipster culture, which has a history that goes back to at least 2000." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**Hipster Culture in 2010s** : By 2010, hipster attitudes, fashion, and trends had become deeply ingrained across society. "By 2010, those attitudes and that fashion and those trends had become ingrained everywhere." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**Hipster Ethos** : A core aspect of hipster identity was reveling in the irony of transforming something perceived as nerdy into something cool. "They reveled in the irony of making something so nerdy, so cool." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**Hipster Fashion** : Hipsters sought distinctive fashion, preferring "silvia plaths cardigans and buddy hollies glasses," and valuing items found in thrift shops or self-made, aiming to look as though they had "never seen television." "Kids wanted to wear silvia plaths cardigans and buddy hollies glasses." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**Hipster Fashion Sourcing** : For hipsters, style was not store-bought but rather acquired from thrift shops or ideally, self-created. "For this new generation, style wasn't something you could buy in a department store. It became something you found in a thrift shop, or ideally, made yourself." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**Hipster Identity** : Above all, hipsters desired recognition for their distinctiveness, aiming to diverge from the mainstream and forge a unique cultural niche. "Above all, they wanted to be recognized for being different, to diverge from the mainstream and carve a cultural niche all for themselves." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**Hipster Lifestyle** : Hipster lifestyle choices included living sustainably and consuming organic, gluten-free grains. "They wanted to live sustainably and eat organic gluten-free grains." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**Hipstermatic Book (2011)** : The book "Hipstermatic," published in 2011, provided insights into hipster culture, contrasting its emergence with mainstream society's focus on reality television and celebrity gossip. "A quote from a book called Hipstermatic, which was published in 2011." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**Historical "Hinge Points"** : Certain historical events are termed "hinge points" by historians because they fundamentally alter the subsequent course of history, with everything that happens next hinging on their outcome. The true significance and impact of such moments are often only fully grasped many years after they have occurred. "Some historians call these moments hinge points, because everything that happened next hinged on the outcome of that one moment." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8UIBWyUt38g
**Historical "Manufactured Consensus"** : In the 1970s, access to music was tightly controlled by traditional gatekeepers like record labels, radio, and printed media, who curated music for the public. This facilitated the "manufacturing of consensus" around certain acts by limiting choices and reinforcing messaging, making it easier for fans to pick a favorite band. "But that was in an era when access to music was tightly controlled by record labels and radio and printed media." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Historical Canadian Bias Against Domestic Music** : For several decades, Canadians largely ignored or disliked Canadian music unless it received validation from outside the country, particularly the United States. This deeply rooted insecurity meant that ambitious Canadian musicians often sought fortune in America, and even those who stayed weren't fully accepted until achieving a US hit. "The majority opinion regarding music was, if it's Canadian, it can't be good. Unless, of course, another country says it is." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6PGgNCaW4xo
**Historical Concert Ticket Underpricing (Pre-Internet)** : Before the advent of the internet, concert tickets were generally "woefully underpriced" relative to the quality and spectacle of the live experience offered. This historical undervaluation meant that artists, managers, and promoters later realized they could significantly raise prices. "In the days before the internet, concert tickets were woefully underpriced relative to the spectacle that you received for the money." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Historical Condemnation of Contemporary Music by Religious Figures** : Over many centuries, various church leaders and philosophers periodically condemned the contemporary music of their day, particularly songs that sounded "out of tune." Some believed anything other than hymns and devotional music was immoral, while others went further, suggesting that any music could ultimately lead one astray into "the pits of hell." "And then after that, a church leader or philosopher would get up on a soapbox every once in a while, and condemn the contemporary music of the day, including and especially songs that sounded out of tune." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=41eKVp8JIhU
**Historical Decades Transition Markers** : The shift between decades is often marked by significant historical events rather than just calendar changes. The assassination of JFK in 1963 is noted as kicking off the 1960s; the death of disco, a recession, and Ronald Reagan's election ended the 1970s; the First Gulf War, another recession, and the rise of alternative music ended the 1980s by 1991; and 9/11, the dot-com bubble burst, and the iPod's introduction concluded the 1990s in 2001. "It took the assassination of JFK to really kick off the new decade." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5Y6nHw5xx88
**Historical Differences of Record Playing Devices** : Historically, "phonograph," "gramophone," "turntable," and "record player" denoted distinct devices, though they are now largely interchangeable. Thomas Edison invented the phonograph, which used rotating cylinders, while Emil Berliner invented the gramophone a decade later, which used spinning discs. Both were brand names (like Kleenex or Aspirin), and for a decade, their formats were incompatible. By World War I, discs superseded cylinders, and even when Edison adopted discs, his format remained incompatible with gramophones. "Thomas Edison invented the phonograph and that word is derived from the Greek sound writing. It played back recordings with a stylus that tracked a groove in a rotating cylinder. That's the brand name phonograph, under which he marketed this device. A decade or so later, Emil Berliner substituted the rotating cylinder for a spinning disc. He called his device a gramophone from the Greek letter writing. Again, that was a brand name, like Kleenex or Asperin. Both systems existed simultaneously for a decade or so, but by World War I, cylinders were considered inferior to discs, so Edison swapped out his cylinder for his own type of rotating discs, but his discs could not be played on a gramophone and vice versa." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TsiYLGGSJ2U
**Historical Disaster Context** : Throughout history, humanity has faced numerous disasters, whether natural or self-inflicted, offering a grim context for music-related tragedies. Examples include the 1918-1920 influenza epidemic (up to 100 million deaths), World War II (120 million deaths), the 14th-century Black Death (up to 200 million deaths), a Moscow indoor water park roof collapse (28 deaths), a Santiago, Chile church fire (3,000 deaths), and two Circus Maximus wall collapses in Rome (13,000 and 1,500 deaths respectively). "The worst disaster of all time? Probably the influenza epidemic of 1918 to 1920." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pgchw2h87aw
**Historical Football Allegiances in Manchester** : Historically, football allegiances in Manchester were geographically and socially divided: Western and North Manchester were primarily United fans, while South and East were City fans. Manchester United was originally seen as the Catholic team and a working man's club supported by trade unions, whereas Manchester City was initially the Protestant team and seen as a richer club. These divisions have largely blurred over time due to modern influences and club ownership changes. "Western North Manchester was mainly United, South and East was City. United was originally the Catholic team, City was, the post-it team, City initially was a rich club, United was a working man's club to sell by the trade unions, but that doesn't, it's not, that's not the case now." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KF_adyXf86Q
**Historical Gender Disparity in Vocal Acceptance** : Historically, male singers have enjoyed greater freedom to deviate from conventional vocal styles compared to women. For many years, female singers were often confined to a specific vocal aesthetic, whereas men faced fewer restrictions. Examples like Bob Dylan, Neil Young, Bon Scott, Brian Johnson, Robert Plant, and Rod Stewart illustrate this broader acceptance of varied male voices. "Dudes have always had it much easier than women when it comes to being different." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7_ZmZxnSOnE
**Historical Precedent for Sampled Sounds (1940s)** : Sampled sounds began to appear in music as early as the 1940s, coinciding with the invention of magnetic recording tape. These early "samples" could include standard sound effects or manipulated shortwave radio interference, predating digital sampling by decades. "Sampled sounds started appearing in music in the 1940s, when magnetic recording tape first appeared." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OiqdZfxdBP0
**Historical Precedent for Singles Dominance (Pre-1965)** : The current emphasis on singles over albums in the streaming era is a "throwback" to how the music industry operated before approximately 1965. In that earlier period, rock and roll and pop music primarily existed as 7-inch 45 RPM singles, with albums reserved for genres like classical, jazz, and soundtracks. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Historical Precedent for Songs About Serial Killers** : The phenomenon of songs being written about murderers and serial killers is not a modern trend, but extends back centuries, with an alleged 16th-century baker-turned-serial killer inspiring the nursery rhyme "The Muffin Man." "Murderers and serial killers have been memorialized in hundreds of songs and, lest you think that this is a modern phenomenon, you're wrong." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Historical Precursors to Fade-Outs** : The concept of a musical piece gradually diminishing in volume has classical precedents. Joseph Haydn's "Farewell Symphony" (c. 1772) ended with musicians leaving the stage one by one, extinguishing candles. Gustav Holtz's "The Planets" (1914) concluded with an offstage female chorus whose volume decreased as a door was gradually closed. "We might go as far back as classical composer Joseph Heiden. In about 1772, he started performances of what would become known as the Farewell Symphony." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**Historical Problem of Event Access Control** : The challenge of managing who could access an event has existed for centuries, with issues arising at the Greek Theater of Dionysus in 325 BCE. This historical context highlights that ticketing complexities are not a modern phenomenon. "Managing who can get into an event has been a problem for centuries and centuries." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OCkov6FTliE
**Historical Sexism in Instrument Appropriateness** : Historically, musical instruments were rigidly divided into categories deemed "feminine" (like piano, harpsichord, gentle violin, acoustic guitar) and all others (drums, brass, wind instruments), with the former being a very small group. This imposed a severe limitation on women's musical expression, deemed "silly" given women's long history with stringed instruments. "Once upon a time, musical instruments were divided into two groups, those appropriate for women to play, and everything else." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hbNJQ6OYmd0
**Historical Shifts in Cultural Dominance of Music Genres** : The 20th century saw jazz dominate its first half, followed by rock and roll taking over in the 1950s and leading popular music for 50-60 years. Now, rock has been eclipsed by hip hop, which currently serves as the driving force in popular culture, although this does not imply the death of jazz or rock. "The first half of the 20th century was dominated by jazz in all its forms. That was the music that pushed Western culture forward. But then in the 1950s, it was pushed aside by this new thing called rock and roll." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Historical Underpricing of Concert Tickets** : Managers and promoters eventually recognized that concert tickets had historically been undervalued compared to other forms of entertainment, such as sporting events or theater, leading them to adjust prices upwards. "Second, managers and promoters finally figured out that concert tickets have long been a bargain when it comes to entertainment." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Historical Uses of Tattoos** : Historically, tattoos have served various identification purposes, including marking prisoners and slaves. They also functioned to display religious connections and associations with groups such as armies, navies, bikers, and criminal gangs. "Tattoos have also been used to identify prisoners and slaves to display religious connections and associations with armies, navies, bikers, and criminal gangs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jZeMmPekKMQ
**History of Alternative Music Re-examination** : A project was undertaken in the late 1990s to recount the entire history of alternative music. This periodic re-examination is deemed important because "history is a moving target," with new details and interpretations constantly emerging, altering understanding of what was once cool, weird, or inconsequential. "We're going to recount the entire history of alternative music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6oUkMOkEr9U
**History of Controversial Lyrics** : The battle over "unacceptable lyrics" is a decades-old issue, not a modern phenomenon. From the gramophone and phonograph's rise around 1900 facilitating "dirty lyrics," to suggestive early blues and jazz in the 1920s, and the "dirty blues" records of the 1950s, lyrics have consistently "rub[bed] people the wrong way." "The battle over unacceptable lyrics goes back decades." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**History of Manufactured Pop Stars** : The concept of "manufactured pop stars" is not new, predating World War I. Music publishers, managers, and record labels have long engaged in finding attractive individuals, giving them a significant marketing push, and then profiting from their fabricated stardom. "Music publishers, managers, and record labels have been doing this since before World War I." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**History of Music Festivals** : Music festivals are not a modern creation; people have gathered in fields for centuries to hear music, with some original festivals, like Fiera de la Frequette No La in Italy (started 1450), still occurring today. Other long-standing festivals exist in Germany, England, India, and Latvia. "People have been gathering in fields to hear music for centuries, and in some cases, those original festivals are still happening." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7JP2tjJibTQ
**History of Picture Discs** : The earliest known picture discs emerged in the UK in the early 1920s, followed by Adolf Hitler's releases of his speeches for the Hitler Youth in the 1930s. The first modern picture disc is believed to be a 1969 compilation by the German label Metronome, featuring bands like The Doors and MC5. These became popular collector's items in the late 1970s and early 1980s, sometimes featuring interviews. "The first modern day picture disc was probably a compilation of bands like the Doors and the MC5 released by a German label called Metronome in 1969." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2g4QSREzpw
**Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy References** : Douglas Adams' "Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy" series provided inspiration for Radiohead. The character Marvin the Paranoid Android lent his name to the song "Paranoid Android," and another character, Zaphod Beeblebrox, provided the quote, "okay, computer, I want full manual control now," which is referenced in the album title "Okay Computer." "It comes from Marvin the Paranoid Android, one of the central characters in the Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy series by Douglas Adams." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JUvDp3_RBH0
**Hitler's Underpants (later Orchestral Manoeuvres in the Dark / OMD) – The Most Deliberately Pretentious Rename in Rock** : OMD's precursor was originally called Hitler's Underpants. They renamed themselves to "the most pretentious name they could think of" — Orchestral Manoeuvres in the Dark — acknowledging that it too is dumb, but intentionally so. "Their original name was Hitler's underpants." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Hockey Card as Knowledge Source** : In a song, Gord Downie confessed to "stealing" information from a hockey card, specifically card number 340 from the 1991 NHL Pro Set Hockey Cards, which detailed the story of Bill Barilko. Downie used this to illustrate how his lyrical character would quote the card to appear more knowledgeable and experienced than he was, relating it to the "50-mission cap" metaphor. "I stole this from a hockey card, specifically card number 340 from the 1991 edition of the NHL Pro Set Hockey Cards that details what happened to Bill Barilco." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dnz_Ycd_q7s
**Hockey Figure - Harry Sinden** : Harry Sinden was Gord Downie's godfather and a friend of the family. He achieved renown as the coach of Team Canada during the 1972 Canada-Russia series and later served as both coach and General Manager for the Boston Bruins, influencing Gord's early fandom for the team. "His godfather was Harry Sinden, a friend of the family. Harry would later go on to be the coach of Team Canada and the 72 Canada Russia series and then work as coach in GM with the Boston Bruins." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oMBmdfrA47Q
**Holly Knight - Hit Songwriter** : Holly Knight is a prolific songwriter known for writing "Ragdoll" for Aerosmith, "The Best" for Tina Turner, and "Love is a Battlefield" for Pat Benatar. "She wrote Ragdoll for Erasmith, the best for Tina Turner, and Love is a battlefield for Pat Benatar." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZcF5JMhBj2M
**Hollywood Talent Scouting for Sitcoms (Mid-90s)** : By the mid-1990s, with networks fully invested in the stand-up-to-sitcom format, TV industry representatives, casting agents, talent managers, and TV development executives actively frequented comedy clubs like The Lafactory and The Comedy Store in Hollywood and New York. They would attend shows two or three nights a week, often watching up to 20 comics per night in their search for the "next big thing." "Casting agents, talent managers, and TV development executives attended shows at places like the Lafactory and the Comedy Store two or three nights a week, and watched up to 20 comics a night." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6O5TCTO-go4
**Home Stereo Catching On (Late 1950s)** : The concept of high-quality home stereo systems with powerful amplifiers and speakers began to gain popularity in the late 1950s. "the idea of a high-quality home stereo with a big amp and powerful speakers didn't really begin to catch on until the late 1950s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Homosexuality Criminalization in Britain** : Before 1967, it was a crime to be a homosexual in Britain, with individuals facing charges and imprisonment under the Sexual Offences Act. Many lives were ruined by this law. Even after decriminalization in 1967, the new conditions only applied to people over the age of 21 in England and Wales. Homosexuality remained a crime in Scotland until 1980 and in Northern Ireland until 1982, and for members of the armed forces in the UK. Post-1967, police arrests for "gross indecency" even tripled, indicating continued fear and persecution. "Until 1967, it was a crime to be a homosexual in Britain." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=P3xjW0A57Wk
**Homosexuality Decriminalization in UK** : Homosexuality was a criminal offense in the United Kingdom until 1967. This historical context made David Bowie's cross-dressing on his 1970 album cover and his public declaration of being gay in 1972 incredibly scandalous and "as radioactive as a lump of plutonium" in 1970s Britain. "See, homosexuality had actually been a crime in the UK until 1967." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0xd0Hf5REg4
**Honesty as the Foundation of Change** : For Hugh Dillon, the fundamental key to overcoming his addiction and achieving real change was developing the capacity for complete honesty, a state he only reached after losing everything else. "if you have the capacity to be honest, you can change." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zBd13KtYlAA
**Hope For The Day** : Hope For The Day is a suicide prevention outreach program that received support from the Warped Tour's philanthropic efforts. "Hope for the day is a suicide prevention outreach program." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jfQaUV6Bq4
**Horror-Core Murders (2009)** : In 2009, Richard McCroskey killed four people in Virginia. His crimes were linked to his deep involvement with "extreme music" and "horror-core" groups, including bands like Dismembered Fetus and Bloodshot, suggesting a thematic connection between his musical tastes and violent acts. "There are the horror-core murders in 2009 in Virginia where a guy named Richard McCrossky killed four people." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Host Bill Grundy's Drinking During Sex Pistols Interview (December 1, 1976)** : During the infamous Sex Pistols interview on the `Today` show on December 1, 1976, the host, Bill Grundy, was also drinking, contributing to the chaotic and scandalous nature of the broadcast. "Didn't help the The Host of the Show, one Bill Grundy, had also been drinking." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SIK2YFSmf9U
**Houdini Estate (2400 Laurel Canyon)** : While the historic mansion used for "Blood Sugar Sex Magik" was rumored to be a former residence of Harry Houdini, the magician definitely lived up the street at an actual location known as the "Houdini Estate" at 2400 Laurel Canyon. "Although he did live up the street at something known as the Houdini Estate at 2400 Laurel Canyon." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TESrEzVHLCc
**Housefly Wing Frequency – Always F, Regardless of Fly Size** : The common housefly flaps its wings approximately 190 times per second. The human ear interprets this frequency as the note F. Because all houseflies, regardless of size, have the same wing-flap rate, all houseflies buzz in the key of F. "The key of F for fly." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**How to Write a Hit Song (Book)** : Rapper Del the Funky Homosapien claimed he used tips from this book to help write the Gorillaz song "Clint Eastwood," purchasing it with a coupon from his mother. "Del the funky homo sapien says he helped write the song using tips from a book called, How to Write a Hit Song, which she bought thanks to a coupon his mom gave him." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iVdTCqpd798
**Hugh Le Caine: Precursor to Modern Electronic Keyboard (1940s)** : Canadian scientist Hugh Le Caine laid the fundamental groundwork for the modern electronic keyboard during the 1940s with his invention, the Electronic Sackbut. His pioneering efforts in electronically generated music and early synthesizer technology set the stage for later well-known inventors like Bob Moog, who gained prominence for their contributions in the 1960s. "So let's give this man this Canadian, some credit for laying the basic groundwork of the modern electronic keyboard." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PNiL63fY1Y8
**Hugh McClellan - Canadian Political Writer** : Hugh McClellan was a Canadian political writer referenced in The Tragically Hip's album "Fully Completely." His works, particularly the concept of "two solitudes," explored the relationship between Quebec and the rest of Canada. "referenced to Canadian political writer Hugh McClellan in his works in two solitudes of Quebec and the rest of Canada" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oMBmdfrA47Q
**Human Brain Memory Capacity** : The average adult human brain possesses a storage capacity of approximately 2.5 petabytes, which equates to 2.5 million gigabytes. This vast capacity stores personal memories, facts, academic knowledge, learned behaviors, muscle memory, and specialized areas for musical memories and lyrics. While some memories remain robust, new experiences can cause others to fade or become corrupted over time. "The average adult brain has a capacity of about 2.5 petabytes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nX8uAFDzDYM
**Human Kindness Day in Washington D.C. (May 10, 1975)** : The fourth annual Human Kindness Day on May 10, 1975, in Washington D.C., attracted 125,000 people. Despite its benevolent title, the event resulted in 600 arrests, 500 robberies, 150 smashed windows, 120 public brawls, 42 looted concession stands, 17 assaults on police officers, and 14 destroyed cars, according to police reports. "May 10th, 1975 was the fourth annual human kindness day." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dVJuITJyNTU
**Human Need to Classify Music** : The eventual adoption of the term "emo" for the genre, despite initial dislike from musicians, stemmed from a fundamental human need to organize and classify music to keep track of various styles and trends. "And since humans need to organize and classify music just to keep track of everything, the name emo began to stick." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=v_am7ifh7Pk
**Human Preference for 120 BPM Tempo** : Across various cultures globally, humans exhibit a consistent preference for music played at a tempo of 120 beats per minute. This tempo is exemplified by popular songs such as Journey's "Don't Stop Believin'," Adele's "Rumor Has It," and Bon Jovi's "It's My Life." "Studies around the world of various cultures point to the fact that humans display a preference for music played at a tempo of 120 beats per minute." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_Pi7JLwAeOI
**Human Preference for 4/4 Time Signature** : Humans generally prefer songs written in 4/4 time, which is characterized by a "one, two, three, four" beat. This time signature is fundamental to many popular genres and is widely recognized as the standard dance beat, rock beat, and disco beat. "We also prefer songs written in 4 or 4 times." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_Pi7JLwAeOI
**Human Speech and Machine Speech Company** : With some of his accumulated wealth, Hugh Everett III founded a company dedicated to researching and understanding the parallels between human and machine speech. "He used some of that cash to found a company that studied the parallels between human speech and machine speech." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QfiRRtsZoYU
**Humpty Dumpty Origin Story** : The nursery rhyme "Humpty Dumpty," first published in 1803, originated as a riddle whose answer is an egg, explaining why Humpty Dumpty is always depicted as such. A story linking the rhyme to the Battle of Colchester in the English Civil War in 1648 was later revealed to be a tourism scam perpetrated by the city of Colchester in 1956. "What we have here is the classic Humpty Dumpty, first published, 1803." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bI6TTA-fn7c
**Hungry Like a Wolf Video (1982)** : The music video for Duran Duran's "Hungry Like a Wolf" (1982) is cited as an example of a captivating music video whose behind-the-scenes story would be fascinating to explore, particularly its jungle setting and crazy narrative. "So imagine if you could go back in the days and sit down with their and their and and talk about the hungry like a wolf video, like what the hell could tell you guys being this jungle and and just going through this crazy, crazy story." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kbFfT0pKchI
**Hydrogen Seven Half-Life Analogy for Genres** : New genres in the rock universe often have an incredibly short half-life, likened to that of Hydrogen Seven (a tiny fraction of a second), indicating their fleeting nature and rapid emergence/disappearance. "More likely though, a new genre will have a half-life shorter than hydrogen seven. And to save you from looking that up, that's a tiny, tiny, tiny fraction of a second, a decimal point followed by 23 zeros." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tdTs-4Irv8c
**Hypnosis Design Group – Pink Floyd Dark Side of the Moon, Led Zeppelin, Genesis; Storm Thorgerson's Repeating Motif** : Hypnosis was a British design company that became famous for album artwork including Pink Floyd's Dark Side of the Moon (the prism), Led Zeppelin's Houses of the Holy, Presence, and In Through the Out Door, plus Genesis, Procol Harum, Paul McCartney, and many others. One of the three principals was Storm Thorgerson, who created the Dark Side of the Moon cover (widely considered the greatest piece of album artwork ever). When the company broke up in 1983, Thorgerson went solo and designed artwork for everyone from Anthrax to the Offspring to Ween. His signature "repeating image motif" — a beach with a silhouetted door leading through the ground into an alternate universe — appeared on Ian Thornley's Come Again album (2004). "Storm was the guy who came up with the Dark Side of the Moon cover, which many people still consider to be the greatest piece of album artwork ever." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JdSUHkXM1mQ
**Hypothalamus and Physiological Responses to Music** : The hypothalamus, a brain region that regulates essential bodily functions such as thirst, hunger, sleep, heart rate, body temperature, and mood, reacts directly to music. Playing relaxing music causes the hypothalamus to produce hormones that reduce heart rate and blood pressure, while high-energy music triggers the opposite effect, increasing these rates and inducing a burst of energy. "If you play some relaxing music, the hypothalamus reacts by producing essential hormones that produce heart rate and blood pressure." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**I Don't Want To Live In Your World / Plastic Pearls (c. 1998)** : This song originated from a Napster-era download, with incorrect or incomplete metadata. It was theorized to be a New Order demo, a claim which was debunked. The lyrics suggest possible titles like "I don't want to live in your world" or "plastic pearls," with a recording date estimated around 1998. "The lyrics may indicate that the track is called, I don't want to live in your world, or it may be called plastic pearls." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UsPoPUByjYw
**IBM 1401 Singing Computer (1964–1971) – Icelandic Engineer Made Machine Sing by Programming Its Memory; Funeral Given on Decommissioning** : Gunnar Jóhannsson, a musician and computer engineer who operated Iceland's first computer (an IBM 1401 data processing unit from 1964), discovered that by programming the machine's memory in specific ways, its strong electromagnetic emissions could be picked up by a nearby radio receiver and translated into notes and melodies, which he then recorded on tape. When the machine was returned in 1971, it was given a funeral at which it "sang" one last time. In 2006, Gunnar's son Jóhann Jóhannsson (an Icelandic avant-garde composer) took these recordings and rearranged them into an album called *IBM 1401: A User's Manual*, described as "hauntingly beautiful." "When the machine was returned in 1971, it was actually given a funeral where it got to sing one last time." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Dt7mb4yyuWM
**IBM 704's Synthesized Human Speech (1961)** : In 1961, Max Matthews and four engineers at Bell Labs advanced computer capabilities by programming an IBM 704 mainframe to sing using synthesized human speech. This impressive feat captivated sci-fi author Arthur C. Clarke, who, with Stanley Kubrick, incorporated the computer's rendition of "Daisy Bell" into the "Hal 9000" computer in their 1968 film *2001: A Space Odyssey*. "1961, Matthews and four other engineers got an IBM 704 mainframe. Not to just play a song, but to sing using synthesized human speech." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**IMS Program and "Hello World" (N/A)** : The IMS program, developed by engineers at the University of Melaga in Spain, was highly capable of analyzing existing music and proposing new compositions. It created "Hello World," a classical composition for clarinet, violin, and piano, which holds the distinction of being the first piece of music entirely conceived and notated by a computer without any human intervention. "IMS composed Hello World, the first piece of music completely created and notated without any kind of human intervention." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**IRA and Northern Ireland** : The Irish Republican Army (IRA) waged a war in the UK, pursuing nationalist aims that sought to inflict serious damage on the ruling political and capitalist system. These goals sometimes shared sympathies with the anti-establishment ethos of punk bands, leading to the IRA even contacting Crass to offer support and reassurance after their hoax. "And there were nationalist organizations like the IRA and Northern Ireland, Waging War in the UK." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YeNWDtN_sHE
**IRC and Early MP3 Sharing** : Before Napster, people discovered they could exchange MP3 files online using Internet Relay Chat (IRC). While awkward and often plagued by incomplete files and requiring significant computer knowledge, IRC represented an early, albeit inefficient, precursor to the widespread illegal file sharing that would soon follow. "People discovered that they could exchange these MP3 files online, especially using something called IRC." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NAroxFbYkRw
**ISRC Number Specificity and Regional Variations** : ISRC numbers are highly specific, with each unique release, remix, live recording, or cover version requiring its own number, further compounded by the need for distinct codes across different geographical territories like Canada, the US, UK, and France. "Each release requires a new ISRC number." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**ISRC System Complexity (Post-1986)** : Since 1986, efforts have been made to assign an ISRC number to every audio recording, but this process is significantly more complex than it appears due to the multitude of versions and releases. "Since 1986, there have been efforts to assign a number to each and every audio recording out there." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Ian Anderson of Jethro Tull – Invested Early in Salmon Fish Farms; Made Significant Money** : Ian Anderson, frontman for Jethro Tull, made considerable money from early investments in salmon fish farms. "Ian Anderson, frontman for Jethro Tull, has made quite a bit of money from some early investments in salmon fish farms." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Iconic Instruments of Rock** : This discussion focuses on the most iconic musical instruments in the history of rock, exploring the "goats of the tools of rock and roll." These instruments are legendary for their sound, playability, and their adoption by famous musicians, becoming part of their signature image. "This is a look at the most iconic instruments. These are the goats of the tools of rock and roll." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Y0HW8LQKvJw
**Iggy's Valium Incident with Dogs** : During his period of mental instability and drug use, Iggy Pop once thought it was a good idea to share a stash of Valium with a friend's dogs. Fortunately, the dogs survived the incident. "He once thought it was a good idea to share a stash of value and with a couple of dogs that belonged to a friend. But don't worry, the dogs survived." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=w-rihv544I8
**Illuminati Conspiracy Theory** : The Illuminati, an alleged secret society, is frequently mentioned in various conspiracy theories, including those involving celebrity abductions and satanic sacrifices. "the illuminati" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pmKjZzypzvg
**Imagine Dragons – An Anagram; Band Won't Reveal the Original Words** : The band has confirmed it is an anagram but has refused to say what words were scrambled to create "Imagine Dragons." They have hinted that apostrophes are required in the source phrase. Online generators suggest possibilities including "adoring images," "a roaming design," and "radioman egg sin." "They're still not saying." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Impact House – Residential Drug Treatment Facility** : Impact House is identified as a residential drug treatment facility where Scott Wyland was transferred for a supposed six-month stay after a week in LA County Jail in April 1996. It later reported Wyland for non-compliance, leading to further probation violations. "From there, he was transferred to the medical unit of the LA County jail. After a week, he was transferred to Impact House, a residential drug treatment facility for what was supposed to be a six-month stay." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TDwagoyMvck
**Impact of "Battle of Britpop" Singles Sales** : In the week of August 15, 1995, the two singles from Blur and Oasis ("Country House" and "Roll With It") collectively accounted for "40% of all the record sold in the UK that week," demonstrating their immense cultural and commercial impact. "those two singles accounted for 40% of all the record sold in the UK that week." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DSvoppf1dGY
**Impact of British Economic Conditions on Music** : The bleak economic situation in Britain during the late 1970s, characterized by high unemployment, strikes, and a struggling economy, profoundly influenced the mood of young people and the type of music they sought, leading some to embrace darker themes of doom and death, such as Gothic music. "The tough economic times facing the empire caused some young people to seek out music that reflected their bleak mood." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9uTORLOm5Yg
**Impact of Cancon on Industry Development** : The legal quotas imposed by Cancon rules led to the creation of a full-fledged domestic music industry. This included access to recording studios, agents, managers, promoters, venues, and distribution networks, all essential elements that had been largely missing previously. "Because there was now a legal quota to fill, an industry was built to fill those quotas." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6PGgNCaW4xo
**Impact of Data on Video Production (Online Era)** : Analysis of online music video data revealed that creators needed to "hit hard with something in the opening seconds of the video," eliminating the time for cinematic build-ups. This led to an increased emphasis on strong visual hooks and a stratification of online videos into visualizers, lyric videos, and official videos, often incorporating original footage, public domain sources, or static images with audio. "You had to hit hard with something in the opening seconds of the video. There was no time for any sort of cinematic build up." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Impact of Early 1970s Oil Crisis on Vinyl Quality** : The oil crisis of the early 1970s led to increased costs for all petroleum products, prompting record labels and pressing plants in the late 1970s and early 1980s to use more recycled and thinner vinyl. This resulted in shallower grooves that stored less sonic information (especially bass) and made records more susceptible to damage, contributing to the public's swift adoption of CDs in the early 1980s. "As a result of the oil crisis of the early 1970s, all petroleum products got more expensive." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IIJySSo276k
**Impact of European Festivals on North America** : The festival culture of the alt-rock nation in North America was heavily influenced by the expertise and experience gained from large European events, with English organizers being particularly skilled in this area. "The festival culture of the alt-rock nation in North America wouldn't have taken root without the expertise and experience gleaned from the big European events." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7JP2tjJibTQ
**Impact of File Sharing (Napster Era)** : The music industry's attempt to eliminate CD singles and compel full album purchases contributed significantly to the rise of file-sharing programs like Napster. Consumers, unable to easily acquire individual songs legally, turned to digital platforms for single-track acquisition. "And that was also a very big reason people jumped into file sharing when programs like Napster came along." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**Impact of Illegal Downloading on Music Industry** : Illegal downloading of music directly reduces artists' income, particularly affecting niche artists for whom "every penny means something." This loss of revenue forces bands, even successful ones like U2, The Rolling Stones, or Oasis, to seek alternative income streams, leading to higher concert ticket prices, expensive band merchandise, and increased instances of bands licensing their songs for TV commercials. "Every free illegal download is money out of an artist's pocket." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yl2poVWguu0
**Impact of Internet on Rock Star Deaths** : In the modern era, the internet's speed has dramatically amplified the spread of gossipy stories, conspiracy theories, and disinformation surrounding the deaths of rock stars, making their legacies even more complicated than in previous eras. "Gossipy stories have always been front and center when things involved the death of rock stars, and now with the internet conspiracy theories and disinformation travel faster than the speed of light." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Impact of MP3s on Dopamine Production (Theory 1)** : One theory suggests that when listening to an MP3, different or fewer neurons fire in the brain compared to uncompressed digital or analog sound. This weaker neural connection between sound reception centers and emotional centers (amygdala, nucleus accumbens, cerebellum, responsible for dopamine secretion) results in less dopamine production. Consequently, the music makes the listener feel less good. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHN6db7m3DM
**Impact of Mastering on Sound Quality** : The mastering process is crucial for both vinyl and CD, requiring specific adjustments to match the characteristics and abilities of each format. An album mastered for one format and "shoe-horned" into another (e.g., a vinyl master directly to CD, or vice versa) will result in suboptimal sound quality, irrespective of the format itself. "If you do not mix and master specifically to vinyl, and specifically for CD, you are going to get music that doesn't sound as good as it should, regardless of the format." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**Impact of Music Lessons on Future Income** : Various research studies indicate a correlation between taking music lessons during childhood and earning a higher income later in life, regardless of the career chosen. The skills and discipline acquired through learning music are believed to be transferable to other aspects of life, including professional development and career building. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHN6db7m3DM
**Impact of Napster and Digital Files on Music Industry** : The advent of Napster and the ease of digital file sharing fundamentally changed the music industry, leading to a situation where record companies felt they were "giving away masters." This shift prompted industry executives to seek "exit plans" as the traditional model of selling physical "pieces of plastic" (CDs) became obsolete. "We're giving away masters, CDs is giving away masters and then Napster and all that started to happen. He goes, I'm looking for my ex a plan." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=W9gdKha0PLg
**Impact of On-Hold Music on Customer Interactions** : On-hold music revolutionized customer interactions by assuring callers they remain connected and preventing hang-ups. Studies suggest that at least 70% of customers subjected to silence while on hold will disconnect within 60 seconds, highlighting that any music, even "bad music," is preferable to dead silence. "Playing the music, even bad music, is way better than the occasional beep or dead silence." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D8GU8ZpSgY8
**Impact of Rocky Mountains on West Coast Touring** : The presence of the Rocky Mountains made touring North-South along the West Coast significantly easier and more affordable for bands compared to East-West routes. This geographical barrier created a semi-distinct ecosystem for West Coast bands, influencing how hardcore punk and other rock genres permeated the region from San Diego to Vancouver. "You can blame the Rocky Mountains. It takes time to cross the range and there aren't that many places to play between the western and eastern slopes. So that means for a certain level of band, you inhabit a semi distinct ecosystem." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PEHkKfmmDiI
**Impact of Skips on Streaming Algorithms** : Streaming music algorithms actively monitor song skips, and a higher skip rate causes a song to be demoted in recommendations and reduces its likelihood of appearing on prominent playlists. "The more people skip a song, the further down the song slides when it comes to recommendations." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Impact of Technology on Playlists (Late 1990s/Early 2000s)** : As listeners gained control over their music with iPods, MP3s, torrents, and eventually streaming, commercial alt-rock radio responded by "tighten[ing] their playlists more and more each year" to maintain ratings and revenues, reinforced by nationally and internationally published charts. "And as listeners empowered themselves with iPods and MP3s and torrents and eventually streaming, millions tightened their playlists more and more each year to keep those ratings and revenues up." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Impact of Too Much Music on Attention Spans** : The overwhelming availability of over 110 million songs on streaming services contributes to shortened attention spans and increased anxiety among listeners, leading to quick "skipping" if a song isn't immediately engaging. This constant search for "something better out there" often results in music becoming "organized noise" lacking context and deeper meaning. "There's just too much music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5Y6nHw5xx88
**Impact of Unmet Rider Requests** : If a promoter fails to meet the artist's requests specified in the rider, it can lead to a "pouty, petulant, and otherwise pissed off performance," as artists may feel disrespected if their needs are ignored. "They mess up the rider and the promoter runs the risk of having a pouty, petulant, and otherwise pissed off performance." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=z2hvUDtBxho
**Impact of the Compact Disc on Artwork** : The advent of the compact disc reduced the available canvas for album art from 12x12 inches to approximately 5x5 inches. Designers adapted by creating CD booklets with multiple pages, allowing for more detailed content, although the smaller front image made it harder to appreciate subtleties. "Then along came the compact disc, reducing the palette art is set to work with from 12 inches by 12 inches to the size of a CD case, which is about 5 by 5 inches." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pwebM3TfSdg
**Implementation of SoundScan (March 1, 1991)** : A new, significantly more accurate system for tracking album sales, named SoundScan, became operational on March 1, 1991. This system moved away from verbal reports and instead counted album sales precisely as barcodes were scanned at checkout, providing a much clearer picture of music consumption. "But then on March 1, 1991, a new system came online. It was called sound scan. And instead of relying on these verbal sales reports, album sales were counted as the barcode was scanned at the checkout." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pE7TaWSUa-o
**Importance of Good Label Relationships** : Trent Reznor expressed a desire to work with record company people who genuinely love music and possess knowledge of the industry, rather than solely focusing on profit. He cited Daniel Miller and people at Sire as examples of "cool record company people" who are in it for the music, contrasting them with the experience of TVT Records. "I'm convinced there are good people, somewhere, you know, I would think Daniel Miller was probably a good, and there's people at Sire, you know, that are a cool record company people that seem to have some knowledge of what's going on, and seem to be in it because they like music, not because you know how much money I can make if I, you know, I mean, it's, they like music, you know, which is why I make music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=M3nUQtja0qc
**Importance of Metadata in Streaming** : The accurate and consistent identification of songs for streaming payouts relies heavily on metadata, which is embedded information within digital music files. "This is where we enter the universe of metadata." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Importance of Music to Personal and Emotional Development** : Music plays a crucial role in personal and emotional development, especially during the "sweet spot" from high school to the early 20s. During these "musical coming of age years," individuals are malleable, confused, and curious, using music to understand themselves intellectually and emotionally, and to create and project their identity to the world. "Music is very important to our personal and emotional development." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m7MbV-eCLyE
**Importance of Radio Advertising for Concerts** : To ensure shows were well-attended, extensive advertising on local AM and FM radio stations was crucial. Spreading the word about concerts and tours was key to attracting larger audiences. "So it was very important to get the word out on these concerts and these tours by advertising very, very heavily on local AM and FM radio stations." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OCkov6FTliE
**Importance of Rhythm Sections** : The rhythm section, typically comprised of the drummer and bass player, forms the fundamental "heartbeat, pace, bounce, slinkiness, foundation for the melody and the lead instruments" of any band. They are essential for a good groove and for making audiences want to dance. "These people form the foundation of any band's sound, the bass and the beat." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CVPPymc_qrk
**Importance of Slowing Tempo in Covers** : Sometimes, slowing down the tempo of a song can make a cover version even better than the original, creating a new mood and demeanor that enhances the composition. "Sometimes to make a song even better than the original, you just gotta slow things down a bit." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yuA9Xor8vfw
**Importance of Zines** : Zines, homemade magazines created on photocopiers and often distributed for free, were crucial to the do-it-yourself (DIY) scene and played a significant role in the American Pacific Northwest, where the zine culture was particularly "well organized and sophisticated," leading to the dissemination of proto-Riot Grrrl manifestos. This grassroots media was vital for communication and organization. "Even more important were the Zines. These were homemade magazines, created on things like photocopiers, and distributed to like-minded people often for free." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qss5kC2FUx0
**Improved Health and Safety Standards** : In earlier times, getting a tattoo was considered "a bit dodgy" due to significant health risks like infection or gangrene. However, advancements in techniques, equipment, inks, and health and safety standards have made the process much safer and more acceptable over time. "Back in the day, it was a bit dodgy getting one because it was the quickest way to get an infection at the best or gangrene at the worst. But I guess, you know, new techniques, new equipment, new inks, new health and safety standards, all these things conspired to help make tattoos more and more acceptable, I guess." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jZeMmPekKMQ
**Improvements in Concert and Festival Safety** : Concerts and festivals have become considerably safer due to significant advancements in crowd control, security, fire safety, and emergency response. Some festivals also proactively implement measures to ensure that harmful drugs are not sold on site, though challenges, as seen with Astroworld, persist. "Concerts and festivals are generally much, much safer than they used to be." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pgchw2h87aw
**In-Dash MP3 Car Players** : Dedicated in-dash MP3 players for automobiles, such as the MP3 car and the Rio car, were developed to integrate digital music playback directly into vehicle entertainment systems. "And for the car, there was the MP3 car and the Rio car, both in-dash MP3 players." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**InXS, the Death of Michael Hutchence (1999) – Documentary** : An Australian TV documentary from 1999 that significantly amplified the story and various theories surrounding Michael Hutchence's death. "Plus, there was an Australian TV documentary in 1999 called Inaccess, the death of Michael Hutchins that blew up the story even further." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Increased Competition for New Artists** : Current artists face immense difficulty breaking through and achieving superstardom in the 21st century due to increased competition from "the entire musical history of the human race" available via streaming, coupled with the inability to achieve the kind of public consensus about music that existed in the pre-streaming era. "Current acts now have to compete with the entire musical history of the human race." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Increased Injuries and Liability Issues (1980s-1990s)** : As moshing became standard, injuries intensified, including groping and sexual assault, and instances of attendees bringing weapons like brass knuckles into the pit. This led to venues, bands, and promoters facing lawsuits, liability issues, and rising insurance premiums, compelling them to discourage moshing. "Venues did what they could to discourage mashing because people were getting hurts or worse. There were lawsuits and issues of liability." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KMx4bmq8o2Y
**Increased Musical Commonality Across Generations** : Unlike in the past when kids avoided their parents' music, contemporary generations of parents and children share much more common musical taste, particularly within the rock genre. "In the old days, kids wouldn't have anything to do with their parents music. Today though, parents and their kids have much more in common musically than ever before, especially on the rock side of things." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Increased Noise Sensitivity Post-Pandemic** : Studies indicate that individuals who experienced pandemic-related lockdowns have developed a heightened sensitivity to noise. One particular study found that 43% of participants reported they could no longer tolerate loud music to the same degree as they could prior to the pandemic. "43% of the people in one study say that they just can't tolerate loud music the way they used to." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**Increased Production Costs for Elaborate Tours** : Contemporary concert tours require extensive budgets for elaborate staging, special effects, video screens, high-quality sound, costume changes, and dancers to meet elevated fan expectations. These enhanced production values significantly contribute to higher ticket prices. "Fourth, to make it worthwhile, artists have had to hit the road with a show that features wild staging, crazy special effects, video screens, high quality sound, costume changes, dancers and more." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
