**Increased Representation of Gay Characters Post-Ellen** : Following Ellen DeGeneres' groundbreaking coming out on her sitcom, gay characters and actors on shows such as *Six-Feet Under*, *Modern Family*, and *Pose* became a regular and accepted part of the television landscape. "Since Ellen ended, gay characters and actors on shows like Six-Feet Under, Modern Family, and Pose have become a regular part of the TV landscape." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6O5TCTO-go4
**Increased Revenue from Streaming Subscribers** : Streaming has converted passive consumers (those who previously paid nothing or bought only a few CDs annually) into consistent payers. A person who might have spent $60-$90 on CDs now pays $120+ annually for streaming, multiplied by millions, leading to significantly higher overall industry revenue. "What used to be the person, you know, in the hundreds of millions that never paid is now paying something. Even the person that, that, you know, would buy six or seven CDs a year for call it, you know, somewhere between 60 and 90 bucks is now paying 120 bucks, right? So, you know, and that's by the millions as opposed to by the tens of thousands, hundreds of thousands." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Increased Visibility of Older Songs in Popular Culture** : The massive investment in catalog acquisition means there will be an intentional effort to extend the lifetime of these songs, leading to their increased presence in popular culture through sync placements in movies, TV, commercials, video games, as well as samples, covers, and interpolations. "Meanwhile, keep an eye and ear out for more and more older songs to show up in popular culture. Love A-X, TV. Commercials, video games. Samples, covers, interpolations. A lot of investment. Billions and billions of dollars has to be made back. Which means the lifetime of these songs has to be extended as long as possible." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Independent Labels and the 45 RPM Single** : Independent labels, particularly those promoting Rock and Roll, favored the 45 RPM single because it was cheap to produce and could generate millions of dollars for a hit song. "Meanwhile, independent labels, the labels pushing Rock and Roll, loved the 45. It was cheap to make, and a hit song could generate millions of dollars." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Independent Record Labels (Indies)** : Independent labels operate separately from the major record companies. They are standalone businesses not controlled by the three big majors, and there are hundreds, possibly thousands, of such labels worldwide. "Indie labels operate independently from the major labels. They are standalone companies that are not under the control of the big three." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Independent Record Labels - Definition** : An independent (indie) label is a company or label completely unaffiliated with a major record label, handling all its business and distribution dealings independently. This can mean operating as a self-contained third-party company or an artist forming their own label, offering total creative control but also demanding full responsibility for risks and hassles. "Indy is short for, of course, independent. It's another way of saying that a particular artist issues their music through a company or label that is in no way affiliated with a major record label." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ft8nruhWCas
**Independent Rediscovery of Musical Styles** : There is a recurring pattern where specific musical styles are "independently rediscovered over and over and over again by subsequent generations." This explains why bands like Greta Van Fleet can sound fresh to young audiences while evoking older bands like Led Zeppelin for experienced listeners. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m7MbV-eCLyE
**Indiana Governor's Ban of "Louie Louie" (1963)** : The controversy surrounding The Kingsmen's "Louie Louie" escalated when a teenager, allegedly traumatized by the rumored "secret lyrics," contacted the governor of Indiana. Outraged, the governor banned the song from the state, prohibiting radio stations from playing it. This ban ironically increased the song's mystique and helped propel it up national charts. "He was so outraged that he banned the song from the state, which included forbidding radio stations from playing it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Indie Artists Maintaining Ethic with Majors** : The transition of bands like Husker Dew and R.E.M. from indie to major labels, despite initial "whispers of sell-out," proved crucial. By sticking to their creative vision, they demonstrated that artists could record for a major label and still maintain their independent attitude, changing industry perceptions. "I need you to hang on to that thought. REM's rise from fratboy band to intelligent, articulate, and politically aware superstars paralleled the ascent of indie music in America." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sCtQqVBtCaI
**Indie Labels Driving Music Forward** : Despite major labels receiving most of the attention and money, independent labels were frequently responsible for pushing music forward. While outsiders might view them as picking up "the drags" (artists rejected by majors), indie labels were, in fact, "investing in the future" by taking chances on music that no one else would touch, thereby providing an outlet for experimental and underground sounds. "And while the major labels and major label artists got most of the attention and the money, the underclass of indie labels were often responsible for driving music forward." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dDcD8kg0wEk
**Indie Labels as "Minor Leagues"** : Before the internet era, the indie label system often functioned like minor league baseball, allowing artists to mature and develop at that level. Once a group gained enough traction and became "big enough," a major label would typically sign them away, as seen with bands like Husker Dew, R.E.M., Sonic Youth, Soundgarden, and Nirvana. "Back in the days before the internet, the indie label system was kind of like the minor leagues. The small indie labels gave the artist the attention they needed as they matured. Groups developed at that level, and once they got big enough, a major label would swoop in and sign them away." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sCtQqVBtCaI
**Indie Labels as "Saviors of Rock and Roll"** : Amidst the major labels' struggles, a new conventional wisdom emerged: "Major labels bad, indie labels good." Indie labels were seen as non-corporate entities run by individuals who genuinely cared about music, leading many fans to view them as "the saviors of rock and roll," despite this being a somewhat simplistic attitude. "The new conventional wisdom was that indie labels were doing God's work and they were the saviors of rock and roll." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=raRCZ0Y5up8
**Indie Releases as Source for Demos** : Indie albums or cassettes released before a band's major label debut often feature early versions of songs that are later refined, re-recorded, and become significant for the band. These early indie releases can be extremely rare, with very limited production runs, making the originals difficult to acquire. "For account list cases of indie albums or indie cassettes that feature early versions of a song that would later be refined, re-recorded and released to become something big for the band." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zT6pRGYLWhw
**Indie Rock Revolution** : A significant "indie rock revolution" emerged in the very late 1990s, led by a new generation of young people who took it upon themselves to "kickstart things themselves" and inject new life into music. This movement subsequently "exploded" throughout the following decade. "First of all, a new generation of young people decided to take matters into their own hands and kickstart things themselves. We saw this with the indie rock revolution that started taking hold in the very late 1990s and then exploded for the next decade." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7dXtgTkdv9w
**Indie as "Underground"** : Beyond its literal meaning of independent, "Indie" often implies "underground" music, referring to music that intentionally flies below the mainstream's radar. This type of music, often synonymous with the new alternative, is typically found in small clubs and niche record stores and appeals to a more selective, cutting-edge audience. "Indie also implies underground, the kind of music that flies below the mainstream's radar." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ft8nruhWCas
**Indie-Major Distribution Deals** : This refers to a mutually beneficial arrangement where a truly independent company continues to develop music independently, and a major label handles the distribution in exchange for a percentage of the profits. This model enables indie artists to achieve wider reach without sacrificing creative independence. "The other option is to forge a mutually beneficial deal between a major and an honest to god Indie." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ft8nruhWCas
**Indigenous Peoples in Rock Music** : Over the last decade, there have been long overdue attempts at reconciliation with First Nations and Indigenous Peoples of North America, leading to increased learning about their music and contributions to the world of rock. This growing awareness is partly credited to Gord Downie's activism and the Polaris Music Prize in Canada. "The treatment of Indigenous Peoples makes for some really ugly history. But it has also been enlightening in positive ways. For example, we now have been learning more about First Nations music and the role people who identify as Native Canadians and Americans have played in the world of rock." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YFv6Ln00XjA
**Individual Dance Styles** : Research conducted by Finnish scientists has concluded that every individual possesses a distinct and unique style of dancing. The specific way a person moves to music is apparently as unique and identifiable as their fingerprints. "more Finnish researchers concluded that everyone has their own unique style of dancing. How you move to music is apparently as distinctive as your fingerprints." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**Industry Agreement Ends Format War** : After a period of intense competition where the LP and the 45 duked it out (with the 78s gradually fading), an "interesting thing happened" in the early 1950s: "everybody started to get along, and for a totally unexpected reason." This paved the way for a new era in popular culture and divergent paths for vinyl record evolution. "Then an interesting thing happened. Everybody started to get along, and for a totally unexpected reason. On part two of the history of vinyl, we'll pick things up in the early 1950s when something wholly unprecedented emerged in popular culture, and it would take the evolution of vinyl records down completely different paths for a while anyway." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VlIErdSSVOM
**Inefficient Early PA Systems (1970s)** : While significant progress was made in PA systems after early festivals, those developed and used throughout most of the 1970s, though loud, "weren't very efficient." They demanded "a lot of power to drive them" and "still didn't sound very good," offering little realism in their audio delivery. "Big strides, yes. But while this system and the new PA systems that followed were quite loud, they weren't very efficient." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qrrwqdpCjpQ
**Inevitable Musical Duplication** : Due to the finite nature of pleasing musical combinations, "songs are not like snowflakes" and "at some point, there's going to be duplication." This duplication may be intentional or unintentional. "In other words, songs are not like snowflakes. At some point, there's going to be duplication." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rfObVld9rIE
**Infamous Rock and Roll Couples – Sid and Nancy** : Sid Vicious and Nancy Spungen are recognized as one of the most infamous couples in rock and roll history, often ranked alongside pairings like John and Yoko, and Kurt and Courtney. Their tumultuous 21-month relationship contributed significantly to their enduring legend. "Rock and roll is filled with stories of infamous couples. There's John and Yoko, there's Curtin Courtney, and right up there in the top three is Sid and Nancy." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OcylgiXkDP8
**Infiltration of Ozzy Osbourne's World** : Christian rock groups managed to "sneak into the mainstream," with some even "infiltrating Aussie Osborne's world," referring to bands like P.O.D. touring with Ozzy. "Some managed to even infiltrate Aussie Osborne's world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g-tBn8dsSqw
**Inflationary Pressures on Industry Costs (Post-COVID)** : General inflation, particularly in the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic, has driven up operating costs for ticket companies and venues, contributing to the overall increase in concert ticket prices. "Seventh, old fashioned inflation, especially in the wake of COVID, the cost of running the companies that sell tickets has gone up, the cost of operating a venue has gone up, in fact, the cost of everything has gone up." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Influence of 1980s Music Videos** : Music videos from the 1980s, during the MTV and MuchMusic era, were highly influential in captivating audiences with their storytelling, dancing, and style. Key artists recognized for their video work include Madonna, Peter Gabriel, Phil Collins, Michael Jackson, Aerosmith, and Duran Duran. "I came up in the 80s era where that's what made me fall in love with music videos, right? The MTV, much music era watching videos by like Madonna and Peter Gabriel and like Phil Collins and Michael Jackson and, uh, and Errol Smith." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pwebM3TfSdg
**Influence of 80s Music Videos** : The 1980s era, particularly with channels like MTV and Much Music, was instrumental in fostering a love for music videos due to their storytelling, dancing, and distinct style. Videos by artists such as Madonna, Peter Gabriel, Phil Collins, Michael Jackson, Aerosmith, and Duran Duran (e.g., "Hungry Like a Wolf") captivated audiences and sparked fascination with the art form. "I came up, I came up in the 80s era where that's what made me fall in love with music videos, right? The MTV, much music era, watching videos by like Madonna and Peter Gabriel and like Phil Collins and Michael Jackson and Errol Smith. And I was always fascinated by music videos and the storytelling and the dancing and the style and all that stuff." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zUQ2vZzmRiI
**Influence of 80s Music Videos on Directors** : The hosts of "Art Context" were inspired to fall in love with music videos by the MTV/MuchMusic era of the 1980s, watching videos from artists like Madonna, Peter Gabriel, Phil Collins, Michael Jackson, and Aerosmith. They were fascinated by the storytelling, dancing, and style of these videos, desiring to learn about the creative journeys of their makers. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Influence of Media on Tattoo Popularity** : The rise in popularity of MTV, VH1, and other video channels played a crucial role in making tattoos mainstream. Viewers saw musicians and bands with tattoos, leading fans to emulate their favorite rock stars by getting inked themselves. "But the culture started to change when I suppose MTV and VH1 and another video channels became popular and you saw these guys and bands were tattoos." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jZeMmPekKMQ
**Influence of Streaming and Parental Music Collections** : Modern young musicians benefit from unprecedented access to the "entirety of the world's music" through streaming platforms. Additionally, many have "music junkie" parents who eagerly shared their record collections, exposing them to a wide range of older music that can shape their initial artistic directions. "Second, today's young musicians have the entirety of the world's music at their fingertips through streaming platforms." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m7MbV-eCLyE
**Influence of The Executioner's Song and In Cold Blood on Lyrics** : The lyrics for the U2 song "Exit" were directly inspired by Norman Mailer's book "The Executioner Song," which recounts the real-life story of Gary Gilmore's execution by firing squad, and Truman Capote's novel "In Cold Blood." "Lyrically, the song draws from a book by Norman Mailer called the Executioner Song, which tells the real-life story of Gary Gilmore, who was executed by firing squad, and also by the Truman Capote novel in Cold Blood." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VR5Fncdc2kQ
**Influence of UFO and Science Fiction on Music** : A strong fascination with UFOs and science fiction can significantly influence artists' creative choices, extending to band names and record label names. Dave Grohl's lifelong interest in UFOs, sparked by reading books like "Above Top Secret," directly led to him naming his record label "Roswell Records" and his band "Foo Fighters." "Well, for a long time, since I was a kid, I'd always been really fascinated with UFOs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_AY5lS-FmlU
**Initial CD Manufacturing Plants (Germany & Japan)** : Two factories were originally established for compact disc production: one run by Philips near Hanover, Germany, and the other by Sony in Japan. These initial plants were crucial for the rollout of the new technology. "Two factories were originally set up. Philips ran one near Hanover, Germany, and the other was run by Sony in Japan." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Initial Inspiration from Female Pioneers** : The Riot Grrrl movement drew initial inspiration from female pioneers of punk and post-punk, including The Slits, The Raincoats, Kim Gordon of Sonic Youth, Siouxsie Sioux ("Suzy Su"), Chrissie Hynde of The Pretenders, and Exene Cervenka of X, with Patti Smith considered the "godmother." These artists paved the way for future generations of women in rock. "Some initial inspiration came from female pioneers of punk and post-punk." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qss5kC2FUx0
**Inner Ears Studios Legacy** : Inner Ears Studios, located in Arlington, Virginia, has been in operation for over three decades. It is historically significant as many of Dave Grohl's musical heroes from the DC hardcore scene made records there. "So the food set up at Inner Ears Studios, a place that's in its third decade. So many of his heroes, so many of the bands he loved as a kid, made records at Inner Ears Studios." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Mh-qg1izM98
**Inside-Out Records – MegaDeath 1993 Sweating Bullets Blue Vinyl; Plays from Center Outward** : In 1993, MegaDeath released their Sweating Bullets single on 12-inch blue vinyl that plays from the inside out — the listener places the tone arm on the innermost grooves and the spiral moves toward the outer edge. This was, to the host's knowledge, the first rock act to try this. A special edition of Camper Van Beethoven's Keyline Pi (2014 reissue) similarly hid the closing theme of side one on innermost grooves playing at 45 RPM while the rest of the record played at 33⅓. "In 1993, MegaDeath released their sweating bullet sing along a slab of 12-inch blue vinyl." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=I291j5WBc4k
**Inspiration for The Orphan Keeper** : The podcast "Stop Rewind, the Lost Boy" is inspired by the book "The Orphan Keeper," written by Cameron Wright. "Stop Rewind, the Lost Boy, is inspired by the book The Orphan Keeper by Cameron Wright." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vFVnhksrFao
**Inspiration from 1980s Music Videos** : Director X was inspired to enter the field of music video direction by the 1980s era, watching videos from artists such as Madonna, Peter Gabriel, Phil Collins, Michael Jackson, and Aerosmith. He was fascinated by the storytelling, dancing, and style of these videos. "I came up in the 80s era where that's what made me fall in love with music videos, right? I was watching music videos by like Madonna and Peter Gabriel and like Phil Collins and Michael Jackson and and Errol Smith." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YFv6Ln00XjA
**Inspiration from Perry Farrell's Performance** : Kevin Lyman's idea for his own tour was sparked by Perry Farrell's performance of "Mountain Song" during the first Lollapalooza road trip in 1991, which Lyman was stage managing. "Jane's performance of this song on that tour. There was just something about Perry Farrell building up Mountain Song that had Kevin Lyman thinking about his own tour." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jfQaUV6Bq4
**Instrument Smashing in Rock** : The practice of bands smashing their instruments at the end of a set dates back to Rockin' Rocky Rockwell on "The Lawrence Welk Show" in 1956, where he smashed his acoustic guitar. The Who, particularly Pete Townsend, popularized this in 1964 after an accidental break led to intentional destruction, making it their trademark. Other notable destroyers include Jeff Beck, Jimi Hendrix, KISS's Paul Stanley, Nirvana's Kurt Cobain, Nine Inch Nails' Trent Reznor, Phoebe Bridgers, and Guinness record holder Matt Bellamy of Muse. "When did it become fashionable for a band to smash their instruments at the end of a set?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**Integrity in Music Production** : Trent Reznor emphasized the importance of integrity in music creation, believing artists should make music they genuinely think is good rather than conforming to corporate trends or label demands. He specifically called out the "ridiculous" notion of labels considering what's "in fashion," like grunge, to dictate music production. "I wouldn't compromise the actual music to fit into some of the ideas of what they think of going on." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=M3nUQtja0qc
**Intellectual Components of Early Goth** : Early Goth music was noted for its intellectual depth, incorporating themes from traditional Gothic horror, existentialist philosophy, romanticism, and nihilism. "There was an intellectual component featuring trappings of traditional Gothic horror, existentialist philosophy, and romanticism, along with a nice dollop of nihilism." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**Interactive Video for "We Used to Wait"** : Chris Milk developed a fascinating interactive video for Arcade Fire's song "We Used to Wait," employing HTML5, which was a relatively new programming language for web applications at the time. This video integrated Google Maps and Google Street View, and was created with the assistance of coders responsible for the Chrome browser, offering a personalized experience when users entered their street address at thewildernessdowntown.com. "Using HTML5, which at the time was a relatively new programming language for web applications, a guy named Chris Milk designed a fascinating interactive video that incorporated Google Maps and Google Street View and developed with the help of some of the coders responsible for the Chrome browser." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ir2FfLQJNtY
**International Nomenclature Rules** : Over decades, international codes have standardized scientific naming rules for all species, from bacteria to whales, and from extinct species hundreds of millions of years old to new discoveries. A key rule allows the discoverer of a new species to name it, provided they follow standard nomenclature, and they can choose to name it after a famous person or group. "One of the rules is that whoever finds a new thing is allowed to give it a name as long as they follow the standard nomenclature rules and should a scientist or whoever so desire, he or she can choose to name their discovery after a famous person or group." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VMPLplhDk8I
**International Standard Recording Code (ISRC)** : The International Standard Recording Code (ISRC) is a method developed to assign a unique identifier number to individual audio recordings, representing one attempt at standardizing music identification. "One method, which is pretty good, is the International Standard Recording Code, or ISRC Number." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Internet Disruption of Music Industry (2007)** : By 2007, the internet had caused significant disruption within the music industry, leading to widespread music piracy and a sharp decline in CD sales. This created a chaotic and uncertain environment where established models for making money from recorded music were no longer viable. "The whole industry was in chaos thanks to the disruption by the internet. Music piracy was rampant, CD sales were falling, no one had any idea of how anyone was going to make money from recorded music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Internet Disruption of Recording Industry (Mid-2000s)** : In the mid-2000s, the entire recording industry faced a significant downturn, described as "melting down," primarily due to rampant music piracy and the broader disruptive impact of the internet on traditional sales and distribution models. "when it looked like the entire recording industry was melting down. Again, thanks to rampant piracy and the disruption brought about by the internet." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vt6DVj7S6nk
**Internet Leaks Era** : The early 2000s are described as "the era of the catastrophic internet leak." This affected Radiohead when their album "Hail to the Thief" was "released into the wild a full 10 weeks before the official release date" as an unmixed and unmastered version, from March until June, with the leakers never being caught. "This was also the era of the catastrophic internet leak." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JUvDp3_RBH0
**Internet Technology Improvements (Summer 1999)** : By the summer of 1999, significant advancements in internet technology had occurred. Computer modems were faster, networks improved, hard drive costs decreased, and web browsers became more user-friendly, setting the stage for more widespread online music activity. "By the summer of 1999 things had improved a lot. Computer modems were better, networks were faster, the cost of hard drives had begun to drop, and browsers were better and easier to use." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Internet and Early Band Websites** : The advent of the internet in the mid-1990s captured the Beastie Boys' attention, and they were among the very first bands to establish a sophisticated and proper website, demonstrating their early adoption of new technologies. "The BC Boy spent a lot of time getting into this new thing called the Internet, and we're in fact one of the first bands to set up a proper sophisticated website." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9_BTe15NooI
**Internet's Disruption of Music Gatekeepers** : The advent of the internet allowed music fans to bypass traditional cultural gatekeepers (labels, radio, stores, magazines, video channels) through a process called "disintermediation," effectively ending "iron fisted control" over music distribution, pricing, and accessibility. "For the first time ever, any music fan could easily bypass these traditional cultural gatekeepers." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Internet's Impact on Bootleg CD Industry** : The advent of the internet led to the sudden collapse of the bootleg CD industry almost overnight. What previously required manufacturing in places like "a backstreet factory in China" could now simply be put online, making physical bootleg production largely obsolete. "Then along came the internet. And the bootleg CD industry suddenly dried up pretty much overnight." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zT6pRGYLWhw
**Internet-Era Music Leaks** : The advent of the internet drastically changed the landscape of music leaks, where a single digital file from a hard drive could instantly put an entire record into the hands of fans, devastating carefully crafted marketing plans and causing substantial lost revenue for artists and labels. "One air at file from a hard drive and suddenly the whole record was in the hands of fans and all those carefully crafted marketing plans were shot to hell." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vt6DVj7S6nk
**Interview Technique - Four Steps to Trust** : A standard four-step process is followed to conduct successful interviews: first, demonstrating propriety (behaving properly); second, competence (showing knowledge as a journalist); third, achieving comfort (the artist relaxes); and finally, establishing trust (the artist shares anything). This process, which has no shortcuts, must be completed quickly within minutes. "The first step is you walk into the room and you have to show propriety. In other words, you are behaving properly. You're not overly familiar. You're not dismissive. You're not rude. You know, okay, any of that sort of stuff. So propriety. That's followed by comfort. If they show you, sorry, propriety, then that's followed by competence... When they get comfortable, they start to trust you. That's step four. And when you get to trust, they will tell you anything." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4xxgPX-aR90
**Intramural Coronary Artery** : An intramural coronary artery is an extremely rare, congenital heart condition where an errant artery grows through the heart muscle. In Joe Strummer's case, this condition silently worsened over time, causing the artery to enlarge and eventually constrict, leading to a massive and unexpected heart attack. "He had something called an intramural coronary artery, a congenital condition that quietly got worse." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Introduction of Canadian Content (Cancon) Rules (January 18, 1971)** : Effective January 18, 1971, Canadian radio stations were mandated to ensure that 30% (three out of every ten) of all songs played were by Canadian artists. This quota system faced initial resistance but became law. "beginning on January 18, 1971, all Canadian radio stations would have to ensure that 30% – three out of every 10 – of all salons they played on their radio station were by Canadian artists." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Introduction of Ecstasy (1985) – Impact on Music** : Ecstasy (methylene dioxianne methyl-emphetamine), a stimulant derived from sassafras oil that also broke down emotional inhibitions, was introduced into the UK and European club scene around 1985. By 1988, it became the substance of choice in clubs and at parties, surpassing even beer. Its widespread popularity fundamentally changed the nature of music over the following years, fostering the rise of raves and shifting musical focus from melody to rhythm, with DJs and the dance floor becoming central to a new youth revolution. "The DJ's beats reign supreme as ecstasy roared through the bloodstream." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Somq2MoRi5Q
**Introduction of Radio (Early 20th Century)** : The radio marked the first convergence of electricity, the vacuum tube (invented 1911), and the speaker (invented 1921). This technology allowed for the amplification of electric signals into sound, paving the way for further advancements in audio. "Electricity, the vacuum tube, and the speaker all first came together with the introduction of the radio." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**Introduction of iPhone (January 9, 2007)** : On January 9, 2007, the iPhone was introduced as a revolutionary device combining three functions: a wide-screen iPod with touch controls, a mobile phone, and a breakthrough internet communications device. While not the first smartphone, its advanced features significantly "upped the game" for mobile technology. "Today, we're introducing three revolutionary products of this class. The first one is a wide-screen iPod with touch controls. The second is a revolutionary moldy thing. And the third is a breakthrough internet communications device." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Introduction of the 33 1/3 Long Playing Vinyl Album (1948)** : The 33 and a third long-playing vinyl album, introduced in 1948, provided art directors with two 12x12 inch canvases (front and back) for artwork. This format was predominantly used for classical recordings, show tunes, and jazz records, with jazz records often featuring the most creative and abstract visual interpretations due to the challenge of visualizing the genre. "In the 33 and a third long playing vinyl album was introduced in 1948. Art directors now had two 12x12 canvases to play with. Front and back." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pwebM3TfSdg
**Introduction of the Alternative Album Grammy (1991)** : The Grammy Awards introduced the "Alternative Album" category in 1991, years after the genre had been established. This marked the first official recognition of alternative music by the Recording Academy. "It wasn't until 1991 that the genre had its own category." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gP3oQeYEvso
**Intuition and Logic Analogy** : An analogy was shared describing how logic and intuition work together: logic involves deducting things and gathering information, which then "informs" or "uploads" capabilities to one's intuition. This process suggests that by paying attention and picking things up, creative "puzzles" will fall into place. "How do you know when to use your intuition and how do you know when to use logic and how it works is that you, when you use logic, you deduct things, you gather information and then that informs your intuition." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=N-3eddy4J7g
**Invention and Introduction of the MP3 (1989)** : MP3 technology originated from the Froenhofer Institute, a German government research group, with Carl Hein's Brendan Berg as the key figure. He started developing the idea in 1977, aiming to send music over phone lines by digitizing and compressing files. By 1989, his team developed the necessary algorithms to shrink music files to one-tenth their original size, releasing the software as shareware for free use. Initially overlooked in Germany, it gained immense traction in Silicon Valley in 1997, leading directly to the creation of Napster and profoundly impacting the music industry and music consumption worldwide. "All this current turmoil in the industry can be traced back to the father of the MP3, Carl Heinz, Brendan Berg." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MTDY6qT11Dc
**Invention of Flat Rotating Disc (Late 19th Century)** : Emil Berliner introduced the flat rotating disc, which significantly advanced recorded music technology. It offered advantages over cylinders, including easier mass production, greater audio capacity (over three minutes per side compared to two), and crucially, two playable sides, enabling over three times the audio of an Edison cylinder. "Everything changed when Emil Berliner came up with the idea of a flat rotating disc. It had several advantages over the cylinder." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**Invention of Recorded Sound (1877)** : Thomas Edison invented the phonograph, initially aiming to preserve the human voice for dictation or as an answering machine for the telephone. His first design in 1877 used a rotating cylinder covered in tin foil, where shouting into a horn moved a needle to cut grooves. Playback involved placing the needle back and turning a crank. The first sounds ever recorded were Edison reciting "Mary had a little lamb." After a decade, he refined the machine, replacing tin foil with wax. This invention fundamentally changed music consumption by allowing it to be enjoyed "wherever and whenever anyone wished." "Without recorded sound, all music would have to be enjoyed as it was being made. A live performance would be the only way to experience music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MTDY6qT11Dc
**Invention of Recording Cylinder (Late 19th Century)** : The recorded music industry began in the late 19th century with recordings on cylinders, invented by Thomas Edison. These cylinders, about the size of a Coke can, could hold approximately two minutes of audio. "When the recorded music industry began in the late 19th century, recordings came on cylinders but the size of a Coke can. It could hold around two minutes of audio and this was the invention of Thomas Edison." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**Inversion of Power in Music Industry** : The power dynamic in the recorded music industry has shifted, with fans now holding all the power and choice, becoming "their own music director" and no longer relying on traditional gatekeepers like radio or record stores. "All the power and all the choice is now in the hands of the fans." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Irish Republican Army vs. British Army Conflict** : The conflict in Northern Ireland during "The Troubles" involved violent clashes between the Irish Republican Army and the British Army, characterized by shootings, nail bombs, and sniper fire. "Shootings, nail bombs, sniper fire, the Irish Republican Army versus the British Army." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cM3-tINKdvo
**Irwin Chisett's Compilations (Songs in the Key of X)** : Irwin Chisett, a music journalist and DJ at WFMU, played a pivotal role in shaping the modern view of outsider music. He championed these artists by featuring their music and releasing a couple of compilation CDs under the title *Songs in the Key of X*. "Chisett loved to feature the music of these artists and even issued a couple of compilation CDs entitled Songs in the Key of X." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HcK4M7-02ik
**Ismail El Jazari's Mechanical Music Machine (1206)** : In 1206, the inventor Ismail El Jazari in Upper Mesopotamia (modern-day Iraq) created a sophisticated mechanical music machine. This device featured four humanoid robots programmed by a complex system of rotating wooden pegs powered by hydraulics, representing an early, purely mechanical attempt at automated music performance without machine learning. "He created a music machine that featured four humanoid robots that could play music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Israeli "Hebrew Con" Initiative** : The Israeli parliament is advocating for a regulation that would require all radio stations to dedicate half of their music playing airtime to songs sung in Hebrew. The primary goal of this initiative is to prevent the "Americanization" of Israeli culture, similar to Canada's efforts to protect its cultural identity. "The Israeli parliament wants all radio stations to devote half their music playing airtime to songs sung in Hebrew." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dMsEj62KFEw
**Israeli Egyptian War of 1973** : The 1973 Israeli-Egyptian War was the geopolitical context for the Arab oil states' decision to limit oil output, leading to the Arab Oil Embargo. "In response to the Israeli Egyptian War of 1973, Arab oil states announced that they were limiting their output." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Issue of "Orphan Songs"** : Hundreds of thousands of "orphan songs" exist because they lack proper metadata, meaning they are unable to earn money they are due, with the associated cash accumulating in accounts while identification efforts proceed. "What we've ended up with is hundreds of thousands of orphan songs orphan because they don't contain the proper metadata." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**It Might Get Loud Documentary (2008)** : This movie is recommended viewing for anyone planning a pilgrimage to Mount Temple Comprehensive School, the formation site of U2. The film features The Edge, Jack White, and Jimmy Page, with The Edge giving a tour through the school. "start by watching the movie It Might Get Loud, which features the edge hanging out with Jack White and Jimmy Page." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sKHdTSfDd6E
**Ivrea Orange Festival, Italy** : For three days leading up to Mardi Gras, the people of Ivrea, Italy, divide into nine teams, dress in battle gear, and throw oranges at each other. The exact origin is unknown but rumored to be linked to defending the town against a tyrant ruler in the 12th or 13th century. This tyrant supposedly assaulted a young woman, who fought back and decapitated him, though the connection to orange throwing is unclear. "For the three days leading up to Mardi Gras, the people of Ivrea, Italy, and this is true, divide themselves into nine different teams. They dress up in battle gear and start throwing oranges at each other." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Ixi Portable Device (1981)** : A patent was filed in the UK in 1981 for a credit-card-sized device named the Ixi (IXI). It was capable of storing 8 megabytes of data, which translated to approximately 3.5 minutes of audio. This device was developed before digital compression technology existed, rendering it largely impractical, and it eventually disappeared from the market. "In 1981, a PAT was filed in the UK for a device called the Ixi, that's IXI. It was about the size of a credit card and could hold a whopping 8 megabytes of data, which was good for about 3.5 minutes of audio. Remember that this was before digital compression was a thing, so although it was a nice start, it was, you know, kind of useless and the Ixi disappeared." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**JFK Assassination Comparison (Post-1994)** : The ongoing questions and conspiracy theories surrounding Kurt Cobain's death have been compared to the enduring mysteries of the JFK assassination. Similar to the 1963 event in Dealey Plaza, there remain "a lot of smoke, a lot of loose sands, and a lot of unanswered questions" in Cobain's case. "It's a new rock version of the JFK assassination." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sKWgbxk46eM
**JFK Assassination Conspiracy Theory** : The theory that JFK's assassination was a conspiracy is mentioned as an example of an "out there" belief. "This is more nutty than even the most out there JFK was assassinated" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pmKjZzypzvg
**JFK Assassination and Conspiracy Theories (November 22, 1963)** : The assassination of President John F. Kennedy on November 22, 1963, remains one of the most famous murders in modern times. More than 60 years later, a significant portion of the population disbelieves the official narrative that Lee Harvey Oswald acted as a lone gunman. The case has been subject to extensive investigation over decades, leading to persistent theories involving entities like the CIA, the deep state, Cuban exiles, and "American monsters." "The most famous murder of modern times was the assassination of JFK on November 22, 1963." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1Jt2fUBDusk
**Jack The Ripper as Subject of Songs** : Songs about the historical serial killer Jack The Ripper are readily available, indicating a long-standing fascination with his unsolved crimes in popular culture and music. "There's also no problem finding songs about John Wayne Gacy, Jack The Ripper, the Unibomber and Richard Ramirez, the Night Stalker." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Jack White Children's Book (2017)** : In 2017, Jack White authored a children's book. The book was based on The White Stripes song "We're Going to Be Friends." "Jack White, wrote a children's book this year based on the white stripes song were going to be friends." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**Jack White's 3 RPM Record – Third Man Label's 3rd Anniversary; Every Blue Series Single** : The slowest-turning record ever made was created by Jack White's Third Man label for the label's 3rd anniversary. The special series of records spun at just 3 RPM and contained every Blue Series single from Third Man. It was a total gimmick, never really designed to be played. White also sold tiny 3-inch records and turntables called the Triple Inch Ophone (2005–2006), and in 2019 collaborated with Crosley to create a working turntable for 3-inch records. "But the slowest turning record ever made was created by Jack White's 3rd Man label." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=I291j5WBc4k
**Jack White's Purchase of "My Happiness" Acetate** : Jack White purchased the one and only copy of Elvis Presley's "My Happiness" acetate in 2015 for $300,000. "Jack White bought the one and only copy in 2015 for $300,000." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IIJySSo276k
**Jack White's Use of St. Vincent's Signature Guitar** : Jack White notably played the Ernie Ball Music Man St. Vincent Signature Model guitar during an appearance on Saturday Night Live. "Jack White played it on the SNL." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oLC49nCf0ZM
**Jacques Cartier - Explorer** : Jacques Cartier was a French explorer who mapped the Gulf of St. Lawrence and established France's claim to what became Canada. His name was famously rhymed by Gord Downie in The Tragically Hip's song "Looking for a Place to Happen" on the album "Fully Completely." "Gord finds a way to rhyme Jacques Carcay." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oMBmdfrA47Q
**Jah Wobble's Prickly Personality Anecdote** : Jah Wobble was known for being prickly, with one story claiming he hated a particular drummer so much that he set him on fire. "There's one story that he hated a particular drummer so much that, well, he set him on fire." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kbFfT0pKchI
**Jamaica as Top Bauxite Source** : Following the large-scale mining operations, Jamaica quickly became recognized as "the world's top source of Boxite." This elevated the country's global economic standing and attracted further foreign investment. "And Jamaica quickly became the world's top source of Boxite." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zNIVqKqAlnk
**Jamaican Independence (1962)** : Jamaica gained independence in 1962. This event coincided with the rise of Ska (SCOP) music, which, being homegrown and celebratory, became a fitting national soundtrack for the newly independent nation. "In 1962, Jamaica became an independent nation, and this new SCOP music was exactly what the country needed." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**Jamaican Mining Industry's Role in Ska History** : The history of ska is deeply intertwined with the Jamaican mining industry. The discovery of bauxite led to significant economic and social changes, including urban migration and poverty, which in turn contributed to the conditions under which ska music coalesced. "And if we're really going to look at the history of ska from the beginning, we have to consider the Jamaican mining industry." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zNIVqKqAlnk
**Jamaican Sound System Culture (Late 1950s)** : As displaced and unemployed Jamaicans moved into expanding ghettos in cities like Kingston, live bands became unaffordable. This led to the development of sound system culture from the late 1950s, where portable DJ setups ("mobile discotheques") were set up in open spaces called "Wands" every weekend. Competing sound system crews played a mix of calypso, soul, American blues, and R&B, which eventually coalesced into Ska. "Jamaicans developed sound system culture. These were portable DJ setups, mobile discodes, if you want, that were set up in open spaces called Wands every weekend." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**James Bond Film Franchise – Overview (1962-Present)** : The James Bond film franchise began with "Dr. No" in 1962 and has since produced 27 films, grossing around $14 billion US dollars at the box office. The process for selecting a theme song involves competitive tenders each time a new chapter is announced, making the commission one of the most prestigious in film music. "There have been 27 Bond films, as I write this, starting with Dr. No in 1962. Box office grosses are now around $14 billion US dollars." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=K0pgJ7XAlpo
**James Bond Theme Origins** : The iconic James Bond theme, commissioned in 1962 for a new movie, was created by composer Monty Norman by adapting his previously written song "Good Sign, Bad Sign" and adding a twangy guitar. "In 1962 a composer named Monty Norman was asked to come up with a theme song for a new movie. He looked at a song he'd written called Good Sign, Bad Sign. Then he added a 20 guitar and submitted it to the producers." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pOeChe_D1Ag
**James Burke's "Connections" Show (Late 1970s)** : The BBC debuted a science education show titled "Connections" in the late 1970s, hosted by James Burke. The program explored disparate developments in science and technology, demonstrating their interconnections and how they led to the modern world, emphasizing that nothing exists in isolation. "Back in the late 1970s, the BBC debuted a science education show called Connections. The host was James Burke, an affable professor-ish guy, usually dressed in a beige polyester leisure suit, who gave the term inter-disciplinary a whole new meaning." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**James T. Russell's Digital Recording Invention (1966)** : In 1966, American inventor James T. Russell devised a method to record digital information, specifically data reduced to patterns of zeros and ones, onto a transparent foil sheet. This foil was illuminated from behind by a halogen lamp, converting the digital patterns into light. "The technology that went into CDs goes as far back as 1966 when an American inventor named James T. Russell figured out a way to record digital information, that is data that had been reduced to patterns of zeros and ones, onto a sheet of transparent foil." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Jan Van Eyck – "The Arnolfini Portrait" (15th century)** : Brian Cannon, the art director behind Oasis's "Definitely Maybe" album cover, cited Jan Van Eyck's 15th-century painting, "The Arnolfini Portrait," as a source of inspiration. Cannon was particularly influenced by the painting's intricate use of visual metaphors in its composition. "Cannon drew inspiration from a 15th century painting by Jan Van Eck, called the Arnoc Fini portrait because of its use of visual metaphors." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Jane Seymour's St Catherine's Court** : Actress Jane Seymour (known as "Dr. Quinn, Medicine Woman") rents out her 14th-century castle, St Catherine's Court in Bath, to bands looking for a "funky sort of place" to record. Radiohead rented it for two months to record part of their third album. The castle boasts "Eight bedrooms, six and a half bathrooms, stables, a tennis court, and even a full church," surrounded by 14 acres of gardens, available for 10,000 pounds a week at the time. "It was actress Jane Seymour's house." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JUvDp3_RBH0
**Jane's Addiction – Named After Perry Farrell's Real Roommate Jane Bainter** : There really was a woman named Jane Bainter who was Perry Farrell's roommate at a shared house on North Wilton Place in Los Angeles in the 1980s. When things went wrong at the house — parties, drugs, alcohol — the trouble was always blamed on "Jane's addiction." The Jane's Addiction logo was designed using Jane Bainter's own handwriting. Jane received no compensation. The song "Jane Says" tells her story: she had a smack habit, a dealer boyfriend named Sergio, and dreams of going to Spain. "How much did Jane get from the band for all her contributions? Nothing. Zero." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Janet Jackson "Rhythm Nation" Crashes Windows XP Laptops** : Playing Janet Jackson's 1989 video "Rhythm Nation" is enough to crash certain Windows XP laptops with 5400 RPM hard drives. The video contains natural resonant frequencies that cause that specific drive model to vibrate so violently it fails — even if the video is playing on a nearby laptop rather than the affected one. "You will find that it features natural resonant frequencies that cause that particular hard drive using that particular operating system to vibrate so violently that it would fail." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**Japan's New Prime Minister Is a Metal Fan Who Played Drums at University** : Japan's new prime minister, Sen-e Takachi, is an avowed metal fan who played drums at university and still loves bands including Black Sabbath, Deep Purple, and Iron Maiden. "Japan's new prime minister is a declared metal fan, played drums at university, and still adores bands like Black Sabbath, Deep Purple, and Iron Maiden." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0DFmS6MPbFI
**Jared Loughner Shootings (2011)** : In 2011, Jared Loughner shot 18 people and killed six at an event for Congresswoman Gabby Giffords (who survived a head wound). Drowning Pool's "Bodies" was his favorite song, for which he created an unofficial YouTube video featuring a burning American flag. "In 2011 Jared Loffner shot 18 people and killed six at an event for Congressman Gabby Giffords." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Jason Blair Plagiarism Scandal (2003)** : Jason Blair, a reporter for The New York Times, was caught plagiarizing in 2003 and subsequently forced to resign in disgrace. This incident serves as an example of how plagiarism can severely damage a professional reputation outside of music. "Just as Jason Blair, he was a reporter for The New York Times and he got caught copying and was forced to resign in disgrace." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L09R2evjyMc
**Jeanine Lindemulder – Blink-182 Album Model** : Jeanine Lindemulder, a former porn star, is the "sexy nurse" featured on the cover of Blink-182's 1999 album "Enema of the State." Her varied career also includes a brief retirement to become a kindergarten teacher, an adult film comeback in 2004 with multiple awards, an appearance in a 2017 documentary about life after porn, a sex tape with Vince Neil of Mötley Crüe, a short marriage to Jesse James, and a six-month jail sentence for tax evasion in 2017. "Well, it's porn star, Jeanine Lindemolder." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Jeff Gutt's X Factor Background** : Jeff Gutt, the current lead singer for Stone Temple Pilots, was a former contestant on the TV talent show _X Factor_. He participated for two seasons, ultimately finishing in second place in 2013. "And get this, a former contestant on the TV talent show X Factor. He was on the show for two seasons, ultimately finishing in 2nd place in 2013." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vLZla5h4xKQ
**Jeffrey Dahmer as Subject of Songs** : Jeffrey Dahmer appears to be the most frequently referenced serial killer in music, with approximately 50 songs written about him. Artists who have covered this subject include Pearl Jam, Slayer, Black Eyed Peas, Katy Perry, Eminem, Dr. Dre, Violent Femmes, and J. Cole, among many others. "Jeffrey Dahmer seems to have the most songs written about him with about 50 Pearl Jam, Slayer, Black Eyed Peas, Katy Perry, Eminem, Dr. Dre, Violent Femmes, J. Cole and many more." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Jeffrey Lims' Thesis on Human-Feel Rhythms (1993)** : In 1993, MIT student Jeffrey Lims authored a thesis exploring how computers could be programmed to generate musical rhythms that possess a more human feel, thereby reducing their robotic quality. His research was foundational in moving computer-generated music toward greater naturalness and expressiveness. "In 1993, an MIT student named Jeffrey Lims wrote a thesis. The topic was, how computers could be programmed to reproduce musical rhythms that had more of a human feel and sounded less robotic." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Jennifer Levin Murder Case (1986)** : On August 26, 1986, 18-year-old Jennifer Levin's body was discovered in Central Park, New York, with signs of severe assault and death by strangulation. Her former boyfriend, Robert Chambers, dubbed the "Preppy Killer," was arrested and claimed the death occurred accidentally during rough sex. This high-profile case inspired songs by Sonic Youth and The Killers. "At 615 in the morning of August 26, 1986, the body of an 18 year old woman named Jennifer Levin was found in Central Park in New York." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Jeremy Dell's Suicide (1991)** : On January 8, 1991, 16-year-old Jeremy Wade Dell, a loner from Richardson, Texas, with divorced parents and struggling in school, committed suicide in front of his English class. After being reprimanded by his teacher, Fay Berrett, for missing classes, he returned to the classroom, said "Miss, I got what I really went for," pulled out a loaded .357 magnum, and shot himself in the mouth. The incident, which happened too fast for anyone to react, received international attention as it was an unprecedented event seven years before Columbine. Jeremy had left suicide notes with classmates, but their contents were never revealed. "At 9.45 in the morning of January 8, 1991, he went to school as usual... Jeremy left suicide notes with several classmates, with the contents of those notes were never revealed." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4PlieAEthSg
**Jerry Bruckheimer and Michael Bay Marketing Strategy (Anecdote)** : An unverified anecdote suggests that producers Jerry Bruckheimer and Michael Bay, known for explosion-heavy movies, capitalized on a specific marketing philosophy in the 1990s. Young audiences would see their films at a multiplex, then immediately visit the mall's record store to buy the soundtrack, a practice that became a huge part of movie marketing. "The story I heard, I can't verify this, but stay with me, was that producers Jerry Brockheimer and Michael Bay, guys who specialized in movies, wear a lot of stuff blew up." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**Jerry Finn's Importance to Blink-182** : Jerry Finn was the longtime producer for Blink-182, considered by many to be the "fourth member" due to his crucial role in shaping the band's sound and extracting optimal performances. His influence spanned back to the 1990s, and he developed strong relationships with everyone associated with the group over a decade. "Jerry had been pretty much the fourth member of Blink 182. He was that important in getting the right sounds and the right performances out of the band, dating all the way back to the 90s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q-QP7ePxkQE
**Jerry Finn's Tragic Death (2008)** : In July 2008, shortly after completing work on a Morrissey album, producer Jerry Finn suffered a cerebral hemorrhage followed by a massive heart attack, which ultimately proved fatal. He was taken off life support and died on August 21st at the age of 39. His death deeply affected all members of Blink-182, contributing to a reassessment of their own lives and relationships. "He was taken off life support and he died on August 21st at the age of 39." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q-QP7ePxkQE
**Jesse James – Jeanine Lindemulder's Ex-Husband** : Jeanine Lindemulder, known for her appearance on Blink-182's "Enema of the State" cover, was briefly married to Jesse James. James is widely recognized as the West Coast Choppers TV personality and was previously married to actress Sandra Bullock. "She was briefly married to Jesse James, the West Coast chopper's TV guy and one time husband of actor Sandra Bullock." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Jet Lag and Gord Downie's Cancer Announcement (May 24, 2016)** : On Tuesday, May 24, 2016, upon landing from a 14-hour flight from Hong Kong, the individual received a flood of emails and texts announcing that The Tragically Hip's singer, Gord Downie, had been diagnosed with brain cancer. The initial shock, compounded by jet lag, led to questions of whether it was a hoax, given Downie's near-superhero status in Canada, but the continuous stream of messages confirmed the truth. "It was Tuesday, May 24, 2016. ... Emails and texts. All about one thing. The tragically hip had just announced that their singer Gord Downey had brain cancer." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dnz_Ycd_q7s
**Jet Magazine (1952) on "Dirty Blues"** : In 1952, *Jet* magazine reported an unprecedented number of "dirty blues records" available, often without any attempt at concealment. The magazine stated that these songs used "cunning inflections" and specific vocabulary like "rock and roll," "writer," "grind," and "grass" to deliver listeners "directly to a bedroom and roughly describe sex activity." "Never have there been as many dirty blues records on sale as today." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Jim Morrison's "Touch Me" – Originally Called "Hit Me" Until Friends Warned Him** : The song "Touch Me" by The Doors was originally called "Hit Me." Friends warned Jim Morrison that people might take him up on that offer with a literal punch, so the title was changed. "People warned Jim Morrison that people might actually take him up on that offer." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**Jim Russell's Optical Recording and Playback Patent (1970)** : Jim Russell, motivated by his frustration with the wear and tear on vinyl records, developed and patented the first optical recording and playback device in 1970. This invention, based on the principle of digitizing music and playing it back without any physical contact, is recognized as the foundational technology for the compact disk. "By 1970, he had it all figured out and received the first patent for an optical recording and playback device." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PNiL63fY1Y8
**Jim Steinman - Songwriter/Producer** : Known for his work on Meat Loaf's "Bat Out of Hell" and Bonnie Tyler's "Total Eclipse of the Heart," Steinman collaborated with The Sisters of Mercy in the late 1980s, cross-pollinating musical styles. "That was most evident in the late 1980s when they teamed up with songwriter Jim Steinman, the guy behind Meatloaf's Battle of Hell." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XaLsZFLrMnI
**Jim Steinman's Final Project – Batman Musical** : When Jim Steinman (Meat Loaf's primary songwriting partner) died in 2022, he had been working on a musical based on Batman. Demos from the project were still on his website. "Last time I checked, some of the demos are still on his website." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**Jimi Hendrix Wrote "The Wind Cries Mary" After a Fight Over Lumpy Mashed Potatoes** : Jimi Hendrix wrote "The Wind Cries Mary" when he felt bad after getting into a fight with his girlfriend because she served him lumpy mashed potatoes. "He felt bad getting into a fight with his girlfriend when she served him some lumpy mashed potatoes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Jimmy Eat World – Crayon Drawing by a Kid Named Ed of His Brother Jim Eating the World** : Guitarist Tom Linton had two younger brothers, Jim and Ed, who fought constantly. After one epic battle, Ed drew a picture of Jim shoving the Earth into his mouth, captioned "Jimmy Eat World." "They fought all the time." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Joanna Stingray and the "Red Wave" LP (1986)** : In 1986, American tourist Joanna Stingray smuggled numerous recordings from Leningrad, primarily from the local rock club, and released them as a double LP called "Red Wave." This compilation featured four underground bands from the USSR. Her actions alarmed both the KGB and FBI, who suspected her of working for the opposing intelligence agency, particularly as she frequently filmed local rock bands in Leningrad. "In 1986, an American tourist named Joanna Stingray smuggled a bunch of recordings from Lennon Gread, mostly from this club, and released them on a double LP called Red Wave for underground bands from the USSR in 1986." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YeNWDtN_sHE
**Joey Ramone's OCD Rituals** : Joey Ramone's lifelong struggle with OCD, diagnosed at 18, manifested in specific and compulsive behaviors. These included having to step on and off a curb a predetermined number of times before he could proceed, and following a strict ritual every time he left his apartment, making these everyday activities a struggle. "For example, he sometimes had to step on and off a curb a prescribed number of times before he could move on." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=j8uk7BnbXwU
**John Cusack Movie Reference** : The date of December 21st, 2012, and its associated doomsday predictions were notably featured in a "bad John Qsack movie." This reference highlights the pop culture impact of the Mayan calendar predictions. "Okay so maybe we'll get another bad John Qsack movie on the subject which is not good and the prospect is admittedly frightening but it's just a movie." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**John D. Luerssen - Biographer** : John D. Luerssen is a biographer who has written books about Nirvana, Weezer, The Smiths, Bruce Springsteen, and U2, highlighting a connection between Nirvana and Weezer. "But John D. Lurison has also written books about the Smiths, Bruce Springsteen, and you too." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pmKjZzypzvg
**John Frusciante's Dental Work and Skin Grafts (1998)** : As part of his recovery from severe drug addiction, John Frusciante underwent extensive medical procedures in 1998. He had a lethal infection in his mouth requiring all his rotted teeth to be pulled and replaced with $75,000 worth of implants to prevent sepsis. He also required skin grafts for abscesses on his arms caused by needle use. "At the time, he had a lethal infection growing in his mouth. And if he didn't have all his rodded teeth pulled out and replaced with implants, he would eventually go into sepsis and die." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TESrEzVHLCc
**John Hinckley Jr. – Reagan's Would-Be Assassin Released and Recording Music (2023 Debut Album)** : John Hinckley Jr., who attempted to assassinate President Ronald Reagan, was released unconditionally in 2022 and has since pursued a music career, releasing a debut album in July 2023. "Was released unconditionally in 2022 and now makes music, having released a debut album in July 2023." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qtdetc5-MQo
**John Hughes Movies' Impact on Adolescence (1980s)** : John Hughes' films of the 1980s, including *Sixteen Candles*, *The Breakfast Club*, and *Ferris Bueller's Day Off*, were significant for treating the experiences and feelings of adolescence with respect and intelligence. These movies resonated deeply with audiences and were accompanied by carefully curated alt-rock soundtracks that further amplified their cultural impact. "They treated adolescence and their feelings and their experiences with respect and intelligence." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**John Hughes Soundtracks' British Performer Exposure (1980s)** : The carefully curated soundtracks for John Hughes' 1980s films were immensely important for British alternative performers, providing crucial exposure on the North American side of the Atlantic. Many acts licensed album tracks or B-sides, and there were numerous proper single releases, significantly boosting the careers of bands like Simple Minds and New Order. "It's pretty hard to understand how important these John Hughes soundtracks were to these British performers and the exposure they were given on this side of the Atlantic." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**John Hughes' Soundtrack Strategy** : Director John Hughes significantly utilized New Wave music for his film soundtracks, including *Sixteen Candles*, *Pretty in Pink*, and *The Breakfast Club*, leveraging its affordability and edgy appeal to fill albums with tracks from new bands, many of them British. "This music was also cheap to license for movie soundtracks, something that director John Hughes used to his full advantage for films like 16 candles and Pretty and Pink and the Breakfast Club." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bYsV3ozbk4M
**John Lennon's Philosophy on Life** : John Lennon famously encapsulated the unpredictable nature of existence, stating that life often unfolds while individuals are occupied with making other plans. "John Lennon said, life is what happens when you're making other plans." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nX8uAFDzDYM
**John S. Dwight's 19th-Century Critique of New Music** : John S. Dwight, a 19th-century composer of hymns, critically described the "new music of his day." He observed that such tunes, though widely whistled and sung, were not truly embraced with "musical emotion" and instead "haunt the morbidly sensitive nerves of deeply musical persons," who might hum them involuntarily while "hating them." He likened their pervasive nature to a "morbid irritation of the skin." "Such tunes, although whistled and sung by everybody, are erroneously supposed to have taken a deep hold of the popular mind. But they are hummed and whistled without musical emotion." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m7MbV-eCLyE
**John Steinbeck's "The Grapes of Wrath"** : The 1939 novel "The Grapes of Wrath" by John Steinbeck features the main character, Tom Joad, whose story inspired Bruce Springsteen's 1995 song "The Ghost of Tom Joad." "it's a quiet folk song that told the story of the main character from the John Steinbeck novel The Grapes of Wrath." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2EerEOuP52I
**John Varvatos Flagship Store at Former CBGB Location** : The flagship store for men's fashion designer John Varvatos is located at the former site of CBGB in Lower Manhattan. CBGB, a legendary punk rock venue, is no longer in operation. "It's been replaced by the flagship store operated by John Vavardos, the man's fashion designer." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WIFxAaReLn0
**John Wayne Gacy as Subject of Songs** : Numerous songs have been written about John Wayne Gacy, a notorious serial killer. This indicates a consistent interest in memorializing or addressing his crimes through music. "There's also no problem finding songs about John Wayne Gacy, Jack The Ripper, the Unibomber and Richard Ramirez, the Night Stalker." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**John Williams Composed the Gilligan's Island Theme Before Star Wars** : Joseph Williams, son of John Williams (composer of Star Wars, Jaws, Indiana Jones, ET, Jurassic Park, Schindler's List, and many others), is the lead singer of Toto, the voice of adult Simba in The Lion King, and the singer of the Gummy Bears theme song. John Williams himself got his career start by writing the original theme to Gilligan's Island. "John Williams everyone, the man who wrote the theme for JAWS, Star Wars... got his start by trying to write the theme song for Gilligan's Island." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Johnny Cash and Stalin's Death (1953) – Possibly Apocryphal** : There is a persistent legend that Johnny Cash, serving as a Morse code operator in the US Air Force in 1953, intercepted the first messages announcing Soviet leader Joseph Stalin's death on March 3, 1953. The story is likely exaggerated: Cash didn't know Russian, let alone Russian Morse code, so the transmission would have been nearly impossible for him to decode. "There's speculation that Johnny may have exaggerated things a little." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**Jon Bon Jovi's New Jersey Quote on Springsteen** : Jon Bon Jovi once humorously remarked on the pervasive influence of Bruce Springsteen in New Jersey, stating, "in New Jersey, if you don't like Springsteen, they raise you taxes." "John Bungeauvich once said, in New Jersey, if you don't like Springsteen, they raise you taxes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2EerEOuP52I
**Jordan Mooney's Early Life and Fashion** : Born Pamela Ruck in Seaford in 1955, Jordan Mooney found herself a misfit in her small British hometown due to her unconventional fashion sense. After being expelled from school for her red and pink haircut (inspired by David Bowie), she moved to London. "She was born Pamela Ruck in 1955 in a British town called Seaford." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Jordan Mooney's Influence on British Punk** : Jordan Mooney was a crucial figure in the original British punk rock scene, influencing its fashion sense through her work at the Sex boutique and her association with the Sex Pistols, whom she followed everywhere as part of the "Bromley contingent." Andy Warhol photographed her and featured her in a film. Although she wasn't a musician, she served as a "magnet and then as glue" for an important part of the scene, potentially even contributing to the Sex Pistols' formation. "Jordan wasn't a singer, she didn't play any instruments but she did act as a magnet and then as glue for an important portion of the original British punk rock scene." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Jordan Mooney's Later Life** : After her job at the Sex boutique ended, Jordan Mooney married Kevin Mooney of Adam and the Ants. She later struggled with drug use, moved back to the seaside, and spent three decades working as a veterinarian's assistant after raising Burmese cats. "She fell into drugs and then moved back to the seaside. She raised Burmese cats which led to a job as a veterinarian's assistant that lasted for three decades." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Jordan Mooney's Role at Sex Boutique and Fashion Influence** : Jordan Mooney, after changing her name, worked at Malcolm McLaren and Vivian Westwood's "Sex" boutique on King's Road, London. She became a muse, modeling daring, unconventional clothes (wild hair, makeup, see-through items, bondage gear) custom-made by McLaren and Westwood. She acted as a "walking billboard" for the shop, which boosted business and attracted the "kids who didn't fit in," including those who would form the Sex Pistols. "She became something of amuse from McLaren and Westwood who began tannering clothes for Jordan who would then wear them everywhere." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Joshua Cook's Parent Murders (2003)** : On February 17, 2003, 19-year-old Joshua Cook murdered his adoptive parents, John and Margaret Cook, in Fairfax, Virginia, using a shotgun he purchased two days prior. Cook, obsessed with "The Matrix" movie and believing life was a simulation, listened to Drowning Pool's "Bodies" repeatedly, including through headphones, during the murders. "On February 17, 2003, while Joshua's parents were out shubbling the driveway, he was inside playing Grand Theft Auto 3. And then something snapped." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Joshua Tree Studio Setup** : A recording studio located in Joshua Tree, California, doubles as the owner's house. Clients using the studio are expected to bunk at the owner's place and often cook for them, creating a unique, communal recording environment. "The studio doubles as the owner's house. And whoever is using the studio basically bunks at the owner's place. Cooks for them and everything." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Mh-qg1izM98
**Journal of Musical Things Website** : A website named "a journal of musicalthings.com" is updated daily and offers a daily newsletter focusing on the newest, coolest, and weirdest music news. "I have a website, a journal of musicalthings.com, which is updated every single day." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=l1jPP1FhGo8
**Joy Division – Section of a Nazi Concentration Camp Novel** : The name comes from the novel House of Dolls (1955) by Yehiel De-Nur, which describes a section of a Nazi concentration camp where female prisoners were kept as sex slaves for officers on leave. That section was called the Joy Division. "This is another reference to a novel." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Judge Larry Fiddler – Los Angeles Superior Court** : Judge Larry Fiddler was a Los Angeles Superior Court judge assigned to Scott Wyland's case, who imposed conditions such as regular court appearances to monitor Wyland's sobriety. He became visibly frustrated with Wyland's repeated missed hearings and probation violations, eventually ordering him back to rehab multiple times. "A Los Angeles Superior court judge named Larry Fiddler was assigned to the Wylandt case, and one of the conditions he imposed on Scott was that he appeared court hearings every so often." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TDwagoyMvck
**Judgment Night (1993) Soundtrack Crossover Impact** : The 1993 *Judgment Night* soundtrack fundamentally changed the course of music by pairing rock bands with hip-hop outfits, a radical concept at the time due to limited crossover. This groundbreaking project served as a major catalyst for the rap rock and nu metal genres, bringing fans from both sides of the divide together and demonstrating shared musical commonalities. "For some reason, the producers came up with the idea of pairing a rock band with a hip-hop outfit. This was pretty radical thinking at the time because there was very little crossover between the two." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**Jukebox Role in Popularity Tracking** : Jukeboxes, which existed for almost 50 years by the 1950s, allowed for gauging a song's popularity by observing which tracks were played most frequently. "The jukebox had been around for almost 50 years." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Jukeboxes Save the Recorded Music Industry** : One of two major factors that rescued the recorded music industry was the jukebox. By the 1940s, a quarter million jukeboxes were installed across North America in bars, restaurants, nightclubs, and truck stops, generating 13 million in annual sales by requiring regular stocking with new records. "The first was the jukebox, an automated machine found in bars, restaurants, nightclubs, and truck stops that could play a favorite song for a nickel. By the 1940s, there were a quarter million jukeboxes installed across North America, and they all had to be regularly stocked with new records, and that accounted for thirteen million in annual sales." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VlIErdSSVOM
**Justin Barber's Murder of His Wife (2002)** : On August 17, 2002, Justin Barber murdered his wife by drowning and shooting her, then shot himself four times before calling police to falsely claim they had been mugged. An investigation revealed he orchestrated the crime for an insurance payout, having listened to Guns N' Roses' "Used to Love Her (but I Had to Kill Her)" for half an hour prior. "On August 17th, 2002, Justin Barber drowned and then shot his wife." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Justine Ferrarra – Korn Album Model** : Justine Ferrarra was the eight-year-old girl who appeared on the controversial cover of Korn's 1994 debut album, for which she received $300. The sinister nature of the artwork was such that she was not shown the final picture until she was in the eighth grade, and her family expressed considerable displeasure with the result. "The little girl's name is Justine Ferrarra." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**KAOS Radio Show "Your Dream Girl"** : In the Olympia area, a campus radio station called KAOS hosted a show named "Your Dream Girl," hosted by Lois Muffet (who later joined several bands). The program was specifically "aimed at angry young women," serving as a crucial outlet for the developing Riot Grrrl ethos and building community. "The show was called Your Dream Girl, and the host was Lois Muffet, who would later be in a bunch of bands. Her program was aimed at angry young women." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qss5kC2FUx0
**KFC Twitter Following Strategy** : KFC's official Twitter account follows fewer than a dozen people.
