Specifically, they follow five individuals named Herb, and the other six followers are the members of the Spice Girls, forming a specific, pun-based joke about their "11 herbs and spices" recipe. "Five of them are named Herb, and the other six are members of the Spice Girls." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**KFC's Twitter Following – Six Guys Named Herb and All Five Spice Girls** : KFC follows exactly 11 accounts on Twitter: six guys named Herb and all five of the Spice Girls. The reference: the chain's original secret recipe uses 11 herbs and spices. "Think about it, and you will get it eventually." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**KGB Concern Over Kiev Youth and Punk** : After the fall of the Soviet Union, researchers examining government archives in Ukraine discovered meticulous reports from KGB agents expressing significant concern about young people in Soviet-era Kiev who were excessively enjoying the new punk music. Fans were investigated, questioned, and potentially faced worse consequences, as the KGB analyzed whether punk could be weaponized against the West as it seemed to be used against them. "Researchers digging through government archives in Ukraine found a meticulous report of agents who were very concerned about youths and Soviet-era Kiev who were enjoying this new music a little too much." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YeNWDtN_sHE
**KGB Control of Music Clubs (Post-1969)** : Beginning in 1969, the KGB became involved in the club business as authorities realized they couldn't stop rock music. To limit its influence, they sought to control musicians and fans under KGB supervision. Any band wishing to play in these KGB-run clubs required state approval for their music, lyrics, clothing, image, and touring schedule, making these "official bands" covertly managed by the secret police. "So starting in 1969, the KGB got into the club business. Anyone who wanted to play these KGB music clubs had to be approved by the state." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YeNWDtN_sHE
**KLH Model 11 - Portable Stereo System (1962)** : The KLH Model 11, released in 1962, was a successful transistor-ized stereo system designed for portability. It integrated a record player, an amplifier, and two speakers into a suitcase-like enclosure. While it still required an electrical outlet for power (lacking batteries), it was considered quite portable under suitable conditions. `"A bigger success was the 1962 KLH Model 11, a pretty nifty transistor-ized stereo system, which featured a record player, an amp, and two speakers tucked into a suitcase design."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Kanda Plaas Rehearsal Space** : Radiohead's rehearsal space, located at an old farmhouse in Sutton Courtney, Oxfordshire. After spending $250,000 to equip it as a studio, the band ultimately had to leave for St Catherine's Court because "no one had ever installed toilets at Kandaplas." "Radio had went back to Kanda Plaas, their rehearsal space at the old farmhouse in Sutton, Cortney in Oxfordshire." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JUvDp3_RBH0
**Kanye West's Malibu Bomb Shelter Renovation (2024) – $57 Million Mansion Gutted** : Kanye West owned a house in Malibu and ordered its project manager to remove all windows, all electrical, and all plumbing to turn the $57 million mansion into a World War One–style bomb shelter. The project manager refused, citing safety, and was fired. "He ordered a project manager to remove all the windows, all the electrical and all the plumbing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Karaoke Rage Incidents Worldwide (2008-2024)** : Beyond the Philippines' "My Way murders," instances of "Karaoke Rage" have occurred globally. In March 2008, a Thai man shot eight people over John Denver's "Take Me Home, Country Road." In December 2008, a Malaysian man was stabbed for hogging the mic. In 2012, two people were killed with a meat cleaver in China for refusing to share, and an American was stabbed in Krabby, Thailand, in 2013 for the same reason. "there is something called Karyoki Rage, which has been felt around the world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dVJuITJyNTU
**Karl Lagerfeld's iPod Collection** : Designer Karl Lagerfeld owned more than 300 iPods when he passed away in February 2019. He was an early adopter, possessing over 70 by 2004, which was less than three years after Apple unveiled the device. This extensive collection suggests his enjoyment of gadgets and his preference for having one readily available. "The only explanation is that he enjoyed gadgets." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HoBx6s3JmVQ
**Keith Richards Wanted to Be a Librarian; Now Has Meticulously Curated Book Collections** : Before becoming one of rock's most famous guitarists, Keith Richards had dreams of becoming a librarian. Today he maintains extensive and carefully curated book collections in all of his homes. "When he was young, he had dreams of being a librarian." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Ken Losen and Wiseguy's Bot Operations** : Ken Losen, recognizing the shift to online ticket sales in the early 2000s, founded Wiseguy to acquire hard-to-get tickets. He hired a 17-year-old Bulgarian programmer to develop bot software that could outpace human buyers. Wiseguy successfully used this software to purchase large quantities of tickets across North America, selling them to clients or brokers at significantly inflated prices. For instance, in 2005, Wiseguy generated $2.5 million from a U2 tour by buying 5,000 fan club memberships for $200,000 and scalping the discounted tickets. The company was eventually busted by the FBI, but the bot software concept persisted and became cheaper and more widespread. "He found a 17-year-old programmer in Bulgaria who knew that computers could be much faster than humans when it came to following the online purchase prompts." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sPj3uxmz8H4
**Kenneth Schaeffer Theory for Rather Attack** : One theory floated regarding the Dan Rather assault was that he was mistaken for a man named Kenneth Schaeffer, an electronics expert who had designed a system for intercepting coded Soviet satellite transmissions. "For a while, a theory was floated that Dan had been mistaken for a man named Kenneth Schaeffer, an electronics expert who had designed a system for intercepting coded Soviet satellite transmissions." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4PlieAEthSg
**Kent State University Killings (May 1970)** : In May 1970, four protesting students were killed by National Guard troops at Kent State University. This tragic event sparked widespread outrage and directly inspired Neil Young to write the song "Ohio," which was released within a month. "when four protesting students were killed by National Guard troops at Kent State University in May 1970, Neil Young was so outraged that he wrote the song Ohio, and it was on sale and on the radio within a month." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0ARYt4sYT90
**Kevin Bacon Pays DJs and Bands Not to Play "Footloose" at Weddings** : Kevin Bacon reportedly pays DJs and bands at weddings to ensure that the song "Footloose" — from the 1984 film in which he starred — is not played. "Pays DJs or bands at weddings to not play 'Footloose.'" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qtdetc5-MQo
**Kevin Lyman's Background** : Kevin Lyman, the founder of the Warped Tour, was born in 1961 and is American. He became involved in booking bands while attending California State Polytechnic University in Pomona, where he majored in recreation administration in the early 1980s. After graduation, he worked as a production manager at Golden Voice, an LA-based concert promotion company, before founding Warped Tour. "We know he's American. He was born in 1961. He got into the business of booking bands while he was in university. He went to the California State Polytechnic University in Pomona where he majored in recreation administration." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jfQaUV6Bq4
**Kierkegaard's Existential Influence on "Reflektor"** : The Danish philosopher Soren Kierkegaard, known for his essays exploring existential concepts, particularly the act of reflecting on life and one's current state, provided significant intellectual inspiration for the thematic content of Arcade Fire's "Reflektor" album. "And then there was some reading of essays by Danish philosopher Soren Kirchegard, who liked to write about existential things like the notion of reflecting on life and one's present condition." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ir2FfLQJNtY
**King Gizzard and the Lizard Wizard – Compromise Between "Gizzard Gizzard" and a Jim Morrison Reference** : Frontman Stu Mackenzie wanted to call the band "Gizzard Gizzard." Another member wanted to honor the Lizard King, Jim Morrison. A compromise (assisted by drinks and spliffs) produced King Gizzard and the Lizard Wizard. "And so I guess there were drinks and slifts." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**King Louis XI's Pig Organ (15th Century France) – Keyboard Connected to Spikes That Stabbed Pigs** : In 15th-century France, King Louis XI commissioned a piano-sized instrument built from pigs of various sizes arranged to form a musical scale. A keyboard was connected to spikes that stabbed each pig as required, producing squeals that formed a melody. "Let's peed around then." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**Kings of Leon – Tribute to Grandfather/Great-Uncle Leon Followill** : The Followill brothers (Caleb, Nathan, Jared) and cousin Matthew are all related to Leon Followill — grandfather of the brothers, great-uncle of Matthew. The name pays tribute to him. "They wanted to pay tribute to him by carrying on his name." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Klingon Opera in Stockholm** : A theater in Stockholm staged an opera production performed entirely in Klingon, the fictional language from Star Trek. The opera was titled "Kono's," and as part of the immersive experience, Klingon Bloodwine was served in the theater. "A theater in Stockholm launched an opera production performed entirely in Klingon. It was called Kono's. And yes, they did serve in the theater." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**Knitting for Dexterity and Relaxation** : Knitting is an ancient pastime known for its relaxing qualities and its practical results in socks, sweaters, and scarves. It also helps improve finger dexterity, a beneficial skill for virtually any musician. "Knitting is an age-old pastime that not only results in sock, sweaters, and scarves, but you can also be very relaxing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xc85sLj0Cnk
**Koi's Plug Design Challenges (Bras)** : In developing the durable and quick-connect/disconnect plugs needed for his central telephone exchange, George Koi experimented with various designs for connections. Interestingly, this included trying "wires from women's bras" as a material for the plugs, highlighting the innovative and unconventional approaches taken to solve early technological challenges. "Koi tried all kinds of designs for the plugs needed by these connections, including believe it or not, using wires from women's bras." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NAroxFbYkRw
**Kong Studios** : The fictional headquarters of Gorillaz, located "high in a mountain in Essex, which is northeast of London." Despite not existing in reality, its appearance could be viewed on gorillas.com. It was depicted as closed and abandoned during the "Slowboat to Hades" phase, with a robot guiding visitors around. "By the way, the gorillas headquarters is called Kong Studios. It's supposedly high in a mountain in Essex, which is northeast of London." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iVdTCqpd798
**Kongsberg Attack (2021)** : On October 13, 2021, a mass killing spree occurred in Kongsberg, Norway. Espen Bråthen, a Danish man with a history of mental illness, armed with a bow and arrow and a knife, attacked people in a supermarket and then a residential neighborhood. In 35 minutes, he killed five people and injured three, including musician Andrea Hogan. Bråthen was diagnosed with chronic paranoid schizophrenia and sentenced to a psychiatric prison. "On October 13, 2021, Andrea was living in Kongsburg, Norway. At around 6 pm that night, police received a call about a man walking around town armed with a bow and arrow and a quiver on his shoulder." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q6dAX6HCjgw
**Kraftwerk's Toy Bee Gees Drum Machine** : When Kraftwerk was making records in the mid-1970s, on the cutting edge of synthesizer technology, they often used a toy drum machine branded with the Bee Gees logo. "So yes, a band that was on the bleeding edge of synth technology used a toy Bee Gees drum machine." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Kurt Cobain's Death and Alt-Rock Vacuum (April 8, 1994)** : Kurt Cobain was found dead on April 8, 1994, the exact same day The Offspring released their album "Smash." This event created a "creative and spiritual vacuum" in alt-rock as grunge's influence waned, which inadvertently paved the way for skate punk's rise. "On April 8th, 1994, the exact same day Kurt Cobain was found dead in a room above a greenhouse in his backyard. The offspring quietly released an album called Smash." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=44dXy0StjIQ
**Kurt Cobain's Death and Punk Revival** : Kurt Cobain's death on April 8, 1994, was a pivotal event that indirectly fueled the 1990s punk revival. In hindsight, it marked the end of the industry's laser focus on grunge, creating a need for new musical trends. This transition smoothly shifted attention to emerging punk bands like Green Day, whose album "Dookie" then soared in popularity. "The world was shocked by the death of Kurt Cobain. And in hindsight, we can see that this event marked the beginning of the end of the public's and the recording industries laser focus on grunge." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gkLMwrthZ9s
**Kurt Cobain's Death as Catalyst** : Kurt Cobain's suicide in April 1994 halted grunge's momentum and shook North American alt-rock, creating a "vacuum" across the Atlantic. This allowed British bands to "exert some kind of influence" and opened the door for Britpop's explosive growth in the subsequent years. "The death of Grunge's biggest star brought all that momentum that Grunge had almost to a dead stop." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5q5EnZj1FwE
**Kurt Glimser – Catalogued Bootlegs in Reference Books Used as Collectors' Bibles (from 1973)** : Beginning in 1973, Kurt Glimser exhaustively catalogued bootleg recordings in reference books including *Bootlegs* and *Hot Wax*, which became the standard reference texts — the "bibles" — for serious collectors. "Catalogued bootlegs in books that became 'bibles' for collectors." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Uly2cT2vc28
**L&M Cigarettes Slogan (1952)** : In 1952, the cigarette brand L&M used the slogan "just what the doctor ordered," a marketing phrase that would be considered highly inappropriate and misleading by modern health standards. "And their slogan, and I'm not making this up, was just what the doctor ordered." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dVJuITJyNTU
**LA Riots (April 29, 1992) Inspiration** : The Los Angeles riots, which began on April 29, 1992, after the verdict in the Rodney King trial, directly inspired the Sublime song "April 29, 1992." "And April 29, 1992 from Sublime is all about the LA riots that erupted after the Rodney King trial." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cM3-tINKdvo
**LA Times Description of Frusciante (1996)** : In 1996, the Los Angeles Times described John Frusciante as "a skeleton covered in skin" due to his extreme physical deterioration caused by severe drug addiction. He weighed only 88 pounds at the time. "By 1996, he weighed just 88 pounds and was described by the LA Times as a skeleton covered in skin." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TESrEzVHLCc
**LG's Touchscreen Phone (2006)** : In December 2006, LG released a pioneering phone featuring a touchscreen, a novel technology at the time. "In December 2006, LG released a phone with a touchscreen. Nobody had ever seen anything like that before." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**LP Playback Speed and Grooves Geometry** : Columbia engineers, utilizing information from expired RCA patents and audio technology pioneered by the film industry in the late 1920s, determined the optimal geometry for the Long-Playing record. They settled on a playback speed of 33 and 1/3 revolutions per minute for a 12-inch record, with grooves cut anywhere from 224 to 300 per linear inch. "Columbia engineers working the geometry of the situation decided to go with 33 and 1-third revolutions per minute of a 12-inch record with grooves cut anywhere from 224 to 300 per linear inch." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VlIErdSSVOM
**LP and 45 Coexistence (Post-Format War)** : The format war between the LP and the 45 had no definitive winner, leading to a situation where both formats coexisted, with adults typically buying albums and kids buying singles. "This format war between the LP and the 45 would have no winner. Just like there's never been a resolution to the Korean War, a situation developed where both formats managed to coexist." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**La Poushiau - First Named Drummer** : The very first drummer known by name is La Poushiau, the granddaughter of Narasim, a Sumerian king. She was likely a priestess, further highlighting the ancient connection between women, religious roles, and drumming. "The very first drummer we know by name is La Poushiau. She was the granddaughter of Narasim, a Sumerian king and she was probably a priestess too." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9GHpC0kCgHY
**La Tomatina Festival** : This festival, occurring on the last Wednesday in August in Spain, is known as the world's largest public tomato fight. The tradition is thought to have begun in 1945 during a brawl in the main square, where tomatoes from vegetable stands served as available weapons. "We think it started in 1945 when there was a brawl in the main square and one of the few weapons available were the tomatoes on carts to the vegetable stands." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bferkEWvJg0
**Labels Retaining Vinyl (Novelty/Promo)** : Many record labels never entirely abandoned vinyl, issuing albums or singles on the format as a novelty or for promotional purposes, which helped keep pressing plants in business. "And many record labels never entirely abandoned the format. Issuing albums or singles on vinyl became something of an novelty." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Labels and Spotify – Major Labels Hold Equity Positions; 85% of Revenue from Streaming** : After being dragged into streaming kicking and screaming, major record labels now love it: 85% of all label revenue comes from streaming. Labels also hold equity positions in Spotify, meaning they profit both from royalties and from the platform's market valuation of approximately $250 billion. Labels benefit from streaming's elimination of manufacturing, warehousing, shipping, and physical retail costs. "Labels would be thrilled if CDs disappeared tomorrow." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MQ7PDi-D9Ig
**Lack of Context and Emotional Attachment in Streaming** : The digital nature of streaming, without physical album covers or accompanying liner notes, leads to listeners knowing less about songs, albums, artists, producers, and the stories behind the music, dulling its meaning. Furthermore, the essentially free access to music diminishes the emotional and financial investment listeners once felt. "It's delivered without context, and lack of context can dull the meaning of music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5Y6nHw5xx88
**Lack of Dedicated Record Stores in Early 20th Century** : In the early 1900s, dedicated record stores were rare. If one wanted to purchase records, they typically did so from the same place they bought their phonograph, which was usually a local furniture store. Spillers Records in Cardiff, Wales, established in 1894, is an exception as the world's oldest record store and is still operating today. "For the most part, record stores did not exist then. I should point out, however, that the oldest record store in the world is Spillers Records in Cardiff Wales. It was established in eighteen ninety-four, and the place is still open." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VlIErdSSVOM
**Lack of Pension Plans in Rock Music** : The absence of a formal pension plan in rock music is a significant motivator for band reunions, as aging rock stars often lack portable skills and miss their previous lifestyle. "The major motivation has been, let's face it, money, because well, there is no pension plan in rock." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yR3xc3ra5tE
**Lady Gaga in The Sopranos (Season 6, Episode 19)** : In Season 6, Episode 19 of The Sopranos, AJ Tony Soprano broke into his high school with a group of friends. One of those friends was a very young Lady Gaga. "One of those friends was a very young lady Gaga." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Lagrange Multipliers for Wealth** : Hugh Everett III successfully applied mathematical principles, specifically "Lagrange multipliers," to generate substantial wealth, ultimately making him a millionaire. "He used something called Lagrange multipliers, which made him a millionaire." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QfiRRtsZoYU
**Lake Ontario Waterkeepers Environmental Organization** : Gord Downie served as a board member for Lake Ontario Waterkeepers, an organization dedicated to keeping the Great Lakes basin clean. They actively fought against companies discharging sewage into public waters and once opposed a company in Bath, Ontario (where The Hip's studio was located) that intended to burn tires. In 2007, they compelled the city of Toronto to explain public beach closures due to pollution. "Gord was a board member of Lake Ontario waterkeepers, which fought to keep the great lakes base and clean." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dnz_Ycd_q7s
**Language Evolution from Bird Song Imitation** : Some scientists hypothesize that human language, specifically the ability to speak, evolved from early humans' attempts to imitate natural sounds like bird songs. This theory could explain the innate "hardwiring" of the brain for music. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHN6db7m3DM
**Lanzarote – Audioslave Cover Location** : Lanzarote, one of the Canary Islands, served as the striking volcanic location for the cover photograph of Audioslave's 2002 debut album. Designer Storm Thorgerson specifically chose this "truly remarkable place" to provide the desolate backdrop for the album's symbolic flame sculpture and solitary figure. "The location is the volcanic island of Lanzarate, a truly remarkable place. It's one of the Canary Islands." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Large Hadron Collider Interest** : The Large Hadron Collider in Europe, a massive scientific instrument designed to explore the fundamental depths of quantum mechanics, is a subject of great interest to musicians like Gary Lightbody and a workplace for scientists such as Professor Brian Cox. "Gary is also very interested in the quantum experiments being carried out with the large Hadron Super Collider in Europe." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QfiRRtsZoYU
**Largest Group Rendition of "Smoke on the Water" (2006)** : In 2006, Kansas City, Missouri, witnessed a record-breaking event where 1,721 people simultaneously played the iconic riff from "Smoke on the Water." This mass performance created one of the largest group renditions of a song. "2006, Kansas City, Missouri, 1,721 people riffing at the same time." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zWNzlSxrj6s
**Largest Group Rendition of Bob Dylan's "Knockin' on Heaven's Door" (2007)** : A new record for a large group rendition was set on October 26, 2007, in Shalong, Mejhalaya, India, where 1,730 people played Bob Dylan's "Knockin' on Heaven's Door" in unison. This surpassed the previous record for "Smoke on the Water." "on October 26, 2007 in the town of Shalong in the state of Mejhalaya in India, 1,730 people played Bob Dylan's Knock on On Heaven's Door in Unison." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zWNzlSxrj6s
**Lars Ulrich Toilet Sculpture – Prince Midnight's Art Project** : A man named Prince Midnight fashioned a toilet that looks exactly like Metallica drummer Lars Ulrich, designed so the user sits in Lars' lap. This is the same person who previously fashioned a working guitar out of his deceased uncle's bones. "It's set up so that you would basically sit in Lars' lap." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**LaserVision Disk Failure** : The initial plan for James T. Russell's digital recording invention was to create a rotating disk capable of playing both audio and video, resulting in the LaserVision Disk. However, this format proved to be a significant commercial failure. "The original plan was to create a rotating disk that could play back both audio and video. And the result was something called the laser vision disk, about which really the less said the better because it was a big flop." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Last Call from Michael Hutchence** : An ex-girlfriend who spoke with Michael Hutchence at 9:54 AM on November 22nd. She reported him sounding "crying and depressed and sounded drunk." She promptly went to his hotel room (524) around 10:40 AM but got no answer, leaving a note at reception. "At 9.54 a.m., this is November 22nd, she did answer. She says that Michael was crying and depressed and sounded drunk." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Last Contact from Michael Hutchence** : Michael Hutchence's personal manager, who received two voicemail messages from him at 9:38 AM and 9:50 AM on the morning of his death. After receiving the messages, she contacted the band's tour manager about Hutchence missing an important rehearsal. "At 9.38 a.m., he left a voice mill for Martha Troop, his personal manager." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Last Factory-Installed Car Cassette Player (2010)** : The 2010 Lexus 430SC holds the distinction of being the last automobile model in which a factory-installed cassette player was available. This marked the end of an era for in-car cassette playback directly from manufacturers. `"The last automobile in which you could get a factory-installed cassette player was the 2010 Lexus 430SC."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Last Guest with Michael Hutchence** : An Australian soap opera star and ex-girlfriend of Michael Hutchence, she visited his hotel room with her boyfriend in his final hours, consuming daiquiris, champagne, and beer with him. "He called up Kim Wilson, an Australian soap opera star and ex-girlfriend. She and her boyfriend came to the hotel where they banged back a bunch of daiquiri, champagne and beer in the hotel bar before going out to Michael's room." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Lasting Impact of Brief Scenes** : The Riot Grrrl movement, much like the original punk rock explosion, demonstrates that a "scene and a sound doesn't have to be around for long, for it to have a lasting impact," creating a new paradigm for "fierce, uncompromising female-oriented rock" whose influence is still felt today. This idea challenges the notion that longevity is essential for profound cultural influence. "Sometimes, a scene and a sound doesn't have to be around for long, for it to have a lasting impact." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qss5kC2FUx0
**Late 1970s / Early 1980s Recession** : A brutal recession hit in the late 1970s and early 1980s, characterized by 12% inflation rates and 22% mortgage rates, causing people to stop buying non-essentials, including music. "Record sales went into a free fall and a brutal, brutal recession kicked in. Imagine inflation rates of 12 percent and mortgage rates of 22 percent." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Late 1970s / Early 1980s Shift to the Right** : The end of the 1970s was a period of intense societal change, characterized by a severe recession and sharp political turns to the right in both the United States and the United Kingdom. In the US, this marked the beginning of the Reagan era, while in the UK, Margaret Thatcher's right-wing policies and austerity measures were deeply affecting the populace. "The end of the 70s was one of those times. There was a terrible recession and both the US and the UK had taken hard political turns to the right." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0ARYt4sYT90
**Late 1970s Music Direction and Openness** : At the end of the 1970s, the direction of music was perceived as "undefined," similar to other periods of musical flux. However, this uncertainty also fostered an environment where "nothing was off limits or out of bounds," allowing for exploration of new musical paths, especially in the post-punk era. "That was also a time when the direction of music seemed undefined." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zNIVqKqAlnk
**Late 1990s Economic and Political Climate** : The late 1990s saw an improved economy with a major bull market under President Bill Clinton, the end of the Cold War, and the first Gulf War becoming a memory, leading to a shift away from the "grimness" of the early 1990s and influencing a turn towards more cheerful pop music. "The economy had improved. In fact, America was enjoying a major bull market under Democrat Bill Clinton. Cold war was over. The first Gulf War was a memory. The grimness of the early 1990s was all gone." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7JP2tjJibTQ
**Late 90s Production Value Soars with New Tech** : The late 1990s saw music video production values soar, primarily due to advancements like desktop editing, computer graphic images (CGI), and green screen technology. These tools enabled "high-concept" clips with wild camera moves, lightning-fast editing, and complex post-production flourishes that were previously impossible or prohibitively expensive. "Production values soared thanks to new technology like desktop editing, computer graphic images, green screen technology, and other tools." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Lawsuit Against BBC Symphony Orchestra Over Asbestos Exposure** : The widow of a musician who performed with the BBC Symphony Orchestra initiated a lawsuit against the network, alleging that her husband died from cancer caused by asbestos exposure in a studio. As a horn player, he frequently took deep breaths, which his widow claims contributed to his development of Mesothelioma. "the widow of a musician who performed with the BBC Symphony Orchestra launched a lawsuit against the network saying that he died of cancer caused by having to perform in a studio that was riddled with his bestest." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-N6IM5Nm5iw
**Le Cold Polytechnique Massacre (December 6, 1989)** : This horrific event in Montreal saw Marc Lépine enter Le Cold Polytechnique with a rifle and hunting knife, shooting 27 people and killing 14 women. This tragedy served as the subject for The Tragically Hip's unreleased song "Montreal." "The track's called Montreal and it's about the events of December 6, 1989 when Marc Le Pen walked into Le Cold Polytechnique in Montreal with a rifle and a hunting knife shooting 27 people and killing 14 women." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Um_KEEcCeT4
**Leakage of Holdback Tickets to Secondary Market** : The system of ticket "holdbacks" is prone to leakage, where tickets intended for fan clubs, seat license holders, venues, or even the artists themselves are immediately resold to scalpers on the secondary market for profit. This practice contributes to the scarcity of tickets for the general public and drives up prices. "Meanwhile, the whole back situation is very leaky. Members of the fan club get tickets and might immediately flip them to scalpers to make some extra money." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Lear Corporate Jet** : The inventor of the 8-track tape was also responsible for the Lear Corporate Jet. "invented by the same dude who gave us the Lear Corporate Jet." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Learning from Lollapalooza's Strengths & Weaknesses** : Lyman observed Lollapalooza's evolution for several years, noting what the festival did correctly and incorrectly, particularly its tendency to book bands that were "too mainstream and not eclectic enough," which informed his approach for the Warped Tour. "After watching Lola Blues evolve over the next couple of years, he saw everything the festival was doing right and what it was doing wrong when it came to the bands that were booked. He thought they were too mainstream and not eclectic enough." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jfQaUV6Bq4
**Lee "Scratch" Perry's Production Techniques – Blowing Pot Smoke Into Mixing Desk; Spritzing Tapes with Whiskey, Blood, and Urine** : The late Jamaican producer Lee Scratch Perry spritzed master tapes with a mixture of whiskey, blood, and urine to "enhance their spiritual fidelity," blew pot smoke into the mixing desk to imbue it with mystical properties, and kept a champagne glass he had defecated into in a cabinet in his house. "He sometimes pooped into a champagne glass, which he then kept in a cabinet in his house." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**Lee DeForest and the Vacuum Tube** : In 1911, Lee DeForest introduced the vacuum tube, an invention that became the fundamental technology for early amplifiers, enabling the significant amplification of quiet electrical signals. "That happened in 1911 when Lee DeForest unveiled a vacuum tube, and that became the foundation for something soon known as an amplifier." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E6cXyaHALBQ
**Legacy of 90s Bands** : Music from the 1990s was so popular that many acts from that era, including Green Day, Blink-182, Pearl Jam, and even defunct bands like Nirvana and Stone Temple Pilots, continue to be "extremely popular with fans," making it challenging for new artists to emerge and capture attention in the 21st century. "That music, in that decade, was so good and so popular that audiences kept demanding to hear it. And so many of the acts from that era never went away." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Legal Loopholes That Enabled Bootleg Distribution – UK, Italy, Greece** : The bootleg trade exploited legal gaps in copyright law across several countries: in the United Kingdom, recordings made in public venues were not initially protected; in Italy, unclaimed artist royalty percentages created opportunities; and in Greece, sales on university campuses existed in a legal grey zone. "Exploited legal loopholes in countries like the UK, Italy (unclaimed royalty percentages), and Greece (university campus sales)." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Uly2cT2vc28
**Legal Protections for Sexual Orientation (Modern Day)** : Today, sexual orientation is legally protected in much of the world. Laws in many places treat anti-gay and sexist comments with the same gravity as racist and anti-Semitic insults. For example, in France, homophobic speech can result in 12 months in jail and a fine equivalent to $75,000. "In many places, the law treats anti-gay and sexist comments in the same way other laws treat racist and anti-Semitic insults." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hswtuenFwvM
**Legal Standard for Copyright Infringement** : In court cases regarding musical copyright, the judge and jury primarily consider the "note by note" comparison of songs as they appear on sheet music, rather than their recorded sound. Elements like production techniques and outboard effects that might make recordings sound similar are disregarded, which can lead to findings of non-infringement even when audible similarities exist, as in the Led Zeppelin vs. Spirit case. "The only thing that matters are just the notes, how the song looks when it's plotted out on sheet music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4cZfegexwwk
**Legality of Portable MP3 Players (June 1999)** : A court ruling in June 1999, concurrent with Napster's release, declared portable MP3 players legal. This decision, coupled with the entry of major consumer electronics companies like Apple (with the iPod in October 2001), solidified MP3s as the dominant format and signaled the obsolescence of CDs. "A court ruling came down in June of 1999, almost exactly the same time Sean Fanning set Napster free. And this court ruling asserted that portable MP3 players were in fact legal." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Legendary Gigs - Criteria** : The discussion identifies several concerts that became legendary not necessarily because of a perfect performance, but due to factors like the crowd size, the social "meeting of the minds," the historical context, or media hype. The focus is on lower-profile gigs that had subtle, long-term effects. "Maybe it was the performance, maybe it was the hype, maybe it was something completely unrelated to the music, whatever the case, they are all unforgettable." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=huAvyInzz3Q
**Leo Fender's Early Life and Career** : As a child, Leo Fender demonstrated a keen interest in disassembling and reassembling mechanical and electronic devices like radios and car parts. After losing his accounting job during the Great Depression, he supported himself by building PA systems for musicians, which eventually led him to open a radio repair shop. "As a kid, Leo Fender was always playing with things like old radios and car parts. He loved to figure out how things could be taken apart, how they worked, and how they could be put back together. After he lost his job as an accountant during the Depression, he fell back on a part-time gig he had building PA systems for his musician friends. That brought it enough money for him to open a radio repair shop." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E6cXyaHALBQ
**Leo Fender's Inability to Play Guitar** : Leo Fender, renowned as the inventor of groundbreaking instruments such as the Stratocaster and Telecaster guitars, as well as numerous iconic guitar amplifiers, was paradoxically unable to play the guitar himself. "Leo Fender, the inventor of the Stratocaster and the Telecaster guitars, as well as the seller of many iconic guitar amplifiers. He couldn't play the guitar." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0D4wGWI-_z8
**Leonard Skinnerd (Gym Teacher)** : The name of a gym teacher who inspired the naming convention of the band Lynyrd Skynyrd, a precedent that Dave Grohl used when naming his first band, HG Hancock. "Leonard Skinnerd's name is a derivation of Leonard Skinnerd, their gym teacher's name." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8ynrwaUi-sA
**Les Paul's Multitrack Recording Invention** : Les Paul invented "Sound on Sound Recording," a technique now universally known as multitrack recording. This groundbreaking innovation was developed in his home studio, which was situated in his garage. "Les Paul went on to invent Sound on Sound Recording, which is what we now call Multitrack Recording. He came up with that in the home studio in his garage." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E6cXyaHALBQ
**Leveraging Parental Contacts** : The progeny of famous parents, sometimes referred to as members of the "Lucky Spurm Club," can potentially parlay their father's contacts into successful careers, a situation that may draw criticism from those without such advantages. "You think he was able to parlay Dad's contacts into something?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dY7uB_QQWBc
**Lexus SC430 Last Model with Factory Cassette Player (2010)** : The 2010 Lexus SC430 was the last car model available to offer a factory-installed cassette player, marking the end of an era for integrated cassette playback in new vehicles. "The last model to offer a factory cassette player was the 2010 Lexus SC430." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Liam Gallagher's Paternity** : Liam Gallagher, known for his work with Oasis, has had children with several different women. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CkSIErb0hxY
**Licensing Fee Disparity** : The influx of artists seeking licensing deals led to a decrease in prices for many, with the exception of "big stars" and "big songs" like AC/DC's "Thunderstruck," which continued to command substantial fees. Indie bands saw the potential earnings from licensing shrink, though it still offered more money than they would otherwise make. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Licensing as "Free Money" for Artists** : Despite declining fees for some, licensing is often considered "basically free money" for artists because it provides an income stream and exposure that they wouldn't otherwise obtain, especially in an evolving music economy. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Lifelong Relationship with Music and Evolving Tastes** : Studies consistently show that an engagement with music in childhood lasts a lifetime. While involvement with new music may decrease with age, music remains central to life, evolving to meet changing psychological and social needs. Tastes may mellow (e.g., from Nine Inch Nails to Fleet Foxes), or individuals might seek greater complexity, challenge, and sophistication in jazz or classical music. The purpose of music shifts from identity formation to intimate relationships and personal enjoyment, focusing on positive and relaxing material chosen strictly for oneself. "The music you're into will change to meet your psychological and social needs. Your relationship with music will evolve, but you will never be able to do without music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m7MbV-eCLyE
**Lifestyle of Music Heroes** : Many music heroes lived hard lives characterized by "sex, drugs, and rock and roll," involving extensive time on the road, late nights, lack of sleep, poor nutrition, and excessive alcohol consumption. "Many of them lived hard lives filled with sex, drugs, and rock and roll." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**Lifetime Gun Ban for Selling Counterfeit Cassette Tapes (1980s)** : The US Supreme Court upheld a lifetime ban on gun ownership for a man who, in the 1980s, had been involved in selling counterfeit cassette tapes. The ruling established that this form of bootlegging constituted a crime that permanently revoked his right to own firearms. "That kind of boot lagging means he could never own a gun again." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-N6IM5Nm5iw
**Like the Wind / Blind the Wind / The Sun Will Never Shine (c. 1984)** : Dubbed "the most mysterious song on the internet," this new wave track was recorded by teenager Darius X from the West German radio station Noit Deutier Rudd Funk in the 1980s, possibly 1984, alongside artists like Depeche Mode and The Cure. Despite widespread circulation, analysis, and numerous theories—including a debunked lead involving Christian Brandy and Ronnie Irrune, and an unhelpful Shazam result "Antwon Zero One"—its true identity remains unsolved. "No one on planet Earth or in this section of the Sagittarius arm of the Milky Way has been able to identify that song so far." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UsPoPUByjYw
**Limitation of iTunes Music Store** : Despite its vast catalog, the iTunes Music Store still required users to purchase individual tracks, which was seen as a limitation compared to emerging models. "But that still doesn't quite cut it because it still involves buying this music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Limitations of 78 RPM Records Drive Innovation** : Several critical shortcomings of 78 RPM records necessitated the development of improved formats. These included low audio fidelity ("low fi"), limited capacity (maximum of about four minutes per side, requiring classical works like Beethoven's Fifth Symphony to be broken into multiple chunks), rapid wear (lucky to get 125 plays), and extreme fragility (described as "worse than glass"). "Number one, audio quality. 78s were low five. They had to sound better. Number two, capacity... The maximum amount of music per side of a 78 was about four minutes... If you got 125 plays out of a 78 RPM record, you were lucky. The damn things wore out fast. And number four, we talked about this earlier, durability. Seventy-eighths were very, very breakable. They were worse than glass." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VlIErdSSVOM
**Limited Combinations of Musical Notes in Rock Music** : There are only a finite number of ways the 12 notes of the musical scale can be combined pleasingly, and this number further reduces within the specific rules and aesthetics of rock music. This suggests that after 60 years of rock and roll, the genre may be running out of unique riffs and melodies. "Now there are only so many ways that the 12 notes of our musical scale can be combined in a pleasing manner. And since rock music has its own set of rules and aesthetics, that number falls even further." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wYD_dIvYY5o
**Limited Portable Skills for Rock Stars** : For rock stars, if their band dissolves, they often find themselves lacking other "portable skills" outside of music. This situation forces them into a "sink or swim" scenario, compelling them to pursue a solo career out of necessity. "as he rockstar, you really don't have a lot of portable skills. It becomes sync or swim as a solo artist." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-f9jhqAHkQo
**Limited Representation of Gay Characters in TV (Pre-Ellen)** : Before Ellen DeGeneres' coming out episode, only a handful of gay characters had been seen on television, and none of them held lead roles in a series. "Up to that point, only a handful of gay characters had been seen on television, and none of them were series leads." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6O5TCTO-go4
**Limited UK TV Channels (December 1976)** : In December 1976, the UK had only three television channels—BBC One, BBC Two, and the independent ITV—making TV personalities "rare things" and "big stars." "In December 1976, there were three TV channels in the UK, BBC One, BBC Two, and the independent ITV." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Limits of Western Musical Structure and Repetition** : The Western musical scale comprises only 12 notes, with a finite number of ways to combine them aesthetically and create chord changes. As the current era of popular music, dating back to early 1950s rock and roll, approaches 70 years of age, repetition and potential "stagnation" are inevitable. "There are only 12 notes in the Western musical scale and there are only so many to put those notes together in an aesthetically pleasing manner. There are finite number of chord changes..." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m7MbV-eCLyE
**Linda Stein's Tragic Death (October 30, 2007)** : Linda Stein, former co-manager of The Ramones and a successful "real estate agent to the stars," died on October 30, 2007, at the age of 62. She was found dead in her Manhattan apartment from blunt force trauma, and her death was immediately ruled a homicide. Her former personal assistant, Natavia Lowry, confessed to the murder, citing Stein's yelling as the motive, and also stole $30,000 in cash, receiving a sentence of 27 years to life, though she later recanted her confession. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PzyvXmDKFUc
**Linkin Park – Chester Bennington Named After a Lincoln Park in Santa Monica; Spelling Changed for Domain** : Chester Bennington named the band after driving past Lincoln Park in Santa Monica. The spelling change from Lincoln to Linkin was made to secure the domain name "LinkinPark.com." Note: that park in Santa Monica has since been renamed Christine Emerson Reed Park. "By the way, don't go looking for that park in Santa Monica because it's been renamed." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Lisa Marie Presley Kept Her Son's Embalmed Body at Home for Two Months** : After her son died by suicide, Lisa Marie Presley kept his embalmed body at home in his bedroom for two months, unable to have him interred. The room was kept at a steady 12 degrees to preserve the body. "She just couldn't bear to have him interned." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**List of Influential LGBTQ/Bisexual Musicians** : Many influential figures across various musical genres identify as gay or bisexual, including classical composers like Chikofsky, Handel, Schubert, George Gershwin, and Brian Epstein (Beatles manager), as well as rock stars like Freddie Mercury of Queen, Fred Schneider of B-52s, Morrissey, punk legend Bob Mold, Michael Stipe of R.E.M., pioneering rock guitarist Sister Rosetta Tharp, Janice Joplin, Joan Jett, Melissa Etheridge, Tegan and Sara, and St. Vincent. "all of the above, and many, many, many more, identify as gay or at very least bisexual." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hswtuenFwvM
**Listen.com** : In 2001, Listen.com acquired the technology and programming developed for Tune2.com, including the "Aladdin" prototype. This acquisition led to the transformation of Aladdin into Rhapsody, the first modern on-demand streaming service. "In 2001, all that tech was purchased by a company called Listen.com." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Listener Frustration with Album-Only Sales (Late 1990s)** : By the late 1990s, many music fans were increasingly frustrated with the industry's phasing out of singles, feeling forced to purchase entire albums perceived as containing only one good track among numerous undesirable ones, leading to resentment. "By this time, many people were really pissed that the recorded music industry had been phasing out singles." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Listener Skipping Behavior on Streaming Platforms** : Data analysis reveals that a significant portion of streaming listeners skip songs very quickly: 24% skip within 5 seconds, another 29% bail after 10 seconds, and 35% are gone by the 30-second mark. This 30-second threshold is critical because streaming services do not pay artists for a song until 30 seconds have passed. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Live Aid Organizer** : He was the primary organizer of the Live Aid concert. "he of the Bumpton Rats and the organizer of Live Aid." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Live Gigs as Primary Revenue for Artists** : For most artists, especially those struggling, recorded songs serve as "loss leaders" or "calling cards" to secure live gigs, which have become one of the few steady revenue streams, leading to a constant cycle of recording and touring that can lead to creative and physical exhaustion. "And playing live is one of the few steady revenue streams. Artists end up on a treadmill of recording and touring and recording and touring and before long they're creatively and physically and mentally spent." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Live Nation Entertainment Formation (February 2009)** : In February 2009, Ticketmaster, the largest ticket seller, merged with Live Nation, the largest concert promoter, to form Live Nation Entertainment. This merger significantly enhanced vertical integration by allowing the combined entity to promote shows nationally and internationally, own venues (amphitheaters), and steer managed artists (via Frontline Management) into venues with Ticketmaster contracts. "In February 2009, ticket master, the biggest seller of tickets merged with live nation, the biggest concert promoter. The new company was called Live Nation Entertainment and this resulted in even more vertical integration." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Live Nation and Ticketmaster Merger (2010)** : Live Nation, which evolved from SFX Entertainment (the largest concert promoter globally by 2000, later Clear Channel Entertainment's spin-off in 2005), acquired Ticketmaster in 2010. Ticketmaster, founded in Phoenix in 1976 and aggressively expanded under CEO Fred Rosen in 1982 by adding service charges and securing exclusive venue agreements, had become a publicly traded company by 2008. Despite regulatory concerns, the merger was approved, creating a dominant force in the live venue world. In 2024, the combined entity ran 11,203 events, sold over 118 million tickets, and owned/managed over 200 venues, effectively shaping ticket purchasing, event promotion, and the overall concert experience worldwide. "The decision was made to buy ticket master. There were regulatory concerns about this, but the merger was allowed by all the authorities. And this made the combo of Live Nation and ticket master the dominant players in all of the live venue world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xid8CZf4oYo
**Locked Grooves – Beatles Sgt. Pepper Final Chord; Flipper "Brainwash" 5.5-Hour YouTube Version; Arcade Fire The Suburbs** : A locked groove turns the final groove on a record side into a continuous loop instead of leading to the silent runoff area. Examples include the Beatles' Sgt. Pepper (the final chord of "A Day in the Life" loops forever in some editions), Sonic Youth's 1986 album Evol (track labeled "Infinity"), San Francisco band Flipper's B-side "Brainwash" (30 seconds that loops 12 times over almost 7 minutes, with a YouTube version running 5.5 hours), and Arcade Fire's The Suburbs (side three ends with a continuous loop of a piano chord from "We Used to Wait"). "There is a version on YouTube that runs five and a half hours. Somebody had way too much time." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=I291j5WBc4k
**Lockerbie Bombing (1988)** : The bombing of Pan Am Flight 103 over Lockerbie, Scotland, on December 21, 1988, stands as one of the worst acts of airline terrorism. A bomb, concealed within a cassette player, was detonated in the forward luggage compartment of a 747 traveling from London to New York. The explosion occurred at 31,000 feet, disintegrating the plane and killing all 243 passengers, 16 crew members, and 11 people on the ground. Several notable individuals, including musicians, narrowly missed the flight. "One of the worst incidents of airline terrorism was the bombing of Pan Am Flight 103 over Lockerby Scotland on December 21, 1988." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3eGBnwPWRGo
**London Gospel Choir** : The London Gospel Choir contributed to the Gorillaz album "Demon Days," along with a children's choir, adding to the diverse range of collaborators on the project. "and even the London Gospel Choir along with the Children's Choir." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iVdTCqpd798
**London Music Scene Stale - Late 1980s** : By the end of the 1980s, the music scene in London had grown "a little stale," prompting the British music press to seek out and hype new sounds from other cities like Manchester. "But by the end of the 1980s, the music scene in London had grown a little stale." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DSvoppf1dGY
**London Paper's Dismissal of Guitar Groups (Early 1960s)** : In the early 1960s, a London newspaper published a dismissive opinion, stating that "by Christmas, the public will have tired of background noise created by three guitars and a drumbeat." This reflects a broader industry and media skepticism towards guitar-centric bands, occurring around the same time The Beatles were rejected by Decca Records for similar reasons. "One of London's papers wrote, by Christmas, the public will have tired of background noise created by three guitars and a drumbeat." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Long Hair as 1960s Counterculture Signal** : With the advent of rock and roll in the 1950s, musicians began to push fashion boundaries. Long hair became one of the most visible ways for men to signal their alignment with the counterculture movement of the 1960s, a trend that intensified despite societal pushback. "Long hair was seen as one of the most obvious ways of signaling your membership in the counterculture of the 1960s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bferkEWvJg0
**Long Hair as Rebellion** : In the 1960s, following The Beatles' popularization of long hair, it became a significant symbol for revolting against conformity and identifying with the counterculture. The establishment viewed long hair on men as an insult and a sign of societal decay. "Long hair became the way to show you were revolting against conformity and that you were a member of the counterculture of the 1960s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Long History of Catalog Trading** : The practice of buying and selling music catalogs, referred to as "horse trading," is not new and has been ongoing for decades, exemplified by the complex history of The Beatles' publishing rights. "Buying and selling music catalogs is not new. This kind of horse trading has gone on for decades. For example, there was the infamous sale and resale of Beatles songs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Long Player - 1000-Year Musical Performance** : Created by Jem Finer, a member of The Pokes, "Long Player" is an ambitious musical project that uses Tibetan singing bowls to naturally generate music for one thousand years. The performance commenced on New Year's Eve 1999, is located along the Thames, and is slated to end on New Year's Day in the year 3000. "Using some Tibetan seeing bowls, he set things up in such a way that these bowls will naturally generate music for a thousand years." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HoBx6s3JmVQ
**Long-Term Artist Development in the 1990s** : The substantial financial resources available to record labels in the 1990s allowed them to invest in long-term artist development. Labels could afford to let bands cultivate their sound over multiple albums (two, three, four, five or more) without immediate pressure for a breakout hit, fostering diverse sounds and backgrounds. "The positive side of all this was that there was money for long-term development of a band." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r8AazBVoS7g
**Longest Hymn in the World** : The hymn titled "Hora Novamisa Pesema Sunt" holds the record for being the longest hymn in the world, comprising an extensive two thousand nine hundred ninety-six lines. "The longest hymn in the world is entitled, Hora Novamisa Pesema Sunt. It has two thousand nine hundred ninety six lines." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**Longest and Shortest National Anthems** : Greece is home to the world's longest national anthem, which features an impressive one hundred fifty-eight verses. In stark contrast, the Japanese national anthem is the shortest, consisting of just four lines. "The longest national anthem in the world comes from Greece. It has one hundred fifty eight verses. And just for contrast, the shortest is the Japanese national anthem, which has just four lines." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**Longest-Running Radio Programs** : Several radio programs have achieved remarkable longevity. The BBC's "Your 100 Best Tunes" aired from November 1959 to January 2007, with Alan Keith hosting for 44 years. CBC's "As It Happens" has run for over 54 years, and "Ideas" and "Cross Country Checkup" for over 58 years. According to Guinness World Records, Switzerland's "Voices of the Italian Grisones" is the longest-running factual weekly radio program at 81 years. "Saturday Children's Class" had an 86-year run. The "Grand Ole Opry" began in 1927 on WSM AM out of Nashville and is still active. The "BBC Shipping Forecast," starting audio broadcast in 1924 (telegraph in 1858), predates the Opry. "BBC's Desert Island Discs" also has an 81-year run. "There was a BBC radio show called your 100 best tunes that started in November 1959 and ran until January 2007." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bI6TTA-fn7c
**Los Angeles Punk Context** : Los Angeles provided a natural environment for punk to flourish, having long been a haven for "the alienated, the outcast and the weird." The rapid spread of New York and Sex Pistols records quickly established a local scene driven by alienation, hopelessness, and a desire to challenge the status quo and the music industry. "LA had always been a place for the alienated the outcast and the weird, so it was a natural place for punk to land." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Los Angeles Riots (1992)** : The Los Angeles riots erupted on April 29, 1992, following the acquittal of four LAPD officers in the beating of Rodney King. This period of civil unrest led to widespread looting, arson, assault, and significant property damage across the city, and is the sole subject of Sublime's song "April 29, 1992." "And April 29, 1992, from sublime, is all about the LA riots that erupted after the Rodney King trial." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4PlieAEthSg
**Los Angeles as a Creative Influence** : John Frusciante expressed that he and the band tried to be sensitive enough to incorporate the good feelings and beauty of Los Angeles and California into their music, even acknowledging the city's "seeminess." He disliked Sunset Boulevard, preferring to drive down Hollywood Boulevard for inspiration. "Yeah, there's a lot of beauty to be found in Los Angeles. There's a lot of in California. There's a lot of good feelings in the air and we try to make ourselves sensitive enough for musicians and songwriters to be able to incorporate where we live into what we do." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CPxy_KOv87o
**Loss of Context and Value in Streaming** : In the streaming era, the absence of traditional gatekeepers like radio DJs or record store clerks means there is "no one to give context to the artist, the song, the album, or the genre," contributing to music becoming "organized noise" and perceived as disposable, with its value diminishing as accessibility increases. "Without a radio DJ or a TV VJ, a writer or a trusted record store clerk, there's no one to give context to the artist, the song, the album, or the genre." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Loss of Key Musicians (Early 21st Century)** : The early 21st century experienced a period of significant loss in the music community, marked by the deaths of prominent figures such as Scott Weiland, David Bowie, Prince, Chuck Berry, Greg Allman, Chris Cornell, and Chester Bennington. "The last few years have been very rough for music fans. Scott Wyland, David Bowie, Prince, and a couple dozen more have left us." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YcdidjWaIqE
**Loss of Musicians in 2016** : The year 2016 was marked by a perceived new era in rock history due to the significant number of beloved musicians who died, including David Bowie, Glenn Fry of the Eagles, Prince, Leonard Cohen, George Michael, Keith Emerson and Greg Lake of Emerson, Lake & Palmer, Paul Cantner of Jefferson Starship, Maurice White of Earth, Wind & Fire, and Beatles producer George Martin. "But 2016 seems to have been the year, for me anyway, when I realized that many of our most beloved musicians were getting older and starting to die off. That one year we lost David Bowie, Glenn Fry of the Eagles, Prince Leonard Cohen and George Michael." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sdPeii-8Ysw
**Loss of Musicians in 2017** : The trend of significant artist deaths continued into 2017, with losses including Gord Downie, Tom Petty, Gregg Allman, Chris Cornell, AC/DC's Malcolm Young, Walter Becker of Steely Dan, and Chuck Berry. "In 2017, it was Gord Downey, Tom Petty, Greg Almond, Chris Cornell, ACDC's Malcolm Young, Walter Becker of Steely Dan and Chuck Berry, among others." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sdPeii-8Ysw
**Loss of Musicians in 2018** : The year 2018 also saw the passing of several notable musicians, such as Dolores O'Riordan of The Cranberries, Mark E. Smith of The Fall, Aretha Franklin, and Pete Shelley of the Buzzcocks. "The following year we lost the worst or reared into the cranberries, Marquis Smith of the fall of Vici, Aretha Franklin and Pete Shelley of the Buzzcocks." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sdPeii-8Ysw
**Loss of Musicians in 2019** : In 2019, the music world lost figures like Keith Flint of The Prodigy, Mark Hollis of Talk Talk, Ranking Roger of The English Beat and General Public, Ric Ocasek of The Cars, and drumming legend Ginger Baker. "Then in 2019, Keith Flynn of the Prodigy, Mark Hollis of Talk Talk, ranking Roger of the English Beat in general public, Rico Cacic of the Cars, Drumming legend, Ginger Baker." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sdPeii-8Ysw
**Lost Media Archive** : Established in 2012, this online resource is dedicated to searching for Lost Wave songs and other lost media. "Start with the Lost Media Archive, which has been looking for Lost Songs since 2012." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UsPoPUByjYw
**Lost Waves-Finist.Fandom.com** : This website provides a comprehensive list of Lost Wave songs from around the world that people are actively trying to identify. "There's also the Lost Waves-Finist.Fandom.com, which is a long list of songs from all over the world that people are trying to identify." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UsPoPUByjYw
**Lou Ottens' Compact Disk Role** : Lou Ottens, the inventor of the compact cassette, led a group of engineers at Philips in 1974 to find ways to improve the vinyl record. They initially explored a 20-centimeter disk before settling on an 11.5-centimeter diameter. This specific size was chosen deliberately, matching the diagonal width of the compact cassette, another of Philips' successful innovations from the early 1960s. `"In 2013, he acknowledged that the time for his invention had passed, and that cassettes were old and outdated and obsolete. His only regret? Seeing Sony introduced the revolutionary Walkman instead of Phillips. But before he retired, Ottens went to work on another portable music format, the compact disk, back with that at a second."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Lou Reed's Avant-Garde Influences** : When explaining his experimental double album "Metal Machine Music," Lou Reed stated he was channeling the spirit of 1960s avant-garde composer La Monte Young, whose groups included John Cale. Reed also cited an interest in Greek composer Iannis Xenakis and claimed allusions to Beethoven's work were present on the album, offering an artistic justification for his controversial noise composition. "He was merely channeling the experimental spirit of an avant-garde composer from the 1960s named Le Mans Young." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iGav9uJwA_w
**Loud Music Increases Beer Consumption Speed** : A study conducted in France investigated the relationship between music volume and drinking speed. It found that, on average, a person would drink a beer in fifteen minutes. However, if the music volume was increased, the same beer would be consumed in a quicker twelve minutes. "According to a French study, a dude will drink a beer in an average of fifteen minutes. But if you turn up the music, that same beer will go down in twelve." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**Loudest Band Measures (Historical Overview)** : Throughout concert history, various bands have pushed the boundaries of live volume. The Who set a record at 126 decibels in 1976, followed by claims of Motörhead hitting 130 decibels (causing structural damage in 1986), Leftfield reaching 137 decibels (shedding plaster in 1998), and Hanson eventually measuring 140 decibels in 1998, the highest the meter could register. "what's the loudest band has ever played?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qrrwqdpCjpQ
**Loudness Wars (Late 1990s - Present)** : The "loudness wars" describe a mastering trend, particularly prevalent with CDs, where music is excessively compressed to achieve a louder overall volume. This process squashes dynamic range, leading to clipping and distortion in the loudest parts of the audio. Notable offenders include Metallica's *St. Anger* (2003) and *Death Magnetic* (2008), Red Hot Chili Peppers' releases since 1999, Green Day's *American Idiot*, Oasis's *(What's the Story) Morning Glory?*, and Foo Fighters' *Echoes, Silence, Patience & Grace*. "This means the dynamic range is squished, and this in turn can result in clipping. That means the loudest bits end up being distorted, and this has become known as the loudness wars." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2g4QSREzpw
**Low Percentage of Women in Production and Engineering** : The music industry still suffers from a severe underrepresentation of women in production and engineering roles, with percentages estimated to be far less than 10%, possibly as low as 2%. This highlights the exceptional accomplishments of female producers like Ethel Gabriel and Sylvia Massy in a male-dominated field. "The percentage of women who work as producers and engineers is, in this no way, rounded, ismo, way less than 10% of all possessions." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oRR7N-YUcRE
**Lunar Crater Naming Rules** : On Earth's moon, craters are generally named after deceased scientists, scholars, explorers, and artists. Thousands of craters require thousands of names, providing ample opportunity for recognition. "On our moon, craters are generally named after deceased scientists, scholars, explorers, and artists." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VMPLplhDk8I
**Lute and Oud as Historical Precursors** : Over the years, prior to the development of modern guitars, instruments like the lute and the oud played significant roles in the history of stringed instruments, representing key stages in their evolution. "Over the years, there was the lute and the ood." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E6cXyaHALBQ
**Luxury Brands Benefiting from Celebrity Wear** : Luxury brands like Prada have seen their fortunes rise significantly in specific market segments when influential musicians such as Beck and Dave Grohl started wearing their products. This phenomenon highlights how young audiences are highly attentive to the fashion choices of celebrities, often driving purchasing trends. "Later, Prada, so their fortunes rise in certain segments after guys like Beck and Dave Grohl started wearing their stuff. They know that young people pay attention to what the stars are wearing when they see those pictures in music magazines and tabloids." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mhQ36Y-c9fM
**Lyrics and Interpretation** : The meaning of song lyrics can vary widely, from being nothing more than words strung together for fun or stream-of-consciousness expressions, to oblique forms of poetry open to individual interpretation. While art is often seen as open to personal meaning, some songs do have a very specific point, telling stories inspired by real-life events that composers tried to capture. "Art is supposed to be open to personal interpretation." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DAjcCk9xYWk
**MAC Cosmetics' Durability for Stage Performers** : MAC Cosmetics is a preferred brand for many performers, including Marilyn Manson and Robert Smith of The Cure. Its popularity stems from its ability to withstand the intense heat of stage lights without deteriorating. "A lot of performers like Manson and Robert Smith are the cure for Mac because it holds up nicely under hot stage lights." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WIFxAaReLn0
**MP1 Technology Overview** : MPIG Layer 1 (MP1) was an early audio compression standard that, while functional, offered suboptimal audio quality. It was limited to compressing audio for only two channels, was incompatible with higher bit rates, and lacked backwards compatibility for higher quality recordings. MP1 ultimately became the standard audio format for VHS tapes, reflecting its limitations. "MPIG Layer 1 or MP1 for short worked, but didn't offer the best audio quality. It could only compress audio for two channels, something that wasn't very helpful for things like home theater. It didn't work at higher bid rates, and it wasn't backwards compatible for higher quality recordings. MP1 became the audio standard for VHS tapes, which should tell you something." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TsiYLGGSJ2U
**MP2 Technology Overview** : MPIG Layer 2 (MP2) represented a significant improvement over MP1 and was adopted as the standard for DVDs, set-top TV boxes, and certain camcorders. Although MP2 streams were not compatible with MP1, this was generally acceptable given MP1's inferior quality. MP2 was also widely used for broadcasting radio stations, which often converted their music from WAV files to MP2 for hard drive playback systems. "MPIG Layer 2 or MP2 was much better and became the standard used for DVDs, set top TV boxes and certain camcorders. However, MP2s were not compatible with MP1 streams, which was fine for most people because MP1 sucked anyway. MP2s, however, were also quite good for broadcasting radio stations, which made the switch to hard drive playback systems for their music, often had their music converted from wave files to MP2." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TsiYLGGSJ2U
**MP3 Compression** : The core innovation of MP3 technology was its ability to compress digital audio files significantly, typically by 10 to 12 times. This compression was crucial for enabling the transmission of music over limited bandwidth connections, such as old copper telephone lines. "The algorithms used made a digital audio file 10 to 12 times smaller, which was enough for transmission down old-fashioned telephone lines." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**MP3 Compression Algorithm Development** : In the mid-1980s, research institutes began developing "codecs" – technologies based on psychoacoustics – to efficiently transmit digital audio signals over copper wires. The goal was to reduce file sizes by stripping out audio material inaudible to the human ear. The Moving Pictures Expert Group (MPEG) standardized three audio versions: MPIG Layer 1 (MP1), MPIG Layer 2 (MP2), and MPIG Layer 3 (MP3). "In the middle 1980s, a number of research institutes embarked on developing a way of sending digital signals down copper wires. These are called codecs, and we're all based on the principles of psychoacoustics. The goal of a codec was to strip out all the material from an audio file that the human ear couldn't hear anyway. That made the file smaller and therefore easier to transmit down the limited capacity of a copper wire." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TsiYLGGSJ2U
**MP3 Compression and Loss of Musical Experience** : MP3s are compressed versions of digital audio, reducing large wave files (dozens or hundreds of megabytes) to much smaller sizes (e.g., 50MB to 5MB) using mathematical algorithms and encoders. This compression strips out "invisible" music, which is theoretically undetectable to the ear. However, some believe the brain perceives MP3s differently from uncompressed sound. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHN6db7m3DM
**MP3 Development** : Carlheinz Brandenburg and his team at the Fraunhofer Institute for Digital Media Technology developed the algorithms for MP3 compression after considering Professor Selzer's rejected patent. Their breakthrough allowed digital audio files to be reduced by 10 to 12 times, making transmission over standard telephone lines feasible. "After much experimentation, Heinsb team figured it out. The algorithms used made a digital audio file 10 to 12 times smaller, which was enough for transmission down old-fashioned telephone lines." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**MP3 Development and Hockey Recordings** : During the development of the MP3 at the Fraunhofer Institute in Germany, engineers faced significant challenges with compression artifacts. To address this, they unexpectedly used recordings of a hockey game as a reference for testing. The distinct sounds of "skates on ice and sticks on pucks and pucks on boards, and the cheer of the crowd" proved exceptionally effective at revealing glitches in early versions of the MP3 encoding software, helping them refine the algorithm to near perfection. "To get MP3s to sound as good as they do, engineers used recordings of a hockey game." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7Zniri9uSlc
**MP3 Impact on Music Industry** : Carlheinz Brandenburg, co-creator of the MP3, was once told that his team had "destroyed the music industry" because the technology facilitated widespread CD ripping and illegal file sharing. This unintended consequence dramatically reshaped how music was consumed and distributed. "Someone once asked Brandon Berg if he realized what his team had created. He looked at them and said, no, what? And that person replied, you've just destroyed the music industry." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**MP3 Invention and Perfection (1987-1991)** : The technology enabling MP3s originated from German engineers using an acapella track from Suzanne Vega's 1987 album "Solitude Standing" to perfect an algorithm for shrinking audio files. This invention, initially called ISO MP1 Audio Lera 3, was perfected by 1991, making it possible to transmit music via the internet. "This new invention was called ISO MP1 Audio Lera 3, which is MP3 to you and me. That was 1991 by the time it was perfected." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**MP3 Milestones** : MP3s became the worldwide standard in 1993, evolving from files with the ".BIT" extension. The technique was formally named Motion Pictures Expert Group 1, Layer 3 (MP3). On July 4, 1994, the Fraunhofer Society released the first MP3 encoder, followed by the first MP3 player software on September 9, 1995. "MP3s were made the worldwide standard in 1993." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**MP3 Piracy Concerns** : The emergence of MP3s, combined with the burgeoning internet's ability to facilitate endless copying and global distribution, caused the recorded music industry to experience its first major panic over digital piracy. This alarm echoed previous concerns that had hampered the commercial viability of Digital Audio Tape (DAT) due to industry-mandated copy protection measures. "This had the recorded music industry freaking out for the first time over digital piracy. A similar freakout was one of the things that killed DAT tape, digital audio tape. The industry insisted upon a whole whack of copy protection measures which rendered the machines basically useless for most consumers. Now though, MP3's could be copied endlessly and sent around the planet at the speed of light." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**MP3 Psychoacoustic Compression** : MP3s employ the science of psychoacoustics to compress digital audio files, primarily by "stripping out all the data that's massed by material in the foreground," meaning data that the human ear is less likely to perceive due to masking effects. Despite its efficiency, this compression algorithm introduces audible artifacts: a "shrillness, a dissonance on the high end" and bass sounds that are "unformed, little mushy and indistinct." "The algorithm shrinks the digital file by stripping out all the data that's massed by material in the foreground." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7Zniri9uSlc
**MP3 Revenue and Fraunhofer Institute Ownership (Unspecified Year)** : The invention of the MP3 by Carl Heinz Brandenburg generated over $150 million in revenues. However, due to Brandenburg's employment status, all his inventions and associated patents legally belonged to his employer, the Fraunhofer Institute. Consequently, the institute, rather than Brandenburg himself, was the beneficiary of these substantial financial returns. "The invention of the MP3 has generated revenues of more than $150 million since it was released.
