However, since Carl Heinz Brennenberg was an employee of the Fraunhofer Institute, all his inventions and all his patents became the property of his employer." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PNiL63fY1Y8
**MP3 Technology and Ubiquity** : MPIG Layer 3 (MP3) developed from the core algorithm of MP2 and rapidly became the preferred standard for transmitting music over the internet. Currently, MP1s are largely obsolete, MP2s are primarily used in industrial applications, while MP3s are pervasive and found everywhere in digital music. "The MP2 algorithm became the core of MPIG Layer 3 or MP3. This third algorithm became the favourite standard, of course, for transmitting music over the internet. Well, there is your answer. MP1s are old and outdated and rarely used anymore. MP2s are now used mostly for industrial applications, and MP3s are everywhere." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TsiYLGGSJ2U
**MP3s and Dopamine Delay** : Neuroscientists suggest that when listening to an MP3, the brain "somehow knows that some of the music has been stripped out." As the brain attempts to internally "fill in the gaps" of missing musical information, there is a delay in the secretion of dopamine, the body's natural "feel good hormone." Consequently, listening to an MP3 "might not feel as good" or as quickly satisfying as an analog recording which retains all musical data. "Neuroscientists believe that when we hear an MP3, the brain somehow knows that some of the music has been stripped out." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7Zniri9uSlc
**MP3s and Dopamine Suppression – Compressed Audio May Reduce Emotional Response to Music** : MP3 compression technology was developed in the 1980s by the Fraunhofer Institute in Germany, shrinking audio files to one-tenth their size by stripping out audio the ear cannot hear. However, when the brain receives an MP3, it apparently detects that audio information has been stripped away and spends a few milliseconds searching for the missing material. This delay suppresses dopamine production slightly. In theory, the hierarchy of dopamine response runs: live performance > vinyl > CD > MP3. The more degraded the playback (poor earbuds, laptop speakers), the less emotional impact the music has. "MP3s cheat you out of getting the maximum emotional impact of music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NMwMwqMQpf4
**MP3s and Shortened Attention Spans (1990s)** : The rise of MP3s in the 1990s normalized track skipping as a fundamental right, which gradually contributed to shortening listeners' attention spans, leading to quick dismissal of unfamiliar or disliked songs. "When we got to MP3s in the 1990s, skipping was our God given right." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**MP3s, Piracy, and Digital Downloads Impact** : The introduction of MP3s, piracy, and legal digital music downloads further contributed to the decline in overall vinyl sales. "The introduction of MP3s, piracy and legal digital music downloads didn't help either." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**MTV Debut and First Videos (1981)** : MTV launched in 1981, famously playing "Video Killed the Radio Star" by The Buggles as its inaugural music video. The second video ever shown on the channel was "You Better Run" by Pat Benatar. "Pretty much everybody knows what the first video on MTV was when they debuted in 1981. It was video killed, the radio star for the Buggles." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gP3oQeYEvso
**MTV Launch and Early Challenges (1981)** : MTV launched on August 1, 1981, despite skepticism and initial refusals from cable companies to carry it, even in New York City. The channel started with a limited library of 250 videos, with Rod Stewart notably dominating the early programming. "Few people believe that a channel devoted to playing something called a music video, 24 hours a day, would be successful, so many cable companies refused to carry it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bYsV3ozbk4M
**MTV and MuchMusic Era** : The 1980s, characterized by the rise of MTV and MuchMusic, was a pivotal era for music videos. Videos by artists like Madonna, Peter Gabriel, Phil Collins, Michael Jackson, and Aerosmith captivated audiences with their storytelling, dancing, and style, fostering a deep appreciation for the art form. "I came up, I came up in the 80s era where that's what made me fall in love with music videos, right? The MTV, much music era, watching videos by like Madonna and Peter Gabriel and like Phil Collins and Michael Jackson and Erro Smith." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**MTV's Early Programming (August 1981)** : Upon its launch, MTV had a limited library of only 250 videos, mostly from Britain. This made British bands, particularly stylish synth-pop acts with existing performance videos, a crucial source of programming until North American labels began investing in video production. "See when MTV first went on the air in August of 1981, they had a grand total of 250 videos, most of which were from Britain. So the UK had this long history of bands performing on TV, so they had a big backlog of stuff to choose from. So until North American record labels in bands started investing in making videos, a big source of programming for MTV were clips from these stylish synth pop bands from the UK." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1KF5MwJVYng
**MTV's Impact on Music Fashion (1981)** : The arrival of MTV in 1981 dramatically altered the music industry, elevating the importance of visual presentation to the point where looking good and trendy almost overshadowed sounding good. This shift led to an explosion of a "billion different looks and styles and trends" in new rock fashion. "And then MTV arrived in 1981, which meant that looking good and trendy almost became more important than sounding good. And from there, fashion and new rock splint to a billion different looks and styles and trends." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mhQ36Y-c9fM
**MTV's Influence on Taste and Trends** : MTV, referred to as a "God" by some, held significant power to dictate musical tastes and fashion. If MTV decided a band was "the next big thing," it often became true, with people foolishly buying into the hype. However, bands lacking substance beyond this hype would falter once MTV moved on, highlighting the transient nature of media-driven fame. "They dictate to people what tastes is and they dictate what fashion is. And for the most part, people foolishly listen to them and believe it and buy it and then they are the big thing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=M3nUQtja0qc
**MTV's Launch and Music Videos** : The advent of MTV in the early 1980s marked a new era for music, making music videos a prominent and novel aspect of the industry and popular culture. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kFLMkvJAaUY
**MTV's Role in Alt-Rock (Mid-1980s)** : MTV and other channels that played music videos were instrumental in the mid-1980s for showcasing "weird new, telegenic bands from the UK." This exposure was vital for introducing these alternative artists to a wider audience in North America, complementing the growth of the alt-rock scene. "But we can't forget the role of MTV and any channel or any show that played videos from all those weird new, telegenic bands from the UK." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zB5zxycrbnY
**MTV's Role in Technopop Success** : MTV played a pivotal role in the commercial success of Technopop, particularly in America. The genre's artists often had a distinctive sense of style that translated well to television, and MTV provided the perfect platform for these performers to gain widespread exposure through their music videos. "There was no stopping electronic music, of course, especially after MTV signed on. Like new wave artists, Technopock practitioners had an interesting sense of style, which looked great on television, and MTV provided these performers with a platform in America." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5PJRCC6EwUY
**MTV/MuchMusic Era Influence on Music Videos** : The 1980s era, marked by the rise of MTV and MuchMusic, profoundly influenced a generation, fostering a deep love for music videos. Iconic videos from artists like Madonna, Peter Gabriel, Phil Collins, Michael Jackson, and Aerosmith captivated audiences with their storytelling, dancing, and style, inspiring future filmmakers like Director X. "The MTV much music era watching videos by like Madonna and Peter Gabriel and like Phil Collins and Michael Jackson and and Errol Smith." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LgJ7ww2AgF4
**MTV/MuchMusic Era Influence – 1980s** : The MTV and MuchMusic era of the 1980s was a significant period that made many fall in love with music videos. Iconic videos by artists like Madonna, Peter Gabriel, Phil Collins, Michael Jackson, and Duran Duran showcased storytelling, dancing, and style that fascinated viewers. "I came up, I came up in the 80s era where that's what made me fall in love with music videos." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OcylgiXkDP8
**Madame de Lebris' Eccentric Will (1876)** : Upon her death in Paris in 1876, Madame de Lebris bequeathed 125,000 francs to be used for buying clothing for snowmen around the city. This instruction, alongside her known habit of dressing her goldfish in miniature suits, was challenged but upheld in court, leading to Parisian snowmen being "dressed really, really well" for a period. "When she died in 1876, she left behind 125,000 francs in her will, with instructions that it be used to buy clothing for snowmen around the city." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dVJuITJyNTU
**Madonna Instrumental in Signing Deftones to Maverick Records** : Madonna played a key role in securing the recording contract of post-rock band Deftones through her Maverick record label, demonstrating an unexpected influence on the band's career. "Madonna was a key figure in Deftones' record deal through her Maverick label." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0DFmS6MPbFI
**Magnetic Recording Tape (1940s)** : The advent of magnetic recording tape in the 1940s simplified the process of making stereo recordings. "But it wasn't until magnetic recording tape came along in the 1940s that it became easier to make stereo recordings." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Magnetic Tape & Multi-track Recording Annoyed Older Generation** : The adoption of magnetic tape and the invention of multi-track recording in the late 1940s and early 1950s represented a technological innovation that significantly "annoyed the older generation." These advancements, while enabling all of today's music, challenged traditional notions of musical authenticity and performance. "This is when another technological innovation, one that made all of today's music possible, really annoyed the older generation." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Magnetic Tape Technology – Developed by Nazis, Adopted by US via Jack Mullin and Ampex** : Magnetic tape recording technology was developed in Nazi Germany and later brought to the United States by Jack Mullin, who worked with Ampex to commercialize it. This technology fundamentally transformed the capacity to record and manipulate audio and made unofficial recordings far easier to produce. "Developed by the Nazis and later adopted in the US by Jack Mullin and Ampex." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OrHtPVBjXw4
**Magnetic Tape's Impact on Recording (Post-WWII)** : Before magnetic tape, recording sessions aimed to accurately capture live performances. However, with magnetic tape, particularly the Ampex 200 reel-to-reel recorder and the advent of overdubbing by Les Paul, music production transformed. Artists could now edit recordings and create multi-layered tracks that were impossible to perform live, fundamentally changing recording technology forever. "But with magnetic tape, you could improve on reality, creating recordings that could never be performed live in real life, and recording technology has never been the same since." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**Mainstream Acceptance and Normalization** : Tattoos have become so mainstream that in some social circles, the absence of tattoos can make one an "outsider" or "weirdo." This shift occurred notably in the early 2000s, around 2001-2002, when tattoo shops became widely accessible and common, akin to Starbucks. "In fact, in some circles, if you don't have any ink, you're the outsider in Weirdo." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jZeMmPekKMQ
**Mainstream Goth Fashion (Early 1983)** : When Goth gained mainstream attention in early 1983, its adherents were characterized by their all-black attire, "deathly white power" makeup, teased black hair, dog collars, ruffled shirts, and abundant leather, maintaining a fascination with the undead. "The indie charts were suddenly filled with people dressed in black. Also sporting a deathly white power. They had teased black hair, dog collars, ruffled shirts, and of course lots of leather." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**Mainstream Industry's View of Christian Themes** : The mainstream music industry perceives explicit religious content or "God business" in music as "weird," struggling to integrate it into its conventional marketing and promotion strategies. "This God business, well that's just-that's just weird, man." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g-tBn8dsSqw
**Mainstream Music Becoming a Niche (Post-2000)** : The concept of mainstream music, once a pervasive cultural force where "everyone generally listened to the same thing" and "big stars were absolutely ubiquitous," has diminished significantly since 2000 with the rise of the internet. Previously, a limited number of gatekeepers—record labels, record stores, radio, TV video channels, and music magazines—filtered music, creating a "mono culture" and a common music vocabulary. With the internet, consumers gained "free and unfettered access" to vast amounts of music, leading to the collapse of traditional gatekeeper business models (e.g., declining physical sales, music magazine revenue). Music culture fragmented into "disconnected or barely connected, self-organizing music communities," making mainstream appeal just "another of many musical avenues and genres" without special status. "A common mainstream music culture we had before 2000 is gone." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xid8CZf4oYo
**Mainstream Music Industry Values** : The mainstream music industry typically finds success by promoting songs that deal with themes of "rebellion and sex and violence and infidelity and immorality and even criminal behavior," themes it understands and knows how to commercialize. "And billions by pushing songs about rebellion and sex and violence and infidelity and immorality and even criminal behavior." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g-tBn8dsSqw
**Mainstream vs. Weird Kids (70s/80s)** : In the 1970s and 1980s, rock fans were rigidly divided into two "tribes": those who listened to mainstream rock (e.g., Led Zeppelin, Pink Floyd) and those who preferred "weird stuff" found in niche record stores (e.g., The Smiths, Depeche Mode). Fans were expected to align with one side, reflecting a strong cultural divide in music appreciation. "In ancient days, say, oh, the 70s and 80s, people were placed into one of two tribes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ft8nruhWCas
**Majestic 12 Conspiracy Theory** : In UFO lore and legend, the Majestic 12 is a group of scientists and military types believed to possess knowledge about UFOs. Tom DeLonge claims modern-day members of this group have been in contact with him. "There's a group known as the majestic 12 which is a group of scientists and military types who know what's happening with UFOs" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pmKjZzypzvg
**Major Bands Burned by Unauthorized Leaks** : Numerous prominent bands, including Korn, U2, Snow Patrol, Metallica, Linkin Park, and Radiohead, have experienced significant frustration and financial loss due to their albums being leaked online weeks or even months before their official release dates. "Corn, U2, Snow Patrol, Metallica, Lincoln Park, and of course, Radiohead." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vt6DVj7S6nk
**Major Label Historical Role** : Historically, major labels, numbering six or seven at one point, "scooped up all the best stuff" and could afford to take chances on artists. This often left indie labels with "the drags" or music considered unsellable, creating a perceived imbalance in quality. "There was a time when the major labels back when there were six or seven of them scooped up all the best stuff and they could afford to take those kinds of chances back then. The indie labels were for the most part left with the drags or the stuff that could not be sold." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sCtQqVBtCaI
**Major Labels' Failed Digital Initiatives** : In response to the Napster crisis and widespread piracy by 2000, major record labels, initially dismissive of MP3s, found themselves technologically unprepared (most executives lacked computers). Their attempts to create their own online stores failed due to conflicting loyalties with retail partners, consumer demand for one-stop shops stocking all labels, and antitrust laws preventing a unified store (especially given past CD price-fixing scrutiny). This led to two disastrous ventures: "Press Play" (Universal/Sony), which lasted two years until March 2003, and "MusicNet" (BMG/EMI/Warner), which was sold off by 2005. Both services were criticized for being difficult to use, restrictive, and laden with complicated digital rights management (DRM). "Well, a couple of problems with that. First, the record labels had to think about their retail partners, the thousands of stores that for decades had sold their product." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nX8uAFDzDYM
**Major Labels' Financial Crisis and Reputation Decline** : The rise of file sharing and the decline in CD sales led to a financial crisis for major record labels, causing their market share to fall and the industry to shrink. This decline was perceived by many fans as "karma" for years of overpricing CDs, offering one-sided record deals that disadvantaged artists, and exhibiting an overall contempt for both artists and fans. "While the majors were being battered by file sharing and falling market share, the Indies were just bobbin and weave and do whatever they had to do to survive." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=raRCZ0Y5up8
**Major Record Labels** : Major labels are very large multinational corporations that maintain offices globally. Currently, there are three primary major labels: Universal, Sony, and Warner. These majors also own and operate numerous sub-labels or imprints, which often specialize in particular genres or niches within recorded music. "Major labels and there were just three of them are the very large multinational corporations that have offices all over the world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Major Record Labels - Definition and Consolidation** : Major record labels are defined as multinational giants that control most of the world's recorded music. Decades of mergers and consolidation have reduced their number to just four primary entities: Universal, EMI, Sony BMG, and Warner, each managing a multitude of subsidiary labels. "A major label is one of the multinational giants that control most of the world's flow of recorded music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ft8nruhWCas
**Major Shift in Music Industry (Present)** : The current era represents an unprecedented, once-in-a-lifetime transformation in how music is created and consumed, constituting a new reality for the industry. "We are seeing a once in a lifetime major shift in music creation and consumption." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Major vs. Indie Label Sales** : Major record labels possess extensive marketing and distribution power globally, allowing their releases to achieve sales in the tens of millions, such as AC/DC's "Back in Black" selling 45 million copies. In contrast, independent labels face significant struggles with worldwide marketing and distribution, making sales of even one million copies for an indie release a highly momentous achievement. "Sales for major label releases are in the tens of millions. ACDC's Back in Black, for example, has sold 45 million copies. See, major labels have the cloud to market and sell records and territories around the world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=T1XSJIm0Nh4
**Malcolm Gladwell's 10,000-Hour Rule** : Hugh Dillon invoked Malcolm Gladwell's concept of 10,000 hours of dedicated practice to achieve mastery, applying it to his own intensive immersion in music through obsessive record collecting and listening. This dedication fostered a deep, almost innate knowledge of rock and roll that aids his songwriting. "I am of the Malcolm Gladwell 10,000 hours because I grew up just obsessively listening to records." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=y7kmkWJ54ww
**Management Company - Gold & Gray** : Alan Gray, a conservative political figure and music enthusiast, was a partner in a management company with Jake Gold. This company signed The Tragically Hip after being impressed by their demos and live performance. "In fact, he was partners in a management company with a guy named Jake Gold." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oMBmdfrA47Q
**Manager Scott Roger's Strategy** : Scott Roger, Arcade Fire's manager, is acknowledged for enabling the band to operate "way above their pay grade." He achieved this by strategically enlisting the right partners to ensure their music reached a wide audience, notably securing a major label distribution deal and arranging an unprecedented SNL broadcast event for the "Reflektor" album. "Their manager is a dude named Scott Roger, and he freely admits that Arcade Fire has a band that is operating way above their pay grade, and that by enlisting the right partners, they can get their music heard." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ir2FfLQJNtY
**Managerial Career Path from Film to Music** : Paul McGuinness, U2's long-term manager, began his career as a film technician in Ireland and the UK, even appearing in the credits of the 1974 sci-fi film *Zardoz*. His heroes were managers like Brian Epstein, Andrew Luke Oldham, and Albert Grossman, and he was drawn to music management for its creative potential to build something with "cultural impact" despite not being a musician himself. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PzyvXmDKFUc
**Managerial Discretion and Professionalism** : John Silva, who runs Silver Artist Management (managing Foo Fighters, Queens of the Stone Age, Beck, and many others), is characterized by his and his staff's extremely low-key approach. There is very little public information about Silva, who prioritizes his clients above personal publicity, rarely doing interviews, posing for photos, or seeking headlines. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PzyvXmDKFUc
**Managerial Longevity and Sale of Company** : Paul McGuinness demonstrated significant longevity in music management, directing U2's career for four decades through his company, Principal Management. He eventually stepped down from managing U2 in 2014 and sold Principal Management to Live Nation, later transitioning to work primarily in television. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PzyvXmDKFUc
**Managerial Prominence and Reputation** : Managers are considered the most important people in any musical organization, second only to the artists themselves. Their impact can be so significant, whether positive or negative, that some managers achieve a level of fame almost comparable to their clients, leading to much written about their careers. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PzyvXmDKFUc
**Managerial Reputation and Fear Tactics** : Some managers gain notoriety for their intimidating and aggressive methods. Peter Grant, Led Zeppelin's manager, was known for protecting the band with "fear and constant threats of violence." Similarly, Don Arden, who managed acts like Black Sabbath and Air Supply, earned monikers such as the "English Godfather" and the "Al Capone of Pop" for his tough approach. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PzyvXmDKFUc
**Managerial Succession and Impact** : The transition of managers can have profound effects on a band's trajectory. After Brian Epstein, who guided The Beatles from a local bar band to global stardom, died in 1967, he was succeeded by Alan Klein. Klein, who also managed The Rolling Stones, is noted for playing a role in the breakup of The Beatles, underscoring the critical influence a manager can wield over a group's fate. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PzyvXmDKFUc
**Managerial Work-Life Balance and Death** : Rob Gretton, a central figure in the Manchester punk and dance scene who managed Joy Division and New Order for 20 years, balanced his management duties with other roles such as a baggage handler at Manchester Airport and a DJ. He was widely regarded as kind and generous, but died prematurely on May 15, 1999, at the age of 46, due to a heart attack. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PzyvXmDKFUc
**Manchester's Cultural Pillars** : Growing up in Manchester, three things were paramount to the city's identity: music, football, and fashion. These three elements were considered equally important and intertwined, mutually influencing each other. "when we grew up, it was, there's three things, it's the older tunit, it's a, it's music, it's football, it's fashion, then three things are really important, no more important than the other, and the go together, the go together." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KF_adyXf86Q
**Manchester's Cultural Transformation (Early 1980s)** : In the early 1980s, Manchester underwent a significant cultural shift, moving from a "drab, Victorian industrial city" to a much more vibrant hub. The city's music scene, influenced by bands from the original punk era like the Buzzcocks, The Fall, and Joy Division, and later The Smiths and New Order, was instrumental in this transformation. "In the early 80s, the city of Manchester was undergoing some kind of cultural transformation." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2RKchFW42Jc
**Manchester's Enduring Musical Legacy** : Manchester has consistently been a significant musical hub, producing a wide array of influential artists and bands across different genres and eras, from the Bee Gees and Davy Jones (The Monkees) to Graham Nash, Buzzcocks, The Fall, James, Godley & Creme, Badly Drawn Boy, Elbow, John Mayall, Starsailor, The Verve, Phil Lynott, The Smiths, and Simply Red. "mancise has always been, I don't know why mancise has been this great musical set, you know, bands from, you know, from the Bee Gees to, you know, the, you know, the singer of David Jones, the monkeys." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KF_adyXf86Q
**Manchester's Political and Economic Identity** : Manchester is characterized as a "very left wing working class city" with considerable wealth, despite the decline of its industrial age, setting it apart from cities like Liverpool which "got left behind." "it's a very, it's a very, or left wing working class city." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=q_Scyr2czh8
**Manchester's Portrayal in UK Media** : Even after the Madchester era, Manchester and its "Mancunian" stereotype continue to be a common setting and subject for musicians, TV shows, and comedy shows in the UK, such as "Shameless." "every TV show, every comedy show in the UK, if you want to portray any musician, if it's all setting man to something, everything's setting man to do." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KF_adyXf86Q
**Manchester's Urban Transformation** : Manchester has undergone significant gentrification and urbanization, now featuring three universities (University of Manchester, Manchester Metropolitan University, University of Salford), Europe's largest student population, and the biggest gay community outside London. It has become a European city, often called the "capital of the north" or "London of the north," though its past "greatness" is perceived by some as having changed. "it's got three universes, Manchester, Manchester, Met and Sulford, it's had the biggest gay community outside London, biggest student population in Europe." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KF_adyXf86Q
**Mancunian "Not Enjoying It" Attitude** : There is a specific English, and particularly Mancunian, working-class attitude that dictates it's "not cool" to appear to be enjoying success or public attention too much. This can manifest as downplaying achievements or even feigning disinterest. "Sean's got this thing where he doesn't like to enjoy it. If he looks like he's enjoying it, he's not, that's a real man's to think, is where he got a lot of you not enjoying it, if you're enjoying it, it's not very cool, you know." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KF_adyXf86Q
**Mandate for Artists to Use Streaming and YouTube** : For contemporary artists, having music on streaming platforms is essential, as is maintaining a presence on YouTube, which remains the single largest global source for music discovery; absence from YouTube effectively means non-existence to a significant portion of the audience. "If you are an artist, you have to stream your music. And you have to get something on YouTube too, because that's still the biggest source of music discovery on the planet." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Manie's Music - Iconic New York Store (1935-2009)** : Manie's Music, located on West 58th between 6th and 7th avenues in New York City, was a legendary music store founded in 1935 by Manie Goldrich and later run by his son Henry. It was known for stocking the greatest musical instruments and accessories, becoming the go-to place for both local and touring musicians, including The Beatles, Pete Townshend, Bob Dylan, and the place where David Gilmour bought his "Black Strat." The store closed in 2009. "Manie's music on West 58th between 6th and 7th avenues was the place everyone in New York City went for their gear, starting in 1935." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6SdPPsmpgJg
**Manipulating Musical Taste** : Researchers at McGill University in Montreal made a discovery that musical taste can be manipulated within minutes using a technique called transcranial magnetic stimulation (a "magnetic zap") to the front of the brain. This method could either increase or decrease the amount of enjoyment individuals derived from music, a process believed to involve the regulation of dopamine within the brain, though the precise mechanism is not yet fully understood. "musical taste can be manipulated in minutes just by a little magnetic zap to the front of the brain." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**Many Worlds Theory (Hugh Everett III)** : Physicist Hugh Everett III developed this theory, also known as the relative state formulation of quantum mechanics, which posits the existence of an infinite number of parallel universes. In this theory, every possible event occurs in an identical street in a different universe, and while they exist alongside ours, they are unobservable to us. "He was a physicist who came up with something called the Many Worlds Theory." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QfiRRtsZoYU
**Many's Music Store (New York City)** : Jimi Hendrix purchased his first Big Muff pedal at Many's Music Store in New York City. This store served as the point of acquisition for one of the most iconic guitarists to adopt the innovative pedal, further solidifying its early reputation. "One of the first people to buy one was Jimmy Hendrix, who walked into many's music store in New York City and loved what he could do with it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iGav9uJwA_w
**March 9, 2017, 30th Anniversary of The Joshua Tree Release** : The 30th anniversary of U2's iconic album, The Joshua Tree, fell on March 9, 2017. "March 9, 2017 was the 30th anniversary of the release of the Joshua Tree." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VR5Fncdc2kQ
**Marfan Syndrome** : Marfan Syndrome is a genetic condition that impacts an individual's joints, muscles, and tendons. Joey Ramone is believed to have potentially suffered from this syndrome, contributing to his overall health profile. "He also probably suffered from Marfan Syndrome, a genetic condition that affects joints, muscles, and tendons." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Margaret Thatcher's Polarizing Figure (1980s)** : Margaret Thatcher, often referred to as the "Iron Lady," was a deeply polarizing figure in 1980s Britain. Her right-wing policies and austerity measures were lauded by those on the political right but despised by those in the center or on the left. Her tenure inspired a multitude of anti-Thatcher songs, both during and after her time in office, reflecting the intense opposition she faced. "Mrs. Thatcher could be polarizing even on her own side of the political spectrum. But if you were in the center or on the left, you despised her." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0ARYt4sYT90
**Mariel Boatlift - 1980 Cuban Criminals** : In 1980, Fidel Castro initiated the Mariel boatlift, an event where he emptied Cuban prisons by placing approximately 2,000 individuals with serious criminal records onto boats and sending them towards Florida, including individuals like Jesus Mezquia. "Jesus Mesquia was a real pony Montana kind of guy. One of the criminals shipped out of Cuba during the burial boat lift in 1980. That's when Fidel Castro made room in his prisons by putting about 2,000 really bad people on boats and shoving them towards Florida." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZcYKZ5ovoy4
**Mark Coyle – Oasis Sound Guy** : Mark Coyle served as the sound guy for Oasis. Along with roadie Phil Smith, he contributed a significant prop to the "Definitely Maybe" album photoshoot: a blow-up globe, which was intended to symbolize the band's ambitious aspirations for "world domination." "Mark Coil, the sound guy for Oasis, and a roadie named Phil Smith found it and brought it along to signal that the bang was bound for world domination." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Market Fragmentation of Digital Players Before iPod** : Before the iPod's widespread adoption, the market for digital music players was fragmented by various proprietary formats. Sony employed its ATRAC codec, while Windows-based players often used the WMA format, leading to compatibility issues and complexity for consumers. "Sony had a couple of digital players, but they used a proprietary codec known as ATRAC, not MP3s. Windows-based players used a format called WMA." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**Marketing Prioritization Based on Streaming Revenue** : The recorded music industry directs its marketing dollars towards genres that generate the most revenue. Since streaming provides over half of total revenues and is dominated by pop, R&B, and hip-hop, labels primarily focus their marketing money and attention on these genres, leaving less for rock. "So streaming, generating more than half your revenues. And if pop, R&B, and hip-hop songs are behind those streams, where do you think the labels are going to focus their money and attention?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Mars Ancient Alien Civilizations** : Tom DeLonge believes that fossilized remains of ancient alien civilizations might be discovered on Mars. "He thinks that will find fossilized remains unmarried quite possibly of ancient alien civilizations" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pmKjZzypzvg
**Mars Express Mission and Beagle 2** : This European Space Agency project aimed to send a probe to Mars, carrying a Mars lander named Beagle 2. Blur helped raise money and did PR for the mission, which unfortunately failed when the craft crashed, preventing an instrumental call sign recorded by the band from being successfully transmitted to Earth. "They spent the next few years helping to raise money and doing PR for what was known as the Mars Express mission, which carried a Mars lander called Beagle 2." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jMfH9xAOpI
**Marshall Allen – Debut Album at Age 100 (2025)** : Saxophonist Marshall Allen released his debut album in 2025 at the remarkable age of 100. "Launched his debut album at the surprising age of 100." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0DFmS6MPbFI
**Marshall Stack's Origin from Bass Amplification** : The development of the Marshall stack, the most iconic guitar amp setup, is directly linked to the Fender Bassman amplifier. Jim Marshall used the Bassman as a starting point for his designs, making the bass player's need for a dedicated amp an unexpected catalyst for a guitar amplification revolution. "And that's because without the Fender Basement, there would be no Marshall stack, which is the most iconic of all the guitar amp setups." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Y0HW8LQKvJw
**Martian Crater Naming Rules** : Craters on Mars are typically named after deceased scientists and writers who have made contributions to the study and lore of Mars. This rule generally guides the recognition of individuals on the red planet. "The rule for Mars is similar. Dead scientists and writers who have contributed to the study and lore of Mars." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VMPLplhDk8I
**Martin Shkreli's CD Purchase and Confiscation (2015-onward)** : Disgraced pharmaceutical CEO Martin Shkreli purchased the sole copy of the Wu-Tang Clan's "Once Upon a Time in Shaolin" CD for $2 million in 2015, only for it to be confiscated by the U.S. government after his arrest and conviction for securities fraud, eventually selling for $4 million to an NFT company. "And the buyer ended up being disgraced farmer bro Martin Shrekeli, the guy who became famous or infamous, I guess, by hiking the price of an anti-HIV drug by 5,000 percent just because he could." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tbmssCZ05ro
**Martin Shkreli's Purchase of _Once Upon a Time in Shaolin_** : Martin Shkreli acquired the sole copy of Wu-Tang Clan's CD album _Once Upon a Time in Shaolin_ for $2 million US at auction. Shkreli had previously gained notoriety for raising the price of an AIDS drug by 5,000%. "The buyer turned out to be Martin Shikreli. The farmer bro became very popular after he raised the price of an AIDS drug by 5,000%." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IIJySSo276k
**Marty's Guitar Torture (Back to the Future)** : In the movie "Back to the Future," the character Marty McFly "tortured" his future dad with uncredited Eddie Van Halen-esque guitar sounds in a scene set in 1955. This cinematic portrayal highlights a time even before distortion became a significant element in modern music. "Remember when Marty tortured his future dad with some uncredited Eddie Van Halen and back to the future?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iGav9uJwA_w
**Mary Shelley's "Frankenstein" (1818)** : Mary Shelley wrote her novel "Frankenstein" in 1818, and her contemporary fashion styles from that era influenced the early Goth aesthetic. "The type of clothes Mary Shelley probably was wearing when she wrote Frankenstein in 1818." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**Mary Shelley's Frankenstein and Gothic Themes (1818)** : Mary Shelley's novel "Frankenstein," published in 1818, is considered the "genesis of everything goth." It pioneered the blending of horror, death, romance, science fiction, and the macabre in a dark, spooky way, laying foundational themes for later gothic subcultures. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Masaru Ibuka and Sony's Transistor Radio** : Masaru Ibuka, head of a Japanese company that initially made rice cookers (Tokyo Telecommunications, later Sony), discovered that AT&T was licensing its transistor technology. His company, inspired by the Regency TR-1, set about building its own transistor radio, the TR-55. Upon renaming to Sony, the company entered the market in August 1955 with a unit priced at $40, which later dropped to $25 and by 1962, to $15, making portable radios widely affordable. `"Mairasaro Abuqi, the head of a company that made rice cookers in Japan, discovered that AT&T, the inventor of the transistor, was licensing the technology to other companies."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Mass Extinction of Big Chain Record Stores** : Starting around 2000, the recorded music industry experienced a "mass extinction event" with the widespread closure of big chain record stores globally. This was driven by the public's shift from physical media to online music access. Major chains like Tower Records (closed all but 1-2 Japan stores by 2006), the Musicland Group (including Suncoast and Sam Goody, bankrupt in 2006), Sam The Record Man (defunct by 2007 except one store), and Virgin Megastores (gone by 2009) disappeared. Record sections also vanished from Best Buy and department stores. The only "bright spots" were HMV's resurrection by Canadian entrepreneur Doug Putnam (who also bought Sunrise Records) and Rough Trade Records maintaining several locations. Most physical record retailing now belongs to independent stores. "This really was a mass extinction event as people moved away from physical media over to online access for music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZzIqIydZTlA
**Mass Extinction of Iconic Musicians – Accelerated Since 2015–2016** : The deaths of iconic musicians have accelerated notably since late 2015 and 2016, when losses including Scott Weiland, Lemmy Kilmister, David Bowie, Prince, Glenn Frey, and George Michael collectively brought mortality awareness to rock culture. This led the industry to begin preparing obituaries in advance. At least one third of a prepared bank of around one hundred such obituaries had already been used. "At least a third of a hundred prepared obituaries had already been used." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZPlsR7_WiZQ
**Massive Annual Revenues (2024)** : YouTube's annual revenues are well over $30 billion US dollars, solidifying its position as a major commercial force in the digital landscape. "Annual revenues are well beyond $30 billion US dollars." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Massive Attack's Robert Del Naja – Strong Theory He Is Banksy** : Massive Attack member Robert Del Naja is the subject of a strong ongoing theory that he is the mysterious and anonymous street artist known as Banksy, whose work sells for millions but whose identity remains unknown. Other theories point to someone born Robin Gunningham or an ex-town councillor from a small town in Wales named Billy Ganon. "There is a very strong theory that he is the anonymous and mysterious artist known as Banksy." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DiyIPBKiVW4
**Massive Dick (Whale)** : In Gorillaz lore, the character 2D was swallowed by a whale named Massive Dick during the band's hiatus. "2D was swallowed by a whale called Massive Dick and Russell, who ended up becoming a giant as part of the plastic beach story." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iVdTCqpd798
**Matt Bellamy (Muse)** : Matt Bellamy, a member of Muse, is mentioned as a benchmark for guitar prowess, used in comparison to St. Vincent's skill. "If there's a female equivalent to Mewz's Met Bellamy, it's probably her." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oLC49nCf0ZM
**Matt Farley – 25,000 Songs, All About Poop and Farts** : Matt Farley is a songwriter who has written more than 25,000 songs, every single one about excrement or flatulence. All are posted on Spotify (each approximately one minute long), and he earns streaming royalties from all of them. "He writes about poops and farts." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Maturation of Musical Tastes (Mid-30s)** : Social scientists observe that by their mid-30s, people's musical tastes have generally "matured." This is linked to one of the five personality traits, "openness to experience," which tends to be higher in youth. As individuals age, they become more discerning, moving beyond "Top 40" pop to seek music that is "cooler, more substantial, more meaningful, more complex, and more cool." "But by the time we're in our mid-30s, the social scientists say that our tastes have matured." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m7MbV-eCLyE
**Max Matthews and Bell Labs' IBM 704 (1957)** : At Bell Labs in 1957, acoustics researcher Max Matthews used an IBM 704 mainframe to capture violin tones, a breakthrough that led to the development of his "Music" synthesis program. This program generated a 17-second tune, marking the beginning of computer-generated music creation, listening, and distribution. "A researcher of acoustics named Max Matthews got some time on the machine and started the world on the road to the creation, making, listening to, and distribution of computer-generated music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Maximum CD Song Length** : When the Compact Disc (CD) was introduced in 1982, its maximum running time was established to be between 74 and 80 minutes. While a popular legend attributes this capacity to the chairman of Sony wishing to fit his favorite Beethoven symphony entirely, this story is not true. "But when the CD was introduced in 1982, that was a little bit different. According to legend, the chairman of Sony, one of the developers of the compact disc, decreed that the CD must be able to hold his favorite Beethoven symphony in its entirety. 74 minutes of uninterrupted Ludwig Van. The story isn't true, but it is true that the maximum running time of a CD is between 74 and 80 minutes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=T1XSJIm0Nh4
**Maximum Cassette Song Length** : A C120 cassette offered the technical possibility of songs up to 60 minutes per side. However, the tape used for C120 cassettes was very thin and prone to stretching, which could cause distortion, leading to this capacity rarely being utilized for extended single tracks. "The cassette offered possibilities of even longer songs. A C120 cassette offered a maximum of 60 minutes per side." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=T1XSJIm0Nh4
**Maximum Rock and Roll Publication** : This new publication, based out of San Francisco, played a key role in the early identification and naming of pop punk. When writing about melodic punk bands like Social Distortion or The Vandals, *Maximum Rock and Roll* began to frequently use the term "pop punk." "This music started to get coverage in a new publication based out of San Francisco, called Maximum Rock and Roll." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tZV1lz-obQE
**Maximum Vinyl Song Length** : In the era of vinyl albums, the maximum length of a song was constrained by the physical capacity of an album's side, typically allowing for tracks of approximately 22 to 25 minutes. "Back in the day, the maximum length of a song was determined by the medium on which it was stored. In the case of a vinyl album, a song could be no longer than the maximum length of an album's side." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=T1XSJIm0Nh4
**Mayan Long Count Calendar End Date (December 21, 2012)** : December 21st, 2012, was widely anticipated due to the ending of the Vaktun cycle of the Mayan Long Count Calendar, after 5125 years. This date generated debate about potential global events, ranging from the end of the world to a new era of spiritual enlightenment. "If the Doom Sairs are correct, something monumental, something transformative is going to happen on December 21st, 2012. This is the day the Vaktun cycle ends." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Mayonnaise Brand Commercial Offer - Black Keys Rejection** : A UK-based mayonnaise manufacturer offered The Black Keys 200,000 pounds for a song for a TV commercial. The band turned it down, fearing it would harm their anti-sell-out image, despite financial hardship. "The manufacturer of a brand of mayonnaise, based on the UK, offered the keys 200,000 pounds for the use of one of their songs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PmwfC4HR3_I
**McCarthyism's Influence on Moral Panic** : The climate of fear created by McCarthyism, rooted in the belief that Communists were embedded in government and sought to undermine the American way, significantly contributed to a broader moral panic. This pervasive anxiety laid the groundwork for ongoing moral panics in the country, including those concerning music and cultural threats. "After the panic of McCarthyism, the idea that Communists been undestroying the American way were embedded in government and other positions of power, put the country on a road to a moral panic that continues to be felt today." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=41eKVp8JIhU
**McDonald's Flexi-Disc Contest (1988)** : In 1988, McDonald's hosted a contest featuring a flexi-disc with school children singing "The Menu song." The grand prize of one million dollars was awarded if the flexi-disc contained the entire song. With odds of approximately one in 80 million, the winning flexi-disc was discovered by a 13-year-old boy in Virginia within a pile of newspapers destined for burning. "In 1988, McDonald's had a contest involving a flexi disk, where the grand prize was a million dollars." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2g4QSREzpw
**Mechanical Fade-Outs in Early Recordings (1894)** : Early recordings achieved fade-outs mechanically, such as an 1894 Berliner Disc of a Fife and Drums Corps playing "Yankee Doodle Dandy" which faded as the band moved away from the recording equipment. At this time, there were no electronic volume controls; everything was mechanical. "There's an 1894 Berliner Disc of a Fife and Drums Corps playing Yankee Doodle Dandy that fades into nothing, and the only way that that could have been accomplished is to have the band move away from the recording equipment until they couldn't be heard." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gP3oQeYEvso
**Mechanical Recording Limitations (Pre-Mid-1920s)** : Before the mid-1920s, sound recording was a purely mechanical process. It involved musicians and singers performing around a large, horn-shaped sound collector that funneled sound to a needle. This needle vibrated and cut a groove into a rotating disc made of soft material, which served as the master. This method produced awful sound quality, offered no editing, overdubs, or special effects, and required restarting from scratch if any mistake occurred. "Up until the mid-1920s, recording sound was a very mechanical process." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PNiL63fY1Y8
**Mechanical Royalty Definition** : A mechanical royalty is the income an artist earns when their song is included on a physical product, such as a compact disc, and from the subsequent sales generated by that CD. "That's what an artist makes for having a song included on a CD and whatever sales that CD achieves." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Mechanization of Metal Flattening (Late 17th Century)** : Attempts to mechanize metal flattening date back to Greece in the 1st century AD, but it wasn't until the late 17th century that water-powered mills became effective. These mills used large rotating iron cylinders to flatten hunks of metal, improving efficiency and reducing the manual labor previously required. "But it wasn't until the late 17th century that mills, using the power of water, were used to flatten hunks of metal using big rotating cylinders made of iron." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**Medical Research on Infant Rhythm Development** : Medical research suggests that bouncing a baby on one's knee or against the chest while music plays can instill a sense of rhythm. This activity is believed to give the child a better than normal chance of appreciating beats and potentially developing an interest in dancing later in life. "medical research says you should bounce the baby on your knee or against your chest while music plays. That'll give the kid a better than normal chance of appreciating beats and maybe want to dance later in life." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**Medieval Church Control over Music and Lyrics** : During medieval times, the Church exerted significant control over both the lyrical content and musical context. Any music or lyrics that did not align with the prevailing religious doctrine were actively suppressed. This practice continued through the Reformation and Counter-Reformation, during which morally objectionable songs were condemned or outright banned. "In medieval times, the church tried to control the content and context of lyrics and music as well as the music itself. If it wasn't in line with the religious doctrine of the day, you can bet that it would be suppressed." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=41eKVp8JIhU
**Meet the Barkers Reality TV Show** : "Meet the Barkers" was an MTV reality show that chronicled the lives of Travis Barker and his wife, Shannon Mochler. The show's filming extended into private spaces, including the Blink-182 rehearsal room, which was a source of discomfort for band member Tom DeLonge. "That was an MTV show that followed Travis and his wife Shannon Mochler everywhere, including into the Blink rehearsal space." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q-QP7ePxkQE
**Meeting Dolly Parton and Emmylou Harris** : Members of 5440 had the opportunity to meet legendary musicians Dolly Parton and Emmylou Harris, an experience noted as a significant highlight. "Meeting dolly parton and Emily Harris. Yeah, yeah, that was certainly." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=W9gdKha0PLg
**Meeting Ronnie Wood** : A member of 5440 had a memorable night hanging out with Ronnie Wood, describing the experience as "legendary" even given Wood's age at the time. "hanging out with Ronnie Wood for a night was legendary Even at his age." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=W9gdKha0PLg
**Mel Ramos – Artist of "Evil Empire" Painting** : Mel Ramos was the artist responsible for the painting of the boy featured on Rage Against the Machine's "Evil Empire" album cover. Ramos achieved significant fame in the art world, with some of his works selling for well over a million dollars. The painting itself was originally a birthday gift for Ari Messle. "That painting was completed by artist Mel Ramos, who went onto fame in the art world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Melodic Appropriation in Songwriting** : The practice of giving songwriting credits to other artists due to melodic similarities in new songs was highlighted by several instances involving The Strokes. Billy Idol and Tony James of Generation X received credit for "Bad Decisions" due to its resemblance to "Dancing With Myself," and Richard and Tom Butler of The Psychedelic Furs received credit for "Eternal Sunday" due to its similarity to "The Ghost In You." Henry Mancini also received a credit for "Juice Box" because of its likeness to the "Peter Gunn Theme." "We'll revisit that topic of melodic appropriation a little bit later on." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jEfofyobGiI
**Melody Maker Magazine - January 1972** : In January 1972, *Melody Maker* magazine published an interview in which David Bowie publicly professed to be gay. This declaration was a major media storm, overshadowing his earlier instances of wearing dresses, and significantly impacted his public image and the course of rock music. "The big media storm came when Bowie publicly professed to be gay in an interview in Melody Maker magazine in January of 1972." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0xd0Hf5REg4
**Meme as a Cultural Genetic Unit** : A meme is defined as a piece of "culture genetics," analogous to a gene in a cell, that reproduces itself and transmits information. It encompasses words, catchphrases, behaviors, concepts, thoughts, theories, or habits that spread through popular culture, moving from person to person until it becomes part of everyday language. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHN6db7m3DM
**Memphis, Tennessee Recording Session (1951)** : Around March 1, 1951, Ike Turner and his band were traveling along Highway 61 to a recording session in Memphis, Tennessee. During this journey, the guitar amplifier belonging to Willie Kitsert was damaged, either by falling off the car or being dropped from the trunk during a flat tire stop. This accidental damage led to the discovery of the fuzzed guitar sound heard on "Rocket 88." "Sometime around March 1, 1951, Ike Turner, future husband of Tina, was driving with his band along Highway 61 on their way to a recording session in Memphis, Tennessee." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iGav9uJwA_w
**Mental Health Awareness and Support** : The tragic death of Chester Bennington highlights the critical need to discuss mental health issues, including depression and addiction. Warning signs are often subtle, and individuals may endure immense pain silently. It is essential to seek help without shame and for others to offer understanding and support rather than simplistic advice. "If there's one thing that we can learn from Chester Bettington, it's that we need to talk about mental health issues, things like depression and addiction." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qQm7u3W13eM
**Mercedes-Benz First In-Dash CD Player (1984)** : In 1984, Mercedes-Benz became the pioneering auto manufacturer to offer an in-dash compact disc player in its vehicles. Other car manufacturers subsequently followed suit, and by the end of the 1990s, CD players were practically standard in cars, often including multi-disc changers. "In 1984, Mercedes-Benz became the first auto manufacturer to offer an in-dash CD player." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Mercer Arts Center Collapse (August 1974)** : The Mercer Arts Center, part of the Broadway Central Hotel, was a hangout for "weirdo" bands like The New York Dolls. On a Friday in August 1974, the building tragically collapsed into the street, killing four people and injuring 19, leaving the burgeoning music scene without a central gathering place and driving artists to venues like CBGB. "On Friday in August of 1974, the building creaked and cracked and then collapsed into the street. Four people died and 19 people were hurt. And that was the end of the Mercer Art Center." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VYi3r0G-ne4
**Mercurian Crater Naming Rules** : Craters on Mercury must be named after "famous deceased artists, musicians, painters, and authors." As scientists are still extensively mapping Mercury, only about a hundred craters have been named, predominantly after classical personalities, though this is beginning to change. "The rule for craters on Mercury is that they must be named after, quote, famous deceased artists, musicians, painters, and authors." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VMPLplhDk8I
**Mesoamerican Temples as Ancient Loudspeakers** : Archaeological research suggests that ancient stone temples in Mexico, such as those at Palenque, Tulum, and Chichen Itza, were deliberately designed approximately 1400 years ago to acoustically amplify and project sounds over vast distances. These monumental structures functioned as effective, unplugged public address systems for various events, including ritual ceremonies, political gatherings, sporting events, and musical performances featuring drums, shells, and flute-like instruments. "Archaeologists now think that the stone temples at Plenke were designed to amplify and project sound across huge distances. And this included music from drums or shells or flute-like instruments. This was 1400 years ago. An efficient and completely unplugged PA system for what were essentially concerts, festivals." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0D4wGWI-_z8
**Metadata in Digital Music** : Metadata refers to "data within data," which is invisibly embedded within digital music files. This includes essential attributes such as the song title, artist name, album title, music genre, track number, release year, associated artwork, lyrics, liner notes, composer, record label, publisher, file creation date, and the unique ISRC number. "metadata is data within data." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Metallica Bassist Audition - "Too Good"** : Les Claypool was reportedly offered the position of bassist in Metallica after Cliff Burton's death but did not get the job because, as the story goes, he was deemed "too good" for the role. "Now, there's a story that says he was offered the job of bassist in Metallica after the death of Cliff Burton, but he didn't get the job because, and this is a quote, he was too good." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kbFfT0pKchI
**Metallica – Ron Quintana Chose "Metal Mania" Instead; Left "Metallica" for Lars** : The name was conceived by Lars Ulrich's friend Ron Quintana, who was starting a fanzine and had narrowed his choices to "Metal Mania" and "Metallica." He chose Metal Mania. Lars kept Metallica for his new band. "He chose Metal Mania in the end for his fan scene, leaving Metallica for Lars' new group." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Metaphorical A-sides and B-sides in Digital Releases** : Even in the digital era, some artists and labels, such as The Smashing Pumpkins and MGMT, metaphorically refer to concurrent digital track releases as A-sides and B-sides. This often involves releasing two songs simultaneously, with a subtle hint that one track is deemed slightly more important or commercially viable than the other. "And there is still some momentum from the old days with artists and labels referring to releases as A-sides and B-sides in a metaphorical way more than anything else." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**Metric – Named After a Keyboard Preset** : The band was previously called Mainstream but changed it after one EP to Metric, named after a preset on one of keyboardist Jimmy Shaw's keyboards. "After a preset on one of Jimmy's keyboards." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Michael Beinhorn as Producer** : Michael Beinhorn produced the Red Hot Chili Peppers' album "Mother's Milk," during which he encouraged John Frusciante to play in a "heavier way" than he might have naturally. Beinhorn was dropped as producer for the subsequent "Blood Sugar Sex Magik" album, replaced by Rick Rubin. "And producer Michael Bynhorn convinced him to play in a more heavier way than he would have naturally." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TESrEzVHLCc
**Michael DeBarr - Actor and Musician** : Michael DeBarr is known as an actor, husband of supergroupie Pamela DeBarr, a member of the punk band Scum of the Earth (featured on "WKRP in Cincinnati"), a member of the band Detective, and for his show on Little Steven's Underground Garage on satellite radio. "You may know him as an actor and the husband of super groupy Pamela DeBarr." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZcF5JMhBj2M
**Michael Hutchence's Death Impact** : The death of Michael Hutchence on November 22, 1997, in a Sydney hotel suite, was a deeply traumatizing event in Australia and a global tabloid sensation due to speculative and lurid details involving "sex, drugs, and rock and roll." The circumstances are still debated a quarter-century later. "for much of the world, this death was treated as a tabloid story because of some speculative and some very lured details involving the three key elements, sex, drugs, and rock and roll." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Michael Hutchence: The Last Rock and Roll Star (2017) – Documentary** : One of at least two major documentaries about Michael Hutchence, released in 2017. "Michael Hutchinson's The Last Rock and Roll Star in 2017" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Michael Jackson Tried to Buy Marvel Comics to Play Spider-Man** : In the late 1990s, Michael Jackson attempted to purchase Marvel Comics, motivated by his desire to star as Spider-Man in his own film. The only way to achieve that was to buy the entire franchise. The acquisition never happened. "He wanted to star as Spider-Man in his own movie." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Michael Miller Family Murders (2009)** : In 2009, Michael Miller went on a stabbing spree against his family in Arizona, killing his wife and daughter, and severely injuring his four-year-old son. He screamed lyrics that the media attributed to an Eminem song ("Here comes Satan, I am the Antichrist, I am going to kill you"), although the transcript states "there is no such M&M song with those lyrics." "In 2009, an Arizona man named Michael Miller went on a stabbing spree against his family at about 4 a.m." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Michael Spencer Jones – Oasis Photographer** : Michael Spencer Jones was the photographer responsible for the distinctive cover art of Oasis's 1994 debut album, "Definitely Maybe." At the time of the shoot in May 1994, Spencer Jones was an emerging talent in the field of professional photography. "The photographer was Michael Spencer Jones, someone who was just getting started in the world of big-time photography." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Michael Stipe's Sexuality** : R.E.M. frontman Michael Stipe has openly admitted to being gay. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CkSIErb0hxY
**Michigan Priest Who "Descended to Hell" Claims Condemned Were Tortured with Rihanna and Bobby McFerrin** : A Michigan priest who suffered a heart attack and claimed to have descended to hell upon returning reported that the damned were being tortured with "Umbrella" by Rihanna and "Don't Worry, Be Happy" by Bobby McFerrin. "Claimed that the condemned were tortured with Rihanna's 'Umbrella' and Bobby McFerrin's 'Don't Worry, Be Happy.'" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qtdetc5-MQo
**Microsecond Delay for Groove** : A refined technique for drummers, learned from studying artists like James Brown and George Clinton, involves inserting a "microsecond delay" into timekeeping. This subtle manipulation of timing is highlighted as an essential element for creating a truly effective and funky groove. "He also studied James Brown and George Clinton records and learned how to insert a microsecond delay into his time keeping something that's essential to a good groove." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CVPPymc_qrk
**Microsoft Dash PC (2006)** : Around 2006, Microsoft also provided a small PC, described as "the size of a cracker jack box," designed to be installed on a car's dashboard. It offered similar functionalities to Ford's built-in laptop, including MP3 playback, and sold for $2,000. "Microsoft also offered a PC the size of a cracker jack box that was installed on the dash and did the same sort of thing and it sold for $2,000." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**Microsoft Windows Revolutionizes Personal Computing** : The personal computer's accessibility dramatically increased in the early 1990s, especially after Microsoft launched Windows 3.1 on March 1, 1992. This "killer operating system" made computers much easier for "normal people" to use, a trend that continued with Windows 95 (launched August 15, 1995), which was Microsoft's most user-friendly creation yet. "But with 3.1, Microsoft hit something of a sweet spot. Who is their killer operating system that made it far easier for normal people to use a computer?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r8AazBVoS7g
**Mid to late 1950s Britain Music Evolution** : During the mid to late 1950s, popular music in Britain was in a rapid state of "evolving and mutating very quickly." This era saw the influence of imported American rock and roll, the skiffle craze, and various folk music styles, against which Trag Jazz emerged as a counter-movement. "In mid to late 1950s, Britain, popular music was evolving and mutating very quickly." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zNIVqKqAlnk
**Mid-1960s Competitions and Sponsorships** : By the mid-1960s, skateboarding competitions emerged, primarily focused in Southern California. A select few skaters achieved sufficient skill to attract sponsors and tour the US, showcasing their tricks. "A few skaters were good enough to get sponsors and tour the US showing off their tricks." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=44dXy0StjIQ
**Mid-1970s British Socio-Economic Crisis** : Mid-1970s Britain was plagued by severe socio-economic turmoil, marked by high unemployment (especially among young people), government debt from decades of a welfare state, a plummeting pound, depleted foreign reserves, and a steep decline in industrial production. Conditions were so dire that three-day work weeks were introduced, and widespread strikes by miners, bakers, nurses, garbage collectors, and even gravediggers led to rolling blackouts due to a lack of coal. This environment, coupled with an entrenched class system that predetermined opportunities, fostered widespread hopelessness, setting the stage for the intensely political British punk movement. "Mid-70s Britain was a time of high unemployment, especially among young people. The government had basically run out of money after running a welfare state for decades." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0ARYt4sYT90
**Mid-1970s Ticketing Landscape** : By the mid-1970s, concert tickets were sold through a mix of traditional and nascent computerized methods: hard tickets at box offices, scalpers and ticket brokers, phone orders with credit cards for box office pickup, and computerized terminals from companies like Ticketron, Select-A-Seat, and BAS. "Contra tickets are being sold in several ways. Number one, same old, same old way with hard tickets at a box office. Number two, scalpers and ticket brokers. Number three, sometimes you could call the ticket seller, give them your credit card number, and then pick up tickets at the box office. And number four, computerized terminals via ticket-tron, select a seat and BAS." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OCkov6FTliE
**Mid-1980s Rock Decline and "Rock is Dead" Cries** : By mid-1984, rock music "had once again blown itself out" as pop ascended, leading to recurring "rock is dead cries" and rock fans turning to "classic rock." "Rocket once again blown itself out. As Huey Lewis and Whitney Houston propelled pop to the top of the cycle, many rock fans turned to the 60s and 70s, creating an insatiable market for what was now being called classic rock." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MK7L0eCpDHc
**Mid-1990s Double Album Stigma** : In the mid-1990s, the notion of releasing a double album was widely considered "crazy," "egotistical," "vain," and "plain dumb" from the perspective of the Alternative Nation. "Now if you remember back then, the Middle 90s, the idea of doing a double album was considered crazy." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Dm5N6yDRxXQ
**Mid-60s Drivers of Rock Protest** : By the mid-1960s, rock music became deeply imbued with political and social protest. This immersion was largely spurred by major societal movements such as the civil rights movement, the women's rights movement, the emerging drug culture, and especially the widespread opposition to the Vietnam War. "by the time we got to the middle 60s, Rock was steeped in political and social protest, thanks to the civil rights movement, the women's rights movement, drug culture, and especially the Vietnam War and all that went with it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0ARYt4sYT90
**Mid-90s Demographics and Media Support** : During the mid-90s, Generation X was at its peak, forming a substantial cohort of young people who "loved this music" (pop punk). MTV and MuchMusic heavily featured pop punk videos, and a growing number of radio stations adopted the "alternative rock format," with more joining the trend during the punk rock revival. "Generation X was at its peak. There were a lot of kids in that cohort, and they loved this music. MTV and much music played all the videos." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pE7TaWSUa-o
**Mid-90s Economic and Political Context** : The mid-1990s were marked by an improved world economy following a "brutal recession" and the presence of a "Democrat in the White House." This period fostered a mood for lighter, more uplifting music, with pop punk serving as a therapeutic "antidote" to the gloom of grunge and the earlier recession. "After a brutal recession earlier in the decade, the world economy began to improve, and people's moods began to improve. While they weren't prepared to leave all their angst behind, they did want to lighten up a little bit." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pE7TaWSUa-o
**Mid-Age Crises and the "Death of Coolness Spiral" (Ages 42-44)** : Around age 42, many individuals experience an "existential mid-age crisis" regarding music, attempting for 12-18 months to reconnect with the music world and recapture the "emotional glory of music discovery" from two decades prior. However, by age 44, most revert to "comfort food music" from their youth, concluding that "this new stuff sucks." This phenomenon is referred to as the "death of coolness spiral," which can be graphically plotted as a line spiraling outwards from the center, showing taste deviating from current music with age. "Around the age of 42, many of us enter existential mid-age crises when it comes to music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m7MbV-eCLyE
**Mighty Mighty Bosstones – Bartender Suggested Adding "Mighty" to Solve a Name Conflict** : There was already a local a cappella group called the Mighty Bosstones. When the band mentioned the conflict to their bartender, he suggested simply adding another "Mighty." Hence Mighty Mighty Bosstones. "He suggested that they just add another mighty to their name." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Mike D's Mountain Bike Injury (2000)** : Beastie Boys member Mike D dislocated his shoulder after driving his mountain bike into a pothole in the summer of 2000. This severe injury led to the cancellation of the entire 30-date "Rhyme and Reason Tour" that the Beastie Boys were scheduled to co-headline with Rage Against the Machine. "But then Mike D drove his mountain bike into a pothole and dislocated his shoulder. Turned out to be so bad that the whole tour had to be canceled." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=t2uXHP9cBdM
**Mikhail Gorbachev's Influence on Rock (1985)** : When Mikhail Gorbachev came to power in 1985, restrictions on amateur rock bands loosened, partly because Gorbachev was a known Elvis fan. He reportedly believed that if young people in the USSR wanted rock and roll, they should have some, leading to greater leeway for amateur groups. "Why? Well, Gorbache was a big Elvis fan. He apparently had the feeling that if the young people of the USSR wanted rock and roll, well, then they should have some." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YeNWDtN_sHE
**Milarepa Fund** : A non-profit organization co-founded by Adam Yauch, dedicated to promoting awareness of Chinese oppression in Tibet and supporting pro-Tibet causes. It received funding through donations from Beastie Boys' tours and the Tibetan Freedom Festivals. "That was the start of the Milarepa Fund, a non-profit organization designed to promote awareness of Chinese oppression in Tibet." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9_BTe15NooI
**Millennials Fueling Piracy and Indie Discovery** : Millennials, being the most internet-savvy generation, were adept at finding music online. Many were too young to have credit cards or sufficient funds to purchase many CDs, which fueled the piracy boom. Through this, they uncovered and shared a vast amount of music not released by mainstream or major labels, contributing significantly to the indie revolution. "At the same time, many were too young to have credit cards or enough money to buy as many CD's as they wanted. That fueled the piracy boom, and in the process, they uncovered and started sharing a lot of music that wasn't in the mainstream or for major labels." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=raRCZ0Y5up8
**Millhaven Penitentiary Escape (1972)** : In 1972, 14 men escaped from Millhaven Penitentiary near Kingston, an event that was fictionalized in The Tragically Hip's song "38 Years Old," which mentioned 12 men escaping in 1973. "There were about 12 men breaking loose in 1973 when it was actually 14 men in 1972." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oMBmdfrA47Q
**Milwaukee Police Shooting (1994) Allegedly Inspired by 2Pac** : In 1994, a 17-year-old in Milwaukee shot a police officer, later claiming that he derived the idea for the act from a 2Pac song. This incident highlights concerns about music's potential influence on violent actions. "In 1994, a 17-year-old shot a Milwaukee cop later claiming that he got the idea from a two-pox song." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Mini Disk Car Players** : In addition to personal players, in-dash Mini Disk players were available for cars, with Sony manufacturing its own models. "There were even in-dash mini disk players for the car, made by Sony, of course." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**Mini Disk Format Details** : The Mini Disk format was described as an intermediary between a CD and an old 1.44 megabyte floppy disk. It consisted of a rotating magnetic disk encased in a plastic shell, played by insertion into a dedicated player. Depending on the version, a single Mini Disk could store 60, 74, or 80 minutes of digital CD-quality music, and notably, it featured recording capabilities. "The mini disk worked like this. A rotating magnetic disk was mounted inside a plastic case, which was played by inserting it into a slot or a bay of a dedicated player. Depending on the version, a single mini disk could hold 60, 74, or 80 minutes worth of data, digital CD quality music. And it essentially worked just like the CD except that you could record on it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**Minus the Bear – From a Story: A Bear-Filled Date That Was "Like BJ and the Bear, Minus the Bear"** : Stems from a friend's description of a date: "Remember the old TV series BJ and the Bear? It was like that, minus the bear." "Remember the old TV series BJ in the bear? It was like that minus the bear." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Misconception of "New Music Dying" vs. Catalog Listening** : The statistic that 70% of listening is to catalog music (defined as music older than 18 months to two years) while only 30% is new music is not a new phenomenon, but rather newly observable due to streaming data. Historically, consumers always balanced new purchases with older, beloved music. "people print these articles saying, oh, you know, 70% of the world is listening to catalog now, and only 30% of people are listening to new music, and new music is dying. That must mean that new music is dying, right? As if that's something new, and that's bulls**t, right? Because the bottom line is, is that that 70, 30 split was always a 70, 30 split. You just didn't have the data before." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Misconception of Direct Artist Payment** : There is a common assumption among users that subscription fees directly pay the specific artists whose songs they stream, but this is not how streaming payment systems are structured. "If you stream a song from an artist, you would expect that your subscription money goes to paying that artist. You know, you're listening to their song, so they should get paid with your money. Not necessarily." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Misconceptions About Streaming Music Payouts** : Complaints that streaming music companies (e.g., Spotify, RDO, Slack, DZR) "are ripping off artists by paying out so little money" are often misplaced. These payouts are not arbitrary but are "negotiated with a bunch of people in the middle," including record labels, music publishers, and copyright boards.
