A music stream is a one-time listen, comparable to radio play (where artists receive a fraction of a cent per play), distinct from purchasing a download. Streaming services pay "billions in fees," representing "70% of what they bring in," but the opaque process by which these fees are distributed to artists by intermediaries is the core issue, as no standalone streaming service is currently profitable. "These payouts are not the doing of the streamers." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7Zniri9uSlc
**Misconceptions of Goth Culture** : Goth culture is often wrongly associated with a "fascination with death" and a desire to "embrace death and wish death upon others," especially when linked to violent incidents in the media. "Hey, these people are fascinated with death. They must embrace death and wish death upon others." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**Missing Money After Nancy's Death – Robbery Theory** : After Nancy Spungen's murder, a substantial amount of money—some sources say $1500 in gig money, others $20,000 in Virgin Records royalties—was missing from the hotel room, fueling theories of a robbery as a motive for her death. "A big chunk of money, some say $1500 in gig money, others say $20,000 in royalties from Virgin Records, was missing for the room." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OcylgiXkDP8
**Mix Tapes and the Blank Cassette Market** : Blank cassettes were immensely popular sellers, available in 30, 45, 60, 90, and 120-minute lengths (e.g., C60 for 60 minutes), enabling "everybody" to create their own mix tapes. This widespread practice, alongside the ease of duplicating pre-recorded cassettes, led to a market saturated with counterfeit releases and posed a significant challenge to the music industry. `"Not only were record stores selling pre-recorded tapes by the Billions, but blank cassettes were huge sellers, as everybody began to make their own mix tapes."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Modern Conspiracy Theories and "Truthers" (Contemporary)** : Certain segments of the population exhibit deep suspicion of official narratives, often rejecting simple explanations in favor of complex conspiracy theories involving secret cabals or organizations. These range from beliefs that the earth is flat, humans never went to the moon, COVID-19 was engineered by media, the Illuminati reside under Denver airport, 5G causes cancer, chemtrails are for mind control, the royal family killed Princess Di, and 9/11 was an inside job, with adherents often referring to themselves as "truthers" who challenge mainstream media. "The world of conspiracy theories is a bottomless pit of weirdness." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1Jt2fUBDusk
**Modern Music Piracy and Its Consequences** : Modern music piracy, which began in the early 1990s and became rampant by 2000, was fueled by consumer frustration over high CD prices, the phasing out of CD singles, and albums often containing only a few good songs. The music industry's attempts to sue fans for stealing music exacerbated the problem. This resulted in a catastrophic decline in sales, with 2.5 billion CDs sold globally in 2000 crashing by over 75% in the subsequent decade. The impact included massive revenue losses, job cuts, artists being dropped from rosters, and the destruction of relationships between labels and record stores. The industry, paralyzed by its lack of technological understanding and hindered by antitrust laws (after being scrutinized for CD price-fixing), struggled to adapt until Steve Jobs' iTunes offered a new path. "Modern music piracy began in the early 1990s. And by the time we reached January 1st 2000, it's starting to really get out of control." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nX8uAFDzDYM
**Modern Music Publishing** : In contemporary terms, music publishing involves a company signing a deal with a songwriter who assigns the copyright (the right to copy) to their song. The publisher then works to place the songwriter's music in various avenues, such as with other artists, in movies, or TV commercials (known as sync or synchronization rights), protects the music from infringement, collects royalties, and subsequently pays the songwriter. "A company signed to deal with a songwriter who assigns the copyright, literally the right to copy, to that song." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Modern Radio Show Production (Digital Era)** : The production process has dramatically evolved with digital technology. The speaker records voice tracks into Pro Tools at his home studio, uploads them to Dropbox, and a producer edits them on a laptop (even on planes). Music is supplied from extensive hard drives. While the radio version airs on Saturday or Sunday, the podcast version is "telescoped down" to 15-second music clips due to complex copyright and rights management issues that prevent playing full songs. "Now I have a studio at home. I have Pro Tools. I record my voice track into Pro Tools. I uploaded to Dropbox, Rob Downs, and look down loads it. Edit it while watching TV on a laptop." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4xxgPX-aR90
**Modern Recording Studio Techniques - Post 1950s** : From the 1950s onwards, modern recording studio techniques, pioneered by Jack Mullin, Bing Crosby, and Les Paul, made it possible to create records that were impossible to reproduce live. The ability to start, stop, edit with razor blades, remove mistakes, overdub, and control individual track volumes transformed the recording studio into a musical instrument with unlimited sonic possibilities, moving beyond direct-to-acetate recordings that allowed no edits. "From the 1950s forward, it became possible to make records that were impossible to reproduce in the real world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6SdPPsmpgJg
**Modern Streaming Music (Present)** : Current streaming platforms provide access to a digital music library of at least 90 million songs, with new additions daily, allowing users to access virtually any recorded song instantly from any device, often at negligible cost or even for free, thus realizing the "theoretical Celestial Jukebox." "Today, of course, we have streaming. All the platforms draw from a digital music library that contains at least 90 million songs and more are being added every day." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Modern Ubiquity of Rock-Rap Hybrids** : Today, the fusion of alt rock and hip hop is so commonplace that artists blending these styles are ubiquitous, and listeners often don't even recognize they're experiencing a hybrid that was considered radical and new until the 1990s. "It's so common that we often don't even notice that we're listening to a hybrid of styles that didn't exist until the late 80s and one that was considered radical and weird until we got into the 90s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5bQz8R0gbXc
**Modern Usage of "Nerd"** : In 1951, Newsweek magazine reported that the term "nerd" was used in Detroit to describe an "awkward sort of dude who really wasn't very cool." The word gained significant popularity in 1974 with the TV series "Happy Days," where Fonzie often called Richie and Potsy "nerds." By the late 1970s, coinciding with the rise of personal computers, consumer technology, and science fiction, its use became widespread, evolving into a compliment in modern technological society, with figures like Bill Gates, Elon Musk, and Mark Zuckerberg celebrated as "nerds." "But the following year, Newsweek magazine reported that nerd was being used in Detroit to describe an awkward sort of dude who really wasn't very cool." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=h6snk0m4200
**Modest Mouse – Passage from Virginia Woolf's Story "The Mark on the Wall"** : The name comes from a line in Virginia Woolf's short story "The Mark on the Wall": "and very frequent in the minds of modest, mouse-colored people." "It's a passage in a story called the mark on the wall by Virginia Woolf." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Monaco's Symphony Orchestra Larger Than Its Army** : In the principality of Monaco, located on the Mediterranean coast, an unusual demographic fact exists: there are more individuals who are members of its symphony orchestra than there are personnel in its army. "If we go to the principality of Monaco on the Mediterranean coast, you'll find that there are more people in its symphony orchestra than in its army." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**Monarchy's Influence in UK (1970s)** : The British monarchy held significant ceremonial power; during the Queen's annual Christmas Day message, all activity in homes across the kingdom would cease, with some older individuals even standing and saluting the TV until her majesty finished speaking. "When the queen delivered her annual message on Christmas Day, all activity in houses across the kingdom would stop." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Mondegreen** : This refers to a misheard lyric, where a listener incorrectly interprets a song's words. A notable example is in Jimi Hendrix's "Purple Haze," where "Excuse me while I kiss the sky" is often misheard as "Excuse me while I kiss this guy." "That's a misheard lyric and a great example is in Jimmy Hendrix's purple haze." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4cZfegexwwk
**Mondegreens: Definition** : Misheard lyrics or lines of poetry are known as Mondegreens. These errors result in the line acquiring a completely new and unintended meaning, which can persist in a listener's mind. "Misheard lyrics are known as Mondegreens." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Mondegreens: Global Spread** : With the rise of mass media in the 20th century, the phenomenon of Mondegreens spread widely, becoming common across various languages and cultures worldwide. "With the emergence of mass media in the 20th century, Mondegrees be in spread and now they're common worldwide and in every language you want to mention." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Mondegreens: Origin (1954)** : The term "Mondegreen" was coined in 1954 by American writer Sylvia Wright, who published an article in Harper's magazine. Wright had misheard a line from the 1765 Scottish ballad "in the Bonnie Earl Morey." She heard "Lady Montagreen" instead of "laid him on the green," and proposed that all such misheard lines be called Mondegreens. "The word goes back to 1954 when an American writer named Sylvia Wright wrote in Harper's magazine that she messed up with an old Scottish ballot entitled in the Bonnie Earl Morey, which dates back to at least 1765." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Mondegreens: Psychological Basis** : Mondegreens arise from a psychological response called confirmation bias. When phrases are heard indistinctly, the brain instinctively attempts to fill gaps with familiar sounds, often leading to cognitive dissonance. The incorrect words become ingrained, making it difficult to hear the correct lyrics, as people may subconsciously prefer their misheard version. "If we hear a phrase indistinctly, our brains automatically, instinctively, tried filling the gaps with something familiar." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Mono Mixing in Live Concerts (1980s)** : Contrary to common assumptions, many live shows are mixed in mono rather than stereo. This practice is chosen because "sound that seems to come from a single source has less distortion," which translates to "better fidelity" for the audience, prioritizing clarity over a stereo image. "Plenty of shows are not mixed in stereo. They're mixed in mono." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qrrwqdpCjpQ
**Mono Records Decline (Post-1968)** : With the introduction of stereo records, mono records began to decline, and by the time The Velvet Underground's 1968 album was released, all major labels had ceased issuing mono records entirely. "When stereo records were introduced, mono records began to go into decline. By the time that Velvet Underground record came out, all the majors had stopped issuing mono records entirely." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Monophonic (Mono) Sound (Pre-1958)** : Until 1958, all recordings were monophonic or mono, meaning they had only one audio channel with minimal sonic differentiation between instruments. "Until 1958, all recordings were monophonic or mono, meaning that there was just one channel and not much sonic differentiation between any of the instruments." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**More Band Name Origins – 60 Additional Examples (Bandminology)** : A second installment of band name origins. "Bandminology" — coined by the podcast in consultation with linguistics academics and branding experts — is proposed as the term for the study of how musical groups choose their names. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Morné Harmse School Attack (2008)** : On August 17, 2008, 18-year-old Morné Harmse carried out a samurai sword attack at Nick Dietrich's Technical School in Kruger's Drop, South Africa, killing one 16-year-old and injuring three others. He initially blamed Satanism but then claimed Slipknot made him "lose control of his body," wearing a mask resembling Slipknot members and referring to it as his "maggot mask." "On August 17th, 2008, there was a horrific incident at Nick Dietrich's Technical School in Kruger's Drop, South Africa." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Morris Albert (1975)** : Morris Albert is mentioned as the artist known for the "super-sappy" 1975 ballad "Feelings." This song was famously covered by The Offspring for ironic effect on their Americana album. "Feelings, a 1975 ballad by a dude named Morris Albert, done for the ironic effect, of course." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pE7TaWSUa-o
**Moshpit Evolution from Pogo and Slam Dancing** : Moshpits are a descendant of the Pogo, a dance where British punks jumped up and down, likely originating from the need to see over crowds at early stage-less punk shows. As music intensified, Pogoing became more aggressive, evolving into "slam dancing," which was intensified further by American hardcore bands like Black Flag and Fear in the late 1970s. "They are the descendants of groups of British punks doing a dance called the Pogo. Basically it involved jumping up and down in place." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bferkEWvJg0
**Moshpit Variations and Dangers** : Modern moshing has diversified into various forms, including "circle pits," "walls of death" (which are explicitly dangerous), "hardcore dancing," and "crowd killing." These practices have frequently resulted in injuries and deaths, leading many bands to actively oppose moshing during their shows. "Since then, moshing has taken on a variety of forms. Circle pits, walls of death, which is as dangerous as it sounds, hardcore dancing and something even called crowd killing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bferkEWvJg0
**Moshpits and Slam Dancing Origins** : Moshpits, characterized by slam dancing, first emerged within the hardcore scene, initially observed in California and Washington, D.C. "It was with hardcore that we saw the first Moshpits with slam dancing and was first seen in California and Washington, D.C." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PEHkKfmmDiI
**Most Covered Christmas Song (Silent Night)** : "Silent Night" is identified as the most covered Christmas song, with no fewer than 137,315 recorded versions according to tabulators. This far surpasses other popular Christmas songs like "White Christmas," "Jingle Bells," "The Christmas Song," and "Winter Wonderland." "It's Silent Night. The people who tabulate such things say that it has been covered on record no fewer than, get this, 137,315 times." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nYPGQiDL5bw
**Most Popular Toilet Song – "Sweater Weather" by The Neighbourhood** : A UK study determined the most popular song people listen to while sitting on the toilet is "Sweater Weather" by The Neighbourhood. "The number one track, Sweater Weather by the Neighborhood." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**Mount Tambora Eruption (April 10, 1815)** : Ejected nearly 200 cubic kilometers of debris into the atmosphere, causing global cooling and making 1816 "the year without a summer." This eruption was the biggest in 1300 years. "All that dust circled the earth, blocking out a significant amount of sunlight." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XaLsZFLrMnI
**Mount Vesuvius Eruption (79 AD)** : The eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD was a cataclysmic natural disaster that famously buried and preserved the Roman cities of Pompeii and Herculaneum under ash and pumice. This historical event has inspired songs such as "Pompeii" by Bastille and "Cities in Dust" by Siouxsie and the Banshees. "Both Pompeii by Bastille and cities and dust from Suzy and the Banshees are about the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4PlieAEthSg
**Mount Vesuvius Eruption (79 AD) Inspiration** : The eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD, which destroyed Pompeii, served as inspiration for songs like "Pompeii" by Bastille and "Cities in Dust" by Siouxsie and the Banshees. "Both Pompey by Bastille and City's Indust from Suzy and the Banshee's are about the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cM3-tINKdvo
**Movement for Canadian Music Support (mid-1960s)** : Starting in the mid-1960s, a movement emerged within Canada to address the neglected state of its domestic music industry. This movement ultimately led to years of discussions, arguments, and pushback regarding the implementation of content regulations. "starting in the mid 1960s, there was a movement within Canada to do something about our more abandoned music industry." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Movie Inspiration: Christian F.** : The movie "Christian F." is a film set in the underground punk rock scene of Berlin, with David Bowie contributing to its soundtrack. A scene from this movie, where the lead female character gives herself a stick-and-poke tattoo, directly inspired Dave Grohl to attempt his first tattoo at age 12 or 13. "I watched this movie that was a movie that took place in Berlin in the Underground punk rock scene. It was a movie called Christian F. Yes, I remember that. It was a great movie. And I think Bowie was in the soundtrack. But there's a scene where the lead character, this woman, gives herself a tattoo." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jZeMmPekKMQ
**Movie Soundtrack as a Breakout Strategy (1990s)** : In the 1990s, a popular method to break new acts was to include their songs on movie soundtracks that appealed to Gen Xers, such as "The Crow," "Dangerous Minds," "Judgment Night," "Singles," "Pulp Fiction," and "Trainspotting," or earlier John Hughes movies. "In the 90s, one popular way to break a new act would be to include one of their songs on the soundtrack of a movie that appealed to Jen Exers." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZcF5JMhBj2M
**Movie Soundtracks as Industry Lifeblood** : At one point in history, movie soundtracks were considered the primary driving force behind the recorded music industry. The introduction of the LP record was partly influenced by movie studios and Broadway producers seeking a superior listening experience, highlighting the intrinsic link between film and record sales. "There was a time when movie soundtracks were the lifeblood of the recorded music industry." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**Movie Studios and Labels Synergy (1990s)** : Throughout the 1990s, a symbiotic relationship emerged between movie studios and record labels, often owned by the same parent company. Artists were offered attractive financial deals, labels aggressively promoted soundtracks, and radio stations were encouraged to play singles from these albums, leading to "boatload" sales due to the quality of the material. "Artis were lured in with nice financial deals. Labels, often known by the same company as the movie studio, promoted the soundtracks." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**MuchMusic's Role in National Culture** : MuchMusic acted as a national pop and rock radio station, creating a shared musical experience for young Canadians across the country. By exposing kids from St. John's to Whitehorse to Canadian artist videos, it helped knit together a national music scene and foster a sense of shared identity. "For the first time, a kid in St. John's Newfoundland could have something in common musically with a kid in Toronto, a kid in Edmonton, and a kid in Whitehorse." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6PGgNCaW4xo
**Mullet Origins and History** : The mullet, a hairstyle characterized by being "business in front, party in the back," has ancient origins found in Assyria, Egypt, and Greece, where indigenous populations considered it a traditional look. It became largely invisible until the late 1960s, when hard rock adopted it, and David Bowie's Ziggy Stardust character helped popularize it as radical and shocking. "Now according to people who study these things, say that the origins of the mullet can be found in ancient Assyria, Egypt, and Greece." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bferkEWvJg0
**Multi-Platinum and Diamond Awards Introduction (1984, 1999)** : Due to ever-increasing record sales, Multi-Platinum awards were introduced in 1984. Later, in 1999, the Diamond Award was established for sales of 10 million copies. In both cases, there were multiple simultaneous recipients, meaning no single "first" awardee. "Multi-platinum awards were introduced in 1984. And then in 1999, they came up with a diamond award, which meant sales of 10 million copies." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gP3oQeYEvso
**Multinational Mining in Jamaica (1952)** : By 1952, multinational mining companies began extensive operations in Jamaica, extracting "millions of tons" of bauxite and sending it overseas. This foreign involvement rapidly transformed Jamaica's economic landscape. "By 1952, multinational mining companies were digging up Jamaican Boxite and sending it overseas, millions of tons of this stuff." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zNIVqKqAlnk
**Multiple Sclerosis (MS) in Canada and UK** : Canada has one of the highest, if not the highest, rates of Multiple Sclerosis in the world, with the UK also experiencing a high incidence. A theory, though unproven, suggests a possible link between MS and a lack of sunlight and vitamin D, suggesting that daily vitamin D supplementation from a young age might help prevent it. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ocMHRLQMQY0
**Multitrack Recording Origins (Nazi Magnetophone)** : The ability to multitrack, overdub, edit, mix, and remix audio today originates from magnetic tape technology, specifically the German Magnetophone machines discovered by Jack Mullen after WWII. This technology, commercialized by Bing Crosby and Ampex, allowed sound to be recorded on tape, then cut, edited, and layered, fundamentally transforming recording capabilities and the modern music industry. "This all led to the modern recording industry and all the music that we listened to today." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NAroxFbYkRw
**Mumford & Sons – Marcus Mumford Wanted to Evoke a Family Business** : Lead singer Marcus Mumford liked the idea of a band name that evoked an old family business. "He'd like the idea of a band evoking an old family business." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Murder in Music - General Context** : Murder, a pervasive and regrettable aspect of human experience, sadly extends into the world of music, affecting real personalities and business people through actions motivated by jealousy, fear, revenge, anger, sex, or power, or simply due to extreme unluckiness. "Yet, being the frail, imperfect creatures we are, murder does happen. And sadly, it happens everywhere, including in the world of music. Musicians and music business people are just like anyone else. They get mad or stoned or drunk and do dumb things. Or someone who's mad or stoned or drunk does something dumb to somebody else. Actions are motivated by jealousy or fear or revenge, anger, sex, power. Or someone just gets very unlucky." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZcYKZ5ovoy4
**Murl Travis and Paul Bigsby's Influence** : Leo Fender met country picker Murl Travis, who owned a solid-body guitar custom-built by motorcycle parts designer Paul Bigsby. This design served as a direct inspiration for Leo Fender's own solid-body guitar prototypes, such as the Esquire. "This is when he met a country picker named Murl Travis, who had a solid-body guitar built for him by a motorcycle parts designer named Paul Bigsby. That design inspired Leo." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E6cXyaHALBQ
**Muscle Shoals Sound Studio - Legendary Studio** : Muscle Shoals Sound Studio in Alabama is a renowned historical recording location where legendary artists like The Rolling Stones, Lynyrd Skynyrd, Bob Dylan, and Bob Seger recorded. The Black Keys later used it for their *Brothers* album, valuing its acoustics despite technical challenges. "A bunch of it was made at the famous Muscle Shoal Sound Studio in Alabama, a place used by the Rolling Stones, and Leonard Skinner, Bob Dylan, and Bob Seeger." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PmwfC4HR3_I
**Muscle Shoals Sound Studio Electrical Issues (2010)** : During the recording of *Brothers* in 2010, The Black Keys encountered significant technical problems at the legendary Muscle Shoals Sound Studio in Alabama. The studio's in-house equipment was unusable due to 30 years of disuse, and newly brought-in digital gear was subsequently fried by stray voltage entering the building from ongoing electrical line work on the streets. "But then work on the electrical lines on the streets and all kinds of stray voltage into the building, frying most of the modern stuff." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ooNAHswiT5Q
**MusiCares** : The Warped Tour raised hundreds of thousands of dollars for MusiCares, an organization dedicated to helping musicians and roadies obtain new health insurance. "The tour has raised hundreds of thousands of dollars for an organization called Music Cares. It helps musicians and roadies with new health insurance." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jfQaUV6Bq4
**Music Appreciation While High (Cannabis)** : The perception that music sounds better when under the influence of cannabis is not an illusion. Cannabis activates CB1 receptors in the brain, causing them to become hyperactive, which in turn enhances activity in the metalymbic dopamine system when music is present. This leads to increased dopamine secretion for every note heard, intensifying the "feel-good" sensation. Additionally, cannabis interferes with short-term memory, fostering a heightened focus on the present moment and suppressing the brain's natural tendency to anticipate what comes next, allowing listeners to perceive previously unnoticed details in both complex and simple arrangements. "Music plus cannabis enhances activity in the metalymbic dopamine system. That means for every note of music we hear, we get extra dopamine secretion caused by cannabis." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**Music Blamed for Violence and Crime (General Pattern)** : Throughout history, new music genres have been blamed for violence and crime. This scapegoating was seen with hot jazz in the 1920s (linked to illegal alcohol and illicit sex), rhythm and blues in the 1930s and 40s (called "the devil's music"), rock and roll in the 1950s (blamed for all post-war generation's ills), and continues with hip hop and rap. "There have always been people who blame music for violence and crime." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Music Business Deals for Sonic Coincidences** : In cases where independently created songs sound similar, composers, publishers, and lawyers sometimes opt for deals rather than lawsuits. This can involve the original writer receiving a share of royalties from the new, similar song, especially if it becomes a hit, serving as an effective way to keep disputes quiet and maintain peace. "So here's how it worked. In exchange for not suing this band from Europe, my friend ended up with a basic piece of the action of this new song." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wYD_dIvYY5o
**Music Business Exploitation** : Ian Thorneley recounts significant personal experiences of being taken advantage of by business associates within the music industry during the mid-2000s, highlighting the exploitative nature that can exist behind the scenes. "Well, where people try to take advantage of you over the time. Oh, big time." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QIA7V4PxCdM
**Music Can Assist Brain Surgery – Patients Play Their Instrument While Surgeons Operate** : For certain neurological procedures, patients are awoken from anesthetic and asked to play a musical instrument while surgeons work inside the skull. If the surgeons disturb an area affecting the patient's playing, they know to find a different route. A notable example: Brad Carter, whose Parkinson's disease tremors had left him unable to play guitar, had deep brain stimulation electrodes installed while fully awake and playing guitar, singing, with his skull screwed into a stabilizing frame. The surgery restored his ability to play. "Music can assist with brain surgery." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NMwMwqMQpf4
**Music Complexity Driven by Technology (Early 1980s)** : The proliferation of new electronic technologies in the early 1980s, including more powerful and affordable synthesizers, drum machines, samplers, sophisticated multi-track recorders, and new outboard effects, enabled music to become more complicated simply "because it could." This facilitated diverse new sounds in the post-punk era. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kFLMkvJAaUY
**Music Creation and Consumption Boom (2000s)** : Despite a challenging business environment in the latter half of the 2000s, more people than ever before were making, distributing, and consuming music. This surge was enabled by new technologies and the bypassing of traditional gatekeepers like record labels and radio stations. "At no point in history, were more people making music, distributing music, and consuming music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Music Cycle and Innovation in Decline** : Music operates in cycles, with genres rising and falling in dominance. When a genre like rock enters a "down part of a cycle," it isn't necessarily negative; rather, uncertainty can lead to "a lot of innovation" and a greater willingness to experiment with new and untested sounds. "And this isn't necessarily a bad thing because being in the down part of a cycle can lead to a lot of innovation. Because when there's uncertainty, there's more willingness to try something, anything new." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_yslM5oanRo
**Music Cycles and Bubbles Bursting** : The music industry operates in cycles, where periods of dominance are inevitably followed by decline. The success of alt rock in the mid-2000s, described as a "bubble," was expected to eventually burst due to evolving social conditions, economic factors, and demographic shifts. "But you know what happens with music cycles, right? What goes up? Must come down." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7dXtgTkdv9w
**Music Cycles and Comebacks** : Music, like many aspects of culture, operates in cycles, with older sounds often experiencing comebacks, resurrections, reestablishments, or reintroductions. These revivals cannot be forced; they require a critical mass of people in different areas to simultaneously decide it's time to revisit and modernize older music. "Bottom line though is that even though many things in this universe come in cycles, we're not always sure when something old will become new again." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UpWz3X33vSY
**Music Cycles and Revivals** : Music often follows cycles where a sound gains popularity, peaks, fades, but rarely disappears completely. Instead, many rock sounds lie in wait for a generation or two before being rediscovered, reactivated, and given a "sonic update," enjoying a new period of popularity before the cycle repeats. "A sound and style will be big for a while, reach a peak with the public, and then slowly fade out." "Instead, what we've seen, rockabilly aside, after enjoying a time at the forefront of music, many of the cycle-prone rock sounds were seeded into the shadows, never really go away, lying in wait until someone comes along, often a generation or two later, to rediscover and reactivate it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HSvgRyGiOpE
**Music Deaths Snowball (2016)** : Following David Bowie's death in January 2016, the number of music hero deaths seemed to "snowball" throughout the remainder of the year. "And from that point on, the deaths just seemed to snowball for the rest of 2016." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**Music Endures Beyond Life** : While musicians pass away, their music continues to live on, providing a lasting legacy for fans. "While their music will continue to live on, they will not." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HBS24NwhrE8
**Music Evolution and Categorization** : Music is constantly evolving, mutating, and modifying itself, leading to new subgenres and offshoots. This process has resulted in a massive increase in music categories, from just four main headings (rock, pop, country, R&B) in the 1950s to nearly 2,000 different classifications on Spotify today. "Music is always evolving and mutating and modifying itself. Start with something and then maybe enough people will agree on a certain direction for this new sound to become a full-fledged new subgenre." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_yslM5oanRo
**Music Fans' Sexual Habits Survey** : A survey conducted by a secondary ticket seller in the UK explored music fans' sexual performance and preferences. It found that these fans reportedly last an average of 16 minutes in bed, and 17% admit to keeping their Apple AirPods in during sex. "And 17% of all music fans admit to leaving in their Apple AirPods while doing it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=72Lei7uug8c
**Music Genre Segmentation – Late 1977** : By the end of 1977, the music landscape was undergoing a significant segmentation into numerous genres. What was once simple R&B had diversified into funk and disco, and was on the verge of producing rap and hip hop. Defining "rock" also became more complex, leading to the identification of separate subgenres such as metal, California rock, hard rock, and punk rock, with these subgenres themselves beginning to fragment further. "As 1977 ended, it seemed that music was segmenting into a million different genres." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gKrAXMG8sTc
**Music Has Become Sadder Over 50 Years – CDC Monitors the Trend** : Analysis of 150,000 songs from the Top 100 charts between 1965 and 2015 revealed that popular music has grown measurably sadder. Use of the word "love" fell by half, negative words increased, and 50% of contemporary songs are in a minor key compared to just 15% in the 1960s. The Centers for Disease Control monitors this trend. "The use of the word 'love' was reduced by half, negative words increased, and 50% of current songs are in a minor key versus 15% in the 60s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qtdetc5-MQo
**Music History Defining Eras** : Throughout history, certain periods, like the early 1990s (Grunge/Alternative), mid-1950s (Rock and Roll), 1960s (Beatles-led), and 1970s (Punk), have created a pervasive sense that music history was actively being made, characterized by a constant influx of new sounds and artists. "It felt like not a day went by without there being a new song, a new artist, a new sound, a new scene worth checking out." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bYsV3ozbk4M
**Music Inadvertently Sent into Space via Radio** : The invention of radio approximately 100 years ago inadvertently began sending music into space. This means that if there is extraterrestrial life on any of the estimated 300 Earth-like planets within 50 light-years, they might be receiving our early broadcasts now. "It started with our invention of radio, which inadvertently sent music into space, starting about 100 years ago." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**Music Industry Challenges in the Aughts** : During the 2000s, the music industry faced severe challenges, including the rise of the internet and file sharing, which led to declining CD sales, job losses, and artists losing their positions on label rosters. Despite this backdrop, alt rock fans experienced a period of great prosperity in terms of music. "Yes, the music industry was still trying to deal with the internet and file sharing declining CD sales. And yes, there were job losses and yes, fans lost their places on rosters." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7dXtgTkdv9w
**Music Industry Connections to "K. West" Sign Purchase** : The "K. West" sign from David Bowie's *Ziggy Stardust* album cover was purchased by an anonymous collector who is said to have music industry connections. "That sign, that k West sign, was purchased some time ago by an anonymous collector with, it is said, music industry connections." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pmKjZzypzvg
**Music Industry Disarray (2009)** : The recorded music industry was in a state of "complete disarray" by 2009, characterized by financial losses, layoffs, dropping artists, and legal battles in an attempt to recover its former prosperity. "The recorded music industry was in complete disarray, bleeding money, laying people off, dropping artists, and still trying to litigate their way back to their former glory." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Music Industry Overlords' Wealth** : Historically, the "overlords" of the music industry (record labels) accumulated immense wealth from music sales, often far exceeding that of the artists. These powerful entities were reluctant to make changes that might undermine their established kingdoms or reduce their profits. "The overlords enjoyed their immense wealth and were loathed to do anything that might jeopardize the power of their kingdoms." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lGNrJgwQjgg
**Music Industry Panic and iTunes Dominance** : The recorded music industry experienced panic due to falling CD sales and rampant piracy, compounded by the realization that iTunes had gained excessive control over digital distribution, with Apple holding at least 70% of the online music sales market at one point. "The labels had begun to realize that they had given too much power and control to iTunes when it came to distribution." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Music Industry Recession (Early 1980s)** : The early 1980s saw the recording industry experiencing a severe recession, with revenues declining for the first time since the Great Depression. This economic downturn made record companies hesitant to invest in new and expensive technologies like the compact disc. "The recording industry was still suffering from a terrible recession in the early 1980s. For the first time ever, first time since the Great Depression, revenues in the industry had gone down. And investing in new tech wasn't something they were too keen on." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Music Industry Rethinks Video Investment (2000s)** : Around 2000, the recorded music industry began to seriously rethink the effort and investment put into making music videos. With MTV, the primary "gatekeeper and disseminator and customer of videos," losing interest and moving on, the future of music videos became uncertain. "By 2000, people in the recorded music industry were really rethinking the efforts being put into making videos." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Music Industry Shifts (Early 21st Century)** : By the mid-2000s, the "traditional label of hegemony over the recorded music industry was eroding," primarily due to piracy "killing CD sales" and a growing movement towards online music. Lawsuits and threats failed to stop this shift, forcing artists like Radiohead to consider major label contracts versus independent options. "the traditional label of hegemony over the recorded music industry was eroding, piracy with killing CD sales." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JUvDp3_RBH0
**Music Industry Shifts and Personal Despair** : During Hugh Dillon's severe addiction, he felt the cultural landscape was overwhelmed by pop acts like the Spice Girls and Backstreet Boys, which he perceived as displacing "great music." This shift contributed to his feelings of anger and despair, making his personal hell worse. "the f**king spice girls and the backstreet boys and all this great music f**king fades into the background." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zBd13KtYlAA
**Music Industry Tailspin (Circa 2009)** : Around 2009, the music industry faced a severe downturn. CD sales were consistently dropping, piracy significantly eroded remaining sales, and overall revenue from music sales had shrunk to half of what it was in 2000. This period was characterized by widespread negative growth across most segments of the industry. "The music industry is in a tailspin. CD sales are dropping week after week. Pairacy is eating into the sales that remain, and revenue from the sale of music is shrinking and shrinking and shrinking to the point where they're half what they were in 2000." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VlIErdSSVOM
**Music Industry Transformation (Post-2005)** : During Blink 182's eight-year hiatus (post-2005), the music industry underwent significant changes. This included a decline in album sales, the detrimental impact of piracy, and the nascent influence of streaming services, all of which contributed to a challenging landscape for artists upon their return. "The industry had changed greatly in that time they were apart. Fewer albums were being sold. The piracy had taken its toll, and streaming was only starting to have an effect." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EaGUC4av1R4
**Music Industry Transition (Physical to Streaming)** : By the time The Strokes released "Come Down Machine" in 2013, the music industry was in a "forced transition" from an era focused on selling physical copies ("pieces of plastic") to the modern era of streaming. In this new landscape, the number of physical copies sold became less important, with fan engagement and reception taking precedence. "They were any forced transition from the days of selling pieces of plastic to the era of streaming. Counting the number of physical copies sold was less important." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jEfofyobGiI
**Music Industry as a "Meritocracy"** : Ian Thorneley challenges the common perception among music fans that the music industry operates as a meritocracy, where good music automatically rises to the top. He states that this is "not necessarily true," indicating that other factors influence success. "first of all, they think the music is a meritocracy and if it's good, it'll rise to the top. Yeah, that's not necessarily true." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QIA7V4PxCdM
**Music Industry in 1995** : When the Warped Tour began in 1995, the music industry was vastly different: the internet was barely used, MP3s were unheard of, streaming was years away, and the compact disc was the dominant music format. These fundamental changes in the industry were a factor in the tour's eventual conclusion in 2018. "when the tour started in 1995, hardly anyone was using the internet. No one had ever heard of MP3s. Streaming was years in the future and the compact disk ruled." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jfQaUV6Bq4
**Music Industry's "Rule of 30"** : Historically, in periods of industry prosperity, a "rule of 30" often governed record labels' financial model: "If a label released 30 records, 25 would lose money, 3 or 4 would break even, and 1 or 2 would become massive enough to pay for all the others." This illustrates the critical role of highly successful bands in generating the profits necessary to subsidize the development of new acts and cover the losses from less successful releases, maintaining the overall music industry ecosystem. "If a label released 30 records, 25 would lose money, 3 or 4 would break even, and 1 or 2 would become massive enough to pay for all the others." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7Zniri9uSlc
**Music Industry's Copy Protection Crisis (2004)** : In 2004, the music industry faced rampant illegal file sharing and responded by implementing various copy protection methods, some of which, like Sony's rootkit, installed malware on users' computers. This era of insidious copy protection software, such as MacroVision CDS2000 used by Capitol Records, caused significant problems and accusations for artists like the Beastie Boys, even if their specific software didn't install malware. "Illegal file sharing was rampant, and the industry was looking to do something, anything, to keep people from stealing music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9_BTe15NooI
**Music Industry's Digital Adaptation and Artwork Costs** : The music industry, recovering from a near-death experience due to its initial struggle with the internet, clawed back profitability by cutting costs, including selling fewer CDs. While vinyl has seen a resurgence with high prices and margins, the vast majority of revenue now comes from streaming, which eliminates physical products entirely. "Meanwhile, the music industry, which had a near-death experience thanks to its initial inability to adapt to music on the internet, has clawed its way back to profitability, partly by cutting costs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pwebM3TfSdg
**Music Industry's Environmental Reckoning** : The music industry, recognized as a major polluter and energy consumer (through tours, non-biodegradable plastics, and the carbon footprint of streaming), has slowly begun an "environmental reckoning" and is moving towards more eco-friendly practices. This includes the emergence of "green records" made from sustainable materials, green platforms aiming to reduce energy consumption, and recording studios adopting high-efficiency gear, carbon-neutral methods, and sustainable energy sources. Remote collaboration now reduces travel needs. Concerts and festivals are increasingly powered by renewable energy, encourage waste reduction, and use digital tickets. Artists are selling merchandise made from organic/recycled fabrics with non-harmful dyes, engaging fans on environmental issues, and organizing benefit concerts. The touring industry is adopting biodiesel/sustainable fuels, electric vehicles, carbon offsets, and local sourcing to minimize its environmental impact. "The music industry is a major polluter and a major devourer of energy. The number of pollutants created by a single tour is incredible." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZzIqIydZTlA
**Music Industry's Failed Response to Piracy (2003)** : By 2003, CD sales had fallen 20% from their peak. The music industry's attempts to regain control through litigation and by launching its own legal digital services proved ineffective, as it was "way too late" to reverse the trend. "By 2003, CD sales had dropped 20% from their all-time highs. The industry was not panic. So they tried to litigate themselves back into control, but it was way too late. They tried to innovate themselves back in control with legal digital services, but nothing they did worked." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Music Industry's Misjudgment of CD Burners vs. Digital Files** : The music industry perceived home CD burners as the primary threat, fearing widespread burning and selling of bootleg album copies. They failed to grasp that consumers would be more interested in individual songs and the convenience of digital files, and struggled to comprehend how digital files could transcend physical formats. "Their big concern was the rise of CD burners in home computers. That was the big threat. This illegal piece of plastic that you could hold in your hands. The industry was positive that this would lead to people burning bootleg copies of legitimately released albums and selling those. They honestly didn't get that people would be more interested in individual songs and the convenience of digital files." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Music Industry's New Trend Exploitation** : Record companies, always eager to identify and exploit new trends, actively began signing and marketing groups from the "alternative side of town" after grunge broke through, extending opportunities even to new groups outside Seattle who fit the grunge sound. "Meanwhile, record companies always eager to identify and exploit new trends and tastes. Started signing up and really marketing groups from the alternative side of town." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tFBSrKMEZxI
**Music Industry's Self-Inflicted Wounds in CD Decline** : Several factors contributed to the "great CD die-off" after 2000, some of which were self-inflicted by the recorded music industry. These included the failure to lower CD prices as promised and the cynical decision to eliminate CD singles, forcing consumers to buy entire albums for just one desired song. "After 17 years of consecutive growth, CD sales peaked in 2000. But then a number of things conspired to create the great CD die-off. And some of the reasons were self-inflicted." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Music Industry's Strategy to Kill CD Singles (Late 1990s)** : In the late 1990s, the music industry actively worked to suppress the CD single format. The strategy was to force consumers to buy full albums, even if they only desired one song, leading to the "full price or nothing" mentality. "in the late 90s, the music industry conspired to kill the CD single. They did not want to put up music this way anymore. The thinking became, what, you want this one song? And buy the full CD." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**Music Industry's Struggle to Create Superstars** : The music industry has done a "lousy job" of creating new rock superstars since the turn of the 21st century, being distracted by file sharing, piracy, falling CD prices, and declining revenues, leading to a reliance on mining the 90s through reissues, box sets, and reunions. "The problem I see is that the music industry has done a lousy job of creating new rock super stars. Since they've been distracted by file sharing, piracy, falling CD prices, falling revenues, and the move from CDs to digital, there's been little time devoted to creating a new generation of massive rock bands." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tFBSrKMEZxI
**Music Journalist's Perspective on Music's Purpose** : One journalist succinctly articulated the multifaceted role of music, stating that it serves for "that head, the heart, and for the feet." "one journalist put it very well, he said he'd use music as for that head, the heart, and for the feet." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8UIBWyUt38g
**Music Landscape in 1974** : The music scene in 1974 was considered to be in "bad shape," characterized by inaccessible prog rock, millionaire rock stars detached from reality, "soft" California rock, and irrelevant AM radio pop songs like "The Way We Were" and "The Entertainer." "Music was in bad shape too. There was Prague Rock with its virtuoso performances, that was a big thing, but it was hard for the average person to identify with that." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Music Licensing Bypasses Gatekeepers** : Licensing music provides artists with a direct route to their audience, bypassing traditional industry gatekeepers such as record labels, radio stations, and video channels. This approach allows artists to get paid, gain exposure, and potentially open doors to further opportunities without relying on conventional intermediaries. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Music Licensing Deal Value** : The financial compensation an artist can receive from a licensing deal varies significantly, ranging from a quick $10,000 for some placements to potentially half a million dollars or more for a 20 or 30-second snippet if the advertiser is highly motivated. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Music Licensing Process - Agency Outreach** : Alternatively, music personnel or creative directors at an advertising agency might already be familiar with and like a particular band or song. In such cases, they may proactively reach out to the artist if they identify a suitable placement opportunity. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Music Licensing Process - Pitching** : One method for securing a licensing deal involves artists or their representatives pitching a song to an advertising agency for potential future use in a campaign. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Music Memories Are Stored Separately from All Other Memories** : In 2005, a 71-year-old professional classical musician suffered severe meningitis that destroyed parts of his brain, leaving him with total retrograde amnesia — his entire life was erased — and an inability to form new memories. Yet he could still play complicated pieces learned decades earlier, and could sight-read and memorize new music. In another case, an Ottawa woman named Allison Wojciechowska survived surgery to remove a golf ball-sized brain aneurysm but lost the ability to speak, concentrate, read, or write. However, the moment she was wheeled to a piano, she could play. Music therapists used musical phrases to reconstruct her spoken language — she eventually regained the ability to speak in three languages. "Our music memories are separate and isolated from our day-to-day memories." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NMwMwqMQpf4
**Music Merch Industry Value** : The music merchandise industry is a $3 billion per year business, predominantly involving items such as T-shirts, posters, and programs. "Music Merch is a $3 billion a year business." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=72Lei7uug8c
**Music Monetization Software (Mod Life)** : Tom DeLonge developed a software company called Mod Life, which was specifically designed to help musicians effectively monetize the music and content they produce. This venture reflected his interest in supporting artists' financial independence. "Then there was his software company called Mod Life, which is aimed at helping musicians monetize what they produced." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q-QP7ePxkQE
**Music Overload and Curation Needs** : The infinite supply of music online has led to "music overload," causing fatigue from the extensive time required to sort, research, find, and organize music. This results in faster consumption and burnout of bands and sounds, suggesting a need for trusted filters or curated lists. "after a period of time when we had an infinite amount of music to choose from, we're all getting a little tired of the time it takes to sort through it all." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Music Phenatologist in Palliative Care** : A music phenatologist (from the Greek word for death) is a palliative care specialist who plays or sings soft music for patients in their final hours. They monitor vital signs and consciousness, adjusting the music to alleviate physical, emotional, and spiritual pain. Music can trigger happy memories or emotional releases, naturally increasing dopamine and providing comfort. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHN6db7m3DM
**Music Piracy and File Sharing Era** : The sales figure of 2 million copies for Weezer's "Make Believe" album was considered "not bad" at a time when music piracy and file sharing were prevalent, indicating a challenging commercial environment for artists. "Not bad at the age of music piracy and file sharing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LYRPxtP61JM
**Music Piracy and Its Impact (Early 2000s)** : The early 2000s witnessed an explosion of music piracy, profoundly impacting the music industry, particularly accelerating the cratering of physical music sales. This phenomenon, alongside the rise of digital access to music, further highlighted the need for artists and the industry to find alternative revenue streams, such as music licensing, as demonstrated by Moby's success with "Play." "music piracy exploded and physical sales cratered." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xid8CZf4oYo
**Music Piracy – Dropped Dramatically After Streaming Arrived** : Before streaming took off in the late 2000s, millions of people were stealing music through file sharing, torrents, and bootlegs. Streaming reduced piracy dramatically by offering high-quality music cheaply with no virus risk, incomplete files, or obligation to seed. A practice called stream ripping — recording songs from streams — persists, but piracy is nowhere near what it was. CD sales continue to decline but more slowly than before. "Why bother going through the hassle of stealing something when you could simply be assured of high quality music from streams." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MQ7PDi-D9Ig
**Music Played During Surgery History (1950s)** : The earliest documented instances of music being played in operating rooms during surgery date back to medical journals published in the early 1950s. This practice has become common, with certain songs showing popularity among surgeons. "The first recorded instances of music being played during surgery goes back to medical journals published in the early 1950s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**Music Preference and Fitness Performance Study** : A study commissioned by the website Test Fitness Equipment revealed correlations between music genres and fitness habits. Heavy metal fans were found to be most likely to run a sub-seven-minute mile, while those who listen to punk music during workouts tended to have the longest average workout sessions, at 53 minutes. "A study commissioned by a website, the Test Fitness Equipment, found that heavy metal fans are most likely to be able to run a sub seven minute mile. And for those who listen to punk while working out, tend to have the longest average workouts at fifty three minutes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HoBx6s3JmVQ
**Music Producer's Comprehensive Role** : In the music industry, a producer supervises the creation of a recorded song or album, with the objective of transforming the artist's material into the best possible final product. This role encompasses helping with songwriting, arranging, technical studio setups, budget management, and mixing the music, sometimes including hiring session musicians. "In the music business, the producer is the person who oversees the making of a recording of a song or an album." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Music Revival Cycle Length** : Based on the concept of a 10-year musical generation, revivals in music are expected to occur every 10 to 12 years. "Well, using the rules I just described, we should experience revivals every 10 to 12 years." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=v_am7ifh7Pk
**Music Revivals Across Decades** : Every decade experiences musical revivals, where older sounds are rediscovered, recycled, and reinterpreted with fresh perspectives, a trend that was evident in rock and alt-rock during the 2010s. "Every decade has its share of revivals. Older sounds are rediscovered, recycled, and approached with fresh ears. And there were plenty of rivals in the world of rock and alt rock in the 2010s too." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tdTs-4Irv8c
**Music Revivals and Generational Cycles** : The existence of periodic revivals in music is a predictable outcome as individuals age through and out of their "musical sweet spot" during their formative years. "Extrapolating this reasoning, and as dodges this might seem to some, it's no surprise that we experience periodic revivals in music as we age into, through, and out of that musical sweet spot in our lives." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=v_am7ifh7Pk
**Music Scene Creation Misconception – Few People Necessary** : There is a common misconception that a viable music scene requires a large number of people to come together. However, historical examples, such as the original punk scenes in New York and the UK, and the early English Technopop and Grunge scenes, demonstrate that only a few dozen, or even a dozen, individuals can initiate a sound that spreads globally. "There's a misconception that it takes a lot of people to come together to create a viable music scene. That's just not true." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lip0bZX7hBE
**Music Segmentation Post-1978** : 1978 was a pivotal year in popular music, as genres began to segment into hundreds of distinct substreams, evolving from a simpler categorization of rock, pop, country, and R&B into complex sub-genres like metal, disco, hip hop, and various rock forms. "1978 was a very, very important year. Because that's when popular music began to segment into 100 different distinct streams, sub streams actually." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bYsV3ozbk4M
**Music Sibling Rivalries** : Beyond the Gallagher brothers, other notable sibling rivalries in music include Ray and Dave Davies of The Kinks, the Jackson family, and the Followill brothers of Kings of Leon, all noted for their complex and often challenging relationships. "Ray and Dave Davies of the Kinks, that one's been going on forever. The weirdness that is the Jackson family, and it hasn't always been chocolate and unicorns for the follow-wells in Kings of Leon." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=S6qB5B1PdyA
**Music Theory and Finite Combinations** : Western music is built upon a 12-note scale, with notes mathematically related by semitone intervals, forming chords, melodies, and harmonies. Despite theoretically vast numbers of possible note combinations (over a quintillion when considering durations), the requirement for music to sound "pleasing to the ear" significantly limits viable arrangements, leading to inevitable duplication of musical ideas over time. "Western music is based on a scale of 12 notes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4cZfegexwwk
**Music Therapy for Physical and Emotional Pain** : Research from India suggests that listening to music can alleviate various forms of pain, including headaches and cancer-related pain. Music therapy is also effective in treating depression, anxiety, autism, memory loss, and high blood pressure, with Finnish research indicating it can accelerate stroke patient recovery by up to 60%. While not a cure, it offers significant short-term improvements and can reduce the need for drugs. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHN6db7m3DM
**Music Trends and Economic Recovery** : Historically, when an economy recovers from a recession and general gloom lifts, music tends to become happier and poppier, at least in the short term. "traditionally in music, when the economy bounces back from something awful and the gloom lifts, music tends to get happier and pop here, at least in the short term." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**Music Video Adaptation and Survival (2000s-2010s)** : Despite drastic budget cuts and the decline of traditional music video channels in the 2000s and 2010s, the music video as an art form was not dead but adapted. It evolved to thrive online, embracing new technologies and production methods, leading to "more music videos being produced than ever before." "The music video was not dead. It just adapted." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Music Video Budgets and Recoupable Expenses** : During the music video's golden age, budgets soared, with productions costing up to a million dollars for even mid-level bands. Artists discovered these expenses were "recoupable," meaning the money was clawed back from their earnings, as videos were considered promotional tools, and all promotion was a recoupable cost. "After being convinced to make a video for a million dollars, something that happened with even mid-level bands, they found that money being clawed back from their earnings." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Music Video Conceptualization Curiosity** : There is a natural curiosity about the creative headspace and origins of the imagery, storylines, and overall concepts behind music videos. The "Art Context" podcast intends to address this by offering insights into the directors' thought processes. "I've always I've often watched music videos. And wondered where the hell did this come from? What kind of head space do you have to be in to come up with these images, these storylines, these, you know, things?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5PJRCC6EwUY
**Music Video Creation Process** : The creative process behind music videos involves more than just the final visual product. It includes intricate steps like storyboarding, art direction, and extensive collaboration with various head departments and artists. The "Art Context" podcast aims to provide a "close set behind experience" to reveal this process. "I feel it was the storyboards and the art direction and sitting down with your head departments and the collaborations. So it's pretty much we're giving them that kind of close set behind experience where you get to see the process of how we get to the finish line." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zUQ2vZzmRiI
**Music Video Director Insights** : The Art Context podcast seeks to provide a behind-the-scenes look into music video production, covering directors' inspirations, highs, lows, breakthrough moments, and collaborative processes, to reveal the effort behind the final visual art. "So, it's pretty much, we're giving them that kind of close set behind experience where you get to see the process of how we get to the finish line." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bYsV3ozbk4M
**Music Video Directors** : Director X and Taj Khrichlo are highlighted as two of the biggest music video directors globally. They are responsible for "game-changing videos" for artists such as Drake, Coldplay, and Kendrick Lamar. "The cool thing is the show is hosted by Director X and Taj Khrichlo, two of the biggest music video directors on the planet." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zNIVqKqAlnk
**Music Video Directors Becoming Stars** : The intense competition and increasing production values in music videos during the golden age led to "big name directors" being brought in, some of whom became stars themselves. Notable examples include David Fincher, Spike Jonze, and Mark Romanek, who successfully transitioned from music videos to directing for TV and movies. "Some video directors were so good that they were able to make the jump to TV and movies. They became stars themselves." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Music Video Directors as Storytellers** : Music video directors are recognized as crucial storytellers and creative artists who produce some of the most impactful content globally, garnering significant viewership. There's a perceived lack of appreciation for these creators, prompting efforts to highlight their work, influences, and creative processes. "I feel like we live in the world where we don't give these amazing creative artists the flowers they deserve. They create some of the most impactful content on the planet that gets a lot of eyeballs on it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5PJRCC6EwUY
**Music Video Directors' Importance** : There's a sentiment that music video directors often don't receive the recognition ("flowers they deserve") for their work. These creators are responsible for some of the most "impactful content on the planet" that garners significant viewership. The "Art Context" podcast aims to highlight their stories, journeys, and creative processes. "I feel like we live in the world where we don't give these amazing creative artists, the flowers they deserve. They create some of the most impactful content on the planet that gets a lot of eyeballs on it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zUQ2vZzmRiI
**Music Video Evolution and Decline** : The traditional music video, popular in the 1970s and 80s when seeing a favorite band was a novelty, is considered "dead" in its original form. Once a valuable marketing tool that could generate significant album sales for a half-million dollar investment, videos are now often "sinkholes with no return" due to the constant availability of content on platforms like YouTube via smartphones. The music video is evolving, with some creators utilizing mobile phone video capabilities for production. "At least in the form in which it was born back in the 70s and 80s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yl2poVWguu0
**Music Video Financial Drain for Artists** : Some artists, noting the significant financial drain of music videos, began to resist their production. This mirrored the stance of Led Zeppelin, who never made promo clips, and led artists like Pearl Jam to refuse making videos for years after their initial singles. "Some artists realized that music videos were a big financial drain." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Music Video Importance in 80s** : The 1980s, characterized by the rise of MTV and MuchMusic, saw music videos become a crucial medium for storytelling, dancing, and style, captivating audiences with visually driven content from artists like Madonna, Peter Gabriel, Phil Collins, Michael Jackson, and Aerosmith. "I came up in the 80s era where that's what made me fall in love with music videos, right? The MTV, much music era, watching videos by like Madonna, Peter Gabriel, and like Phil Collins and Michael Jackson and Eros Smith." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bYsV3ozbk4M
**Music Video Influences of Director X** : Director X developed his profound appreciation for music videos during the 1980s MTV and Much Music era, being particularly captivated by the storytelling, dancing, and distinctive style found in videos by artists such as Madonna, Peter Gabriel, Phil Collins, Michael Jackson, and Aerosmith. "I came up I came up in the 80s era where that's what made me fall in love with music videos, right? The MTV, much music era watching videos by like Madonna and Peter Gabriel and like Phil Collins and Michael Jackson and Errol Smith." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IIJySSo276k
**Music Video Matures into Art Form (Early-Mid 90s)** : By the early to mid-1990s, the music video had matured into a "serious art form," moving beyond simple filler or substitutes for live appearances. Videos became "events," often talked about more than the songs themselves, and were expected to "make a statement" or "mean something." "The music video had matured into a serious art form. What used to be filler and ways for artists to skip live appearances became the be-all and end-all." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Music Video Production (Art Context Podcast)** : The "Art Context" podcast, hosted by Director X and Taj Khrishlo, aims to celebrate music video directors as storytellers and creative artists, exploring their journeys, processes, and influences in creating impactful music videos and films, addressing the lack of recognition for their contributions. "I feel like we live in the world where we don't give these amazing creative artists, the flowers they deserve." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bYsV3ozbk4M
**Music Video Production Process** : The "Art Context" podcast aims to delve into the unseen aspects of music video creation, going beyond the final product. It explores the storyboards, art direction, collaboration with department heads, and the entire process of how filmmakers reach the finish line to create a piece of art. "But you don't see what went into to make that product and that piece of art. I feel is the storyboards and the the art direction and sitting down with your head departments and the collaboration." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LgJ7ww2AgF4
**Music Video Production Trends and Budgets** : In the late 1990s/early 2000s, music videos often involved substantial budgets, reportedly up to $200,000, and frequently featured "hot" young directors who produced abstract or unappealing concepts, such as standing in a "cold, cold puddle in a warehouse." This traditional, expensive approach eventually clashed with the rise of cheaper, more accessible video production methods. "We would do these videos for 200 grand and they'd hire some student out of Mexico City. He was all the hot **** and he'd make a stand in a cold, cold puddle in a warehouse and I'm like, well, what the hell is this about? This is stupid." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=W9gdKha0PLg
**Music Video Revolution - 1980s** : The 1980s saw a "music video revolution" sparked by "telegenic British bands on MTV." "The 80s began with all those telegenic British bands on MTV, which set off the music video revolution." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DSvoppf1dGY
**Music Video Status Decline (2000)** : By 2000, the status of music videos had significantly declined; they were no longer regarded as major events or standalone art productions. Instead, they reverted to being short-term promotional vehicles, as the concept, "having been perfected in the 80s," was felt to be "exhausted." "The status of videos began to decline. They were no longer events, or these standalone artsy productions. They morphed back into short-term promotional vehicles." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Music Video Storytelling Process** : The podcast aims to provide a behind-the-scenes look at music video creation, exploring elements like storyboards, art direction, collaboration with head departments, and the overall process from conception to completion. "But you don't see what went into to make that product and that piece of art, I feel, there was the storyboards and the art direction and sitting down with your head departments and the collaborations." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kbFfT0pKchI
**Music Video Transformation (Early 1980s)** : The appearance of music videos in the early 1980s marked a pivotal moment that fundamentally transformed the entire music industry. "You might remember the appearance of the music video in the early 1980s, which transformed the industry." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Music Video as Art Form** : The hosts of "Art Context" express a deep fascination with music videos, stemming from the 1980s MTV and Much Music era, appreciating their storytelling, dancing, and style as a significant art form. "And I was always fascinated by music videos and the storytelling and the dancing and the style and all that stuff." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g-tBn8dsSqw
**Music Video as Impactful Content – Underrated Creative Art** : Music video directors believe their work creates some of the most impactful content globally, garnering many eyeballs, yet these creative artists often don't receive the recognition they deserve. The "Art Katex" podcast aims to give them their "flowers." "I feel like we live in the world where we don't give these amazing creative artists the flowers they deserve." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OcylgiXkDP8
**Music Videos Escaped Television** : A summarizing sentiment about the evolution of music videos is that "Music videos aren't dead. They just escaped television." This implies that the art form continues to thrive and evolve, but its primary distribution and consumption channels have moved from traditional broadcast television to online platforms. "Music videos aren't dead. They just escaped television." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Music Videos Lose Novelty and MTV Shifts Focus (Late 90s)** : By the late 1990s, music videos began to lose their novelty, and fewer people were excited about them as they had been in earlier years. This decline in interest contributed to MTV's decision to broaden its programming beyond music, shifting towards reality TV and personality-based shows. "Music videos had begun to lose their novelty, and fewer and fewer people were excited about them, like they were in the old days, which really was just a few years earlier." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Music Videos as Impactful Content** : Music video directors are credited with creating "some of the most impactful content on the planet that gets a lot of eyeballs on it," highlighting their significant contribution to visual culture. "They create some of the most impactful content on the planet that gets a lot of eyeballs on it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kbFfT0pKchI
**Music and Brain Lobes** : The experience of music actively engages all four major lobes of the brain, with different regions responsible for specific functions such as emotional reactions, memory formation, and movement motivated by music. Scientists have only recently begun to precisely map which brain parts are dedicated to processing music. "Experiencing music requires all four of the brain's major lobes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**Music and Dopamine – Neurologically, Sex, Drugs, and Rock and Roll Are the Same Thing** : Deep inside the brain, the amygdala, hippocampus, and cerebellum produce a hormone called dopamine — the body's feel-good chemical. The body produces dopamine during orgasm, during cocaine use, and when hearing a great song that gives you chills and makes you want to dance. Neurologically speaking, sex, drugs, and rock and roll produce the same chemical reward. "Our brains are built to respond to sex drugs and rock and roll." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NMwMwqMQpf4
**Music and Satanic Allegations** : Throughout human history, certain types of music have been feared due to alleged connections to Satan or the "Dark Lord," who is blamed or credited with inspiring "evil music" that is believed to incite "bad things" or "bad thoughts" in mortals. "It is a fact of human history that certain types of music will scare people. They get scared because this music allegedly comes from Satan himself." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**Music as Identity and Comfort Food** : Music consumed between the ages of 14 and 24 becomes deeply intertwined with an individual's identity, used first to understand oneself and then to project that identity to the world (e.g., "I'm a punk kid," "I'm a metal kid," "I'm a goth kid"). This music remains "comfort food forever" for the rest of their lives. "If you grew up in the 90s, if you were between 14 and 24 in the 90s, that will be your music for the rest of your life. It is going to be your comfort food forever." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4xxgPX-aR90
**Music as Sustenance During Slavery** : Over centuries of forced displacement to the West, music, including work songs, protest songs, and satirical songs, served as a crucial means of sustenance for enslaved African people. These songs, with regular or syncopated rhythms, helped them endure brutal times and preserved their identities, history, and traditions. "And these songs and rhythms helped sustain them through those awful brutal times." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hQJ3r-DCoMQ
**Music as a Cultural Feedback Loop** : Music functions as a "cultural feedback loop," inherently reflecting "the attitudes, hopes, wishes, fears and anger of society at large," especially when driven by the 15-25 age demographic, as Alt Rock was with Gen X in the 90s. "Again, music is a cultural feedback loop. It tends to reflect the attitudes, hopes, wishes, fears and anger of society at large." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=amHre9ZZFkU
