"Open AI was a nonprofit founded in San Francisco in 2015 with the goal of researching AI and creating safe and beneficial, that's their words, artificial intelligence." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Openness to Music (Adolescence vs. Adulthood)** : Young people, particularly adolescents, exhibit greater "open iridness" or openness to music, including unfamiliar styles. A 2013 study of 250,000 people found that adolescents devoted 20% of their waking time to music, compared to 13% in adulthood. This decline in adulthood is partly due to increased busyness but also to "psychosocial maturation," where adults rely less on external factors like music to define their identity. "In fact, our desire for musical discovery and preference for new music is at its all-time peak when we are 23 for women and 24 for men." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m7MbV-eCLyE
**Opera Singer Projection Techniques** : Opera singers developed their distinctive vocal projection techniques out of necessity, needing to project their voices to "vast crowds" without any microphones or amplification. This need directly influenced the architectural design of opera houses, which were built to facilitate the natural flow of sound from the stage back over multi-level audiences. "No mics or amps. They needed to project to vast crowds, which is also where the design of opera houses came in." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qrrwqdpCjpQ
**Opposition to Digital Audio Tools** : Modern opposition to digital audio manipulation, such as Auto-tune and digital editing/composing tools, mirrors the historical resistance to multi-track recording innovations in the early 1950s. This reflects a recurring pattern of discomfort with new technologies that alter the perceived "purity" of musical creation. "Their opposition was just like what we see with digital trick-or-age such as auto-tune and the editing and composing tools available to any musician today." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Opposition to New Technology** : Historically, the introduction of new technology consistently meets two reactions: some believe the old ways are inherently superior and will always be, while others confidently embrace and adapt to the new. This pattern applies across various technological shifts in music, from recording methods to instruments. "When something new is introduced, there will always be people who say A, the old ways are better and will always be better. And B, oh I can figure out how to work the new ways, no problem." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Optimal Daily Music Listening for Health** : A study conducted in France has determined that listening to music for exactly 78 minutes daily can contribute positively to overall health. For maximum health benefits, this daily listening should be structured into specific emotional categories: 14 minutes of uplifting music for happiness, 16 minutes of calming music, 16 minutes of music to counteract sadness, 15 minutes of motivational music for concentration, and 17 minutes of music specifically for dealing with anger. "A study from France determined the exact amount of music that we should listen to every day so we stay in good health, and that number is exactly 78 minutes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**Optimal Ticket Purchase Time for Fair Value** : Studies suggest that the "fair market value" for a concert ticket, balancing demand and willingness to pay, is often achieved 24 to 48 hours before the showtime, regardless of the seller. This indicates that waiting closer to the event may result in better prices as demand moderates or secondary sellers try to unload inventory. "Study show that the fair market value of a concert ticket, that balance between demand and what we're willing to pay, is usually achieved 24 to 48 hours before showtime." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Orgy – Named for an Encounter with a Drag Queen That Embodied Their Mix of Influences** : Inspired by an encounter with a drag queen that the band felt encapsulated their sound — an orgy of multiple influences coming together. "It was inspired by some kind of encounter with a drag queen." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Origin of "Record Album" Term** : The terms "phonograph album" or "record album" originated from the early 20th-century practice of packaging multiple 10-inch, 78 RPM discs. These individual records were bound together in book-like formats with paperboard covers and sleeves, resembling a photo album. The German company Odeon first introduced such bundles in 1909. "It was an album for records, and this is where the term phonograph album or record album came from." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Origin of "Smells Like Teen Spirit" Title** : The title for Nirvana's iconic song "Smells Like Teen Spirit" originated from a comment made by Kathleen Hannah to Kurt Cobain in 1991. She remarked that he smelled "sweet, almost like a particular type of deodorant marketed to teens," unaware that "Teen Spirit" was an actual brand of deodorant that Cobain did not use. "She remarked that Kurt smelled sweet, almost like a particular type of deodorant marketed to teens. Kurt was confused because he never wore deodorant. He thought she was referring to his undying punk rock ethos and was so inspired by what she said that he used her remark as the title of a new song. Smells like teen spirit. In the case you don't know, teen spirit is actually the name of deodorant." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Eo32mAZd8DI
**Origin of "Stick a Sock in It"** : In the early days of phonographs and gramophones, the volume of a record was fixed, determined by the size of its acoustic horn. To reduce volume, listeners would insert a ball of fabric, known as a "sock," into the horn to muffle the sound. This practice is the origin of the common phrase "stick a sock in it." "That ball of fabric was known as a sock. And now you know where the phrase stick a sock in it comes from." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NAroxFbYkRw
**Origin of HTTP and WWW in URLs** : The "HTTP" in a website address stands for Hypertext Transfer Protocol, a set of rules for transferring files online. "WWW" stands for World Wide Web, where the URL resides. The "slash slash" (//) is a holdover from computer code written for the Apollo missions to the moon. Tim Berners-Lee, who first assembled this in spring 1992, has expressed regret for the complexity and wished he had designed something better. "The www is worldwide web, of course, which is where the URL lives. What's with that slash slash thing? That's a holdover from the computer code that was written for the Apollo missions to the moon." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**Origin of On-Hold Music (1962)** : The concept of on-hold music originated in 1962 with Alfred Levy, who owned a factory in New York. A loose wire in his phone system touched a metal girder, turning his building into a giant radio antenna that picked up a signal from a nearby radio station. Callers placed on hold would hear this music, leading Levy to realize it kept them occupied and confirmed they were still connected. "This meant that every time someone was put on hold, this interference with the phone system resulted in music leaking through to the callers who were waiting." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D8GU8ZpSgY8
**Origin of Paid Public Concerts (1672)** : The modern concept of a paid public concert, where tickets are sold in advance for a specific performance in a private venue, is traced back to 1672. In London, John Bannister organized and performed a concert for which the general public paid one shilling for admission in his home, marking the earliest known evidence of such an event. "The general public was allowed into the gig after they bought a ticket, which sold for the price of one shilling. And this happened in 1672." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D8GU8ZpSgY8
**Origin of the Name "Compact Disc"** : The name "Compact Disc" was proposed by Philips, drawing inspiration from their earlier invention, the "compact cassettes" developed in 1963. This name was mutually agreed upon for the new music storage format. "As for the name, well, that was Phillips idea. They invented the compact cassettes in 1963. That's what you actually call these things compact cassettes. So it was agreed that the new format should be called the compact disk." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Origin of the Phrase "Stick a Sock in It"** : In the early days, if one wanted a phonograph to play music more quietly, a ball of fabric, referred to as a "sock," would be placed in the acoustic horn that served as a speaker. This practice is the origin of the common idiom "stick a sock in it." "That ball of fabric was called a sock, and this is the origin of the phrase, stick a sock in it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gP3oQeYEvso
**Origin of the Term "Grunge"** : Mark Arm, a member of Green River, worked at the warehouse at MuZac Corporation and, in the early 1980s, wrote a letter to a local entertainment weekly describing his pre-Green River band as "Pure Grunge." This letter is believed to be the first recorded instance of anyone referring to the Seattle sound using the "G word." "In the early 80s, he wrote a letter to one of the local entertainment weeklies describing his pre-Green River band as Pure Grunge. And this letter seems to be the first time anyone referred to this Seattle sound using the G word." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sCtQqVBtCaI
**Origin of the Term "Record Album"** : The term "record album" originated from the practice of packaging collections of 78 RPM records. Due to their limited capacity, classical works or longer pieces required 9, 10, 12, or even 15 individual records. These were bound into sleeves resembling photo albums, which then became known as "record albums." Such collections were "hideously expensive," costing $100 or more in the early 20th century. "So they stuffed 9, 10, 12, 15 individual records into sleeves that were bound into books that looked like photo albums. Except these were called record albums. Now, you know where the term came from." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VlIErdSSVOM
**Origin of the Term "Riot Girl"** : The precise origin of the term "Riot Girl" is uncertain. Possible inspirations include the 1991 Mount Pleasant disturbance (race riots in Washington, D.C.), Jen Smith of Bratmobile using "Girl Riot" in a letter, and Kathleen Hanna and Tobi Vail's 1991 zine titled "Riot Girl" with three 'R's, aiming to convey fierceness and reclaim the word "girl" with a "growl." These various influences converged to define the movement's name. "So why the name Riot Girl? We're not entirely sure." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qss5kC2FUx0
**Origin of the Word "Bootleg" – Prohibition-Era Hidden Alcohol** : The term "bootleg" dates to the Prohibition era in the United States, when illegal alcohol was transported and sold clandestinely, often hidden inside the shaft of tall boots. "The word 'bootleg' dates to Prohibition, when illegal alcohol was hidden in the shaft of boots." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OrHtPVBjXw4
**Original "Ghostbusters" Film Theme** : The original "Ghostbusters" film, starring Bill Murray, Harold Ramis, Ernie Hudson, and Dan Aykroyd, did not initially feature Ray Parker Jr.'s iconic theme song. A different, less effective theme was used in an early film plea to Canadian movie chains to encourage them to carry the movie before Ray Parker Jr. was brought in to create the well-known version. "The original theme was used in a film plea to Canadian movie chains to please carry the film. It wasn't very good." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HoBx6s3JmVQ
**Origins and Reasons for the Musical Fade Out** : The introduction of magnetic tape into the recording process made the musical fade-out possible, as earlier mechanical recording methods directly onto master disks did not allow for such editing or manipulation and imposed strict length constraints (e.g., 4 minutes per side for 10-inch 78 RPM records). The precise origin of the fade-out is unknown, but it gained popularity for several practical and artistic reasons: composers and arrangers sometimes lacked definitive endings for their pieces; it allowed songs to be shortened for radio play (especially 7-inch singles often limited to under three minutes); producers or artists could emphasize a song's hook repeatedly; some appreciated the impression of a song continuing indefinitely; and others simply found it aesthetically pleasing. This technique has been a part of recorded music since at least the late 1940s or early 1950s. "We do know that the first song to fade out came after the introduction of magnetic tape in the recording process. Before tape, all music was recorded mechanically, directly onto a master disk. No overdubs, no punching into fixed up mistakes, no editing. What was played was what was captured. The first successful recording format was the 10-inch 78 RPM record. It had a maximum capacity of about four minutes per side. And that gave songwriters musicians and recording engineers a hard deadline for the length of any piece. When tape came along, various manipulations like editing and overdubs became possible, and so did the idea of fading out a song. ... Well, some composers and arrangers didn't have an ending, so it was just easier to let everything fade away. Sometimes songs were just too long for radio. Radio stations didn't like songs that were more than three minutes long back then, so the quickest way to solve that time issue was to fade out the song before the three minute mark on the seven-inch singles. Sometimes the producer or the artist wanted to emphasize the hook of the song over and over again as the song ended to burn it into the listener's mind. Some people like the fade because it left the impression that the song never ended and that it could go on forever. And others did it because, well, they just thought it sounded cool. Whatever the true history, the fade out has been with us since at least the late 1940s or the very early 1950s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0D4wGWI-_z8
**Orphaned Royalties and Performing Rights Organizations** : Revenues from songs with insufficient metadata, like "I Love You," may be held in an account while performing rights collectives globally attempt to identify and compensate the rightful author. "And any number of performing rights collectives around the planet will try to track down the rightful author of that particular song called, I love you and pay them." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Osama Bin Laden Had Photos of Avril Lavigne and the B-52s on His Hard Drives** : During the raid on his compound in Pakistan, files recovered from Osama bin Laden's hard drives were found to include photos of Avril Lavigne and the B-52s. "During the raid on his compound in Pakistan, photos of Avril Lavigne (and the B-52s) were found on his hard drives." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0DFmS6MPbFI
**Osama bin Laden's Music Preference** : Following his death by US Seals in May 2011, Osama bin Laden's hard drives were searched, revealing a significant amount of pornographic material, but also indicating he was a fan of The B-52s, particularly their song "Rock Lobster." "After Osama bin Laden was killed by US seals in May 2011, they went through his hard drives and they found a lot of porn. But he was also apparently a fan of the B-52s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HoBx6s3JmVQ
**Oscar Wilde's Trial and Imprisonment (1895)** : In 1895, the renowned English playwright Oscar Wilde was put on trial for "homosexual practices," found guilty, and sentenced to two years in jail. He never recovered from the ordeal and died shortly after his release. "In 1895, the famous English playwright Oscar Wilde was put on trial for homosexual practices." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hswtuenFwvM
**Other Canadian Punk Scenes** : While Toronto's punk scene was the largest, other Canadian cities such as Hamilton, London, Winnipeg, and Vancouver also developed punk rock scenes. "There were punk rock scenes in other Canadian cities, Hamilton, London, Winnipeg, Vancouver. But none of them were quite as big as what took root in Toronto." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Other Early MP3 Players (Post-Rio)** : Following the Diamond Rio, the market saw the introduction of several other MP3 players, including one from Audible.com designed for audiobooks (capable of holding up to two hours of highly compressed audio), the Sensory Science Rave, the iJAM IH100, and the Creative Labs Nomad. These devices contributed to the growing diversity of portable digital music options. "Audible.com had an MP3 player for audiobooks, which held up to two hours of really compressed audio. Then there was the sensory science rave, the IJAM IH100. And the Creative Labs Nomad, which I do." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**Outcry Against Early "Dirty Lyrics" (c. 1900)** : When gramophones and phonographs became popular around 1900, the rapid recording and spread of songs with "dirty lyrics" quickly led to public outrage. Critics expressed alarm at what "this new technology is bringing us," decrying it as "foul." "It's foul, I tell you. Fowl!" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Outsider Art Origin (Art Brut)** : The concept of "outsider art," originally known as "art brute," first emerged in France during the 1920s. This term specifically referred to art that transcended the avant-garde, created by individuals who were either children or those suffering from mental illness. "The progenitor of outsider music is outsider art, a concept that first arose in France in the 1920s when it was simply known as art brute." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HcK4M7-02ik
**Overcoming Algorithm Bubbles** : To discover new music and avoid being trapped in an "algorithm bubble" that only suggests more of what you already like, it is recommended to listen to online radio stations from different parts of the world, which offer diverse musical viewpoints, or to seek out live music performances. "Don't rely on the algorithm if you're listing, if you're streaming because the algorithm only gives you more of what you've already listened to and you end up being in a bubble and you really, you know, explore beyond that bubble." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4xxgPX-aR90
**Overmorrow** : An older or more efficient word meaning "the day after tomorrow." "What's overmoral? Well, that's a more efficient way of saying the day after tomorrow." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Overwhelming Preference for Singles in Streaming** : Listeners overwhelmingly prefer singles on streaming services, with album tracks receiving almost zero attention. This questions the purpose of recording non-single tracks for albums in the current environment. "If you look at what people are listening to, it's overwhelmingly to singles. Film tracks on streaming services get almost zero attention." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Ownership of Master Sound Recordings** : Some artists are savvy or fortunate enough to also own their master sound recordings, giving them control over copying and distribution instead of the record label. Owning masters is a significant deal and another source of income, highly desired by catalog acquisition companies. "However, some artists are savvy or lucky enough to also own their master sound recordings. They dictate who can copy and distribute their work, not the record label. Many of your masters is a big, big deal. It's another source of income." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Oxnard Skate Park (1975)** : In 1975, the city of Oxnard, California, built a skate park that became a significant magnet for many kids, establishing the location as a "ground zero" for skate punk. "If there is a ground zero for skate punk, it's probably an Oxnard California. And that's because in 1975, the city built a skate park." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=44dXy0StjIQ
**Oz Beldon's High-Altitude Ground Performance** : Oz Beldon, the founder of "Music for Children," achieved the record for the highest musical performance with his feet remaining on the ground. He delivered a 40-minute show for a group of hikers on the summit of Mount Mera in the Himalayas, performing at an impressive altitude of 21,825 feet. "He performed a 40-minute show for a group of hikers on the summit of Mount Mira in the Himalayas. And this took place at an altitude of 21,825 feet." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3eGBnwPWRGo
**Ozzy Osbourne Worked in a Slaughterhouse Before Black Sabbath – Threw Animal Organs at Concert Audiences** : Before forming Black Sabbath, Ozzy Osbourne worked in a slaughterhouse. On tour he requested animal organs — livers, testicles — to throw at the audience, who would often respond by throwing dead animals back at him. "Worked in a slaughterhouse before Black Sabbath, and on tour requested animal organs to throw at the audience." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qtdetc5-MQo
**Pacific Northwest Music Wave** : The next significant indie wave emerged in the Pacific Northwest, characterized by slower music, louder and lower guitars, and a "uniform" of flannel, indicative of the grunge movement. "It was a continental way in the Pacific Northwest, where the music was slower, the guitars louder and lower, and where the uniforms were made a flannel." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sCtQqVBtCaI
**Pacific Northwest as a "Petri Dish"** : The Pacific Northwest fostered grunge by blending diverse musical influences like Black Sabbath, Kiss, Led Zeppelin, and hardcore punk (Dead Kennedys, Black Flag, Bad Religion) with local conditions such as rainy weather, coffee, drugs, universities, and isolation from major tours, allowing these influences to develop "unmolested." "Mixing rainy weather, lots of coffee, a variety of drugs, a ton of universities, and the fact that the area wasn't included on the itineraries of a lot of major tours, and you end up with a petri dish, all these influences that were allowed to develop unmolecced it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tFBSrKMEZxI
**Pain and Addictive Nature of Tattoos** : While getting a tattoo is painful, particularly in areas like the elbow and knee where bone is close to the surface, this pain can induce an "adrenaline rush" and "endorphin rush." This physiological response contributes to a "dicking element" (addictive quality), making the process pleasurable for many and leading them to seek more tattoos. "There's this adrenaline rush. There's this endorphin rush that you get. That's why I think it's a dicking element because a lot of people get that rush. And the pain has gone and now it's pleasurable. Not feels good." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jZeMmPekKMQ
**Palace Hotel / Hilly's on Bowery** : Before becoming CBGB, the location at 315 Bowery in Manhattan was a dive bar beneath the inappropriately named Palace Hotel. It was a dark, dingy, narrow room (167 feet long, 25 feet wide) frequented by the Hells Angels. "The Hells Angels used to hang out at a dive located beneath the very inappropriately named Palace Hotel, a 315-barry and bleaker in Manhattan." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VYi3r0G-ne4
**Pan Am Flight 103 Missed Flights (1988)** : Several notable figures narrowly avoided the Pan Am Flight 103 bombing over Lockerbie, Scotland, on December 21, 1988. Actress Kim Cattrall changed her flight for last-minute shopping, then-world No. 1 tennis player Matt Zvilandr did not board for an unknown reason, The Four Tops overslept after performing on BBC's Top of the Pops, and Johnny Lydon and his wife Nora missed the flight by minutes due to packing delays, a fight, and traffic. "What's not widely known are the people who didn't die because they weren't on the plane when they should have been." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hmnMG6f80Sg
**Panda Mating Songs** : When a male panda is interested in mating, it communicates this interest by singing. The sound produced by a male panda is described as more akin to a bleat, but for a female panda, this sound is perceived as "sweet, sweet music." "The sound of a male panda is more like a bleat. But as far as the female panda is concerned, it's sweet, sweet music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**Panic at the Disco – From a Line in One of Their Own Early Songs** : The band had an early song called "Panic" containing the line "panic at the disco, sat back and took it slow." The exclamation point (!), which migrated in and out of the name, was never officially part of it from the band's perspective. Guitarist Ryan Ross said fans added it, and it was eventually officially dropped. "It was never part of the name to us." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Parallel Groove Records – Two Independent Spirals on One Side; Monty Python's Matching Tie and Handkerchief (1973)** : A parallel groove record places two completely independent audio spirals on the same side, creating three sides on a two-sided record — whichever groove the stylus catches first determines what you hear. Monty Python's 1973 album Matching Tie and Handkerchief confused people who kept getting different audio each time they dropped the needle on side one. This technique dates to 1901 when Victor issued a fortune-telling record with three different fortunes on the same side. Artists who have used it include Laurie Anderson, the Sugar Cubes, Stereolab, Fine Young Cannibals, Marillion, Mr. Bungle, Kate Bush, Garbage, and Jack White. Tool's 1992 Opiate EP has a double groove that plays either the listed track or a hidden bonus track ("The Gaping Lotus Experience"). "People couldn't figure out why they kept getting different audio each time they dropped the needle on side one." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=I291j5WBc4k
**Parallel Universe of Alt-rock** : Before the 1990s, various alt-rock scenes and sub-genres had been "happily evolving almost unseen for years" in a "parallel universe," leading to a situation where the people making this music were seasoned and knew what they were doing when they finally broke into the mainstream. "And because these sounds and scenes and sub-genres had been happily evolving almost unseen for years, the people making this music knew what they were doing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=amHre9ZZFkU
**Parallel Universe of Alternative Fans (Late 1980s)** : At the end of the 1980s, fans of non-mainstream rock in North America existed in a "parallel universe," deliberately separating themselves from mainstream culture including hair metal, classic rock, and Top 40 radio. They found camaraderie in small, dark clubs and indie record stores, sharing similar views and musical sensibilities. "You're living in this parallel universe, a petri dish with a lot of people who share your views and sensibilities and tolerances for music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OiqdZfxdBP0
**Partial Catalog Sales** : Artists can choose to sell only a portion of their song catalog, rather than the entire portfolio, as demonstrated by figures like Korn, Tom DeLonge, and Dave Navarro. "You don't have to sell all your catalog either. It's like corn, tom de long, Dave Navarro and many others have sold just a portion of their portfolio of songs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Pasturella Pestus Bacteria** : Pasturella Pestus is identified as the bacteria responsible for the Black Plague. Ash Boo Shultz of USS used this scientific name as the title for a song part he had developed, demonstrating a unique source of lyrical inspiration and linking it to the concept of depression. "Pasturella Pestus. That's the bacteria that was responsible for the black plague." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=N-3eddy4J7g
**Patent Disputes in Early Electric Guitar Industry** : The burgeoning electric guitar industry was marked by numerous patent fights and disputes over who held the rights to various technologies. One individual even attempted to sue all competitors, claiming sole ownership of all electric guitar patents. "There were patent fights and arguments about who had the right to do what with the technology. And one guy tried to sue everyone, saying that he and he alone owned all the patents to the electric guitar." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E6cXyaHALBQ
**Patience in Music Industry** : Success stories of bands like Red Hot Chili Peppers, Muse, R.E.M., and The Black Keys emphasize the critical importance of patience and foresight from record labels, managers, and all parties involved in an artist's welfare. If genuine potential is recognized, a long-term strategy, potentially spanning years, is required. "These success stories underscore the need for patience and foresight on behalf of record labels, managers and everyone else associated with the welfare of a particular artist." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8sAxFvktx-A
**Paul Allen's Investment in Ticketmaster (1990s)** : Paul Allen, co-founder of Microsoft, recognized the growing business of concert ticket sales and invested $325 million USD to acquire an 80% stake in Ticketmaster. His involvement, providing access to the latest Microsoft technology, significantly contributed to Ticketmaster's early lead in online ticketing. "Paul Allen, the co-founder of Microsoft, recognized this and he spent $325 million US dollars to acquire an 80% stake in the company." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Paul McCartney's Health Regimen – Eye Yoga and Daily Unsupported Headstands** : Paul McCartney does "eye yoga" (exercises to maintain sharp sight) and every morning performs an unsupported headstand for five minutes. "Dude, whatever works." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**Paul Mooney's World's Largest Private Record Collection (2008)** : Paul Mooney of Pittsburgh amassed the world's largest private record collection, which started about 50 years prior with a Frankie Lane 45. By early 2008, it contained over 3 million records (LPs, EPs, 78s, 45s, cassettes, eight tracks, CD singles, full albums) and 300,000 compact discs, covering every genre. The collection grew so vast that it required a 16,000 square foot climate-controlled warehouse, and was put up for sale on eBay for a minimum bid of $3 million, with an estimated worth closer to $50 million, though the auction fell through. "Paul Mooney, reluctant owner, still, of the world's largest private record collection." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zWNzlSxrj6s
**Paul Pontius – Immortal Records Executive** : Paul Pontius worked at Immortal Records, Korn's label in 1994. He is notable as the uncle of Justine Ferrarra, the eight-year-old model who posed for Korn's self-titled debut album cover. His family was significantly displeased with the creepy and sinister aesthetic of the final artwork. "She's the niece of Paul Pontius, who worked at Immortal Records, Korn's label at the time." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Payola** : Payola refers to the illegal practice of paying radio stations or DJs to play a song without disclosing the payment. While illegal, it has historically occurred, particularly in the US, which is described as the biggest payola market worldwide. "no one pays a radio station to play a song, at least not legally. That's called Peola and such pay for play is illegal." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Pearl Jam – Named After Neil Young; Earlier Named After Basketball Player Mookie Blalock** : The fabricated story about Eddie Vedder's grandmother Pearl and her hallucinogenic jam spread was invented by Eddie himself, who has admitted it. The band was originally called Mookie Blalock after the New Jersey Nets point guard, but lawyers made noise about trademark use. They liked the word "Pearl" and after seeing Neil Young jam at a New Jersey concert, combined the two. "He made that all up." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Peer Licensing by Independent Bands** : Around the same time The Apples in Stereo, a fiercely independent band, made their licensing deal with Sony, similar indie bands they admired, such as Spiritualized and Stereolab, were also making deals, specifically with Volkswagen. This trend highlighted a new form of legitimacy and financial opportunity for independent artists through commercial placements. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Percentage-Based Service Charges** : Since the 1990s, service charges have shifted from flat fees to being calculated as a percentage of the ticket's face value. This change means that as ticket prices increase, the service charge also rises significantly, impacting the final cost for consumers. "And remember, service charges are often set as a percentage of the face value of the ticket." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Perception of Canadian Music (Pre-1990s)** : Before the early 1990s, there was a prevalent belief across Canada that Canadian music was substandard. This perception significantly changed with the emergence of successful Canadian rock bands, leading to a strong nationalistic pride and demand for local talent. "Up until that point, there was generally across the country the feeling the Canadian music was substandard." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=y7kmkWJ54ww
**Perfect Cultural Storm** : A "perfect cultural storm" occurs when musical, social, political, and demographic factors align at the right time, creating trends that capture imagination. These storms typically last about six years: two years of build-up, a 12 to 18-month "frenzy peak," followed by a "long, slow denouement." "Every once in a while we end up with a perfect cultural storm." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5q5EnZj1FwE
**Performing Rights Organization (PRO)** : A PRO is an organization, found in most countries, responsible for collecting royalties on behalf of artists and copyright holders for the public performance of songs. They grant licenses to any entity that plays music in public—such as clubs, venues, stores, and radio stations—and then distribute the collected money to the music's owners. "These are companies that collect money on behalf of artists and copyright holders for public performances of songs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Periods of Cultural Revival** : History shows a cyclical pattern where old productions, movies, TV shows, and musical sounds are constantly brought back and renewed, sometimes by generations removed from the original. Examples include Broadway revivals (West Side Story, Porgy and Bess), movie reboots (Ghostbusters, King Kong, Spider-Man, A Star Is Born), and TV remakes (90210, Twin Peaks, Star Trek). "It is a fact of life that there are periods where everything old is new again." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9CNNsqXiL6U
**Permanence of Tattoos** : Unlike other trends such as clothing or hairstyles, tattoos are permanent. Once they are acquired, they are "there for life," and cannot simply be shed as one "moves on to other forms of addictions or life forms," emphasizing the lasting commitment involved. "But what tattoos is, once you got them, you got them. And you can move on to other forms of addictions or life forms, you know, being, I'm going to add a lessons to adults and having a real job, again, a quote unquote, a real job. But what you have those tattoos, they're there for life." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jZeMmPekKMQ
**Persistent Decline in Album Sales** : Album sales have been consistently dropping at a double-digit rate for years, indicating a long-term downward trend. "Things have been dropping at a double digit rate for years." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Persistent Problem of Ticket Scalping** : Ticket scalping, involving resellers and speculators, remains a significant issue for live music fans. Despite nearly two centuries of legal attempts to eliminate it, the practice continues to thrive. "The law has been trying to stamp them out for almost two centuries. And guess what? No luck." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D8GU8ZpSgY8
**Peter Capaldi (12th Doctor) Was in a Punk Band Called The Dream Boats with Craig Ferguson** : Actor Peter Capaldi, who played the 12th Doctor on Doctor Who, was in a punk band called The Dream Boats when he was younger. Fellow bandmate was comedian Craig Ferguson. "Also when the band was comedian Craig Ferguson." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Peter Saville – Joy Division Unknown Pleasures (Pulsar CP1919 Signal); New Order Blue Monday (Lost £9,000 on Every Copy)** : Peter Saville from Manchester became the official go-to designer for Factory Records. His inspirations were classical arts, religious imagery, and the industrial world. Joy Division's Unknown Pleasures album cover shows 100 consecutive pulses from pulsar CP1919 — a dying star in the center of the Dumbbell Nebula in the constellation Vulpecula, essentially screaming radio waves. The Blue Monday 12-inch (1983) was designed to look like a computer floppy disk, with no band name or song title visible, and a series of colored blocks encoding a message that could only be cracked with a key included in New Order's next album Power Corruption and Lies. The code was supposed to say "Blue Monday" but due to an error it actually reads "X96." The sleeve was so expensive to produce that Factory Records lost 5p (about £0.05 / 30p accounting for 1983 values) on every single copy sold — and Blue Monday went on to sell 3 million copies, the biggest-selling 12-inch single ever, generating a net loss of approximately £9,000 on the artwork alone. "New Order and Factory actually lost 30 pints on every single copy they sold." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JdSUHkXM1mQ
**Petrochemical Products and Vinyl Costs** : The Arab Oil Embargo's impact on oil prices directly raised the cost of all petrochemical products, including the raw material used to manufacture vinyl records. "This not only affected the price of gas, but also the price of all petrochemical products, including the raw material used for vinyl records." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Phantom of the Paradise Box Office & Cult Status** : 'Phantom of the Paradise' was a box office flop globally, except in Winnipeg, Canada, where it became a cult classic, staying in theaters for months and becoming an "essential part of the musical DNA of that city," inspiring tribute bands and annual festivals. "The movie was complete box office flop everywhere in the world except bizarrely in Winnipeg, where it stayed in theaters for months." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Phantom of the Paradise Plot** : The 1974 rock musical horror comedy 'Phantom of the Paradise' tells the story of Winslow Leech, whose music is stolen by the evil Swan (Paul Williams' character), leading to Leech being disfigured in a "bizarre vinyl pressing plant accident" and subsequently terrorizing Swan's music venue, The Paradise, in disguise. "It tells the story of poor Winslow Leech whose music is stolen by the evil Swan. That's Paul Williams character. And then horribly disfigured in a bizarre vinyl pressing plant accident." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Phil Daniels - Quadrophenia Actor** : Phil Daniels is an actor known for playing Jimmy the mod in the film Quadrophenia. He provided the Cockney bits for Blur's 1994 title track "Park Life." "The Cockney Bits are courtesy of an actor named Phil Daniels. If you ever saw the movie Quadrophynya, he's the guy who played Jimmy the mod." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DSvoppf1dGY
**Phil Smith – Oasis Roadie** : Phil Smith was a roadie for Oasis who, together with sound guy Mark Coyle, brought a blow-up globe to the "Definitely Maybe" album cover photoshoot. This prop was included as a visual representation of the band's collective ambition for "world domination." "Mark Coil, the sound guy for Oasis, and a roadie named Phil Smith found it and brought it along to signal that the bang was bound for world domination." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Philanthropy and Investment Opportunities** : The lump sum payment from a catalog sale allows artists to engage in activism, philanthropy, investments in non-music ventures, or experiments with other art forms. It also protects them from future economic downturns, such as pandemics, and intelligent investments can yield far more money than they would have earned from music alone. "Now think about what else a band could do with all their future earnings right now. Well, activism, philanthropy, investments in non-music ventures, experiments with other forms of art. And of course, you're protected from any future economic downturn that may result from, say, I don't know, a worldwide pandemic." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Philco's First Battery-Powered Turntable (1955)** : In 1955, Philco, a Philadelphia-based battery company, introduced the first transistor-based, battery-powered turntable. This innovation allowed users to play their 33-30 LPs, 45 RPM singles, and 78s anywhere, as it did not require a power outlet. It featured a built-in, powerful amplifier, a "modern-ish" speaker, and a volume control, retailing for $59.95 US (equivalent to nearly $750 today), offering unprecedented convenience for its time. `"It wasn't until 1955, when Philco, the Philadelphia-based battery company, put out a transistor-based battery-powered turn table on sale."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Philips (Company)** : Philips invented the cassette and later co-developed the compact disc. "Phillips, the company that later co-developed the compact disc." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Philips CD100 Introduction (November 1982)** : Philips, having collaborated with Sony on Compact Disk Technology, released their own CD player, the CD100, approximately one month after Sony introduced its CDP 101. "Sony had been working on Compact Disk Technology with a Dutch company called Phillips for a number of years, which released their own machine, the CD100, a bit of a month later." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Philips' Early CD Diameter Experiments (1974)** : In 1974, Phillips engineers began seeking ways to improve upon the vinyl record. They initially experimented with a disk diameter of about 20 centimeters before settling on one that was 11.5 centimeters, a size influenced by the diagonal width of the compact cassette they had developed earlier. "They first experimented with a disk that was about 20 centimeters in diameter, but eventually settled on one that was 11.5 centimeters." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Philosophical Condemnations of Youth Music (Ancient Greece)** : Beyond Plato, prominent figures such as Socrates and Pythagoras also expressed strong disapproval of the music favored by the youth of their time. This historical pattern illustrates a recurring tension between older generations and new musical styles embraced by younger audiences. "Socrates and Pythagoras also had their beasts with the damn kids in their music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=41eKVp8JIhU
**Philosophy of Found Sound** : The philosophy of "found sound" involves incorporating the sounds of everyday life into music. This approach expands the palette of musical composition beyond traditional instruments, using environmental or pre-existing audio as source material. "Some musicians employed the philosophy of found sound, meaning that they incorporated the sounds of everyday life into their music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OiqdZfxdBP0
**Phoenix – Either Named After a Daft Punk Song or After a Band Member's Cleft Palate Scar** : Two theories: the name comes from a song on Daft Punk's Homework album; or one of the band members was born with a cleft palate that required surgery, leaving a scar similar to actor Joaquin Phoenix's scar. "That's a bit of a stretch, but all right, fine." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Phonograph Birth to Vinyl Records (Pre-1940s)** : The history of recorded music began with the birth of the phonograph, leading to the development of actual vinyl records by the 1940s. "We went all the way back to the birth of the phonograph and traced everything that had to happen before we got to actual vinyl records in the 1940s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Photography Analogy (Film vs. Digital)** : A comparison is drawn to photography, where digital cameras offer excellent resolution, but some photographers still appreciate the quality of old-fashioned fine-grain film. "Some of my photographer friends feel the same way about film." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Physical Bootleg Market Collapsed Around 2008 – Internet and Legal Crackdowns** : The physical market for bootleg recordings effectively collapsed around 2008 as a result of the crackdown on intellectual property exploitation, the closure of specialist shops, and above all the rise of online file sharing. "The physical bootleg market collapsed around 2008 due to crackdowns, shop closures, and the rise of online file sharing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Uly2cT2vc28
**Physical Media Sound Quality Comparison** : Alex Kapranos offers a vivid analogy to describe the difference in sound quality across various music formats. He likens listening to vinyl to watching a film in a cinema, a CD to watching a film on an HDTV, and an MP3 to watching a film on YouTube, emphasizing the "huge difference" in quality that makes vinyl a distinct and devoted listening experience. "The way I think about it is listening to vinyl is like going to see a film in the cinema. Listening to a CD is like watching a film on an HDTV. And listening to an Mb3 is like watching a film on YouTube. That's the difference in the quality." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RWbKqqW78yg
**Physical Pain as an Outlet for Emotional Distress** : Richie Edwards articulated that he used physical pain from cutting himself as a deliberate and logical outlet for his emotional suffering. He found that concentrating on the physical pain made his other anxieties and annoyances seem trivial, providing a form of distraction and release. "When I cut myself, he said, I feel so much better. All the little things that might have been annoying me certainly seemed so trivial because I'm concentrating on the pain. I'm not a person who can scream and shout, so this is my only outlet. It's all done very logically." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sCwgvHg86Oc
**Physical and Electronic Ticket Varieties** : Concert tickets exist in various forms, including pre-printed card stock, machine-printed tickets, home-printable barcodes or QR codes, and digital e-tickets stored on phones. They may incorporate security features like holograms and can be linked to the credit card used for purchase. "A concert ticket can be pre-printed on card stock. It can be printed by a machine the moment you buy it. It can be a barcode or QR code on a piece of paper that you print out at home." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OCkov6FTliE
**Pierre Laporte - Quebec Deputy Premier** : Pierre Laporte was the Quebec deputy premier who was kidnapped and murdered during the FLQ crisis in 1970. His tragic story was the basis for The Tragically Hip's song "Locked in the Trunk of a Car." "the kidnapping and murder of Quebec deputy premier Pierre La Port during the FLQ crisis." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oMBmdfrA47Q
**Pierre Trudeau's Premiership (1970s Canada)** : Prime Minister Pierre Trudeau was increasingly perceived as arrogant and ineffective in addressing Canada's economic problems during the mid-to-late 1970s. "The Prime Minister Pierre Trudeau was seen as increasingly arrogant and unable to fix the economy." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Pink Duck Studios - Josh Homme's Recording Space** : Pink Duck Studios in Burbank, owned by Josh Homme, is described as having an unassuming exterior, resembling "an old corner store with the door blocked up" from the front. The back of the studio, accessed via an alley, features a small parking area with a deck and picnic table, creating a unique and private creative environment. "Josh's Pink Duck Studios is within stupidly easy walking distance from the Burbank airport. From the front, it looks like an old corner store with the door blocked up." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hjProJd_JrM
**Pink Flamingo – Photography Prop** : A pink flamingo prop, which belonged to Paul "Bonehead" Arthurs, was deliberately included in the foreground of Oasis's "Definitely Maybe" album cover. Its presence was part of a larger directive by the photographer to have band members bring personal objects to the shoot. "The pink flamingo belongs to Bohnhead." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Pink Floyd Engineer for Blink 182** : For their self-titled 2003 album, Blink 182 hired an engineer who had previously worked on Pink Floyd's "The Wall" to assist with the recording. "To engineer part of the record, they hired one of the guys who helped sort up the wall for Pink Floyd." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tkLRJiOBu0
**Pioneering Record Cover Artwork (1938)** : Prior to 1938, record artwork was generally dull, featuring only text or generic designs. Alex Steinweiss, a 23-year-old designer, convinced Columbia Records to create original graphic designs for record sleeves, starting with Rodgers and Hart's "Smash Song Hits." This innovation significantly boosted sales and led other labels to adopt original artwork as a standard. "It wasn't until 1938 that original graphics were designed for the sleeve of a record." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gP3oQeYEvso
**Pitfalls of the Artistic Brain** : Individuals wired as pure artists, with a supernatural ability to create beauty, often face pitfalls such as trouble with daily tasks (money, groceries, schedules, social situations), and susceptibility to depression, anxiety, bipolar disorder, autism spectrum, and addictions (alcohol, drugs, sex). "They might have trouble with day-to-day tasks like handling money, shopping for groceries, keeping a schedule, or being able to deal with everyday social situations." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CPxy_KOv87o
**Plagiarism Definition** : Plagiarism is defined as "the act of appropriating someone else's work and passing it off as your own." "That's plagiarism. The act of appropriating someone else's work and passing it off as your own." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rfObVld9rIE
**Plato's Philosophy on Music and Societal Change** : Plato held that alterations in "news and rhythms in music" invariably lead to changes in the "entire fabric of society." He cautioned that new musical forms, though seemingly harmless diversions, creep in imperceptibly, gradually spreading into manners and pursuits, eventually attacking laws and the Constitution with impudence, ultimately overthrowing public and private life. Plato personally disliked slow music, believing it fostered "laziness and sloth," and fast music, as he thought it hindered "sober contemplation." "News and rhythms in music are never altered without producing changes in the entire fabric of society." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m7MbV-eCLyE
**Plato's Views on Music's Power and Danger (Ancient Greece)** : In "The Republic," Plato acknowledged music's profound capacity to "penetrate to the very core of the self," stating it "gives soul to the universe, wings to the mind, flight to the imagination, and charm and gady to life." While recognizing rhythm and harmony's strong hold on the soul and their educational value for youth, he also cautioned against "the introduction of a new kind of music," viewing it as a threat to the entire state, specifically referring to the music embraced by ancient Greek youth. "Music is a moral law. It gives soul to the universe, wings to the mind, flight to the imagination, and charm and gady to life and everything." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=41eKVp8JIhU
**Podcast Production Team – Blanchard House** : For "Stop Rewind, the Lost Boy," the creative director of Blanchard House is Rosie Pye, and the head of content is Lawrence Grisel. The executive producers for Blanchard House are Amika Shultino Nolan and Lawrence Grisel. "The creative director of blanchard house is Rosie Pye, the head of content at Blanchard House, is Lawrence Grisel." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vFVnhksrFao
**Podcast Production Team – Core** : The podcast "Stop Rewind, the Lost Boy" is hosted, written, and produced by Emma Jane Kirby. Additional production for the series was handled by Himmert Schalagram. "It's hosted, written and produced by me, Emma Jane Kirby, with additional production by Himmert Schalagram." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vFVnhksrFao
**Podcast Production Team – Curious Cast** : The executive producers for Curious Cast, involved in the production of "Stop Rewind, the Lost Boy," are Deed of Alaska's and Chris Duncan. "For Curious Cast, the executive producers are Deed of Alaska's and Chris Duncan." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vFVnhksrFao
**Podcast Production Team – Research and Interpreting** : Smitter TK provided on-the-ground interpreting for the "Stop Rewind, the Lost Boy" series, and Catherine Gielon conducted additional research for the podcast. "On the ground interpreting by Smitter TK, additional research by Catherine Gielon." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vFVnhksrFao
**Podcasts Released on Vinyl via Kickstarter (2021)** : In 2021, a Kickstarter campaign emerged offering Chicago-based podcasts pressed onto vinyl records. This initiative presented a physical, collectible format for content typically consumed digitally, blending old and new media. "a Kickstarter campaign appeared this year that offered podcasts all based out of Chicago on vinyl." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-N6IM5Nm5iw
**Poet Laureate of Ontario Act (December 17, 2017 / 2019)** : Weeks after Gord Downie's death, a bill was introduced in the Ontario legislature on December 17, 2017, titled the Poet Laureate of Ontario Act, specifically in his memory. Two years later, it became law, establishing an official provincial poet laureate to continue Downie's legacy and love for poetry. "On December 17, a few weeks after Gord died, a bill was introduced in the Ontario legislature. It was the Poet Laureate of Ontario Act in memory of Gord Dowdy." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dnz_Ycd_q7s
**Poetry: Economical Use of Words** : Poetry can be defined as the economical use of words, aiming for maximum information, emotion, entertainment, and enlightenment in the fewest possible words, regardless of the subject's complexity. This concept of concise yet impactful language is reflected in Alex Turner's lyrical style. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YvcnHKI4oIw
**Pogues – Short for the Irish Gaelic Phrase "Pog Mahon," Meaning Kiss My Ass** : The name is an abbreviation of the Gaelic phrase "póg mo thóin," which translates as "kiss my ass." "Which translates as Kiss My Ass." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Political Influence on Music (Republican Presidents)** : A historical observation suggests that during periods when a Republican president is in the White House, music tends to become heavier, angrier, and more "rocky." This trend correlated with Generation X's preference for making and listening to such music, aligning their disillusionment with a perceived political climate. "If you match up history with politics, you'll see that whenever there's a Republican in the White House, music tends to turn heavier, angrier and more rocky." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r8AazBVoS7g
**Political and Economic Influence on Music** : Music is consistently influenced by societal events, functioning "downstream" from political and economic conditions, often reflecting prevailing moods. "music, which you remember, is always downstream from what's happening in society, kept humming a happy tune." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC) Properties and Environmental Impact** : Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) is the third most popular plastic globally, with applications ranging from bottles and plastic cards to plumbing products, having been used in sewer pipes since the 1920s. Most PVC is produced in China and Southeast Asia. PVC production is a byproduct of fossil fuels. In its raw, brittle state, it requires stabilizing chemicals known as plasticizers, which historically included toxic substances like lead and cadmium. Carbon is added to make the plastic black. As plastic, vinyl is non-biodegradable and records in landfills can leach hazardous chemicals for centuries. Melting old records is costly and releases highly toxic hydrogen chloride gas, a significant environmental problem given the billions of records produced since 1948 (e.g., 160 million kilos of PVC for records in a typical 1970s year). "Polyvinyl chloride is plastic. It's the third most popular type of plastic ever." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2g4QSREzpw
**Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC) Shortage (2020 onwards)** : A global shortage of polyvinyl chloride (PVC) began in 2020, primarily due to supply chain disruptions caused by the pandemic. While increased online record sales and backed-up pressing plants contributed, a major factor in the shortage was the surging demand for plastic containers used by take-out restaurants. "Starting in 2020, the world suffered a shortage of polyvinyl chloride because of supply chain issues during the pandemic." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2g4QSREzpw
**Polyvinyl Chloride (Vinyl) Saves the Industry** : The second major factor that saved the recorded music industry was polyvinyl chloride, a century-old chemical compound commonly known as plastic. Chemists and engineers had discovered its durability and resistance to corrosion, making it ideal for applications like sewer pipes, and eventually, for manufacturing superior records. "The other thing that saved the industry was a century-old chemical compound called polyvinyl chloride, which, for lack of a better term, is plastic." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VlIErdSSVOM
**Pono Music Player (Neil Young)** : Neil Young launched his Pono music player in 2012, an endeavor aimed at popularizing high-resolution audio. The device was distinctively shaped like a Toblerone bar, which made it awkward to fit into a pocket. Despite its reported excellent sound quality and functionality, it failed to capture widespread consumer interest and was ultimately withdrawn from the market in early 2017. "Just ask Neil Young, who tried really hard to get his Pono music player off the ground. He started with his playback device in 2012. It was shaped like a Tobler-owned bar, which means it didn't really fit that well in your pocket. And although it worked and sounded great, nobody cared. It disappeared from the market in early 2017." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**Pop Culture Inspiration for Goth** : Goth drew inspiration from various popular culture sources, including monsters, The Addams Family, B-horror movies from the 1950s and 1960s, and The Rocky Horror Picture Show. "But then you have to add in the inspiration provided by monsters and the Adam's family and some of those great, great B-horror movies from the 50s and 60s, not to mention the rocky horror picture show." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**Pop Music Disruptions History** : Every ten years or so, significant events disrupt the pop music landscape. This pattern traces back to the 1950s with the birth of rock (initially dismissed as a fad), continued with The Beatles and the rise of the album in the 1960s, the emergence of Punk in the 1970s, the ascent of Hip Hop and Rap alongside music video channels in the 1980s, and the Alt Rock explosion of the 1990s. "every ten years or so, something happens to disrupt everything." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=raRCZ0Y5up8
**Pop Music Dominance in 2000** : By the end of 2000, "vacuous pop music had taken over the universe," selling hundreds of millions of CDs, with rap and hip hop in second place, and rock struggling, leading to a grim prognosis for the genre. "When the dust cleared at the end of 2000, it was very clear that vacuous pop music had taken over the universe." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MK7L0eCpDHc
**Pop Music's Ascendancy in 1988** : The year 1988 saw pop artists like New Kids on the Block, Antiphany, and Debbie Gibson rising, prompting renewed "rock as dead" cries. "It looked especially bad through 1988 when it looked as if the future belonged to new kids on the block, Antiphany and Debbie Gibson, and the same rock as dead cries that we heard in 1960 and 1972 were trotted out once again." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MK7L0eCpDHc
**Pop Punk Turning Point (1992)** : The year 1992 marked a crucial turning point for pop punk, as the emerging genre began to gain broader attention and wider retention. This was notably spurred by articles in national publications, such as *Spin Magazine*'s "California Dreaming." "In 1992, some kind of a turning point, a year when this emerging pop punk thing started to gain some wider retention." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tZV1lz-obQE
**Pop Punk as a Juxtaposition** : The term "Pop Punk" itself is highlighted as a contradictory combination of words. This is because punk music was originally conceived as an "attack on pop," making their eventual fusion somewhat incongruous. "Pop, punk. But those words should not go together. Punk was originally created as an attack on pop." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pE7TaWSUa-o
**Pope Gregory's Musical Decree (Late 6th Century)** : In the late sixth century, Pope Gregory mandated that music be structured into a system of eight modes, asserting that all these modes had "God's approval." This decision aimed to standardize acceptable church music. "By the late sixth century, Pope Gregory had decreed that music be organized into series of eight modes, all of which he said, met with God's approval." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g-tBn8dsSqw
**Porta-Potty Shortage at UK Festivals (2021)** : A significant issue encountered when restarting festivals in the UK during the summer of 2021 was an severe shortage of porta-potties. There were insufficient units available to accommodate the biological needs of all festival attendees. "one of the problems with restarting festivals in the UK this past summer was an acute shortage of porta-potties." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-N6IM5Nm5iw
**Portable CD Players in the 1980s** : In the 1980s, portable CD players were available in two primary forms: units designed for installation in cars and more fragile versions intended for personal, mobile use. While both delivered high-quality sound, they were limited to playing pre-recorded discs, unlike cassettes which offered the flexibility of creating custom mix tapes. "In the 80s, portable CD players came in two flavors, the kind you installed in your car after you bought it and the fragile sort that you could walk around with. Both delivered great sound, but you were still stuck with pre-recorded disks." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**Portable Music Evolution since 1954** : Since the introduction of the transistor radio in 1954, portable music has undergone a profound transformation. The concept of not being able to take one's music anywhere is now considered "alien," contrasting sharply with a past where the ability to listen to music while jogging was a significant technological milestone. "We have come such a long way with portable music since that first transistor radio appeared on the market in 1954. The idea of not being able to take your music wherever you go is so alien. It used to be such a big deal, such a technological advance to be able to go for a jog while listening to music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**Portable Personal Stereo Revolution** : The introduction of the Sony Soundabout in 1979 is widely recognized as "the big bang of the portable personal stereo revolution," a defining moment in the history of music technology. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z9T9wi0vEvc
**Portugal. The Man – A Period Separating the Collective (Portugal) from the Individual (The Man)** : The period separating "Portugal" and "The Man" symbolically separates the many (a country full of people, like a band of multiple members) from the individual (a specific person, like each band member). The name was intended to feel larger than life. "The period that we see between Portugal and the man is to separate the many from the one." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Possible Combinations of 12 Notes (Once Each)** : According to Frank Barons, who wrote in the Arts Times in 2004, there are 479,160,000 possible combinations if you play the 12 notes of the chromatic scale just once each. This figure highlights the vast but finite number of basic musical sequences available. "A quick bit of factoring... reveals that there are 479 million 1,600 possible combinations of those 12 notes. If you just play them once each, 479 million combinations." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L09R2evjyMc
**Possible Combinations of Notes (with Durations)** : When accounting for the various ways a single note can be played (e.g., whole, half, quarter, eighth, sixteenth, thirty-second notes), the number of possible note combinations expands exponentially to just over one quintillion (a one followed by 18 zeros). This illustrates the immense complexity and theoretical diversity in musical composition. "Something just north of one quintillion possible combinations. That's a one followed by 18 zeros, one quintillion." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L09R2evjyMc
**Post-*Paul's Boutique* Sampling Costs Escalation** : The era in which *Paul's Boutique* was made, with its 105 meticulously cleared samples costing around $250,000, predated a wave of major lawsuits involving sampling that dramatically "pushed the cost of clearing samples way, way up." This subsequent increase in costs means that an album of similar complexity would today cost "in the millions of dollars," making it almost impossible to stitch together such a record in the current environment. "Paul Sputig came out in the time before a bunch of big lawsuits involving sampling pushed the cost of clearing samples way, way up." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=l1jPP1FhGo8
**Post-2010 Influences on Pop Punk Tone** : The "darker" tone observed in the "new generation of pop punk bands" emerging after 2010 is speculated to be a possible consequence of significant societal events such as the "2008 financial crisis or the election of Donald Trump." "Maybe that's the result of the 2008 financial crisis or the election of Donald Trump." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pE7TaWSUa-o
**Post-9/11 Emo Explosion** : The traumatic events of September 11th marked a turning point for emo music, which had previously remained an underground phenomenon. In the years immediately following 9/11, emo bands experienced an explosion in popularity, becoming ubiquitous in the early 2000s. "Emo stayed in its own corner of the alternative universe until after the traumatic events of 9-11. For the following several years, emo bands exploded in popularity." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_yslM5oanRo
**Post-Bowie Bonds Catalog Acquisition Trend** : Modern music business is dominated by well-capitalized companies like Roundhill, Primary Wave, and Concord, which permanently buy up the catalogs and publishing rights of major artists for hundreds of millions or even billions of dollars, a long-play strategy that Bowie foresaw with his Bowie Bonds in 1997. "Today, the biggest deals in the music business involve well capitalized companies who are buying up the catalogs and publishing rights of major artists." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yq2A_o9-ulM
**Post-Breakup Litigation in Music** : The music industry, particularly after band breakups or disputes, can be plagued by lengthy legal battles. The Sex Pistols, for instance, endured years of litigation with Malcolm McLaren's Glitter Best management company following their 1978 implosion, finally regaining control of their music only in 1987. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PzyvXmDKFUc
**Post-Breakup Professional Diversification** : After band breakups, members often pursue varied careers outside of music, some finding success in new fields like television or even professional pumpkin growing, while others continue within the music industry in different capacities like composing or production. "During this entire period, wherever he was doing their own thing, everybody denied that they were even thinking about a reunion." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DJGFUlQTUhQ
**Post-Disco Crash and CD's Economic Impact** : Following the "post-disco crash," the recorded music industry faced a significant downturn, experiencing a drop in profits for the first time since the Great Depression, compounded by a global recession. The introduction of the compact disk (CD) was heralded as a solution, offering superior sound quality that was guaranteed to entice consumers to repurchase their entire music collections at higher prices, successfully revitalizing industry profits. `"The next big change in portable music came just as the recorded music industry was dealing with a big post-disco crash."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Post-Gen Xer Music Consumption** : The emerging generation of "post-Gen Xers" approached music without preconceptions about rock's sound, having grown up with rap as an omnipresent genre. They favored loud, energetic, and angry music that sounded good on car stereos and headphones, often specifically enjoying music that their parents despised. "Fans ate this up, especially the emerging post-gen exers who had zero preconceptions about what rock should sound like." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5bQz8R0gbXc
**Post-Grunge Music Landscape** : In the early 1990s, following the massive success of grunge, the traditional barriers between mainstream rock and alternative music collapsed entirely, creating an environment where previously niche genres like Goth, industrial, various forms of electronica, and especially punk rock, began to emerge and gain wider attention. "After Grunge blew up in the early 1990s, the walls between mainstream rock and the alternative universe crumbled completely." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eljIIqCu5gU
**Post-Hutchence Troubles and Paternity Scandal** : After Michael Hutchence's death, she undertook numerous trips to rehab, attempted suicide at least once, and sold her story to a news organization for nearly a million dollars. A major scandal unfolded when DNA tests revealed her biological father was British TV personality Huey Green, contradicting her mother's claim that it was Jesse Yates. "There were numerous trips into rehab, at least one botched suicide attempt on her part, and the sale of her story to a news organization for close to a million dollars." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Post-Modernism in Architecture** : Frank Geary is mentioned as a post-modernist architect, illustrating how the "post-" prefix applies to disciplines beyond music, including various forms of art and literature. "Frank Geary was a post-modernist architect." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9-v4qnOO8wY
**Post-Napster File Sharing Landscape** : After Napster was sued out of existence, numerous other file-sharing programs like Audio Galaxy, Gnutella, LimeWire, Morpheus, Kazaa, and BearShare emerged, ensuring that music became universally available, never out of print, no longer rare, and free of charge. "And while Napster was soon sued out of existence, other file sharing programs popped up." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Post-Punk Era Musical Diversity** : The post-punk era, immediately following the punk rock explosion of the 1970s, witnessed a significant "blossoming of all kinds of new and interesting music." This period was characterized by a wide range of genres, including new wave and its variant Technopop, all enabled by punk's foundational influence. "This is part two of our exploration of the post-punk era, those years immediately following the punk rock explosion of the 1970s, which saw a blossoming of all kinds of new and interesting music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5PJRCC6EwUY
**Post-Punk Explosion of the Late 70s and Early 80s** : This era followed the original punk rock explosion, serving as a "great musical decluttering" and attitude shift towards DIY. It resulted in a "big bang" for alternative music, unleashing chain reactions of new ideas, sounds, attitudes, fashion, and belief systems. This period laid "so many of the basic foundations of the music that we hear today," giving rise to genres like New Wave, Techno Pop, Industrial, Goth, and Ska. "We now look back on this as the great post punk explosion of the late 70s and early 80s, an era that created so many of the basic foundations of the music that we hear today." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LgJ7ww2AgF4
**Post-Punk Segmentation** : In the early and mid-1980s, while major labels still controlled most of the music market, the market share for independent music steadily increased. As post-punk music continued to fragment and segment into an expanding array of styles, the independent music scene became increasingly interesting and diverse. "But started in the punk years just kept gaining in momentum. Let's stay in the UK for a bit. The left of center slash underground stuff that was being released by the indie labels kept on coming. And as post punk music continued to separate and segment into an ever growing number of streams, things kept getting more and more interesting." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VOv5mT1dJd8
**Post-Sex Pistols Influx to UK Punk** : The day after the Sex Pistols' infamous Bill Grundy appearance (December 2, 1976), the entire UK became aware of punk rock, leading to a "massive influx of adherence" into what had previously been a "tiny, tiny, tiny scene" across all parts of the country. "But a week later, December 2, 1976, the day after the Bill Grundy appearance, the entire kingdom knew about punk rock. And what had been a tiny, tiny, tiny scene, all of a sudden was seeing a massive influx of adherence in all parts of the country." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Post-WWII Shift to Smaller Combos** : After World War II, the high cost of maintaining large big bands led to a trend towards "smaller combos of four and five people." This shift forced musicians to "play harder in order to be heard," consequently affecting the music's composition and performance style, making the beat "bigger and more important." "But after World War II, it became awfully expensive to maintain a big, big band, so the trend was towards smaller combos of four and five people, and everybody was back to playing harder in order to be heard." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qrrwqdpCjpQ
**Power Soaks** : Power soaks were devices developed to allow guitarists to emulate the sound of big, loud amplifiers while still using a small amplifier at a low volume. This addressed the issue of achieving desirable distortion from overdriven vacuum tubes without ear-splitting volume, making practice and performance in small spaces more feasible. "These became known as power soaks." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iGav9uJwA_w
**Pre-Artwork Record Packaging (1909-1938)** : For three decades following Odeon's 1909 innovation of bundling records, all records were sold in plain paper sleeves, typically with minimal text. These early "phonograph albums" were largely anonymous, consisting of simple paperboard bindings without any decorative or photographic artwork. "For the next 30 years, all records were sold in plain paper sleeves." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Pre-CanCon Canadian Radio Bias – The Guess Who Had to Pretend to Be British (1965)** : In January 1965, Canadian band Chad Allan and the Expressions recorded "Shakin' All Over" on Quality Records. Quality Records knew Canadian radio would not add it because stations only played Canadian songs after they had become US hits. The label shipped 17 singles to stations across Canada with no band name listed — just a question mark reading "Guess Who?" — implying it was a mystery British band. The ruse worked. When stations realized in mid-March 1965 they had been tricked into playing a Canadian group, the name had already stuck. The band later officially became The Guess Who. "Quality purposely left the name of the group off the record." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-JWO_jYXwEM
**Pre-Internet Music Era Characteristics (Pre-2000)** : The pre-internet music era (before 2000) was characterized by peak industry revenues, bustling record stores, the crucial role of MTV and MuchMusic in artist development, the possibility of acts filling stadiums, and record labels' long-term investment in artists, often over several albums, with radio hits guaranteeing success. "The industry revenues were peaking at all time highs. Record stores were busy places, MTV and much music were very important to breaking an act." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Pre-Internet Music Leaks** : In earlier times, before widespread internet access, the creation, manufacturing, and distribution of records were tightly controlled, making it relatively easy to prevent leaks. Marketing, press, and tour schedules could be carefully synchronized for maximum impact, and any rare leaks, usually from inside jobs at studios or pressing plants, were manageable. "Now, in olden times, when the creation, manufacturing, and distribution processes were so tightly controlled, it was easy to keep a record under wraps until the label let it out." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vt6DVj7S6nk
**Pre-Internet Music News** : Before the internet, news traveled slowly and was heavily filtered, often stripping out or drastically altering information. This was particularly evident around sensitive events like the deaths of figures such as Kurt Cobain and Michael Hutchence, leading to much confusion, speculation, and conspiracy theories. "News traveled slowly and often passed through many filters." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Pre-Internet Music Research** : Before the internet became widespread in 1996, researching music was a manual and costly endeavor. The speaker personally spent around $3,000 annually on magazines, as an independent contractor unable to expense them. Additionally, radio stations maintained libraries of press clippings and press releases from record labels, alphabetized by artist, serving as the primary source of information. "Well, what we would do is, you know, I would go and at one point I was buying, and because I was an independent contractor, as you'll recall, I didn't get to expense anything. So I was buying $3,000 worth of magazines every year." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4xxgPX-aR90
**Pre-Show Music Practices** : The music played before and between acts, often called PA music or pre-show music, is chosen by various parties: the venue, the PA operator (to tweak sound), the gig sound designer (to create a vibe), or the artist (to expose the audience to music they enjoy).
