A common technique is to slowly increase the volume as showtime approaches to build energy. "And there's no hard and fast rule about what this music should consist of. Sometimes it's the choice of the venue. Sometimes it's the choice of whoever's running the PA because maybe they need a way to tweak the sound before the band comes out." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bferkEWvJg0
**Pre-SoundScan Chart Compilation (Pre-March 1, 1991)** : Before March 1, 1991, weekly album charts were compiled based on "merely estimates." Billboard magazine would gather verbal sales reports from "a select group of record stores," then extrapolate this data across the country for its top 200 chart, a method acknowledged as highly susceptible to "mistakes, lies, bribery, in all manner of manipulation." "Up until February 28, 1991, album sales were merely estimates. Billboard magazine, the keeper of the charts, would phone around to a select group of record stores and get verbal reports of what albums were selling." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pE7TaWSUa-o
**Pre-SoundScan Chart Manipulation (Before March 1991)** : Before March 1, 1991, the music industry lacked an accurate method for tracking record sales, relying on Billboard magazine's calls to a sample of stores since 1945. This "honor system" was prone to errors, manipulation, bad memory, bias, bribery, lies, and fraud, leading to "chart fixing" dating back to the 1930s. Consequently, chart positions often did not reflect actual sales or popularity, misleading radio stations, video channels, and fans. "With zero accountability, the system was wide open to errors, manipulation, bad memory, bias, bribery, lies, and fraud." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r8AazBVoS7g
**Pre-iPod Portable Music Landscape (2000)** : By the year 2000, the landscape of portable music was rapidly evolving with the increased prevalence of custom-burned CDs, MP3s, and file-sharing platforms like Napster, collectively setting the stage for subsequent innovations in portable audio. "By 2000, portable music was reaching new levels thanks to custom-burned CDs, MP3s, and file sharing, especially this new thing called Napster." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**Pre-recorded Digital Audio Tapes (DAT)** : For a period, pre-recorded Digital Audio Tapes (DAT) were available for purchase. However, due to the high cost of DAT machines, which prevented them from becoming a general consumer product, supplies of these pre-recorded tapes were scarce. Today, DAT tapes are highly sought after by collectors, commanding prices of hundreds of dollars each. "As a sidebar, it's probably worth mentioning that in addition to pre-recorded mini disks, you could, for a while, buy pre-recorded date tapes. But like I said before, date machines were too expensive for the general public. So supplies of pre-recorded tapes were tight. And today, collectors pay way too much for these things." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**Precursors to "Jackass" Stunts** : Hugh Dillon recalled engaging in dangerous and reckless activities like "bumper hitching" with Frankie Venom during their youth, stunts that predated and resembled the actions later popularized by the television show "Jackass." "before Jackass was invented. We were playing Jackass." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zBd13KtYlAA
**Predominance of Older Guitar Buyers** : Currently, the majority of guitars being bought and sold are acquired by older players, with their numbers decreasing annually. "Most guitars today are being bought and sold by older players, and there are fewer and fewer of them each year." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oLC49nCf0ZM
**Prejudices Against Women in Rock** : Historically, it was a "common knowledge" prejudice that girls "just couldn't rock," lacked the "feel," were "built wrong," and it wasn't "in their DNA" like it was for guys. This prejudice took a long time to be defeated, highlighting the societal barriers women faced in music. "There was a time when it was common knowledge that girls just couldn't rock." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qss5kC2FUx0
**Prescription Painkiller Addiction** : Addiction to prescription painkillers was a recurring issue for several musicians, including Tom DeLong (Blink 182) and Scott Stapp (Creed), highlighting its impact on personal health and band dynamics. "Tom broke his addiction to prescription pain killers by going cold turkey." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yR3xc3ra5tE
**Pressure for Vinyl Production** : It requires between 100 and 150 tons of pressure to transform a lump of raw vinyl into a playable record. "It takes between 100 and 150 tons of pressure to squeeze a lump of raw vinyl into a playable record." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=72Lei7uug8c
**Presumed Dirty Lyrics** : During eras of lyric controversy, "conservative people didn't even have to understand the words to get all worked up." If they couldn't clearly make out the lyrics, the default assumption was that they "had to be dirty," reflecting a tendency to project fears and moral anxieties onto unintelligible content. "If they couldn't make up the lyrics, well, then they had to be dirty, right? What other explanation could there be?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Prevalence of Stand-up Sitcoms in 1995** : In 1995, all six broadcast networks, including the newer WB and UPN, had at least one show featuring a comic in a central role. ABC notably had seven of its 15 sitcom slots filled by stand-up shows, including *Roseanne*, *Home Improvement*, *Grace Under Fire*, *All-American Girl*, *Allen*, *Hanging with Mr. Cooper*, and *On Our Own*. Meanwhile, Fox aired a George Carlin sitcom and *Martin*, while NBC had *Mad About You*, *The Nannies* (mummies), and *Seinfeld*. "In 1995, all six broadcast networks, including the WB and UPN, had at least one show with a comic front and center." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6O5TCTO-go4
**Private Jet Crash (September 21, 2008)** : Travis Barker (Blink 182) and DJ AM were the sole survivors of a private Learjet crash on this date, which occurred during takeoff, slamming into a fence and exploding, resulting in severe burns for Travis. "Travis and his buddy DJ Am were the only two survivors of a jet plane crash." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yR3xc3ra5tE
**Pro Rata Payment Model – Drake Gets Paid from Jazz Fans' Subscriptions** : Spotify does not pay artists based on who specifically listened to them. Instead, artists are paid based on their percentage of overall streams in a given month. If Drake accounts for X percent of all streams, he receives X percent of all subscription revenue — regardless of who subscribed or why. This means a jazz fan's subscription money does not go to Miles Davis; it goes into a collective pool that flows primarily to whoever dominates the stream count. Small-audience artists with loyal but numerically limited fan bases are effectively squeezed out. "Musicians that used to have a small but loyal fan base are now making next to nothing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MQ7PDi-D9Ig
**Pro Tools Software Overview** : Pro Tools, a digital audio workstation, emerged as the standard recording software in most studios, originally conceived by students at the University of California at Berkeley in 1989 and launched in 1991. Initially costing $6,000 for a four-track system, it now offers extensive capabilities, including waveform visualization, cutting, pasting, and infinite sound tweaking, enabling precise correction of vocal errors, rhythmic lags, or incorrect notes. It has made recording significantly easier, though it can also serve as "compensation for a lack of talent," allowing for less precise initial performances. GarageBand, Audacity, and Adobe Edition are its technological descendants. "Most studios use pro tools, which is pretty much the standard for recording software." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7Zniri9uSlc
**Processing Death of Famous Musicians** : The death of a famous musician affects fans profoundly, despite not knowing them personally, because their music often serves as a means for fans to learn about themselves. This deep connection leads to a natural desire for information to process the news and the accompanying emotions. "Although we never knew them, it was through their music that we learned more about ourselves." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hhii2_BVE7A
**Producer Payment Models** : Producers are compensated in one of two ways: either through a flat fee for their time, or by taking a percentage of the sales and other revenue generated by the music they produce. "Some take a flat fee. Just pay me for my time and we're done. Others take a piece of the action when it comes to the sales and other revenue generated by that music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Producer Royalty Sales** : Record producers, who typically receive a share of royalties for their involvement in records, can also sell these rights. Examples include Bob Rock (Metallica's Black Album), Steve Lillywhite (for artists like Siouxsie and the Banshees, The Psychedelic Furs, The Killers, Peter Gabriel, U2), Bob Ezrin, Jimmy Iovine, and Jack Antonoff. "Record producers usually have a piece of the action for any record in which they're involved. They can sell those rights too. Bob Rock, for example, sold his stake in Metallica's black album." "Steve Lillewight, who produced records for everyone from Susie in the Banshees and the psychedelic furs to the killers in Peter Gabriel and you too, sold his royalties. Bob Ezorin, Jimmy Iving and Jack Antennaugh have also sold their producers portions." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Producer's Role in Songwriting** : According to John Frusciante, a producer like Rick Rubin does not write or play music but rather "organizes things" for the band. "Well, how about you and Rick Rubin? I mean, well, he doesn't write the music, he doesn't play anything. He more organizes things." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CPxy_KOv87o
**Producers as Coaches and Mediators** : Beyond technical expertise, the most effective producers also act as coaches, psychologists, "lion tamers," and peacemakers. These interpersonal skills are essential for managing the often-tricky dynamics and personalities of musicians during the creative and recording process. "The best producers are also coaches and psychologists and lion tamers and peacemakers." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Professor Dieter Selzer's Patent Rejection** : Professor Dieter Selzer's 1980s patent application for his "digital jukebox" concept, which involved storing and transmitting all music via telephone lines, was rejected. The examiner deemed the proposal "impossible," stating, "It'll never work." "Selzer applied for a patent, but it was rejected on the grounds that the examiner decided that what he proposed was impossible." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Profound Impact on Music Consumption** : YouTube has profoundly transformed how individuals share, access, and consume music, acting as a global, user-driven platform for diverse musical content. "YouTube has changed so much about what we share, how we access and how we consume music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Progressive Networks** : Founded by ex-Microsoft employee Rob Glazer in 1994, Progressive Networks developed the Real Audio system, a proprietary technology for streaming audio in real-time over the internet. This company was a pioneer in early internet broadcasting. "Rob Glazer, an ex-Microsoft employee who created a company called Progressive Networks in 1994." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Prohibition's Influence on Music Criticism** : The Prohibition era (1920s) contributed to hot jazz being blamed for encouraging illegal alcohol consumption and illicit sex. This demonstrates how contemporary social and legal contexts often shaped moral criticisms against new music forms. "This was prohibition, remember, as well as illicit sex." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Proliferation of Peer-to-Peer File Sharing Sites** : Following Napster's success, numerous other peer-to-peer file-sharing sites emerged, including Scour, Qtmx, Freenet, Imesh, Bear Share, Edonkey, Audio Galaxy, Rapstar, Rockstar, Limewire, and Kazaa. This proliferation significantly amplified the scale of unauthorized music distribution. "There were dozens of other peer-to-peer file sharing sites. It was Scour and Qtmx and Freenet and Imesh, Bear Share, Edonkey, Audio Galaxy, Rapstar, Rockstar, Limewara, Kazah, endless list of these things." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Promise of Computerized Ticketing (1968)** : A newspaper article from 1968, referencing Ticket Reservation Systems Inc. (TRS), proclaimed the radical potential of computerized ticketing, stating that "pre-printed tickets are a thing of the past" and that "seat inventories exist only in the memory banks of our computers." It envisioned a future where anyone could easily purchase tickets for events anywhere. "pre-printed tickets are a thing of the past. Seat inventories exist only in the memory banks of our computers. A West Coast musical enthusiast can buy tickets for the Broadway show he planned to see on his next trip with as much ease as a native New Yorker." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OCkov6FTliE
**Promoter Needs and Nostalgia Driven Tours** : Large promoters like Live Nation and AEG faced a challenge in filling venues as fewer new acts reached superstar status. The solution was to tap into the nostalgia of boomers, Gen Xers, and millennials, who possessed disposable income and a desire to relive the music of their youth, making reunion tours a highly reliable and profitable strategy. "Meanwhile, large promoters like Live Nation and AEG needed to sell tickets for festivals, stadiums, amphitheaters and arenas." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GYUuTbOvyak
**Promoter Test Riders** : Many bands incorporate unusual or seemingly trivial requests into their riders, such as Van Halen's brown M&M's clause or Queens of the Stone Age's map of Ireland, as a test. These clauses help determine whether a promoter has thoroughly read the entire document, indicating their attention to detail and likelihood of fulfilling more critical requirements. "This was a test to see if the promoter actually read through the entire document, which is something that had happened far too few times in the past, resulting in, well, let's just call them fractious relations." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=z2hvUDtBxho
**Propaganda Magazine** : This magazine was considered a "Goth Bible for a while" and played a role in formalizing aspects of Goth culture. "the biggest of which was probably propaganda." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**Proposed Shocking Mobile Phones (London)** : A design firm in London once proposed the concept of mobile phones that would deliver a shock to their owners. This mechanism would be activated if the phone detected that the user was speaking too loudly during a call. "A design firm in London once proposed phones, mobile phones, that would shock their owners if the phone perceived the owner was talking too loud." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**Protest Themes in the 1980s** : The 1980s saw a rich landscape of alt-rock protest songs addressing various critical issues. Themes included war (e.g., U2's "Sunday Bloody Sunday"), apartheid (e.g., Peter Gabriel's "Biko" and Artists United Against Apartheid's anthem about Sun City), nuclear war (e.g., Nena's "99 Red Balloons," Frankie Goes to Hollywood's "Two Tribes," Metallica's "Blackened"), racism (e.g., Lenny Kravitz's "Mr. Cab Driver," Midnight Oil's "Beds Are Burning," Sinéad O'Connor's "Black Boys on Mopeds"), and widespread opposition to Ronald Reagan's policies, which inspired millions of songs. "A partite was a very big topic. Peter Gabriel's Beacon, about the act of a Stephen Beacon who died in police custody." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0ARYt4sYT90
**Proto-Goth/Death Metal in Phantom of the Paradise** : The 1974 film 'Phantom of the Paradise' features an opera within the story by the character Swan, which includes the construction of a Frankenstein-like monster named Beef and a fictional band called The Undead, whose singer, Peter Ibling, and the rest of the band are depicted in "corpse paint," a visual style associated with modern death metal or black metal, suggesting a proto-goth or proto-death metal aesthetic from 1974. "In Phantom of the Paradise, Swan's Rock Opera, which is the story within the story, contains a sequence, featuring the construction of a Frankenstein-like monster named Beef. The entire scene of the movie features a made up band called the Undead." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Psychic Link in Rhythm Sections** : The most effective drum and bass combinations are characterized by a "psychic link" between the players, allowing their styles and timing to be seamlessly complimentary. This connection ensures that once the beat and groove begin, everything flows and falls into place, whether they are tight and accurate or have a relaxed feel. "The drummer and the bass player have some kind of psychic link so that once the beat and the groove starts, everything just flows and everything just falls into place." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CVPPymc_qrk
**Psychological Link: Introverts as Front Persons** : Psychologists theorize that introverted musicians often excel as band front persons. This is because shy musicians tend to be more attuned to a crowd's energy, which enables them to establish a closer and more authentic connection with the audience. "psychologists say that introverts often make the best front persons for bands. Why? Well because shine musicians tend to pick up on the crowd's energy and can create a closer, more authentic connection with the audience." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**Psychopathy and Music Taste Study (NYU)** : A study conducted by psychology professor Pasco Walsh at New York University involved 190 students who completed psychopathy tests and then rated over 100 unfamiliar songs across various genres. The study found that students with psychopathic tendencies strongly preferred songs like Eminem's "Lose Yourself," Blackstreet's "No Diggity," and Justin Bieber's "Boyfriend" (likely "Do What You Mean"). Conversely, they disliked songs such as Dire Straits' "Money for Nothing," The Knack's "My Sharona," and classical music. "Psychopathic trending students really dug M&M's lose yourself, black streets no diggity, and Justin Bieber's do what you mean." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**Public Backlash Against Service Charges (1970s)** : The introduction of service charges, even as low as 50 cents per ticket (around 8% of a $6 ticket face value in the 1970s), led to widespread public dissatisfaction and accusations of price gouging, with many screaming "rip off." "50 cents represents about 8% added on to the cost of the ticket. And many people were not happy about this. They would scream, rip off." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OCkov6FTliE
**Public Confusion during Format War (1949-1954)** : From 1949 through approximately 1954, consumers experienced significant confusion due to the ongoing "format war" between the 12-inch, 33 1/3 RPM LP (supported by most major labels except RCA) and the 7-inch, 45 RPM single (pushed by RCA). Additionally, 78 RPM records remained very popular and widely manufactured. Consumers were annoyed by having to choose sides and potentially buy proprietary hardware, leading to a "tailspin" in record sales regardless of format. "By the end of 1949, consumers were rather confused. There was the 12-inch 33-in-a-third long playing album... RCA was determined to push ahead with their 7-inch 45 RPM single... And 78 RPM records were still very, very popular... Music fans were very annoyed for a while. They didn't like having to take sides... As a result, record sales, regardless of format, went into a tailspin." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VlIErdSSVOM
**Public Curiosity Regarding Musician Deaths** : There is a natural public curiosity surrounding the unexpected deaths of music personalities. People often seek answers to questions like "How did this happen?", "How could it happen?", and "What were the circumstances?". This curiosity is not necessarily ghoulish but rather a search for a reasonable explanation for what often seems like an unreasonable death. "It's not that we're necessarily being ghoulish or voyeuristic, but most of them we're just looking for a reasonable explanation for what seems to be an unreasonable death." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sP75_gbZzIU
**Public Curiosity and Record Access** : There is a natural, albeit morbid, sense of human curiosity about the deaths of famous musicians, which often drives people to seek explanations from public records. These can include family statements, doctors' accounts, police reports, coroner's testimonies, toxicology examinations, and autopsy results to help make sense of the event. "And often we just can't look away because, well, we just need to know." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hhii2_BVE7A
**Public Domain Christmas Songs** : Among the top five most covered Christmas songs, "Silent Night" and "Jingle Bells" are in the public domain, meaning their composers' rights holders no longer generate royalties from them. "Of that top five, two are in the public domain, meaning that they don't generate any royalties for the composer." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nYPGQiDL5bw
**Public Interest in Reopening Kurt Cobain's Case (1997)** : Public sentiment regarding Kurt Cobain's death was significantly divided, with a notable portion of the population advocating for a reopened investigation. A 1997 survey conducted on the Jane Houghton Show revealed that approximately 95% of listeners were interested in revisiting the case, reflecting widespread skepticism about the official suicide ruling. "I was astounded to hear that about 95% of the people listening to the Jane Houghton show at that time were interested in reopening the case and voted that way, called in and voted that way." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1Jt2fUBDusk
**Publishing Deals and Artist Advances** : New bands signing publishing deals often receive an advance payment against the future earnings of their songs. This advance helps them get established financially. Once the advance is recouped, the publisher continues to receive a commission for their ongoing work on behalf of the songwriter. "When you hear of a new band signing a publishing deal, it's very possible that they get an advance against future earnings of their songs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Publishing Income as Primary Artist Revenue** : Publishing income, generated whenever a song is played, performed, sold, or streamed, is typically an artist's greatest source of income from their music. It is paid out regularly, varying by contract. This income is the main focus for new catalog-buying companies. "publishing is usually the artist's greatest source of income when it comes to their music." "And this is the publishing income that's the main focus of this new breed of companies." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Publishing as Music Monetization** : "Publishing" is the primary method to monetize music, a practice dating back to the late 19th century when professional songwriters sold music as printed sheet music. Publishers collected money from sales and shared profits with songwriters. "Publishing is how you monetize music. We call it publishing because this practice descended from the time professional songwriters sold their music as printed sheet music. This dates from at least the late 19th century." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Punched Card Data Tabulation for US Census (1860, 18xx Census)** : Herman Hollerith, born in Buffalo in 1860, developed an ingenious system to tabulate US Census data using cards with holes punched in them. This innovation drastically reduced the time required for census completion, enabling the 18xx Census (likely 1890 based on historical context) to be finished in just three months, a massive improvement from the previous 10-year timeframe. "Hollerith figured out a way to tabulate all the data using cards with holes punched in them." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PNiL63fY1Y8
**Punk's Influence on Music Creation** : By the late 1970s, the punk explosion had highlighted that one didn't need to be a technically proficient musician to create music, provided there was desire and something meaningful to express. "See, punk had opened everyone's eyes to possibilities. The message was, you didn't have to be the world's greatest musician. But if you had the desire and something to say, well then you should say it, dammit." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1KF5MwJVYng
**Purist Reaction to Tape Editing** : The ability to edit magnetic tape, allowing for splicing multiple performances together, provoked outrage from purists. They argued that if musicians could not perform a song correctly in a single take, they had "no right to call yourself a musician," viewing editing as "cheating." "If you can't play the song right the first time or in the way you intended, then you have no right to call yourself a musician." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Purpose of Art Context Podcast** : The podcast "Art Context" aims to give "amazing creative artists" from the music video world the recognition they deserve. The hosts believe these directors create some of the "most impactful content on the planet" and seek to uncover their stories, influences, highs, lows, and breakthrough moments, providing a "close set behind experience" of the music video production process. "I feel like we live in the world where we don't give these amazing creative artists the flowers they deserve." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LgJ7ww2AgF4
**Purpose of the Large 45 RPM Hole** : The large hole served multiple purposes for RCA: it required consumers to buy RCA's turntables with a special spindle; it made it easier for records to drop onto the platter in a stacking system; and it spread the turntable's torque over a greater circumference, reducing wear on the hole. "The bigger hole served several purposes. First of all, if you wanted to play these records, you had to buy an RCA record player with a special spindle." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**Pursuit of Individual Recognition** : A motivation for a band member to go solo can be the desire for personal recognition, seeking to be known as an "individual artist" rather than merely a part of a collective "team" or "group." This often leads to them not returning to the band. "the person in question wants a little extra recognition of their talents. Instead of continuing to be seen as a part of a whole, a team, a group, they would like to be known as an individual artist." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-f9jhqAHkQo
**Putamen and Movement to Music** : The putamen, an area of the brain responsible for processing rhythm, body movement, and coordination, becomes active when music-generated dopamine flows into it. This often results in a spontaneous need to move with the music, such as tapping a foot, banging a beat, or dancing, as the body instinctively synchronizes. "Once music-generated dopamine gets into this area of the brain, we may feel a spontaneous need to move with the music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**Pythagoras and the Institutionalization of Music (c. 570 BC)** : Pythagoras, known for his mathematical contributions, is considered arguably the most important figure in the history of music for institutionalizing musical sounds. Around 570 BC, he sought to bring order to the unconstrained and undefined state of music, using math and science to coordinate and confine musical notes and tuning. His work forms the basis for distinguishing melody from noise and underpins virtually all music today. "But Pythagoras was also arguably the most important person in the history of music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_Pi7JLwAeOI
**Pythagorean Tuning System** : Pythagoras developed a fundamental tuning system based on mathematical ratios by experimenting with string lengths. He observed that a string half the length of another produced a tone exactly twice as high, leading to the organization of tones using a 3:2 ratio. This mathematically pure system became the foundational method for tuning instruments and composing Western music, influencing intonations and scales. "And Pythagorean tuning became the basis for tuning instruments and thus composing music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_Pi7JLwAeOI
**Pythagorean Tuning in Western Music History** : Pythagorean tuning was the dominant and almost exclusive method for tuning Western music from around 570 BC until the early 1500s. Although Western music began to experiment with and move beyond this tuning by the 1800s, Pythagoras's foundational argument that music should be based on mathematics profoundly influenced all subsequent musical development, laying the groundwork for the music listened to today. "In fact it was pretty much the only accepted way of tuning for Western music until the beginning of the 1500s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_Pi7JLwAeOI
**Python Programming for Songwriting** : Rivers Cuomo hired programmers who used Python to organize his lyrical data in a highly granular way for the "Pacific Daydream" album, tagging everything with specific attributes. He later learned Python himself to create his "Drive Times" app. "In case you're wondering, the programmers did everything in Python." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LYRPxtP61JM
**Q Prime's Impact and Privacy** : Cliff Bernstein and Peter Mensch, founders of Q Prime, have collectively helped their artists sell hundreds of millions of records over the years. Despite their enormous success and influence, they maintain a very private profile, rarely giving interviews, posing for photos, or being seen partying, actively staying out of headlines. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PzyvXmDKFUc
**QAnon Conspiracy Theory** : QAnon is a conspiracy theory asserting that a government insider known as "Q" drops "breadcrumbs," understood by believers to mean that Donald Trump is leading a secret global fight against a cabal of Satan-worshipping politicians, celebrities, and journalists involved in child sex trafficking and cannibalism. Adherents believe figures like Barack Obama, George Soros, and Tom Hanks will inevitably be arrested for hideous crimes. "A government insider known as Q drops these breadcrumbs, and those who believe in Q understand that Donald Trump is leading a secret global fight against a cabal of Satan worshipping politicians and celebrities and journalists who are engaged in everything from child sex trafficking to cannibalism." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pmKjZzypzvg
**Quadrophonic Sound (Early to Mid-1970s)** : An attempt to improve stereo recording in the early to mid-1970s, quadrophonic sound required specially recorded quad albums, a decoder amplifier, and four speakers, but it was primitive and didn't catch on due to expense and lack of industry standards. "In the early to mid-70s, there were something called quadrophonic sound, which involved specially recorded and pressed quad albums." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Quadrophonic Sound Introduction** : Quadrophonic sound was an emerging technology that added depth to concert audio by utilizing additional speakers, specifically "front left and right, and rear left and right." This innovation aimed to create a more immersive and spatially rich listening experience for the audience. "Another new trick was quadrophonic sound." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qrrwqdpCjpQ
**Quality vs. Convenience** : The enjoyment of music is presented as not needing to be a rational experience, suggesting that society often sacrifices quality for convenience, a trend some believe should stop. "In terms of convenience, there's no rational reason to choose vinyl, compared to MP3's and digital music players, the whole thing's a pain in the ass. But since when does the enjoyment of music have to be a rational experience?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Queen Elizabeth II's Silver Jubilee – 1977** : A year-long celebration was staged in 1977 to mark Elizabeth II's 25th anniversary of her ascension to the throne. This event was intended as a way to "soothe the subjects" amidst the nation's economic and social challenges. "What better way to soothe the subjects than to stage a year-long celebration of Elizabeth II's 25th anniversary of her ascension to the throne?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gKrAXMG8sTc
**Queen Mary University Chills Playlist** : Queen Mary University in London compiled an extensive Spotify playlist featuring 700 songs that were found to be most likely to create a chills and/or goosebumps response in listeners. Their research highlighted that sadder, slower, less intense, and more instrumental songs were generally the most effective in inducing these physiological reactions. "Queen Mary University in London went so far as to create a Spotify playlist of the 700 songs, most likely to create a chills and or goosebumps response in people." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**Queens of the Stone Age – Chris Goss Suggested Changing "Kings" to "Queens"** : Producer Chris Goss of Masters of Reality suggested the name during recording sessions. The band had been considering "Kings of the Stone Age." Goss's argument: "queens" implied they were heavy enough for boys and sweet enough for girls. "Which seems to have worked." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Quentin Tarantino's Songwriting Credit** : Quentin Tarantino received 37% of all royalties and a songwriting credit for Fun Lovin' Criminals' "Scooby Snacks" because the song featured multiple samples from his movies "Reservoir Dogs" and "Pulp Fiction." "And when Tarantino heard the song, he called his lawyers and demanded 37% of all royalties and a songwriting credit, which he got." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZcF5JMhBj2M
**Québécois Separatism (1976)** : The Parti Québécois was elected in Quebec in 1976, declaring its intention to separate Quebec from Canada, adding to national political instability. "The party came back why I've been elected in Quebec in 1976, and had let it be known that they wanted to carve Quebec away from the rest of Canada." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**R. Budd Dwyer Suicide (January 22, 1987)** : R. Budd Dwyer, the Pennsylvania state treasurer, was convicted of accepting a $300,000 bribe from CTA, a company selected to resolve a state tax problem. The morning before his sentencing on January 23, 1987, Dwyer called a press conference, reiterated his innocence, and then fatally shot himself in the mouth with a .357 Magnum, an event captured on video by at least five cameras. The broadcast of this footage sparked widespread debate on appropriate news coverage. "The whole thing was caught on video by at least five people." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cM3-tINKdvo
**R. Budd Dwyer's Public Suicide (1987)** : R. Budd Dwyer, the State Treasurer of Pennsylvania, faced a corruption scandal involving a $300,000 bribe from CTA, a company selected to resolve a state tax problem. Convicted for bribery, Dwyer called a press conference on January 23, 1987, the morning before his sentencing. Reiterating his innocence, he pulled out a .357 magnum from a vanilla envelope and shot himself in the mouth, dying instantly. The suicide was captured on video by at least five cameras and widely broadcast, igniting a national debate about appropriate news coverage. "The morning before Dwyer called a press conference in front of reporters and TV cameras. He made a statement, reiterating his innocence, pulled out a 357, maged him from a vanilla envelope, and shot himself in the mouth." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4PlieAEthSg
**R.E.M. – Michael Stipe Randomly Pulled It From a Dictionary** : The band needed a name before their first gig at a church in Athens, Georgia. Michael Stipe opened a dictionary at random and pulled out REM, which stands for Rapid Eye Movement and relates to dreaming. For the band, the letters don't stand for anything. "Singer Michael Stipe just randomly pulled it out of a dictionary." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Rabena – Photography Prop** : Rabena is a British soft drink that was intentionally diluted and used as a prop in place of real red wine for the "Definitely Maybe" album cover photoshoot. This creative decision was made because actual red wine tends to appear black in photographs, whereas the diluted Rabena successfully mimicked the visual appearance of red wine. "It's a British soft drink called Rabina that was diluted just enough to look like red wine because if you photograph real wine, it tends to look black in the photographs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Radio "Power Gold" Song Categories** : Rock radio stations, in addition to current "power" songs, often have a "power gold" category. These are songs typically over two years old that have been established as all-time favorites through rigorous audience research. Such songs might be played five to eight times a week, depending on the station's audience and philosophy. "A rock station will have a power gold category or something like it. These are songs usually more than two years old that have established themselves as all time favorites with the station's audience, something that's determined through rigorous audience research and testing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Radio Announcer Leeway (Pre-Grunge)** : In the pre-grunge era of alt-rock radio, announcers had "a tremendous amount of leeway in choosing the music" played, often preferring extended mixes over album or single versions because their longer duration provided practical benefits like time to file records, answer request lines, and plan the next song. "Back then, the announcer had a tremendous amount of leeway in choosing the music that we played, and many of us found these extended mixes to be better than the album with the single mix, that they were longer to help." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Radio Broadcasting Shift in Late 1940s/Early 1950s** : The emergence of television caused major networks (NBC, CBS, Mutual) to pivot away from radio programming, leaving hundreds of local radio stations without content. This led to a major shift where stations, seeking cheap and fast solutions, began hiring DJs to play popular records, moving radio's focus from dramas and variety shows to music and aiding the mixing of R&B, Blues, Country, and Western into rock and roll. "The networks began to pivot away from cross-country radio program to cross-country TV program." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hQJ3r-DCoMQ
**Radio Call Letter Conventions – Why Canadian Stations Begin With C, US East Is W, West Is K** : Radio call letters function as a station's license plate. In Canada, all station call signs must begin with the letter C, with the second letter being F, H, I, J, or K (e.g., CFTO, CHUM, CKCO). Most Canadian stations have four letters; the few three-letter stations (e.g., CKX Brandon) date from the 1920s and 1930s. In the US, stations east of the Mississippi must begin with W; stations west of it must begin with K — with historical exceptions grandfathered in from before the rule change. "Why do all Canadian radio stations have call letters that begin with the letter C?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JvOEAzmC6l0
**Radio Programming Shift During WWII** : During World War II, local American radio stations were permitted to broadcast local programming after 7 pm. Playing records became more cost-effective than live acts, creating airtime for genres like country, western, hillbilly, and R&B, which previously had limited exposure. This change fostered a "melting pot" of new sounds. "First, local American stations were allowed to have local programming after 7pm...But it also made room for country, western, hillbilly, and R&B records." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**Radio Ratings vs. Artistic Fulfillment** : While extended mixes and diverse playlists were artistically fulfilling for 80s alt-rock radio staff, they were detrimental to ratings. Commercial radio relies on appealing to the largest possible audience for extended periods, and for many, this means easily singable songs, not necessarily "good music" in the hardcore fan sense. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Radio Star Maker Program (2000)** : A new funding program, Radio Star Maker, was added in 2000 to supplement the money available from Factor. This cash proved instrumental in furthering the careers of artists such as Metric, Broken Social Scene, Alexisonfire, and City and Colour, often converting loans into grants and increasing federal government involvement. "In 2000, a new program called Radio Star Maker was added to the money available from Factor, and soon this cash was instrumental in helping further the careers of metric, broken social scene, Alexis on fire, city and color, and many more." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6PGgNCaW4xo
**Radio Station Hijack in Malmö, Sweden** : A radio station in Malmö, Sweden, experienced a signal hijack for half an hour. During this time, someone managed to repeatedly play an English-language ISIS recruiting song over the airwaves. "A radio station in Malmo Sweden had their signal hijacked for half an hour this year by someone who managed to play an English language ISIS recruiting song on repeat." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**Radio Stations Switching to All-Christmas Format** : In 2020, radio stations began switching to all-Christmas formats earlier than usual, with one station in Ohio starting on September 28th at 12:25 PM. This trend, which began about 20 years ago, is driven by ratings, as stations adopting this format in November and December consistently achieve huge audience totals and outperform competitors. "And they started with the Christmas tunes on September 28th at 1225 PM." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nYPGQiDL5bw
**Radio as a Source of Real-time Information (Pre-Internet)** : Before the widespread adoption of the internet (prior to 1996), radio was the primary and often sole source for real-time information. Record labels and international contacts, such as Lee Carter in the UK, would feed stations the latest music information weeks before music magazines arrived in Canadian stores. "radio at that time was where everyone went to get real-time information." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bI6TTA-fn7c
**Radio's Impact on Wired Music Services** : The invention of radio ultimately led to the demise of the Tiatrophone/Electrophone service in 1931. Radio's ability to transmit audio wirelessly, without the need for extensive physical telephone lines running to individual homes, made it a superior and more convenient technology for accessing music. "This lasted a long time, too, all the way to 1931, in fact, and the thing that killed it, radio, which required no wires running all over the place." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Radio's Initial Portability Challenge** : Early private company radios, available after World War I, were heavy devices powered by equally heavy batteries, limiting their convenient movement. The subsequent shift to wall-plug radios further tethered them to fixed locations, hindering the development of truly mobile listening. `"Private companies started making radios, but most of them were big heavy things containing a big heavy battery."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Radio's Shift to Pre-Recorded Music (Post-TV Rise)** : With the rise of television, radio experienced a programming deficit as many stars and shows transitioned to TV. To save money and compete, radio stations began playing more pre-recorded music, predominantly 7-inch singles, which became the standard currency for radio airplay. "So to save money and to find a way of competing with television, radio started playing more and more pre-recorded music. In other words, records. And the records they chose to play most often were the 7-inch single." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**Rage Against the Machine – A 1979 Chevy Van That Kept Breaking Down, or Political Metaphor** : The phrase "Rage Against the Machine" was used in a punk fanzine called No Answers in 1989 by a writer covering the pre-RATM band Inside Out, which featured Zack de la Rocha. Conventional wisdom holds that "the machine" represents oppressive capitalism. However, de la Rocha once noted literally: the band's constant-breakdown 1979 Chevy van was the thing they raged against, taking sledgehammers to it when they finally had money to buy a new one. "So the machine that they were raging against was 1979 Chevy van." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Rainbow Butt Monkeys (later Finger Eleven) – Joke Name for a High School Band That Got a Record Deal** : Named as a joke because no one expected the high school band to amount to anything. After signing to a label and releasing four EPs and an album under that name, they changed to Finger Eleven — but Rainbow Butt Monkeys artifacts remain in existence. "They picked it as a joke because there was no way their high school band would amount to anything." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Ramones – Paul McCartney Once Checked Into Hotels as "Paul Ramone"** : When the Beatles were touring, Paul McCartney sometimes checked into hotels under the pseudonym Paul Ramone. That name was good enough for the Ramones. "When the Beatles were touring, Paul McCartney would often sign into hotels as Paul Ramone." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Ramones' First Gig Sound** : At their debut performance at CBGB on August 16, 1974, the Ramones' sound was described by scene-ster Ligs McNeil as "just this wall of noise." Their appearance, featuring leather jackets, t-shirts, torn jeans, and Converse high-tops, struck him as "completely new" and distinctly non-hippie. "And it was just this wall of noise. They look so striking. These guys were not hippies. This was something completely new." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=j8uk7BnbXwU
**Rampant Drug Use and Its Toll** : Heroin and cocaine use became "rampant" among musicians and the industry during Britpop's decline, taking a significant toll. This drug use severely impacted creative output, as seen with Oasis's *Be Here Now* album and Elastica's inability to record a second album. "And fourth, and this is something that no one really wanted to talk about, was all the heroin and all the cocaine." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5q5EnZj1FwE
**Rampant Drug Use in Britpop** : Heroin and cocaine use became "rampant" within the Britpop scene and industry, taking a significant toll on musicians' minds and creative output, a factor largely unspoken at the time but contributing to the era's decline. "was all that heroin and cocaine that was messing everybody's minds. Drug use was rampant, and it was starting to take its toll, not only amongst musicians, but in the industry in general." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vzyMSSlD7Yg
**Ranking Methodology** : Ranking for alt-rock one-hit wonders involved consulting alt-rock radio charts over decades to create a list of around 100 candidates. Then, Spotify plays and YouTube views were summed, and a power ranking scale from 1 to 10 (created by "Walter the Mathematician") was assigned for "one-hit wonder-ness." "I looked up the number of Spotify plays and YouTube views. Those two numbers were added together." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZcF5JMhBj2M
**Rap/Hip Hop's Expanding White Audience** : Thanks to pioneering groups like Body Count, Living Color, the Beastie Boys, and Rage Against the Machine, rap and hip hop successfully broadened its reach to encompass both white rock fans and people of color who were early adopters of the genre. "rap and hip hop was able to reach both more white kids, the rockers, and people of color, those who embraced hip hop early." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5bQz8R0gbXc
**Rapid Burnout of 90s Alt Rock** : The focus on primarily guitar-based alt rock in the 1990s, driven by the massive popularity of grunge, led to the music industry and fans chasing those specific sounds excessively. This resulted in a "rapid burn down," with music becoming increasingly diluted and derivative, putting alt rock and rock in general "in big trouble" by the end of the decade due to a limited artistic sandbox. "But in the end, this resulted in a rapid burn down. There was only so many good artists who could play in this particular sandbox. Things got more and more diluted and derivative and by the end of the decade, alt rock and rock in general wasn't big trouble." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_yslM5oanRo
**Rapid Disruption in 2007** : Around 2007, the music landscape underwent a "shockingly short period of time" of rapid disruption. During this period, guitars unexpectedly fell out of favor, pop music experienced a major comeback, and an economic disaster significantly altered the overall mood and direction of alt rock. "In a shockingly short period of time, guitars fell out of favor and popped met a huge comeback. Meanwhile, an economic disaster seemed to change the entire mood of alt rock." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7dXtgTkdv9w
**Rapprochement Between Rockers and Rappers** : After years of mutual wariness, rockers and rappers in the 1990s discovered a significant amount of common ground and shared interests, leading to notable collaborations. "After years of being wary of each other, rockers and rappers found that they had a substantial amount of common ground and shared interests." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5bQz8R0gbXc
**Rarity of Moon Crater Recognition** : Having a moon crater named after an individual is an extremely rare honor. John Lennon and Michael Jackson share this distinction with historical giants such as Leonardo da Vinci, Christopher Columbus, Isaac Newton, Jules Verne, and Julius Caesar. "Very, very few people get moon craters named after them." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VMPLplhDk8I
**Rationale Behind RCA's 45 RPM 7-inch Design** : RCA designed its 7-inch, 45 RPM record with grooves placed on the outer half of the disc to prevent distortion and wear that typically occurred closer to the center where grooves were more tightly packed. This necessitated a two-and-a-half-inch radius from the center for the label and a tone arm buffer zone, leaving less "real estate" for music. To increase capacity beyond a single song, RCA implemented stackable records on a special spindle, with a slight lip on the outer edge of each record to prevent groove grinding. "Using a little geometry, engineers put the record grooves on the outer half of the record. This presented distortion and wear when you got towards the center when the grooves were packed more closely together... By manufacturing a record player with a special spindle so you could stack these new records on top of each other. When one finished playing, the tone arm would swing back and the next record would drop into place. A slight lip on the outer edge of each record would help keep the delicate grooves from grinding together." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VlIErdSSVOM
**Rationale for Radio Song Repetition** : Radio stations repeatedly play popular songs to cater to listeners who tune in for short durations (e.g., 10-20 minutes). Since listeners cannot customize their playlists, frequent repetition increases the chance that any individual tuning in will hear a song they like, thereby maximizing audience satisfaction and retention. "So the only way to make sure as many people as possible here, the biggest and most popular songs is to play those songs more often, thereby increasing the chances that any individual when they tune in will hear in what they're hoping for." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Rave Culture's Focus on the DJ** : Rave culture, which emerged in the UK in 1988, was distinct in that it was not centered around a band or a specific song, but rather on the DJ. The DJ, often anonymous, became the performer and star, responsible for making mixes and generating the dance atmosphere. "It wasn't about a band or song. It was about the DJ, the often anonymous guy or woman, up in the booth, making the mixes and making the people dance." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OiqdZfxdBP0
**Ray Kurzweil's Early Composing Computer (1967)** : At just 17 years old, Ray Kurzweil demonstrated a computer he had personally built and programmed to compose music for the piano on the CBS show "I've Got a Secret." This early innovation showcased the potential for young minds and technology to merge in artistic creation, long before modern AI. "He demonstrated a piece of music for the piano that was composed by a computer that he built in programs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Real Audio Development** : Rob Glazer, an ex-Microsoft employee, founded Progressive Networks in 1994, viewing the internet as a new broadcasting system. His team created Real Audio, a proprietary system designed to stream audio in real-time and from dedicated servers, pioneering internet audio delivery. "He and his team created a proprietary system called Real Audio, which was designed to stream audio in both real-time and from a dedicated server." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Real Grooves vs. Machine-Made** : The discussion emphasizes the value of "real grooves" created by human rhythm sections, distinguishing them from those "made with a machine or cooked up on Pro Tools." A skilled rhythm section can elevate even an average band into something truly special. "And we're talking real grooves too, nothing made with a machine or cooked up on Pro Tools." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CVPPymc_qrk
**Real Networks** : Real Networks is the later name for Rob Glazer's pioneering company, Progressive Networks. It eventually acquired Rhapsody, which later rebranded itself as Napster. "And then it was bought by Real Networks, which was, of course, the new name for Rob Glazer's progressive networks." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Real-Time Data Analytics in Music Industry** : Streaming platforms generate vast amounts of data, enabling unprecedented real-time identification of music trends and career opportunities; record labels now have internal analytics departments, and specialized companies assist musicians in tracking global song performance and consumption. "The insane amount of data that can be harvested from the streaming platforms makes it possible to identify trends and opportunities in ways never before possible." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Reality Bites (1994) as Slacker Culture Key Movie** : *Reality Bites* (1994), starring Winona Ryder and Ethan Hawke, was identified as a "key movie" in the emergence and definition of "slacker culture." Its soundtrack featured artists like The Knack, U2, and produced a massive hit with Lisa Loeb's "Stay (I Missed You)," reflecting the era's cultural landscape. "Only bites, a key movie and the whole slacker culture thing featuring Winona Ryder and Ethan Hawk, the NAC, you two, and the Massive Hit for Lisa Loeb stay, I missed you." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**Reasons for Canadian Producers to Work Abroad** : Some Canadian producers choose to move outside Canada, notably to the U.S., due to the perception of greater financial opportunities ("no U2 style money") and high taxes at home. A trend also shows American labels signing Canadian bands directly to U.S. deals, with the bands often bringing their Canadian producers with them. "There's no U2 style money producing bands at home and besides he says the taxes are too high and more Canadian producers may follow simply because more American labels are sounding Canadian bands directly to American record deals and what we've seen is that the Canadians are bringing their producers with them." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkSKxm_U9Yc
**Reasons for Catalog Sales (Older Artists)** : Older artists, particularly those in their 60s or 70s, sell their catalogs for various reasons: to cash out and enjoy retirement, to address financial needs, concerns about future touring revenue (exacerbated by COVID-19), or a struggle to accept declining record sales in the streaming era. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Reasons for Decline in Prominent Alt-Rock Protest in 21st Century** : The first half of the 2010s was remarkably quiet for protest songs in alt-rock, despite persistent global conflicts, the 2008 financial meltdown, soaring student debt, the stance of the religious right, the refugee crisis, terrorism, and global warming. Several theories explain this phenomenon: 1) Protests shifted to social media platforms like Facebook and Twitter. 2) Rock music's cultural influence waned, with hip hop and rap becoming the dominant vehicles for protest. 3) Alt-rock fans may feel powerless to effect change. 4) Some suggest this generation grew up without a fighting spirit, accustomed to universal praise ("everybody gets a trophy"). "But the first half of the second decade of the new millennium has been awfully quiet when it comes to protest songs. Despite the never ending wars in the Middle East, the financial meltdown of 2008, crushing student debt, the attitude of the religious right in the US, Republican presidential candidates, Wall Street Crooks who weren't sent to jail, the refugee crisis, terrorism, global warming, more globalization." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0ARYt4sYT90
**Reasons for Electronic Fade-Outs** : Fade-outs offered several benefits: artistically, they freed artists from composing definitive endings, allowing endless repetition of hooks; aesthetically, they reinforced the song's impact by making it feel like it continued indefinitely; technically, engineers used fades to prevent audio degradation towards the center of master disks; and for radio, fades were used to shorten songs over three minutes and provide a cue for DJs to talk over. "Artistically, it meant that the artist did not have to come up with a definite ending." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**Reasons for Music Licensing** : Artists increasingly pursue music licensing opportunities (for commercials, TV shows, movies, video games) as a crucial replacement for lost revenue. Factors like crashing physical music sales, inadequate income from touring or streaming, and global events such as pandemics have driven musicians to find alternative income streams. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Reasons for Slam Dancing's Emergence** : Several factors contributed to the rise of slam dancing: it was a rejection of "hippie" culture, it served as a defiant response to the prevalent disco and "Saturday Night Fever" culture, and it offered an outlet for aggressive expression, particularly among young men. Bands sometimes actively provoked audiences to increase the intensity. "But slam dancing is something hippies would not do." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KMx4bmq8o2Y
**Rebecca Schaefer Murder by John Bardo (Year not given)** : Actress Rebecca Schaefer, 21, was stalked for three years by John Bardo, who eventually murdered her by shooting her at point-blank range in Los Angeles. His obsession escalated after seeing her in a movie scene in bed with another actor, believing she needed to be "punished for becoming another Hollywood whore." "Rebecca Schaefer was having a good life. She was 21 years old and already had a run on a CBS sitcom called My Sister Sam." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Recent Musician Plane Crash Fatalities (Post-2000)** : The incidence of musician deaths in plane crashes has drastically decreased since the turn of the millennium. Notable, though infrequent, incidents include singer and actress Aaliyah, who died in a twin-engine Cessna crash in the Bahamas in 2001. That same year, Melanie Thornton of the group LaBouche perished in a Crossair plane crash in Switzerland. In 2009, two members of Chuck Mangione's band were killed in a commuter plane crash outside of Buffalo. "The last big name to die in a small plane crash was Alia, the singer in actress. That was a twin-engine Cessna in the Bahamas in 2001." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dgzF_KBSQWM
**Reciprocal Recording – Indie Studio Hub** : Reciprocal Recording was a local recording studio run by Jack Endino in Seattle, known for catering to indie groups with very modest budgets. It became a crucial hub for the burgeoning grunge scene, recording bands like Green River, Screaming Trees, and Nirvana, as well as contributions for the *Deep Six* compilation. "As well as playing in Skinyard, he had a local recording studio called Reciprocal Recording, whose clientele consisted of indie groups with very modest budgets. This included Green River, Screaming Trees, and some little band called Nirvana." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lip0bZX7hBE
**Record Collecting and Valuation** : The value of a collectible record or CD depends on its condition, rarity, and market demand. A unique item, like a one-of-a-kind purple vinyl, might be worthless if there's no demand. Historically, prices were set by dealers and collectors at record fairs, and guides like Record Collector and Goldmine (Record Collector still active) provided pricing. The internet, particularly sites like eBay, gemm.com, and 991.com, has made valuation easier but has also caused prices for some items to spike dramatically. Collectors are advised to be aware of "buyer beware" situations where online prices might be inflated compared to established guides. "See how much a record is worth is dependent on its condition, its rarity, and how much demand there is for it in the marketplace." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zUQ2vZzmRiI
**Record Collector Magazine** : A magazine specifically for record collectors, highlighting the established culture of vinyl collecting. "magazines like record collector." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Record Collector Obsession** : Engaging in record collecting can become an "expensive obsession," metaphorically described as being "sucked into a very, very deep rat hole." "if you get into record collecting, you could end up being sucked into a very, very deep rat hole where a hobby becomes an obsession, a very expensive obsession." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IIJySSo276k
**Record Collectors and Vintage Vinyl** : Record collectors, dedicated to hunting for rare and interesting vinyl, formed a culture around collecting vintage vinyl, which became highly valuable and collectible. "Then we have record collectors, consumers who got into the hunt for rare and interesting vinyl. Certain vintage vinyl became highly collectible and very valuable." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Record Condition Factors** : The price of any used record is directly affected by its physical condition, encompassing both the packaging (e.g., absence of marks, water damage, written names, and the presence of all original inserts) and the vinyl itself (e.g., free from scratches, scuffs, warping, or a misshapen spindle hole). "The price of any used record also depends on its physical condition, both in terms of the packaging and the vinyl itself." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IIJySSo276k
**Record Industry "Herd Mentality"** : The record industry exhibits a "herd mentality," wherein once a successful trend like grunge or punk is identified, there is an immediate "stampede" to sign and promote similar acts. This often progresses from original bands to "like bands," then "adjacent bands," and finally "any sort of band that could be classified as alternative." "Once a successful trend is identified, there's a stampede to sign and promote acts for that space." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gkLMwrthZ9s
**Record Industry Phasing Out Singles** : In the late 1990s, the record industry began "phasing out singles," forcing consumers who wanted just one song from a band to "cop up 20 bucks and get the full CD." "At the same time, the record industry was phasing out singles. You want that one song from a band? Sorry, cop up 20 bucks and get the full CD." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=amHre9ZZFkU
**Record Industry Recession (1922) and Great Depression Impact** : After experiencing booming sales post-World War I, the record industry plunged into recession in 1922 with the rise of radio. This decline worsened dramatically with the onset of the Great Depression, causing the industry to shrink by an astonishing 95%, with unit sales plummeting from 104 million in 1927 to under 6 million by 1932. This period saw terrible price wars, with 78s selling for under ten cents, severely impacting artists and songwriters. "But then a new technology called Radio came along, and in nineteen twenty-two, the record industry was thrust into recession... By the time the depression started, the record industry had shrunk by ninety-five percent, ninety-five percent." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VlIErdSSVOM
**Record Industry Revenues (1990s)** : The record industry experienced immense financial prosperity in the 1990s, with revenues flowing freely. In the US alone, revenues grew from $8 billion in 1990 to $14 billion by the end of the decade, with global revenues on track to reach $37 billion. This financial boom was largely driven by the high profit margins of compact discs and the strategy of selling full albums. "In the US alone, revenues were $8 billion in 1990, with a trajectory that pointed almost straight up." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r8AazBVoS7g
**Record Industry Sales Crisis and Home Taping (Late 1970s - Early 1980s)** : The record industry faced a major sales crisis in the late 1970s and early 1980s, triggered by the burst of the disco bubble, which caused record sales to enter "free fall." The industry identified "home taping" – people copying albums onto cassettes for friends – as the primary problem. "Last time the record industry found themselves facing a major sales crisis was in the late 1970s and the early 1980s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2RKchFW42Jc
**Record Industry's Emphasis on Songs vs. Albums** : The recorded music industry, struggling with the breakdown of old physical sales models, shifted focus to individual songs and their placement on playlists rather than nurturing artists over multiple albums. This "WAM-BAM Thank You MAM" speed makes it harder for new acts to develop, affecting all genres. "There's been a bigger emphasis on individual songs and where they fit on a Spotify playlist than there is for an album that might be promoted over two or three or even four years." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5Y6nHw5xx88
**Record Jacket as an Art Form** : The introduction of the cardboard record jacket, patented by the Imperial Paper Box Company, transformed packaging into a new art form. Unlike fragile paper sleeves, these tougher jackets could be printed with designs without damage, opening up opportunities for visual creativity. "The Imperial Paper Box Company held the patent, and the jacket soon became the palette for a new art form." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pwebM3TfSdg
**Record Label Embrace of SoundScan** : Although initially resistant, record labels quickly adopted SoundScan technology because it provided invaluable hard data on who was buying what music and where. This accurate information enabled labels to identify genuinely popular genres, such as alternative rock, and allocate significant marketing and talent scouting resources accordingly, fueling an "Alt Rock Gold Rush." "Now that, thanks to Sound Scan, labels saw that this weirdo music was actually selling tons of units without much marketing power." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r8AazBVoS7g
**Record Label Environment in Late 60s/Early 70s** : The late 1960s and early 1970s were characterized by an "everything goes" attitude within many record labels, a period before the industry became highly corporatized and formal. As rock music was still a young and rapidly evolving art form, labels and their personnel were more inclined to take chances on new and experimental sounds, without clear expectations of where musical trends were heading. "in the late 60s and early 70s, there was a certain everything goes attitude at a lot of record labels." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7_ZmZxnSOnE
**Record Label Pressure for Hits** : Record labels often demand specific musical directions from artists, especially when quarterly revenues are looking bleak, pushing for hits to secure executive jobs and bonuses. This pressure can lead to creative struggles and rebellion from the artists. "Mark claims it was because the label's quarterly revenues were looking bleak and they needed a hit so that the executives could keep their jobs and maybe get some bonuses." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tkLRJiOBu0
**Record Label Streaming Revenue (Mid-2017)** : In mid-2017, record labels were collectively earning $150,000 USD every minute from streaming. "In mid-2017, we learned that the labels were making a hundred and fifty thousand dollars US per minute from streaming." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Record Label Warning on UK Records (Pre-1988)** : Prior to 1988, British records manufactured in the UK frequently included a notice stating, "unauthorized public performance or broadcasting of this record is strictly prohibited." This warning directly referred to the "Needle Time" provisions, which regulated the broadcasting of recorded music on British radio. "if you look at British records manufactured in Britain before 1988, you'll see a notice that says, unauthorized public performance or broadcasting of this record is strictly prohibited. And that refers to the Needle Time provision in the UK." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0D4wGWI-_z8
**Record Labels Phasing Out Singles** : Record labels, enjoying fantastic album profits, gradually phased out singles, coercing fans to buy full albums even if they only desired one song, a move described as "arrogant and cynical." "In fact, they were enjoying such fantastic profits that they gradually phased out singles." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Record Labels' Initial CD Hesitation** : Record labels initially resisted the compact disc concept due to the expense of building new factories, warehousing and shipping challenges, anticipated resistance from retailers who would need to redesign stores, and fears over lack of copy protection allowing easy home copying. "Believe it or not, though, the labels wanted no part of the CD at first. They hated the concept." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Record Labels' Panic and iTunes Adoption** : Faced with panic over Napster's digital future, record labels felt compelled to accept Steve Jobs' iTunes online music store proposal, as they were in a vulnerable position. "The record labels began to panic." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Record Size Extremes – Largest: 50 cm (Europe 1900–1930); Smallest: 2-Inch Compilation; 16-Inch for Film/Radio** : The biggest records ever made were 50 centimeters (about 20 inches), seen in Europe between 1900 and 1930. Sixteen-inch records were used for movie soundtracks (the Vitaphone system) and radio transcription discs for pre-recorded programming before magnetic tape arrived in the 1940s. The smallest known records are 2 inches in diameter: one was a P-brain compilation of six bands each playing a 10-second song; the other was a 20-second hardcore song called "Soda Tax" by Two Minute Minor, protesting Cook County, Illinois accidentally imposing a tax on soft drinks. "The smallest record I know of has a diameter of just two inches." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=I291j5WBc4k
**Record Store Day (2008)** : Born from the inspiration of Comic Book Day, the first Record Store Day in 2008 successfully boosted vinyl sales, initiating years of "double digit growth" globally as people rediscovered or newly embraced the format. "After the first record store day in 2008, vinyl sales took off as more and more people got back into the format or into it for the first time." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Record Store Day Founding and Impact (2007)** : Record Store Day originated in 2007 from a meeting of independent record store owners in Baltimore who sought to revitalize their struggling businesses. Inspired by "Free Comic Book Day," they developed an event featuring special products and appearances to draw customers back to stores. The first Record Store Day in 2007 successfully spread worldwide, with hundreds of thousands of independent stores participating annually. Record labels recognized the marketing opportunity and joined with special, limited-edition vinyl releases, leading to the saving of numerous small shops and a significant increase in vinyl sales. "This led to the first record store day in 2007." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jD1dWxNLvfE
**Record Store Day Genesis (2008)** : The idea for Record Store Day originated from independent record store owners in Baltimore who were struggling due to digital music and piracy. Inspired by "comic book day," they conceived an annual event featuring free and exclusive products to attract customers, increase revenues, and boost vinyl sales. The first Record Store Day took place in 2008. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Record Store Day Gougers** : "Record Store Day gougers" are individuals who exploit the annual event by lining up early, or paying others to do so, to purchase limited edition stock specifically created for the day. Their intent is not personal enjoyment but rather to resell these items "for inflated prices on eBay," demonstrating a form of opportunistic entrepreneurship that disrupts the spirit of the event for genuine collectors. "These are the people who line up early on record store day or pay people to line up for them and then buy up all the limited edition stock created just for that day and then sell it all for inflated prices on eBay." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7Zniri9uSlc
**Record Store Day Scheduling and 2020 Changes** : Record Store Day is typically held on the third Saturday of April, with a secondary event scheduled for Black Friday in November. In 2020, due to the COVID-19 pandemic, the schedule was adjusted to include three Record Store Days in addition to the Black Friday sale. "Normally, record store day is held on the third Saturday of April with a secondary event scheduled for Black Friday in November." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jD1dWxNLvfE
**Record Store Growth and Great Depression Impact** : New record stores emerged throughout the 1920s, indicating a period of growth. However, this expansion was severely interrupted by the Great Depression, which caused an astonishing 95% drop in record sales between 1930 and 1932. The market eventually recovered, albeit slowly. "There was a big setback with a great depression, which saw sales dropped by an astounding 95% between 1930 and 1932." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jD1dWxNLvfE
**Record Store Resistance to CDs** : Record stores were initially resistant to the introduction of CDs due to significant logistical challenges. They already had extensive shelving for vinyl LPs, 7-inch singles, and were still accommodating 8-tracks, while also grappling with the explosive popularity of pre-recorded cassettes driven by devices like the Sony Walkman. Retailers were reluctant to install more shelving for another format, especially one requiring customers to purchase expensive new playback machines, and expressed concerns about the CD's small size making it easily shoplifted. "Record stores also weren't too happy about CDs. They already had miles and miles and miles of shelving for vinyl LPs. Plus, they had to devote space to 7-inch singles. People were still coming in to buy music on 8-track, and they were grappling with the explosive rise in the popularity of pre-recorded cassettes, thanks to this new thing called the Sony Walkman and similar portable music devices." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Record Store-Scented Candle from DW Campbell's** : A company called DW Campbell's now offers a candle designed to evoke the scent of a record store. The candle's fragrance is described as a blend of "press vinyl, warm woods, accented by notes of sweet blossoms, hints of amber and soft fruit." "You could now buy a record store-scented candle. It's from a company called DW Campbell's." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-N6IM5Nm5iw
**Recorded Music Industry Prosperity in the 1990s** : The 1990s were a period of immense financial prosperity for the Canadian recorded music industry, driven by high record sales. This influx of money translated into significant investment in advertising for both Canadian and international releases, supported flourishing music magazines, and funded extensive touring and festivals. "In today's digital world, it's hard to cast your mind back and imagine exactly how much money was flowing through the system because people were buying so many records." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6PGgNCaW4xo
**Recording Process: Mastering Stage** : Mastering is the final phase of preparing a two-track recording. This involves precisely balancing the overall volume without altering the existing mix and polishing the ultimate sound of the songs using tools like equalization and other specialized studio equipment, performed by a mastering engineer. "This is the final preparation of that two track recording, balancing up the volume, without messing with the mix, polishing the final, final, final sound of the songs with things like equalization and other studio tools." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Recording Process: Mixing Stage** : After songs are recorded, the mixing stage involves balancing all the individual components, such as vocals, guitars, and drums, to achieve the most aesthetically pleasing sound. These numerous tracks are then combined and reduced to a final two-stereo-track mix. "songs are recorded, then the songs are mixed, which means all the different parts like the vocals and the guitars and the drums and so on are balanced in the most pleasing ways and then mixed down from all those extra tracks to just two stereo tracks." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Recordings by Black Musicians on White-Owned Labels** : Throughout the 1910s and 1920s, many Black musicians were recorded by white-owned record labels. These recordings, released on 10-inch, 78 RPM records, gained widespread popularity across all audiences, highlighting the broad appeal of early Black music. "Through the 1910s and into the 1920s, many black musicians found themselves being recorded by white-owned record labels." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hQJ3r-DCoMQ
