**Recycled and Thinner Vinyl (Post-1973)** : To reduce costs after the 1973 oil shock, record labels began producing records that were thinner and made from recycled vinyl, leading to poorer sound quality, more skipping, and increased surface noise. "To cut costs, record labels began issuing records that were thinner and made of recycled vinyl." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Red Army Faction and Red Brigade** : Violent political activist groups in Western Europe, such as the Red Army Faction in West Germany and the Red Brigade in Italy, were known for their commitment to revolution and causing serious damage to the capitalist system. The KGB had interests in these groups, and their aims often aligned with the anti-establishment sentiments expressed by some punk bands, though without the bands resorting to armed resistance. "The KGB would have also had their fingers in violent political activists like the Red Army Faction in West Germany, the Red Brigade in Italy, and any number of leftist organizations committed to revolution." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YeNWDtN_sHE
**Red Book CDDA Standard Establishment (1979)** : The collaboration between Sony and Philips resulted in the "Red Book CDDA standard" (Compact Disc Digital Audio), a comprehensive set of specifications designed in 1979 that are still universally applied in the manufacturing of compact discs today. "The result was something known as the Red Book CDDA standard, which is a collection of specifications that are still used to make compact discs everywhere on the planet today." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Red Hot Chili Peppers vs. Red Hot Chili Pipers Mix-up** : A man from the UK purchased a ticket for approximately fifty dollars to see the Red Hot Chili Peppers perform in Dublin. Upon arrival, he was confused by the presence of numerous people wearing kilts, only to realize he had mistakenly bought a ticket to see the Red Hot Chili Pipers, a renowned bagpipes band. "He bought a ticket for around fifty bucks to see the Red Hot Chili Peppers play in Dublin. And when he got there, he was confused by all the people wearing kiltz." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**Red Stripe Beer – Photography Prop** : During the photoshoot for Oasis's "Definitely Maybe" album cover, cans of Red Stripe beer were initially present. However, they were deemed unsuitable for the desired aesthetic and were subsequently replaced by glasses containing a diluted soft drink. "At first, there were cans of empty red-strike beer all over the place." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Red Wax Cheese Sealant Invention** : The distinctive red wax sealant commonly used to wrap certain types of cheese was invented by Huey Lewis's grandfather. "He's the guy who came up with that red wax sealant that you find wrapping certain types of cheese." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QfiRRtsZoYU
**Rediscovering "Missed" Music from Youth** : When current music loses its appeal, many adults revert to their "coming of age years" (14-24) to explore songs, albums, and artists they might have missed. This includes music that was "too radical and too different" or simply "fell through the cracks" due to the rapid absorption of new sounds. This material, though decades old, feels "new to you" and offers the familiar power and energy of that era. "So what do many of us do? We go back to those coming of age years between 14 and 24 and start exploring songs, albums, and artists that we missed the first time around." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m7MbV-eCLyE
**Reformulated Sales Awards (1976)** : In 1976, the RIAA reformalized its sales awards criteria. A gold album in America was redefined as sales of 500,000 copies, and a new Platinum Award was introduced for sales of one million copies in the US. "Awards were based on dollar sales until 1976 when things were reformalized. A gold album in America meant that it had sales of 500,000 copies. And then there was a new thing called a Platinum Award, which meant it had sold a million copies in the US." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gP3oQeYEvso
**Refusal of Christmas Visit** : He refused Michael Hutchence's request to allow his children with Paula Yates to visit Australia for Christmas, leading to a loud argument witnessed by a hotel guest in Hutchence's final hours. "But Geldoff refused." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Regency TR-1 - First Pocket Transistor Radio (1954)** : The Regency TR-1, introduced in 1954 by an Indiana-based company (after larger manufacturers showed no interest), was a revolutionary battery-powered radio utilizing transistors. This enabled it to be shrunk down to a pocket-sized device. Announced on October 18, 1954, and available weeks later, it retailed for $49.95 (over $500 today), with an extra $7 for a headphone jack. This device became the ancestor of billions of portable radios worldwide and was crucial for the spread of Rock'n'Roll among teenagers. `"The big change came in 1954 when the Regency TR1 was introduced. This was a battery powered radio that used transistors, which meant that it could be shrunk down to a size that could fit in your pocket."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Reginald Fessenden – Canadian-Born Engineer, First to Broadcast a Human Voice (1906)** : While Guglielmo Marconi is commonly credited with inventing radio, he actually invented the wireless telegraph, capable of sending Morse code but not voice or music. The first person to actually broadcast a human voice through the air was Reginald Fessenden, a Canadian-born engineer working in a lab near Washington, D.C., who achieved intelligible transmission as early as 1900. On December 24, 1906, from Brant Rock, Massachusetts, he presided over the first proper radio broadcast: he played a Handel piece on a phonograph, read from the Bible, and played "O Holy Night" on his violin. Atlantic sailors accustomed to Morse code were bewildered. Fessenden became simultaneously the first DJ, first radio performer, and first radio announcer. "In the space of those few minutes, Fessenden became the first DJ, the first radio performer, and the first radio announcer." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JvOEAzmC6l0
**Regional Goth Terminology** : Goth followers have different names in various regions: "Gogan" in Australia, "Ku Khoracha" in Spain, and "dark" in Italy. "If you're an Australian, you're a Gogan. In Spain, you might be called a Ku Khoracha. In Italy, the term is dark." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**Regional Naming Preferences (Phonograph vs. Gramophone)** : Due to Edison's American origin and the word's integration into the language, North Americans commonly referred to any record-playing device as a "phonograph." Conversely, people in the UK and Europe tended to use the name "gramophone." By the late 1920s, these terms began to be used interchangeably, and Edison exited the record business entirely by the Great Depression. "But because he was American and the phonograph had worked its way into the language, people in North America still referred to any record playing device, any record playing device, as a phonograph. Meanwhile in the UK and Europe, they stuck with the name Gramophone. However, by the time we got to the late 1920s, people were using the terms phonograph and gramophone interchangeably." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TsiYLGGSJ2U
**Reissues of Older Albums as Source for Demos** : Reissues of older albums, often presented as "new and improved versions" or "Deluxe Editions" with extra bonus material, serve as a common method to entice long-term fans to repurchase records they already own. This strategy allows artists to release previously unavailable material. "One way to get fans to buy another copy of a record that they already own is to put out a new and improved version." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zT6pRGYLWhw
**Relationship Between Rock, Sex, and Rebellion** : From its very inception, rock music has been fundamentally intertwined with themes of "sex and rebellion set to music." This foundational connection established a genre that inherently challenged norms and expressed counter-cultural sentiments. "From the very beginning, rock has essentially been sex and rebellion set to music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hswtuenFwvM
**Relationship with Michael Hutchence** : Described as a "Bond girl from *The Living Daylights*," she was one of Michael Hutchence's partners. "He went out with Virginia Hay, a bond girl from the living daylights." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Reliability of Lie Detector Tests** : While a lie detector test, such as the one Elden Hoke took regarding his claims against Courtney Love, may indicate a high probability of truthfulness (e.g., 99.99%), these results are generally not considered definitive proof. Such tests are often treated as interesting but ultimately unreliable forms of evidence within legal and investigative contexts. "He concluded that there was a 99.99% chance that Elden Hoke was telling the truth." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1Jt2fUBDusk
**Religion's Love-Hate Relationship with Music** : Across millennia, religion has exhibited a complex, often contradictory, relationship with music. While music is universally integrated into rituals and prayer, only specific styles or chords have been deemed acceptable at various times. "Religion has always had this love-hate thing with music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g-tBn8dsSqw
**Remastering Process** : Remastering involves retrieving older two-track recordings from archives and enhancing them using modern studio tools to clean up and improve their sound quality. These "new and improved" versions are then re-released into the marketplace. "This is when the old recordings are brought out of the vault, the old two track recordings, and are cleaned up and otherwise enhanced with modern studio tools." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Repetitive Listening by Young Streamers** : Young users, who are the heaviest consumers of streaming services, frequently listen to their favorite songs on repeat, a behavior historically observed in youth (dating back to portable turntables and 45s) but now quantifiable through data analytics to inform the creation of new music designed to encourage similar repetition. "The heaviest users of streaming music services tend to skew young. They also tend to listen to their favorite songs over and over and over and over and over and over and over and over and over again." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Repetitive Listening's Impact on Charts and Payouts** : Repetitive listening by users propels songs up streaming charts and through algorithms, increasing their exposure and leading to more repeat listens, ultimately resulting in higher payouts for creators and greater profits for record labels. "Repetition then pushes the song up the streaming charts and up through the algorithms, so it's exposed to more people who also put it on repeat." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Republican Presidency and Rock Music Resurgence Theory** : Historically, the election of a Republican president, especially a polarizing one, has often correlated with young people responding through loud, angry rock music, as seen during the Nixon (1968), Reagan (1981), George H.W. Bush (1991), and George W. Bush (2001) administrations. However, this trend did not significantly materialize following Donald Trump's election in 2016, with only isolated examples. "usually whenever there's a Republican in the White House and the more polarizing and controversial, the better. Young people have traditionally responded to that, to that political and social circumstance with a lot more loud angry rock." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5Y6nHw5xx88
**Residential School Program** : This was a Canadian government program designed to forcibly assimilate Indigenous children into Western society. The program's horrors were brought to national attention by Gord Downie, particularly through his "Secret Path" project which recounted the story of Chanie Wenjack, a 12-year-old Anishinaabe boy who died attempting to escape one such school. "To recap, this was a government program designed to assimilate Indigenous children into Western society." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dnz_Ycd_q7s
**Resurgence of Concept Albums (Present Day)** : The negative stigma once associated with concept albums has mostly vanished, leading to a contemporary resurgence where many artists, including those influenced by bands that once dismissed the format, are now creating them. "But if you look around today, you'll see that the stigma surrounding making a concept album has pretty much disappeared." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Dm5N6yDRxXQ
**Return of Band Uniforms in the 21st Century** : In recent years, the practice of bands dressing uniformly has experienced a resurgence, possibly fueled by a boredom with the "we don't have an image" attitude prevalent in earlier decades. Current musicians are drawing inspiration from the structured looks of punk and new wave eras, as well as the early Beatles and Kraftwerk, embracing a formal, clean-cut style as a means to differentiate themselves. "But in the last few years, the idea of a band dressing the same has come roaring back. ...So what is this deal with this new love of uniforms? Maybe it's because the current generation of musicians are bored with the, we don't have an image attitude. They're looking back at the punk and new wave errors with admiration. They're even looking back at the early days of the Beatles with their matching narrow jackets. They look at the clean lines of a band like Kraftwerk, which became an art statement in itself. These generation Y bands are emulating that formal, clean cut nicely pulled together look. There was so big 20 and 25 years ago. In other words, it's currently a way to stand out from the crowd, which is what it's all about, right?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mhQ36Y-c9fM
**Return to Singles Focus (Modern Era)** : Thanks to streaming, playlists, and a renewed emphasis on individual songs, the music industry appears to be "heading back to the way things were before about 1965," when singles dominated popular music. "But now, thanks to streaming and playlist and this renewed emphasis on single songs, we seem to be heading back to the way things were before about 1965." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Reunion Fever Due to Declining CD Sales (2000s)** : Beginning in 2000-2001, a significant "reunion fever" swept the music industry, leading many bands from the 60s, 70s, 80s, and 90s to get back together. This trend was primarily driven by the dramatic decline in CD sales, which cratered healthy revenue streams and drastically reduced artists' royalty checks. "Once CD sales started to crater in 2000 and 2001, once healthy revenue streams began to shrink, acts that had seen nice royalty checks every six months for years, decades even, saw those numbers on those checks getting smaller and smaller and smaller." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GYUuTbOvyak
**Revenue Splitting in Licensing Deals** : When an artist licenses a song for third-party use, such as a movie trailer, the revenue generated usually needs to be split. This split occurs between the artist and any other entities, such as music publishers and record companies, who hold ownership stakes in pieces of the song. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Revolution Summer in D.C.** : The "Revolution Summer" was a period in the mid-1980s when hardcore bands in Washington D.C. actively sought to break free from the restrictive dogma that had traditionally limited the hardcore genre. "They became part of what is now remembered as the Revolution Summer when DC hardcore bands tried to break out of the dogma that had limited what hardcore could be." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=v_am7ifh7Pk
**Rhapsody Acquisition and Rebranding** : After its launch, Rhapsody was acquired by Real Networks (formerly Progressive Networks, founded by Rob Glazer). In November 2011, Rhapsody acquired the intellectual property of Napster. By 2016, Rhapsody fully rebranded itself as Napster, operating under that name today. "And then it was bought by Real Networks, which was, of course, the new name for Rob Glazer's progressive networks. Nobody still exists. Except that in November 2011, it bought what was left of Napster, all the intellectual property. And in 2016, Rhapsody completely rebranded itself as Napster, which is how it operates today." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Rhapsody Development** : In 1999, a team created a program for Tune2.com, a customizable internet radio station, and developed a prototype of a "celestial jukebox" called Aladdin. In 2001, Listen.com purchased this technology, transforming Aladdin into Rhapsody, which launched on December 31, 2001, as the first modern on-demand streaming service offering an "all-you-can-eat buffet" of music for a flat monthly fee. "In 2001, all that tech was purchased by a company called Listen.com. And from there, Aladdin was transformed into the first modern on-demand streaming service, which was called Rhapsody." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Rhapsody's Launch and Rebranding** : Rhapsody, the first non-label streaming music service, launched in December 2001 and continues to exist today, having been rebranded as Napster after acquiring the assets of the original file-sharing program. "The first non-label streaming music service was Rhapsody, which went online in December of 2001." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Rhythm Machines with Tape Loops (Late 1950s)** : Following early, complicated rhythm machines, a series of devices emerged that operated using tape loops, featuring recordings of real drums. These machines allowed for manipulation of playback, offering more flexibility than pre-programmed standard beats. "Then came machines that operated with tape loops, featuring recordings of real drums." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OiqdZfxdBP0
**Richard Kraus and the Eight-Track (1963)** : Richard Kraus, working with LearJet, developed a new and mechanically superior cartridge design for private jets, which he named the eight-track. This format featured eight tracks, significantly increasing the capacity of each tape to approximately 80 minutes of music. `"And he came up with a new cartridge design that was mechanically better and featured music on eight tracks."` ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuFvfZJKcpc
**Richard Nixon's Resignation** : President Richard Nixon was identified as a criminal who resigned from office in disgrace, contributing to the era's political turbulence. "where Jornixson was a criminal and resigned in disgrace." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Richard Ramirez (The Night Stalker) Killing Spree (1984-1986)** : Richard Ramirez, known as the Night Stalker, conducted a killing spree in Los Angeles and San Francisco between 1984 and 1986, breaking into homes and killing at least 14 people. His favorite band was AC/DC, an association that stigmatized the band for years. "Richard Romeroz, also known as the Night Stalker, went on a killing spree in Los Angeles in San Francisco between 1984 and 1986. He killed at least 14 people and maybe more during that time." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Richard Ramirez (The Night Stalker) as Subject of Songs** : Songs about Richard Ramirez, known as the Night Stalker, are also common, including Michael Jackson's "Smooth Criminal." This shows the lasting impact of his crimes on popular culture. "There's also no problem finding songs about John Wayne Gacy, Jack The Ripper, the Unibomber and Richard Ramirez, the Night Stalker." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Richmond Spree Murders (2006)** : Over seven days in January 2006, seven people were killed in the Richmond Spree murders by Ricky Gray and his nephew Ray Dandridge, aided by Ray's girlfriend Ashley Baskerville. This series of murders included the brutal killing of musician Brian Harvey and his family. Ricky Gray was executed on January 18, 2017, and Ray Dandridge is serving a life sentence. "This brings us to the Richmond Spree murders in 2006. Over seven days that January, seven people were killed by Ricky Gray and his nephew, Ray Dandridge, who got help from Ray's girlfriend, Ashley Baskerville." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q6dAX6HCjgw
**Rick Rubin as Producer** : Rick Rubin was hired by the Red Hot Chili Peppers to produce their album "Blood Sugar Sex Magik," replacing Michael Beinhorn. Rubin played a key role in shaping the album's recording environment, selecting a historic mansion and converting its basement into a studio. "Second, they switch producers, dropping Michael Bynhorn and hiring Rick Rubin." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TESrEzVHLCc
**Rick Rubin's Role in Audioslave's Formation** : Producer Rick Rubin, a friend to the instrumental members of Rage Against the Machine (Tom Morello, Brad Wilk, Timmy C) after Zach de la Rocha's departure, played a crucial role by connecting them with Chris Cornell. This connection facilitated the initial jams and songwriting sessions that ultimately led to the formation of Audioslave. "producer Rick Rubin, who was a friend, connected them with Chris Cornell." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=t2uXHP9cBdM
**Rickrolling Goes Viral (2007-2011)** : The substitution of the duck with Rick Astley's video first appeared in May 2007, initially disguised as a link to a Grand Theft Auto trailer. It rapidly exploded, with YouTube rickrolling all homepage videos on April Fool's Day 2008. By 2011, it was ubiquitous, used by diverse groups like Anonymous against Scientology, the US Army, Apple support, WikiLeaks, and the Foo Fighters. "But then in May 2007, we saw the first example of substituting the duck with Never Gonna Give You Up by Rick Astley." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bferkEWvJg0
**Rickrolling Origin (4chan)** : The "Rickrolling" meme, where a deceptive link leads to Rick Astley's "Never Gonna Give You Up" video, originated on the 4chan message board in 2006. It initially started with "duck rolling," where a script changed "egg" to "duck" and later involved an image of a duck on wooden wheels, used in bait-and-switch pranks. "This little bait and switch seems to date back to the 4chan message board in 2006." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bferkEWvJg0
**Rickrolling's Cultural Impact and Reach** : Rickrolling evolved into a genuine cultural phenomenon, estimated to have affected at least 18 million people in the US alone by 2008. It has been integrated into various aspects of culture, including the White House, TV shows, movies, video games, Major League Baseball, Zoom meetings during the pandemic, and stealth QR codes. "Rick rolling is not only a viral video movie, it's also a genuine cultural phenomenon." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bferkEWvJg0
**Right Track (Cleveland Recording Studio)** : This Cleveland recording studio is where Nine Inch Nails was conceived. Trent Reznor, working as a janitor there, convinced the owner to let him record demos during unused studio hours, which led to the band's formation. "Things started to get better when he got a job as a janitor at a Cleveland recording studio called Right Track. Trent convinced the owner to let him record demos when the studio wasn't being used. And this is how and where Nine Inch Nails was born." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DKdF8NgF5Bw
**Rikers Island – Jail Warning** : Rikers Island was explicitly mentioned as the destination for Scott Wyland if he were to violate his probation in New York, facing a 30-day sentence. It was highlighted as a place "nobody wants to be sent to," with an anecdote about Sid Vicious being gang-raped there in 1979 after being sent on drug charges. "Any screw ups and Wylandt would be sentenced to 30 days at Reikers Island and believe me, nobody wants to be sent to Reikers Island." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TDwagoyMvck
**Riot Grrrl Movement (1990s)** : The 1990s saw the emergence of the riot grrrl movement, characterized by its "ultrafeminism." This era also featured protests against discrimination faced by individuals with AIDS, and opposition to globalization, consumerism, racism, sexism, foreign wars, and civil wars. "This was the era of riot girls and their ultrafeminism." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0ARYt4sYT90
**Riot Grrrl Movement as a Reaction** : The Riot Grrrl movement was created, at least in part, as a reaction to the male-dominated, testosterone-heavy, and often exclusive world of hardcore, demonstrating how social dynamics within genres can spur new movements. "Some women repulsed by the testosterone of hardcore, and its exclusive women took matters into their own hands and created the entire Riot Girl movement as a response. Yes, that was, at least in part, a reaction to the male-dominated world of hardcore, and look at all the stuff that came out of the Riot Girl movement." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0qigzi1j81U
**Rise of Artist Residencies (21st Century)** : The 21st century saw a significant rise in artist residencies, especially in Las Vegas, offering a less grueling alternative to traditional touring. This trend, pioneered by Elvis Presley historically and revitalized by Celine Dion in 2003, allowed artists to have fans come to them, while cities like Vegas diversified their entertainment offerings beyond gambling. "What if you could get fans to come to you? The Rolling Stones tried this on a limited basis a while back. But then Las Vegas really got into the game." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GYUuTbOvyak
**Rise of DJ Culture and Electronic Music** : The popularity of hip-hop and electronic music exploded towards the end of the 1990s, leading to a decrease in guitar sales and an increase in sales of keyboards, samplers, turntables, and mixers, as a new generation became captivated by DJ culture. "Sales of guitars fell. Sales of keyboards and samplers increased. So did turntables and mixers, as this new generation became besotted with DJ culture." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7JP2tjJibTQ
**Rise of File Sharing and Digital Downloads** : By the mid-2000s, the recording industry began to experience a "total collapse in CD sales" due to the rapid emergence and widespread adoption of file sharing and digital downloads. This shift profoundly impacted record labels' financial stability and their operational strategies. "this was happening as the rest of the recording industry was seeing the beginnings of total collapse in CD sales, thanks to file sharing and emerging digital downloads." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YcdidjWaIqE
**Rise of Indie Labels** : Beyond the three major record labels (Universal, Sony, and Warner), there are hundreds, even thousands, of independent labels that operate autonomously. These "indie labels" and their "indie artists" are considered unaffiliated with the big three, representing the underground, non-mainstream, experimental, daring, outlier, unusual, and non-conformist segments of the music industry. "But beyond those three companies are hundreds, thousands of labels that do their own thing. And because they are unaffiliated with the big three, we consider them to be independent." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VOv5mT1dJd8
**Rise of Indie Labels for Discovery** : In the contemporary music industry, the discovery and nurturing of artists with unusual or experimental styles are increasingly confined to indie labels. While such discoveries can still occur, major labels are less likely to invest in unconventional artists compared to the more open-minded approach historically taken by smaller, independent imprints. "these kinds of discoveries are almost exclusively the domain of indie labels." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7_ZmZxnSOnE
**Rise of Indie Record Labels** : Hardcore music played an essential role as a catalyst for the rise of indie record labels in an industry traditionally dominated by major corporations. Many early hardcore bands formed their own labels due to a lack of interest from commercial radio or major labels, popularizing the idea of a do-it-yourself approach. "it is part of a much larger community of music that's been influential in many different ways throughout the decades, including being an essential catalyst for the rise of indie record labels in a world dominated by majors." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0qigzi1j81U
**Rise of MP3 and University Student Adoption (Late 1990s)** : Towards the end of the 1990s, the MP3 format began gaining significant traction, particularly among university students who leveraged their schools' high-speed internet connections for file sharing. "Then, towards the end of the 1990s, a new thing called the MP3 was seen to be picking up steam. University students glomed onto the runaway using their school's high speed internet connections." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Rise of Music Licensing as a Strategy** : The success of early licensing deals, particularly the Volkswagen "Milky Way" ad, marked the beginning of a completely new strategy for musicians to earn income and gain exposure. This strategy has since "exploded," becoming a common and often crucial source of revenue for artists. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Rise of Music Magazines** : New monthly magazines like *Select* and *The Face* began dedicating extensive coverage to the new generation of homegrown indie bands, while weekly papers like *The NME* and *Melody Maker* also enthusiastically promoted the emerging scene. "There was a new monthly magazine called Select, which began to devote many, many pages to this new generation of homegrown indie bands." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5q5EnZj1FwE
**Rise of One-Hit Wonders in Streaming Era** : The song-centric nature of streaming makes it exceptionally challenging for new artists to forge lasting careers, resulting in a noticeable increase in one-hit wonders over the past decade. "That makes it extremely difficult for any new artist to build a career. The result? A growing number of one-hit wonders in the last decade." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Rise of Personal Computers and CD-ROMs** : Personal computers became widespread in the 1990s, driven by Microsoft's MS-DOS and Windows operating systems. By 1995, Windows installations moved from floppy disks to CD-ROMs, which are data-containing compact discs, leading to more PCs being equipped with CD-ROM drives. "personal computers had started to take off thanks largely to Microsoft, first with its MS-DOS operating system, and then with Windows." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Rise of Photography in Album Art (1956)** : In 1956, photography gained vogue in album artwork, with stylish pictures of artists, particularly jazz performers and early rock and roll figures like Elvis Presley, replacing conceptual illustrations. This marked a shift in visual trends, leading to a mix of illustrations and photographs in subsequent album art. "Photography came into vogue. Photographers were hired to take stylish pictures of artists, starting with jazz performers. But then there was Elvis Presley too." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pwebM3TfSdg
**Rise of Raves in England** : In the late 1980s, driven by the popularity of ecstasy, gigantic and often illegal outdoor parties known as raves began to emerge across England. These events were held in various unconventional locations such as fields, warehouses, and abandoned buildings, becoming a significant part of a new youth revolution centered around dance music and DJ culture. "Raves, gigantic and sometimes illegal outdoor parties started popping up across England, fields, warehouses, abandoned buildings." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Somq2MoRi5Q
**Rise of Teenagers (1950s)** : In the 1950s, millions of post-war children emerged as a new demographic construct known as "teenagers." Their gravitation towards new radio stations playing a mix of blues, country, and R&B and its subsequent annoyance to their parents, made this new music dangerous and attractive. "Elvis came along in the 1950s, just as millions of post-war kids, these new constructs that were now being called teenagers, began gravitating to new radio stations that played music derived from a mix of the blues, country and R&B." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Rise of Turntable and Audio Gear Purchases** : The vinyl resurgence, which began around 2008, has stimulated a significant increase in the sales of turntables and various audio equipment. This trend is driven by music fans across generations, including millennials and Gen Z, who are seeking a more immersive and higher-quality music listening experience, mirroring the considerable investments in stereo equipment seen in the 1970s and 1980s. "This is led to an upswing in the purchase of turntables in all manner of audio gear, as people crave a deeper and better sounding music experience." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2g4QSREzpw
**Rise of the 60s Counterculture** : The 1960s witnessed the growth of a counterculture that actively challenged and cast aside traditional norms. This movement was met with considerable shock, disgust, and anger from conservatives, who frequently blamed contemporary music for its perceived influence on youth behavior and societal shifts. "The 60s counterculture grew, old traditions were pushed aside, much to the shock discussed an anger of conservatives." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=41eKVp8JIhU
**Rise of the Teenager (1950s)** : The concept of the "teenager" as a post-child young adult with leisure time and disposable income emerged in the 1950s, becoming a target demographic for record companies. "by the 1950s, the concept of the teenager, this post-child young adult who had lots of leisure time and a disposable income, began to reach into poverty culture." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Rising Cost of Concert Tickets (2000-Present)** : The average cost of concert tickets has dramatically increased in the 21st century. From approximately $50 in 2000 (equivalent to $83 today), prices rose to $110 by 2019 and currently stand around $180, with tickets for popular artists costing significantly more. "In 2000, the average cost of a ticket was about $50...By 2019, the average price of a concert ticket was 110. And today, it's about 180." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Rising Fortunes of Indie Artists (2004-2006)** : As indie artists and labels gained popularity and influence, a wave of other independent acts saw their fortunes improve. This included bands like Bloc Party, Editors, Bat for Lashes, The Kooks, Babyshambles, and The Libertines, who all experienced increased success between 2004 and 2006. "Other artists saw success too. Block party, editors, bat for lashes, the cookes, baby shambles, libertines, editors. A lot of them saw their fortunes rise in 2004, 2005, and 2006." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=raRCZ0Y5up8
**Rising Tour Insurance Expenses** : The cost of insuring concert tours has increased significantly due to past incidents involving audience harm, overdoses, and tour cancellations (e.g., pandemics), as well as a heightened risk of liability lawsuits. "Sixth, the cost of ensuring a tour has gone up. There have been too many situations where people have died, got hurt or overdosed." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Riskiness of Bowie Bonds** : While Bowie Bonds were an innovative financial instrument, the practice is no longer used today because they proved to be too risky, especially after the music industry was ravaged by piracy and declining physical sales shortly after their issuance. "The practice of Bowie bonds is no longer used because they're too risky." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yq2A_o9-ulM
**Ritual of Physical Music Consumption** : There is a unique ritualistic aspect to consuming physical music, such as taking a CD or vinyl record from a shelf, holding it, and engaging in the process of playing it. This experience is distinct from simply accessing a digital file. "But a digital file is still not the same as taking something down from the shelf, holding it in your hands and going through the ritual of playing it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Road Manager / Tour Manager Responsibilities** : Also known as a tour manager, this individual travels with the artist to ensure that all tour logistics are meticulously organized and executed according to plan. Their duties include ensuring the band's transportation, timely arrival at gigs, and that venues adhere to their contractual commitments. "This is the person who goes on the road with the artist to make sure that everything is organized and goes according to plan." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Road Manager's Role in Rider Enforcement** : The road manager serves as the point person once the band arrives at a venue, responsible for ensuring that the promoter adheres to all the terms outlined in the rider, from logistical details to artist demands. "Once the band arrives, the road manager is the point person. He or she has to make sure that the promoter adheres to all the terms outlined in the rider." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=z2hvUDtBxho
**Roadies and Tech Support** : The critical role of crew members is highlighted in an anecdote where Timmy, described as a "second dad," retrieved fresh pants, and Shayne, the guitar tech, attempted to use gaffer tape to address a wardrobe malfunction during a live show. "While Shayne, who's my guitar tech, pulls out the gaffer tape and proceeds to start gaffer taping my, and I was like, dude, don't, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QIA7V4PxCdM
**Rob Cavallo's Family Music Management History** : Rob Cavallo, the Warner Brothers A&R representative who signed Green Day, came from a family deeply involved in the music industry. His father had managed well-known groups, including The Lovin' Spoonful and Earth Wind & Fire. "his father had managed such groups as the Love and Spoonful and Earth Wind and Fire." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nyfdGYWelic
**Rob Gretton's Naming Inspiration** : Rob Gretton, manager for Joy Division and later New Order, came up with the name "New Order" after seeing the phrase in a newspaper headline about the mass killings under Pol Pot in Cambodia. "Their manager, my name Rob Greten, is the one who came up with it. He found the name in a newspaper headline about the mass killings in Pol Potts, Cambodia. The phrase was new order." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vLZla5h4xKQ
**Rob Johnston** : Technical productions for the podcast are credited to Rob Johnston. "Technical productions by Rob Johnston." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=44dXy0StjIQ
**Rob Johnston (Technical Production)** : Rob Johnston is credited for technical productions. "Technical productions by Rob Johnston." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vLZla5h4xKQ
**Rob Johnston - Global Productions** : Rob Johnston is credited for "Global productions," indicating his role in the production aspects of the audio content. "Global productions by Rob Johnston." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ft8nruhWCas
**Rob Johnston - Musical Productions** : Rob Johnston is specifically credited for "Musical Productions" within the context of the audio content. "Musical Productions for Rob Johnston." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UpWz3X33vSY
**Rob Johnston - Technical Production** : Rob Johnston is credited for the technical production of the broadcast. "Technical production is by Rob Johnston." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dDcD8kg0wEk
**Rob Johnston's Technical Productions** : Rob Johnston is credited for the technical productions of the podcast, described as "saintly." "Technical productions by the saintly Rob Johnston." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g-tBn8dsSqw
**Robert Baden Powell – Founder of The Boy Scouts** : Robert Baden Powell founded The Salford Lads' Club in Manchester in 1904. He is also recognized as the founder of The Boy Scouts. The club gained fame after The Smiths used its facade for their *The Queen is Dead* album cover. "Now this place has stood there since 1904 when it was founded by Robert Baden Powell, the guy who founded The Boy Scouts." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sKHdTSfDd6E
**Robert Kalina – New York Attorney** : Robert Kalina was Scott Wyland's New York attorney, known for representing prominent rap artists such as Biggie Smalls and Run DMC. He successfully negotiated continuances for Wyland's court dates due to his client's repeated rehab stints in California. "Wylandt's New York attorney, Robert Kalina, who worked with rap artists like Biggie Smalls and run DMC." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TDwagoyMvck
**Robert Lang Studios Historical Significance** : Robert Lang Studios, situated north of Seattle, holds a unique place in rock history. It was a favorite recording location for Alice in Chains, the studio where the very first Foo Fighters record was made, and also where Nirvana recorded their final completed song, "You Know Your Right." "The Fuz used Robert Lang Studios, a place north of the city that was a favorite spot for Alice and Chains. It's where the very first Fufite's record was made. And it's also the studio where Nirvana recorded, you know your right. Their last ever completed song." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Mh-qg1izM98
**Robert Plant Paid a Radio Station to Stop Playing "Stairway to Heaven"** : Robert Plant was so sick of hearing "Stairway to Heaven" that he donated to a public radio station in Oregon that was running a pledge drive offering never to play the song again in exchange for contributions. Plant heard the station while driving down the coast and called in a pledge. "He called up and made a pledge." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Robert Smith of The Cure – Replies to All Emails in All Caps, Only on Thursdays** : Robert Smith of The Cure writes all email replies in all capitals and only reads and responds to emails on Thursdays. "He only reads and replies to emails on Thursdays." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**Rock Albums in the Mid-1960s** : Prior to 1965, rock albums were scarce as they were considered too expensive for teenagers, who preferred 45 RPM singles. The trend began to change with artists like The Beatles, Bob Dylan, The Rolling Stones, and The Beach Boys, who started releasing full albums with multiple songs, necessitating artwork that appealed to rock fans. "1965 seems to be a dividing line between the era of the seven-inch single and the era of album rock. If a rock band was going to issue albums, then they needed to come up with artwork that appealed to rock fans." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pwebM3TfSdg
**Rock Concerts as World-Changing Events** : The concept of rock concerts having the power to change the world is explored, with high-profile examples like Woodstock, Altamont, and Live Aid cited for their lasting political, social, and artistic implications. The discussion then focuses on "lower profile gigs" that also had subtle but long-term musical effects. "can something like a rock concert change the world? Well, I think the answer might be yes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qC0Ee4-dpnI
**Rock Hudson's Death (October 3, 1985) – First Famous AIDS Fatality** : Actor Rock Hudson passed away on October 3, 1985, becoming the first famous public figure to die from AIDS. His death brought significant, albeit delayed, public attention to the escalating health crisis. "Actor Rock Hudson passed away on October 3, 1985." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Somq2MoRi5Q
**Rock Music Book Library Significance** : A personal collection of over a thousand, possibly closer to two thousand, books on rock music is highlighted as a precious and increasingly important source of information, especially in the age of Wikipedia where books still offer invaluable depth. "I have well over a thousand books on rock music at home. Actually, maybe closer to 2000. It's very precious stuff." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yl2poVWguu0
**Rock Music Evolution since 1950s** : Rock music, which began in the early 1950s as a fresh take on the 12-bar blues, has continuously evolved, segmenting and mutating with increasing speed, leading to an exponential proliferation of new genres, especially in the 2010s. "rock has been evolving since the 1950s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tdTs-4Irv8c
**Rock Music Longevity** : Rock music is "never, ever going to go away," despite periodic downturns or claims of its demise. This resilience is attributed to a fundamental human need to create music, the increasing volume of music being made and consumed in diverse forms, and record attendance at live shows. "rock music, despite how bad it may look at times, is never, ever going to go away. Humans have a hardwired need to make music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Rock Music Peak and Decline (2005-2006)** : According to the rock vs. pop cycle theory, rock music peaked in 2005 and continued its momentum for about a year, but by the end of 2006, it was clearly in a slow decline. However, the period of 2006-2007 also saw a slight opening up of musical variety within rock. "2005, the last year rock music peaked as far as our theory of rock versus pop cycles are concerned. That was Green Day's American Idiot sold 3.4 million copies." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Rock Music Repeating Itself** : Rock music is observed to be "starting to repeat itself," a trend also seen in pop and hip hop, due to the limited number of pleasing combinations of musical notes and the exhaustive exploration of riffs and melodies over decades. "Like I said earlier, Rock is starting to repeat itself." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wYD_dIvYY5o
**Rock Music Stratification** : From the days of Elvis, rock music began to cleave, separate, and stratify into "a million different subtypes" over the decades, leading to numerous related subcultures. "Over the decades, rock music is cleaved and separated and stratified into a million different subtypes" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**Rock Music's Satanic Association** : Rock music, in certain parts of the world, continues to be regarded as a product of the "wicked one," the "Dark Angel," or the "father of lies and deceit." "Now, rock music was, and in some corners of the planet still is, considered to be the product of the wicked one, the Dark Angel, the father of lies and deceit." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**Rock Star (Reality TV Series)** : This reality television series was created to find a new lead singer for InXS after Michael Hutchence's death, ultimately resulting in the selection of Canadian singer JD Fortune. "signing on to do a reality TV series called Rockstar that ultimately resulted in JD Fortune, a Canadian singer, getting the gig." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Rock Star Murders Compared to Hip Hop** : While rock star murders are tragic, they are far less common than in the world of hip hop. Figures like Tupac, Biggie Smalls (Big E), Jam Master Jay, Nipsey Hussle, and Pop Smoke, along with dozens of others, represent a much higher incidence of murder in the hip hop community. "There haven't been that many rock musician murders. It's far, far worse in the world of hip hop. DuPock, Big E, Jam Master J, Nipsy Hussle, Pop Smoke, and dozens of others." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q6dAX6HCjgw
**Rock and Fashion Symbiotic Relationship** : Rock music and fashion have shared a symbiotic relationship since their inception, with fashion serving as a crucial component of rock's identity as both show business and theater. The visual aspect is deemed highly important, as it suggests that one must "look good if you're going to sound good." "Rock and fashion have had a symbiotic relationship from the very beginning. After all, rock is also equal part show business and theater, so it's important to look good if you're going to sound good." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mhQ36Y-c9fM
**Rock and Hip Hop Hybrid Genres since 1980s** : Rock and hip hop, two major musical genres, have been merging to create new hybrid genres since at least the 1980s, demonstrating continuous cross-pollination and innovation. "While rock is still massively popular around the world, hip hop is the musical genre, driving culture in many countries and territories. Yes, there's competition, but these two major types of music often come together to create new and interesting hybrid genres. This has been happening since at least the 1980s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tdTs-4Irv8c
**Rock and Roll Dances Banned in Connecticut** : In response to the moral panic surrounding rock and roll, dances associated with the genre were explicitly banned in Connecticut. This local prohibition demonstrates the tangible impact of public and official disapproval on the new musical movement. "Dances were banned in Connecticut." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Rock and Roll Hall of Fame** : David Bowie is an inductee into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame, a testament to his enduring impact and contributions to music. "He's in the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yq2A_o9-ulM
**Rock and Roll as "Devil Dancing"** : In the mid-1950s, amidst the backlash against rock and roll, some critics claimed it was "proof of a revival of devil dancing, the sort of thing done in black magic rituals." This extreme condemnation highlights the deep-seated fears and religious anxieties that rock music provoked. "Others said that rock was proof of a revival of devil dancing, the sort of thing done in black magic rituals." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Rock and Roll's "Fad" Declaration (Early 1960s)** : During rock's decline in the early 1960s, many record executives "declared that Rock had been nothing more than a fad," advocating for a return to "real music" with artists like Frank Sinatra. "Many record executives declared that Rock had been nothing more than a fad." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MK7L0eCpDHc
**Rock vs. Pop Cycle Theory** : A theory posits that rock and pop music are in a cyclical "death struggle" that repeats every 13 years. Rock music rises in popularity and creativity, peaks, then declines, while pop music follows an inverse pattern, dropping as rock ascends and rising as rock falls. "That theory contends that rock and pop music are locked in a death struggle that plays itself out like clockwork every 13 years." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Rock vs. Pop Cycle Timeline (1951-2010)** : The rock versus pop cycle theory suggests four complete cycles have occurred since 1951. In 2010, the music world was in its fifth cycle, which began in the spring of 2003, with rock peaking in 2005 and hitting bottom in the preceding couple of years. "the rock versus pop cycle has played itself out through four complete cycles since 1951. If you want to know why, you don't have to go back and review chapter 14." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Rock's Decline in Late 1990s** : At the end of the 1990s, rock music was perceived to be in a state of "doldrums," overshadowed by the dominance of pop, electronic, and hip-hop. Rock fans anxiously hoped for a new leader or movement to revitalize the genre. "Rock itself had fallen into the doldrums and every fan was hoping and praying that someone or something would come along to inject new life into the genre." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7dXtgTkdv9w
**Rock's Overall Consumption in the 2010s** : Despite hip hop being the dominant cultural force in many territories, rock was still performing well in the 2010s in terms of overall consumption, which included records sold, songs streamed, and videos watched. Furthermore, rock remained the most consumed genre by concert tickets sold in countries like Canada, the US, and the UK. "rock was still doing well in terms of overall consumption." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HSvgRyGiOpE
**Rock's Resurrection 2001-2004** : Between 2001 and 2004, rock music experienced a robust resurrection, marked by a surge of successful records from numerous bands, with many achieving multi-platinum, gold, or platinum status across various international markets. This period signaled a strong comeback for the genre. "By the end of 2003, Rock's Resurrection was well underway with plenty of big records." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7dXtgTkdv9w
**Rock, Drugs, and Dopamine – A Neurological Connection** : The symbiotic relationship between rock music and drug use has a neurological explanation: dopamine, the "feel-good hormone," is released by sex, drugs, and music alike, suggesting a deep biological connection between all three experiences. "The 'feel-good hormone' dopamine is activated by sex, drugs, and music, suggesting a deep biological connection." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RKvFJMkZdBE
**Rockabye Baby Lullaby Series** : Rockabye Baby is a series of CDs that transform popular rock songs from bands like Nine Inch Nails, Coldplay, The Cure, Metallica, Radiohead, and Tool into lullaby renditions. These adaptations reduce the intensity of the original tracks, making them suitable for infants and "baby-fying" cool rock songs. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHN6db7m3DM
**Rockets Redgler's Death and Film Appearance – 2001** : Rockets Redgler, a drug dealer and aspiring actor whom some believe was Nancy Spungen's actual murderer, died of liver failure and hepatitis C in 2001. He appeared as a cab driver in the 1985 Madonna film "Desperately Seeking Susan." "Rockets Red Glare, the drug dealer and wannabe actor that some say was Nancy's actual murderer, died of liver failure and hepatitis C in 2001." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OcylgiXkDP8
**Rod's Hickory Pit Restaurant** : This restaurant was where Billy Joe Armstrong's mother worked as a waitress after his father's death. It later served as the venue for Sweet Children's first public performance, which was attended by approximately 30 people. "His father died of cancer when he was 10. He was just one of five kids being raised by a mother who could only find work as a waitress at a place called Rod's Hickory Pit." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nyfdGYWelic
**Rodin's "Orpheus and Eurydice" on Album Cover** : The cover artwork for Arcade Fire's "Reflektor" album prominently features a photograph of Rodin's renowned sculpture of Orpheus and Eurydice. This artistic choice directly ties into the album's thematic underpinnings, particularly its connection to the "Black Orpheus" film and themes of myth and reflection. "When reflector finally came out, the cover artwork featured a picture of Rodin's famous sculpture of Orpheus and Eurides." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ir2FfLQJNtY
**Rodney Marsh – Footballer** : Rodney Marsh was a prominent football star for Manchester City during the early 1970s. A portrait of him was prominently displayed above the fireplace on the cover of Oasis's "Definitely Maybe" album, a deliberate inclusion reflecting the fandom of Liam Gallagher, Noel Gallagher, and Paul "Guigsy" McGuigan for the team. "They put a portrait of Rodney Marsh, a big star for the team in the early 1970s above the fireplace." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Roger Daltrey's Flat Stomach – Caused by Talcum Powder Scarring from 1948 Surgery** : When Roger Daltrey was four years old, he swallowed a two-inch nail. Surgeons removed it by cutting him open; at the time (around 1948), surgical gloves were dusted with talcum powder, which was later discovered to cause significant scar tissue. Those scars are why Daltrey's stomach has remained so flat. "If you look up pictures of a shirtless Roger Daltrey, you'll see the scars." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Role of Music Managers** : Managers are crucial figures who handle all the business aspects of an artist's career, allowing musicians to focus on making music. Their responsibilities include booking gigs, marketing, promotions, publicity, managing support staff and road crews, collecting money, paying bills, and overseeing legal and accounting infrastructure. Beyond business, they act as advisors, sounding boards, fixers, and even informal psychologists or physicians, needing to be informed on trends, connected within the industry, and adept at navigating record companies and contracts. This is a 24/7 job where they can "make or break a career," typically earning around 15% commission. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PzyvXmDKFUc
**Role of Music Supervisor** : For projects involving TV, movies, or video games, a music supervisor is the key individual responsible for sourcing and selecting appropriate music. This role is crucial in identifying and securing the right tracks for a given project. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Role of Record Labels (Traditional Model)** : Record labels functioned as filters, signing acts they believed would be profitable, then nurturing, marketing, and distributing their music globally, thereby generating revenue. They controlled distribution, leading to a "controlled supply of new music." "Their job was to sort and filter through all the new music being made, sign the acts that they thought could help make them a buck, and then nurture and market and distribute that music to the rest of the planet." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Role of a Record Producer** : A record producer's role is complex, requiring both hands-on and hands-off approaches. It involves coaching, teaching, encouraging, being tough, pampering, manipulating, tolerating, communicating, and articulating to extract the best performance from a band and maximize the available music, all while staying on time and budget. "Well, let's just say it's a tough gig because you have to be both hands on and hands off at the same time. You have to coach, teach, encourage, get mean, pamper, manipulate, tolerate, communicate, articulate whatever it takes to coax the best performance out of a band and the best out of the available music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkSKxm_U9Yc
**Ronald Reagan – "Evil Empire" Speech** : The title of Rage Against the Machine's 1996 album, "Evil Empire," was directly sourced from a speech delivered by U.S. President Ronald Reagan. In this speech, Reagan famously used the phrase to characterize the Soviet Union, highlighting the band's politically charged appropriation of the term. "It comes from a Ronald Reagan speech where he called the Soviet Union just that the evil empire." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Ronald Reagan's Impact on Hardcore** : Ronald Reagan's presidency, characterized by deeply conservative and hawkish Republican policies, was a significant catalyst for the growth of independent music in North America. His policies were unpopular with many young people, leading to the manifestation of hardcore punk as an anti-establishment and anti-commercial response. "If we're going to be honest in telling this story, we have to give a substantial amount of credit to Ronald Reagan. You heard me. Reagan was a deeply conservative and hawkish Republican president and his policies did not sit well with a lot of young people." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VOv5mT1dJd8
**Ronald Reagan's Political Context** : The presidency of Ronald Reagan is highlighted as the political backdrop against which the hardcore punk genre emerged, serving as a direct response to his conservative policies. "a genre that was created in response to Ronald Reagan." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8ynrwaUi-sA
**Ronald Reagan's Presidency and Opposition (1980s)** : Ronald Reagan's presidency, which began in the US after a dysfunctional 1970s, saw him promise to "make America great again" with conservative policies. This was met with enthusiasm by many voters but generated strong opposition from young people on the left. This discontent was a significant factor in the development of hardcore punk, and his policies inspired countless protest songs. "When Republican Ronald Reagan promised to make America great again with his no nonsense conservative policies, it was just what the electorate wanted." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0ARYt4sYT90
**Ronnie Piltech's Murders (2002)** : In the fall of 2002, Ronnie Piltech of Medford, New Jersey, who suffered from paranoid schizophrenia and had stopped his medication, brutally murdered his mother with a 9-kilogram dumbbell and stabbed 11-year-old Gregory Ketnellson to death. Piltech told the court he honestly believed Metallica's song "Ronnie" was directed specifically at him. "In the fall of 2002, Ronnie had stopped taking his meds and was drinking very, very heavily, making his behavior even more erratic." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Royal York Hotel - Meeting William Shatner** : The Royal York Hotel in downtown Toronto was the location where an interviewer was instructed to meet William Shatner for the first time, using a specific code name. The meeting led to discussions about Shatner's "How Time Flies" stage production. "go to the Royal York Hotel in downtown Toronto and ask for a person by this code name." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hjProJd_JrM
**Rude Boy Gangs and Sound System Rivalry** : Rude Boy gangs were often hired by sound system operators who ran dances. Their role was to "crash the events set up by their rivals," highlighting the intense competition and often violent rivalry within the early Jamaican music scene. "Guys who ran dances using their big sound systems often hired Rude Boy gangs to crash the events set up by their rivals." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zNIVqKqAlnk
**Rude Boy Geography** : Rude Boys emerged from specific Jamaican ghettos, including "Kingston, Ocho Rios and Montego Bay." These urban centers became hotbeds for this subculture. "Rude Boys emerged from the ghettos of Kingston, Ocho Rios and Montego Bay." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zNIVqKqAlnk
**Rules for Naming Elements after People** : The rules for naming a new element state that its name must originate from a mythological concept, a mineral, a place, a country, a property, or a scientist. Musicians are explicitly not permitted under these rules. "the rules regarding the naming of a new element say that its name must come from a mythological concept, a mineral, a place, a country, property, or scientist. Musician does not count." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VMPLplhDk8I
**Russian Actors Using Britney Spears Instagram for Cyberattacks** : A group of Russian actors known as Turla leveraged Britney Spears' official Instagram account for cyber operations. They used comments posted to her account to gain access to servers, which subsequently allowed them to offload stolen data to and from infected computers. "A group of Russian actors known as Turla used comments posted to Britney Spears official Instagram account to gain access to servers which then allowed them to offload stolen data to and from infected computers." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**Ryerson Film Crew** : During a Thorneley tour, a film crew consisting of students from Ryerson University, led by Michael Fano (who later became president of Apple Canada), accompanied the band, traveling in an RV and filming performances on stage. "We had a film crew with us. I was a group of students from Ryerson. Two sweet kids, all of them. Mike, Michael Fano, he's like, he's like president of Apple Canada now." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QIA7V4PxCdM
**SNK "The Nerds" Sketch** : The phrase "nerd rock" as uttered by Tom Davis after Elvis Costello's SNL performance led to the creation of "The Nerds," a recurring sketch on Saturday Night Live. The sketches featured characters played by Gilder Radner, Jane Curtain, and Bill Murray, solidifying the cultural recognition of "nerd" archetypes. "And that led to the creation of the nerds, a reoccurring set of sketches featuring characters played by Gilder Radner, Jane Curtain and Bill Murray." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=h6snk0m4200
**STP Oil Treatment Can and Richard Petty** : The initials "STP" for Stone Temple Pilots originated from an old STP oil treatment can that Scott Weiland kept as a "shrine" at his home. This was a nostalgic reference to a childhood STP sticker on his bicycle and his admiration for NASCAR driver Richard Petty, who was sponsored by STP. "He used to pretend that he was NASCAR driver Richard Petty, who had STP as a sponsor." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r6vVw66-gmI
**SX2 Adapter for Club Mixing** : In the 1980s, a Boston nightclub DJ designed the SX2, a large frisbee-like adapter that fit over the entire turntable platter. It had a central space for the 7-inch single and ample room around the circumference, making it easier for DJs to mix 45s, scratch, and slipcue in clubs. "In the 1980s, we had something called the SX2, designed by a Boston nightclub DJ that made it easier to mix 45s while spinning in clubs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**Sales Awards Based on Dollar Sales (1958-1976)** : In 1958, the RIAA initially defined a gold record as any single or album that achieved more than a million dollars in sales, specifically in the United States. These awards were based on dollar sales, not copies, and remained in effect until 1976. "In 1958, the Recording Industry Association of America decided to formalize everything. They announced that every single or album that achieved more than a million dollars in sales, not copies, sales, would be a gold record." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gP3oQeYEvso
**Sam Phillips' "White Boy" Quote** : Record producer Sam Phillips famously stated his desire to find a white male vocalist who could sing with the soul of a black man, believing it would make him a million dollars. "The record producer Sam Phillips famously said, if I could find a white boy who could sing like a black man, I'd make a million dollars." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PdCAjFW04Oc
**Sam Phillips' Discovery of Elvis Presley** : Sam Phillips discovered Elvis Presley after he recorded the acetate "My Happiness" as a birthday gift for his mother, which ultimately led Phillips to recognize Presley's talent. "This acetate was recorded for his mum as a birthday present, which led to his discovery by Sam Phillips." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IIJySSo276k
**Sam Smith's Oscar and Golden Globe Win (2015)** : Sam Smith's song "Writing's on the Wall," chosen as the theme for the James Bond movie "Spectre" over Radiohead's submission, achieved significant critical acclaim, winning both an Academy Award and a Golden Globe for Best Original Song. "Sam Smith song called The Riddings on the Wall, which, by the way, ended up with both an Academy Award and a Golden Globe for Best Original Song." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vt6DVj7S6nk
**Sampling Legal Costs Evolution** : The cost of clearing music samples dramatically increased after "Paul's Boutique" was released, due to a series of major lawsuits involving sampling. The $250,000 the Beastie Boys paid to clear 105 samples for "Paul's Boutique" was considered reasonable for its time, but today, a similar album would cost millions of dollars in sample clearances, making such complex, sample-heavy albums nearly impossible to produce. "Paul Sputie came out in the time before a bunch of big lawsuits involving sampling pushed the cost of clearing samples way, way up." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cYwk-B3qwwk
**Sampling Rate Theory (1928, 1949)** : The theory that a musical signal sampled at 44,100 times per second is indistinguishable to the human ear and brain from a smooth analog source was first proposed in a paper in 1928 and experimentally proven in 1949. This underpins the fidelity of compact disc audio. "This sampling rate theory was first discussed in a paper printed in 1928 and then proven experimentally in 1949." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D8GU8ZpSgY8
**Sampling Technology Analogy for AI Regulation (N/A)** : The current legal and ethical challenges posed by AI in music are compared to the early days of sampling technology in the 1980s and 90s. Initially, unauthorized sampling led to disputes over credit and compensation, and fears of music's demise. This eventually resulted in new laws and creative integration of sampling, suggesting a similar path for AI. "Think back to the 80s and early 90s when sampling technology was taking off." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Sampling as a Foundational Change** : Sampling, defined as surgically excising and reusing audio from one source as a building block for new compositions, was a critical foundational change enabling the rise of alt-rock in the 1990s. While rudimentary experimentation with deconstructive audio concepts dates back to the 1950s (Jamaican DJs in the 60s, hip-hop in the 70s), electronic, computer-controlled sampling became prevalent in the 1980s, allowing old sounds to be made fresh again. "Sampling is when you surgically excise a piece of audio from one source and use it as a building block for a brand new composition." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r8AazBVoS7g
**San Diego's Music Scene in 1994** : In 1994, San Diego was not considered the central hub for alternative music; aspiring artists often had to relocate to Los Angeles, as Scott Wyland did with Stone Temple Pilots, to achieve significant success. "San Diego wasn't exactly the epicenter of alternative music in 1994. If you wanted to make it big, you'd have to be like Scott Wyland and Lee for Los Angeles." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eljIIqCu5gU
**San Francisco Gay Lesbian Freedom Band (1978)** : The San Francisco Gay Lesbian Freedom Band was founded in 1978, billing itself as the "first openly gay musical organization in the world." This establishment marked a significant step for LGBTQ visibility and organization within the music community. "In 1978, Mark the founding of the San Francisco Gay Lesbian Freedom Band, which billed itself as the first openly gay musical organization in the world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hswtuenFwvM
**San Francisco's Radical History** : San Francisco has historically been a hub for "the left leaning and the radical," making it a fitting location for punk rock to take root and develop its unique, eclectic style that blended artsy, angry, hippie, drug culture, and left-wing politics. "It has long been a place for the left leaning in the radical. In fact, you can make an argument that San Francisco punk was the most eclectic of all the punk that we saw back then." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Sandra Bullock – Jesse James' Ex-Wife** : Actress Sandra Bullock was formerly married to Jesse James, who also had a brief marriage to Jeanine Lindemulder, the model who appeared on the cover of Blink-182's "Enema of the State" album. "Jesse James, the West Coast chopper's TV guy and one time husband of actor Sandra Bullock." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Sandy Hook False Flag Theory** : The claim that the Sandy Hook shooting was a "false flag event" is referenced as a deeply controversial conspiracy theory. "or that Sandy Hook was a false flag event" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pmKjZzypzvg
**Santana High School Shooting (2001)** : On March 5, 2001, 15-year-old Charles Andrew Williams, described as unpopular and bullied, carried out a shooting at Santana High School in Santee, California. He killed two students and wounded 13 others, influenced by Linkin Park songs "Papercut," "One Step Closer," and "In The End," even quoting "In the End" in a note to his father. "In 915 in the morning of March 5, 2001, a 15-year-old unpopular and uncool kid named Charles Andrew Williams went into the boys' bathroom at Santana High School in Santé, California." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Sarajevo Cafe and World War I Trigger (April 1914)** : In April 1914, a newspaper clipping in a letter arrived at the Salat Number Runa cafe in Sarajevo, detailing Archduke Franz Ferdinand's upcoming visit on June 28th. This information prompted a terrorist group to plan his assassination, which Gavrilo Princip carried out, triggering World War I and fundamentally shaping the 20th century. "That assassination was the trigger for World War I and everything in the 20th century that followed." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qC0Ee4-dpnI
**Sassy Magazine's Influence** : The Riot Grrrl movement found some inspiration in "Sassy" magazine, a publication directed at teenage girls that, despite its target audience, "dealt with some pretty heavy subjects," contributing to the feminist discourse of the time. It provided a mainstream-accessible platform for critical thought. "One magazine was called Sassy, which, although directed at teenage girls, dealt with some pretty heavy subjects." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qss5kC2FUx0
**Satanic Panic (1980s-1990s)** : In the 1980s and 1990s, a "satanic panic" took hold among certain segments of traditional religious society. They believed that Satan's influence was pervasive in music, seeing it as Lucifer's cunning method to capture the souls of vulnerable young people, and insisted that evil and demonic forces needed to be exposed and eradicated from rock music. "Then, in the 1980s and 90s, a portion of society lost its collective mind. To these people, Satan was everywhere in music. His work and influence needed to be exposed and exercised from culture." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=41eKVp8JIhU
**Satanic Pseudonyms** : Satan is known by various pseudonyms, including Lucifer, Beelzebub, Abaddon, Appollyon, Apollion, and the "Prince of Darkness," all of which have historically been used to instill fear in people. "Satan, working under pseudonyms such as Lucifer, Biel's Abub, Abaddon, Appelon, Apollion, and the ever-popular Prince of Darkness, has always had the ability to freak people out." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**Saturday Night Live Guest Host (1980)** : Terry Gar served as the guest host for Saturday Night Live on January 26, 1980, the night The B-52s' performance of "Rock Lobster" deeply affected an 11-year-old Dave Grohl. "Terry Gar was the guest host that night." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8ynrwaUi-sA
**Scalper Bots and Early Exploitation (Early 2000s)** : Scalper bots are software programs designed to purchase thousands of concert tickets exponentially faster than humans, exploiting computerized ticketing systems' ability to process many transactions per minute. Ken Losen, founder of Wiseguy, identified this opportunity in the early 2000s, hiring a 17-year-old Bulgarian programmer to develop such software. These early bots bypassed security measures like CAPTCHA challenges by discovering that Ticketmaster used a limited number of character combinations (around 30,000). Wiseguy's team copied these as JPEG files into a database, enabling their bots to break through codes using brute force. Ticketmaster did not update these CAPTCHA codes for years, allowing bots to acquire millions of tickets. "Louson and others discovered the ticket master only used about 30,000 combinations." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sPj3uxmz8H4
**Scalping Methods: "Diggers" and "ICE"** : Early ticket scalpers, often called "sidewalk men," used specific methods to obtain tickets. They would hire individuals, known as "diggers," to stand in line for them. Another tactic was "ICE," which involved bribing box office personnel for a share of the ticket proceeds. "Some would hire people to stand on the sidewalk in line for them, and these folks were called diggers." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D8GU8ZpSgY8
**Scandinavian Metal Scene Controversy** : Certain "fun groups out of Scandinavia" within metal scenes are associated with members and followers who "kill each other and burned down churches," implying a notorious, violent aspect to some extreme metal subgenres. "You know the ones whose members and followers kill each other in burned down churches?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**Scarcity of 21st Century Arena-Filling Rock Bands** : A challenge posed is to list rock bands that emerged since 2000 capable of drawing tens of thousands of fans to a single gig. The observation is that most acts still filling arenas or stadiums first appeared in the 1990s, 1980s, 1970s, or even 1960s, indicating a difficulty in creating new superstars in the current century. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**School Band Programs and Gender Stereotypes** : In the early 1900s, American schools began offering band programs, partly driven by instrument manufacturers seeking new customers after the public shifted away from marching bands. While young women were encouraged to join, they were largely restricted to "feminine instruments" like the marimba, with most others deemed unsuitable. "Young women were encouraged to join band programs, but only if they played the so-called feminine instruments." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9GHpC0kCgHY
**School Shooting Precedent (1991)** : The suicide of Jeremy Wade Dell in front of his classmates on January 8, 1991, predated the Columbine High School massacre by seven years and was considered "absolutely insane" for a student to bring a gun to school to kill himself. "It was seven years before Columbine, and the idea of a kid bringing a gun to school to kill himself was absolutely insane." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cM3-tINKdvo
**School of Rock (Movie)** : The movie "School of Rock" is presented as an example of the "ecumenical musical aesthetic" prevalent in modern times. This reflects a broad appreciation for diverse musical genres and artists, illustrating the contemporary shift away from rigid genre-based fandom. "Just think of the ecumenical musical aesthetic of movies like School of Rock and you get an idea of what we're dealing with here." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ft8nruhWCas
**School of Rock - "Set You Free" Feature** : The Black Keys' song "Set You Free" was featured in the popular movie *School of Rock*, starring Jack Black. "If you ever saw the movie School of Rock with Jack Black, you might recognize this." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PmwfC4HR3_I
**Science Fiction and Fantasy Hall of Fame** : Reflecting his diverse artistic interests and personas, David Bowie is also inducted into the Science Fiction and Fantasy Hall of Fame. "He's in the science fiction and fantasy Hall of Fame." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yq2A_o9-ulM
**Scientific Evidence for Brain Benefits of Drumming** : A scientific publication from June 2020, titled "Drum Training Induces Long-Term Plasticity in the Sarah Bellum and Connected Cortical Tissues," and authored by thirteen researchers, concluded that playing the drums is beneficial for brain health. "The scientific publication, which was written by thirteen researchers, is entitled Drum Training Induces Long-Term Plasticity in the Sarah Bellum and Connected Cortical Tissues." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HoBx6s3JmVQ
**Scotland's Music Ban in Bars Due to COVID-19** : Scotland implemented a ban on playing music in bars and pubs, specifically to mitigate the spread of COVID-19.
