Its raw materials, primarily iron bars, were imported from Sweden, an industry that was initiated by Louis de Geer, a French slave trader. Steel proved to be one of the best metals for the new mechanized flattening processes. "the manufacturer of which had started in England by about 1614. The best source of raw materials came from iron bars imported from Sweden, an industry that was kick-started by a slave trader from France named Louis de Geer." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**Stephen Hawking – Anton Corbijn Subject** : Renowned theoretical physicist Stephen Hawking was among the notable individuals photographed by Anton Corbijn, the celebrated Dutch photographer known for his iconic work with numerous influential music artists. This collaboration highlights Corbijn's diverse artistic reach beyond the music industry. "The photographer was Anton Corbin... and get this physicist, Stephen Hawking." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Stereo/Mono 7-inch Singles** : In the 1960s, as stereo recordings grew popular, some 7-inch singles featured the same song on both sides: a stereo version on the A-side and a mono version on the B-side. This dual-format release catered to listeners with different record player capabilities and allowed for servicing both mono AM and stereo FM radio stations with a single physical product. "The A song was the stereo recording and the B side was the mono version for those who had yet to upgrade their record players. This also served the purpose of servicing both mono AM radio stations and stereo FM stations with the same piece of plastic." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**Stereophonic (Stereo) Sound (Post-1958)** : Stereophonic sound, introduced after 1958, utilizes two speakers with slightly different audio material to create an effect of depth and separation, aiming for realism as if performers are present. "One came stereophonic sound." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Stereotypes Against Female Bassists (1970s)** : In the 1970s, it was considered shocking and "unladylike" for a woman to play bass, with the expectation that girls would primarily sing or perhaps strum an acoustic guitar. Tina Weymouth's role as a bassist with Talking Heads significantly challenged these preconceptions. "But in 1976 and 1977, no way. It was somehow unladylike. It was almost as bad as a woman playing the drums." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kbFfT0pKchI
**Stereotypical Rock Star Traits** : The traditional portrayal of a rock star often includes traits like laziness, self-centeredness, and a disgusting nature, suggesting their brains are capable of understanding only "sex, drugs, and rock and roll." "The stereotypical rock star is very similar to what we might think of when we're asked to describe a very backward caveman." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QfiRRtsZoYU
**Steve Albini - Legendary Producer** : Steve Albini is described as a legendary producer known for his work on albums by prominent bands such as Nirvana, Pixies, The Breeders, Bush, and Godspeed You! Black Emperor, and recorded Mono's *Hymn to the Immortal Wind*. "This is from a 2009 album entitled Him to the Immortal Wind, which is a big fan favorite and recorded by the legendary Steve Elbeni, who did all those albums with Nirvana and the Pixies and the Breeders and Bush." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9-v4qnOO8wY
**Steve Jobs and iTunes' Individual Song Sales Policy** : Steve Jobs, leveraging the record labels' desperation, controversially insisted that all songs on iTunes be sold individually, ending the traditional practice of forcing consumers to purchase entire albums to get a single track. "He demanded that all songs be made available individually." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Steve Jobs' Digital Music Proposition to Labels** : Steve Jobs approached record labels, arguing that piracy was devastating their business and that they needed to transition to digital. He presented iTunes as his solution, and the labels, having struggled to find their own effective digital strategy, had little choice but to agree. "Steve Jobs went to all the record labels and basically said, look, piracy is killing you. You have to go digital. You can't do it right, so here is my solution. And the labels really had no choice but to agree." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Steve Lillywhite (Producer) - Working Philosophy** : Steve Lillywhite, a renowned British producer who has worked with dozens of major artists, maintains a philosophy of never listening to his past projects. He intentionally avoids revisiting old work, stating that he doesn't "want to be distracted by the past." "Of all the albums he's worked on, has he ever gone back and wished he did something different? No, he never listens to past projects because he doesn't want to be distracted by the past." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hjProJd_JrM
**Steve Lillywhite (Producer) - Working Style** : Steve Lillywhite is characterized by his multifaceted and consistently cheerful approach in the studio. He acts as a "coach, a teacher, a lion tamer, a psychologist, a cheerleader, a sounding board," always enthusiastic, and never pushy, demonstrating a supportive yet directive presence. "He was a coach, a teacher, a lion tamer, a psychologist, a cheerleader, a sounding board, and he was always, always cheerful, never pushy and always super enthusiastic." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hjProJd_JrM
**Stomach Problems Theory** : Similar to Michael Hutchence's alleged brain tumor theory, Kurt Cobain reportedly suffered from stomach problems, which some suggested may have contributed to his state. "just like Kurt Cobain and his stomach problems, at least one friend maintained that Michael said he, quote, would do a curt before the cancer got to him." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**Stone Temple Pilots – Reverse-Engineered from an STP Oil Treatment Sticker** : Originally called Mighty Joe Young, the band had to change names due to a blues musician using the same name. In their backyard was an STP oil treatment sticker on a shrine-like structure. They tried to make the initials stand for something: after considering "Stinky Toilet Paper" and obscene options, they landed on Stone Temple Pilots. STP is also slang for the hallucinogen 2,5-dimethoxy-4-methylamphetamine. "They went through a bunch of possibilities, including stinky toilet paper." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Stop Rewind, the Lost Boy – Podcast Series** : This is a ten-part podcast documentary series that unravels the true story of a child from India who was kidnapped, falsely declared an orphan, and then adopted by a family in Utah. The series uses rare recordings, immersive sound design, and unforgettable first-person testimony to tell the story of a stolen childhood, the quest for truth, and the long road back home. "It tells the story of a child from India who is kidnapped, falsely declared an orphan, and then adopted by a family in Utah." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vFVnhksrFao
**Storm Thorgerson – Legendary Album Art Designer** : Storm Thorgerson is widely celebrated as one of the most famous and influential album art creators of all time. Alongside his partner Aubrey Powell, he was responsible for designing iconic covers for Pink Floyd (including "The Dark Side of the Moon," "Wish You Were Here," "Animals"), Led Zeppelin (logo, "Presence," "In Through the Out Door"), Black Sabbath, AC/DC, The Police, Genesis, Peter Gabriel, ELO, Paul McCartney, and Wings. "The cover was designed by Storm Thorgerson, probably the most famous album art creator of all time." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Straight Edge Movement** : The straight edge movement, a lifestyle based on abstaining from alcohol, drugs, and promiscuous sex, originated directly from the hardcore scene, particularly from Ian MacKaye and a Minor Threat song of the same name. This philosophy continues to be a way of life for many. "The idea of straight edge and living a life without alcohol and drugs, totally a hardcore thing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0qigzi1j81U
**Straight Edge Movement Philosophy** : The straight edge philosophy, which originated from a Minor Threat song, advocates for a lifestyle free from alcohol, drugs of any kind, and promiscuous sex. It continues to be a lifestyle choice for many people globally. "And the straight edge philosophy continues to this day. It's a lifestyle for a lot of people." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PEHkKfmmDiI
**Strategic Use of Data Analytics by Artists** : Artists are advised to utilize available streaming data analytics to inform career decisions such as tour planning (where and when), merchandise sales management, and optimal new music release timing, mirroring strategies employed by major stars to enhance their success. "You need to get into data analytics. That's what all the big stars are doing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Stratification of Online Videos** : The online landscape led to a stratification of music videos into various forms: visualizers (abstract images with audio), lyric videos (often a step before an official video), official videos (original, shot on smartphones, or found footage), and static images with audio. This multi-layered approach reflects the diverse ways a song can be visually expressed. "We began to see stratification of videos online. Maybe start with a visualizer, abstract images as the song played." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**Stream Ripping and Fake Artists – New Forms of Music Piracy in the Streaming Era** : The streaming era has generated new forms of piracy: "stream ripping" (saving streamed songs to a local file), "fake artists" (uploading another artist's music under a false identity, as in the case of "LaTesha Escalero" uploading Van Morrison recordings), and fake streams (purchasing plays to inflate numbers, divert royalties, and manipulate algorithms). "New forms of piracy include 'stream ripping,' 'fake artists' (uploading others' music under a false identity), and fake streams." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Uly2cT2vc28
**Streaming History – Rhapsody Launched December 3, 2001; Now Trades as Napster** : The modern all-you-can-hear streaming subscription model traces back to Rhapsody, which first went online on December 3, 2001. Over the following year, Universal, EMI, BMG, Warner, and Sony all joined. Rhapsody still exists but rebranded as Napster after purchasing that trademark in 2011, making it the legitimate successor to the original file-sharing service. "This is the legitimate form of Napster." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MQ7PDi-D9Ig
**Streaming Library Size – 90 Million Songs, 60,000 New Tracks Uploaded Daily** : As of early 2021, the available streaming universe contained approximately 90 million songs, all of which are accessible on any major platform. Spotify reported that 60,000 new tracks were being uploaded every single day — 2.2 million per year. Between 20 and 25% of all Spotify songs have never been streamed even once, meaning 30 to 35 million songs have been heard by no one. A site called Forgotify lets users stream only songs with zero plays using a Spotify login. "There's even an app for that." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MQ7PDi-D9Ig
**Streaming Music Context Deficiency** : While streaming offers vast access to tens of millions of songs across various eras, its major downside is the lack of context. A continuous stream of music provides no information about the artist or the song's background, leaving the music unanchored to any deeper meaning or history. "The downside of streaming is that it doesn't provide any context to what we're hearing. A continuous stream of music tells us nothing about the artist or the song." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4PlieAEthSg
**Streaming Music Uploads (Daily)** : At least 110,000 songs are uploaded to streaming music services every day. Of this volume, only about 4% originate from the three major labels (Universal, Sony, Warner Music), with the remaining 96% coming from indie labels and do-it-yourself musicians. "At last estimate, about 110,000 songs are uploaded to the streaming music services every day." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**Streaming Payouts – Platform by Platform Comparison** : Streaming payout rates vary significantly between services. For 100 listens, Spotify pays approximately 40 US cents; Apple Music and Amazon Music each pay about 50 cents; Deezer (France) pays 47 cents; YouTube pays only 18 cents; SoundCloud pays 13 cents; and Tidal may pay as much as $1.20. These figures are estimates because the system is opaque even for artists. A single Spotify stream pays roughly 6 tenths of a cent on the subscription tier, but the freemium (ad-supported) tier pays less — hence the blended average of approximately 40 cents per 100 streams. "The whole system is very, very opaque, even for the artist." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MQ7PDi-D9Ig
**Streaming Platforms – Approximately Three Dozen Active Services Worldwide** : Most streaming conversations focus on Spotify, Apple Music, Amazon Music, Tidal, YouTube Music, and Deezer, but there are approximately three dozen active services globally at any given time. These include Anghami (Abu Dhabi, the Arab world's first streaming platform), Gaana and JioSaavn (India's two largest), Patari (Lahore, Pakistan), KKBox (Taiwan), and several large Chinese services including QQ Music. Defunct services include Songs, Mog, Xbox Music, and Slacker Radio. "There are about, and it changes from day to day, three dozen active companies operating around the world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MQ7PDi-D9Ig
**Streaming Pricing – Same Service, Wildly Different Prices by Country** : Spotify is available in approximately 170 countries and charges different rates adjusted for local currencies and economic conditions. In Canada, the price is $9.99 CAD; in the US, $9.99 USD (about $12 CAD); in Egypt, roughly $4 CAD; in Vietnam, $3 CAD; and in India, $1.97 CAD. These represent different tiers of Spotify Premium, which grants full functionality and no commercials. "If you're in Japan, you're paying $980 per month." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MQ7PDi-D9Ig
**Streaming Revenue Flow – Labels Take Their Share Before Artists See a Cent** : Before any payout reaches an artist, it passes through the record label, which deducts its share first. At the high end, Spotify might pay out $4 for 100 streams, but the label absorbs approximately $3.60 before the artist sees anything. Whatever the artist does receive is almost always less than the 10% they received from physical CD sales. The 10% figure on CDs assumed a $10 wholesale price, so $1 per album; streaming makes even that look generous. "Think about that the next time you don't pay for a streaming music service and go with a freemium tier." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MQ7PDi-D9Ig
**Streaming Revenue Surpasses Physical Media (April 2018)** : The music industry, no longer concerned about declining CD sales, began to make substantial revenue from streaming. In April 2018, revenue generated from streaming services first surpassed that from physical media sales. "Revenue from streaming first eclipse that of physical media in April of 2018. The industry still isn't making as much as it did in the glory days of the CD but the bleeding has stopped. They're actually making cash." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Streaming Revolution's Impact on Catalog Value** : The streaming revolution has significantly increased the value of music catalogs. The number of paid music streaming subscribers has grown from 30 million to over 600 million globally, with projections of up to 2 billion in a decade. This provides unprecedented real-time data on consumption and creates a predictable, reliable income stream, making music an attractive asset class. "What that does is it gives the music industry a level of data that it's never had before, right? Because everything is digital and everything is real time, and there's real data there, right?" "So the customers, the paying customers gone from one in 360 to being one in 3.6." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Streaming Services and CanCon – Unregulated Platforms Drain Canadian Revenue** : Streaming platforms — all foreign-owned — operate in Canada without CanCon obligations. Unlike Canadian radio stations, they are not required to invest in Canadian culture or contribute to funds like FACTOR. Critics argue this drains revenue from the country while providing nothing back to the domestic music ecosystem. Canada, France, Australia, New Zealand, Mexico, South Africa, Nigeria, Jamaica, Venezuela, Israel, and the Philippines all have domestic music quotas for radio for this reason. "Streaming services put nothing back into the system." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-JWO_jYXwEM
**Streaming Skip Rates and Payment Threshold** : Data analysis indicates that 24% of streaming listeners skip a song within the first five seconds, 29% within ten seconds, and 35% within 30 seconds, a critical threshold because streaming services do not pay artists until a song has been listened to for at least 30 seconds. "This is important at 30 second number because a streaming service does not pay out for a song until 30 seconds have passed." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Streaming Skip Statistics** : Data indicates that 24% of streaming listeners skip unfamiliar songs within the first five seconds, 29% within ten seconds, and by the crucial 30-second mark, 35% of listeners have already moved on. "Twenty-four percent of streaming listeners will skip an unfamiliar song in the first five seconds." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Streaming Surpassing Physical Sales (2016)** : In 2016, streaming officially became the leading revenue generator for the American recorded music business, surpassing all other forms of music distribution combined for the first time. This marked a watershed moment following years of disruption from illegal file sharing and the rise of iTunes, as streaming offered a convenient, low-cost, and legal way to consume music. By 2023, streaming accounted for 67% of the industry's total revenue, signaling an irreversible shift away from physical products, though vinyl has seen a comeback with "nice fat margins." "In 2016, there was a tipping point. For the first time, streaming generated more money for the American recorded music business than all other forms of music distribution put together." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZzIqIydZTlA
**Streaming and the 30-Second Payout Rule** : As streaming gained popularity in the 2000s, rampant skipping necessitated a universal rule: artists are only paid for a song play if it runs for a minimum of 30 seconds. "No one gets paid for a play on a streaming music platform unless the song runs for at least 30 seconds." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Streaming and the Environment – Streaming 27 Times Uses More Energy Than Manufacturing a CD** : A 2017 Norwegian study found that, despite involving only digital zeros and ones, streaming music is actually worse for the environment than manufacturing physical media. Downloading one gigabyte of music data uses roughly the energy required to run a light bulb for an hour. Data centers consume approximately 2% of the world's energy to run and to keep cool. Streaming an album 27 times uses more energy than manufacturing a single CD or vinyl record. The amount of greenhouse gases generated by music streaming reaches up to 350 million kilograms per year. "Streaming an album over the internet just 27 times will use more energy than it takes to produce and manufacture a single CD or vinyl record." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MQ7PDi-D9Ig
**Streaming as Cheaper and More Convenient Consumption** : Streaming music services revolutionized music consumption by offering a cheaper alternative to buying downloads and a far more convenient method than purchasing physical media. By the mid-2010s, it became evident that streaming represented the future of music consumption. "Streaming was cheaper than buying downloads and far more convenient than traveling through time and space to buy your music on a piece of plastic." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5Y6nHw5xx88
**Streaming as Primary Revenue for Labels** : Streaming music services have become the largest source of revenue for record labels, accounting for over half of their earnings. Physical products like CDs contribute progressively less each quarter. "Streaming is now the greatest source of revenue for record labels. Over half the money they make comes from streaming, with physical product like CDs offering up less and less and less every quarter." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Streaming's Dominance and CD Market Collapse (2008-2018)** : By the time Spotify launched in 2008, the CD market had already shrunk by at least two-thirds, and by 2018, it had plummeted by 90% from its peak. This "total and complete collapse" of the CD market was driven by the overwhelming convenience of digital streaming, which became the primary way people accessed music. "By the time Spotify came along in 2008, the CD market had dropped by at least two thirds. By 2018, that drop was 90% from its all-time highs, 90%. And it's not done falling yet. It has been a total and complete collapse as we all have collectively abandoned the CD for the unbelievable convenience of digital." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Streaming's Erosion of Album Concept (Present)** : Streaming services have further diminished the album's relevance, as the internet generation prioritizes individual songs and playlists over cohesive album experiences, leading to deep album cuts being lost among millions of tracks and effectively returning music consumption to a 1964-era single-focused model. "Streaming has continued the assault on the concept of the album. The internet generation as a whole doesn't care about albums." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Streaming's Focus on Singles** : With streaming, the emphasis has shifted entirely to "individual songs, specifically singles," with album tracks receiving significantly less attention, leading to a question about the future relevance of recording full albums. "With streaming, it's all about individual songs, specifically singles, album tracks. They don't get much love at all." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Streaming's Focus on Singles Over Albums** : In the streaming world, the emphasis is heavily on individual songs, specifically singles, with album tracks receiving much less attention. This shift raises questions about the future relevance and purpose of recording full albums when consumption patterns are so fragmented. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Streaming's Impact on Albums and Playlists** : Streaming services further diminished the album concept, with people primarily streaming playlists rather than full albums, and album tracks receiving almost no traction in the streaming world. "Then came streaming. Instead of albums, people are streaming playlists. Album tracks get almost zero traction in the streaming world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Streaming's Impact on Attention Spans and Song Structure** : Streaming, with its vast supply of music, contributes to shrinking attention spans. Listeners are quick to skip songs they dislike (25% in 5 seconds, 35% before 30 seconds), fundamentally changing the nature of music as artists focus on retaining attention within the crucial 30-second Spotify payout window. "It is changing the very nature of music. And it has to do with our ever shrinking attention spans." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Streaming's Impact on Music Consumption** : Streaming has fundamentally changed how people interact with and value music, liberating it from physical limitations but also altering listening behaviors; unlike physical formats that encouraged repeated listening and financial investment, streaming offers access and convenience, leading to frequent skipping. "Streaming is not about possession. It's about access and convenience. And this has resulted in some very different interactions with music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Streaming's Impact on Music Consumption and Value** : Streaming has liberated recorded music from physical limitations, making the entire history of music accessible as pure data. However, this has changed user behavior; without the financial investment of buying physical music, listeners are less inclined to fully engage with an album, often skipping songs quickly and valuing music differently. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Streaming's Impact on the Album Concept** : Streaming music is observed to be gradually eroding the traditional concept of the album as a cohesive artistic work. "Another thing that's happening with streaming is that we're slowly killing the concept of the album." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Streaming's Power Shift to Individuals** : With the advent of streaming in the 2000s, power shifted "away from the star-making machinery behind the popular song to the individual," as each person became their "own music directors" with instant access to over 70 million songs on their phones. "when streaming took hold in the 2000s, all the power swung away from the star-making machinery behind the popular song to the individual." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Street in Paris Named After Bowie** : A street in Paris has been named after David Bowie, an international recognition of his global cultural impact and legacy. "there's a street in Paris named after him." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yq2A_o9-ulM
**StubHub's Role in the Secondary Market** : StubHub is a prominent online ticket exchange company in the secondary market, allowing individuals to buy and sell tickets they no longer need or want. Contrary to a common urban legend, StubHub is not owned by Ticketmaster; it is a subsidiary of eBay. The platform provides a mechanism for fans to potentially recover their investment or profit from tickets, acting as a form of insurance, particularly for shows booked far in advance. Unlike illicit underground markets, StubHub offers some consumer protections for both buyers and sellers. "StubHub is owned by, wait for it, eBay." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sPj3uxmz8H4
**Stylus Force during Playback** : As a record plays, the stylus vibrates a thousand times per second, subjecting it to a force of 90 Gs. This force is comparable to an airplane accelerating to Mach 2.6 in one second. "This subjects the stylus to a force of 90 Gs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=72Lei7uug8c
**Stylus Issues (Post-1973)** : Shallower grooves on thinner vinyl records meant the stylus did not seat as securely, leading to more frequent skips and scratches. "Shalor grooves also meant that the stylist didn't seat us securely, and that meant that records skipped and scratched more often." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Sub-Culture Lifespans** : Some rock-related subcultures, like New Wave and Grunge, have a lifespan corresponding to a particular musical trend, withering and dying once the trend concludes. "Some of these sub-cultures come and go." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**Subjectivity of Musical Beauty and Imperfection** : The perception of music, particularly singing voices, is highly subjective. What one person finds pleasant, another might consider mere noise, highlighting the need for patience with art forms whose beauty isn't immediately obvious. Often, imperfections in technical execution can make music more beautiful or interesting, challenging the notion that perfection is required for artistic merit. "Sometimes imperfection makes something more beautiful, or at least more interesting." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7_ZmZxnSOnE
**Sublime's Slightly Stoopid Connection – Bradley Nowell Signed Them While They Were in High School** : Slightly Stoopid is a very rock/reggae/ska/punk band from San Diego. Bradley Nowell of Sublime liked them so much he signed them to his label while they were still in high school. In a stunt, they pressed a record not on vinyl but on hash resin — it took $6,000 worth of bubble hash, $1,000 to press, and the record sounded terrible. The failed hash record was simply crushed up and smoked. "Because once it didn't work, you just crush it up and smoke it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Success and Impact of Cancon** : After about a decade, the Canadian content rules proved highly successful. They stimulated a strong domestic music scene and robust music exports, enabling Canada to produce and export more music globally than a country of its size would typically achieve. "But now, the Canadian system cranks out more music to the rest of the world than a country of our size should otherwise. We have a very strong domestic scene and strong music exports. The Cancon rules have been a great success." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Sum 41 – Three Competing Stories; Random Number Most Likely** : Three theories: (1) band formed 41 days into summer 1996 (which would be September 28th, past summer's end — problematic); (2) a penny, nickel, dime, and quarter sum to 41 cents; (3) on a computer keyboard, Shift+4 gives a dollar sign and 1 gives an exclamation mark, together expressing a wish to get rich. No definitive answer. "Take your pick." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Sum 41's Congo Documentary with War Child** : Sum 41 traveled to the Congo with the humanitarian organization War Child to shoot a documentary detailing the devastating effects of the Civil War. This trip, intended for filming, unexpectedly placed the band in the middle of renewed conflict. "We went with a group called War Child. And we were to come back and sort of finish up the album." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6L4xKBFEYkE
**Summa College, Eindhoven – University Degree in Heavy Metal** : Summa College in Eindhoven, Netherlands, offers university-level degrees in heavy metal, allowing students to formally study singing and instruments within the genre. "Offers university degrees in heavy metal, allowing students to study singing and instruments in this genre." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0DFmS6MPbFI
**Summa Studio - Black Keys' First Professional Studio** : Summa, located in the small college town of Painsville, Ohio, was The Black Keys' first professional studio choice for their *Attack and Release* album, though it was not considered extravagant. "The studio they chose wasn't exactly what you'd call extravagant. It was a place called Summa, in the tiny college town of Painsville, Ohio." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PmwfC4HR3_I
**Summary of Music's Evolutionary Roles** : Music evolved to hold a special place in the human brain due to its multiple crucial roles in survival and cultural development: facilitating mating, serving as a survival mechanism for protecting infants via lullabies, uniting tribes, and preserving cultural stories and history before the advent of written language. "Music therefore evolved in a special place in our brains because we needed to mate. We needed to keep our babies from turning us all into food. We needed to bring the tribe together and we needed to preserve our stories and thus our culture." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_Pi7JLwAeOI
**Summer Misery in Britain (Late 1970s)** : Consecutive "stink and hot" summers contributed to the general misery felt by the British populace. In Manchester, the 100-year-old sewer system began to collapse, causing an "awful lingering smell" during these terrible heat waves. "The following summers were stink and hot. And that only contributed to the misery the people of Britain felt. In Manchester, the 100-year-old sewer system began to crumble so much of the city had to deal with an awful lingering smell during those terrible heat waves." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Summer of 2005 - Rock's "Embarrassment of Riches"** : This summer saw a peak in the fifth rock cycle, described as an "embarrassment of riches" with numerous bands (Coldplay, Green Day, Foo Fighters, etc.) having hit albums simultaneously, reminiscent of the summer of 1992. "The summer of 2005 was an embarrassment of riches. Rock, and remember this is music based on alt-rock heritage, ruled." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MK7L0eCpDHc
**Sunmi and the Seven Princesses – South Korean Rapping Grandmothers** : A roaming group of grandmothers from Chilgok County in North Gyeongsang Province, South Korea, call themselves Sunmi and the Seven Princesses. They rap about things like picking chilies and watermelons. "They wander the area, wrapping about things like picking chilies and watermelons." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Super Secret iPod for US Department of Defense** : The US Department of Defense once requested Apple to develop a unique iPod unit equipped with special, undetectable hardware. Apple created the device, but its specific purpose remains unknown. "Apple did, but we still don't know what this iPod was supposed to do." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HoBx6s3JmVQ
**Supermassive Black Hole's B Flat Emission** : A supermassive black hole located in the Perseus cluster, approximately 250 million light-years away, emits a profound acoustic wave. This wave is identified as a B flat, positioned an astonishing 57 octaves below middle C, representing an incredibly deep bass sound. "There is a singing black hole, a supermassive black hole in the Perseus cluster, which is a group of galaxies about 250 million light years away. It emits a super deep acoustic wave. And just so you know, it's a B flat, 57 octaves below middle sea." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**Supernova Explosion ~41,000 Years Ago – Possible Cosmic Origin of Music** : A theory proposes that a supernova explosion approximately 41,000 years ago may have caused genetic mutations in *Homo sapiens*, resulting in enlarged brains capable of complex thought, language, visual art (as in cave paintings), and ultimately music. The oldest known musical instruments date to approximately 30,000 years ago, consistent with this timeline. "A supernova explosion about 41,000 years ago may have caused mutations in Homo sapiens leading to larger brains and, ultimately, music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=f2KA97T4u08
**Supply and Demand for Tour Resources** : The increased demand for complex tour productions has driven up the cost of essential services and materials, including sound and light companies, tour bus rentals, and roadies, due to basic supply and demand economics. "Fifth, this is put a greater demand on companies to supply the raw materials for tours, sound and light companies, tour bus randles, roadies, supply and demand has pushed prices up." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Suppression of Women in Music (526 AD)** : In the year 526, the church enacted a ban on all women from participating in any kind of music lessons, including singing. This prohibition significantly limited women's musical involvement for a thousand years. "Then in the year 526, the church banned all women from taking part in any kind of music lessons, including singing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9GHpC0kCgHY
**Surf Music's Enduring Influence** : Surf music's sound and aesthetic have permeated various other genres, including punk rock, hardcore punk, and even indie rock, demonstrating its adaptability and lasting impact on the rock ecosystem. Its influence is noted in amplifier design and recording studio innovations. "You can see how a form of music can worm its way through the rock ecosystem." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pOeChe_D1Ag
**Surprise Album Release Trend (2010s)** : David Bowie's 2013 album, *The Next Day*, which was released without prior announcement, was the first in a series of "surprise albums" by superstar acts in the 2010s, predating Beyoncé's well-known use of the strategy. "It was the first of a series of surprise albums recorded and released by superstar acts in the 2010s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yq2A_o9-ulM
**Swim Drink Fish Music Club (2009)** : In 2009, Gord Downie and the rest of The Tragically Hip became part of the Swim Drink Fish Music Club. This initiative brought together musicians and environmentalists with the goal of raising money and awareness for the preservation of clean rivers and lakes. "in 2009, Gord and the rest of the band were part of the Swim Drink Fish Music Club, which brought together musicians and environmentalists to help raise money to preserve clean rivers and lakes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dnz_Ycd_q7s
**Synergy Between College Bands and Radio** : A perfect synergy existed between college bands and college radio: many indie bands were formed by college students, played their first gigs on or around campus, and toured by playing other colleges, making college radio their natural supporter. This created a well-organized musical subculture. "The synergy was perfect, indie bands were often formed by college students. The first gigs were usually in and around the campus and when they went on the road, they most likely played in and around other colleges. So it was only natural for these college bands to be supported by college radio." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sCtQqVBtCaI
**Synesthesia** : Synesthesia is a neurological condition where the brain perceives the world in different, cross-sensory ways. Bob Hardy of Franz Ferdinand has a form of synesthesia where he can "see smells," which he considers a gift rather than a handicap, and uses this perception as inspiration for his painting. Some people with synesthesia can also "see music." "Bob has a condition known as synesthesia. It's really hard to explain, but Bob's brain, his actual brain, is wired in a way that he can see smells." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RWbKqqW78yg
**Synesthesia – Music Experienced as Colors; Affects Kanye West, Stevie Wonder, Tori Amos, Eddie Van Halen, and Others** : Synesthesia is a neurological condition where the senses overlap. Musical synesthesia — where notes, chords, and combinations of sounds are perceived as specific colors — has been reported by Kanye West, Stevie Wonder (who is also totally blind), Tori Amos, Billy Joel, Patrick Stump of Fall Out Boy, Eddie Van Halen, and Pharrell Williams. Growing up with synesthesia, these artists generally assumed everyone experienced music this way. Singer Sam Endicott of The Bravery, who also writes songs for Shakira and Christina Aguilera, has credited synesthesia with giving him an unusual range. "They all say that they have music or sound to color synesthesia." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NMwMwqMQpf4
**Synesthesia – Overlapping Senses with Music** : Synesthesia is a neurological quirk where senses overlap, leading to interesting perceptions. One type involves seeing music, where notes or timbers appear to have colors (e.g., a middle C might be red, a piano might sound green, or a song might have a golden halo). This experience is consistent for individuals, usually present from birth, and they find it difficult to describe to non-synesthetes due to its inherent nature. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHN6db7m3DM
**Synonymity with Online Video** : YouTube has become globally synonymous with online video, fundamentally altering how video content is accessed and consumed. "YouTube is now synonymous with online video." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Synthesizers in Early 1980s** : Around 1980, synthesizers became widespread because the gear became sufficiently cheap, portable, and powerful for everyday musicians. This accessibility fostered a "punk" approach to the technology, as many users lacked formal musical training but enjoyed experimenting to create cool, unprecedented sounds. "it took until the beginning of the 80s for the gear to become cheap enough, portable enough, and powerful enough to be taken up by everyday musicians." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9CNNsqXiL6U
**Syrac Machine's Popular Tunes (early 1951)** : In early 1951, Jeff Hild, a programmer at the University of Sydney, figured out how to make the Syrac computer emit steady tones from its speaker, initially used for program indicators. He then developed the capability for Syrac to play melodies of popular tunes, including drinking songs, marking one of the first uses of a computer to create recognizable music, reconstructed from surviving punch paper tapes. "He started messing about and got Syrac to play the melodies of popular tunes of the day." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**System of a Down – Changed from "Victims of a Down" to "System" for Shelf Proximity to Slayer** : Originally called Soil, then "Victims of a Down" — referencing the Armenian genocide, drawn from a poem by guitarist Daron Malakian. Bassist Shavo Odadjian suggested replacing "Victims" with "System" to attract more attention, and the change would place them closer to their idols Slayer on record store shelves alphabetically. "Base player Shavo Odagyan suggested that they might find more attention if they changed victims to system." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**TV Landscape in 1987 (Opulence vs. Blue-Collar)** : In 1987, television was dominated by opulent programming, including lavish soaps like *Dallas* and *FalconCrust*, and sitcoms featuring upper-middle-class families such as *The Cosby Show* and *Empty Nest*. Against this backdrop, the idea of centering a show around a "salt of the Earth" family of factory workers from the Midwest, like *Roseanne*, was considered a "pretty radical idea." "Opulence dominated TV at the time with lavish soaps like Dallas and FalconCrust, and sitcoms that followed upper middle-class families like the Cosby Show and Empty Nest." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6O5TCTO-go4
**TV Show Theme Song Licensing** : Securing a deal for a song to be used as a TV show's theme is another significant source of revenue for artists. Examples include The Who's "Who Are You" for "CSI," Cake's "Short Skirt/Long Jacket" for "Chuck," and Alabama 3's "Woke Up This Morning" for "The Sopranos." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Tabloid Scandals** : Her separation from Bob Geldof and subsequent relationship with Michael Hutchence generated sensational tabloid headlines, including "the six illegal things Michael did to me on our first night," extensive coverage of her new breast implants, and frequent stories of parties and drug use. "One of the biggest stories was the six illegal things Michael did to me on our first night. Paul's new breast implants received major coverage. So did the tales of parties and drugs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**TacoTron's Jay-Z Deep Fake (2022)** : In the summer of 2022, the text-to-speech program TacoTron generated a rap track that utilized a sampled and cloned version of Jay-Z's voice to rap the "to be or not to be" soliloquy from Shakespeare's Hamlet, which was then posted on YouTube. This incident was one of the first public examples of a "deep fake" of a well-known musician, and Jay-Z's representatives expressed their displeasure. "In the summer of 2022, they generated a rap track that sounded exactly like Jay-Z and posted it on YouTube." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Tactile Experience and Analog Sound Preference** : A segment of the population desires the "old school tactile experience" of playing vinyl, appreciating the artwork, liner notes, and the "warm analog sound" in contrast to impersonal digital tracks. "A certain segment of the population wants that old school tactile experience of playing vinyl, ruminating over the artwork and liner notes, and basking in the warm analog sound." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Tag Team Mixing Technique** : A collaborative mixing method was described where two individuals take 15-minute turns at the mixing board, with the key rule that "nothing sacred" from the previous person's work is off-limits for alteration. This rapid-fire process results in four different mixes within an hour and is likened to "speed chess." "You get 15 minutes to mix. You finish a mix. You leave the room. The other guy comes in, and the only rule is nothing sacred." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VR5Fncdc2kQ
**Tangential Excision Medical Procedure** : Travis Barker underwent tangential excision as part of his recovery from severe burns. This surgical procedure involves a surgeon scraping away dead, burned skin until viable, bleeding tissue is reached. While necessary for healing, it carries risks of significant blood loss and infection. "He ended up in something called tangential excision. This is where a surgeon scrapes away dead, burned skin until it starts to bleed." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q-QP7ePxkQE
**Tape-Trading Culture – Non-Commercial Code of Ethics** : Pre-internet tape-trading networks operated under an informal but widely observed non-commercial code of ethics centered on documentation, archiving, and love of the artist's legacy — not profit. "Operated under a non-commercial code of ethics focused on documentation, archiving, and love of the artist." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OrHtPVBjXw4
**Tate-LaBianca Murders (1969)** : These murders, which occurred in Los Angeles in the summer of 1969, were the direct consequence of Charles Manson's belief that The Beatles were communicating with him through their song "Helter Skelter." Manson interpreted these messages as an encouragement to start a race war. "The ultimate result was the Tate La Bianca murders in Los Angeles in the summer of 1969." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Tattoos as Family and Life Markers** : Tattoos are often used to commemorate family members and significant life events. Darren Fyfer has tattoos for his mother and father, and plans to get tattoos of his dogs when they pass away, indicating that even in his early 50s, he continues to plan for new tattoos to mark personal connections. "Family, and tattoos like family, dates. I got my mother. I got my father. I got my family. I got... I'm going to get my dog when my dog passes away several years from now on, I'm going to get my two dogs tattooed on me somewhere." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jZeMmPekKMQ
**Tattoos as a Mainstream Commodity** : The trend of tattoos is becoming increasingly pervasive and commercialized, with tattoo parlors now found inside malls across America. This allows consumers to purchase "rock and roll clothes," shoes, and fresh ink all in one location, leading to a sense of lost uniqueness for early adopters. "It's like you can go to a mall now in the middle of America. And you can buy all the rock and roll clothes at Spencer or whatever the store is in your town. And then you can go and get the shoes over at a rock and roll shoes. And then right next to it, there's tattooed parlors inside malls now. So you can walk out and be fully rock and roll from head to toe, including the fresh ink at your local mall in any city in America." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jZeMmPekKMQ
**Tax Advantages of Catalog Sales (Capital Gains)** : Selling an entire catalog as a lump sum payment often results in the income being treated as a capital gain for tax purposes, which has a substantially lower tax rate (e.g., 20%) compared to regular income tax (e.g., 50% or more). This difference in tax rates can be tens of millions of dollars. "If an artist sells their catalog to one of these companies, governments tend to look at that income that lump sum as a capital gain. And the tax rate for capital gains is substantially lower than standard income tax. So instead of paying, I don't know, 50% in taxes, the artist pays 20%." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Tax Benefits of Catalog Sales (US)** : In the United States, selling a song catalog offers significant tax advantages. Regular royalty income is taxed at high personal income rates (40-50%), but taking a large lump sum from a catalog sale is treated as capital gains, which may be taxed at a much lower rate (around 20%). ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Taylor Hawkins and Luke Spiller (The Struts) Were Working on a James Bond Theme** : At the time of his death, Foo Fighters drummer Taylor Hawkins was working on a James Bond theme with Luke Spiller of The Struts. They did not get the assignment — it went to Billie Eilish. "He was working on a James Bond theme with Luke Spiller of the band The Struts." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Tears for Fears – From Psychologist Arthur Janov's "Primal Scream" Therapy Book** : Named after the writings of American psychologist Arthur Janov, whose book Prisoners of Pain discussed "primal scream" therapy — releasing emotional blockage through crying and expressing pain. The same source inspired the name Primal Scream (#23 on this list). "Inspired by the writings of American psychologist Arthur Janov." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Technical Limitations and Future of Holograms** : Despite advancements, current hologram technology primarily relies on projecting images onto a physical surface like glass, with no practical method yet found for projecting 3D images into thin air. However, ongoing developments in augmented reality, virtual reality, and artificial intelligence suggest a promising future for more sophisticated virtual performances. "Right now, you need to know that holograms still need to be projected onto something like glass. No one has found a practical way, at least not yet, to project a 3D image in thin air." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GYUuTbOvyak
**Technical Productions - Rob Johnston** : Rob Johnston was responsible for the technical productions. "Technical Productions by Rob Johnston." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Technological Advancements and Progressive Rock** : The increasing complexity of progressive rock in the late 1960s and early 1970s was enabled by technological advancements. Musicians became more skilled, leading to a focus on virtuosity, and new equipment alongside more sophisticated recording studios allowed for the creation of new and intricate sounds. "New equipment allowed for new sounds, and recording studios installed ever more sophisticated gear." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HSvgRyGiOpE
**Technological Advancements in Music Production (1970s-1980s)** : The 1970s and 1980s saw exponential growth in computing power, which translated into advancements in music technology. Faster processors, increased memory, and better storage were integrated into synthesizers, samplers, and digital recording software, enabling humans to program machines to produce music with greater complexity and fidelity. "This tech was incorporated into machines that made music, synthesizers, samplers, digital recording software, and the computer that could run it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Technological Disruption in the 2000s** : The first decade of the 21st century was defined by "massive technological disruption" that profoundly impacted the music industry and how music adapted. "The first decade of the 21st century was an era of massive technological disruption." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Technological Landscape in 2000** : On January 1st, 2000, several key technologies and platforms did not exist or were not widely known, including iTunes, iPods, iPhones (or any smartphone), YouTube, Facebook, Twitter, Snapchat, Spotify, Netflix (as it's known today), MySpace, and Instagram. "Let's make a list of all the things we did not have on January 1st, 2000." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Technological Landscape in 2000 - Existing Technologies** : At the beginning of 2000, common technologies included dial-up modems, Windows 98 or 95, and Google was less than 18 months old. Apple was considered "mostly a corporate basket case." "Even Google was less than 18 months old at the time." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Technological Landscape in 2009** : By December 31, 2009, smartphones were becoming ubiquitous, digital music files were widely used, and streaming services were gaining traction, with Apple, Google, and Facebook becoming some of the world's most powerful companies. "Everyone was getting smartphones." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Technology as Driver in Fifth Rock Cycle** : During rock's fifth cycle, technology emerged as "something completely new" and "so big that it might just shatter everything" in how music is created and consumed, possibly leading to a paradigm shift around 2012. "Technology has always been a driver of the way we create and consume music. But during this fifth cycle, we saw something completely new in terms of technology. It was so big that it might just shatter everything." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MK7L0eCpDHc
**Technology as a Driving Force** : Technology is an unparalleled driver in the creation, distribution, and consumption of music, shaping expectations that any song should be accessible anytime, anywhere, on any device. This fundamentally alters behaviors across the entire music ecosystem, including artists, fans, promoters, record companies, and stores. "technology is driving the creation of music, the distribution of music, and the consumption of music as never before." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-CT7yYXonIk
**Ted Bundy as Subject of Songs** : A similar number of songs about serial killer Ted Bundy can be found across various genres, including rock, pop, metal, and hip hop. This widespread musical attention reflects his notoriety. "There are a similar number of songs about Ted Bundy, rock, pop, metal, hip hop." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Ted Cruz - 2016 Presidential Campaign** : Senator Ted Cruz used Explosions in the Sky's song "Your Hand in Mine" in his 2016 presidential campaign video, an action that greatly annoyed the band and led to the video's removal. "Your Hand in Mind, which incidentally was used by Senator Ted Cruz in his 2016 presidential run, something that greatly annoyed the band because they don't like Ted." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9-v4qnOO8wY
**Teenagers as a New Classification (1950s)** : In the 1950s, "teenagers" emerged as a new social classification, marking the period between childhood and adulthood. This demographic was particularly receptive to new forms of music like rock and roll. "Teenagers, a new classification of that time between being a child and a dover, loved this stuff." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6oUkMOkEr9U
**Televangelist Selling Jesus' Tears** : Nathan Followill recounted a bizarre experience with a televangelist who was attempting to sell "the tears from Jesus that he wept on the cross" for $3,999, claiming they would never evaporate. "I actually saw one guy, a telebandalist one time that was actually trying to sell the tears from Jesus that he wept on the cross. Like the most ridiculous like. And there were people that were literally for $3,999. You could actually get, they never evaporated or anything." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gxbuajGKbko
**Temporal Lobe and Music Recognition** : The temporal lobe, situated centrally in the brain, has two hemispheres: the left hemisphere is responsible for interpreting speech, while the right hemisphere is crucial for recognizing the structures and patterns inherent in music. This right-side region is interconnected with the auditory cortex. "The temporal lobe, a hunk of brain right in the middle, has two hemispheres. One, the one on the left, interprets speech. The one on the right helps us recognize the structures and patterns of music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**The "10,000 Hours" Concept in Addiction Recovery** : Hugh Dillon referenced the Malcolm Gladwell concept of "10,000 hours" in the context of his multiple attempts at rehab, implying the immense time and effort required for successful recovery from addiction. "It's like the Malcolm Blackwell, 10,000 hours of rehabs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zBd13KtYlAA
**The "Art Context" Podcast (General Information)** : This new podcast, hosted by Director X and Taj Tajes-Krishnark, is titled "The Architects of Art." It focuses on telling the stories of "storytellers that come from music videos," aiming to give these creative artists the recognition they deserve. The show explores their "come-ups, their journey, their process of creating some of the most iconic music videos, films, and content on the planet." The debut episode featured director Dave Myers, discussing his artistic philosophy, work, and larger world issues like Black Lives Matter. "Art Show is pretty much about, it's in the world of music. It's pretty much us sitting down with storytellers that come from music videos." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zUQ2vZzmRiI
**The "Minor Ninth" in Bond Themes (All Eras)** : After extensive analysis of past James Bond themes, co-writer Paul Epworth discovered a recurring musical theory element: all successful Bond themes contain a variation of something called a "minor ninth." This harmonic commonality proved to be a critical insight for creating Adele's "Skyfall," which blended modern sound with traditional Bond elements. "It turns out that if you dig into music theory, they all contained a variation of something called a minor ninth." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=K0pgJ7XAlpo
**The "No Image" Image of the 1990s** : In the 1990s, the concept of standardized uniforms in music fashion fell out of favor, with many musicians adopting a deliberate "we don't have an image, image." This approach marked a period where a lack of overt visual branding became a statement in itself. "The concept of the standardized uniform fell into disfavor for the 1990s when most people adopted the, we don't have an image, image. That was the way of thinking." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mhQ36Y-c9fM
**The "Rock Dot" Umlaut** : The umlaut, a diacritic form of punctuation, appears as two dots over letters in certain languages like German and Swedish to indicate pronunciation. In music, particularly rock and metal, these have become known as "rock dots." Bands like Blue Oyster Cult were the first to adopt them in 1970 purely for aesthetic reasons, as they looked "cool," "foreign," "Gothic," "exotic," "mean," "frightening," or "evil." "If you're unclear, the umlot is the two dots that appear over letters in certain languages like German and Swedish." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**The "Say Cheese" Trick** : The practice of saying "cheese" for a photograph to encourage a smile is of uncertain origin, but the earliest reference dates to an October 1943 Texas newspaper report. Joseph E. Davies, a former ambassador to Moscow, revealed it as his secret for looking pleasant in photos, though he learned it from another politician. In early photography, smiling was considered undignified, with photographers often instructing people to say "prunes," which resulted in a "duck face." "The earliest reference to say cheese comes from a Texas newspaper report in October 1943." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**The "Tower of Babel Society" of Music Consumption** : The current music landscape is described as a "Tower of Babel Society," where individuals prefer their niches and unique tastes, choosing music accordingly. This stands in stark contrast to the communal viewing experiences of the past, when everyone watched the same music videos on channels like MTV. "Today, though, we live in a tower of Babel Society. And we like it. People love their niches. We're individuals. We're unique. We choose our music accordingly." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7IDbqdIw3WU
**The 13-Year Rock-Pop Cycle Theory** : Since 1951, rock and pop music have been "locked in a battle" where their popularity and creativity are 180 degrees out of phase, with each cycle lasting approximately 13 years. Rock consistently proves "very good at resurrection" after periods of decline. "Each of these cycles lasts about 13 years. And if there's one thing that's been proven over and over again, it's that Rock is very good at resurrection." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MK7L0eCpDHc
**The 13th Amendment's Indirect Role in Rock and Roll** : The ratification of the 13th Amendment, which outlawed slavery on December 16, 1865, set in motion a chain of events that profoundly influenced American music. It enabled the Great Migration of African Americans from the South to the North in the early 20th century, which in turn fostered the cultural conditions for the birth of genres like blues and jazz, ultimately leading to the rise of rock and roll in the 1950s. "All that, the rise of rock and roll, began with the great migration of the early 20th century, which was made possible by the ratification of the 13th Amendment, outlawing slavery on December 16th, 1865." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_Pi7JLwAeOI
**The 1990s as a Pre-Digital, Rock-Dominant Era** : The 1990s were marked as the final decade where rock music held a dominant position, preceding the widespread adoption of the internet, iPods, iTunes, and smartphones. "the 90s were the last decade where rock was the dominant form of music. It was also the last pre-internet decade, and the last decade, without things like iPods, iTunes and smartphones." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5bQz8R0gbXc
**The 1992 Grunge Era** : The year 1992 is identified as "the era of grunge," characterized by dominant bands such as Nirvana, Pearl Jam, Soundgarden, Alice in Chains, The Smashing Pumpkins, and Stone Temple Pilots. These bands were recognized for their distinctive "slow and low" sound, often employing a "quiet loud dynamic." "Now, this of course was the era of grunge. We had Nirvana, Pearl Jam and Sound Garden, Analysis and Chains and so on." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pE7TaWSUa-o
**The 45 RPM Adapter (Spider)** : The large hole of 45 RPM records necessitated adapters for playback on standard multi-speed turntables designed for LPs. Early adapters were solid zinc (Webster Chicago Corporation) or similar (Fidelatone), and difficult to use. Capitol Records developed an "optical center" cardboard adapter. RCA later introduced the iconic "spider" – a three-armed plastic insert with "drive dots" to prevent slippage when records were stacked. "And so was born the 45 RPM adapter." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**The 7-Inch Single's Large Hole (45 RPM)** : Unlike the standard 9.30-second hole of 33 1/3 RPM LPs (standardized since 1948), 7-inch 45 RPM singles, introduced by RCA in 1949, feature a full one-and-a-half-inch hole. This design facilitated RCA's proprietary system for stacking multiple 45s on a special spindle for continuous playback. "Most seven inch singles have a hole that is much, much bigger than what we see on 33 and a third vinyl albums." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**The Ambiguity of Morphine Toxicology Counts** : In toxicology, there is a significant difference between a "free morphine count" and a "total morphine count," which can critically impact the interpretation of drug levels in a body. A free morphine count measures the immediate level of drugs injected, while a total morphine count includes the cumulative build-up of morphine over weeks and months from long-term heroin use, a method that is often cheaper and faster but can misrepresent the acute effects of a drug dose. "We're not sure if the number represents something called a free morphine count or a total morphine count." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1Jt2fUBDusk
**The Amen Break (1969) – 6-Second Drum Sample from The Winstons That Became the Foundation of an Era** : The Amen Break is a six-second drum solo performed by GC Coleman on "Amen Brother," the B-side to a 1969 Grammy-winning single called "Color Him Father" by a funk soul group called The Winstons. When sampling became widespread in the 1980s and 1990s, this passage was discovered and became one of the most recycled drum breaks in history, appearing in thousands of songs across hip-hop, rave, techno, jungle, and drum and bass. Documented uses include Linkin Park's "Faint," N.W.A.'s "Straight Outta Compton," Public Enemy's "Fight the Power," Oasis's "D'You Know What I Mean," and The Prodigy's "Firestarter." Despite this ubiquity, neither GC Coleman the drummer nor Richard L. Spencer the composer has received a single cent in royalties from any of these uses. "Since then, the Amen Break has been sliced and diced and rearranged into a million different ways in a million different songs." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mmI2oN9zolQ
**The Bathhouse Recording Studio** : The Bathhouse is a local recording studio where Cassadour's first full album, *Brude and Bloom*, was recorded. "recording was done at a local recording studio called The Bathhouse." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MmXDbXI6Sqk
**The Bathhouse Studio** : Located in Bath, Ontario, this studio is owned by The Tragically Hip and served as the recording location for Arkells' album "Michigan Left." The band lived in the same building as the studio during recording, allowing for extensive experimentation until late hours (sometimes 5 AM) with various equipment and effects like an old plate reverb. It provided an ideal environment for the then "23 year olds" to explore new sounds and production techniques. "The bathhouse you can, it's the Tragically Hips studio in Bath, Ontario and upstairs there's all these rooms. And so we were living in the same studio, in the same building as the studio." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kKNn01M8GG4
**The Battle of Britpop (August 1995)** : This epic showdown involved Blur's "Country House" and Oasis's "Roll With It" being released on the same day, August 14, 1995. The rivalry, fueled by genuine hatred between the bands and extensive media coverage (including bookie bets), led to record sales across Britain increasing by 41% that week. Blur ultimately won the single sales battle, selling 274,000 copies of "Country House" compared to Oasis's 216,000 of "Roll With It." "The two hottest, biggest, most hyped bands since the Beatles and the Stones were going head-to-head for chart supremacy in the same week, so there could only be one winner." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vzyMSSlD7Yg
**The Beatles and James Bond Shared Debut Day (1962)** : The release of The Beatles' first single, "Love Me Do," and the premiere of the first James Bond film, "Dr. No," both occurred on the same day, October 5th, 1962. This marks a unique simultaneous debut for two major cultural phenomena. "The Beatles and James Bond debuted on the same day." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-N6IM5Nm5iw
**The Big Breakfast (British TV Show)** : Paula Yates's new show in 1994, which featured an infamous and highly sexually charged seven-minute interview with Michael Hutchence conducted on a bed, watched by two million people. "In 1994, they met again for Paula's new show The Big Breakfast." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**The Big Breakfast Interview (1994)** : In 1994, she hosted Michael Hutchence on her new show *The Big Breakfast*, conducting a highly suggestive, seven-minute interview on a bed, watched by two million viewers. This public display, while both were in other relationships, led to widespread tabloid speculation and tipped off Helena Christensen and Bob Geldof. "In 1994, they met again for Paula's new show The Big Breakfast." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**The Black Keys – Possibly from Patrick Carney's Father's Insult, or a Homeless Man's Farewell** : One story: Patrick Carney's father called him and Dan Auerbach "a couple of Black Keys" meaning they were slightly weird or off. Another story: a homeless schizophrenic man who asked people for crayons always said goodbye with "Don't be a Black Key. Be a B flat." "The best, most calming song that you can play for an anxious dog in the car." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**The British Press's Role in Hype Cycles** : Similar to MTV, the British press was observed to pick a band, heavily hype them for approximately six months, and then discard them. If the hyped band lacked genuine talent or substance, they would struggle to build a sustainable career after being dropped by the press, indicating a cyclical pattern of promotion and abandonment. "They'll pick a band, they'll hype the shit out of them for six months. And then they discard them and if that band isn't really that great, they can't go anywhere from them." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=M3nUQtja0qc
**The Can Rock Renaissance of the 1990s – Tragically Hip, Alanis Morissette, Nickelback, and Dozens More** : By the 1990s, after two decades of CanCon nurturing, Canadian music had come of age. Major domestic acts selling tens or hundreds of thousands of records included Matthew Good, Tea Party, I Mother Earth, Barenaked Ladies, Pursuit of Happiness, Sloan, The Watchmen, Moist, The Headstones, Econoline Crush, Limblifter, Bif Naked, Real McKenzies, Tragically Hip, and Alanis Morissette. Alanis Morissette, Sarah McLachlan, Shania Twain, and Céline Dion became international megastars. In the 2000s, Nickelback's "How You Remind Me" became the most-played song on American radio between 2001 and 2009. More recently, Michael Bublé, Justin Bieber, Drake, Shawn Mendes, and The Weeknd have all emerged from the Canadian system. "Depending on the year, we are either the fifth or sixth largest music market in the world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-JWO_jYXwEM
**The Can Rock Revolution of the 1990s** : This term describes a transformative period in the 1990s when Canadian attitudes towards homegrown talent completely shifted. Not only did Canadian music fans embrace domestic bands, but Canadian music became highly sought after worldwide, marking a significant change from previous decades of insecurity. "Some have called this the great Ken Rock Revolution of the 1990s. It changed everything." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6PGgNCaW4xo
**The Canadian Indie Rock Scene (90s-2000s)** : The Canadian indie rock scene of the late 1990s and early 2000s was characterized by unique and influential bands such as Head, Hayden, Treble Charger, and The Killjoys. These artists, particularly Head with its personal songwriting and victory in the 102.1 New Music Search, profoundly influenced Ash's musical development and shaped the landscape for emerging musicians. "all that incredible, like unique Canadian, all that can rock stuff. Indy rock music. It just, it just came flooding in." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ayPJfDAF_to
**The Chemical Brothers – Changed from "The Dust Brothers" After Name Conflict** : Originally called The Dust Brothers, they encountered a band already using that name. They took a word from the title of their third single, "Chemical Beats," and became The Chemical Brothers. "They were originally called the Dust Brothers, but there was another band using that name." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**The Chromatic Scale and Musical Building Blocks** : The chromatic scale is the standard scale of pitches or notes used for centuries in Western music, consisting of 12 notes within an octave that then repeat. These notes are mathematically related through a set series of ratios, or semitone intervals, and form the basic DNA for all music, from Mozart's operas to punk songs. "For centuries, we've used a standard scale of pitches or notes, called the chromatic scale. 12 notes in an octave, then repeat." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L09R2evjyMc
**The Clash – Newspaper Headline: "Clash with Police"** : When looking for a name, a story in the Daily Telegraph caught their attention: a headline about a clash with police. "Simple, newspaper headline." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**The Concept of Sampling** : Sampling involves surgically excising a sound from any source for use in another musical composition. The length of the sample can vary from a fraction of a second to an extended duration, and once isolated, it can be used as-is or further manipulated with computers or specialized devices. "This is where the composer surgically excises a sound from a source, any source, for use in another composition." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OiqdZfxdBP0
**The Cranberries – Accidental Rename via Typo on a Returned Demo Tape** : Formed as "The Cranberry Saw Us" — a pun the band themselves considered stupid. When they submitted a demo to a record label, it was returned addressed to "The Cranberries" (a typo). They decided it was a better name and kept it. "It was returned, addressed to the cranberries. So it was a typo." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**The Crow (1994) Film Production Tragedy** : The filming of *The Crow* in 1994 was infamously marked by the death of its star, Brandon Lee, during filming. A blank ammunition prop malfunction resulted in his death during surgery just eight days into shooting. This tragedy necessitated script rewrites, the use of body doubles, and CGI, adding a new level of darkness to an already dark movie. "This is the film that became infamous with the fact that its star, Brandon Lee, died during filming." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**The Crow (1994) Soundtrack Commercial Success** : *The Crow* soundtrack, released in 1994, was a major commercial success, reaching number one on the Billboard Album Charts and selling approximately 4 million copies. Its release coincided with the peak of the alternative and Lollapalooza period, and its carefully curated music effectively reflected the film's dark themes and tragic production. "That was from the first movie in 1994, and it reached number one on the Billboard Album Charts, selling about 4 million copies." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**The Crow Soundtrack (March 1994)** : The soundtrack for "The Crow" movie, released at the end of March 1994, was highly acclaimed and described as "one of the best of the decade." It featured contributions from major artists including The Cure, Nine Inch Nails, Rage Against the Machine, and Stone Temple Pilots. "It's great soundtrack, too. One of the best of the decade, and at the cure in 9 inch nails and rage against the machine and other big names." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TDwagoyMvck
**The Crow: City of Angels (1996) Soundtrack Sales** : The soundtrack for *The Crow: City of Angels*, the 1996 sequel, was considered "pretty much as good as the first one" and sold approximately 3 million copies. It featured contributions from bands like Bush, Korn, Deftones, and a hit cover by Hole. "That collection sold about 3 million copies." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**The Crow: Salvation (2000) Soundtrack vs. Movie Release** : *The Crow: Salvation* soundtrack was released in the spring of 2000, significantly before the movie itself appeared in 2004. Even then, the film went straight to DVD, indicating a disconnect between the soundtrack's release strategy and the film's distribution. "But what's interesting is that while The soundtrack was released in the spring of 2000, the movie didn't appear until 2004, and even then it went straight to DVD." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**The Crow: Wicked Prayer (2006) Film Failure** : *The Crow: Wicked Prayer*, the final film in *The Crow* series in 2006, received "very bad" reviews and likely went straight to DVD. Notably, the transcript states there was no accompanying soundtrack for this installment, highlighting a decline in the franchise's commercial and critical success. "Reviews were very bad. And I also think it went straight to DVD. No soundtrack either." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**The Day the Music Died (1957)** : February 3, 1957, is historically recognized as "the day the music died." This date refers to the tragic small plane crash in a cornfield outside Mason City, Iowa, that killed rock and roll pioneers Buddy Holly, Ritchie Valens, and Big Wopper. "February 3rd, 1957 has gone down in history as the day the music died." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3eGBnwPWRGo
**The Day the Music Died (1959)** : The infamous event known as "The Day the Music Died" occurred in 1959, claiming the lives of Buddy Holly, The Big Bopper, and Ritchie Valens. Their chartered single-engine aircraft crashed in an Iowa cornfield, a tragedy that left a profound mark on music history. "There was the infamous The Day the Music died in 1959 that saw Buddy Holly, the big bopper and Richie Valens die when their chartered single engine aircraft went down in an Iowa cornfield." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dgzF_KBSQWM
**The Dead Milkmen – From a Tony Morrison Novel ("Song of Solomon")** : There is a character in Toni Morrison's novel Song of Solomon called "Milkman Dead." The band followed the Dead Kennedys model of pairing "Dead" with an unexpected noun, but the specific choice is literary. "There's a character in a Tony Morrison novel called Song of Solomon called Milkman Dead." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**The Death of the Album Concept** : The traditional rock album, conceived as a coherent collection of carefully recorded and sequenced songs to be consumed as a whole (a concept that solidified in the mid-1960s with *Sgt. Pepper*), is undergoing a "long, slow death." "The concept of the rock album, this collection of songs carefully recorded and sequenced and meant to be consumed as a whole, really began to take form in the middles 60s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**The Decline of Western Civilization (Film)** : This film, made by Penelope Spheeris, documented the peculiar punk scenes in D.C. and Southern California. It captured footage of predominantly male audiences moshing to bands like the Circle Jerks and Black Flag, and its global distribution played a significant role in spreading moshing behavior to other parts of the world. "A filmmaker named Penelope Sphereis decided she needed to document this peculiar form of punk scene in DC and Southern California." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KMx4bmq8o2Y
**The Face Magazine** : This mainstream music publication from the UK began to pay more attention to Goth, largely due to its fashion sense. "Mainstream music publications, most notably the enemy and the face, both out of the UK, began to be more attention, drawn largely out of fashion sense of the whole thing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**The Face Magazine - "B Word" Publisher** : The Face magazine is credited with publishing "the B word" in May 1994, an abbreviation of British pop, which subsequently led to the widespread adoption of the term "Britpop." "Then a magazine called The Face published the B word in May of 1994 as an abbreviation of British pop." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DSvoppf1dGY
**The Fall of the Roman Empire** : This historical event is cited as one of the alleged inspirations for Radiohead's song "Paranoid Android." "And also the Fall of the Roman Empire." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JUvDp3_RBH0
