**The Firm Management Agency** : The Firm was a powerful management agency representing Linkin Park, alongside other major artists such as Korn, Ice Cube, and American Idol winner Taylor Hicks. This agency played a central role in Linkin Park's contentious dispute with Warner Brothers Records. "the manager was a powerful agency called The Firm, which also runs the careers of everyone from corn to ice cube to American Idol Taylor Hicks." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YcdidjWaIqE
**The First Audio Recording (1860)** : On April 9, 1860, French inventor Edward-Léon Scott de Martinville created the Phono Autograph, a device that scribbled sound waveforms on paper. This produced the first ever audio recording, although it was not designed for playback and could only be heard after laser scanning technology was used in 2008. "The first ever audio recorded dates back to April 9th of 1860." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gP3oQeYEvso
**The First Band Picture on the Internet (1992)** : Les Horribles Cernettes, a singing group formed by CERN graphic designer Michelle DiGenerro and three co-workers in 1990, became the subject of the first picture ever posted on the internet. In 1992, Tim Berners-Lee scanned their image and published it on info.cern.ch, establishing them as the first band on the internet. "And that's how the Cernets not only became the first band on the internet, but their image was also the first picture ever posted on the internet." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gP3oQeYEvso
**The First Commercial Recordings (1890s)** : Commercial recordings began to be released in the 1890s. However, due to the niche nature of the market at the time, it is not entirely clear who was the absolute first to issue a commercial record, though a few early hits are recognized. "The first commercial recordings were released in the 1890s. We're not entirely sure who was the first to issue a commercial record because this was such a niche thing to do at the time" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gP3oQeYEvso
**The First Commercially Available Recording (1888)** : In November 1888, the first commercially available recording was an Edison cylinder inserted into a talking doll that recited "Twinkle Twinkle Little Star." The doll retailed for $10, an "insane price" at the time. "It was a tiny cylinder, an Edison cylinder inserted into a talking doll that went on sale for the insane price of $10 in November of 1888 and she recites Twinkle Twinkle Little Star." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gP3oQeYEvso
**The First Flat Rotating Disc Record (1889)** : Emil Berliner, the inventor of the flat rotating disc, created the first record of this type in 1889, on which he recorded a German ballad. Though the original disc was later destroyed, a sound historian recreated its audio in 1890 by scanning a picture of the record found in a magazine. "Emil Berliner, the inventor that came up with the idea of a flat rotating disc in the first place, was the guy that made the first record as well." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gP3oQeYEvso
**The First Playable Audio Recording (1877)** : Thomas Edison made the first audio recording that could also be played back in 1877, using his new "talking machine" invention to recite "Mary Had a Little Lamb." Although the original recording was later destroyed during his experiments, it established the principle of sound reproduction. "The first audio ever recorded and played back was by Thomas Edison who recited Mary Haddle Little Lamb into his new talking machine invention in 1877." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gP3oQeYEvso
**The Flatlander Documentary** : A documentary titled "Flatlander" chronicles Trevor Hurst's experience working deep within the indigenous culture of the Kenna Poaca Dakota community in southwest Manitoba as a psychiatric nurse. "A documentary called Flatlander covers his experience." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jMfH9xAOpI
**The Genesis of Songs** : Beyond the meaning of lyrics, the stories of how songs are created are often rich with context, serendipity, accidents, and crazy coincidences that contribute to a great song's birth. These behind-the-scenes narratives reveal the circumstances that brought a piece of music to life. "Something happened for that song to be born. What was it? And what were the circumstances, the serendipity, the accidents, the crazy coincidences that needed to manifest for a great song to come to life?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DAjcCk9xYWk
**The Golden Age of Album Art (Late 1960s - Early 1980s)** : This period is recognized for the amazing caliber, quality, and artistic achievements in album design. Design studios like Hipgnosis, along with individual designers, created highly conceptual, surreal, and often puzzling artwork that encouraged deep viewer engagement and often influenced purchase decisions. "The golden age of album art was from the late 60s until the early 1980s. The caliber and quality and overall artistic achievements were amazing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pwebM3TfSdg
**The Good, The Bad, and The Ugly (1966 film)** : This Clint Eastwood film, released in 1966, provided thematic and musical inspiration for the Gorillaz song "Clint Eastwood." Bits of the film's theme are audible throughout the song. "He started that movie back in 1966. And as far as anybody knows, Clint Eastwood has never actually commented on the song, but he certainly never complained about it either." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iVdTCqpd798
**The Good, the Bad and the Ugly (1966) – Movie** : The classic 1966 Spaghetti Western film "The Good, the Bad and the Ugly," starring Clint Eastwood, is shown playing on the television set in the background of Oasis's "Definitely Maybe" album cover. Visible in the shot are co-stars Eli Wallach and Antonio Casal, as the film is Noel Gallagher's favorite movie. "The TV is playing the good, the bad and the ugly, a 1966 Pieddie Western starring Clint Eastwood, although we see coasters Eli Wallach and Antonio Casal in the shot. That's Nol's favorite movie." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**The Great Depression's Impact on Music Industry (1930)** : In 1930, the recorded music industry experienced a spectacular collapse due to the Great Depression, leading to a 90% to 95% drop in record sales. "Then in 1930, the recorded music industry collapsed spectacularly. The great depression set in and record sales dropped somewhere between 90 and 95%." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pwebM3TfSdg
**The Great Metric Shift to 4/4 Time (c. 1910)** : Prior to 1900, the most popular time signature in Western music was 3/4 time, or waltz time, exemplified by songs like "The Star-Spangled Banner" or "Take Me Out to the Ball Game." However, around 1910, a significant change known as the "Great Metric Shift" occurred, moving Western music's preference from 3/4 to 4/4 time. Musicologists largely credit ragtime, with its highly rhythmic, syncopated, and choppy nature, as the primary catalyst for this fundamental shift in musical rhythm. "But then, about 120 years ago, western music underwent something that might be called the Great Metric Shift." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_Pi7JLwAeOI
**The Great Migration and the Birth of Jazz and Blues (1910-1940)** : The Great Migration, occurring between 1910 and 1940, saw approximately 2 million African Americans move from the impoverished, racially discriminatory US South to the Midwest and Northeast, enabled by the expanding railroad system. This mass movement followed the abolition of slavery in 1865 by the 13th Amendment and years of living under Jim Crow laws. As they settled in cities like Memphis, St. Louis, Chicago, and Detroit, they brought their rich cultural traditions, including work songs, spirituals, gospel, and African musical influences, which collectively led to the birth of jazz and the blues. "And this led to the birth of two extremely important musical genres, jazz and the blues." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_Pi7JLwAeOI
**The Grey Ghost - Chrysler Tour Van** : In their early touring days, The Black Keys used a 1994 Chrysler van affectionately named "the Grey Ghost" to travel from gig to gig. "chugging from gig to gig in a 1994 Chrysler van that they called the Grey Ghost". ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PmwfC4HR3_I
**The Group of Seven - Art Movement** : The Group of Seven was an influential collective of Canadian landscape painters, whose work was notably influenced by artist Tom Thompson. Thompson's life and death, particularly his canoeing accident in Algonquin Park, inspired The Tragically Hip's song "Three Pistols." "an artist who would influence the group of seven" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oMBmdfrA47Q
**The Grunge Gold Rush** : Prompted by the accurate sales data from SoundScan, which highlighted the unexpected popularity of alternative rock, record labels "pivot[ed]" their strategies. This led to an aggressive pursuit of new alt-rock acts, initiating what became known as the "Grunge Gold Rush." "So this caused the record labels to pivot. If that's what was selling, well then let's go find more of it. And so began the Grunge Gold Rush." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pE7TaWSUa-o
**The Hunger (1983) Film** : This 1983 movie, starring David Bowie as a vampire, was a "big favorite among the Goth kids," particularly for a nightclub scene featuring Bauhaus performing "Bella Lugosi's Dead." "Now Bowie starred as a rather elegant vampire in a movie called The Hunger back in 1983." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**The Importance of Providing a Sense of Purpose** : The band members, when returning home from tour, quickly become restless after a few days off, seeking a "sense of purpose." They find that the "best and most interesting work" is creative, collaborative, and can be shared with the world, driving them to continuously work on new material and projects rather than resting for long periods. "After a few days, we're like, all right, what are we doing? Like, you know, like, we, we want to wake up every day with a sense of purpose." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kKNn01M8GG4
**The Innocence Project** : This non-profit legal clinic offers DNA testing services to individuals who have been convicted of crimes, providing a pathway for them to prove their innocence based on scientific evidence. "Dexter works with a charity called Innocence Project, which is a non-profit legal clinic that offers DNA testing for people convicted of crimes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QfiRRtsZoYU
**The Internet Underground Music Archive (1992)** : The Internet Underground Music Archive, launched in 1992 by computer enthusiasts at the University of California at Santa Cruz, is recognized as potentially the very first online repository of music. It featured early online bands like Death Specula. "This was launched by some computer nerds at the University of California at Santa Cruz in 1992. That may have been the very first online repository of music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gP3oQeYEvso
**The Internet's Commercialization and Impact (1990s)** : While the internet originated as a Defense Department project in the 1960s, its widespread civilian adoption began after Tim Berners-Lee introduced the World Wide Web in 1990. Mark Andreessen's 1993 browser, Mosaic (later Netscape), made it user-friendly. A pivotal moment occurred in 1995 when all restrictions on commercial use were dropped, opening the internet to the public and leading to a rapid transformation of music distribution through email, websites, file sharing, MP3s, torrents, iPods, and iTunes. "In 1995, all restrictions on commercial use of the Internet were dropped. All of the public was allowed in." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r8AazBVoS7g
**The KLF's Pyramid of Human Ashes** : The KLF, a renowned music and art collective, is undertaking a project in Liverpool to build a pyramid from the ashes of 34,592 deceased individuals. Participants can have their cremated remains transformed into a brick for inclusion in the pyramid, with a reservation fee of £99. "When you die, you get cremated and have your ashes formed into a brick. That brick will be added to this pyramid being built in Liverpool." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HoBx6s3JmVQ
**The Killers Sam's Town Cover (2006) – Anton Corbijn; Dead Stuffed Longhorn Sheep; Damaged Beauty Queen Production Assistant** : The Killers hired Anton Corbijn again for Sam's Town. Corbijn, put in a similar desert headspace as with U2, wanted a dead sheep for the shoot — the Longhorn is the official sheep of Nevada, and a live one would be too camera-shy and vain. He rented a dead, stuffed Longhorn from the local museum. Corbijn also wanted someone who looked like a "damaged beauty queen" and found production assistant Felice LaZay at the Las Vegas recording studio where the Killers recorded the album. He took Felice and the dead stuffed sheep to an abandoned mobile home in the blazing desert and photographed them. The best shot of the dozens taken became the album cover. "So he rented one, a dead one, a stuffed one from the local museum." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JdSUHkXM1mQ
**The Killers – Named After a Fictional Band in a New Order Music Video** : The name came from the name of a fictional band appearing on the bass drum in the video for New Order's "Crystal." "That was the name of a fictional band in the video." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**The Lonely Death of Charlie Wenjak (February 1967)** : This article in McLean's magazine, discovered by Gord Downie and his brother Mike, detailed the tragic story of 12-year-old Chanie Wenjack. Chanie, an Anishinaabe First Nations boy, ran away from the Cecilia Jeffrey Indian Residential School in Canora, Ontario, on October 16, 1966, hoping to walk 600 kilometers home. He died of exposure and hunger after walking for about 36 hours in late October, and his body was found by a passing train engineer. "This told the story of 12-year-old Cheney Wenjak, a young boy from the Inish Nabi First Nations, who was sent to the Cecilia Jeffrey Indian Residential School in Canora, Ontario." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dnz_Ycd_q7s
**The Loudness Wars (Early 2000s Peak)** : The "loudness wars" was a regrettable trend, peaking in the early 2000s, where songs were mastered with excessive compression to make them sound louder and "pop more" on radios and speakers. This technological race in the digital era flattened the dynamic range of original recordings, boosting quiet parts to be as loud as the loudest parts, creating an illusion of volume but resulting in distortion, lack of clarity, and significant loss of detail, leading to listener fatigue. Fortunately, pushback from organizations like Dynamic Range Day and streaming platforms (Apple Music, Spotify, YouTube) normalizing playback levels has reduced the incentive for loud mastering, though some "audio atrocities" like CD editions of Californication and Metallica's St. Anger remain. "The loudness wars refers to mastering songs with tons of compression, making them sound louder than they actually are." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZzIqIydZTlA
**The Loudness Wars Explained** : The "Loudness Wars" refer to a trend where artists, producers, and record labels aim to make their music sound louder by applying more compression to recordings, squishing the natural dynamic range of songs. This practice is driven by the belief that louder music stands out more, but it often leads to a lack of dynamic range, distortion even at low volumes, and listener fatigue. Key concepts include 'level' (technical sound pressure), 'volume' (subjective device-related setting), and 'loudness' (perceived difference between soft and loud parts, which increases with compression but not necessarily with level or volume). "artists, producers and record labels have gotten to this arms war over loudness. They believe that if something sounds louder it will stand out." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yl2poVWguu0
**The Loudness Wars Phenomenon** : Over the past two decades, the music industry has seen "the loudness wars," a trend driven by record labels, producers, managers, and artists insisting on maximizing the intrinsic loudness of recordings during the mastering process. This is achieved through aggressive audio compression, which "squeezes in as much musical data as you can," resulting in "really squished music" with significantly reduced dynamic range, making quiet passages sound as loud or louder than intended. Modern CDs can be up to "20 decibels louder than they were back in the 80s and 90s," which flattens subtleties, increases perceived distortion, and leads to "listener fatigue." This practice is based on the unproven belief that louder recordings stand out more and are more successful, and has been exacerbated by the rise of MP3s and low-quality earbuds. "The trend has become that to make the default loudness, the default force by which the music seems to come out of the speakers and headphones, to be as great as possible." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7Zniri9uSlc
**The MAPL System – Defining What Counts as Canadian Content** : Stan Klees developed the MAPL system (after a pie chart with four quadrants printed on Canadian releases): M = Music composed by a Canadian; A = Artist performing is principally Canadian; P = Production in a Canadian studio (or live Canadian broadcast); L = Lyrics written by a Canadian. A song qualifies as CanCon if it meets at least two of these four criteria. This creates surprising outcomes: Lenny Kravitz's "American Woman" qualifies because music and lyrics were written by Canadians (Burton Cummings and Randy Bachman), even though Kravitz was born in New York. Bryan Adams's "Everything I Do (I Do It for You)" did NOT qualify because collaborators Mutt Lange (British) and Michael Kamen (American) brought it below the two-point threshold — which led to the so-called "Brian Adams Rule" that now allows more leniency for international collaborations. "Still my least favorite record." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-JWO_jYXwEM
**The Magic Shop Studio** : The Magic Shop was a studio located literally steps from David Bowie's New York apartment, where he secretly recorded his 2013 comeback album *The Next Day* with a skeleton staff to maintain absolute secrecy. "No hangers on, no unnecessary people in the building at the magic shop. Name of the studio, which was literally steps from his apartment." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yq2A_o9-ulM
**The Mansion Recording Studio (351 Laurel Canyon Blvd)** : A historic mansion built in 1918 at 351 Laurel Canyon Boulevard served as the isolated, phone-free recording studio for the Red Hot Chili Peppers' "Blood Sugar Sex Magik" album in April 1991. Reputedly haunted with a history of death and gangster activity, its basement was converted into a studio by a Canadian company under producer Rick Rubin's direction. The house was once believed to be a residence of Harry Houdini, though this is unproven. "In April 1991, the Chili Peppers moved into a historic mansion at 351 Laurel Canyon, Boulevard that was built in 1918." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TESrEzVHLCc
**The Masked Wrestler Theobald Bauer (1865) – New York State Prohibited Wrestling Masks Until 1972** : The first professional wrestler to appear in a mask was Theobald Bauer, a French circus performer who began a masked wrestling career in 1865. The concept eventually took off in Mexico and Japan but was slow to arrive in the United States because a New York state law prohibited professional wrestlers from wearing masks during matches — a law that stayed in effect until 1972. "I have no idea why that law existed in the first place and it stayed in effect until 1972." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DiyIPBKiVW4
**The Me Too Reckoning in the Music Industry** : The music industry has undergone a slow but significant "Me Too reckoning" since around 2006, addressing decades of unchecked predatory behavior, sexual assault, and misogyny. Historically, the industry's "sex, drugs, and rock and roll" ethos allowed abusers to operate with impunity, shielded by wealth, privilege, and "fixers." The Me Too movement, which gained traction on MySpace around 2006 and spread across social media, empowered women to share their stories and expose abusers. This led to perpetrators being held accountable through legal action, "cancellation" of careers, increased attention to challenges faced by female performers, and efforts to create safer spaces. The proliferation of smartphones and video recording also made bad behavior riskier. While significant progress has been made compared to earlier eras, further work is still needed to dismantle deeply ingrained systemic issues. "It's the slow, me too reckoning within the music industry. For decades, predators, sexual assaults, and other awful people got away with the craziest and most vile things when it came to the treatment of women, stuff that goes far beyond, you know, usual misogyny." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZzIqIydZTlA
**The Modern Secondary Ticket Market** : The secondary ticket market is where tickets are resold after the primary sale, often after venues declare a "sell-out." Bot operators frequently act as wholesalers, selling large blocks of tickets to brokers, who then resell them to the public at marked-up prices. Companies like StubHub (owned by eBay, not Ticketmaster) facilitate this market, promoting themselves as online ticket exchanges. This market can serve as an "insurance policy" for buyers, allowing them to recoup costs or even profit if their plans change, especially for shows sold far in advance. There are theories that promoters might use this market to sell their own holdback tickets for extra profit. Speculative buying, where sellers offer tickets before they are officially on sale, is also common, though risky for buyers. Prices are dynamic, fluctuating based on demand and the artist's popularity, with about 50% of buying activity occurring in the 48 hours before a show. "The secondary market is where you go to buy a concert ticket after the venue says that it's sold out." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sPj3uxmz8H4
**The Muffin Man Nursery Rhyme's Alleged Origin** : The nursery rhyme "The Muffin Man" is alleged to have been inspired by Frederick Thomas Lindwood, a 16th-century baker who became a serial killer. Lindwood reportedly lured 16 children by attaching a muffin to a string and dragging it down the street, and also murdered seven rival pastry chefs. "It's alleged that it was inspired by a 16th century baker named Frederick Thomas Lindwood who turned into a serial killer." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**The Munsters Theme (1964)** : The theme from the TV show *The Munsters*, which debuted in 1964, is another example of how the surf sound extended beyond radio music into popular culture. "And then there was a theme from the Monsters, which debuted on TV in 1964." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pOeChe_D1Ag
**The New Pornographers – Named After a 1966 Japanese Film; Has Caused Misunderstandings** : Guitarist A.C. Newman caught the name from a 1966 Japanese film called The Pornographers. It was also a nod to bandmate Dan Bejar, who had a band called Destroyer with a track called "The Pornographers." The name has caused many real-world misunderstandings over the years with people assuming the band is involved in pornography. (Note: drummer Joe Seiders was later charged with possession of child pornography and is no longer with the band.) "The name has caused many misunderstandings over the years." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**The Nuggets Compilation (1972)** : This double album, compiled by guitarist Lenny Kay (who worked at a Greenwich Village record store), was released by Elektra Records in 1972. It compiled material issued between 1964 and 1968, serving to memorialize and codify the entire Garage Rock era. The compilation became essential listening for those disaffected with the state of rock in the early 1970s and heavily inspired the emerging punk rock movement. "It wasn't titled Nuggets. The compiler was a guitarist named Lenny Kay, who worked at a record store in Greenwich Village in New York City." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UpWz3X33vSY
**The Obelisk / Easy Cure (later The Cure) – The Cure's Pre-History** : The Cure were first called The Obelisk, then the Easy Cure, before finally settling on The Cure. "They turned into the easy cure and finally just the cure." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**The Oldest Playable Musical Recording (1888)** : An Edison cylinder recording from 1888 of 500 musicians and 4,000 singers performing Handel's "Israel and Egypt" at London's Crystal Palace is considered the oldest playable musical recording. This monumental recording captures a massive musical event of its era. "The oldest playable musical recording is thought to be an Edison cylinder of 500 musicians and 4,000 singers performing Handel's Israel and Egypt which was made at London's Crystal Palace." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gP3oQeYEvso
**The Origin of the Term "Record Album"** : In the early days of recorded music, long performances had to be split across multiple discs due to limited capacity. These discs were then gathered and packaged in books with sleeves, which resembled photo albums, thus giving rise to the term "record album." "This reminded people of a photo album, a book that contained a collection of pictures. Therefore it wasn't much of a stretch to call this collection of records, a record album." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gP3oQeYEvso
**The Phenomenon of Unexpected Digital Hits** : In the contemporary digital music environment, songs can achieve hit status and widespread popularity through non-traditional means, such as influential playlists or movie soundtracks, even without being released as official singles, receiving radio promotion, or being actively pushed by the band. This can lead to surprising scenarios where a band discovers a song's immense popularity years after its release, as observed by Sum 41 with their song "With Me," which gained huge YouTube numbers and was sung by entire festival crowds despite minimal band promotion. "And to have one of those now of our own, I see how it can happen." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=puFgo2ft7hQ
**The Pogo Dance** : The Pogo dance, a precursor to moshing, involves standing in place and jumping up and down. It emerged during the original punk rock era in the UK in the 1970s, partly as a necessity in venues without stages where jumping was the only way to see the band. Sid Vicious is often credited with its invention, either to mock non-punks or due to movement restrictions from his attire. "The most direct ancestor of mashing is the Pogo." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KMx4bmq8o2Y
**The Police – Stuart Copeland's CIA Father Made "Police" More Subversive** : "Police" was provocative in the punk era. Other names considered included Hypochondria, Grand Larceny, and Assault. "Police" won because drummer Stuart Copeland's father worked for the CIA, making the name feel doubly subversive. Interpol (the band) was subsequently named after the real international police organization headquartered in Lyon, France. "Police worn because drummer Stuart Copeland's father was in the CIA." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**The Power Station Recording Studio A (New York)** : Studio A at The Power Station in New York was a highly sought-after location for recording drums in the 1980s. Its large rooms with hard walls, floors, and ceilings were ideal for capturing the big, boomy, and powerful drum sounds that embraced echo and long decay, a key characteristic of the era's production aesthetic. "The sound of the room became as important as the sound of the drums, and one of the best places to record that kind of big, boomy, powerful drum sound was in Studio A of a facility in New York called the Power Station." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TsiYLGGSJ2U
**The Power of Music Video (1990s)** : The 1990s saw the music video industry expand significantly, building on MTV's launch in 1981. Initially skeptical, record labels soon recognized that music videos effectively "moved merchandise" and began spending vast sums on them, with budgets routinely exceeding $250,000 per video, charged back to the artists. Being featured on channels like MTV or MuchMusic could make a band "blow up." "The video channels controlled what we saw, and people sat in front of their TVs for hours, and hours and hours, waiting for their favorite video to come up." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r8AazBVoS7g
**The Power of Shared History and Conversation** : Memories can be unreliable, but engaging in conversation, especially within a group that shares a common history, can trigger a flood of forgotten details and serve as a cathartic, therapeutic, nostalgic, and enjoyable experience. "if you have a group of people with a shared history and they start talking, it's amazing what comes flooding back." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zBd13KtYlAA
**The Record Store Experience (Pre-Internet Era)** : In the era before widespread internet access, acquiring music involved a significant experience: leaving home, traveling to a record store, and physically browsing shelves. This process offered opportunities for discovery, interaction with new music, special ordering specific titles, and social engagement with friends or store clerks who often provided recommendations. "In order to acquire this music, you had to leave your home, find your way to a record store, and search through all the shelves hoping to find something." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jD1dWxNLvfE
**The Rocky Horror Picture Show Initial Flop (1975)** : *The Rocky Horror Picture Show* was a total commercial flop upon its initial release in 1975, and its soundtrack was also a disaster, leading to its deletion by record companies everywhere except Canada. It was only through Canadian imports and the emergence of midnight screenings that the film and its music found cult status and long-term success. "Rocky Horror was a total flop when it came out. The soundtrack was a disaster too and was deleted by the record company everywhere." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**The Slits – Self-Explanatory All-Female Punk Band Name** : The all-female punk band The Slits chose a name whose anatomical connotation was clearly intentional. "I'll leave you to figure that one out on your own." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**The Sound of Music Soundtrack Success (1965-1968)** : *The Sound of Music* soundtrack was an immense commercial success, spending 109 weeks in the US Top 10 between May 1, 1965, and July 16, 1967. It was the best-selling album in the UK in 1965, 1966, and 1968, and for years, the Guinness Book of World Records listed it as the best-selling album of all time, with an estimated 20 million copies sold. "It was a top 10 record in the US for 109 weeks between May 1st, 1965 and July 16th, 1967." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**The Sound of an Era and Societal Influence** : The prevailing sound of music in any given era is consistently influenced by and a little "downstream from what's happening in society at large." This connection means global events and societal shifts directly impact musical attitudes and outlooks. "the sound of an era's music is always just a little downstream from what's happening in society at large." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_yslM5oanRo
**The Spars Code for Digital Audio (AAD, ADA, ADD, DDD)** : Introduced in 1984, about a year after the CD, the Spars Code uses three letters (A for analog, D for digital) to indicate the type of audio recorder used for recording, mixing, and mastering. The first letter denotes recording method, the second for mixing, and the third for mastering. For CDs, the final letter is always D as it's a fully digital format. "This is something called the Spars Code. It appeared in 1984, about a year after the CD was introduced." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**The Stooges – Iggy Pop Named After an Iguana and a Drug Dealer Named Jim Pop** : Iggy was named "Iggy" because he once played drums in a band called The Iguanas. The "Pop" came after he shaved his eyebrows and someone pointed out he looked like a local drug dealer named Jim Pop. The "Stooges" came from the Three Stooges comedy group. "Then the Pop part came when he shaved off his eyebrows." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**The Stranglers - Concert Performance (1976)** : The Stranglers performed as part of a triple bill alongside the Ramones and Flaming Groovies at the Roundhouse in London on Sunday, July 4th, 1976. This concert was a key event for the "infant UK punk community." "The Ramones, who had released their debut album back in April, were part of a triple bill with the stranglers and the flaming grueves at a venue called the Roundhouse." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MTDY6qT11Dc
**The Studio as an Instrument (Late 1950s - 1970s)** : Developments in electric tape and multi-track recording made it possible to create entirely new soundscapes that could not be achieved in real-world live performances. This innovation effectively transformed the recording studio itself into a musical instrument, enabling new sonic frontiers. "The studio became an instrument for all these new sonic frontiers." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0fQQubt9UTI
**The Summer of Love (1988) in the UK** : The summer of 1988 is referred to as "The Summer of Love" in the UK, marking the beginning of a new dance culture—rave culture. This period saw English music fans enthusiastically embrace Detroit Techno, Chicago House, and the New York dance scene, coinciding with the popularization of the drug Ecstasy. "This was called The Summer of Love. The beginnings of a new dance culture in the UK." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OiqdZfxdBP0
**The Ten Commandments - Interpretation of "Thou Shalt Not Kill"** : There is confusion surrounding the sixth commandment (in Jewish, most Protestant, and Orthodox traditions; fifth in Catholic and Lutheran). While Catholics translate it as "You shall not kill," other major Old Testament religions use "You shall not murder," highlighting a significant difference in interpretation regarding the act of taking a human life. "There's apparently some confusion about one of the ten commandments, especially the one that says, thou shalt not kill. Exodus 20. In the Jewish most Protestant and Orthodox traditions, its commandment number 6. Catholics and Lutherans, it's number 5. And then there's the translation problem. Catholics aren't very specific. They say, you shall not kill. All the other major religions with roots in the Old Testament go with, you shall not murder. It's a difference." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZcYKZ5ovoy4
**The The – Deliberately Hardest Band Name to Search Online; Punk Trend of "The" Bands** : Singer Matt Johnson was advised by friend Keith that in the late 1970s, all new punk bands were "The [something]." So the most trendy and punk name would be "The The" — the ultimate example of the genre's naming convention. It was also nearly impossible to search for online when early search engines ignored definite articles. "What could be more trendy in punk than calling your group the the?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**The Tritone and Church Condemnation (323 AD)** : Around 323 AD, shortly after the Nicene Council, the early Christian church deemed the musical interval known as the Augmented Fourth, or Tritone, as an "evil" and "hideous" chord. It was believed to be composed by Satan, leading to the persecution of those who played it, with the severe consequence of "immediate burning at the stake." "Today we'd call it the Augmented Fourth, a type of musical interval known as the Tritone." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g-tBn8dsSqw
**The Troubles in Northern Ireland** : The period known as "The Troubles" involved intense fighting, clashes, and numerous deaths between Irish Protestants and Catholics in Northern Ireland, marked by shootings, nail bombs, and sniper fire involving the Irish Republican Army and the British Army. "It was the time of the troubles fighting between Irish Protestants and Catholics." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cM3-tINKdvo
**The Tube (British TV Show)** : This British TV program was the setting for Michael Hutchence and Paula Yates's first meeting in 1985, where their undeniable connection first emerged. "In 1985, Hutchins was a guest on the British TV show The Tube." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=23OgreyK66o
**The Voice as a Platform** : The television show "The Voice" serves as an "incredibly gracious platform" for artists, especially mentors, to promote new records and gain public visibility. It offers a contemporary, albeit unconventional, route for aspiring singers to develop their careers, differing from traditional club circuits. "It means an incredibly gracious platform with which to announce that I have a new record." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lGNrJgwQjgg
**The Wall for Generation X** : Billy Corgan's ambitious declaration that The Smashing Pumpkins' *Mellon Collie and the Infinite Sadness* would be "The Wall for Generation X" (referencing Pink Floyd's *The Wall*) was perceived as highly pompous at the time, given the mid-90s cultural climate. "Before the Pumpkins' third album came out, he was insistent that they were going to release, and this is a quote, The Wall for Generation X." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Dm5N6yDRxXQ
**The White Stripes – Meg White's Love of Red and White Peppermint Candies** : When Jack met Meg, she was obsessed with red and white peppermint candies. The original band name idea was The Peppermints, but since their shared last name was White, they went with The White Stripes. "She really, really liked red and white peppermint candies." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**The Whole Is Greater Than the Sum of Its Parts** : In certain musical contexts, the collective synergy and talent of a band as a unit far exceeds the individual output or success of its members once they pursue solo careers. This phenomenon is observed when individual solo endeavors are "spotty" and do not approach the level of the group's achievements. "the whole is actually greater than the sum of its parts." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-f9jhqAHkQo
**The Worst Song Ever Scientifically Engineered – 20 Minutes; Only 200 People Would Like It** : Three scientists (Vitaly Komar, Alex Melamid, and David Soldier) gathered data on musical elements that people dislike and used AI to engineer the worst song possible. The resulting 20-minute composition features cowboy lyrics, bagpipes, bossa nova synth beats, a children's choir, nonsensical spoken word, a man yelling through a bullhorn, and more. Their calculation: only approximately 200 of 8 billion people on earth would find it pleasurable. "Only about 200 of the 8 billion people on this planet would find their composition pleasurable." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**The Worther Effect (Copycat Suicides)** : The Worther Effect describes a phenomenon where widely publicized suicides, particularly those of well-known musicians, can lead to an increase in "copycat" suicides among fans. Kurt Cobain's death is cited as an example, while Marilyn Monroe's death saw a 12% rise in US suicides, making it the most pronounced example. "When a well-known musician commits suicide, psychologist warn of copycat suicides by fans. This is known as the Worther Effect." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**The Wrecking Crew – Legendary LA Session Musicians** : The Wrecking Crew was a renowned group of Los Angeles-based session musicians who contributed to "literally thousands of songs," including hits for Phil Spector girl groups, The Beach Boys, The Partridge Family, and Simon & Garfunkel. Wendy Melvoin's father, Mike Melvoin, was a pianist in this influential group. "Her father is pianist Mike Melvoine. He was part of the famous group of LA musicians known as The Racking Crew, who played on literally thousands of songs from the Phil Spector girl groups to the Beach Boys to the Parkridge family to Simon and Garfunkel." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hbNJQ6OYmd0
**The Young Aborigines (later Beastie Boys) – Beastie Boys' Original Name** : The Beastie Boys were originally called The Young Aborigines. "The young Aborigines, who became the Beastie Boys." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Theory of Early Rock Artists Claiming Best Riffs (1960s-1970s)** : A theory posits that during the late 1960s and early 1970s, as guitar amplifier and recording studio technology modernized, rock music was still young and developing. This allowed talented bands like The Beatles, The Rolling Stones, Led Zeppelin, and Black Sabbath to discover and claim the best, most basic rock riffs and melodies first due to an "accident of the space-time continuum" or "accident of birth." "And just because they were around bands like the Beatles and the Stones and Led Zeppelin and Black Sabbath got to all the best rock riffs and rock melodies, first, it's an accident of the space-time continuum." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wYD_dIvYY5o
**Thomas Edison's Phonograph** : Thomas Edison devised the phonograph, an early sound storage machine that used a cylinder coated with hard wax to record sound in grooves. "While Thomas Edison figured out how to store sound in grooves, he used a cylinder coated with a hard wax and his machine was called the phonograph." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IIJySSo276k
**Thomas Edison's Phonograph (1877) – Not Originally Conceived for Music** : Thomas Edison's phonograph, patented in 1877, was not initially conceived as a device for music reproduction. Edison listed music very low on his enumerated potential uses, prioritizing speech preservation, dictation, and remote learning. "Edison listed music reproduction very low on his list of potential uses." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OrHtPVBjXw4
**Thomas Wilhelm's Murder of Girlfriend (Year not given)** : Thomas Wilhelm fatally shot his live-in girlfriend after listening to Guns N' Roses' "Used to Love Her (but I Had to Kill Her)" on repeat while he was drunk. This incident highlights the song's tragic association with real-world violence. "That same song resulted in the death of the live-in girlfriend of Thomas Wilhelm. He was drunk and listened to the song on repeat before he shot her." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Thought So At The Time (c. 2013)** : This mystery song has absolutely no known information surrounding it, beyond the placeholder title "Thought So At The Time" which was given to it by searchers. The online search for its identity began in 2013. "There is zero information about it. The placeholder title given to it is, Thought So At The Time, the search began in 2013." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UsPoPUByjYw
**Thrasher Magazine** : This magazine played a crucial role in connecting skaters with the associated bands, covering both skateboarding and the new breed of punk acts from regions like Oxnard, Orange County, and San Francisco. "They often connected through a magazine called Thrasher, which not only covered skating, but the band's associated with skateboarding." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=44dXy0StjIQ
**Three Body Problem (Music Theory)** : The "Three Body Problem" in physics is referenced to explain the increased complexity in predicting music trends due to the rise of Rap, Hip Hop, and R&B as powerful genres, alongside Rock and Pop, disrupting the traditional two-genre cycle. "This adds a third element to our music cycle theory, and that makes things more complicated. Just look up a physics principle called the Three Body Problem." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Three Types of Bands** : There are three types of bands: the extreme (doing anything for attention), the traditional (earnest and honest), and the quirky, eccentric, and weird. The third type, which Weezer falls into, makes music fun and unpredictable, balancing uniqueness with widespread appeal. "The way I see it, there are three types of bands that stretch across a spectrum." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LYRPxtP61JM
**Three Types of Bootleg Recordings – Audience, Soundboard, and Studio Outtakes** : There are three main categories of bootleg recordings: audience recordings (variable quality, with ambient crowd noise), soundboard recordings (professionally mixed, clean audio), and studio outtakes (demos, alternate takes, and rehearsals — the most coveted type for their insight into the creative process). "The three types are: audience recordings, soundboard recordings, and studio takes (demos, outtakes, rehearsals — the most valued)." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Uly2cT2vc28
**Thunderbirds (TV Show)** : The fictional "Plastic Island" headquarters for Gorillaz, depicted in the "Plastic Beach" album, was explicitly modeled after "Tracy Islands" from the classic Supermarionation TV show "Thunderbirds." "It was modeled after Tracy Islands. Now, if you know anything about the old Super Mario Nation TV show called Thunderbirds, you know exactly what Damon and Jamie were thinking about as this album came together." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iVdTCqpd798
**Tiatrophone Expansion and Decline** : The Tiatrophone, or Electrophone in the UK, saw services roll out across Europe and was even demonstrated in New York. It operated as a subscription service for live music until 1931, when it was ultimately rendered obsolete by the advent of radio, which offered "wireless" transmission. "The Tiatrophone services rolled out across Europe. It appeared in the UK under the name Electrophone. And the technology was even demonstrated in New York." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Ticket Guardian and Concert Ticket Insurance** : Ticket Guardian is a company that has proposed an innovative solution to the complexities of the secondary ticket market by offering concert ticket insurance. For a small fee, such as three dollars per ticket, this insurance remains active until the show's start time. If a ticket holder cannot use their ticket for any reason, they can enter its number into the Ticket Guardian app. The ticket is then invalidated, the buyer receives a full refund credited to their credit card, and the promoter regains the ticket, which can then be resold at its current market rate. "Their idea is to offer concert ticket insurance." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sPj3uxmz8H4
**Ticket Pricing Mechanics and Responsibility** : Concert ticket pricing is a complex process involving economic projections, estimations, and considerable guesswork. An artist's manager negotiates with a promoter on a show-by-show basis, considering market demand, available inventory, and the desired earnings from the tour. "Hard costs" for a stage show typically account for 30-50% of the ticket price, with the remaining revenue split approximately 85% to the act and 15% to the promoter. Artists may receive an advance or a guaranteed amount per show or tour to cover startup costs. Ticketmaster, as the agent for venues, adds a "service fee" to cover its costs and a small profit. Additionally, a "venue fee" for electricity, security, and staffing, and various taxes (including special amusement taxes) are added. The actual base ticket price is set by the promoter and artist, not Ticketmaster, yet Ticketmaster often receives all the blame for high prices, acting as a "deflector of criticism" to insulate artists from fan wrath. "Ticketmaster has almost nothing to do with the price of a concert ticket other than their service fee. Yet it actually gets all the blame." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sPj3uxmz8H4
**Ticketmaster Acquisitions (1998-2000)** : Ticketmaster expanded its market presence through strategic acquisitions. In 1998, it absorbed City Search, and in 2000, it purchased Ticket Web, further consolidating its position as the internet became increasingly prevalent for ticket sales. "Ticket master absorbed a company called City Search in 1998. Another ticketing company called Ticket Web was bought in 2000." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Ticketmaster Service Charges Breakdown** : When purchasing tickets through Ticketmaster, customers encounter several fees in addition to the ticket's face value. These include taxes (for the government), a facility fee (collected for venues), a convenience charge (also known as a service or order processing charge, which is where Ticketmaster generates most of its profit, sometimes shared with venues/promoters/artists, often based on a 4% margin of the ticket price), and a delivery charge. "In addition to the face value of the ticket, you might be hit with a convenience charge, a facility charge, an order processing fee, and a shipping charge plus tax." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TsiYLGGSJ2U
**Ticketmaster Website Launch (June 1995)** : Ticketmaster launched its first website in June 1995, initially serving as a searchable database of events without direct purchasing capabilities. Users still had to call a phone line or visit a physical outlet to buy tickets. "Ticketmaster's first website was launched in June 1995." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Ticketmaster's Dominance and Monopoly (Established 1976)** : Ticketmaster, established in Arizona in 1976, has grown through expansion and acquisition to become the largest primary seller of tickets globally, including acquiring Ticket Fraud in 1991. Its near-monopoly on concert sales has led to numerous legal challenges, most notably by Pearl Jam in 1994, although that case collapsed. Now owned by Live Nation, the world's largest concert promoter, Ticketmaster's control over the market has intensified. "Through expansion and acquisition, Ticketmaster has become the largest primary seller of tickets in the known universe." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sPj3uxmz8H4
**Ticketmaster's Emergence and Strategy (Early 1990s)** : Ticketmaster emerged as the dominant computerized ticket seller by 1991, outlasting rivals like Ticketron. Its success stemmed from its approach as a ticket service utilizing computers, rather than a computer company dabbling in tickets, and its reputation for a "rock-solid backend" in technical operations. "We finished the last episode in 1991 with ticket buster emerging as the dominant seller of concert tickets." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Ticketmaster's Free Hardware/Software Model** : Ticketmaster provided its clients (venues, promoters) with ticketing software and hardware for free. This model was financially sustainable through the imposition of service charges on every ticket sold, which covered Ticketmaster's costs and generated profit without taking a cut of the ticket's face value. "Like some of its predecessors, Ticketmaster gave its clients the software and hardware for free." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Ticketmaster's Past Home Printing Fee** : Historically, Ticketmaster imposed a charge (e.g., $2.50) for customers to print their own tickets at home, justifying it as a fee for the "convenience" of instant access rather than waiting for mailed tickets. However, this practice was successfully challenged and discontinued due to intervention from consumer watchdogs. "But here's the thing. Ticket master used to charge you for the privilege of printing your own tickets at home. Why? Because they could. The company's thinking was, well, if we make it possible for you to have instant access to your tickets instead of waiting for them to be mailed to you, that's worth something, right? $2.50, please. Fortunately, though, consumer watchdogs had that little trick squash." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TsiYLGGSJ2U
**Ticketmaster's Vertical Integration Strategy (2008-2009)** : Ticketmaster pursued vertical integration within the concert industry, aiming to own key elements of the music business to become stronger. This strategy led to the acquisition of artist management company Frontline Management in 2008 and the major merger with Live Nation, the biggest concert promoter, in February 2009, forming Live Nation Entertainment. "Plus, ticket master began to see the possibilities of vertical integration within the concert industry." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Ticketron's Introduction of Electronic Box Offices (1969)** : Ticket Reservation Systems Inc., later known as Ticketron, was a pivotal player in modernizing concert ticket sales. Established in 1969, it pioneered the use of electronic box offices. This innovation eliminated the need for pre-printed tickets that were previously randomly distributed to various physical box office locations, marking a significant step towards computerized ticketing. "They were the first to use electronic box offices that eliminated the need for pre-printed tickets that were then randomly distributed to physical box offices." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sPj3uxmz8H4
**Ticketron's Role in Ticketing History** : Ticketron was a predecessor in the computerized ticketing space that eventually became defunct and was later absorbed by Ticketmaster. It was characterized as a "computer company that sold tickets," contrasting with Ticketmaster's identity as a "ticket service that made use of computers." "Ticketmaster was the former, the defunct Ticketron was the latter." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Tiered and VIP Pricing** : Concert ticket pricing has evolved to include tiered pricing, where seats closer to the stage cost more, and VIP sections that offer additional benefits like soundcheck access or meet-and-greets for even higher prices. This allows artists to maximize revenue from higher-demand seating and unique fan experiences. "First, there was tiered pricing. Better seats closer to the stage cost more. Believe it or not, that wasn't always the case." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**Tim Burton's Goth Aesthetic Connection** : It is suggested that director Tim Burton's artistic vision, heavily influenced by Goth aesthetics, would not exist without the Goth subculture. "I mean, if there wasn't such a thing as Goth, director Tim Burton wouldn't exist." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**Tim Burton's Influence** : The goth scene is credited as a foundational influence for film director Tim Burton's aesthetic and cinematic style. "Would there be a Tim Burton, if not for the Goth scene? Nope." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XaLsZFLrMnI
**Time Healing Wounds for Reunions** : The passage of time often helps to mellow out interpersonal conflicts and allows band members to reconsider past animosities, paving the way for potential reunions. "The time tends to mellow people out." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DJGFUlQTUhQ
**Time to Play All Unique Combinations** : If one were to play a new combination of notes every second, it would take an astounding 33 billion, 63 million, 236,360 years to exhaust all one quintillion non-repeating sequences using the 12 notes of the chromatic scale. This is approximately two and a half times the age of the universe, demonstrating the immense, though finite, potential of musical expression. "if you played a new combination of notes every second, it would take you 33 billion, 63 million, 236,360 years, which is about two and a half times the age of the universe." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L09R2evjyMc
**Times Square Hotel - New York Dolls' First Gig** : The New York Dolls played their very first gig at a "sleazy hotel in Times Square" in the spring of 1972. This venue served as the initial stage for their shocking and provocative image, complete with makeup, spandex, and fishnets, as they introduced their unique take on glam rock. "Their first gig was at a sleazy hotel in Times Square." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0xd0Hf5REg4
**Tin Pan Alley Origin in New York City** : The term "Tin Pan Alley" emerged around 1885 to describe a concentrated area of music publishing companies and songwriters along West 28th Street in Manhattan, between 5th and 6th Avenue. The name is popularly attributed to a grumpy writer for the "New York Herald" who, hearing the cacophony of numerous cheap upright pianos playing different songs simultaneously from open windows, likened the sound to "someone was banging crappy tin pans together." Another account from a 1930 book suggests a journalist coined the term after observing an expensive piano modified for a percussive sound, commenting "that piano sounds like a tin can." "It is said that a grumpy writer in the New York Herald, a newspaper of the day, described the sound being made by all these cheap upright pianos, all playing different songs, was as if someone was banging crappy tin pans together. Hence tin pan alley." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TsiYLGGSJ2U
**Tina Bell's Posthumous Recognition Efforts** : At the time of Tina Bell's death in 2012, efforts began to recognize her legacy, including a documentary and memoir titled "Conversations with the Grunge Queen." Her son, TJ, an Academy Award-winning documentary co-producer, is actively involved in these efforts to ensure her contributions are acknowledged. "At the time of Tina's death in 2012, there was an effort to finally recognize her legacy with a documentary in memoir called Conversations with the Grunge Queen." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Tina Bell's Racial and Gender Barriers** : One theory for Tina Bell and Bam Bam's lack of recognition in grunge history is that audiences and critics might have been unable to accept a black woman fronting a "punky rock band in a man's world." This suggests that race and gender played a role in their being overlooked. "I mean, a black woman, fronting this kind of punky rock band in a man's world. Were audiences and critics unable to grasp such a person doing such a thing? Possibly." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Tina Turner Music for Bird Control at Airport** : An airport situated in Gloucestershire, UK, successfully resolved an issue with birds on its runway by implementing an unconventional solution. They continuously blasted music from Tina Turner, twenty-four hours a day, effectively deterring the birds from the area. "With an airport and Gloucestershire in the UK had a problem with birds on the runway, they solved that by blasting them with music from Tina Turner, twenty four hours a day." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**Tinplate Sheet Metal Production (Late 18th Century)** : By the late 18th century, "Tinplate sheet metal" was a widely used product for various applications, including roofing and carriages. It was valued for being cheap, lightweight, fireproof, and tough, and could be pressed very thin with razor-like edges. "By the latter part of the 18th century, a product called Tinplate sheet metal was being used for everything from roofing to carriages." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**Tiny Early UK Punk Scene (Late 1975)** : Before the Sex Pistols' TV appearance, word of the New York punk scene reached the UK by late 1975, attracting only "dozens" of young people who kept mostly to themselves and were largely ignored by others, forming a "tiny, tiny, tiny scene." "By the end of 1975, word had drifted across the Atlantic that something was happening in New York. Some kids picked up on this, but they kept mostly to themselves, and they were pretty much ignored by everyone else." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Toilets Flush in the Key of E-flat** : It is reported that the sound of most standard toilets flushing resonates in the musical key of E-flat. "Most standard toilets flush in the key of E-flats." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0D4wGWI-_z8
**Tom Morello of RATM – Harvard Political Science Degree; Bandmate in a Band With Nobel Chemistry Prize Winner** : Tom Morello holds a degree in political science and social studies from Harvard. In college, he played in a band called "Board of Education" (spelled B-O-R-E-D) with a woman named Carolyn Bertozzi, who won the Nobel Prize for Chemistry in 2022 for the development of click chemistry and bioorthogonal chemistry. "Tom Morello of rage against the machine has a degree in political science and social studies from Harvard." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**Tom Morello's Acting Role in Iron Man (2008)** : Tom Morello has ventured into acting, notably appearing in the movie Iron Man. In the film, he plays "the first terrorist to be killed when Tony Stark breaks out of the cave prison." "If you watch the movie Iron Man, he's the first terrorist to be killed when Tony Stark breaks out of the cave prison." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=t2uXHP9cBdM
**Tom Morello's Ownership of St. Vincent's Signature Guitar** : Tom Morello is mentioned as one of the notable guitarists who owns an Ernie Ball Music Man St. Vincent Signature Model guitar. "Tom Morello has one." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oLC49nCf0ZM
**Tom Thompson - Artist** : Tom Thompson was an artist whose work influenced the Group of Seven. He tragically died at the age of 39 while canoeing through Algonquin Park, an event that served as inspiration for The Tragically Hip's song "Three Pistols." "The song itself is about Tom Thompson, an artist who would influence the group of seven, and who died at age 39 while canoeing through Algonquin Park." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oMBmdfrA47Q
**Tommy Gallagher's Abusive Parenting** : The father of Noel, Paul, and Liam Gallagher, Tommy, was an alcoholic who physically abused his children. His abuse was so severe that it caused Paul and Noel to develop stuttering problems during their youth and left lasting emotional scars, particularly on Liam. "They had a bad relationship with their father, Tommy. He was an alcoholic. He beat his kids." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_b3KRS-Yx_s
**Tonearm Tracking Improvement** : Thicker, flatter records with deeper grooves, such as 180 gram virgin vinyl, lead to more accurate tonearm tracking. "Tonarm tracking is more true, and the grooves can hold more information, meaning that there's the potential for higher quality audio." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Tony Blair - Music Fan Prime Minister** : Tony Blair, the new Prime Minister in the mid-90s, was a young leader who was not only a music fan but had also been in a rock band during his university days, symbolizing the cultural shifts of the Britpop era. "The new Prime Minister was Tony Blair, a young guy who was not only a music fan, but it would also been an rock band himself back in university." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DSvoppf1dGY
**Tony Blair's Britpop Connection** : Prime Minister Tony Blair, who was elected during Britpop's wane, was a fan of the genre and even invited Noel Gallagher to 10 Downing Street. This interaction, however, created "bad optics," suggesting the "music of the people" had been co-opted by the establishment. "Tony Blair was elected Prime Minister. Back in university, he played an arachbat, and he loved Britpop." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5q5EnZj1FwE
**Tony Tiller – Godsmack Album Model** : Tony Tiller is the woman with red hair and piercings who graces the cover of Godsmack's 1998 self-titled album. Her photograph was taken in Brooklyn in 1994, during a period when she was immersed in the club scene. She only discovered her image was used when the album was released, and she currently lives off the grid, bald, barefoot, and pursuing eclectic interests. "Her name is Tony Tiller." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Tool – From a 1994 Interview; Possibly Just Short for "Toolshed"** : The best explanation comes from a 1994 drummer Danny Carey interview stating the band conceived of themselves as a tool for understanding the (non-existent) science of lacrimology — the study of tears. It is more likely just a shortened form of their original name, Toolshed. "The band has always loved to twist the truth." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Top 10 Bass Players Criteria** : The list of influential bass players is based solely on "influence," rather than technical adaptness, focusing on individuals who inspired many aspiring musicians. "So here's the criteria. Influence. That's it." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kbFfT0pKchI
**Top 40 Radio Development** : The principle of tracking song popularity via jukeboxes was later applied to radio, leading to the creation of Top 40 radio, which played the 40 most popular songs repeatedly. "This principle was later applied to radio, top 40 radio, where the 40 most popular songs of the day were played over and over and over again, and it was a massive success." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Top 40 Radio – Invented in Omaha by Todd Storz After Observing Jukebox Repetition** : The origin of top 40 radio traces to Todd Storz and Bill Stewart's efforts to rescue a failing Omaha AM station called KOWH. One story holds that around 1951 they observed bar patrons repeatedly playing the same songs all night on a jukebox, inspiring the idea of doing the same on radio. A more documented version: Storz ran a New Orleans station and, competing against a rival show called "The Top 20 at 1280," launched "The Top 40 at 1450" one hour earlier. When he moved to Omaha, he expanded the concept to 24 hours. KOWH went from a 4% to a 45% market share within six months. The format spread worldwide and dominates commercial radio to this day. "You can thank or blame Todd Storz for top 40 radio." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iyGUXdDqIlI
**Top Album Choices for Mars** : If only three albums could be taken to Mars, the selections would be Nine Inch Nails' "Pretty Hate Machine," Joy Division's "Unknown Pleasures," and the first album by The Stone Roses. "if I had to take three albums with me to Mars, it would be pretty hate machine, the first 90s nails album, unknown pleasures by Joy Division and the first Stone Roses album." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yl2poVWguu0
**Tori Castellano and The Donnas** : Tori Castellano, the drummer for the band The Donnas, was involved in a failed romantic relationship with Tre Cool. "There's also a failed romance with Tori Castelano, the drummer for a band called the Donas." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nyfdGYWelic
**Toronto Hard Rock Cafe Event (2017)** : On March 12, 2017, U2 fans from the Toronto area gathered at the Hard Rock Cafe in downtown Toronto to commemorate the 30th anniversary of "The Joshua Tree" album. The event featured an interview with the album's co-producer, Daniel Landwom. "On March 12th, 2017, U2 fans from around the Toronto area gathered at the Hard Rock Cafe in downtown Toronto." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L03Yk1SHRNM
**Toronto International Film Festival Award** : The animated film accompanying Angels & Airwaves' album "The Dreamwalker" garnered an award at the Toronto International Film Festival. This recognition highlighted Tom DeLonge's success in multimedia projects beyond traditional music albums. "An album called The Dreamwalker came with a short animated film that won an award at the Toronto International Film Festival." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q-QP7ePxkQE
**Toronto Punk Triangle** : The small but significant punk scene in Toronto established itself as the "third leg of a triangle" that connected New York and London, characterized by a constant exchange of bands with New York and mandatory stops for UK touring acts. "And Toronto ended up forming the third leg of a triangle that extended through both New York and London." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Toronto's Urban Decay (Mid-Late 1970s)** : Similar to New York, parts of Toronto were experiencing decay, with Yonge Street described as a "sleazy place where no respectable person went." "And in some ways, Toronto was rotting like New York. Young Street was a CD-place where no respectable person went." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Toto Toilet Sound Decorator** : Toto, a Japanese company specializing in bathroom fixtures, offers a toilet model equipped with a feature called the "sound decorator." This device electronically masks potentially embarrassing bathroom sounds, providing users with a greater sense of privacy. "Total, the Japanese bathroom fixtures company has a toilet that comes with something called the sound decorator, which electronically masks any embarrassing bathroom sounds." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**Tour Bus Experience** : Graduating from a van to a tour bus is considered a significant upgrade, offering more comfort, reduced accommodation costs, and providing a private, sanctuary-like space that becomes a home for months. Tour coaches can range from basic setups with bunks, a TV, and VCR to luxurious "penthouses on wheels" equipped with full bathrooms, complete kitchens, video game systems, and state-of-the-art audio/video systems with satellite dishes that function even when moving at 60 mph. "If your career is going well enough, you may be able to graduate from traveling in a van to your own tour bus." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DgN0vBIugLc
**Tour Bus Layout** : A standard tour bus layout includes a driver's compartment, a common area with tables, seating, a fridge, stove, microwave, and AV system, a hallway with sleeping bunks (typically three per side), a bathroom with possible shower facilities, and a rear bedroom featuring a double bed, more seating, and amenities. "The layout of a tour bus is pretty standard." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r6vVw66-gmI
**Tour Finance and Advances** : When signing a recording contract, bands often immediately go into debt due to an advance check from the record company, which can range from a few thousand to several million dollars. This money is intended to cover expenses such as new gear, stage clothes, album recording, and tour financing (including transportation, accommodation, and road crew). An advance is a loan that must be repaid, typically through album sales, meaning a band may not see any profit from album sales for years as the record company "claws back" the advance. Touring is a strategy to counteract this debt by encouraging album sales and generating direct income from tickets, cover charges, bar sales percentages, and merchandise like t-shirts, which bands get to keep. "An advance is nothing more than a loan that has to be repaid." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DgN0vBIugLc
**Touring Hardships and Backstage Sanctuary** : Touring is depicted as arduous, involving months away from home, little sleep, bad food, substance use, late nights, temptations, and difficult interpersonal dynamics. In this environment, the dressing room becomes a "sanctuary," offering artists a measure of control and comfort amidst the chaos of the road. "For that time on the road, your sanctuary really becomes the dressing room, the one place over which you have some measure of control." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=z2hvUDtBxho
**Touring Van Experience** : The cheapest and most common method for bands, especially in their early careers, to travel between gigs is by using a used van. These vehicles are often described as crowded, stinky, and prone to breaking down, yet they are the starting point for virtually every band. "The cheapest way is with a used van. That's virtually every band gets going." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DgN0vBIugLc
**Tracking and Payouts of Official Plays** : Streaming platforms meticulously log every official song play, compiling these statistics monthly to calculate and distribute payments to artists based on their previously discussed percentage share formula. "The streaming platforms are able to log each and every song with an official play." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Tracy Bradford's Record Collection** : Dave Grohl's cousin, Tracy Bradford, owned an "incredible" record collection featuring valuable punk singles, including first pressings from Misfits and Minor Threat, which would be worth a "mint" today. "She had this incredible record collection, which it would be worth a mint today, I'm sure, because it's all, you know, the first misfit single, the first minor threat single, these things that you go down to bleaker bobs, you see like 400 bucks on pop." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8ynrwaUi-sA
**Traditional Artist Development** : Historically, record labels could afford to invest in an artist's long-term development, allowing bands to mature and refine their sound over multiple albums, often 2, 3, 4, or even 5 records, rather than demanding immediate success. "Back in the day when music was harder to come by, a record label could afford to wait for a band to develop and mature through 2, 3, 4, 5 albums." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ooNAHswiT5Q
**Traditional Cultural Gatekeepers (Early 2000s)** : At the turn of the millennium, traditional cultural gatekeepers, primarily record labels, controlled what music reached the public. Labels filtered new music, signed artists deemed financially or artistically viable, and managed marketing and global distribution, creating an "artificial scarcity" that also maintained quality by excluding amateurs. "At the beginning of the 2000s, the traditional cultural gatekeepers ruled." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Traditional Headbanging in Sufi and Koali Music** : Headbanging-like movements have a long history outside of rock music, associated with traditional Islamic Sufi music and Koali music from India and Iran for centuries. These movements are linked to worshipers entering trance-like states. "A similar activity was associated with traditional Islamic music in the Sufi tradition. Koali music from India and Iran has also employed head banging movements." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bferkEWvJg0
**Traditional Music Industry Ecosystem (Pre-Internet)** : The traditional music industry operated as a symbiotic ecosystem where record labels, radio stations, record stores, music magazines, and music video channels mutually supported each other. These institutions, operating at their own expense, sold and promoted label-offered products, leading to a system that effectively discovered and distributed music to the masses and generated substantial profit for approximately 100 years. "And the result was an ecosystem designed to discover and disseminate music to the masses, all while making oodles of money along the way." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Traditional Record Deal Duration** : Historically, a first record deal typically spans around seven albums, a structure designed to provide both security for the artist, allowing them time to develop and mature their sound over several releases, and an opportunity for the record label to see a return on their significant upfront investment. "Traditionally, when you sign your first record deal, it's usually for around seven albums." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vt6DVj7S6nk
**Tragically Hip – From a Michael Nesmith VHS Skit (1981) and an Elvis Costello Song (1982)** : Michael Nesmith of the Monkees released a VHS tape of comedy sketches in 1981 called Elephant Parts, which included a piece called "The Tragically Hip." Gord Downie then heard the phrase in an Elvis Costello song called "Town Cryer" from the 1982 album Imperial Bedroom. Two references to the phrase felt like a sign. "The two references to the phrase seemed to be like some kind of sign." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Trainspotting (1996) Soundtrack Acclaim** : The soundtrack for the brilliant 1996 film *Trainspotting*, which depicted doomed Scottish heroin addicts, "exploded off the screen." Its first volume has since appeared on lists of the best movie soundtracks of all time, acclaimed for its mix of old and new rock and electronic music that captured both desperate and uplifting themes. "In fact, the first volume has appeared on lists of the best movie soundtracks of all time." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**Trainspotting 2 (1997) Soundtrack Release** : The immense success of the first *Trainspotting* soundtrack led to a second volume being released in 1997. This follow-up featured songs that either didn't make the cut for the first volume or didn't even appear in the movie, yet it was ranked "almost as good as the first one," demonstrating the strong appeal of the film's musical curation. "The first Train Spotting soundtrack was such a big hit that a second appeared in 1997, featuring songs that didn't make the first volume, along with songs that didn't even appear in the movie." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**Transistor Radio (1954)** : The transistor radio first appeared on the market in 1954, marking a significant advancement in portable audio technology and initiating the long journey towards ubiquitous portable music. "We have come such a long way with portable music since that first transistor radio appeared on the market in 1954." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**Transistor Radio (1954) – Portable Device That Made the Teenage Cultural Revolution Possible** : Until 1954, radios were large pieces of furniture that provided communal family listening experiences. The transistor radio changed everything: pocket-sized, portable, and — crucially — equipped with an earphone that allowed private listening. It arrived at precisely the same moment as rock and roll and the newly constructed social category of the teenager. Young people could take their forbidden music anywhere, out of sight and out of earshot of parents. "The transistor radio and rock and roll arrived at exactly the same time. This new social and economic construct called a teenager could then take his or her music with them everywhere." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iyGUXdDqIlI
**Transition Interlude - "I thought I was mistaken"** : A brief musical interlude featuring the lyrics "I thought I was mistaken. I thought I'd get your worst. Tell it how you do I feel. Tell it now how do I feel?" "I thought I was mistaken. I thought I'd get your worst. Tell it how you do I feel. Tell it now how do I feel?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xc85sLj0Cnk
**Transition from Analog to Digital Studios** : Recording studios were predominantly analog for a long time, relying on massive reel-to-reel machines. The shift to hard drive systems linked to Digital Audio Workstations (DAWs) like Pro Tools began in the very late 1980s and early 90s, though experiments with full digital recordings date back to 1973. "Recording studios were very analog for a very long time. It wasn't until the very late 1980s and early 90s that they started switching away from massive real-to-real machines to hard drive systems linked to a digital audio workstation like Pro Tools." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**Transition from Big Bands to Smaller Combos (Post-WWII)** : After World War II, the big band era ended, and musical trends shifted towards smaller ensembles of four to six people. This change, coupled with returning soldiers, reduced the need for female musicians who had filled in during the war, leading to a resurgence of sexist attitudes and fewer opportunities for women in music.
