"By the time World War II ended, the big band era was over. The trend was towards smaller combos for four, five, or six people." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9GHpC0kCgHY
**Transition from Visual to Audio Storytelling for Music Video Directors** : The hosts of the "Art Context" podcast, experienced in visual storytelling through music videos, are adapting to an audio-only medium. They aim to tell stories about the "maker" (the director/creator), their philosophy, influences, highs, lows, and breakthrough moments, giving listeners a "close set behind experience" of the creative process. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Trend Towards Shorter Songs on Streaming Platforms** : Spotify's algorithms increasingly favor shorter songs because listeners tend to stick with them longer. This incentive drives artists to write shorter tracks, as more listens translate to more money. The average song length on the Billboard Hot 100 decreased by half a minute from 2000 (4 minutes) to today (3 minutes 30 seconds), with a rising number of songs under two and a half minutes. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Trend of Artists Selling Song Catalogs** : Towards the end of the 2010s, a notable trend emerged where musicians began selling their entire life's work – their song catalogs and publishing rights – to large corporations for tens, and in some cases, hundreds of millions of dollars. These "huge deals" became a significant business story in the music industry. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Tribal Tattoos as a Fading Trend** : Dave Grohl remarked that tribal tattoos, popular in the 1980s, have become outdated, comparing them to "wearing stone wash jeans" in terms of fashion relevance. "It's funny because in the 80s, it was like, yeah, tribal tattoos. I mean, that now, that's like wearing stone wash jeans." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jZeMmPekKMQ
**Trilobites** : Trilobites were among the planet's most successful early animals, appearing over 500 million years ago and surviving for more than 270 million years. There are believed to be at least 17,000 different types of these beetle-like creatures that once populated the oceans. "We think that there were at least 17,000 different types of these beetle-like things, applying the oceans beginning over 500 million years ago and we think they survive more than 270 million years." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VMPLplhDk8I
**True Nature of Goth Community** : The vast majority of people associated with Goth culture are described as "gentle, literate people" who enjoy associating with like-minded individuals, similar to other musical scenes. "The truth is that the vast, vast majority of people associated with Goth culture are gentle, literate people who just like to hang out with like-minded folks." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**Tune2.com** : In 1999, a team of six individuals created a program for Tune2.com, which functioned as a customizable internet radio station. This site also featured a prototype of Professor Selzer's "Celestial jukebox" concept, named Aladdin. "In 1999, a team of six guys created a program that was used on a site called Tune2.com." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Turkmenistan Ban on Lip Syncing (2005)** : In 2005, Turkmenistan's President Sapa Maradnazov outlawed lip syncing indefinitely, stating it had a "negative effect on the development of singing and musical art." This ban followed his earlier prohibitions on opera and ballet in 2001, which he deemed not to "correspond to the national mentality." "Back in 2005, the Badpube Crazy President, Sapa Maradnazov, said that lip syncing created, quote, a negative effect on the development of singing and musical art, and therefore outlawed it in Turkmenistan forever." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**Turntableism Skill** : Turntableism refers to the "ability to manipulate sounds and create cool effects club DJ style," showcasing the technical artistry involved in DJing beyond simply playing records. "That ability to manipulate sounds and create cool effects club DJ style." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=amHre9ZZFkU
**Turntableism and Creating New Music** : Turntableism describes the technique of "chopping records into sounds that are then manipulated to something totally new." Skilled DJs became so adept at this that they transformed turntables and mixers into genuine musical instruments capable of creating brand new compositions from fragmented sounds, without needing expensive studio equipment. "Now today we would call this turntableism. Chopping records into sounds that are then manipulated to something totally new." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OiqdZfxdBP0
**Twenty One Pilots – From Arthur Miller's Play "All My Sons" About 21 Pilots Who Died** : Singer Tyler Joseph was studying Arthur Miller's World War II–set play All My Sons in film class, about a man who knowingly allowed defective plane parts to go into service, killing 21 pilots. The lack of a hyphen in the band name remains unexplained and annoying. "I've never found a reason why there is no hyphen in the band name 21 Pilots." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Twilight Soundtracks' Significance** : The *Twilight* soundtracks were deemed significant enough that they "should have probably made the main list" of key alt-rock soundtracks. Comprising multiple discs, these soundtracks heavily featured artists like Muse (influenced by the author Stephenie Meyer), Paramore, Linkin Park, and others, mostly through album cuts and outtakes, offering a "really nice selection of tunes." "There were a bunch of Twilight discs to go with the movies." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zIkjd1OjCXM
**Two Fly Studio (Sheffield)** : In 2003, the Arctic Monkeys began recording their initial demos at Two Fly, a local studio in Sheffield. The band recorded a total of 22 tracks there over two years. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YvcnHKI4oIw
**Two Solitudes - Concept** : The concept of "two solitudes" refers to the distinct cultural and linguistic divisions between Quebec and the rest of Canada, a theme explored by Canadian political writer Hugh McClellan. This idea was referenced by The Tragically Hip in their album "Fully Completely." "in his works in two solitudes of Quebec and the rest of Canada" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oMBmdfrA47Q
**Two Types of Live Bootleg Recordings – Audience vs. Soundboard** : There are two primary categories of live bootleg recordings: audience recordings (captured with concealed microphones, raw and ambient) and soundboard recordings (tapped directly from the venue's PA system, offering clean, professional-quality audio). "There are mainly two categories: audience recordings (raw, with public atmosphere) and soundboard recordings (connected directly to the PA, offering clean audio)." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OrHtPVBjXw4
**Two Types of Music Copyrights** : There are two main types of music copyrights: the composition (the song itself, written as notation) and the sound recording (the audio representation, the master). The composition copyright usually stays with the artist, while the sound recording copyright usually resides with the record label. "If we look at music, there are two types of music copyrights. The first is the composition, which is the song itself... Then there's the sound recording. This is the audio representation of the song... The master sound recording is your finished product" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Two-Person Band Novelty (2004-2005)** : In the years 2004-2005, the concept of a two-person band, such as USS, was considered "a bit odd" and relatively new. Despite the emergence of groups like The White Stripes and The Black Keys, a minimalist lineup was still uncommon, making USS's eventual success as a duo particularly noteworthy. "the idea of a two person band was fairly new in 2004, 2005." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ayPJfDAF_to
**Types of CD Singles (5-inch and 3-inch)** : Two main types of CD singles existed: the standard 5-inch CD, which typically contained 1 to 5 songs, often including bonus tracks unavailable elsewhere; and the 3-inch mini CD (or CD3), introduced in 1987, which could hold up to 20 minutes of music. The smaller CD3s were largely gone by 1985 (likely meant later than introduced) and were problematic with self-loading CD players without an adapter ring, sometimes becoming unplayable or trapped. "There were two types of CD singles. The first one was the same size as a regular CD, but had between 1 and 4, maybe 5 songs on it. The actual single plus a couple of bonus tracks that often weren't available anywhere else. The second type of CD single was just 3 inches in diameter, which was introduced in 1987." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**Tyranny of Endless Choice in Music** : While Napster initially offered an "endless buffet of music," the abundance eventually led to confusion and the "tyranny of endless choice," causing listeners to constantly wonder what they were missing and to "skip, skip, skip, skip, skip" through songs. "It was the tyranny of endless choice." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**U2 The Joshua Tree Cover (1986) – Anton Corbijn; 20-Minute Rest Stop in Mojave Desert; Joshua Tree Died and Fell ~2000** : While finishing The Joshua Tree, U2 hired rock photographer Anton Corbijn and flew to Los Angeles in December 1986 to shoot in the Mojave Desert near Death Valley, driving around in a hired bus and staying in crappy motels. When someone asked the bus driver about the strange cactus-like plants, he said they were called Joshua trees — Bono thought it was a great name for a tree and they stopped for 20 minutes to take photos. When examining the contact sheets, those 20-minute rest-stop shots were judged the best, somehow capturing and encapsulating everything the band was about. U2 never revealed the specific tree's location to prevent vandalism. Despite precautions, the tree died and fell over sometime in fall 2000, about 200 years old. Intrepid fans have since found its remains; some leave a ring of stones in the shape of a heart. "When it came time to examine the contact sheets of all the photos that have been taken, the shots from that 20 minute rest stop were judged to be the best." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JdSUHkXM1mQ
**U2 – Name Suggested by Steve Averill of Radiators from Space; Chosen from a List of Ten** : When Adam Clayton called Dublin ad agency worker Steve Averill asking for a band rename (from The Hype), Averill suggested a list including U2 because abbreviations were fashionable (XTC, etc.). The band chose U2. Averill is now U2's art director. "Steve is now working as U2's art director." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**UFO Cover-Up Theory** : The belief that there is a government cover-up regarding UFOs is cited as an "oldie but a goodie" among conspiracy theories. "The UFO covered up to an oldie but a goodie." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pmKjZzypzvg
**UK CD Single Market** : CD singles were particularly popular in the UK, largely due to the high price of full compact discs compared to other markets. Megastores like Virgin and HMV on Oxford Street in London stocked numerous CD singles, some containing 3-6 extra songs, blurring the lines with EPs but sold as singles. "CD singles were especially big in the UK because the price of a full compact disc was really, really high compared to other markets around the world." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBCEmZ4Jheo
**UK Club Culture Diversification** : Over a few years, British club culture diversified into many distinct scenes, including acid, tribal, hardbag, Balearic, trance, drums and bass, and jungle. This fragmentation reflected the rapid evolution and creative energy within the electronic dance music landscape. "Anyway, over the next few years, British club culture split into many different distinct scenes. There was acid and tribal and hard bag and balleric, trans, drums and bass and jungle and a bunch of others." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OiqdZfxdBP0
**UK Economic Devastation (1975)** : In 1975, Great Britain faced an economy even worse than that of the US, still recovering 30 years after World War II with visible bombed-out buildings. The government, burdened by a welfare state derailed by the 1973 oil crisis, ran out of money. Industrial production was in steep decline, leading to three-day workweeks and widespread union strikes, culminating in the Bank of England exhausting its foreign reserves and requiring an IMF bailout. "As bad as the economy in the US was, it was worse in the UK. Even though the war had been over for 30 years, the empire had still not recovered." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**UK Music Industry Advantages for Indies** : The UK music industry provides several advantages for independent artists to penetrate the mainstream more easily than in North America. These include the UK's smaller geographical size, national radio networks like BBC Radio 1 dedicated to pop and rock, national TV music programs such as Top of the Pops, and influential weekly music press like the New Musical Express, which actively champions unknown bands. "But because of the makeup of the UK music industry, an Indie artist has a better shot of seeping into the mainstream than they do in North America." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ft8nruhWCas
**UK Musicians Union vs. Synthesizers** : On May 23, 1982, the UK Musicians Union publicly expressed grievances over the increasing use of synthesizers, which they believed threatened the jobs of "real musicians." This culminated in a motion passing at a union meeting that called for a ban on all synthesizers, a stance that was met with ridicule from the music press and fans. "On May 23, 1982, which just happened to be the 48th birthday of Synth Pioneer Bob Mogue, the UK Musicians Union went public with their grievances." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5PJRCC6EwUY
**UK Scheduled Blackouts (1970s)** : As a consequence of the UK's economic crisis, scheduled rolling blackouts were implemented, with areas receiving prior notification of when their electricity would be cut, forcing residents to adapt daily routines like cooking around these outages. "There were scheduled rolling blackouts. Areas would receive notification as to when the electricity would be cut." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**UK Socio-Economic Climate in 1977** : In 1977, particularly in the UK, the economy was severely struggling, unemployment rates were soaring, especially among young people, the British Empire was in decline, and the entrenched class system was under threat. "The economy was in the toilet, unemployment was skyrocketing, especially amongst young people." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gKrAXMG8sTc
**UK Unemployment and Hopelessness (1970s)** : The severe recession in the UK led to soaring unemployment, crushing many people with hopelessness and forcing millions to "resign themselves to a life of living on the dole." "Some people were crushed by this recession. They couldn't find work, unemployment, soared, hopelessness set in." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**UN Treaty Threatening Podcasting (Past Decade)** : At one point in the past decade, a proposed UN treaty from the World Intellectual Property Rights Association aimed to prohibit the aggregation and mirroring of online content, regardless of its copyright status. Had it passed, this treaty could have rendered podcasting illegal. "The UN's World Intellectual Property Rights Association wanted to prohibit the ability to aggregate and mirror content online, whether it's copyrighted or not." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**US Cultural Attaché Missions (1950s)** : Under President Eisenhower in the 1950s, the CIA initiated cultural attaché missions. These involved sending American entertainers to international hotspots to showcase American culture beyond consumerism, inject American ideology into local populations, and embed CIA agents within goodwill tours for espionage. These missions continued for decades. "This sneaky bit of subterfuge, as it's roots in the 1950s, when the CIA, under President Eisenhower, were involved in a series of cultural attaché missions, sending American entertainers to hotspots to A, prove that there was more to American culture than Pepsi and baseball, B, inject a little American ideology into the local population, and C, embed CIA agents in these goodwill tours, who would do whatever Spooks do." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YeNWDtN_sHE
**US Historical Censorship of Music – Jazz Banned on Sundays (1920s), R&B Called "Dirty Music" (1930s–50s)** : Despite the First Amendment, the United States has a long history of attempting to censor music. In the 1920s, there were efforts to ban jazz on Sundays on the grounds that it was "evil." In the 1930s through 1950s, rhythm and blues — music primarily by Black artists — was routinely described as "dirty music." "In the 1920s there were attempts to ban jazz on Sundays as 'evil,' and in the 30s–50s R&B was called 'dirty music.'" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=f4TOWLYRnGs
**US Housing Bust** : The housing market collapse in the United States impacted musicians like Tom DeLong (Blink 182), who faced significant financial losses trying to sell a large, expensive home. "Tom DeLong and his wife have been caught up in the housing bust in the US." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yR3xc3ra5tE
**US Justice Department's Antitrust Scrutiny (1991-1995)** : The US Justice Department began monitoring Ticketmaster following its 1991 merger with Ticketron. Their interest intensified with Pearl Jam's public disputes over service charges, leading to an encouraged antitrust complaint in 1995, though the investigation ultimately closed without significant government action, with a promise to continue monitoring the industry. "They'd been keeping tabs on ticket master since the 1991 merger with Ticketron." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8q2bRZyHnHE
**US Music Scene (Early 1990s)** : In the US during the early 1990s, the music landscape was dominated by grunge and a punk revival, alongside the Lollapalooza generation which also embraced industrial music and hip hop. "The US was mainly about grunge in the punk revival, but also the Lala Pulosa generation." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vzyMSSlD7Yg
**US Radio Airplay Royalties for Cover Songs** : In the United States, artists performing cover songs on American radio do not receive airplay royalties. These royalties are exclusively directed to the original composers and publishers of the song, meaning bands like Hootie & the Blowfish only benefited from mechanical royalties (sales) for their covers. "if you are an American artist on American radio and you're playing a cover, you don't reap any radio airplay royalties from that because it only goes to the composers." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=W9gdKha0PLg
**US Radio Royalty System Limitations** : In the United States, when songs are played on commercial radio, stations only pay fees to the songwriters and the publisher. No royalties are paid to the performers or the holder of the song's copyright, based on the belief by US radio stations that their airtime provides promotional value. "But when songs are played on American radio, the only fee stations pay are to the songwriters and the publisher." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Ubiquity of Cell Phones at Concerts (Post-2007)** : Before the advent of smartphones around 2007, concert tickets often carried warnings against cameras and recording devices, reflecting a strict industry stance against bootlegging and unauthorized photography. However, with the rise of smartphones, enforcing these rules became a "losing battle," leading promoters and artists to generally accept or adapt to their presence. Reactions vary from making concerts "Instagramable and TikTok Ready" (e.g., Coldplay) to strict crackdowns by artists who demand fans be "in the moment" (e.g., Green Day) or even requiring phones to be locked in bags (e.g., Jack White). This shift has also created a "generational divide" between younger fans who integrate phones into the concert experience and older attendees who find it disruptive, becoming part of a larger conversation about technology in public spaces. "But with the rise of smartphones after about 2007, this became a losing battle." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xid8CZf4oYo
**Ugandan Radio Stations Shut Down for Promoting Witchcraft** : Only Radio Stations, with its twenty-three outlets in Uganda, faced government-mandated shutdown. The charges against the stations were for promoting and advertising witchcraft. "Only radio stations twenty three outlets in Uganda were shut down by the government on charges of promoting and advertising witchcraft." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**Unabomber as Subject of Songs** : Songs about the Unabomber can be easily found, reflecting the public and artistic interest in his crimes and manifesto. "There's also no problem finding songs about John Wayne Gacy, Jack The Ripper, the Unibomber and Richard Ramirez, the Night Stalker." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FtGJHhqXcDI
**Uncharted Podcast** : The Uncharted podcast, which debuted in October 2023 with episodes released every two weeks, is a true crime podcast. It explores the intersection of music with various criminal activities, including crime, murder, robberies, strange deaths, disappearances, and organized crime. "This is a true crime podcast that debuted back in October of 2023 and it covers crime, murder, robberies, weird deaths, disappearances, the mob and a ton of other instances where music intersects with crime and baddies." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4xxgPX-aR90
**Uncharted: Crime and Mayhem in the Music Industry** : A podcast that explores true crime stories in the music industry, including robberies, plane crashes, cults, mysterious disappearances, and murders. It delves deeply into various instances of bad behavior within the music world. "Again, if you like the idea of stories of true crime and music, check out my podcast Uncharted, Crime and Mayhem in the Music Industry." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q6dAX6HCjgw
**Undead Persistence of Goth** : The Goth music and subculture proved enduringly resilient, described as "just as undead as some of their subjects of fascination," highlighting its sustained presence beyond initial trends. "But the music and the subculture proved just as undead as some of their subjects of fascination." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**Underground Goth Preference** : Many Goth bands and their fans prefer to remain "deep underground," indicating a subcultural preference for exclusivity and niche appeal. "Most bands have remained deep underground, which is where they and their fans prefer to remain." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evlGhfUe6QQ
**Underground Punk Network** : Dave Grohl was captivated by the underground punk network revealed to him by his cousin Tracy, which included fanzines, pen pals, and flyers from shows extending across the country, even from distant locations like San Diego and Alaska. "I just fell in love with the network, like the fanzines, and just this network, the underground network, and how everyone was connected, and everyone, you had pen pals, and she had flyers from shows, and you know, San Diego." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8ynrwaUi-sA
**Underserved Cities and Towns as Tour Stops** : The North-South touring flow meant punk and hardcore bands frequently played in underserved cities and towns, including the Seattle area, which were often off the grid for major touring acts. This accessibility of punk shows in such regions contributed to the local music culture, eventually crossbreeding with hard rock influences to form grunge. "The North South flow of punk and hardcore bands meant that these groups played a lot of underserved cities and towns when it came to tours. They had to play somewhere so they would play anywhere." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PEHkKfmmDiI
**Unfair Competition** : This is a legal charge related to commercial activities, often brought alongside copyright infringement, implying that one party is unfairly benefiting from another's work. "He charged Trent with copyright infringement and unfair competition." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wYD_dIvYY5o
**Unfettered Consumer Choice and Spread Attention** : The current era of unlimited and unfettered consumer choice means that the public's attention is spread much thinner, leading to less agreement on which acts should receive time, attention, and money, making it harder for any single act to achieve widespread consensus like in the past. "Instead, the public's attention has been spread thinner and thinner and thinner." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Unfortunate Sonic Coincidences** : These refer to situations where two distinct songs end up sounding remarkably similar despite what should be extremely astronomical odds, suggesting independent creation rather than deliberate copying. The internet's reach has made people more aware of such coincidences. "Things were, despite what should be very astronomical odds, we end up with two songs that sound very, very similar." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L09R2evjyMc
**Unidentified "Steve" Retires from Music (October 30, 1997)** : On October 30, 1997, an individual named Steve made the decision to retire from music. "Steve decision to retire from music on October 30th 1997." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8UIBWyUt38g
**Unintentional F-Bombs on Record (1964, 1968)** : Before any deliberate F-bombs, there were alleged unintentional instances on record. Dave Davies of The Kinks supposedly uttered one during the 1964 recording of "You Really Got Me" due to anger, and during The Beatles' "Hey Jude" (1968), John Lennon or Paul McCartney might have exclaimed "F-ing hell" after hitting a bum note. "When it comes to unintentional F-sharps, we can look at the King's song You Really Got Me. Someone, maybe John, maybe Paul, hits a bum note at about two minutes and 57 seconds into the song." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gP3oQeYEvso
**Unintentional Musical Element** : The ending of Blink 182's "Feeling This" included an engineer's mistake where a fade-out command was forgotten for the vocals. The band chose to keep the error, noting it sounded "like the beach boys." "That extra by the way was a mistake. The engineer had set up the studio to fade out the song at the end, but forgot to apply that command to the vocals." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tkLRJiOBu0
**Union Leader's View on Rock and Roll** : A union leader in Britain expressed strong disapproval of rock and roll, claiming that "The British public doesn't want any rock and roll, only a few silly kids do." This statement reflects the dismissive attitude of established figures towards the genre and its audience. "The British public doesn't want any rock and roll, said one union leader, only a few silly kids do." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Union of Sound Synthesists Formation** : In direct response to the UK Musicians Union's call for a ban on synthesizers in May 1982, an opposition party named the Union of Sound Synthesists was formed. This group countered the perceived absurdity of limiting technological progress, standing up for electronic music and its practitioners. "The whole situation did not go down well. It prompted the formation of an opposition party called the Union of Sound Synthesists, and the Music Press and Music Fans called this idea the product of a bunch of loonies." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5PJRCC6EwUY
**United States Annexation of Hawaii (1893)** : The United States annexed the Kingdom of Hawaii following the overthrow of Queen Liliukalani in 1893. This annexation sparked a mainland craze for Hawaiian culture, including its music and instruments. "the United States was sinking its hooks into the Kingdom of Hawaii, which it had annexed following the overthrow of Queen Liliukawani in 1893." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**Universal Decline in Record Sales** : No artist, including established "Heritage Acts" like Pearl Jam, Green Day, Nine Inch Nails, and Metallica, is currently selling records in the same numbers as they did in the past, and such high album sales (e.g., 20 million copies) are never expected to be seen again. "None of them are selling records in the same numbers they used to in the past." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**University of Leeds - Britpop Symposium** : The University of Leeds began offering a symposium on Britpop, indicating the genre's academic and cultural recognition during its peak. "heck even the University of Leeds began offering a symposium on Britpop." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DSvoppf1dGY
**University of Oregon AI Music Competition (1997)** : In 1997, a competition at the University of Oregon challenged music experts to identify a computer-composed piece among three performances, one of which was generated by EMI (Experiments in Musical Intelligence) in the style of J.S. Bach. Although the experts correctly identified the computer piece, they also believed it to be an actual Bach composition, highlighting AI's growing ability to mimic human creativity. "In 1997, there was a competition at the University of Oregon. Three pieces were performed in front of an audience of music experts." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QFtS8fl553Y
**Unknown Mystery 60s Group (1960s)** : A reel-to-reel tape found in a flea market in Philadelphia contained a series of songs by unknown musicians, seemingly recorded in the 1960s, with song titles listed and two photos inside the box. Distortion Records later released these songs under the name "Unknown Mystery 60s Group," issuing two other volumes, though questions persist about whether the group was real or a modern fabrication. "It contained a series of songs by unknown musicians that appeared to have been recorded in the 1960s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UsPoPUByjYw
**Unnamed Band's Debut Album Sales (from Dualtone)** : A band, whose identity is not specified, released a debut album that sold three million copies. This album was a product of the indie label Dualtone. "A band that's only three million copies of their debut album, the products of an indie label called Dualtone." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sOU54d8mr5k
**Unnamed Song - Exponential Licensing Earnings** : One particular song, despite being a "huge single on its own," earned "exponentially more" through its extensive licensing for various TV commercials, including campaigns for Maxwell House coffee and All-State insurance, demonstrating the lucrative potential of commercial placements beyond standalone release sales. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Unnamed Voice Actor/Singer** : The person who voiced Tony the Tiger, the iconic Frosted Flakes character, also performed all the singing throughout the classic holiday special, *How the Grinch Stole Christmas*. "The guy who voiced Tony the Tiger, the frosted flakes character, is also the same person who sang throughout the holiday classic How the Grinch Stole Christmas." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=72Lei7uug8c
**Unprofitable Streaming Services** : Despite being the primary revenue source for labels, streaming services like Spotify, Apple Music, and Google Play are unprofitable because licensing deals compel them to pay out over half of their income before any other expenses. However, streaming is now "too big to fail" for the music industry. "And even though Spotify, Apple Music, Google Play and all the rest of them are unprofitable, thanks to the licensing deals that they've been forced to take, which sees them pay out more than half of all they take in." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**Unusual Artist Merchandise of 2020** : In 2020, artists diversified their revenue streams with a variety of interesting merchandise. This included jigsaw puzzles from The Clash, ACDC, and Nirvana; Kanye West cosmetics; Grateful Dead deodorant (smelling of "matured"); Alice Cooper bread; chocolate milk; Deftones beer; Anthrax bourbon and hand sanitizer; Motorhead candles (smelling of whiskey and smoke); Sigeru CBD products; Cradle of Filth T; and soap from Varg Vikernes (Uranus), a notorious black metal figure murdered by a bandmate. "twenty twenty, was the year when artists looked for new ways of bringing in revenue. That meant a lot of interesting merch. And here is a short list of actual things you can now buy." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HoBx6s3JmVQ
**Upgraded Audio CDs (SACD/DVD-Audio)** : Certain CDs offer enhanced audio quality, such as specialized versions included in box sets (e.g., DVD versions) or the now less common Super Audio CDs (SACDs). These discs are produced with higher sampling rates and bit rates, often 96 kilohertz 24-bit, and are noted for their superior sound quality. "you can buy CDs that have upgraded audio, a DVD version that comes in a box set for example, or maybe a now hard to come by super audio CD." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2g4QSREzpw
**Urban Migration due to Bauxite Mining** : While selling land to bauxite miners was profitable, it resulted in a significant demographic shift as "more and more young people were moved out of the country where the Boxite mines were set and into the city." This rapid urbanization had profound social consequences. "Selling land to Boxite miners proved to be very profitable, but as a result, more and more young people were moved out of the country where the Boxite mines were set and into the city." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zNIVqKqAlnk
**Urban Poverty and Ghettos in Jamaica** : The influx of unskilled young people into Jamaican cities led to poor job prospects and their settlement in ghettos, specifically mentioning "trench chown and Kingston." This poverty contributed to political unrest and, ultimately, the emergence of ska music. "But because they were unskilled, the jobs they could get in the city weren't very good, and they settled in ghettos like trench chown and Kingston." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zNIVqKqAlnk
**Uzi Vert's Planet Ownership Plan – Wasp-127b, 332 Light Years Away** : Rapper Uzi Vert (known for having a $24 million diamond embedded in his forehead) declared his intent to buy the gas giant planet Wasp-127b, 332 light years from Earth. "He once declared that he wanted to buy his own planet." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**Value of Album Ownership** : In the past, buying an album was considered an investment, creating a fiscal relationship between the listener and the artist. This investment encouraged listeners to stick with an album longer, delve deeper into the artist's work and songs, and avoid the feeling of wasted money or not fully appreciating the music. "If you bought an album, damn it, that was an investment. You paid money for it, which created a fiscal relationship with the artist." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4PlieAEthSg
**Vampire Weekend – Band Member Ezra Koenig Filmed a Low-Budget Horror Movie of That Name** : More than two years before the band formed, Ezra Koenig was the cameraman for an ultra-low-budget horror film called Vampire Weekend. "He was behind the camera for an ultra low budget horror film." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Vancouver's Premier Studio** : Founded by Bruce Fairbairn in Vancouver, Little Mountain Sound was developed into one of the greatest recording studios in North America. "Bruce built his Vancouver recording studio, which is called Little Mountain Sound by the way, into one of the greatest recording studios in North America." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkSKxm_U9Yc
**Vanity Label Definition** : A vanity label is an imprint specifically created for a particular artist. While it gives the impression that the artist has their own record company, in reality, it functions as an arm of a larger major label. "You might even come across on the called Av vanity label, which is an imprints set up for a specific artist." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcADOuQUiAQ
**Vanity Labels** : Vanity labels are a specific type of boutique label, often indie-looking imprints, that are set up by major labels on behalf of important artists. These labels grant artists additional creative control or a platform to develop other talent, all while remaining within the major label system. "Some boutique labels are also vanity labels. Indie-looking imprints set up on behalf of important artists." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ft8nruhWCas
**Variety Magazine's Criticism of Rock** : *Variety* magazine famously described rock music as "the most destructive force in the country," characterizing it as a "lewd, lascivious, and larceness influence on youth." This powerful condemnation reflects the widespread moral panic and cultural clash that rock generated. "*Variety* magazine said it was the most destructive force in the country, a lewd, lascivious, and larceness influence on youth." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L0j9iDuHQVA
**Varying Manager Commission Structures** : While a typical manager's commission is around 15% of an artist's earnings, arrangements can vary. Paul McGuinness, for example, negotiated a 20% commission with U2, which was considered "unusually high." However, in this case, the deal also stipulated that all profits would be shared equally among the five individuals involved, demonstrating a commitment to the band's collective success. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PzyvXmDKFUc
**Vasilie Archipop – Prevention of Nuclear War (1962)** : On October 27, 1962, during the height of the Cuban Missile Crisis, Vasilie Archipop, the second in command on the Soviet Foxtrot-class submarine B-59, prevented a global nuclear war. The B-59, armed with nukes, was under attack from the USS Randolph, which was dropping depth charges. Despite damage to the sub and the crew's desire to retaliate, Archipop refused to agree to launch missiles, citing a lack of proof that war had officially begun and the immense risk of an all-out nuclear war. His decision overruled the sub commander and political officer, saving humanity. "Yet, we do not fire. We have no proof that we are at war. But if we're wrong, if we launch, we risk starting an all-out nuclear war and wiping out all life on planet Earth." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6SdPPsmpgJg
**Vast Majority of YouTube Videos Have Low Views** : A significant majority of YouTube videos, specifically 88.4%, have fewer than one thousand views. "88.4% of YouTube views have less than one thousand views." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HoBx6s3JmVQ
**Vast Music Content Archive** : YouTube provides an immense repository of music content, including user-uploaded concert footage, archival music videos, interviews, performances from obsolete formats like VHS, and ripped audio from obscure vinyl and tapes, making even the most obscure songs widely accessible. "There is an insane amount of music content available through the video and the streaming platform, YouTube music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**Vastness of Streaming Libraries** : With an internet connection and a suitable portable device, users can instantly access an expansive library of over 75 million songs and counting from virtually any location worldwide. Video content is also readily available through platforms like YouTube on mobile devices. "As long as you have an internet connection and the right kind of portable device, you can instantly access somewhere beyond 75 million songs and counting from wherever you are on the planet. And if you want the video, that's no problem. Get that on your phone too. Everybody's got a YouTube app, right?" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**Velvet Underground – Lou Reed Found a Sadomasochism Novel in a New York Gutter** : Lou Reed found a trashy paperback novel about sadomasochism in a New York City gutter. It was called The Velvet Underground. "That was the best thing that ever happened to author Michael Lee." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Venezuelan Cultural Protection** : Venezuela implements cultural protection laws for music, requiring radio stations to play two Venezuelan records for every foreign song broadcast. This policy aims to promote domestic music within the country's airwaves. "Last I heard for every foreign song or radio station plays, it has to play to Venezuelan records." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dMsEj62KFEw
**Venusian Feature Naming Rules** : Due to its murky atmosphere, features on Venus are difficult to observe. However, scientists have agreed that any discovered features must be named after females, such as goddesses. "Venus, well, it's very tough to see anything through the murky atmosphere, but scientists have agreed that if they do spot anything, it has to be named after females, goddesses and the like." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VMPLplhDk8I
**VideoFACT Establishment (1984)** : In 1984, VideoFACT was established, funded entirely by MuchMusic. This program was a government agreement where MuchMusic would fund the nascent Canadian music video industry in exchange for keeping MTV out of the country. This ensured MuchMusic had Canadian videos to play, vital for artist exposure. "Yet much music signed on in 1984, another program called Video Fact was established. This was funded entirely by much music in exchange for the government keeping MTV out of the country." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6PGgNCaW4xo
**Vietnam War Disaster** : The Vietnam War was unequivocally described as a "disaster," adding to the widespread disillusionment and societal unrest of the mid-1970s. "The Vietnam War, a disaster." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Villa Diodati Gathering (Summer 1816)** : During the "year without a summer," four artsy types, including John William Polidori and Mary Shelley, were holed up in a mansion near Geneva, Switzerland. To entertain themselves during the dark, cold, wet, rainy days, they drank, had sex, took opium, and competed to create the best horror story, laying the foundation for gothic fiction. "To entertain themselves through these dark, cold, wet, rainy days, these people drank, had sex, and tocopium. And they tried to outdo each other by coming up with the best horror story." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XaLsZFLrMnI
**Vinyl Distros at Shows** : In the early days of the aggressive music scene, attending shows at local halls often involved "vinyl distros." People would bring bins of their own records and set up flea market-style stalls to sell music, which was a primary way for fans to discover and acquire new and niche music. "Back in the day, when you go and see a show at a hall, too, there'd be vinyl distros. People would come with bins of their own records, and they'd set up like almost like a flea market, and they'd sell records." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nEPT8ZVZ0so
**Vinyl Industry Supply Issues (2020)** : In early 2020, a fire at a California plant, which produced 80% of the world's lacquer masters (essential for manufacturing new vinyl), caused significant production delays and higher prices across the vinyl industry due to the lack of these critical components. "In early 2020, there was a fire at a plant in California that made 80% of the world's locker masters." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Vinyl Longevity** : Plastic, the material for vinyl, is highlighted as the best medium for preserving music for future generations due to its durability over hundreds or even thousands of years. "when it comes to music, nothing, and I mean nothing, is better for longevity than vinyl." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Vinyl Manufacturing Quality Eras (Late 1970s - Early 1980s)** : The quality of vinyl record manufacturing significantly declined between the late 1970s and early 1980s. During this period, raw polyvinyl chloride (PVC) was often of poor quality, frequently recycled with impurities. Labels also reduced the amount of vinyl used per record, resulting in lighter discs (e.g., 90 grams) compared to the 180-gram vinyl commonly produced today. Consequently, a record manufactured in 1972 is likely to sound superior to one made in 1979. "When CDs first came out, there was no question that they sounded better than the vinyl records that were coming out at the time. That's because the manufacturing quality of vinyl in the late 70s and early 80s was unmitigated crap." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2g4QSREzpw
**Vinyl Manufacturing Resurgence** : The booming vinyl sales led to old, "mothball pressing plants" being brought back online, and new manufacturers starting from scratch with "brand new, just designed state of the art equipment" to meet the surging demand. "not only did old, mothball pressing plants come back online, but new manufacturers started up from scratch with brand new, just designed state of the art equipment." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Vinyl Market Share (Current)** : Despite its renaissance, vinyl still constitutes a "very, very, very, very, very, very, very, very tiny portion of the market," but it is growing. "vinyl is still a very, very, very, very, very, very, very, very tiny portion of the market. But it is growing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Vinyl Material Origin (1920s)** : Polyvinyl chloride, or vinyl, is a plastic polymer made from petroleum that was first commercially used in the 1920s for the manufacture of sewer pipes, later finding applications in electrical cables, clothing, and flooring. "The word vinyl is short for polyvinyl chloride, which chemist tell us is a plastic polymer made from petroleum that was first used in the 1920s for the manufacturer of sewer pipes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IIJySSo276k
**Vinyl Record Groove Length** : A standard 12-inch, 33 1/3 RPM vinyl album contains a spiral groove approximately 427 meters long, capable of storing about 20 minutes of music per side. "A standard 12-inch, 33-in-a-third, RPM vinyl album, features a spiral groove that runs about 427 meters, the store about 20 minutes worth of music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=72Lei7uug8c
**Vinyl Record Origin Date (1948)** : The modern vinyl record format was introduced in 1948. "It dates back to 1948." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2g4QSREzpw
**Vinyl Record Revival (2008-present)** : The vinyl record format, once "all but dead," experienced a significant revival starting in 2008 with the invention of Record Store Day, leading to double-digit increases in sales year after year. This boom is attributed to factors like audio quality, the ability to display music, and the tactile experience of holding the disc, artwork, liner notes, and lyrics. "The format was all but dead until some desperate record store owners invented record store day in 2008. And since then, we've seen double-digit increases in vinyl sales year after year after year." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tbmssCZ05ro
**Vinyl Record Sizes** : Vinyl records are produced in a range of diameters, including common sizes such as 5, 7, 10, and 12 inches, with some even reaching 16 inches. Nine Inch Nails, for instance, famously released a record with a 9-inch diameter. "Vile can come in all sizes, 5 inches, 7 inches, 10 inches, 12 inches, even 16 inches" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2g4QSREzpw
**Vinyl Records Warping Due to High Temperatures (2021)** : In 2021, exceptionally high temperatures caused vinyl records to warp during transit. This issue affected records moving from warehouses to retail stores and even those shipped through postal services, highlighting the fragility of vinyl in extreme heat. "In fact, it was so hot that vinyl records were getting warped in transit from warehouses to record stores and even in the mail." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-N6IM5Nm5iw
**Vinyl Resurgence and Appeal** : Vinyl records have experienced an unexpected comeback, now outselling CDs in countries like Canada, the US, the UK, and Australia. Many consumers are purchasing vinyl even without a turntable, drawn to its tangible and physical nature as a representation of music. Fans often stream music while holding the vinyl, appreciating the physical ownership, liner notes, lyric sheets, and artwork. "Vinyl is once again outselling CDs and a surprising number of people are buying vinyl, even if they don't have a turntable." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sn5TWBFd0XM
**Vinyl Resurrection (21st Century)** : In the second decade of the 21st century, vinyl experienced an unprecedented resurrection, reversing earlier declarations of its demise. "in the second decade of the 21st century, vinyl began to experience a resurrection unlike anything else we've seen in the history of recorded music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Vinyl Resurrection Origin** : The modern vinyl resurrection can be traced back to independent record store owners in Baltimore who, struggling against digital music and piracy, were inspired by the success of Comic Book Day to create an annual event for records. "The whole vinyl resurrection can be traced to a bunch of dudes in Baltimore who ran independent record stores. They were getting killed." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Vinyl Resurrection from Near Death** : Despite predictions in the late 1980s and early 1990s that vinyl was dying, with CDs dominating the market, vinyl experienced an improbable comeback. By 1999, new turntables were thought to be nearing obsolescence, and for the next 8-9 years, vinyl was on "life support" due to CDs, digital downloads, and piracy. However, around 2006-2007, indie bands began ironically embracing vinyl, and audiophiles continued to resist digital formats. This contrarian movement, combined with the inspiration of "Free Comic Book Day" leading to the creation of Record Store Day in 2008, sparked a consistent double-digit growth in vinyl sales, ultimately leading to vinyl outselling CDs in revenue and units in some countries by 2020, and in Canada by 2024 for the first time in 35 years. "around 2006 and 2007, indie bands started being contrarian and maybe ironic by embracing vinyl." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nX8uAFDzDYM
**Vinyl Revenue Surpassing CD Sales** : As a result of the ongoing boom, revenue generated by vinyl record sales in several countries has surpassed the revenue from compact disc sales, a phenomenon not seen since the late 1980s. "Things are so good that in several countries, the revenue brought in by selling vinyl is greater than the revenue generated by compact disc sales." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tbmssCZ05ro
**Vinyl Revival** : Despite a period when vinyl records were widely dismissed as "toxic landfill" and digital was proclaimed to be the future, the format has experienced a significant revival, contradicting earlier predictions. "There was a time when vinyl was considered nothing more than toxic landfill." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UpWz3X33vSY
**Vinyl Sales Decline (Post-1985)** : After 1985, vinyl sales began to slide rapidly, seemingly an "extinction level event" due to the introduction of the CD. "after 1985, vinyl sales began to slide faster and faster and faster. It seemed that the introduction of the CD was an extinction level event." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Vinyl Sales Exceeding CDs (2020)** : In 2020, the dollar value of vinyl sold in the United States surpassed that of CDs for the first time since 1986, indicating a significant financial turnaround and profitability for the vinyl format. "It got to the point that in 2020, the dollar value of vinyl sold exceeded that of CDs in the United States. That hadn't happened since 1986." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Vinyl Sales Resurgence (Post-2008)** : Following the first Record Store Day in 2008, vinyl sales experienced consistent "double digit growth" year after year, globally. This boom was so significant that old pressing plants were brought back online, and new state-of-the-art manufacturing facilities were established from scratch to meet demand. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Vinyl Sales as Niche Growth** : Vinyl sales represent the sole positive trend in physical music formats, though it remains a niche market when viewed within the broader music industry. "The only bright spot has been vinyl, which if we're honest, is just a niche in the grand scheme of things." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Vinyl Sound Characteristics** : Vinyl recordings are particularly effective at preserving mid-range frequencies, which benefits sounds like "fuzzy rock guitars." However, the format can struggle with extremely high and low frequencies, where CDs excel. The perceived "warmth" of vinyl might be attributable to slight distortion in the bass frequencies. Additionally, sound quality on a vinyl record can degrade as the stylus moves closer to the center, where the smaller circumference and increased stylus speed make accurate groove tracing more difficult. "Vinyl recordings are very good at preserving the mid-range frequencies. That's where fuzzy rock guitars live, so they might sound better on vinyl to some people." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2g4QSREzpw
**Vinyl Surpassing CD Sales (2020)** : In 2020, the dollar value of vinyl sold in the United States exceeded that of CDs, a phenomenon that had not occurred since 1986. This milestone underscored the remarkable resurgence of the vinyl format. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Vinyl as "Rock Snobbery" (21st Century)** : Sourcing vinyl from "cool bands" became a "badge of honor" and a form of "rock snubbery," demonstrating devotion to a band/genre or a deep care for music, even sacrificing money and convenience. "In other words, it became a form of rock snubbery." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Vinyl as High Margin Product (21st Century)** : In the resurgence, vinyl records sell for two to five times the price of compact discs, making them high-margin products for record labels once again. "Vinyl records now sell for two or three or four or five times the price of a compact disc. They are once again high margin products for record labels." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Vinyl vs. CD Audio Quality Debate** : The debate over whether vinyl or compact discs offer superior audio quality is both scientific and emotional, influenced by subjective factors like emotional state and ear health. Technically, CDs are often superior, but perceived "warmth" and mastering differences create a complex listening experience. "To explain this is kind of difficult because the appreciation of audio quality is a very, very subjective thing that involves everything from your emotional state to the health of your ears." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D8GU8ZpSgY8
**Vinyl vs. CD Sound Quality Debate** : Technically, CDs capture an audio signal with more accuracy due to factors like bit depth, sample rate, and Red Book standards. However, the perceived sound quality (warmth, smoothness) is highly dependent on how the music was mixed and mastered for each specific format. Mastering for vinyl involves subtle frequency tweaks to compensate for physical limitations (e.g., deep bass), while CDs can handle a full range of frequencies but require specific mastering to avoid harshness. "From a sheer technical point of view, the CD captures an audio signal with more accuracy." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**Vinyl's Decline and Resurgence (Early 2000s - Present)** : At the start of the 21st century, vinyl records were largely considered an obsolete format, viewed as dusty, scratchy, noisy, space-consuming, and prone to damage, especially in contrast to the then-emerging portable MP3s. Manufacturing significantly declined, with pressing plants closing. Only hardcore collectors and DJs maintained interest. However, from 2008 onwards, vinyl experienced a remarkable "zombie-like" resurgence, and by the 2020s, it began generating more revenue than compact discs due to premium pricing, despite ongoing challenges like supply chain issues and rising costs. "Starting in 2008, a weird thing happened. Like some zombie in one of those old Italian horror movies of the late 70s and early 80s, the format rose from the dead." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2g4QSREzpw
**Vinyl's Near Demise (Early 2000s)** : At the beginning of the 21st century, vinyl records were considered "almost stone-cold dead," with pressing plants closing and remaining ones struggling to find parts for old machines, leading to the format being "relegated to the bin of history." "At the beginning of the decade, vinyl was almost stone-cold dead." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Vinyl's Resurgence (2009)** : By 2009, vinyl records, previously considered dead, were beginning to experience a comeback. "Meanwhile, vinyl was starting to come back." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Vinyl's Resurgence in the 21st Century** : After bottoming out in 2006, vinyl record sales began an unexpected increase. Sales doubled by 2008, doubled again by 2011, and doubled yet again by 2013, indicating a "fascinating resurrection" for a format once thought dead. Despite being a small fraction of the market, it was growing, profitable, and perceived as "cool." "After bottoming out in 2006, a very strange thing happened. Sales of vinyl records started to go up. In 2008, sales had doubled. By 2011, they doubled again, and doubled yet again by 2013. Something weird was going on." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VlIErdSSVOM
**Vinyl's Status in 2000** : At the start of the 21st century, vinyl was considered "dead," with only club DJs keeping the format on life support due to their preference for records over CDs in the booth. "Vinyl, dead, dead, dead." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Vinyl's Unique Artifacts** : Vinyl records inherently come with noise, rumble, hiss, and clicks due to their mechanical playback nature. These artifacts can contribute to a "feeling of realness and warmth" for some listeners. "And vinyl does have problems with noise, rumble, hiss, clicks. Those are artifacts of the mechanical nature of vinyl playback and may contribute to its feeling of realness and warmth." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPok3MemDmE
**Virgin Vinyl (21st Century)** : Modern vinyl production uses "virgin vinyl" instead of recycled chemicals, resulting in higher quality records. "No more recycled chemicals. Instead, what we have is called virgin vinyl, pressed into heavyweight records." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GES0Ms_kaCI
**Virtual Artists (General)** : Before Gorillaz, a variety of virtual artists and performers existed, including the Archies, Josie and the Pussycats, Dr. Teeth and the Electric Mayhem (from The Muppets), Death Clock, The Chipmunks, Prozac, Crazy Frog, Banana Splits, Miss Tula (from the Philippines, represented by female dolls), The Bots (CG creations), and Hatsune Miku (Japanese hologram), as well as Jem and the Holograms. "Back in the 1960s, we had the Archies, who were followed by Josie and the pussycats, then Dr. Teeth and the Electric Mayhem on the Muppets." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iVdTCqpd798
**Visigoths as Historical Precedent** : The Visigoths, marauding tribes who contributed to the fall of the Roman Empire, offer an even earlier historical reference point for the term "Goth." "And if you want to go back, even further, there were the Visigoths, the marauding tribes who helped with the fall of the Roman Empire." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd9sO7c3N1s
**Vision for New Tour: Music & Extreme Sports** : Kevin Lyman's goal for his new touring festival was to differentiate it by combining music with his passion for extreme sports, specifically skateboarding. "And the way he'd stand out is to pair music with his love of extreme sports, skateboarding specifically." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jfQaUV6Bq4
**Vlad Drakuhl / Vlad the Impaler / Dracula Prototype** : Jay Parsons wrote his first-year university History 120 thesis paper on Vlad Drakuhl, also known as Vlad the Impaler, a brutal 15th-century tyrant from Transylvania and Romania. Vlad punished enemies by burning villages and impaling heads on stakes outside his castle to intimidate foes, a practice that Jay humorously likened to a "human kabab scenario." "I decided to write about Vlad Drakuhl, also known as Vlad the Impaler from Transylvania and Romania, 15th century. And he was a brutal tyrant." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=N-3eddy4J7g
**Vocables: Cultural and Historical Use** : Vocables are found in various musical traditions, including many indigenous songs and some Jewish melodies that consist solely of vocables. Historically, classical composers in the 15th century used specific vocables as filler words in their compositions, with "Dildo" being a notable example from the 1500s. "many indigenous songs use vocables. Some Jewish melodies are nothing but vocables." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Vocables: Definition** : Vocables are placeholder or nonsense sounds used in singing, such as "la la la," "na na na," or "doot doot doot." They do not carry any inherent meaning but are used for their phonetic or musical quality. "Those placeholders or nonsense sounds are called vocables. They don't have any meaning." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Vocables: Purpose and Audience Participation** : While vocables lack semantic meaning, they can convey feeling and emotion. They are particularly effective for audience participation, allowing listeners to sing along to a song even without lyrics, enhancing the communal concert experience. "sometimes a song screams for singing that doesn't say anything, doesn't mean anything, but feels good." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Vocal Adaptation Techniques for Aging Singers** : Older singers, whose voices change due to the deterioration of the larynx and vocal cords, often employ two primary strategies: modifying performances to avoid numerous high notes, or lowering the key of the song. Lowering the key transposes high notes to a more manageable range, allowing them to continue performing without damaging their voice. "some older singers will be forced to do one of two things. First, they'll change the performance so they avoid hitting too many high notes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_YjwRApoJhA
**Vogue Magazine - 1972 Puffball Hairdo** : In 1972, David Bowie's wife, Angela, discovered a "puffball hairdo" in *Vogue* magazine. This hairstyle, with its distinctive moment-like profile, was adopted by Bowie and became incredibly trendy at the time, although it looks dated now. "His new wife, Angela, found a puffball hairdo in Vogue magazine." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0xd0Hf5REg4
**Void in British Music (Early 1990s)** : Following the decline of 1980s club and rave culture, the end of the Madchester scene, the failure of shoegaze, and stalled attempts to revitalize the dance scene, there was a "big void in British music," which the British music press was desperate to fill with new scenes and sounds. "Following the decline of the club and rave culture of the 1980s, there was this big void in British music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5q5EnZj1FwE
**Volkswagen "Milky Way" Ad Impact** : The Volkswagen ad featuring "Pink Moon" debuted in 2000, causing sales of Volkswagen cabriolets to jump, achieving its primary mission. Simultaneously, the commercial acted as a powerful sales driver for the song itself, demonstrating how a commercial could sell both a product and the music used in it. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Volkswagen "Milky Way" Ad Turning Point** : The year 2000 marked a turning point in music licensing when the Boston-based agency Arnold World Wide created the "Milky Way" TV spot for Volkswagen's new cabriolet model, targeting 20-somethings who perceived VWs as "cooler" and "more underground." The romantic one-minute ad, featuring four hip young people driving in the countryside, sought a unique musical backdrop after testing songs from The Church, The Cure, and Tones on Tail, ultimately stumbling upon Nick Drake's obscure "Pink Moon." The choice was partly driven by budget and the desire for "indiness." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**Voyager Golden Disk** : Launched in 1977, the Voyager I and Voyager II spacecraft each carry a gold phonograph record called the "Voyager Golden Disk." This durable disk contains images, coordinates, drawings, sounds of Earth, spoken word recordings, and a significant amount of music, intended as an introduction to humanity for any advanced alien species that might discover the probes in interstellar space. "This is the Voyager Golden Disk. It's a phonograph record made of gold, one of the toughest elements in the universe." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hQJ3r-DCoMQ
**Voyager Spacecraft Eight-Track Recorders** : Despite significant advancements in technology on Earth, the Voyager spacecraft, launched in 1977, remain technologically frozen in time. They were equipped with the best available technology of the era, including eight-track tape recorders for data storage, and have continued to function, maintaining a technological state forever fixed in 1977. "The two spacecraft are frozen, technologically speaking, because they were sent on their missions with the best equipment available at the time, including an eight-track tape recorder for data storage." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GO0K_N7Ys7I
**Walkman's Impact on Music Sales and Copyright Debate** : The immense success of The Walkman led to a significant increase in sales for both pre-recorded cassettes and, notably, blank cassettes, which saw sales "go through the roof." This surge directly triggered "the crisis in home taping" and ignited widespread debate about individuals' rights to copy music from various sources. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z9T9wi0vEvc
**Wallet Text Portable Device** : The Wallet Text is a credit-card-sized portable device that notably lacks a screen or a headphone jack. Its primary interface is a USB connection, presumably used for connecting to other devices or for data transfer. "There's the wallet text, which is about the size of a credit card, no screen, no headphone jack, just a USB connection that you connect to whatever I guess." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**Walmart & Kmart – "Godsmack" Boycott (1998)** : In 1998, both Walmart and Kmart, major U.S. retail chains, refused to sell Godsmack's self-titled album due to complaints from a parent. The complaints cited the album's liner notes, which contained a Wiccan pentagram and references to "Salem Witches," as well as "outrageously offensive" lyrics and the absence of a parental advisory warning. "Both Walmart and Kmart wouldn't sell the record." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_tbKuJR0cPs
**Walter the Mathematician's Role** : Walter the Mathematician was enlisted to create a scale from 1 to 10 to assign a "power ranking" for each song's "one-hit wonder-ness." "I also enlisted Walter the Mathematician to create a scale from 1 to 10, which assigned each song a power ranking when it came to its one-hit wonder-ness." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZcF5JMhBj2M
**Warped Magazine as Name Origin** : The name "Warped" for the tour was adopted after Kevin Lyman decided against "The Bomb." The name came from a magazine he had previously worked on. "That's when he went with Warped, the name of a magazine he'd worked on in the past." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jfQaUV6Bq4
**Warped Tour Wikipedia Page** : There is a Wikipedia page dedicated to tracking all the acts that have played the Warped Tour, but keeping it updated is a challenging task due to the sheer volume and variability of performers. "There is a Wikipedia page that does its best to keep track of all the Warp acts, but it's been a struggle." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jfQaUV6Bq4
**Warren Cuccurullo (Duran Duran) – Fired 2001; Went Into Gay Porn and Sold His Own Dildo** : Warren Cuccurullo was a one-time Duran Duran member fired from the band in 2001. He subsequently appeared in gay pornography and sold a sex toy called the Rock Rod modeled after himself. "He went on to do gay porn." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnZx8EGnszw
**Warren Hill's Discovery of The Velvet Underground Acetate** : Warren Hill, a record collector from Montreal, discovered a rare 1966 Velvet Underground acetate at a flea market in the Chelsea neighborhood of New York, acquiring it for 75 cents. This acetate contained unique recordings that preceded their debut album. "a guy named Warren Hill, a record collector from Montreal, stumbled across it at a flea market in the Chelsea neighborhood of New York." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IIJySSo276k
**Weather Control Machines Conspiracy Theory** : The existence of "weather control machines" is included in a list of conspiracy theories. "weather control machines" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pmKjZzypzvg
**Website for Song Similarities** : For those interested in discovering songs that sound alike, the website SoundsJustLike.com is recommended. "If this is your thing, these days, you tunes, try a website called SoundsJustLike.com." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rfObVld9rIE
**Weezer – Rivers Cuomo's Childhood Asthma Nickname** : Rivers Cuomo had bad asthma as a kid and was called "Weezer" by friends. "Rivers Cuomo had bad asthma as a kid, so his friends called him Weezer." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Weezer's Roomba Merch – "Weezer Branded Robot Vacuum Cleaner" with Silent W** : Weezer offered a branded robot vacuum cleaner called a Roomba (with a silent W). They gave five away in a contest surrounding an album release. "They once offered a Weezer branded robot vacuum cleaner." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZbHRIh3RUoo
**Werner Herzog's "Strozek" (1977) and Ian Curtis** : Ian Curtis watched the 1977 Werner Herzog film "Strozek" just before his suicide. The movie centers on a musician and former mental patient who travels to the US, encounters severe money and relationship problems, and whose main character may or may not have killed himself. "Once Deborah was gone, Ian sat down to watch a movie on the telly. A 1977 Werner Herzog film entitled Strozek." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZhzjZixCcK8
**Wernicke's Area and Music Appreciation** : Wernicke's area, positioned near Broca's area and the auditory cortex, is a region of the brain that plays a role in the analysis and enjoyment of music. "We use this part of the brain to analyze and enjoy music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mTbagQE7akQ
**West Coast Hardcore Tour Network** : Hardcore scenes across the West Coast, though emerging almost simultaneously, were interconnected through bands that toured relentlessly. This was facilitated by a network of hardcore-friendly venues and fans, ensuring the genre's widespread reach. "These scenes all emerged practically simultaneously, and were linked together by bands who toured non-stop using a network of hardcore friendly venues and fans." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0qigzi1j81U
**West German Radio Electronic Studio (1950s)** : In the 1950s, West German Radio established an experimental electronic studio. This studio played a crucial role in exposing German musicians to nearly 20 years of electronic sounds by the turn of the 1970s, significantly influencing artists like Kraftwerk and the development of a distinct German musical identity. "In the 1950s, West German radio built an experimental electronic studio" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OiqdZfxdBP0
**Western Music Theory - 12-Note Scale** : Western music is fundamentally structured around "a scale of 12 notes." These notes are "related to each other mathematically by a series of ratios known as intervals of a semitone." "Western music is based on a scale of 12 notes." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rfObVld9rIE
**Western Music Theory - Building Blocks** : Combinations and patterns of the 12 notes in Western music reveal "chords, melodies, harmonies, and so on," which are considered "the building blocks of our music," from symphonies to pop songs. "Playing those notes in certain combinations or patterns reveal things like chords, melodies, harmonies, and so on." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rfObVld9rIE
**Wet Leg – Random Emoji Combination on a Keyboard** : Rhian Teasdale and Hester Chambers found the name by randomly combining emojis together on a keyboard. "Re and T-Stale and Hester Chambers found the name by combining random emojis together on a keyboard." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Whatzatsong.com** : A prominent online platform that serves as a valuable resource for discussion and research related to the identification of unknown songs. "You can also try whatzatsong.com, which is a great place for discussion and research." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UsPoPUByjYw
**White Bell Bird's Loudness** : Scientists successfully measured the song of the white bell bird, determining it to be 125 decibels. This volume is louder than the PA systems at most rock concerts, equivalent to standing near a jet engine, and reaches the threshold of physical pain. "The answer is 125 decibels." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=72Lei7uug8c
**White Zombie – Rob Zombie's Love of a 1932 Bela Lugosi Horror Film** : Rob Zombie (real name Rob Cummings) named the band after a 1932 Bela Lugosi horror film called White Zombie. "One of his favorite was a 1932 Bela Lugosi movie called White Zombie." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NCQ1ERgSfeE
**Wholesale Devaluation of Music in the 21st Century** : The most significant shift in music this century is the wholesale devaluation of its monetary worth. In the 2000s, CDs cost $10-$20, requiring financial investment and careful selection by consumers who would then listen repeatedly. Today, music is an oversupplied product, effectively free or very cheap through streaming services offering millions of songs and 100,000 new uploads daily. This abundance leads to consumers skipping songs frequently (e.g., 25% skip unfamiliar songs in 5 seconds, 35% by 30 seconds), indicating a lack of financial investment, risk, and short attention spans. While music retains emotional and cultural value, its monetary worth has plummeted. Despite complaints about low artist payouts, many users utilize free tiers or pay minimal subscription fees, often unwilling to pay more to better compensate artists. This situation has made music a low-cost, low-margin commodity, impacting how and by whom music is made for the foreseeable future. "So this, the devaluation of music is my pick for the most important thing to happen to rock at all music so far in this century." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nX8uAFDzDYM
**Why 33⅓ RPM? – Derived from Vitaphone Film Sync System; Math: 3,600 ÷ 108 = 33⅓** : The 33⅓ RPM speed was not arbitrary but derived from the Vitaphone film-sync system introduced in 1931 when movies first had synchronized sound. Film reels were 1,000 feet of 35mm film, good for 11 minutes of viewing. To make audio last exactly 11 minutes to match one film reel, the 3,600 RPM motor required a 108:1 gear ratio: 3,600 ÷ 108 = 33⅓. RCA tried to introduce this format to the public but the Great Depression killed consumer interest, and RCA foolishly let all the patents expire. Columbia Records later picked up the expired technology. "RCA let all the patents expire. Fast forward to the late 1940s." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=I291j5WBc4k
**Why 45 RPM and the Large 1.5-Inch Hole? – RCA's Revenge; Jukebox Torque Distribution** : When RCA discovered Columbia had beaten them using their own expired patents, they fought back with a 7-inch, 45 RPM single also made from PVC. The 45 RPM speed delivers a 35% increase in groove velocity compared to 33⅓, greatly reducing distortion and loss of high frequencies. The unusually large 1.5-inch center hole served two purposes: (1) RCA initially sold turntables that could only play 45s to lock consumers into their format; (2) the larger hole distributed the sudden rotational torque over a longer path (π × 1.5 ≈ 4.71 inches), preventing the hole from going out of round — crucial for the jukebox industry, which was enormous business in the 1940s–50s. "The larger hole allowed the sudden rotational force to be distributed over a longer path, pi times 1.5 or about 4.71 inches." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=I291j5WBc4k
**Why 78 RPM? – The Handbook Writer Just Had a Machine That Ran at That Speed** : The first turntable (the gramophone) was introduced in the early 1890s playing at "about 70 RPM." By the end of the 19th century a handbook for record manufacturers recommended the use of governors to standardize speed at 78 RPM. The reason that exact speed was chosen: whoever wrote the handbook had a machine that turned at that speed. The standard propulsion motors selected to power electric turntables ran at 3,600 RPM with a 46:1 gear ratio, yielding 78.26 RPM at 60 Hz — close enough. "The most likely reason is that whoever wrote the handbook had a machine that turned at that speed." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=I291j5WBc4k
**Widespread Adoption of Licensing Strategy** : Following successful campaigns by artists like Moby and Sting, thousands of other acts were inspired to pursue similar music licensing strategies. This led to a significant increase in demand for licensing deals across the industry. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**William Burroughs' Paintings** : Two paintings by the poet William Burroughs were owned by John Frusciante and were among the possessions lost in his house fire in 1996. "He lost all his paintings, including two that had been painted by poet William Burrows." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TESrEzVHLCc
**William Morris Endeavor Agency** : The William Morris Endeavor agency provided the tour routing for Weezer's spring tour with The Pixies, which Rivers Cuomo demonstrated using his "Drive Times" app. "Here is the routing we got from Weezer's agent, William Morris, Endeavor agency." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LYRPxtP61JM
**William Tanger's Connection to Rather Attack** : Later in the decade, William Tanger, a mentally ill man who believed the media, particularly Dan Rather and CBS News, was beaming signals into his head, was identified as the likely attacker of Dan Rather. Tanger was eventually convicted for the August 31, 1994 murder of an NBC technician, and at his trial, a psychologist testified that he was definitely the one who assaulted Rather. Tanger was jailed and paroled in 2010. "Later in the decade, we heard that the attacker had been identified as a guy named William Tanger, a mentally ill man who believed that the media, specifically Dan Rather and CBS news, had been beaming signals into his head." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4PlieAEthSg
**WinAMP and MP3 Compression (April 12, 1997)** : WinAMP, released as a free download on April 12, 1997, revolutionized digital music by enabling easy "ripping" of data from CDs to computer hard drives.
