Critically, it used the Motion Picture Expert's Group Layer 3 (MP3) technology, which compressed audio files significantly compared to giant wave files, allowing users to store a "lot more" music on their computers and facilitating early online file sharing. "WinAMP showed up as a free download on April the 12th, 1997 for all operating systems. WinAMP allowed anyone to easily transfer the data from a CD to a computer's hard drive." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NAroxFbYkRw
**Windows 95's Impact on Digital Music** : The "fat" nature of the Windows 95 operating system, requiring nearly 60 megabytes of hard drive space and 26 floppy disks for installation, led Bill Gates to bundle CD-ROM drives with PCs. This made CD-ROM drives standard, allowing users to realize they could transfer music data from audio CDs to their computers using programs like WinAMP and the MP3 format, initiating the digital music revolution. "We got here because the Windows 95 operating system was so fat." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NAroxFbYkRw
**Winnipeg's Obsession with Phantom of the Paradise** : The 1974 film "Phantom of the Paradise" was a complete box office flop everywhere in the world except Winnipeg, where it stayed in theaters for months and became an "essential part of the musical DNA" of the city. This local phenomenon is so ingrained that annual festivals are held, attracting stars from the movie. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqTFinLk3oU
**Winter of Discontent (1978-79)** : This period was marked by the UK being paralyzed by widespread strikes and experiencing the coldest winter in 15 years, with blizzards and coal shortages, all of which occurred amidst ongoing rolling blackouts. This severe climate of social and economic hardship intensified the punk movement. "The winter of 1978-79 was known as the winter of discontent. Not only was the country paralyzed by strikes, but it was the coldest winter in 15 years." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YaYaFW2q6qI
**Wolf River Harbor Drowning Incident** : Wolf River Harbor in Memphis is connected to the Mississippi River and was the location where Jeff Buckley accidentally drowned on May 29, 1997. He had swum there before, but this time, he was fully clothed and wearing heavy boots, and a boat's wake led to his disappearance. "Wolf River Harbor, which is connected to the Mississippi River, listening to music. Jeff had gone swimming there a few times before, and decided to do it again." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MmXDbXI6Sqk
**Wolfmother – Chosen at Last Second From a Tom Robbins Novel ("Skinny Legs")** : When the band showed up for their first gig without a name, bassist Chris Ross had been reading a Tom Robbins novel called Skinny Legs that contained the word "wolfmother." With no time left, they adopted it. Singer Andrew Stockdale and drummer Myles Heskett weren't crazy about it but accepted it on the spot. "With time running out before they were due on stage, they said, oh, okay, we're Wolfmother." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkYHaJKohVQ
**Women Creating Their Own Space** : In response to male-dominated music scenes like grunge, a group of women became "determined to carve out their own space," creating music "about them and for them" that addressed issues they cared about and could rock as hard, or harder, than male-led bands. This self-determination was the genesis of the Riot Grrrl movement. "This time, though, a group of women were determined to carve out their own space." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qss5kC2FUx0
**Working as a Musician in the 21st Century** : Being a musician in the 21st century requires a strong focus on business issues due to the changing economy, which differs significantly from previous eras where selling records and playing gigs sufficed. Multiple revenue sources, including sales, streaming, record labels, patrons, crowdfunding, merchandise, and licensing, are now necessary for survival. ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sfie4YYncxk
**World News Daily Report - Satirical News Source** : The story about the KGB financing the punk movement, attributed to an alleged retired agent Alexander Vaternukhov Volition, originated from World News Daily Report, which is a satirical news website similar to The Onion. This source is known for publishing fabricated stories, making the KGB punk plot theory entirely false. "It came from a site called World News Daily Report, which is a satirical site like the Onion." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YeNWDtN_sHE
**World War II Impact on Music Industry** : World War II significantly disrupted the music industry, particularly Big Bands, by drafting musicians, closing dance halls, and restricting touring due to gasoline rationing. These disruptions, coupled with the American Federation of Musicians strike, indirectly led to the rise of new genres like rock and roll and the prominence of solo singers. "When the war started, Big Band started to suffer." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**World War II Shellac Crisis and Record Shortage (1940s)** : The Japanese invasion of the Malay Peninsula on December 8, 1941, severely impacted the recording industry by cutting off the world's primary supply of shellac. Shellac, derived from the waxy resin secreted by female lac bugs found in the region (modern-day Malaysia, Thailand, Myanmar, Singapore), was a crucial component (one-third) of phonograph records in the 1940s, alongside minerals, cotton fibers, and carbon. This interruption led to a significant record shortage, forcing consumers to trade in old records to acquire new ones. "But because the Meile Peninsula was now under hostile control by the Japanese Imperial Army, the world's supply of raw materials for shellac, and therefore records, was shut down." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_Pi7JLwAeOI
**World War II Surveillance Method** : A specific surveillance method used during World War II, involving "pigeon camera," was the subject of The Tragically Hip's song "Pigeon Camera" from the album "Fully Completely." "Pigeon camera was about a World War II surveillance method." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oMBmdfrA47Q
**World War II and Paul McCartney's Parents' Meeting (1941)** : Paul McCartney's parents, Jim Mohin and Mary, met and fell in love during a German Luftwaffe bombing raid (The Blitz) in Liverpool in 1941. Jim, 38, was excused from military service due to a childhood ear injury (from a 1912 fall), and the air raid led him to his mother's house where Mary was visiting, creating the circumstances for their relationship. "had the Luftwaffe not bomb liverpool that night in 1941, he would have never had a chance to get to know Mary so well." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V7WjNR1ujhk
**World War II's Impact on Big Dance Bands** : During World War II, big dance bands experienced a significant decline as many of their members were drafted into military service. The prohibitive cost of maintaining large groups of ten or more musicians led to the emergence of smaller combos of three to five players in the 1940s. "During World War II, the big dance bands of the era were stripped of their members as many were drafted, maintaining a group of ten or twelve or more members became too expensive. And the big bands gave way to smaller combos of three, four or five players." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dJiSZ4X0F2g
**World's Biggest Record Show (Utrecht)** : The world's largest record show takes place every November in Utrecht, Netherlands. This event features at least 500 dealers spread across a giant convention hall over a two-day period. "The World's biggest record show was held every November in Utrecht in the Netherlands. At least 500 dealers set up for two days inside a giant convention hall." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aSk1XQHNkd8
**XWA Montreal – First Licensed North American Radio Station; First Entertainment Broadcast May 20, 1920** : The first licensed radio station in North America was XWA (the X standing for "experimental"), run by the Marconi Wireless Telegraph Company of Canada. Voice transmission tests began in spring 1919 using a 500-watt transmitter at 173 Williams Street in Montreal; bored engineers started grabbing phonograph records from the nearby Layton Brothers record store to play instead of reciting the alphabet — giving Layton Brothers an inadvertent claim as radio's first advertiser. The first documented entertainment broadcast for the general public occurred on May 20, 1920, when soprano Dorothy Hutton sang two ballads from the XWA studio. KDKA in Pittsburgh — often cited by Americans as the first radio station — did not sign on until November 2, 1926. "KDKA didn't go on the air until November 2, 1926 — later." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JvOEAzmC6l0
**Yakuza** : In Gorillaz lore, during the band's hiatus, the character Noodle returned to Japan and took over the Yakuza. "Nudel went back to Japan and took over the Yakuza." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iVdTCqpd798
**Year 2000 as Music Industry Divide** : The year 2000 is identified as a clear dividing line, distinguishing between artists who rose to prominence in the pre-internet era and those who emerged in the post-internet landscape. "The year 2000 is a nice, neat division between pre-internet and post-internet artists." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Year Without a Summer (1816)** : Resulted from the Mount Tambora eruption, causing a global temperature drop of almost a full degree. Led to food shortages, famines, disease outbreaks, and severe storms in Europe, providing the dark atmosphere for the birth of gothic fiction. "1816 has gone down in history as the year without a summer." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XaLsZFLrMnI
**You Hear Me** : You Hear Me is a mental health organization that addresses issues of bullying and peer pressure, and it was among the causes supported by the Warped Tour. "And you hear me as another mental health organization that also deals with bullying and peer pressure." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7jfQaUV6Bq4
**YouTube Acquisition by Google (Late 2006)** : Within months of its launch, YouTube was acquired by Google for $1.65 billion. Critics initially viewed this as Google buying "nothing but headaches" due to the rampant illegal uploading of copyrighted material by users. "In months after its launch, YouTube was acquired by Google for $1.65 billion. Critics said that Google was buying nothing but headaches because so many users insisted on illegally uploading copyrighted material." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**YouTube Origin Story: Janet Jackson Halftime Show (February 1, 2004)** : The most cited origin story links YouTube's creation to Jawed Karim's frustration in 2005 over the inability to easily find and share video of Janet Jackson's nipple exposure during the 2004 Super Bowl halftime show, which was the most replayed DVR moment at the time. "Now here's what we think is the real story. We begin on the evening of February 1, 2004...But if there was a way for people to easily upload and share video, whatever the true origin story, YouTube was born." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**YouTube Origin Story: Video Dating Site Pivot (Early 2005)** : Another proposed origin suggests that Hurley and Chen initially conceived YouTube as a video dating site, attempting to compete with "Hot or Not" by offering women $100 on Craigslist to upload pictures, but pivoted when the response was tepid. "Another story is that Hurley and Chen had an idea for a video dating site. They wanted to compete with a site called Hot or Not. They began by offering women $100 on Craigslist to upload pictures of themselves." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**YouTube Origin Story: Video Sharing Frustration (2005)** : One account attributes YouTube's inception to Chad Hurley and Steve Chen's difficulty sharing videos they shot at a party in 2005, highlighting the lack of an easy online platform at the time. "The story goes that Hurley and Chen were at a party in 2005. They had shot some videos and wanted to share them online, but there was no way to do that easily." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**YouTube and iTunes/iPod Impact** : In the years following the 1990s, YouTube would emerge to disrupt traditional music video channels, and iTunes and iPods would further transform music consumption and the industry. "In a few years, something called YouTube would come along and kill off the traditional music video channel. Then we had iTunes and iPods." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7JP2tjJibTQ
**YouTube's Impact on CD Sales (2005)** : The launch of YouTube in 2005 led to another significant drop in CD sales, as consumers increasingly turned to the platform to find and consume music online. "Then, in 2005, YouTube went online and there was another big drop as people started looking for music that way." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**YouTube's Launch and Origin** : YouTube went online on February 14, 2005, initially envisioned as a video posting site. Its musical inspiration stemmed from founder Jawed Karim's frustration over the lack of online clips of Janet Jackson and Justin Timberlake's Super Bowl halftime show wardrobe malfunction (February 1, 2004), and the massive Indian Ocean tsunami at the end of that year, leading him and partners Chad Hurley and Steve Chen to create a searchable video repository. "On February 14, 2005, YouTube went online for the very first time." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**YouTube's Musical Content and Copyright Issues** : After its launch, users quickly began uploading songs, music videos, and live gig footage to YouTube, creating "a huge, huge copyright problem," which did not deter Google from acquiring the platform on October 9, 2006. "But then people started uploading songs. They started uploading music videos." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**YouTube's Scale and Content Volume** : YouTube hosts tens of millions of songs, receives 400 hours of video uploads every minute, and garners billions of views daily, making it an expansive repository for both mainstream and obscure content. "Tens of millions. And 400 hours of video are uploaded every minute." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**Younger Writers and Background Contributors Selling Rights** : The trend of selling music rights extends beyond established artists to include young writers and individuals who often work in the background, also participating in these transactions. "Young writers and also people who often work in the background are also in on the act." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pe40SqDrpZ0
**Youth Music Criticism (Historical)** : The phenomenon of older generations complaining that the music of young people is worse than music from "their day" is a historically recurring argument that "you will never win," with similar complaints documented since the days of Aristotle concerning the youth of ancient Greece. "We all need to remember that every generation has the right to believe that the music of their youth is the greatest music of all time. This is an argument that you will never win, even though we've heard the same complaints about the music of young people since, and I'm serious about this, since the days of Aristotle, who was always complaining about the music of the youth of ancient Greece." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2wZydttn4Cs
**Youth as Driver of Music Trends** : Youth is consistently identified as the primary force driving music trends and changes in popular taste. Young people who had embraced pop music in the mid-1990s matured into the early 2000s, developing a desire for music with "more teeth, with more bite," which bands like The Strokes were poised to provide. "Music is always driven by youth. The young people that started getting into music with pop in the middle of the 90s had aged into a time when they wanted something with more teeth, with more bite." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UpWz3X33vSY
**Youth as Drivers of Music and Society** : Music is consistently driven by youth and is always downstream from societal trends. Young people, especially with access to vast musical libraries today, absorb diverse influences, which will ultimately lead them to create new music that could "change the world in a way that we can't even comprehend." "Music is always, always driven by youth and music is always downstream from society. So whatever is happening in society, we'll get into music because of youth." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4xxgPX-aR90
**Yurgen Volmer's Origin of Beatles Haircut** : Yurgen Volmer, a friend of Astrid Kircher and Stu Sutcliffe, met John Lennon and Paul McCartney in Paris in 1961. Volmer wore his hair long and over his forehead, a style that "entranced" Lennon and McCartney, who asked him to cut their hair this way, initiating the Beatles' iconic mop top look. "There is some photographic evidence to suggest that Volmer had been wearing his hair long and in a part, something that Paul described as a long-haired Hitler thing." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VnXBJqKwkrw
**Zeke Zettner's Death** : Zeke Zettner, who was Dave Alexander's first replacement on bass for The Stooges for a time, died in 1973. "The exetner, a one-time rode in Alexander's first replacement on bass, died in 1973." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=w-rihv544I8
**Zero Freitas's Record Collection** : Zero Freitas, a collector from Brazil, embarked on a mission to amass a private collection encompassing every record ever made, accumulating at least 8 million records and over 100,000 compact discs, though even this vast collection doesn't cover all music. "He has at least 8 million records and more than 100,000 compact discs, which is nice, but it still doesn't cover everything." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**Zero Point Energy** : This concept figures prominently in investigations of "unidentified aerial phenomenon." Tom DeLonge suspects the CIA may have tapped his phone regarding his knowledge of zero point energy. "Maybe it's about zero point energy, which is something that figures prominently an unidentified aerial phenomenon investigations" ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pmKjZzypzvg
**Ziggy Stardust's Cultural Legacy** : Beyond music, David Bowie's Ziggy Stardust character had a profound cultural impact, inspiring books, PhD theses, and even having a hotel in Thailand and a village in Cyprus named after him. Ziggy also influenced subcultures like Goths and future industrial stars, making him arguably the "most famous and influential fictional rock star ever conceived." "Ziggy Stardust was quite simply the most famous and influential fictional rock star ever conceived." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2RKchFW42Jc
**Zoroastrianism (Religion)** : The ancient religion of Zoroastrianism is referenced in 21 Pilots' *Trench* album mythology through the city of Dema. In Zoroastrianism, Dema refers to a "tower of silence," a structure where followers traditionally placed dead bodies to be consumed by vultures. "It describes a world built around the city of dma, which translated from the religion of Zoroastrianism, means tower of silence." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8sAxFvktx-A
**iGoGo MP3 Player and Shoulder Massager** : The iGoGo is a unique, three-part device that combines an MP3 player with a shoulder massager. It comprises a central unit that holds the music and two separate pads designed to be placed on the shoulders. "Then there's IgoGo, which is a combination MP3 player in shoulder massager. No, really, it comes in three parts. The unit with the music and two pads you place in your shoulders." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**iPhone Launch and Definition (2007)** : Launched on June 29th, 2007, the iPhone was introduced by Apple as a singular device combining three functionalities: a widescreen iPod with touch controls, a revolutionary mobile phone, and a breakthrough internet communications device. "But then it all changed forever on June 29th, 2007, when this happened." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**iPhone as a Convergent Device** : The iPhone was presented as a single, unified device, converging the functionalities of an iPod, a mobile phone, and an internet communicator, which was a key aspect of its revolutionary impact on personal technology. "These are not three separate devices. This is one device. And we are calling it iPhone." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19Z_AAEy8Bc
**iPod Classic Capacity** : The iPod Classic was widely regarded as "the best iPod" by many users, offering a substantial storage capacity of up to 160 gigabytes. This allowed it to hold approximately 40,000 songs or 3,500 albums, marking a dramatic increase in portable music storage compared to earlier devices like the Diamond Rio, which could hold only about an hour of music. "For many, the best iPod was known as the Classic, which could hold up to 160 gigs of songs. That's good for about 40,000 songs in your pocket. That's like 3,500 albums, which is a hell of a jump from those old diamond reo players that could hold maybe an hours with the music." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fhq9WVeiE_U
**iPod Introduction (October 2001)** : The iPod was introduced in October 2001 and, despite not being the first digital music player, was significantly superior to existing alternatives. Its arrival quickly made portable cassette and CD players seem outdated. "In October 2001, the iPod was introduced. This wasn't the first digital music player, but it was way, way better than anything else out there. An overnight portable cassette and CD players seemed to be about as cool as wind up gramophones." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**iPod Launch and Impact (2001)** : Introduced on October 23rd, 2001, following the 9/11 events, the iPod became a significant MP3 player and a status symbol, credited with saving Apple from bankruptcy. It sold nearly 400 million units worldwide, revolutionized music portability, allowed for custom playlists and song skipping, and, combined with iTunes, shifted control from record labels and stores to consumers. "That's when Apple introduced the iPod, this little slab of metal glass and plastic." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**iPod Sales Figures** : From its launch until Apple ceased reporting separate sales, nearly 400 million iPod units were sold worldwide, underscoring its massive impact and commercial success. "nearly 400 million units were sold worldwide." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**iTunes Evolution from Management Software to Store** : Initially, iTunes, introduced for Mac users, served solely as music management software to organize ripped music. It evolved into an online music store in April 2003, with its popularity significantly expanding after becoming available for Windows in late 2003. "It wasn't until April of 2003 that iTunes became an online music store." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XM1RvL7Kdts
**iTunes LP (2009)** : Apple introduced iTunes LP in 2009 as a form of interactive artwork, aiming to preserve and evolve the art form in the digital realm. This format allowed for interactive content and images connected to individual tracks, exemplified by Nine Inch Nails' "The Slip." "In 2009, Apple introduced something called the iTunes LP, which is a form of interactive artwork." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pwebM3TfSdg
**iTunes Music Store Launch (2003)** : The iTunes Music Store debuted in 2003, initially offering hundreds of thousands of songs for sale, and has since grown to include approximately 60 million purchasable tracks. "Then in 2003 came the iTunes music star." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**iTunes Music Store Launch (April 28, 2003)** : The iTunes Music Store officially opened for business on April 28, 2003. It quickly dominated the digital music market, eventually capturing up to 70% of it, a development that record labels disliked due to their loss of control over distribution and pricing. "And on April 28, 2003, the iTunes Music Store opened for business. iTunes would eventually have up to 70% of the marketplace for digital music, something that the labels absolutely hated because they'd lost control." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**iTunes Music Store and Legal Single Purchases** : The introduction of the iTunes Music Store on January 9th, 2001, dealt another "big stake through the heart" of the album era, as it allowed consumers to legitimately and legally purchase individual tracks instead of full albums, leading to a decline in album sales. "Another big stake through the heart was the introduction of the iTunes music store on January 9th, 2001." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7glrAoXtkA
**iTunes Music Store and the Return of the Single (2003)** : The launch of the iTunes Music Store in 2003 brought about the resurgence of the single, as albums were dismantled and individual tracks became available for purchase at 99 cents each, significantly impacting the traditional album concept. "Then along came the iTunes music store in 2003. Albums were broken up and sold as individual tracks for 99 cents." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sc9HApnyBLM
**iTunes Software Deterioration** : Introduced on January 9, 2001, after Apple acquired Sound Jam, iTunes software began as a simple and functional tool. However, "over the years, though, it has become bloated and dysfunctional and tasked with doing too many things." It struggles as a combined music store, iPhone/iPad/iPod device manager, often exhibiting buggy syncing, archaic library management, high memory consumption, slow performance, difficulty with large libraries (leading to seizing or crashing), and lack of support for high-resolution audio files like FLAQ, performing "awful on Windows machines." "Over the years, though, it has become bloated and dysfunctional and tasked with doing too many things." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7Zniri9uSlc
**iTunes Software Introduction (January 2001)** : Apple introduced iTunes in January 2001. Initially, it served as music management software for Mac users to organize CDs ripped to their hard drives or other collected digital music. It also allowed users to burn compilation CDs, analogous to old cassette mixtapes. "In January 2001, iTunes was introduced. This was originally Music Management Software for people with Macs, who had ripped CDs to their hard drive or had collected music from somewhere online. You could also use iTunes to burn compilation CDs, the digital equivalent of the old cassette mixtape." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BHkJFA0LMro
**iTunes and Album Sales** : The rise of iTunes contributed to a shift in music consumption habits, with fans becoming accustomed to buying single songs rather than full albums, which prompted record labels to promote concept albums as a way to encourage full album purchases. "Itunes and downloading. See fans were getting far too used to the idea of being able to just buy one song at a time and not buying full albums." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1KyNY3zv6uc
**iTunes and Streaming Saving the Music Industry** : After Napster caused CD sales to plummet, Steve Jobs' iTunes Music Store offered the record labels a legitimate digital download option, quickly dominating the market. Subsequently, the rise of streaming services, starting with Rhapsody in 2001 and led by Spotify, became the primary revenue source for labels (65-80% today), effectively "saving the music industry" from the brink of collapse caused by illegal file sharing. "Streaming saved the music industry." ← https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NAroxFbYkRw
